虞初新志 - New Records of Yu Chu

    Home
    1

    虞初新志 清 张潮

    　　自叙
    　　古今小说家言，指不胜偻，大都饾饤人物、补缀欣戚，累牍连篇。非不详赡，然优孟、叔敖，徒得其似而未传其真，强笑不欢，强哭不戚，乌足令耽奇揽异之士心开神释、色飞眉舞哉！况天壤间灏气卷舒，鼓荡激薄，变态万状，一切荒诞奇僻、可喜可愕可歌可泣之事，古之所有不必今之所无、古之所无忽为今之所有，固不仅飞仙盗侠、牛鬼蛇神如夷坚、艳异所载者为奇矣。此虞初一书，汤临川称为小说家之珍珠船，点校之以传世，洵有取尔也。独是原本所撰述，尽摭唐人轶事，唐以后无闻焉。

    Explanation

    1

    Yu Chou's New Collection by Zhang Chao of the Qing Dynasty

    Preface
    Classical Chinese novels are too numerous to count, mostly piecing together characters and emotions in a disjointed manner, spanning many pages. They are not lacking in detail, yet they only capture their semblance and not their true essence; forced laughter is not joyful, forced tears are not sorrowful. How could they satisfy the curiosity and delight of those who seek the strange and extraordinary? The vast universe with its dynamic forces, agitated and stirred, undergoes a myriad of transformations. All absurd and peculiar, joyful, startling, worthy of song, and worthy of tears, what was possible in the past is not necessarily impossible today, and what was impossible in the past may be possible today. It is not just flying immortals, thieves, demons, and ghosts like those recorded in the Yi Jian Zhi and Yan Yi Zhi that are considered strange. This book, Yu Chou's New Collection, was called the "Pearl Ship of the novelists" by Tang Lin-chuan, who edited and annotated it for posterity, indeed, it has its merits. However, the original work compiled mostly anecdotes from the Tang Dynasty, with little known about it after the Tang Dynasty.

    2

    临川续之，合为十二卷，其间调笑滑稽、离奇诡异，无不引人着胜。究亦简帙无多，搜采未广，予是以慨然有虞初后志之辑，需之岁月，始可成书。先以虞初新志授梓问世。其事多近代也，其文多时贤也，事奇而核，文隽而工，写照传神，彷摹毕肖，诚所谓古有而今不必无、古无而今不必不有，且为理之所无，竟为事之所有者，读之令人无端而喜、无端而愕、无端而欲歌欲泣，诚得其真，而非仅得其似也夫！岂强笑不欢强哭不戚、饾饤补缀之稗官小说可同日语哉！学士大夫酬应之余、伊吾之暇，取是篇而浏览之，匪惟涤烦祛倦，抑且纵横俯仰，开拓心胸，具达观而发旷怀也已。康熙癸亥新秋心斋张潮撰

    Explanation

    2

    The Continuation of Linchuan, compiled into twelve volumes, contains humorous, witty, strange, and bizarre stories that all attract readers to delight. However, the work is not extensive, and the collection is not broad. Thus, I have determined to compile a work similar to the Yu Chou Hou Zhi (A Collection of Strange Tales from the Later Yu Chou), which will take time to complete. I first published the Yu Chou Xin Zhi (New Collection of Strange Tales from Yu Chou) to make it known to the world. The stories in this work are mostly modern, and the authors are contemporary scholars. The stories are strange yet factual, and the writing is elegant and skillful. They capture the essence and spirit, imitating perfectly. They are truly capturing the essence, not just the appearance. They suggest that what was possible in ancient times is still possible today, and what was impossible then might be possible now. Moreover, they are beyond reason but are real. Reading them can make one feel inexplicably joyful, inexplicably startled, and inexplicably moved to sing or weep. They are truly capturing the essence, not just the appearance. How can they be compared to forced laughter and crying, or patchwork novels? Scholars and officials, during their leisure time, can read this piece. It not only relieves boredom and fatigue but also broadens the mind and fosters a broad perspective. The Yu Chou Xin Zhi was compiled in the autumn of the year 1693 by Zhang Chao under the pseudonym Xinzhai.

    3

    凡例
    　　文人锐志钻研，无非经传子史；学士驰情渔猎，多属世说稗官。虽短咏长歌允称游戏，即填词杂剧备极滑稽，未免数见而不鲜，抑亦常谈而多复。兹集仿虞初之选辑，仿若士之点评，任诞矜奇，率皆实事；搜神拈异，绝不雷同。庶几旧调翻新，敢谓后来居上。
    　　虞初志原本不载选者姓名，汤临川续编未传作者氏号，俱为憾事，或属阙文。载考委宛余编：虞初为汉武帝时小吏，衣黄承辎，采访天下异闻。以是名书，亦犹志怪之帙，即齐谐以为名；集异之书，本夷坚而著号。
    　　一切选家，必以作者年代为准；百凡评次，鲜以其事时世为衡。如史记追溯三代以前，而选文止称一字曰汉是也。故志中之事，或属前时，而纪事之人实生当代，自应入选，讵可或遗。

    Explanation

    3

    Translation

    Preface

    Scholars with sharp ambitions to study deeply focus on the classics, commentaries, works of the Hundred Schools of Thought, and historical records; scholars who pursue knowledge widely and extensively often turn to stories and anecdotes, often from informal sources. Though short poems and long songs can be considered playful, and dramatic works and plays are highly comedic, they are not uncommon and are often repeated, or they are common sayings that are repeated often. This collection imitates the selection and compilation of Suyu Zhi, and follows the style of Tang Xianzu’s commentary, being unrestrained and proud of being unique, and are all based on real events; it searches for and highlights the supernatural and unusual, and is not at all similar or repetitive. It hopes to revitalize old themes with new interpretations, and dares to claim that those who come later will surpass those who came before.

    The original Suyu Zhi does not record the names of the selectors, and Tang Linchuan’s continuation did not pass down the names of the authors, both of which are regrettable, or perhaps due to omissions. According to supplementary materials: Suyu Zhi was compiled by a minor official during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, wearing yellow robes and riding in a carriage, collecting unusual news from around the world. It is named after Qi Hé, a book of strange tales, and is similar to Suyu Zhi; a book that collects unusual stories, named after Yijian.

    All selectors must base their selections on the author's era; all evaluations and rankings rarely consider the context and time period of the events. For example, the Shiji traces back to before the Three Dynasties, but the selected texts only mention "Han" as a single character. Therefore, events in the collection may belong to earlier times, but the people who recorded them were actually born in the contemporary era, and should naturally be included, how could they be omitted?

    4

    一事而两见者，叙事固无异同，行文必有详略。如大铁椎传，一见于宁都魏叔子，一见于新安王不庵。二公之文，真如赵璧隋珠，不相上下。顾魏详而王略，则登魏而逸王。只期便于览观，非敢意为轩轾。
    　　赖古堂藏弆结邻诸选，汇其人之文，专系于姓名之下；蜩寄斋尺牍新语三编，别其文之类，分叙于卷页之中。固云整整齐齐，未觉疏疏落落。今兹选错综无次，庶不涉于拘牵；且其事荒诞不经，无庸分夫门类。读书之暇，展卷尽可怡神；倦息之余，披翻自能豁目。
    　　序爵序齿，从来选政所无；或后或先，总以邮简为次。不能虚简以待，亦难缩地以求。随到随评，即付剞劂之手；投函投刺，勿烦酬酢之劳。次第未可拘拘，知交定称尔尔。
    　　文自昭明而后始有选名，书从匡郑以来渐多笺释。盖由流连欣赏，随手腕以加评；抑且阐发揄扬，并胸怀而迸露。

    Explanation

    4

    A Matter Seen Twice

    In cases where the same event is described in two different texts, the narrative is fundamentally the same, but the writing must have detailed and brief parts. For example, the "Great Iron Club" account appears once in the works of Wei Shuzi of Ningdu and once in the works of Wang Bu'an of Xin'an. The writings of these two gentlemen are truly like the Zhao Bi and Sui Zhu, equally valuable. However, Wei's writing is detailed while Wang's is brief, so Wei's account is more comprehensive and Wang's is more concise. The purpose is only to facilitate easy viewing, not daring to make a distinction.

    The Lai Gu Tang collection of neighboring selections compiles the works of various authors, listing them under their names; the Tiao Ji Zhai collection of letters and new sayings in three volumes categorizes the texts by type, dividing them into volumes and pages. It is indeed neat and tidy, not feeling scattered or disorganized. This selection is complex and lacks order, perhaps avoiding being overly bound by constraints; and the matters are absurd and unverified, no need to categorize them. During leisure time, reading can be refreshing; after a break, flipping through can refresh the mind.

    Arranging by rank and age has never been used in the selection process; sometimes later, sometimes earlier, overall, arranged by postal letters. Cannot wait for empty letters, also difficult to compress the space. Review as it arrives, immediately given to the engraver; sending letters and visiting cards, avoid the trouble of reciprocation. The order cannot be strictly adhered to, friends will surely say so.

    Literature began to have a name for selection after Zhaoming; books have gradually gained more annotations since Kuang Zheng. Because of lingering appreciation, adding comments with the hand; or elaborating and praising, revealing the heart and mind.

    5

    兹集触目赏心，漫附数言于篇末；挥毫拍案，忽加赘语于幅余。或评其事而忼慨激昂，或赏其文而咨嗟唱叹，敢谓发明，聊抒兴趣；既自怡悦，愿共讨论。
    　　鄙人性好幽奇，衷多感愤。故神仙英杰，寓意四怀；外史奇文，写心一启予向有才子、佳人、英雄、神仙四怀诗，及征选外史启。生平罕逢秘本，不惮假抄；偶尔得遇异书，辄为求购。第愧搜罗未广，尤惭辨辑无多。凡有新篇，速祈惠教，并望乞邻而与，无妨举尔所知。
    　　是集只期表彰轶事，传布奇文，非欲借迳沽名，居奇射利。已经入选者，尽多素不相知；将来授梓者，何必尽皆旧识。自当任剞劂之费，不望惠梨枣之资，免致浮沉，早邮珠玉。
    　　海内名家尚多未传之作，坊间定本俱为数见之书，幽人素嗜探奇，尤耽考异。此选之外，尚有嗣选古世说、古文尤雅、古文辞法传集、布粟集、壮游便览诸书，次第告竣，就正有道。凡有缪盭，幸赐教言。

    5

    This collection is pleasing to the eye and heart, and I have casually added a few words at the end of the article; with a brush in hand and a bang on the table, I suddenly added some superfluous words to the margin. Some may comment on the matter with great enthusiasm and passion, while others may appreciate the text and sigh or sing praises. Dare I say that it is an innovation? Just to express interest; already self-pleased, I wish to discuss it together.

    My nature loves the strange and mysterious, and my heart often feels indignation. Therefore, legendary heroes and immortals convey four kinds of feelings; unusual texts from the Shiji inspire me to have four kinds of poems about talented men, beautiful women, heroes, and immortals, along with an appeal for selecting texts from the Shiji. I rarely encounter secret editions in my life, but I am not afraid to borrow and copy them; occasionally, I encounter strange books and immediately seek to purchase them. But I feel ashamed that my collection is not wide enough, and especially ashamed that I have not compiled much. Whenever there are new works, I quickly ask for your guidance, and I hope to ask neighbors to share, it does not matter if you share what you know.

    This collection aims only to highlight unusual stories and spread unusual texts, not to seek fame by indirect means or to profit from rarity. Those who have been selected are mostly people I do not know; those who will be published in the future do not have to be all old acquaintances. I will bear the cost of engraving, and I do not expect any financial support, to avoid floating and sinking, and to send precious things early.

    There are still many works by famous scholars within the country that have not been transmitted, and the fixed editions in the market are all rare books. A recluse has always loved exploring the strange, especially enjoys studying differences. In addition to this selection, there are subsequent selections of ancient anecdotes, elegant ancient prose, ancient prose methods, Bulusi Ji (A Collection of Millet), and Zhuangyou Bianlan (Convenient Guide for Grand Tours). One by one, they will be completed, and I will seek correction from the wise. If there are any errors, please give me your advice.

    6

    心斋主人识于广陵之诒清堂
    　　
    　　总跋
    　　予辑是书竟，不禁喟然而叹也，曰：嗟乎，古人有言，非穷愁不能著书，以自见于后世。夫人以穷愁而著书，则其书之所蕴必多抑郁无聊之意，以寓乎其间，读者亦何乐闻此如怨如慕、如泣如诉之音乎？予不幸，于己卯岁误堕坑阱中，而肺附中山，不以其困也而贳之，犹时时相嘬啮。既无有有道丈人相助举手，又不获遇聂隐娘辈一泣愬之，惟暂学羼提波罗密，俟之身后而已。于斯时也，苟非得一二奇书消磨岁月，其殆将何以处此乎？然则予第假读书一途以度此穷愁，非敢曰惟穷愁始能从事于铅椠也。夫穷愁之际，尚欲藉书而释，况乎居安处顺心有余闲，几净窗明，焚香静读，其乐为何如乎。因附记于此，俾世之读我书者，兼有以知我之境遇而悯之。世不乏有心人，然非予之所敢望也。康熙庚辰初夏三在道人张潮识

    Explanation

    6

    Master of the Heart-Chamber, Zhang Chao, inscribed this in the Tranquil Hall of Guangling.
    　　
    　　General Preface
    　　After completing the compilation of this book, I could not help but sigh and said: Alas, the ancients have said that without hardship and sorrow, one cannot write books to reveal oneself to future generations. When a person writes under such conditions, their work must inevitably contain much of the depression and aimlessness, which is expressed within it. How can readers find joy in hearing such sounds of lamentation and longing, weeping and pleading? Unfortunate for me, in the year Jimo, I mistakenly fell into a pit, and my lungs were afflicted by a mountainous illness, not sparing me from its torment, but still nibbling at me. I had no wise elder to help me rise, nor did I encounter a person like Nie Yinniang to weep and plead for me. I can only temporarily practice the patience and forbearance of the Kshanti Paramita, waiting for it to be recognized after my death. In such a time, if I had not found a few extraordinary books to pass the years, how could I have endured this situation? Therefore, I merely used the path of reading to overcome this hardship, not daring to say that only hardship and sorrow can lead one to engage in writing. Even in times of hardship, one still desires to find solace in books; how much more so in times of peace and comfort, with ample leisure, a clean desk, bright windows, burning incense, and quiet reading, what kind of joy would that be? I have therefore added this note here, so that those who read my books may also understand my circumstances and feel compassion for me. The world is not lacking in compassionate people, but it is not something I dare to expect. Zhang Chao, a Daoist, inscribed this in the early summer of the year Gengchen during the Kangxi era.

    7

    卷一 姜贞毅先生传：魏禧 大铁椎传：魏禧 徐霞客传：钱谦益 秋声诗自序：林嗣环 盛此公传：周亮工 汤琵琶传：王猷定 小青传：佚名 义猴传：宋曹

    Explanation

    7

    Volume One: The Biography of Mr. Jiang Zhenyi by Wei Xi; The Biography of the Great Iron Spike by Wei Xi; The Biography of Xu Xiake by Qian Qianyi; The Preface to the Autumn Sounds Poem by Lin Sihuang; The Biography of Mr. Sheng Ci by Zhou Lianggong; The Biography of the Pipa Player Tang by Wang Youding; The Biography of Xia Qing by an Anonymous Author; The Biography of the Righteous Monkey by Song Cao

    Volume Two: The Biography of Liu Jingting by Wu Weiye; The Biography of Wang Shi by Xu Shijun; The Biography of Wu Fengzi by Fang Hengxian; The Record of the Old Immortal by Fang Hengxian; The Short Biography of the Flower History of the Fairy Palace by You Tong; The Record of Watching the Wrestling at Jiuniu Dam by Peng Shiwang

    Volume Three: The Biography of Ma Ling by Hou Fangyu; The Biography of Gu Yuchuan by Cao He; The Biography of Dong Xiaowan by Zhang Mingbi; The Biography of the Wine Seller by Wei Xi; The Biography of Yi Piaozi by Yan Shousheng; The Biography of Song Lianbi by Li Huanchang

    Volume Four: The Record of the Righteous Tiger by Wang Youding; The Outer Biography of Ding Yaoyuan by Lin Lu; The Record of Hearing Songs at Jichang Garden by Yu Huai; The Biography of Chen Xiaolian by Du Jun; The Biography of the Old Flower Seller by Zong Yuanding; The Record of the Divine Axe by Xu Fang; The Biography of the Burnt-Qin Man by Gu Cai; The Biography of the Four Surnamed Son by Zhang Mingbi

    Volume Five: The Short Biography of Lady Liu by Xu Fang; The Record of the Heart Exchange by Xu Fang; The Biography of the Strong Men of Qinhuai by Li Yu; The Record of the Shrine of the Four Women of Shandong by Huang Shi; The Biography of Lu Dian by Zhu Yishi; The Biography of Lin Siniang by Lin Yunming; The Biography of Beggar Wang Old Man by Xu Fang; The Record of the Bandits of Leizhou by Xu Fang; The Biography of the Hidden Flower Taoist by Zhu Yishi

    8

    卷六 张南垣传：吴伟业 孙文正、黄石斋两逸事：方苞 郭老仆墓志铭：侯方域 五人传：吴肃公 箫洞虚小传：傅占衡 鬼孝子传：宋曹 黄履庄小传：戴榕
    　　卷七 书戚三郎事：周亮工 象记：林璐 纪周侍御事：陆次云 姚江神灯记：朱一是 记盗：杨衡选 化虎记：徐芳 义犬记：徐芳 奇女子传：徐芳 曲全节义疏：阿毕阮
    　　卷八 江石芸传：吴良枢 耕云子传：洪嘉植 吴孝子传：魏禧 李一足传：王猷定 孝贼传：王猷定 王翠翘传：余怀 戴文进传：毛先舒 髯樵传：顾彩 赵希乾传：甘表 万夫雄打虎传：张惣
    　　卷九 剑侠传：王士祯 皇华纪闻：王士祯 毛女传：陈鼎 宝婺生传：陆次云 王义士传：陈鼎 纪陆子容事：王晫 雌雌儿传：陈鼎 再来诗谶记：沙张白

    Explanation

    8

    　　卷十二 邵士梅传：陆鸣珂 彭望祖传：陈鼎 程弱文传：罗坤 薜衣道人传：陈鼎 刘医记：陈玉璂 湖堧杂记：陆次云 看花述异记：王晫 孝犬传：陈鼎
    　　卷十三 曼殊别志书砖：毛奇龄 补张灵、崔莹合传：黄周星 陈老莲别传：毛奇龄 桑山人传：毛奇龄 李姬传：侯方域 记缢鬼：王明德
    　　卷十四 平苗神异记：王谦 纪老生妄讼：吴陈琰 会仙记：徐喈凤 太恨生传：徐瑶 瘗水盏子志石铭：毛奇龄 姗姗传：黄永

    Explanation

    9

    Volume Ten: Casual Notes from the Junlang Pavilion

    • Biography of Jin Zhongjie: By Dong Yining
    • The Carved Boat Record: By Wei Xueyi
    • Biography of Shen Fu Zhong: By Lu Ciyun
    • Biography of the Iron Roadman: By Chen Ding
    • Strange Books of the North Villa: By Lu Ciyun
    • The Ghost Mother Biography: By Li Qing
    • The Dog-Skin Taoist Biography: By Chen Ding
    • The Biography of the Fiery Fox: By Chen Ding

    Volume Eleven: The Biography of Guo Bailin: By Qin Songling

    • The Biography of the Eight Great Mountain Man: By Chen Ding
    • The Biography of Yuan Yuan: By Lu Ciyun
    • The Biography of the Howling Old Man: By Chen Ding
    • Random Notes from the Guest Window: Anonymous
    • A Collection of Observations and Words: By Gu Chengmei
    • The Book of the Woodcutter: By Lai Jizhi
    • The Three Generations of Yu Sheng from Qiantang: By Chen Yuqi
    • The Biography of the Living Dead Man: By Chen Ding
    • The Biography of the Righteous Ox: By Chen Ding

    Volume Twelve: The Biography of Shao Shimei: By Lu Mingke

    • The Biography of Peng Wangzu: By Chen Ding
    • The Biography of Cheng Ruowen: By Luo Kun
    • The Biography of the Purple-Cloth Taoist: By Chen Ding
    • The Record of Liu the Physician: By Chen Yuqi
    • Miscellaneous Notes from the Lake Wasteland: By Lu Ciyun
    • The Record of Flower Viewing and Strange Tales: By Wang Zhuo
    • The Biography of the Filial Dog: By Chen Ding

    Volume Thirteen: The Separate Record of Manshu: By Mao Qiling

    • The Combined Biography of Zhang Ling and Cui Ying: By Huang Zhouxing
    • The Separate Biography of Chen Laolian: By Mao Qiling
    • The Biography of Sang Shanren: By Mao Qiling
    • The Biography of Li Ji: By Hou Fangyu
    • The Record of the Hanged Ghost: By Wang Mingde

    Volume Fourteen: The Divine Record of Pacifying the Miao: By Wang Qian

    • The Record of Elder Lao's Frivolous Lawsuit: By Wu Chenyan
    • The Record of Meeting the Immortal: By Xu Jifeng
    • The Biography of the Greatly Resentful Man: By Xu Yao
    • The Inscription of the Water-Buried Cup: By Mao Qiling
    • The Biography of Shanshan: By Huang Yong
    10

    卷十五 记同梦：闺秀钱宜 述怪记：缪彤 哑孝子传：王洁 孝丐传：王晫 乩仙记：洪若皋 中泠泉记：潘介 髯参军传：徐瑶 李丐传：毛际可 书钿阁女子图章前：周亮工 书王安节、王宓草印谱前：周亮工 书姜次生印章前：周亮工
    　　卷十六 因树屋书影：周亮工 记桃核念珠：高士奇 核工记：宋起凤 张南邨先生传：先著 刘酒传：周亮工 记古铁条：詹钟玉 唐仲言传：周亮工 李公起传：周亮工 书郑仰田事：钱谦益 记吴六奇将军事：钮琇
    　　卷十七 记袁枢遇仙始末：毛际可 闵孝子传：吴晋 人觚：钮琇 事觚：钮琇 物觚：钮琇 名捕传：姚口 南游记：孙嘉淦
    　　卷十八 圣师录：王言 海天行记：钮琇
    　　卷十九 七奇图说：南怀仁 讱庵偶笔：汪口口 柳轩丛谈 啸虹笔记 燕觚：钮琇 豫觚：钮琇 秦觚：钮琇 吴觚：钮琇

    Explanation

    10

    Volume Fifteen: Record of Shared Dreams: Miss Qian Yi; Account of Strange Events: Miutong; The Story of the Silent Filial Son: Wang Jie; The Story of the Filial Beggar: Wang Zhuo; Record of the Medium Spirit: Hong Ruogao; The Story of the Zhongling Spring: Pan Jie; The Story of the Bearded Military Officer: Xu Yao; The Story of the Beggar Li: Mao Jike; Preface to the Seal of the Lady of the Dian Pavilion: Zhou Lianggong; Preface to the Grass Seal Spectrum of Wang Aijie and Wang Mici: Zhou Lianggong; Preface to the Seal of Jiang Cisheng: Zhou Lianggong.
    Volume Sixteen: The Shadow of the Tree House: Zhou Lianggong; The Record of the Peach Kernel Rosary: Gao Shiqi; The Record of Craftsmanship: Song Qifeng; The Biography of Mr. Zhang Nanzun: Xianzhuo; The Story of Liu Jiu: Zhou Lianggong; The Record of the Ancient Iron Bar: Zhan Zhongyu; The Biography of Tang Zhongyan: Zhou Lianggong; The Biography of Li Gongqi: Zhou Lianggong; The Story of Zheng Yangtian: Qian Qianyi; The Record of the Military Affairs of Wu Liuqi: Niu Xiu.
    Volume Seventeen: The Record of Yuan Shu's Encounter with the Immortal: Mao Jike; The Story of the Filial Min: Wu Jin; The Human Agaric: Niu Xiu; The Event Agaric: Niu Xiu; The Object Agaric: Niu Xiu; The Story of the Famous Capturer: Yao; The Southern Journey: Sun Jiagan.
    Volume Eighteen: The Record of the Sage Teacher: Wang Yan; The Record of the Sea Sky Journey: Niu Xiu.
    Volume Nineteen: The Explanation of the Seven Wonders: Nan Huairen; The Miscellaneous Notes of Ren'an: Wang (last name); The Conversations at the Willow Pavilion; The Notes of the Whistling Rainbow; The Yan Agaric: Niu Xiu; The Yu Agaric: Niu Xiu; The Qin Agaric: Niu Xiu; The Wu Agaric: Niu Xiu.

    11

    卷二十 三侬赘人广自序：汪价 板桥杂记：余怀
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷一 姜贞毅先生传　宁都魏禧冰叔魏叔子文集
    　　公名埰，姓姜氏，字如农，山东莱阳人也。高祖淮，以御寇功拜怀远将军。父泻里，诸生。崇祯癸未，北兵破莱阳，泻里守城死，幼子、三子妇、一女皆殉节。事闻，赠泻里光禄寺卿，予祭葬，谥忠肃。
    　　公之将生也，王母李感异梦。其生，衣胞皆白色。三岁失乳。母杨太孺人置水酒床头，夜起饮之，一瓿立尽。万历乙卯，山东大饥，盗蜂起。公时九岁，与兄圻夜读，书声咿唔不绝。盗及门，叹息去。年二十，补诸生第一。明年乡试，经义中式，主司以五策指斥崔、魏摈之。崇祯庚午，举于乡。往见中表李笃培。李负清正名，谓公曰：“子富贵何足异？士大夫立身，要当为朝廷任大事耳！”公敬而受之。明年举进士，出倪文正元璐门。殿试赐同进士出身，授知密云县，未行，改仪征县。

    Explanation

    11

    Volume Twenty, Three Nong Zui Ren Guang Zizhu: Wang Jia, Banqiao Zaji: Yu Huai

    Suluchu Xin Zhi, Volume One, Biography of Mr. Jiang Zhenyi, Collection of Writings by Wei Xi of Ningdu, Ice Uncle and Uncle Shuzi

    His name was Cen, his surname was Jiang, his courtesy name was Ru Nong, and he was from Laiyang, Shandong. His great-grandfather, Huai, was appointed as the General of Huaiyuan for his achievements in repelling bandits. His father, Xiali, was a student of the Imperial Academy. In the year Guiwei of the Chongzhen era, the northern army conquered Laiyang, and Xiali died defending the city. His youngest son, his third son's wife, and one daughter all died in accordance with filial piety. The matter was reported, and Xiali was posthumously granted the title of Chief of the Imperial Food Administration, granted a funeral and sacrifices, and given the posthumous name of Zhongsu.

    Before his birth, his mother, Lady Li, had a strange dream. At his birth, his swaddling clothes and umbilical cord were all white. At the age of three, he lost his mother's milk. His mother, Lady Yang, a senior lady, placed water and wine by his bedside. He got up at night and drank it, and a large jar of it was finished immediately. In the year Bingmao of the Wanli era, there was a great famine in Shandong, and bandits rose up like bees. At the age of nine, he and his brother Qi read at night, and the sound of their reading never ceased. The bandits reached the door, sighed, and left. At the age of twenty, he was admitted as the top student. The following year, in the provincial examination, he passed in the Confucian classics section, but the chief examiner criticized him with five points, accusing him of opposing Cui and Wei. In the year Gengwu of the Chongzhen era, he was recommended in the provincial examination. He went to visit his cousin Li Ducepei. Li had a reputation for integrity and uprightness, and he said to him, "Your wealth and status are not remarkable. Scholars and officials should establish themselves by taking on major responsibilities for the court!" He respectfully accepted it. The following year, he passed the imperial examination, studying under Ni Wen Zheng Yuanlu. He was granted the rank of Jinshi in the palace examination, appointed as the magistrate of Miyun County, but before he could take up the post, he was reassigned to Yizheng County.

    12

    公为政廉仁，十年无所取于民，不受竿牍。客至，去，题其馆壁曰：“爱民如子，嫉客若仇。”尝捐俸请托，免泗州修河夫五百名，百姓不知也。又请革过闸粮船纤夫，著为令。旧例，掣盐封引，仪征令皆有赂。公独绝之。商人感激，为代备修河银一万两。下车日，廉得大憝董奇、董九功等，置于法；窝访之，害遂除。袁公继咸备兵扬州，见，下堂揖之，曰：“吾间行真州，见先生听断，不觉心折矣！”
    　　辛巳，改礼部仪制司主事。明年，巡抚南直隶朱公大典疏表公贤劳。上谕一体考选，因目阁臣曰：“有臣如此而不用，朕之过也！”三月，上御宏政门召见，应对称旨，擢礼科给事中，赐糕果汤饼。

    Explanation

    12

    Mr. Zhao Nanxing governed with integrity and benevolence, taking nothing from the people for ten years and not accepting written petitions. When guests arrived and left, he inscribed on the walls of his residence: "Loving the people like one's own children, hating guests like enemies." He once donated his salary to make requests, exempting 500 workers from the repair work on the river in Sizhou, a fact unknown to the common people. He also requested the abolition of the practice of using coolies for grain ships passing through the lock, establishing this as a regulation. According to the old custom, when drawing and sealing salt permits, all the officials in Yizheng had bribes. Mr. Zhao alone cut off this practice. Merchants were deeply moved and donated 10,000 taels of silver to support the river repairs. On the day he took office, he uncovered the great evildoers Dong Qi and Dong Jiugong, etc., and punished them according to the law; by rooting out the evil, the harm was eliminated. Governor of Nanjing, Zhu Gong Dadian, submitted a memorial praising Mr. Zhao's virtue and diligence. The emperor ordered that he be included in the examination for promotion and said to the cabinet ministers: "To have a minister like this and not use him is my fault!" In March, the emperor summoned him at the Hall of Great Government. His responses pleased the emperor, and he was promoted to the position of Censor in the Department of Rites, being bestowed with cakes, fruits, and noodles.

    13

    公既拜官，五月中条上三十疏，上每采纳。十一月，东方告急，公受诏分守德胜门。自元勋以下，惮公不敢归休沐。时宰相大贪婪，都御史黄宗周有“长安黄金贵”之疏。宰相惧，卸其罪于言官，又欲引用逆辅口口相表里为奸恶。公上疏极论罪在大臣，不在言官，并及涿州知府刘三聘疏荐口口事，触首辅怒。又有“上谕：代人规卸、为人出缺，陛下果何见而云然？”及“二十四气蜚语，腾闻清禁，此必大奸巨憝恶言官不利于己”等语。上大怒，闰十一月二十三日，御皇极门召见群臣，谓：“埰欺肆，敢于诘问朕何所见，二十四气之说，不知所指何人何事？着革职，锦衣卫拿送北镇抚司打问！”时行人司熊开元面劾首辅，既以补牍语不相应，同时下狱，几死，后并得赦。

    Explanation

    13

    After being appointed to an official position, in the fifth month, he submitted thirty memorials to the throne, which the emperor often adopted. In the eleventh month, an emergency arose in the east, and he received an imperial edict to take charge of the defense of De Sheng Gate. From the highest-ranking officials down, they were all intimidated by him and dared not take leave. At that time, the prime minister was extremely corrupt, and the censor-general Huang Zongwu submitted a memorial titled "Gold is Valuable in Chang'an." The prime minister, fearing exposure, shifted the blame onto the officials responsible for remonstrance and intended to use the traitorous minister [name omitted] as an accomplice in doing evil. He submitted a memorial strongly arguing that the fault lay with the high-ranking officials, not the remonstrant officials, and also mentioned the recommendation by Liu Sanping, the governor of Zhuozhou, regarding [name omitted]. This angered the chief minister. The emperor's edict read: "Delegating blame and creating vacancies for others, what do you mean by this, Your Majesty?" Rumors about the twenty-four solar terms have spread to the inner palace, this must be the work of great traitors who hate the remonstrant officials. Ordering his dismissal, the Imperial Guard to arrest and send him to the Northern Censorate for interrogation! Xiong Kaiyuan, the official of the Imperial Messenger Office, publicly impeached the chief minister. After his memorial was found to be inappropriate, he was imprisoned along with [the subject], nearly dying, but was later pardoned.

    14

    初，公下北镇抚司狱三日，勺水不得入口。冰雪交积，公僵卧土室，无袱被，身婴三木，血流贯械。九卿台省屡疏救，不报。此处缺二十二字例凡一拶敲五十，一夹敲五十，杖二十，名曰一套。公既备刑，谳狱者必欲得二十四人姓名以报上。公以诸人皆正人，恐祸不已，忍死弗肯列。气垂绝，唯以指染口血书“死”字，卧阶下。半日稍苏，清宏令尉灌酒一杯，使毕谳。公终不肯承。

    Explanation

    14

    At first, the public was imprisoned in the Northern Supervisory Bureau for three days, unable to even drink a spoonful of water. The accumulation of ice and snow made the public lie stiff in a dirt room, without blankets or bedding, bound with three wooden restraints, and blood flowed through the restraints. High-ranking officials repeatedly petitioned for the public's release, but their requests were ignored. Here are the standard punishments: one拶 (a type of torture) for fifty blows, one夹 (another type of torture) for fifty blows, and twenty strokes with a stick, called a set. The public had already endured the punishments, and the judge must get the names of twenty-four people to report to the higher authorities. The public believed the accused were upright people and feared further harm, so he endured the pain but refused to list them. On the verge of death, he only used his finger to dip in his mouth's blood and write the character for 'death,' lying on the steps. After half a day, he regained consciousness, and the clean and generous commandant gave him a cup of wine to complete the judgment. The public ultimately refused to comply.

    15

    疏入，上大怒，谓考击缓，情实未当，诘责卫司官令再讯，一拶一夹，各敲八十，杖三十。俄出密谕一小纸曰：“姜埰、熊开元即取毕命，只云病故。”卫臣骆养性具奏，有曰：“即二臣当死，陛下何不付所司书其罪，使天下明知二臣之罚？若生杀出匿等，天下后世谓陛下何如主？”又密言于诸大臣。而都御史刘宗周上殿力争，自辰至午不肯退。上怒其执拗，非对君礼，将下宥司治罪。既矜其耄，特革职，放归田。佥都御史金公光宸奏宗周清直，愿以身代宗周。上怒，以为雷同罔上，夺职谪籍。而兵部侍郎马公元飙、都给事吴公麟征，开陈大指，婉辞规劝，上心为少移，旋出密旨谕卫司缴昨旨毋行。于是公及开元始得移刑部狱矣。

    Explanation

    15

    The memorial was submitted, and the emperor flew into a rage, saying that the punishment had been too lenient and the facts and circumstances not accurately determined. He rebuked the officials of the imperial guard and ordered a re-investigation, subjecting them to the torture of zā and jiá, each receiving eighty blows with a stick and thirty strokes with a stick. Soon after, he issued a secret decree on a small piece of paper: "Jiāng Cán and Xióng Kāiyuán shall be executed immediately, but report their deaths as due to illness." The official Lù Yǎngxìng of the imperial guard submitted a memorial, saying, "Even if these two officials deserve death, why did His Majesty not have them recorded by the proper authorities, so that the whole world would know their punishment? If the emperor secretly orders executions, how will future generations view His Majesty?" He also spoke secretly to the other officials. The Imperial Inspector Liu Zōngzhōu ascended the hall to strongly argue against the execution, refusing to leave from morning until noon. The emperor was angry at his stubbornness, which he considered disrespectful to the ruler, and intended to have him punished by the office of pardons. Considering his advanced age, he specially relieved him of his position and allowed him to return to his hometown. The Inspector-in-Chief Jīn Guāngchén submitted a memorial on behalf of Liu Zōngzhōu, offering to take his place. The emperor was angry, thinking that they were all deceiving him, and stripped them of their positions and exiled them. Meanwhile, the Deputy Minister of Military Affairs Mǎ Yuánbiāo and the Censor Wú Línzhēng presented the main points, advising gently, and the emperor's mind was somewhat moved. He soon issued another secret decree instructing the imperial guard to revoke the previous order. Thus, Jiāng Cán and Xióng Kāiyuán were finally transferred to the prison of the Ministry of Justice.

    16

    刑部尚书徐公石麟拟附近充军。上怒。公、开元各杖一百。
    　　是日，特遣大珰曹化淳、王德化监视，众官朱衣陪列午门外西墀下。左中使、右锦衣卫各三十员，下列旗校百人，皆衣襞衣，执木棍。宣读毕，一人持麻布兜，自肩脊下束之，左右不得动。一人缚其两足，四面牵曳，唯露股受杖。头面触地，地尘满口中。杖数折，公昏绝不知人。
    　　弟垓，时官行人，口含溺吐公饮之。名医吕邦相夜视公，曰：“杖青痕过膝者不治，吾以刀割创处，七日而痛，为君贺矣！”半月，去败肉斗许，乃苏。邦相曾活黄公道周廷杖，京师号“君子医”也。
    　　大珰复命。上曰：“二臣顾何言？”曰：“二臣言皇帝尧、舜，臣得为关龙逢、比干足矣。”上曰：“两人舌强犹尔！”

    Explanation

    16

    The Minister of Justice, Xu Gong Shilin, was proposed to be exiled to serve in the military nearby. The emperor became angry and ordered that both Xu Gong and Kaiyuan each receive one hundred strokes of the cane.

    On that day, the emperor specially dispatched high-ranking eunuchs Cao Huachun and Wang Dehua to supervise. Many officials stood in a row wearing red robes outside the western steps of the south gate of the palace. Thirty officials from the Left Eunuch Office and thirty from the Right Imperial Guard stood below, along with one hundred soldiers, all wearing folded robes and holding wooden sticks. After the decree was read aloud, one person held a hemp cloth sack and bound it from the shoulders and back down, preventing Xu Gong from moving left or right. Another person tied his two feet and pulled from all sides, exposing only his thighs to receive the beating. His head and face touched the ground, and dust filled his mouth. The cane broke several times, and Xu Gong fainted and lost consciousness.

    His younger brother, Gai, who was serving as a messenger, put urine in his mouth and spit it into Xu Gong's mouth for him to drink. The famous doctor Lu Bangxiang visited Xu Gong at night and said, "Those with bruises extending past the knees cannot be treated, but I will cut the wounds with a knife. It will hurt for seven days, so congratulations to you!" Half a month later, after removing about a dou of rotten flesh, Xu Gong revived. Doctor Lu had once saved Huang Gongdaozhou, who was beaten in the court, and was called the "Gentleman Doctor" in the capital.

    The eunuchs reported back to the emperor. The emperor asked, "What did the two ministers say?" They replied, "The two ministers said the emperor is like Yao and Shun, and we can be like Guan Longfeng and Bi Gan." The emperor said, "Even with their tongues tied, they still said that!"

    17

    明年春，莱阳破，公父死于难。垓请身系狱，而释埰归治丧，不许。台省亦交章请释公。上曰：“垓在！”七月疫，上命刑部清狱，公暂出。上召见刑部，以墨笔叉埰、开元名，曰：“此两大恶，奈何释之！”于是再入狱。十二月，首辅伏诛，有新参请释二臣者。上曰：“朕怒二臣，岂为罪辅哉？”不许。
    　　甲申正月，闯贼猖獗，阁臣李建泰奉命督师山西。上御正阳门，行推毂礼。建泰请释埰、开元，上报可，谪公戍宣州卫。
    　　公过故乡，哭光禄公。闻京师陷，上殉社稷，公恸哭。南之戍所。未至，弘光即位，赦，公遂留吴门，不肯归。会马士英、阮大铖用事。大铖往被垓劾，必杀公兄弟。复窜走。丁亥，避地徽州，绝食。樵子宋心老时以菜羹啖之。或徒步数十里，走吴孝廉家得一饱。祝发黄山丞相园，而自号“敬亭山人”，盖不敢忘先帝不杀恩也。

    Explanation

    17

    In the following spring, Laiyang was captured, and the public's father died in the disaster. Gai requested to be imprisoned himself, while asking for Cai to be released to return home for mourning, but this was not permitted. The Ministry of Personnel and the Board of Censors also jointly submitted petitions to release him. The emperor said, "Gai is still here!" In July, an epidemic broke out, and the emperor ordered the Ministry of Justice to clear the prisons, allowing him to temporarily leave. The emperor summoned him to the Ministry of Justice, where he crossed out the names of Cai and Kaiyuan with a black pen, saying, "These two great evils, how can they be released?" Thus, he was imprisoned again. In December, the chief minister was executed, and there was a new petitioner requesting the release of the two ministers. The emperor said, "I am angry with these two ministers, but it is not because they are guilty of being the chief minister," and refused the request.

    In the first month of the year 1644, the rebel bandits were rampant, and the cabinet minister Li Jiantai was ordered to lead the troops in Shanxi. The emperor personally went to the Zhengyang Gate to perform the 'pushing the chariot' ceremony. Jiantai requested the release of Cai and Kaiyuan, and the emperor approved, exiling him to garrison in Xuanzhou. He passed through his hometown and cried for his uncle, the Grand Master of the Imperial Household. Hearing that the capital had fallen and the emperor had sacrificed himself for the state, he wept bitterly. He went south to his garrison. Before he arrived, Hongguang ascended the throne, granted amnesty, and he stayed in Wu Gate, refusing to return. When Ma Shiying and Ruan Dacheng were in power, Dacheng had previously been impeached by Gai and would definitely kill the brothers. He fled again. In Dinghai, he fled to Huizhou and went on a hunger strike. The woodcutter Song Xinlao sometimes fed him vegetable soup. Sometimes he walked dozens of miles to the home of Wu, a filial scholar, to get a full meal. He shaved his head and lived in the garden of the former chancellor in Huangshan, calling himself "Respectful Mount Jingting Man," because he could not forget the emperor's grace of not killing him.

    18

    后还吴门，终僧服，不与世人接。二子安节、实节，才，亦不令进取。戊子，奉母归莱阳。母疾甚，公默祷，愿减算延母。
    　　山东巡抚重公名，下檄招公。公故坠马以折股，召疡医，竹箯舁之。使者归报。公夜驰还江南，自号“宣州老兵”。尝欲结庐敬亭山，未果。癸丑夏，公疾病，呼二子谓曰：“吾受命谪戍。今遭世变，流离异乡，生不能守先墓，死不能正首丘，抱恨于中心。吾当待尽宣州，以绝吾志。”越数日，则曰：“吾不能往矣！死必埋我敬亭之麓。”口吟《易箦歌》一章，呕血数升而殁，时年六十有七。遗命碑碣神主不题故官，棺用薄材，不营佛事。二子皆遵行之。葬敬亭日，远近吊者如市。同人私谥曰“贞毅先生”。

    Explanation

    18

    After returning to Wu Gate, he continued to wear the robes of a monk and did not interact with the world. His two sons, Anjie and Shijie, were talented, but he did not encourage them to pursue official careers. In the year of Wu Earth, he served his mother and returned to Laizhou. When his mother fell gravely ill, he silently prayed, wishing to shorten his own lifespan to extend her life.

    The governor of Shandong Province valued his reputation and issued an edict to recruit him. The subject deliberately fell off a horse to break his leg, called in a surgeon, and was carried in a bamboo stretcher. The envoy returned to report. The subject rushed back to Jiangnan at night and called himself the "Old Veteran of Xuanchou." He once wanted to build a hut on Jingting Mountain but did not succeed. In the summer of the year of Gui Chou, the subject fell ill and called his two sons to say, "I was ordered to be exiled and guard the frontier. Now facing the changes of the times, wandering in a foreign land, I cannot protect my ancestors' graves in life, nor can I face them in death. I hold regret in my heart. I will wait to die in Xuanchou to end my aspirations." After several days, he said, "I cannot go anymore! I will be buried at the foot of Jingting Mountain." He recited the "Song of the Easy Mattress" in one chapter, vomited blood several liters, and died at the age of sixty-seven. He left a will that the stone tablets and ancestral tablets should not bear the title of his former office, the coffin should be made of simple wood, and no Buddhist ceremonies should be held. His two sons both followed his wishes. On the day of his burial at Jingting Mountain, people from far and near came to mourn as if it were a market. His peers privately posthumously honored him as the "Virtuous and Resolute Master."

    19

    公隐居后，多著述，自选所为诗文，刻《敬亭集》藏于家，绝不示人。传甲乙以来殉节诸贤曰《正气集》，自题己亥后诗文曰《餺飥集》，又著《纪事摘缪》。皆藏之。
    　　魏禧曰：公有赠禧序及见怀诸诗，皆未出。公死，而公二子乃写寄禧山中也。予客吴门，数信宿公。每阴雨，公股足骨发痛，步趾微跛踌。哀哉！北镇抚司狱廷杖、立枷诸制，此秦法所未有，始作俑者，罪可胜道哉！宣城沈寿民曰：谥法：秉德不回曰孝。经曰：事君不忠，非孝也。公死不忘君，全而归之，可以为孝矣，宜谥曰贞孝”。

    19

    After the public retired to seclusion, he wrote extensively. He selected his own poems and writings, carved them into the Jingteng Collection, and kept them at home, never showing them to others. He compiled the Zhengqi Collection of the many virtuous people who died for their principles since the Jia and Yi periods. He titled his poems and writings after the year Ji Hai as the Huashu Collection. He also wrote the Jishi Zhai Mi. All were kept.

    Wei Xi said: "The public had a preface for Wei Xi and poems expressing his feelings, but they were not published. After the public's death, his two sons wrote and sent them to Wei Xi in the mountains. I was a guest in Wu Gate, and stayed with the public several times. Whenever it rained, the public's legs and feet would hurt, and he would walk with a slight limp. Alas! The regulations of the Northern Censorate's Prison, including public flogging and the standing collar, were not present in Qin law. The originators of these practices are guilty beyond measure." Shen Shoumin of Xuancheng said: "The rules of posthumous titles state: 'To hold virtue without deviation is called filial piety.' The classics say: 'To serve one's ruler unfaithfully is not filial.' The public died without forgetting his ruler, returning in full, which can be considered filial. He should be posthumously titled 'Zheng Xiao'."

    20

    [金棕亭曰：余游黄山，访先生祝发处。山僧犹藏手迹数纸。诗格豪放，字画遒劲，真希世宝也！以魏公文、姜公事作《新志》压卷，足令全书皆生赤水珠光。] 大铁椎传　魏禧冰叔魏叔子文集
    　　大铁椎，不知何许人。北平陈子灿省兄河南，与遇宋将军家。宋，怀庆青华镇人，工技击，七省好事者来学；人以其雄健，呼“宋将军”云。宋弟子高信之，亦怀庆人，多力善射，长子灿七岁，少同学，故尝与过宋将军。时座上有健啖客，貌甚寝，右肋夹大铁椎，重四五十斤，饮食拱揖不暂去；柄铁摺叠环复如锁上练，引之长丈许。与人罕言语，语类楚声。扣其乡及姓字，皆不答。
    　　既同寝，夜半，客曰：“吾去矣！”言讫不见。子灿见窗户皆闭，惊问信之。信之曰：“客初至，不冠不袜，以蓝手巾裹头，足缠白布。大铁椎外，一物无所持，而腰多白金。吾与将军俱不敢问也。”子灿寐而醒，客则鼾睡炕上矣。

    Explanation

    20

    Jin Zongting said: "I traveled to Mount Huang, visiting the place where Master had taken Buddhist vows. The mountain monks still kept several pieces of his handwriting. His poetry was bold and unrestrained, and his calligraphy was strong and powerful—truly a rare treasure of the world! By including Wei Gong's writings and Jiang Gong's deeds in the new historical record, it is sufficient to make the entire book shine like pearls from the Red River." The Great Iron Maul by Hui Xi, Ice Uncle Wei Shu Zi's Collection

    The Great Iron Maul, a man of unknown origin. From Beiping, Chen Zican traveled to Henan to visit his elder brother, where he met the Cao General at his home. Cao, from Qinghua Town in Huaiqing, was skilled in martial arts, and people from seven provinces came to learn from him. People called him "Cao General" because of his strength and vigor. Cao's disciple, Gao Xinzhi, was also from Huaiqing, strong and skilled in archery, seven years older than Zican, and they had been classmates when they were younger, so they often visited Cao General together. At the time, there was a hearty-eating guest at the gathering, who looked quite unkempt, with a large iron maul clutched under his right armpit, weighing forty to fifty catties. He did not leave his seat even during meals or bows. The handle of the iron maul was folded and looped like a chain, and when extended, it was about ten feet long. He rarely spoke, and when he did, his speech sounded like that of a person from Chu. When asked about his hometown and name, he did not answer.

    After they went to bed together, at midnight, the guest said, "I am leaving!" And then he was gone. Zican saw that the windows and doors were all closed and asked Gao Xinzhi in surprise. Gao Xinzhi said, "When the guest first arrived, he was not wearing a hat or socks, wrapped his head with a blue handkerchief, and his feet were bound with white cloth. Besides the great iron maul, he did not carry anything else, but he had a lot of silver in his waist. Both I and the general dared not ask about it." Zican woke up from sleep and saw that the guest was snoring soundly on the kang.

    21

    一日，辞宋将军曰：“吾始闻汝名，以为豪，然皆不足用。吾去矣！”将军强留之，乃曰：“吾尝夺取诸响马物，不顺者辄击杀之；众魁请长其群，吾又不许，是以仇我。久居此，祸必及汝。今夜半，方期我决斗某所。”宋将军欣然曰：“吾骑马挟矢以助战！”客曰：“止！贼能且众，吾欲护汝，则不快吾意。”宋将军故自负，且欲观客所为，力请客。客不得已，与偕行。
    　　将至斗处，送将军登空堡上，曰：“但观之，慎勿声，令贼知汝也！”时鸡鸣月落，星光照旷野，百步见人。客驰下，吹觱篥数声。顷之，贼二十余骑四面集，步行负弓矢从者百许人。一贼提刀纵马奔客，曰：“奈何杀吾兄！”言未毕，客呼曰：“椎！”贼应声落马，人马尽裂。众贼环而进，客从容挥椎，人马四面仆地下，杀三十许人。宋将军屏息观之，股慄欲堕。忽闻客大呼曰：“吾去矣！”但见地尘起，黑烟滚滚，东向驰去。后遂不复至。

    Explanation

    21

    On one day, the guest bade farewell to General Song, saying, "At first, I considered you a hero, but now I find you all to be of no use. I am leaving now!" General Song tried to detain him, and then said, "I have often taken things from bandits, and those who disobey are immediately killed. The leaders asked to lead their group, and I refused, so they hate me. If I stay here for too long, disaster will surely come to you. Tonight at midnight, we are scheduled to fight at a certain place." General Song happily said, "I will ride my horse and carry arrows to help in the battle!" The guest said, "Stop! The bandits are both capable and numerous. If I want to protect you, it will not be to my liking." General Song was proud of himself and wanted to see what the guest would do, so he strongly invited the guest. Reluctantly, the guest agreed to go with him.

    As they approached the fighting place, the guest sent General Song to climb onto an empty watchtower and said, "Just watch, be careful not to make a sound, lest the bandits know you are here!" At the time when the rooster crowed and the moon set, starlight illuminated the vast wilderness, and one could see a person from a hundred steps away. The guest rode down and blew the bili several times. Soon after, more than twenty riders gathered from all sides, and a hundred or so people followed on foot, carrying bows and arrows. One bandit brandished a knife and galloped towards the guest, shouting, "Why did you kill my brother?" Before he finished speaking, the guest commanded, "Hammer!" The bandit fell off his horse, and the people and horses were all torn apart. The bandits surrounded and advanced, but the guest calmly swung the hammer, causing the people and horses to fall to the ground on all sides, killing about thirty people. General Song held his breath and watched, shaking with fear and almost falling. Suddenly, he heard the guest shout, "I am leaving now!" They only saw dust rising and black smoke billowing as the guest galloped eastward. Afterward, he never came again.

    22

    魏禧论曰：子房得沧海君力士，椎秦皇帝博浪沙中。大铁椎其人与！天生异人，必有所用之。予读陈同甫《中兴遗传》，豪俊侠烈魁奇之士，泯泯然不见功名于世者，又何多也！岂天之生才，不必为人用与？抑用之自有时与？子灿遇大铁椎为壬寅岁，视其貌，当年三十，然则大铁椎今四十耳。子灿又尝见其写市物帖子，甚工楷书也。
    　　[张山来曰：篇中点睛，在三称“吾去矣”句。至其历落入古处，如名手画龙，有东云见鳞、西云见爪之妙。] 徐霞客传　钱谦益牧斋文津选本
    　　徐霞客者，名宏祖，江阴梧塍里人也。高祖经，与唐寅同举，除名。寅常以倪云林画卷偿博进三千，手迹犹在其家。霞客生里社，奇情郁然，玄对山水，力耕奉母。践更徭役，蹙蹙如笼鸟之触隅，每思飏去。

    Explanation

    22

    Hui Xi says: Zi Fang obtained a servant or assistant from the sea, who struck the Qin emperor in Bolang Sands. Is this the same person as the Great Iron Mallet? Heaven produces extraordinary people, who must have a purpose. I read Chen Tongfu's "Zhongxing Yichuan," which contains stories of extraordinary and heroic figures who are obscure and fail to achieve fame or success in the world. How many such people are there? Is it that heaven produces talent that is not necessarily meant to be used by others? Or is it that such talent is meant to be used at a certain time? Zi Can met the Great Iron Mallet in the year Renyin. Looking at his appearance, he was thirty years old then, so now the Great Iron Mallet is forty years old. Zi Can also saw him writing a note about market goods, which was very skilled in regular script.

    [ Zhang Shanlai says: The highlight of the piece is the repeated use of the phrase "I must go." Its vivid depiction, like a master painter's brushstrokes, reveals the scales of a dragon on the east and the claws on the west. ]

    Meng Xiangke, whose given name is Hongzu, was from Wucheng Village in Jiangyin. His great-grandfather, Jing, was a fellow examinee with Tang Yin, but was later removed from the list. Tang Yin often exchanged a painting by Ni Yunlin for three thousand coins with a gambler, and the original still exists in his family. Meng Xiangke was born in his hometown, with a passionate spirit that was deeply connected to the mountains and rivers. He worked hard to support his mother. He served his military duties, feeling constrained like a caged bird that constantly bumped into the corners, always thinking of flying away.

    23

    年三十，母遣之出游。每岁三时出游，秋冬觐省，以为常。东南佳山水，如东、西洞庭、阳羡、京口、金陵、吴兴、武林、浙西径山、天目、浙东五泄、四明、天台、雁宕、南海、落伽，皆几案衣带间物耳。有再三至，有数至，无仅一至者。其行也，从一奴，或一僧，一杖，一袱被。不治装，不裹粮。能忍饥数日，能遇食即饱。能徒步走数百里。凌绝壁，冒丛菁，攀援上下，悬度绠汲，捷如青猿，健如黄犊。以崟岩为床席，以溪涧为饮沐，以山魅木客、王孙玃父为伴侣。儚儚粥粥，口不能道词，与之论山经，辨水脉，拽讨形胜，则划然心开。居平未尝鞶帨为古文辞，行游约数百里，就破壁枯树，燃松拾穗，走笔为记，如甲乙之簿，如丹青之画，虽才笔之士无以加也。

    Explanation

    23

    At the age of thirty, his mother sent him out to travel. He traveled three times a year, visiting his family during the autumn and winter seasons, making it a regular practice. The beautiful landscapes of southeastern China, such as the East and West Dongting Lakes, Yangxian, Jingkou, Jinling, Wuxing, Wulin, Jing Mountain in western Zhejiang, Tianmu Mountain, Wuxie Waterfall in eastern Zhejiang, Siming, Tiantai, Yandang, the South China Sea, and Loka, were all familiar places to him, as if they were within reach of his desk or belt. He visited some places multiple times and others several times, but never visited any place only once. When he traveled, he followed a servant or a monk, carrying a staff and a bundle of bedding. He did not prepare luggage or pack food. He could endure hunger for several days and eat until full when food was found. He could walk several hundred miles on foot. He climbed sheer cliffs, passed through dense vegetation, climbed up and down, crossed by rope or bamboo pole, as agile as a green monkey and as strong as a young buffalo. He used rocky cliffs for beds and streams and brooks for drinking and washing. He considered forest spirits, tree dwellers, princes, and ape fathers as his companions. Sometimes he was dizzy and confused, unable to speak coherently, but when he discussed the Shan Jing (Classic of Mountains and Seas), analyzed water systems, and explored geographical features, his mind suddenly opened. In his daily life, he never prepared to write ancient literary works. While traveling, he would sit by broken walls and dead trees, burn pine branches and gather pine cones, and write quickly. His writings were precise and vivid, like a ledger of A and B or a painting in red and blue. Even talented writers could not surpass his work.

    24

    游台、宕还，过陈木叔小寒山。木叔问：“曾造雁山绝顶否？”霞客唯唯。质明已失其所在。十日而返，曰：“吾取间道，扪萝上龙湫三十里，有宕焉，雁所家也。攀绝磴上十数里，正德间白云、云外两僧团瓢尚在。复上二十余里，其颠罡风逼人，有糜鹿数百群，围绕而宿。三宿而始下。”其与人争奇逐胜，欲赌身命，皆此类也。

    Explanation

    24

    After visiting Tai and Dong, he passed by Chen Mushi's Xiao Han Mountain. Chen Mushi asked, "Have you ever reached the summit of Yan Mountain?" Xiahua replied affirmatively. By dawn, he had already lost his whereabouts. After ten days, he returned and said, "I took a shortcut, climbed up to Longqiu by hand-holding vines for thirty miles. There is a cave here, where geese live. I climbed up steep stairs for several miles. During the Zhengde era, two monks named Baiyun and Yunwai still lived in their huts. Then I climbed another twenty miles, where the fierce wind at the summit was intense. There were hundreds of deer herds surrounding and resting. I stayed for three nights before descending." His pursuit of unusual sights and competitive spirit, even risking life and limb, were all of this nature.

    25

    已而游黄山、白岳、九华、匡庐。入闽，登武夷，泛九鲤湖。入楚，谒玄岳。北游齐、鲁、燕、冀、嵩、洛，上华山，下青柯坪。心动趣归，则其母正属疾，啮指相望也。
    　　母丧服阕，益放志远游。访黄石斋于闽，穷闽山之胜，皆非闽人所知。登罗浮，谒曹溪，归而追石斋于黄山。往复万里，如步武耳。由终南背走峨眉，从野人采药，栖宿岩穴中，八日不火食。抵峨眉，属奢酋阻兵，乃返。只身戴釜，访恒山于塞外，尽历九边阨塞。
    　　归，过予山中，剧谈四游四极，九州九府，经纬分合，历历如指掌。谓昔人志星官舆地，多承袭傅会。江河二经，山川两戒，自纪载来，多囿于中国一隅。欲为昆仑海外之游，穷流沙而后返。小舟如叶，大雨淋湿，要之登陆，不肯，曰：“譬如涧泉暴注，撞击肩背，良足快耳！”

    Explanation

    25

    Afterward, he traveled to Mount Huang, Mount Baiyue, Mount Jiuhua, and Mount Kuanglu. He entered Fujian, climbed Mount Wuyi, and boated on the Nine Dragon Lake. He entered Hubei and paid homage to Xuanwu Mountain. He traveled north to Qi, Lu, Yan, Ji, Songshan, and Luoyang, climbed Mount Hua, and descended to Qingkeping. His heart moved to return home, and his mother was just then suffering from an illness, biting her finger in anticipation of his return.

    After completing the mourning rituals for his mother, he further let his aspirations wander far and traveled extensively. He visited Huang Zizhao in Fujian, exploring the finest scenery of Fujian mountains, all of which were unknown to the locals. He climbed Lufu Mountain, paid homage to Caohexi, and returned to pursue Huang Zizhao on Mount Huang. His travels spanned ten thousand miles, yet they seemed like a short step. He traveled from Zhongnan Mountain to Emei Mountain, gathering herbs with the villagers, living in caves and rock shelters for eight days without fire. Upon reaching Emei Mountain, due to the obstruction by the Shu chief, he returned. Traveling alone with a cooking pot, he visited Mount Heng in the border regions, visiting all the strategic passes.

    Returning, he passed through my mountain retreat, engaging in lively discussions about his four journeys to the four corners of the world, the nine provinces, and the nine regions. He spoke of the division and combination of the celestial and terrestrial coordinates, recounting them as if they were right before his eyes. He said that ancient people's records of star officials and geography were often inherited and adapted. The two rivers and the two boundaries of mountains and rivers, since their recording, were mostly confined to one corner of China. He wished to make a journey beyond the Kunlun Mountains and the seas, traveling to the end of the Gobi Desert before returning. In a small boat like a leaf, drenched by heavy rain, he was persuaded to disembark, but he refused, saying, "It is like a sudden rush of mountain spring water hitting the shoulders and back, truly exhilarating!"

    26

    丙子九月，辞家西迈。僧静闻愿登鸡足礼迦叶，请从焉。遇盗于湘江，静闻被创死。函其骨，负之以行。泛洞庭，上衡岳，穷七十二峰。再登峨眉，北抵岷山，极于松潘。又南过大渡河，至黎、雅，登瓦屋、晒经诸山。复寻金沙江，极于犁牛徼外。由金沙南泛澜沧，由澜沧北寻盘江，大约在西南诸夷境，而贵竹、滇南之观，亦几尽矣。过丽江，憩点苍、鸡足，瘗静闻骨于迦叶道场，从宿愿也。由鸡足而西，出玉门关数千里，至昆仑山，穷星宿海，去中夏三万四千三百里。登半山，风吹衣欲堕，望见外方黄金宝塔。又数千里，至西番，参大宝法王。鸣沙之外，咸称胡国，如述庐、阿耨诸名，由旬不能悉。《西域志》称沙河阻远，望人马积骨为标识，鬼魅热风，无得免者。玄奘法师受诸魔折，具载本传。霞客信宿往返，如适莽苍。

    Explanation

    26

    In the month of September in the year of the Fire Rat, I left home and traveled westward. The monk Jingwen wished to ascend Mount Jizu to pay respects to Kasyapa, and I accompanied him. We encountered bandits on the Xiang River, and Jingwen was wounded and died. I encased his bones in a box and carried them along. We traversed Dongting Lake, ascended Mount Heng, and explored the seventy-two peaks. We ascended Mount Emei again, traveled north to Mount Min, and reached Songpan. Then we traveled south over the Dadu River, to Lizi and Yaxi, and climbed Mount Wahu and Mount Shaijing. We then explored the Jinsha River, reaching beyond the border of Ligniu. From the Jinsha River, we traveled south along the Lancang River, and from the Lancang River, we traveled north along the Pan River. This was approximately in the territory of the southwestern barbarians, and the sights of Gui Zhu and Dian Nan were almost all seen. We passed through Lijiang, rested at Diancang and Mount Jizu, and buried Jingwen's bones in Kasyapa's shrine, fulfilling his long-held wish. From Mount Jizu, we traveled west, passing through the Jade Gate Pass for thousands of miles, to Mount Kunlun, and reached the Star Sea, a distance of 34,300 li from Central China. Climbing halfway up the mountain, the wind blew my clothes almost off, and I saw a golden stupa in the distance. Traveling another several thousand miles, we reached the Western Regions and met the Great Treasure Dharma King. Beyond the singing sands, all lands were called the Huns, such as Shule and Anou, whose names cannot be fully listed. According to the Western Regions Gazetteer, the sandy river is remote and dangerous, with human and horse bones serving as markers, and no one can escape the ghosts, demons, and hot winds. Master Xuanzang suffered from various demons, as detailed in his biography. I stayed for two nights and returned, as if I had been in a vast wilderness.

    27

    还至峨眉山下，托估客附所得奇树虬根以归，并以《溯江纪源》一编寓予。言《禹贡》岷山导江，乃泛滥中国之始，非发源也。中国入河之水，为省五；入江之水，为省十一。计其吐纳，江倍于河；按其发源，河自昆仑之北，江亦自昆仑之南，短而河源长也。又辨三龙大势，北龙夹河之北，南龙抱江之南，中龙中界之，特短。北龙只南向半支入中国，唯南龙磅礴半宇内，其脉亦发于昆仑，与金沙江相并南下，环滇池以达五岭。龙长则源脉亦长，江之所以大于河也。其书数万言，皆订补桑经郦注及汉、宋诸儒疏解《禹贡》所未及，予撮其大略如此。
    　　霞客还滇南，足不良行，修《鸡足山志》，三月而毕。丽江木太守偫餱粮、具筍舆以归。病甚，语问疾者曰：“汉张骞凿空，未睹昆仑。唐玄奘、元耶律楚材衔人主之命，乃得西游。吾以老布衣，孤筇双履，穷河沙，上昆仑，历西域，题名绝国，与三人而为四，死不恨矣！”

    Explanation

    27

    Upon returning to the foot of Mount Emei, he entrusted a merchant to bring back the strange trees and twisted roots he had obtained, and also gave me a copy of Sù Jiāng Jì Yuán. He stated that according to the Yǔ Gòng (Classic of History), the Minshan Mountains are where the Yangtze River begins to flood, not its source. China's waters flowing into the Yellow River come from five provinces, while those flowing into the Yangtze come from eleven provinces. Calculating the outflow and inflow, the Yangtze is twice as much as the Yellow River. According to the sources, the Yellow River originates north of Kunlun, and the Yangtze originates south of Kunlun, but the Yellow River's source is longer. He further distinguished the three major mountain ranges: the northern range flanks the Yellow River, the southern range surrounds the Yangtze, and the central range is shorter. Only the southern range extends halfway into China, while the southern range dominates half of the country, its veins also originating from Kunlun, alongside the Jinsha River flowing southward, encircling Dianchi Lake and reaching the Five Ridges. The longer the mountain range, the longer the source, and thus the Yangtze is larger than the Yellow River. His book contains tens of thousands of words, correcting and supplementing previous works on the Yǔ Gòng. I have summarized the main points here.

    Xu Xiake returned to southern Yunnan, his feet not being able to walk well, and completed the Jīzú Mountain Gazetteer in three months. The local governor of Lijiang provided food and bamboo sedan chairs for his return. He was very ill and spoke to those who visited him, saying: "Han dynasty's Zhang Qian explored the unknown, but did not see Kunlun. Tang dynasty's Xuanzang and Yuan dynasty's Yelü Chucai were granted imperial orders to travel west. I, an old commoner, with a single staff and two shoes, traversed the sands of the rivers, climbed Kunlun, traveled through the Western Regions, inscribed my name in distant lands, and joined the ranks of the three, dying without regret!"

    28

    余之识霞客也，因漳人刘履丁。履丁为予言：霞客西归，气息支缀，闻石斋下诏狱，遣其长子间关往视，三月而返，具述石斋讼系状。据床浩叹，不食而卒。其为人若此！
    　　梧下先生曰：昔柳公权记三峰事，有王玄冲者，访南坡僧义海，约登莲花峰。某日届山趾，计五千仞，为一旬之程，既上，煹烟为信。海如期宿桃林。平晓，岳色清明，伫立数息，有白烟一道，起三峰之顶，归二旬而玄冲至，取玉井莲落叶数瓣及池边铁船寸许遗海，负笈而去。玄冲初至，海谓之曰：“兹山削成，自非驭风冯云，无有去理。”玄冲曰：“贤人勿谓天不可登，但虑无其志耳！”霞客不欲以张骞诸人自命，以玄冲拟之，并为三清之奇士，殆庶几乎？

    Explanation

    28

    I came to know Xu Xiake through Zhangren Liu Lüding. Liu Lüding told me: When Xu Xiake returned from his western journey, he was weak and frail. Upon hearing that Zhang Han had been imprisoned in the imperial prison, he sent his eldest son on a difficult journey to visit him. After three months, the son returned and detailed the situation of Zhang Han's imprisonment. Xu Xiake sat on his bed and sighed deeply, refusing to eat, and eventually died. This is how he was as a person!

    Mr. Wu said: In the past, Li Gongquan recorded the story of the Three Peaks, mentioning a man named Wang Xuanchong who visited the monk Yihai on the southern slope of Mount Hua and agreed to climb the Lotus Peak. On a certain day, they reached the foot of the mountain, which was calculated to be 5,000 feet high and would take ten days to ascend. After reaching the summit, they burned incense as a signal. Yihai stayed at Taolin as planned. Early in the morning, the mountain was clear and bright. Yihai stood still for several breaths, and a white smoke rose from the top of the Three Peaks. After twenty days, Wang Xuanchong returned, taking a few petals of the Jade Well lotus and a small piece of the iron ship from the pool to give to Yihai, then left with his books. When Wang Xuanchong first arrived, Yihai said to him, "This mountain is so steep that unless one rides the wind and clouds, there is no way to ascend." Wang Xuanchong replied, "A wise person should not say that heaven is unattainable, but only worry about lacking the will to reach it!" Xu Xiake did not want to compare himself to Zhang Qian and others, but compared him to Wang Xuanchong, considering both of them to be extraordinary figures of the Three Pure Ones, almost certainly so.

    29

    霞客纪游之书，高可隐几，全属其从兄仲昭仇勘而存之，当为古今游记之最。霞客死时，年五十有六。西游归以庚辰六月，卒以辛巳正月，葬江阴之马湾。亦履丁云。
    　　[张山来曰：叙次生动，觉奇人奇情跃跃纸上。快读一过，恍如置身蓬莱三岛，不必更读霞客游记矣。] 秋声诗自序　晋江林嗣环铁崖文津选本
    　　彻呆子当正秋之日，杜门简出，毡有针，壁有衷甲，苦无可排解者。然每听谣诼之来，则濡墨吮笔而为诗。诗成，以“秋声”名篇。

    Explanation

    29

    Xiahke's travelogue, substantial enough to hide a desk, was entirely preserved through the efforts of his cousin Zhongzhao Chou, and it should be considered the greatest travelogue of all time. Xiahke died at the age of fifty-six. He returned from his western journey in the sixth month of the year Gengchen and passed away in the first month of the year XinSi, being buried in the Ma Bay area of Jiangyin. This is also recorded by Lü Ding.

    [Zhang Shanlai said: The narrative is vivid, making the extraordinary person and their emotions leap off the page. Reading it quickly, one feels as if they are in the Three Islands of Penglai, and there is no need to read Xiahke's travelogue again.]

    Autobiographical Preface to the Autumn Sounds Poem by Lin Sihuan of Jinjiang, Iron Cliff, and Wenjin Selections

    On a day of autumn, the thoroughly dull person stayed indoors and rarely went out. The felt mat had needles, and the walls had inner armor, indicating a state of great distress with no way to alleviate it. However, whenever rumors and slanders came, he would wet his brush and write poetry. Upon completing the poem, he named it "Autumn Sounds."

    30

    适有数客至，不问何人，留共醉。酒酣，令客各举似何声最佳。一客曰：“机声，儿子读书声佳耳。”予曰：“何言之庄也！”又一客曰：“堂下呵驺声，堂后笙歌声，何如？”予曰：“何言之华也！”又一客曰：“姑妇楸枰声最佳。”曰：“何言之玄也！”一客独嘿嘿，乃取大杯满酌而前曰：“先生喜闻人所未闻，仆请数言为先生抚掌，可乎？京中有善口技者，会宾客大讌，于厅事之东北角施八尺屏障，口技人坐屏障中，一桌、一椅、一扇、一抚尺而已。家宾团坐。少顷，但闻屏障中抚尺二下，满堂寂然，无敢哗者。遥遥闻深巷犬吠声，便有妇人惊觉欠伸，摇其夫语猥亵事。夫呓语，初不甚应。妇摇之不止，则二人语渐间杂，床又从中戛戛。既而儿醒大啼，夫令妇抚儿乳。儿含乳啼，妇拍而呜之。夫起溺，妇亦抱儿起溺。床上又一大儿醒，狺狺不止。

    Explanation

    30

    There happened to be several guests who arrived, and without asking who they were, I kept them company and drank together. When the wine was flowing freely, I asked the guests to name the best sound they could imagine. One guest said, "The sound of a loom, the sound of a son reading are best." I replied, "How serious your words are!" Another guest said, "The sound of a servant shouting in the hall, the sound of music behind the hall, how are they?" I replied, "How luxurious your words are!" Another guest said, "The sound of a woman playing chess with her husband is best." I said, "How mystical your words are!" One guest remained silent, then took a large cup, filled it, and came forward to say, "Sir, you enjoy hearing what others have not heard. I will offer a few words to make you clap, if you allow it. In the capital, there is a skilled oral artist. When the guests were gathered for a grand feast, in the northeast corner of the hall, a screen eight feet high was placed. The oral artist sat behind the screen, with only a table, a chair, a fan, and a clapper. The guests sat in a circle. After a while, only the sound of the clapper being struck twice behind the screen could be heard. The entire hall fell silent, and no one dared to make a sound. Far away, the sound of a dog barking in a distant alley could be heard. Then a woman woke up with a start and yawned, shaking her husband and telling him something indecent. The husband mumbled in his sleep, not responding much. The woman kept shaking him, and their conversation gradually became mixed. The bed creaked in the middle. Soon the child woke up and cried loudly. The husband told the woman to nurse the child. The child cried while nursing, and the woman patted and hummed to soothe it. The husband got up to urinate, and the woman also held the child and went to urinate. Another child on the bed woke up and barked incessantly.

    31

    当是时，妇手拍儿声，口中呜声，儿含乳啼声，大儿初醒声，床声，夫叱大儿声，溺瓶中声，溺桶中声，一齐凑发，众妙毕备。满座宾客，无不伸颈侧目，微笑嘿叹，以为妙绝也。既而夫上床寝，妇又呼大儿溺，毕，都上床寝。小儿亦渐欲睡，夫鼾声起，妇拍儿亦渐拍渐止。微闻有鼠作作索索，盆器倾侧，妇梦中咳嗽之声。宾客意少舒，稍稍正坐。忽一人大呼火起。夫起大呼，妇亦起大呼，两儿齐哭。俄而百千人大呼，百千儿哭，百千犬吠。中间力拉崩倒之声，火爆声，呼呼风声，百千齐作。又夹百千求救声，曳屋许许声，抢夺声，泼水声。凡所应有，无所不有。虽人有百手，手有百指，不能指其一端；人有百口，口有百舌，不能名其一一处也。于是宾客无不变色离席，奋袖出臂，两股战战，几欲先走。而忽然抚尺一下，群响毕绝，撤屏视之，一人、一桌、一椅、一扇、一抚尺而已！”

    Explanation

    31

    At that time, the sound of the wife patting her child, the sound of her making a soft, soothing sound, the sound of the baby crying while nursing, the sound of the older child waking up, the sound of the bed creaking, the sound of the husband scolding his older child, the sound of water being poured into a bottle, and the sound of water being poured into a bucket all came together at once, and all wonders were fully present. The entire audience, without exception, stretched their necks, turned their eyes, smiled, and murmured in admiration, thinking it was marvelous. Soon after, the husband went to bed, and the wife called the older child to use the bathroom. Afterward, they all went to bed. The younger child also began to fall asleep, and the husband's snoring started, while the wife's patting of the child gradually slowed and stopped. Barely heard were the sounds of mice scurrying, pots and containers being knocked over, and the woman coughing in her sleep. The audience's tension slightly relaxed, and they gradually sat up straight. Suddenly, a man shouted that a fire had broken out. The husband jumped up and shouted, and the wife also jumped up and shouted, and the two children cried together. Soon after, hundreds of people shouted, hundreds of children cried, and hundreds of dogs barked. In the midst of the sound of collapsing and collapsing, the sound of fire crackling, and the sound of the wind blowing, all sounds came together at once. Additionally, the sounds of people calling for help, the sounds of pulling houses, the sounds of struggling, and the sounds of splashing water were heard. Everything that should have been present was there. Even if a person had a hundred hands, each with a hundred fingers, they could not point to one end, and even if a person had a hundred mouths, each with a hundred tongues, they could not name one place. At this point, the audience could not help but change color, leave their seats, fling their sleeves, extend their arms, and their legs trembled, almost wanting to run away first. Suddenly, the clapper was struck, and all sounds ceased at once. When the screen was removed and it was seen, there was only one person, one table, one chair, one fan, and one clapper.

    32

    嘻！若而人者，可谓善画声矣！遂录其语，以为《秋声序》。
    　　[张山来曰：绝世奇枝，复得此奇文以传之。读竟，辄浮大白。] 盛此公传　大梁周亮工减斋赖古堂集
    　　盛此公，名于斯，南陵人。家故不资。先世有义声。屋以内多藏书，外多良田。此公年十数龄，即能读等身书，有声邑里。长肆力为古文词，虽不中有司尺度，而声称籍甚。然是时，此公但闭户读书，固不出与人见也。

    Explanation

    32

    Ah! Such individuals can truly be said to excel in depicting sounds! Their words were then recorded and used as the preface to Autumn Sounds.
    [Zhāng Shānlái said: An extraordinary and unparalleled branch, now to have this extraordinary text to pass on. After finishing reading, one would immediately drink a large cup of wine.] Biography of Mr. Sheng from Daliang Zhou Lianggong Jiǎnzhāi Láigǔtáng Collection
    Mr. Sheng, named Yú Sī, was from Nánlíng. His family was not wealthy. His ancestors had a reputation for righteousness. Inside the house there were many books, and outside there were many good fields. In his teens, he could read as much as his own body weight in books and had a reputation in the village. When he grew up, he devoted himself to writing classical prose, although it did not conform to the standards of the examiners, yet his reputation grew widely. However, at that time, Mr. Sheng only stayed home and read books, and did not come out to meet people.

    33

    会其尊人捐馆舍，乃抗傸好交。邑里人才智咸出此公下，此公乃以为无足语。去而之秣陵，欲尽交东南士，东南士亦愿交此公。此公以为：“世且乱，吾当见天子，慷慨言当世事。彼经生何足语，会求其人于屠狗间。”于是益散金结客，遂为广陵儿所绐。
    　　是时边事急，广陵儿讽此公出家资备公家缓急。此公故慷慨欲见天子言当世事，乃为所中。久之，事卒不济，而金垂尽。嗒然与世无所合，退而返里闬，里闬又嗤笑之。此公益不复事事，产益落，所为文益不合有司尺度。侘傺无聊，多饮酒，与妇人近。不数年，病矣。少愈，右臂诎伸不已，若指遂不诎伸。此公故工书，丐其书者，辄以左手濡墨，纳右指窍中。见者以为苦，顾其书则益工，时为人据石擘窠书。好为诗，酒后呜呜吟不已。间至秣陵，遴制举义行之，非其志也。

    Explanation

    33

    When his father passed away, he began to make friends. The talents and wisdom of the townspeople were all inferior to this gentleman, and he considered them not worth talking to. He left and went to Moling, wanting to make friends with all the scholars in the southeast, and they also wished to befriend him. This gentleman thought, "The world is about to be in chaos; I will meet the emperor and speak boldly about the affairs of the world. Those scholars are not worth talking to; I will seek out the right person among the common folk." Thus, he began to spend more money to make friends, and he was deceived by the people of Guangling.

    At that time, there was an urgent situation on the frontier, and the people of Guangling persuaded this gentleman to use his family wealth to prepare for the state's needs. This gentleman was indeed bold and wanted to meet the emperor and speak about the affairs of the world, but he was deceived. After a long time, the matter ultimately failed, and his money was nearly exhausted. He became desolate and no longer fit in with the world, returning to his hometown, where the people of his hometown mocked him. This gentleman no longer cared about anything, his property declined even more, and the writing he produced became even less in line with the standards of the officials. He was dejected and bored, drank a lot, and got close to women. Not many years later, he fell ill. After a slight recovery, his right arm could not stop moving, as if his finger could no longer move. This gentleman was skilled in calligraphy; those who begged for his calligraphy would dip his left hand in ink and insert his right finger into the ink. Those who saw it thought it was difficult, but his calligraphy became even more skilled. He would sometimes write on stone tablets for others. He liked to write poetry, and after drinking, he would hum poetry endlessly. Occasionally, he would go to Moling to select and compile examples of proper conduct, but it was not his true intention.

    34

    岁在辛未，予自大梁来秣陵省家大人。家大人好此公诗，语亮曰：“此间有盛此公，工为诗，儿识之。”亮因以父命往交此公。此公独异予，以为恨不十载前识。明年，此公目病，数明晦，或不能视。予窃忧之，讽其勿读书饮酒。此公曰：“如是，不如其遂盲也！”会目病甚，又念母老，乃别予归，意怆然，若不复与予见者。予私以为予当复见之，意以其盲而止耳，孰意遂不复见耶？
    　　此公归，吾师静原相公方督学江以北，耳其名，询之郡大夫，郡大夫以盲告。公曰：“江以北其不盲者何限耶？”于是邑令盲试之，旅诸士进于郡大夫。郡大夫复盲试之，旅诸士进于公。公大奇之，乃得补博士弟子员。

    Explanation

    34

    In the year of Xin Wei, I traveled from Daliang to Moling to visit my father. My father admired this gentleman's poetry and told Liang, "There is someone here who excels in this gentleman's work, skilled in writing poetry, let me introduce him to you." Liang then, following his father's instruction, went to meet this gentleman. This gentleman was unique to me and regretted not meeting me ten years ago. The following year, this gentleman suffered from eye disease, frequently experiencing light and darkness, sometimes he could not see. I secretly worried about him and advised him not to read or drink alcohol. This gentleman said, "If so, it would be better if I became completely blind!" When his eye disease worsened, and he also thought of his old mother, he then bid farewell to me and returned home, feeling sorrowful, as if he would never see me again. I privately thought that I would see him again, thinking it was just because he was blind, who would have thought that I would never see him again?

    This gentleman returned home. My teacher, Jingyuan Xianggong, was supervising education in the northern part of Jiang. He heard of his name and asked the prefect. The prefect told him about his blindness. The official said, "In the northern part of Jiang, there are countless people who are not blind!" The county magistrate then tested him with blindfolds, and the scholars were then presented to the prefect. The prefect then tested him again with blindfolds, and the scholars were then presented to the official. The official found him very remarkable and allowed him to join the ranks of the doctoral students.

    35

    嗟夫！此公盲矣，犹不忘视，屈其二十年锐往之气，俯而与邑之黄口儿扶掖彳亍，旅进旅退，争有司阶前盈尺地而不惭，岂不悲哉！试后，犹寄语予曰：“盲儿无以慰老亲，子毋嗤。”予为悲动者久之。因慨夫祖宗立法过严，士即负奇材，抱异质，魁奇特起，不俯首就有司尺度，他途无由进。又慨夫吾师静原相公，能于成格之中破例待人，使既盲之士犹得出而就有司尺度，且不惜阶前盈尺地，与盲士娓娓不休。嗟夫！此固昌黎代张太祝，望之当世而不得者，今得之公，岂不甚盛举哉！
    　　又明年癸酉，予自秣陵返大梁。闻此公以目久不愈，愈愤激，家益窘乏，无从得医药，于是遂长盲矣。然呜呜吟如往昔。丐其书者，以笔濡墨纳右指窍中，如其不盲时。此公以手扪幅，兔起鹘落，神采奕奕，视不盲时有加，环观者自愧其双眸炯炯也。益好读书，危坐绳床，听他人诵，更番不令休，入耳辄记忆不遗。有所撰述，口授友人，滔滔汩汩，凡数人不能供笔札。

    Explanation

    35

    Alas! This gentleman is now blind, yet he still does not forget to look, bending his twenty years of sharp, forward-moving energy to walk with the children of the town, supporting them step by step, advancing and retreating together, striving for a tiny space before the official's steps without shame. How sad indeed! After the examination, he still entrusted me with a message: "The blind son has nothing to comfort his old parents, so do not mock me." I was deeply moved by his sorrow for a long time. I then lamented that our ancestors' laws were too strict, causing scholars with extraordinary talents and unique qualities, who rise uniquely and boldly, to not bow their heads to the official's standards and have no other way to advance. I also lamented that my teacher, the respected official Jingyuan, could break the rules within the established norms to treat people, allowing a blind scholar to still emerge and gain the official's attention, and even sparing a tiny space before the official's steps to speak with the blind scholar at length. Alas! This is indeed the case where Han Yu of Changli, who hoped to find Zhang Taizhu in his time, now finds it in this gentleman, which is not a great achievement?

    The following year, in the year of Guiyou, I returned from Moling to Daliang. I heard that this gentleman's eyes had not healed for a long time, becoming more and more agitated, and his family was increasingly impoverished, unable to obtain medicine. Thus, he became completely blind. Yet, he still sang softly as before. Those who begged for his writing would place a brush dipped in ink in the opening of his right finger, just as he did when he was not blind. This gentleman would feel the paper with his hand, his brush moving swiftly and gracefully, his spirit vibrant, even more so than when he was not blind, causing those around him to feel ashamed of their own bright eyes. He became even more fond of reading, sitting upright on a rope bed, listening to others recite, taking turns without letting them rest, and remembering everything he heard. When he had something to write, he would dictate to his friends, speaking continuously and rapidly, so that several people could not keep up with writing.

    36

    尝以书寄予大梁，至数千言。言“子当不长贫贱。他日拥节江上，取道南陵，魁湖之北，桃源之南，予墓在焉。子当登我堂，拜我老母，为我书石曰：盛此公埋骨处，予愿足矣！他则子之事也，予何言。”予得其书，忽忽如失者数日，知此公将不永矣。
    　　不数日，凶问至，予为位哭之。会予成进士，官山左，不能即至秣陵。比至秣陵，欲买舟省盛母，会乱甚，又不果行。乃使掾往慰盛母。掾归，为予言盛母年且开八袠，妻倍孝谨。故无子，一女先盛没。一老仆，樵以供两孀妇，糠豆不赡，裋褐不完，败屋数楹，不蔽风雨。行道见之盗嗟，而为之友者帛唁阙然。嗟夫天乎！孰使此公而至此极耶！予解橐金，复促掾往，赎其田之易与族人者，佐盛母饘粥。市石，檄南陵令碑其墓，予自书“盛此公埋骨处”，从其生时请也。

    Explanation

    36

    I have attempted to translate the provided Classical Chinese text into English, adhering to the requirements and guidelines you've outlined. Here is the final translation:

    I once tried to send a letter to me in Daliang, which was as long as several thousand words. It said, "You will not remain poor and lowly for long. One day, you will hold the seal of office on the banks of the river, taking the road through Nanling, north of Kui Lake, south of Peach Blossom Spring, where my tomb is. You will enter my hall and pay respects to my old mother. Write on the stone for me, saying: 'Here lies the bones of this esteemed gentleman.' That will be enough for me! The rest is your responsibility; I have nothing more to say." Upon receiving the letter, I was in a daze for several days, knowing that this gentleman would not live long.

    Not long after, the news of his death arrived, and I mourned for him. When I became a Jinshi and served as an official in Shandong, I could not immediately go to Moling. When I finally reached Moling, I wanted to hire a boat to visit Mrs. Sheng, but there was great turmoil, and I could not go. I then sent an assistant to comfort Mrs. Sheng. The assistant returned and told me that Mrs. Sheng was nearly eighty years old, and her wife was twice as filial and respectful. She has no children; she has one daughter who died before him. She has one old servant who cuts wood to support the two widows, barely having enough coarse food, their clothes not complete, their dilapidated house having only a few rooms, not sheltering them from wind and rain. Passersby see them and sigh, but those who are friends do not send silk condolences. Alas, heaven! Who made this gentleman come to such a state? I took out my money and then sent the assistant again to redeem the land that had been sold to relatives, to help Mrs. Sheng with her porridge. I bought a stone and ordered the magistrate of Nanling to erect a stele at his tomb, writing myself, "Here lies the bones of this esteemed gentleman," following his request when he was alive.

    37

    西蜀蝶庵陈公时守宛陵。公在大梁，盖常闻予数言南陵盛此公不置。邑属公，公乃檄令视盛母无恙，手书“盛此公读书处”为额，悬其常危坐绳床侧；复允予请，以其行谊补郡乘。其读书之屋，盖已受值，期以盛母存殁，不能待盛妻也。予归其值，祀此公于中，俾其老仆守之。
    　　此公好为古文词。盲而死，无子弟为之收拾，故多散乱。其所著，如《毛诗名物考》三十卷，《休庵杂钞》十卷，《历法》二卷，《舆地考》十卷，《群书考索》十二卷。今所传者，独《名物考》耳，他皆不传。予遣掾就其家钞遗书。盛母泣而曰：“儿著书咸为人窃去，唯存诗若干卷，老年人坐则悬之肘，卧则枕之。老年人不即填沟壑者，怜吾儿并数寸之书亦不传耳！今且托之周君！”予受而泣，因为之次第寿之梓。

    Explanation

    37

    Chen Gong of Diean in Western Shu was serving as the governor of Wanling at the time. While he was in Daliang, he often heard me speak highly of the gentleman from Nanling without ceasing. The county belonged to him, so he issued an order to see if Madam Sheng was well. He wrote a handwritten note, "The Study of Sheng This Gentleman," and hung it beside the rope bed where he often sat upright. He also agreed to my request to include his deeds and virtues in the local annals. The house where he studied had already been sold, depending on whether Madam Sheng was alive or deceased, and could not wait for Sheng's wife. I returned the money and enshrined him in the middle, having his old servant guard it.

    This gentleman loved to write classical prose and poetry. He died blind and had no children to collect his works, so they were scattered and disorganized. His writings included the Mao Shi Mingwu kao (Examination of the Names and Objects in the Mao Poetry) in thirty volumes, the Xiuan Zhaocao (Miscellaneous Notes of the Xiuan) in ten volumes, the Lifa (Calendar) in two volumes, the Yudi kao (Geographical Examination) in ten volumes, and the Qunshu kao suo (Examination of Various Books) in twelve volumes. Nowadays, only the Mingwu kao is transmitted; the others are not. I sent a clerk to copy his remaining books from his home. Madam Sheng wept and said, "All my son's books have been stolen by others. Only a few volumes of his poems remain. The old lady would hang them on her elbow when sitting and use them as a pillow when lying down. The old lady did not immediately fall into the ditches because she pitied her son and wanted even these small books to be transmitted. Now I entrust them to Zhou Jun!" I received them and wept, therefore arranging them in order and having them printed.

    38

    嗟夫！此公能文章，而不以文显；好弯弓驰驱，而不以将名；行谊不愧古人，而不以行征；工为诗，而不以诗辟。黄金既尽，日徒愤激，退而自悔，又以盲死。筦簟未占，嗣续中绝。老母寡妻，形影相吊。生平故旧，不为存问。遗书狼籍，行谊莫传。徒存此数卷之诗，悬命于七十余年母氏之手，使不知此公者，读其诗，以为其才且尽于此，而知者因其已然，想其未然，咨嗟太息不能已已。嗟夫！孰使此公而至此极耶？夫士既不能块然独处，则不得不出而与人交；与人交不受其益，徒为所害如此！此虽其不慎交游所致，然孰非天哉！孰非天哉！天为庸流俾长守富贵，少为姱节奇行者，必阴摧折之，从来久矣！予又何憾于广陵儿哉？此公初名篯，今尺牍中所传盛篯侯是也。

    Explanation

    38

    Ah! This gentleman was skilled in writing articles, yet he did not gain fame through his writings; he enjoyed drawing a bow and riding fast, yet he did not become renowned as a general; his conduct did not disgrace ancient people, yet he did not earn recognition for his moral character; he was skilled in writing poetry, yet he did not achieve fame through his poetry. His gold has been exhausted, and he daily just in a state of anger and agitation, retreating and regretting, and dies blind. He has not yet occupied bamboo mats, and his descendants are interrupted. An elderly mother and a widow, shadows and forms console each other. Friends from the past, not visited or inquired about. Abandoned books are scattered, and his conduct is not passed on. Only these few scrolls of poetry remain, his life hanging by the hands of his seventy-year-old mother, making those who do not know him think that his talent is limited to this, while those who know him, based on what has happened, imagine what might have been, sighing and lamenting endlessly. Ah! Who made this gentleman come to this extreme? Scholars cannot live alone in isolation, so they must interact with others; interacting with others does not bring benefit, only to be harmed in this way! This is due to his carelessness in choosing friends, but who is not fated by heaven! Who is not fated by heaven! Heaven makes common people long hold wealth and rank, while those who are virtuous and have extraordinary conduct are inevitably crushed, a phenomenon that has existed for a long time! What regret do I have for the man from Guangling? Originally named Jian, now in letters, the one known as Sheng Jianhou.

    39

    [张山来曰：古今盲而能文者，自左卜以下，推吾家张藉。今得此公，亦不寂寞矣。然诸人仅工诗文，而此公复能书，则尤奇也。] 汤琵琶传　南昌王猷定于一四照堂集
    　　汤应曾，邳州人，善弹琵琶，故人呼为“汤琵琶”云。贫无妻，事母甚孝，所居有石楠树，构茅屋，奉母朝夕。幼好音律，闻歌声辄哭。已学歌，歌罢又哭。其母问曰：“儿何悲？”应曾曰：“儿无所悲也，心自凄动耳。”
    　　世庙时，李东垣善琵琶，江对峰传之，名播京师。江死，陈州蒋山人独传其妙。时周藩有女乐数十部，咸习蒋技，罔有善者，王以为恨。应曾往学之，不期年而成。闻于王，王召见，赐以碧镂牙嵌琵琶，令着宫锦衣，殿上弹《胡笳十八拍》，哀楚动人。王深赏，岁给米万斛，以养其母。应曾由是著名大梁间，所至狭邪争慕其声，咸狎昵之。然颇自矜重，不妄为人奏。

    Explanation

    39
