    Zhang Shanlai said: From ancient times to the present, there have been blind people who are capable of writing, starting from Zuǒ Bǔ and including our family's Zhang Jie. Now, with this gentleman, we are no longer lonely. However, the others were only skilled in poetry and prose, but this gentleman also excels in calligraphy, which is even more remarkable.

    Biography of Tang Yingzeng by Wang Youding in Yi Sizhaotang Ji (The Collection of Yi Sizhaotang)

    Tang Yingzeng was from Pizhou and was skilled in playing the pipa, hence people called him "Tang Pipa." He was poor and had no wife, but he was very filial to his mother. He lived in a house made of thatch, where there was a stone nan tree, and he took care of his mother day and night. As a child, he loved music and would cry when he heard songs. After learning to sing, he would cry again after singing. His mother asked him, "Why are you sad?" Tang Yingzeng replied, "I am not sad, my heart just feels sad."

    During the reign of Emperor Jingtai, Li Dongyuan was skilled in playing the pipa, and Jiang Duifeng learned from him, becoming famous in Beijing. Jiang died, and Chenzhou's Jiang Shansheng alone mastered the art of playing the pipa. At that time, the Prince of Zhou had several groups of female musicians who all practiced Jiang Shansheng's techniques, but none of them were good, which made the prince regret it. Tang Yingzeng went to learn from him and mastered the art in less than a year. He was summoned by the prince, who gave him a beautifully crafted pipa with inlaid ivory and carved decorations, ordered him to wear official robes, and had him play "The Eighteen Beats of the Huqin" on the palace steps, which was so moving and sorrowful that it touched the heart. The prince greatly appreciated his performance and provided him with a thousand measures of rice annually to support his mother. Tang Yingzeng thus became famous in Daliang (Kaifeng), and wherever he went, the courtesans vied to admire his talent and treated him affectionately. However, he was somewhat proud and did not lightly play for others.

    40

    后征西王将军招之幕中，随历嘉峪、张掖、酒泉诸地。每猎及阅士，令弹《塞上》之曲。戏下颜骨打者，善战阵，其临敌，令为壮士声，乃上马杀贼。
    　　一日至榆关，大雪，马上闻觱篥，忽思母痛哭，遂别将军去。夜宿酒楼，不寐，弹琵琶作觱篥声，闻者莫不陨涕。及旦，一邻妇诣楼上，曰：“君岂有所感乎？何声之悲也！妾孀居十载，依于母而母亡，欲委身，无可适者，愿执箕帚为君妇。”应曾曰：“若能为我事母乎？”妇许诺，遂载之归。
    　　襄王闻其名，使人聘之。居楚者三年，偶泛洞庭，风涛大作，舟人惶扰失措。应曾匡坐弹《洞庭秋思》，稍定。舟泊岸，见一老猿，须眉甚古，自丛箐中跳入蓬窗，哀号中夜，天明，忽抱琵琶跃水中，不知所在。自失故物，辄惆怅不复弹。

    Explanation

    40

    After the Western Campaign General invited him into his camp, they traveled through places such as Jiayu Pass, Zhangye, and Jiuquan. Whenever he went hunting or reviewed the troops, he ordered him to play the tune of "The Frontier." Under his command was Yan Gudada, who was skilled in battle formations. When facing the enemy, he ordered him to make a heroic sound, and then he would mount his horse and kill the enemy.

    One day, they reached the Yuyang Pass, where it was heavily snowing. While on horseback, he heard the sound of the bili and suddenly thought of his mother, crying bitterly. He then left the general and departed. That night, he stayed in a tavern and could not sleep, playing the pipa and imitating the sound of the bili. Those who heard it all wept. By dawn, a neighbor woman came to the upstairs room and asked, "Are you moved by something? Why is the sound so sad! I have been a widow for ten years, relying on my mother until she died. I want to commit myself, but there is no one to marry. I wish to serve you as a wife." Ying Zeng said, "Can you take care of my mother for me?" The woman agreed, and they then took her home.

    The King of Xiang heard of his name and sent someone to invite him. He lived in Chu for three years. Once, he sailed on Dongting Lake, and a great storm arose. The boatmen were panic-stricken and at a loss. Ying Zeng sat upright and played the tune of "Autumn Thoughts on Dongting Lake," which gradually calmed the situation. The boat docked at the shore, and he saw an old monkey with very old-looking whiskers and eyebrows, jumping from the dense forest into the thatched window. It cried mournfully through the night. At dawn, it suddenly grabbed the pipa and leaped into the water, disappearing without a trace. After losing his old instrument, he became melancholy and no longer played.

    41

    已归省母，母尚健而妇已亡，唯居旁抔土在焉。母告以“妇亡之夕，有猿啼户外，启户不见。妇谓我曰：『吾待郎不至，闻猿啼，何也？吾殆死？唯久不闻郎琵琶声，倘归，为我一奏石楠之下。』”应曾闻母言，掩抑哀痛不自胜，夕陈酒浆，弹琵琶于其墓而祭之。自是猖狂自放，日荒酒色。值寇乱，负母鬻食兵间。耳目聋瞽，鼻漏，人不可迩。召之者隔以屏障，听其声而已。
    　　所弹古调百十余曲，大而风雨雷霆，与夫愁人思妇，百虫之号，一草一木之吟，靡不于其声中传之。而尤得意于《楚汉》一曲，当其两军决战时，声动天地，瓦屋若飞坠。徐而察之，有金声、鼓声、剑弩声、人马辟易声。俄而无声。久之，有怨而难明者，为楚歌声；凄而壮者，为项王悲歌慷慨之声、别姬声；陷大泽，有追骑声；至乌江，有项王自刎声、余骑蹂践争项王声。使闻者始而奋，既而恐，终而涕泪之无从也。其感人如此！

    Explanation

    41

    He had returned to visit his mother, who was still in good health, while his wife had passed away, leaving only a small mound of earth beside her grave. His mother told him, "On the night my wife died, there was a monkey crying outside the door. When I opened the door, I saw nothing. She said to me, 'I waited for you, but you did not come. I heard the monkey cry, what does it mean? Am I about to die? I have not heard your pipa sound for a long time. If you return, play for me under the stone nandana tree.'" He had heard his mother's words and was overwhelmed with grief, unable to bear it. At dusk, he prepared wine and set out to play the pipa at her grave as a memorial. From then on, he became reckless and indulgent, daily wasting himself in wine and women. During a time of war, he carried his mother and sold food among the soldiers. His senses were dulled, his nose ran, and he was unpleasant to be around. Those who summoned him kept him separated by screens or partitions, listening to his music alone.

    He played over a hundred ancient tunes, ranging from the sounds of wind, rain, thunder, and lightning, to the cries of sorrowful people and thinking women, the calls of a hundred insects, and the songs of every plant and tree—all were conveyed through his music. He was particularly skilled at the Chu-Han piece. When it reached the part where the two armies were in battle, the sound moved the heavens and earth, and the tiles of the house seemed to fly off. Carefully listening, one could hear the sounds of gongs, drums, swords, crossbows, and the retreat of horses and men. Soon, the sound ceased. After a while, there was a mournful and difficult-to-understand sound, which was the song of Chu; a mournful yet grand sound, which was the sorrowful and heroic song of Xiang Yu, and the farewell to his concubine; the sound of pursuing cavalry as they fell into a marsh; and the sounds of Xiang Yu's suicide and the trampling and fighting over his body by his remaining soldiers at the River Wu. Those who listened were first inspired, then afraid, and finally overwhelmed with tears, unable to hold them back. Such was the power of his music to move people.

    42

    应曾年六十余，流落淮浦，有桃源人见而怜之，载其母同至桃源，后不知所终。
    　　轸石王子曰：古今以琵琶著名者多矣，未有如汤君者。夫人苟非有至性，则其情必不深，乌能传于后世乎？戊子秋，予遇君公路浦，已不复见君曩者衣宫锦之盛矣。明年复访君，君坐土室，作食奉母。人争贱之，予肃然加敬焉。君仰天呼呼曰：“已矣！世鲜知音，吾事老母百年后，将投身黄河死矣！”予凄然，许君立传。越五年，乃克为之。呜呼！世之沦落不偶而叹息于知音者，独君也乎哉！

    Explanation

    42

    Ying Zeng, in his sixties, had wandered and settled in Huai River. The people of Peach Blossom Spring saw him and pitied him, carrying his mother to Peach Blossom Spring together. Later, it is unknown where they ended up.

    Zhenshi Prince said: Throughout history, many people have gained fame for playing the pipa, but none have been as remarkable as Master Tang. If a person does not possess a profound nature, then their feelings will not be deep, how could they be passed down to future generations? In the autumn of the year Wu Zi, I met Master Tang at Gong Road, and I no longer see the grandeur of your former attire of palace brocade. The following year, I visited you again, and you were sitting in a humble earthen room, preparing food to serve your mother. People competed to look down on you, but I felt a deep sense of respect. You looked up to the sky and said, "It is over! The world lacks true appreciators, after taking care of my mother for a hundred years, I will throw myself into the Yellow River and die!" I felt sad, and I agreed to write a biography for you. After five years, I finally managed to do it. Alas! Those in the world who are unrecognized and sigh for true appreciators, is it only you?

    43

    [张山来曰：韩昌黎《颖师琴》诗，欧阳子谓其是听琵琶。予初疑之，盖以琵琶未必能如诗中所云之妙也。今读此文，觉尔汝轩昂，顷刻变换，浔阳江口，尚逊一筹耳。] 小青传　佚名
    　　小青者，虎林某生姬也。家广陵，与生同姓，故讳之，仅以小青字云。姬夙根颖异，十岁，遇一老尼授《心经》，一再过了了，覆之不失一字。尼曰：“是儿早慧福薄，愿乞作弟子。即不尔，无令识字，可三十年活尔。”家人以为妄，嗤之。母本女塾师，随就学，所游多名闺，遂得精涉诸技，妙解声律。江都固佳丽地，或闺彦云集，茗战手语，众偶纷然。姬随变酬答，悉出意表，人人唯恐失姬。虽素娴仪则，而风期异艳，绰约自好，其天性也。

    Explanation

    43

    Translation

    Zhang Shanlai said: Han Changli's poem "The Poem of the Master of the Spring Water" was interpreted by Ouyang Zi as being about listening to a pipa. I initially doubted this, thinking that the pipa might not be able to achieve the beauty described in the poem. Now reading this text, I feel that you are both majestic and lively, changing in an instant, and even the mouth of the Xunyang River falls short by a little bit.

    小青传　佚名

    Xiao Qing, a concubine of a man from Hu Lin, comes from Guangling and shares the same surname with her master, so she is only referred to as Xiao Qing. From a young age, she was exceptionally gifted; at ten years old, she met an elderly nun who taught her the Heart Sutra. She read it twice and understood it thoroughly, recalling it without a single word missing. The nun said, "This child is very intelligent but has a thin fate; I wish to take her as my disciple. If not, do not let her learn to read, for she will live for another thirty years." Her family thought this was absurd and laughed at her. Her mother was a female teacher, and Xiao Qing followed her to school. She mingled with many distinguished women and thus became skilled in various arts and had a deep understanding of poetry and music. Jiangdu is indeed a beautiful place, and sometimes gatherings of talented women would convene, engaging in literary contests and conversations. Xiao Qing responded to them all with ease, often surprising everyone with her unexpected replies. Everyone feared losing her company. Although she was naturally well-versed in etiquette, her grace and charm were unique and captivating, a natural part of her character.

    44

    年十六，归生。生，豪公子也，性嘈唼憨跳不韵。妇更奇妒；姬曲意下之，终不解。一日，随游天竺，妇问曰：“吾闻东方佛无量，而世多专礼大士者何？”姬曰：“以其慈悲耳。”妇知讽己，笑曰：“吾当慈悲汝！”乃徙之孤山别业，诫曰：“非吾命而郎至，不得入；非吾命而郎手札至，亦不得入！”姬自念彼置我闲地，必密伺短长，借莫须有事鱼肉我，以故深自敛戢。妇或出游，呼与同舟。遇两堤之驰骑挟弹游冶少年，诸女伴指点谑跃，倏东倏西，姬淡然凝坐而已。

    Explanation

    44

    At sixteen, she married into the family of Sheng. Sheng was a young man from a wealthy and influential family, known for being loud, restless, and lacking in refinement. His wife was extremely jealous; the bride tried to be excessively accommodating, but her efforts were ultimately futile. One day, they went on a trip to Tianzhu. The wife asked, "I have heard that there are countless Buddhas in the East, yet why do people often focus on worshipping bodhisattvas?" The bride replied, "It is because of their compassion." The wife understood this as a veiled criticism of herself and laughed, saying, "I shall show you compassion!" She then moved her to a separate residence on Solitary Mountain and warned her, "If my husband comes without my permission, she may not enter. If my husband's letter arrives without my permission, she may not enter either!" The bride realized that her wife had placed her in a position of isolation and suspected that she would be closely watching for any missteps, possibly fabricating false charges to oppress her. Therefore, she deeply restrained herself. When the wife went out, she sometimes invited the bride to join her on outings. During these trips, they encountered young men riding horses and shooting birds, who were playful and flirtatious. The other women pointed and laughed, moving about restlessly. The bride, however, remained calm and composed, sitting quietly.

    45

    妇之戚属某夫人者，才而贤，常就姬学奕，绝爱怜之。因数取巨觞觞妇，瞷妇已醉，徐语姬曰：“船有楼，汝伴我一登。”比登楼，远眺久之，抚姬背曰：“好光景可惜，毋自苦！章台柳亦倚红楼盼韩郎走马，而子作蒲团空观耶？”姬曰：“贾平章剑锋可畏也！”夫人笑曰：“子误矣！平章剑钝，女平章乃利害耳！”顷之，从容讽曰：“子既娴仪则，又多技能，而风流绰约复尔，岂当堕罗刹国中？吾虽非女侠，力能脱子火坑。

    Explanation

    45

    A certain relative of the woman, a lady who was both talented and virtuous, often went to learn chess from Ji and was deeply loved and pitied by her. She often took large cups of wine to toast the woman, waiting until she was drunk, and then spoke to Ji calmly and said, "There is a ship with a cabin; let me accompany you up." After climbing the cabin, they gazed far into the distance for a long time, and she patted Ji's back and said, "Such a beautiful scene is a pity to waste; don't trouble yourself! The story of Zhangtai Willow waiting by a red building for Han Lang to ride by is well-known, but are you going to sit on a meditation cushion in vain contemplation?" Ji replied, "The swordsmanship of General Jia Pingzhang is formidable!" The lady laughed and said, "You are mistaken! General Jia's sword is dull, but the female version of General Jia is even more dangerous!" After a while, she spoke gently and hinted, "You are already skilled in etiquette, and have many skills, and are so graceful and charming. How could you fall into the realm of demons? Although I am not a female hero, I have the power to rescue you from the fire pit."

    46

    顷言章台柳，子非会心人耶？天下岂少韩君乎？且彼纵善遇子，子终向党将军帐下作羔酒侍儿乎？”姬曰：“夫人休矣！妾幼梦手折一花，随风片片著水，命止此矣！夙业未了，又生他想，彼冥曹姻缘簿，非吾如意珠，再辱奚为？徒供群口画描耳！”夫人叹曰：“子言亦是，吾不子强。虽然，子亦宜自爱。彼或好言饮食汝，乃更可虑。即旦夕所须，第告我无害。”因相顾泣下沾衣。徐拭泪还座，寻别去。夫人每向宗戚语及之，无不咨嗟叹息云。
    　　姬自后幽愤凄恻，俱托之诗或小词。而夫人后亦旋宦远方。姬益寥閴，遂感疾。妇命医来，仍遣婢捧药至。姬佯感谢，婢出，掷药床头，叹曰：“吾即不愿生，亦当以净体皈依，作刘安鸡犬，岂以一杯鸩断送耶？”然病益不支，水粒俱绝，日饮梨汁盏许。益明妆冶服，拥袱欹坐，或呼琵琶妇唱盲词以遣。虽数昏数醒，终不蓬首偃卧也。

    46

    If you were not someone who understands the meaning of the Changtai Willow, then you are not the one who grasps its essence. Is there not already enough Han Jun in the world? And even if he treats you well, will you ultimately become a concubine in Dang Jun’s tent?" The lady replied, "Madam, please stop! When I was young, I dreamed of plucking a flower, which fell in pieces into the water, symbolizing that my fate is sealed. My past karma is not yet resolved, and I have other thoughts. The marriage records in the underworld are not my ideal match, so why should I endure further humiliation? I would only be a subject for others to mock and paint!" The madam sighed and said, "What you say is true, and I will not force you. However, you should also take care of yourself. If he flatters you with food and drink, it becomes even more concerning. If you need anything daily, just tell me, and there will be no harm." They looked at each other and cried, tears wetting their clothes. After wiping their tears, they returned to their seats and soon parted ways. Whenever the madam mentioned it to her relatives, they all sighed and lamented.

    Afterward, the lady vented her hidden resentment and sorrow through poetry or short verses. Meanwhile, the madam also went on an official tour to a distant place. The lady became even more lonely and eventually fell ill. The wife sent a doctor and had a maid bring medicine. The lady pretended to be grateful, but when the maid left, she threw the medicine on the bed and sighed, "If I do not wish to live, I should purify my body and seek enlightenment, becoming like Liu An’s chickens and dogs. How could I end my life with a cup of poison?" However, her condition worsened, and she could no longer eat or drink, only drinking a cup of pear juice daily. She continued to dress beautifully and sit elegantly, sometimes calling for a woman to play the pipa and sing folk songs to pass the time. Although she often fell in and out of consciousness, she never let her hair go unkempt or lie prostrate.

    47

    忽一日，语老妪曰：“可传语冤业郎，觅一良画师来。”师至，命写照。写毕，揽镜熟视曰：“得吾形似矣，未尽吾神也。姑置之。”又易一图，曰：“神是矣，而风态未流动也，若见我目端手庄，太矜持故也。姑置之。”命捉笔于旁，而自与妪指顾语笑，或扇茶铛、简图书，或代调丹碧诸色，纵其想会。久之，复命写图。图成，极妖艳之致，笑曰：“可矣！”师去，即取图供榻前，爇名香，设梨酒奠之，曰：“小青！小青！此中岂有汝缘分耶？”抚几而泣，泪雨潸潸下，一恸而绝。时万历壬子岁也。年才十八耳。哀哉！人美如玉，命薄于云，琼蕊优昙，人间一现，欲求如杜丽娘牡丹亭畔重生，安可得哉！

    Explanation

    47

    One day, she spoke to an elderly woman and said, "Please convey a message to the wronged soul, and find a skilled painter to come." When the painter arrived, she ordered him to paint her portrait. After the painting was completed, she took a mirror and looked at it intently, saying, "The likeness is captured, but the essence is not fully conveyed. Let it rest for now." She then changed the painting, saying, "The essence is there, but the grace and fluidity are lacking. If you see me with my eyes fixed and hands rigid, it's because of excessive restraint. Let it rest for now." She then instructed the painter to hold his brush beside her and gestured and laughed with the elderly woman, sometimes fanning a tea kettle, sometimes arranging books and scrolls, or sometimes mixing various colors. She allowed her thoughts to wander freely. After some time, she ordered the painter to create another portrait. When the new painting was finished, it was extremely beautiful and alluring, and she smiled, saying, "It's perfect!" The painter left, and she immediately took the painting and placed it before her bed, burning incense, setting up a libation of pears and wine, and said, "Xiao Qing! Xiao Qing! Is there any fate for you here?" She wept, tears streaming down her face, and sobbed once before passing away. It was during the Renzi year of the Wanli era. She was only eighteen years old. Alas! Beautiful as jade, her life was as fragile as a cloud. Like the rare and beautiful flower that appears only once in a lifetime, she was a fleeting presence in the world. To seek a rebirth like Du Liniang at the Peony Pavilion, how could it be possible!

    48

    日向暮，生始踉跄来，披帷，见容光藻逸，衣袂鲜好，如生前无病时，忽长号顿足，呕血升余。徐简得诗一卷，遗像一幅，又一缄寄某夫人，启视之，叙致惋痛，后书一绝句。生痛呼曰：“吾负汝！吾负汝！”妇闻恚甚，趋索图。乃匿第三图，伪以第一图进，立焚之。又索诗，诗至，亦焚之。广陵散从兹绝矣，悲夫！楚焰诚烈，何不以纪信诳之？则罪不在妇，又在生耳！及再简草稿，业散失尽。而姬临卒时，取花钿数事赠妪之小女，衬以二纸，正其诗稿。得九绝句、一古诗、一词，并所寄某夫人者，共十三篇。古诗云：“雪意阁云云不流，旧云正压新云头。米颠颠笔落窗外，松岚秀处当我楼。垂帘只愁好景少，卷帘又怕风缭绕。帘卷帘垂底事难，不情不绪谁能晓？炉烟渐瘦剪声小，又是孤鸿唳悄悄。”绝句云：“稽首慈云大士前，莫生西土莫生天。愿为一滴杨枝水，洒作人间并蒂莲。

    Explanation

    48

    As the day drew to a close, the man staggered in, drew back the curtain, and saw his radiant and elegant appearance, his clothes fresh and fine, as if he were healthy as before. Suddenly, he let out a loud wail, stamped his feet, and vomited blood, more than a cup. Slowly, he picked up a scroll of poetry, a posthumous portrait, and another sealed letter sent to a certain lady. He opened and read it, finding it filled with deep regret and sorrow, followed by a quatrain. The man cried out in pain, "I have failed you! I have failed you!" The woman, upon hearing this, became very angry and rushed to demand the picture. He then hid the third picture, pretended to offer the first one, and immediately burned it. She demanded the poetry again, and when it arrived, he burned it too. The famous "Guangling San" was lost from this point on, alas! The flames of Chu were indeed fierce; why not use Ji Xin to deceive her? Then the blame would not be on the wife, but on the husband! When he tried to find the drafts again, they were already scattered and lost. Yet, as the woman was on her deathbed, she took several pieces of jewelry and gave them to the old woman's little daughter, placing two sheets of paper underneath, making it the correct poetry draft. He found nine quatrains, one ancient poem, one ci, and the one sent to a certain lady, totaling thirteen pieces. The ancient poem reads: "The intention of snow is blocked by clouds, not flowing; old clouds are pressing down on new clouds. The eccentric calligraphy of Mi Fu falls outside the window; the beautiful mist of pines is in front of my tower. Hanging the curtain, only worried about the good scenery being scarce; rolling up the curtain, afraid of the wind swirling around. Whether the curtain is rolled up or down, the matter is difficult; no emotion, no mood, who can understand? The smoke from the stove gradually thins, the sound of the scissors is small; again, the lonely wild goose cries quietly." The quatrain reads: "I bow before the Bodhisattva of Mercy, may I not be born in the West or in the heavens. I wish to be a single drop of water from the willow branch, sprinkled to become a pair of lotuses on earth."

    49

    /春衫血泪点轻纱，吹入林逋处士家。岭上梅花三百树，一时应变杜鹃花。/新妆竟与画图争，知在昭阳第几名。瘦影自临秋水照，卿须怜我我怜卿。/西陵芳草骑辚辚，内使传来唤踏春。杯酒自浇苏小墓，可知妾是意中人？/冷雨幽窗不可听，挑灯闲看《牡丹亭》。人间亦有痴于我，岂独伤心是小青。/何处双禽集画栏，朱朱翠翠似青鸾。如今几个怜文采，也向秋风斗羽翰。/脉脉溶溶滟滟波，芙蓉睡醒欲如何。妾映镜中花映水，不知秋思落谁多。/盈盈金谷女班头，一曲骊珠众伎收。直得楼前身一死，季伦原是解风流。/乡心不畏两峰高，昨夜慈亲天梦遥。见说浙江潮有信，浙潮争似广陵潮？”其《天仙子》词云：“文姬远嫁昭君塞，小青又续风流债。也亏一阵黑罡风，火轮下，抽身快，单单别别清凉界。原不是鸯鸳一派，休算作相思一概。自思自解自商量，心可在？魂可在？著衫又捻裙双带。

    Explanation

    49

    A woman's crimson robe is stained with tears, her light gauze shimmering with sorrow, carried by the breeze to the hermitage of Lin Bu. On the mountain, three hundred plum trees bloom, yet they transform into azaleas in an instant, symbolizing a shift in her emotions. Her new makeup rivals the beauty of a painting, and she wonders where she ranks among the beauties of the imperial harem. Her slender silhouette is reflected in the autumn waters, and she whispers, "You must love me, and I will love you." Through the West Slope, lush grass stretches as far as the eye can see, and an imperial messenger summons her for a spring outing. She pours wine at the tomb of Su Xiaoxiao, asking if she is known as the beloved one. The cold rain taps against the dim window, and she picks up her lamp to read "The Peony Pavilion," reflecting on the devotion of others, not just the sorrow of Xiao Qing. Where are the two birds perched on the painted railing? Their colorful feathers resemble the mythical green phoenix. How many appreciate her literary talent? They compete with each other in the autumn wind. The gentle, flowing, and shimmering waves seem to ask, "What will happen now?" Her reflection in the mirror mirrors the flowers in the water, and she wonders who has more autumnal thoughts. Yingying, the leader of the women at the Jin Garden, sings a song that is highly valued by many. She would rather die than be separated from the man she loves, for Shi Chong understands the beauty of romance. Her homesickness is not deterred by the height of two mountains, and she dreams of her mother the night before. The tides of the Yangtze River are reliable, but the tides of the Grand Canal are even more impressive. Her Tianxianzi poem reads: "Wenji was married far away to the land of Chaojun, and Xiao Qing continued the legacy of romance. It was fortunate that a black wind blew, allowing her to escape quickly, leaving behind the cool realm of the heavens. It is not a line of swans, nor should it be counted as a general expression of longing. She thinks and resolves things on her own, wondering if her heart and soul are still present. She dresses herself and adjusts her skirt, tying the double ribbons.

    50

    ”与某夫人书曰：“元元叩首沥血致启夫人台座下：关头祖帐，迥隔人天；官舍良辰，当非寂度。驰情感往，瞻睇慈云，分燠嘘寒，如依膝下。糜身百体，未足云酬。娣娣姨姨无恙？犹忆南楼元夜，着灯谐谑，姨指画屏中一凭栏女曰：『是妖娆儿，倚风独盼，恍惚有思，当是阿青？』妾亦笑指一姬曰：『此执拂狡鬟，偷近郎侧，将无似娣？』于时角采寻欢，缠绵彻曙，宁复知风流云散，遂有今日乎？往者仙槎北渡，断梗南楼，狺语哮声，日焉三至。渐乃微词含吐，亦如尊旨云云。窃揆鄙衷，未见其可。夫屠肆菩心，饿狸悲鼠，此直供其换马，不即辱以当垆。去则弱絮风中，住则幽兰霜里。兰因絮果，现业谁深？若使祝发空门，洗妆浣虑，而艳思绮语，触绪纷来。正恐莲性虽胎，荷丝难杀，又未易言此也！乃至远笛哀秋，孤灯听雨，雨残灯歇，谡谡松声。罗衣压肌，镜无千影，晨泪镜潮，夕泪镜汐。今兹鸡骨，殆复难支。痰灼肺然，见粒而呕。

    Explanation

    50

    To the Honorable Madam,

    The humble writer, prostrating himself and offering blood as a token of sincerity, respectfully addresses the Madam at her seat:

    A critical moment, a threshold, a vast separation between the human and divine realms; an official residence, an auspicious occasion, would not be spent in solitude. Deeply feeling the past, gazing upon the benevolent presence, sharing warmth and coolness, as if close to one's knees. Exhausting one's body and soul, not enough to repay. Are the younger sisters and aunts well? I still recall the Lantern Festival at the southern pavilion, playing under the light of lanterns. The aunt pointed to a girl leaning on the wind and looking out alone in the painted screen, saying, "This charming girl, leaning on the wind and looking out alone, seems lost in thought—could it be Aluqing?" I also laughed and pointed to a young woman holding a fan, saying, "This cunning and mischievous girl, sneaking up beside the man, is she not like you?" At that time, competing in beauty and charm, seeking pleasure, deeply entangled in affection until dawn, how could this be today? In the past, a magical boat crossed northward, a broken stem at the southern pavilion, barking and roaring sounds came three times a day. Gradually, delicate hints and suggestions, as if following Your instructions. I privately ponder my own heart, finding it not right. In a butcher's shop, a compassionate heart, a hungry cat pities a mouse—this is merely to provide horses for exchange, not to be humiliated by serving at a wine shop. To leave is to be like weak fluff in the wind, to stay is to be like a secluded orchid in the frost. The cause is like an orchid, the effect is like fluff, who has deeper karma? If I were to cut off my hair and enter the Buddhist path, wash off makeup and cleanse my thoughts, and yet passionate thoughts and elaborate speech come in a flurry when touched upon. I fear that even in the womb, lotus threads are hard to eliminate, and it is not easy to speak of this! Even now, a distant flute evokes autumn's melancholy, a lone lamp listens to the rain, the rain ceases, the lamp goes out, the sound of rustling pines. A silk robe presses against the skin, the mirror shows no thousand reflections, morning tears are like the mirror's tide, evening tears are like the mirror's tide. Now, these chicken bones, hardly can they be supported. Phlegm burns the lungs, vomiting upon seeing grains.

    51

    错情易意，悦憎不驯。老母娣弟，天涯间绝。嗟乎！未知生乐，焉知死悲？憾促欢淹，无乃非达？妾少受天颖，机警灵速；丰兹啬彼，理讵能双？然而神爽有期，故未应寂寂也。至其沦忽，亦非自今。结褵以来，有宵靡旦，夜台滋味，谅不殊斯！何必紫玉成烟，白花飞蝶，乃谓之死哉？或轩车南返，驻节维扬，老母惠存，如妾之受，阿秦可念，幸终垂悯。畴昔珍赠，悉令见殉；宝钿绣衣，福星所赐，可以超轮消劫耳。

    Explanation

    51

    Emotions change and intentions shift, joy and hatred are uncontrollable. My mother and siblings are separated by the ends of the earth. Alas! If one does not know the joy of life, how can one know the sorrow of death? Regretting the shortness of joy, is this not a failure to understand? I was born with natural intelligence and quick wit; if one thing is abundant, another must be scarce; how can both be equally present? However, there is a time for the spirit to revive, so it should not be silent. Even when it fades suddenly, it is not something new. Since our marriage, there has been no day without night, the taste of the netherworld is not different from this! Why must death be as dramatic as purple jade turning to smoke or white flowers flying like butterflies? Perhaps returning south by carriage, stopping in Yangzhou, may my mother be well, just as I have been, may Qin be remembered, and may mercy be shown to her in the end. All the precious gifts from the past are now offered as sacrifices; the precious jewels and embroidered clothes, bestowed by the lucky star, can transcend the cycle of rebirth and eliminate calamities.

    52

    然小六娘竟先期相俟，不忧无伴。附呈一绝，亦是鸟语鸣哀。其诗集小像，托陈媪好藏，觅便驰寄。身不自保，何有于零膏冷翠乎？他时放船堤下，探梅山中，开我西阁门，坐我绿阴床，髣生平于响像，见空帏之寂飚。是耶非耶？其人斯在！嗟乎夫人！明冥异路，永从此辞！玉腕朱颜，行就尘土，兴思及此，恸也何如！元元叩首叩首上。”后附绝句云：“百结回肠写泪痕，重来唯有旧朱门。夕阳一片桃花影，知是亭亭倩女魂。”生之戚某集而刻之，名曰“焚余”。
    　　[张山来曰：红颜薄命，千古伤心。读至送鸩、焚诗处，恨不粉妒妇之骨以饲狗也！
    　　又曰：小青事，或谓原无其人，合“小青”二字，乃“情”字耳。及读吴口口《紫云歌》，其小序云：“冯紫云，为维扬小青女弟，归会稽马髦伯”。则又似实有其人矣。即此传亦不知谁氏手笔，吾友殷日戒仿佛忆为支小白作，未知是否，姑阏疑焉。] 义猴传　盐城宋曹射陵会秋堂文集

    Explanation

    52

    Yet, Little Six Sister had already waited in advance, without worry of being alone. I append a short poem, which is like a bird's mournful cry. The small portrait in her poetry collection, I entrusted Aunt Chen to keep well, and found a convenient opportunity to send quickly. If one cannot even protect oneself, what use is there for the remnants of beauty? In the future, let the boat drift by the embankment, explore the plum blossoms in the mountains, open the west pavilion door, sit on the bed shaded by green trees, glimpse one's life in the echoes, and see the empty curtain with a silent, cold breeze. Is it or is it not? The person is here! Alas, Lady! Different paths in life and death, forever parting! The beauty of jade-like wrists and red face, turning to dust, thinking of this, how great is the sorrow! Yuan Yuan bows deeply and deeply.

    After the poem, a quatrain follows: "A hundred knots of the heart write tears, returning only to the old vermilion gate. A sunset casts the shadow of a single peach blossom, knowing it to be the soul of the graceful lady." A relative of the deceased collected and engraved this, naming it "The Ashes Remain."

    [Ouyang Shanlai said: "A beautiful woman with a short life, a sorrow that has moved people for thousands of years. Reading to the point of sending the poison and burning the poem, one regrets not grinding the bones of the jealous woman to feed the dogs!

    Again, he said: "The story of Xiaoqing, some say there was no such person, combining the two characters 'Xiaoqing' to make the character 'qing' (love). Upon reading Wu Moumou's "Purple Cloud Song," its preface says: 'Feng Ziyun, the younger sister of Xiaoqing of Weiyang, married Ma Maoboshi of Kuaiji.' Then it seems there was indeed such a person. Even this account does not know who wrote it, my friend Yin Rijie vaguely remembers it being written by Zhi Xiaobai, but it is unknown if this is correct, so I will set aside the doubt for now." The Story of the Virtuous Monkey, by Song Cao of Yancheng, from the Collection of Autumn Hall at She Ling.]

    53

    建南杨子石袍告予曰：吴越间，有鬈髯丐子，编茅为舍，居于南坡。尝畜一猴，教以盘铃傀儡，演于市以济朝夕。每得食，与猴共，虽严寒暑雨，亦与猴俱。相依为命，若父子然。
    　　如是者十余年，丐子老且病，不能引猴入市。猴每日长跪道旁，乞食养之，久而不变。及丐子死，猴乃悲痛旋绕，如人子躃踊状。哀毕，复长跪道旁，凄声俯首，引掌乞钱。不终日，得钱数贯，悉以绳钱入市中，至棺肆不去。匠果与棺，仍不去，伺担者辄牵其衣裾。担者为舁棺至南坡，殓丐子埋之。猴复于道旁乞食以祭。祭毕，遍拾野之枯薪，廪于墓侧，取向时傀儡置其上焚之，乃长啼数声，自赴烈焰中死。行道之人，莫不惊叹而感其义，爰作义猴冢。

    Explanation

    53

    Jian Nan Yang Zi Shipo reported to me: In the region between Wu and Yue, there was a beggar with curly whiskers who wove reeds into a hut and lived on the south slope. He once kept a monkey and taught it to perform with a puppet show using a disk and bells, performing in the market to make a living day by day. Whenever he got food, he shared it with the monkey, and even in severe cold, heat, or rain, he stayed with the monkey. They relied on each other for survival, like father and son.

    For over a decade, the beggar grew old and ill, unable to lead the monkey into the market. The monkey would kneel by the roadside every day, begging for food to feed the beggar, and this continued without change. When the beggar died, the monkey became sorrowful and circled around, like a son in despair. After the mourning was completed, the monkey again knelt by the roadside, bowed its head in grief, and extended its hand to beg for money. Within a day, it collected several strings of coins, tied them up, and brought them to the market. It did not leave until it reached a coffin shop, where the carpenter eventually gave it a coffin. The monkey still did not leave, waiting for the man carrying the coffin to pull its sleeve. The man then carried the coffin to the south slope, where he buried the beggar. The monkey again begged for food by the roadside to offer as a sacrifice. After the sacrifice, it gathered dry twigs from the wild, piled them beside the grave, and placed the puppet it had used earlier on top, burning it. It then let out a few long cries and threw itself into the flames, dying.

    People passing by were all amazed and moved by its loyalty, and they built a tomb for the loyal monkey.

    54

    [张山来曰：有功世道之文，如读《徐阿寄传》。]
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷二 柳敬亭传　太仓吴伟业梅村梅村文集
    　　柳敬亭者，扬之泰州人，盖曹姓。年十五，犷猂无赖，名已在捕中。走之盱眙，困甚，挟稗官一册，非所习也，耳剽久，妄以其意抵掌盱眙市，则已倾其市人。好博，所得亦缘手尽。有老人，日为醵百钱，从寄食。久之，过江，休大柳下，生攀条泫然。已抚其树，顾同行数十人曰：“嘻！吾今氏柳矣！”闻者以生多端，或大笑以去。
    　　后二十年，金陵有善谈论柳生，衣冠怀之，辐辏门，车常接毂，所到坐中皆惊。有识之者，曰：“此固向年过江时休树下者也！”柳生之技，其先后江湖间者，广陵张樵、陈思，姑苏吴逸，与柳生四人者，各名其家，柳生独以能著。或问生何师，生曰：“吾无师也。吾之师乃儒者云间莫君后光。”莫君言之曰：“夫演义虽小技，其以辨性情，考方俗，形容万类，不与儒者异道。

    Explanation

    54

    Final Translation

    Zhang Shanlai said: "Writing that has merit for the world, such as reading The Biography of Xu Ajì."

    Yu Chū Xīn Zhì Volume 2, The Biography of Liu Jingting by Wú Wěiyè of Taicang, from the Méicūn Wénjí collection.

    Liu Jingting was from Taizhou in Yangzhou, originally surnamed Cáo. At fifteen, he was wild and unruly, already wanted by the authorities. He fled to Xuyi, where he was in dire straits, carrying a volume of official records, not something he had studied, but having heard it many times, he boldly used his own interpretation to impress the people in Xuyi market, and they were all captivated by him. He loved gambling, and whatever he won was quickly spent. There was an old man who gathered a hundred coins every day to support him. After a long time, he crossed the river and rested under a large willow tree, where he climbed the branches and wept. Then he touched the tree and looked at the dozens of people traveling with him and said, "Ah! Now I am of the surname Liu!" Those who heard this thought he was being whimsical, and some laughed at him and left.

    Twenty years later, in Nanjing, there was a skilled storyteller named Liu who attracted scholars and officials, who flocked to his door, their carriages often touching wheels, and wherever he went, everyone was amazed. Those who recognized him said, "This is indeed the man who rested under a tree when he crossed the river years ago!" Among the storytellers of the Jianghu (the world of performers and wanderers), there were four famous ones: Zhang Qiáo and Chén Sī of Guangling, Wú Yì of Gusu, and Liu Jingting. Each had their own reputation, but Liu Jingting alone was renowned for his skill. Someone asked him who his teacher was, and he replied, "I have no teacher. My teacher is the Confucian scholar Mò Hòuguāng of Yúnjiān." Mò Hòuguāng said, "Narrative fiction may be a minor art, but it discerns character and temperament, studies local customs, and describes myriad things, not differing from the path of the Confucian scholars."

    55

    故取之欲其肆，中之欲其微，促而赴之欲其迅，舒而绎之欲其安，进而止之欲其留，整而归之欲其洁。非天下至精者，其孰与于斯矣？”柳生乃退就舍，养气定词，审音辨物，以为揣摩。期月而后请莫君。莫君曰：“子之说未也。闻子说者，欢咍嗢噱，是得子之易也。”又期月，曰：“子之说几矣。闻子说者，危坐变色，毛发尽悚，舌桥然不能下。”又期月，莫君望见惊起曰：“子得之矣！目之所视、手之所倚，足之所跂，言未发而哀乐具乎其前，此说之全矣！”于是听者傥然若有见焉；其竟也，恤然若有亡焉。莫君曰：“虽以行天下莫能难也！”

    Explanation

    55

    In all performances, the reception should be expansive and free, the core subtle and refined, the movement swift when quickening, and the unfolding calm and stable when expanding. Advancing and stopping should leave a lingering impression, and concluding neatly should be pure and clean. Who among all the world's most skilled could compare to this?" The student, Liu, then retired to his room to cultivate his breath, stabilize his words, attend to tones, and distinguish objects, refining his performance. After a month, he asked Morun. Morun said, "Your performance is not yet complete. Those who hear you laugh and joke, indicating that it is easy to grasp." After another month, he said, "Your performance is nearly there. Those who hear you sit upright and change expression, hair standing on end, and tongue unable to move." After another month, Morun, upon seeing him, was startled and said, "You have it now! What the eyes see, what the hands touch, and what the feet feel, even before the words are spoken, joy and sorrow are already present. This is the complete performance!" Thus, the listeners seemed to see something; when it ended, they felt as if something were lost. Morun said, "Even if it were to travel the world, nothing could challenge it!"

    56

    已而柳生辞去，之扬州，之杭，之吴。吴最久。之金陵，所至与其豪长者相结，人人昵就生。其处己也，虽甚卑贱，必折节下之；即通显，敖弄无所诎。与人谈，初不甚谐谑，徐举一往事相酬答，淡辞雅对，一座倾靡。诸公以此重之，亦不尽以其技强也。
    　　当是时，士大夫避寇南下，侨金陵者万家。大司马吴桥范公，以本兵开府，名好士；相国何文瑞，阖门避造请。两家引生为上客。客有谓生者曰：“方海内无事，生所谈，皆豪猾大侠、草泽亡命。吾等闻之，笑谓必无是，乃公故善诞耳；孰图今日不幸竟亲见之乎！”生闻其语慨然。属与吴人张燕筑、沈公宪俱。张、沈以歌，生以谈。三人者，酒酣，悲吟击节，意凄怆伤怀。凡北人流离在南者，闻之无不流涕。

    Explanation

    56

    After bidding farewell and departing, Liu Sheng traveled to Yangzhou, then to Hangzhou, and finally to Wu. He stayed in Wu for the longest time. He also visited Jinling, where he formed alliances with local influential figures, and everyone showed affection and approached him. In dealing with others, he would humble himself and treat even the most humble with respect; if they were prominent and powerful, he would show off without being humiliated. When conversing with others, he did not initially engage in much jesting, but gradually brought up past events to respond to them, with simple and elegant responses that completely captivated the entire audience. The dignitaries respected him for this, though not solely because of his skills.

    At that time, scholars and officials fled south to avoid bandits, and ten thousand families temporarily resided in Jinling. Grand Marshal Fan of Wuqiao, famous for loving scholars, established a command post by means of the military. Chancellor He Wenrui, however, refused visitors and avoided receiving them. Both invited Liu Sheng as a distinguished guest. A guest said to him, "When the country was at peace, the stories you told were all about fierce and cunning heroes, outlaws, and fugitives. We heard them and laughed, saying it was impossible, thinking you were just good at telling tall tales; who would have thought that today, unfortunately, we actually see them!" Liu Sheng heard this and sighed deeply. He was associated with people from Wu named Zhang Yanzhu and Shen Gongxian. Zhang and Shen were singers, while Liu Sheng was a conversationalist. The three of them, when drunk, sang mournfully, clapped in rhythm, and had a melancholic and sorrowful mood. All displaced people from the north who were in the south shed tears when they heard it.

    57

    未几而有左兵之事。左兵者，宁南伯良玉军。噪而南，寻奉诏守楚，驻皖城待发。守皖者，杜将军弘域，于生为故人。宁南尝奏酒，思得一异客，杜既已泄之矣。会两人用军事不相中，念非生莫可解者，乃檄生至。进之，左以为此天下辩士，欲以观其能，帐下用长刀遮客，引就席，坐客咸震慑失次。生拜讫，索酒，诙啁谐笑，旁若无人者。左大惊，自以为得生晚也。居数日，左沉吟不乐，熟视生曰：“生揣我何念？”生曰：“得毋以亡卒入皖而杜将军不法治之乎？”左曰：“然。”生曰：“此非有君侯令，杜将军不敢以专也。生请衔命矣。”驰一骑入杜将军军中，斩数人，乃定。
    　　左幕府多儒生，所为文檄，不甚中窾会。生故不知书，口画便宜辄合。左起卒伍，少孤贫，与母相失，请貤封，不能得其姓，泪承睫不止。生曰：“君侯不闻天子赐姓事乎？此吾说书中故实也。”大喜，立具奏。左武人，即以为知古今、识大体矣。

    Explanation

    57

    Not long after, there was the matter of the Left Army. The Left Army, the army of Duke Nanning Liangyu, raised a commotion and moved south. Soon after, they received an imperial order to guard Chu and were stationed in Wancheng awaiting deployment. The one guarding Wancheng was General Du Hongyu, a former acquaintance of the author. Duke Nanning once proposed a toast, hoping to find a remarkable guest, but General Du had already revealed it. Coincidentally, the two did not see eye to eye on military matters. Thinking that no one but the author could resolve this, they issued an order for the author to come. When introduced, Duke Nanning thought this person was a wise man of the world and wanted to test his abilities. Under his tent, they used long swords to escort the guest and led him to his seat. All the guests were terrified and lost their composure. The author bowed, asked for wine, and laughed and joked as if no one else were present. Duke Nanning was greatly surprised and thought he had gotten the author too late. After several days, Duke Nanning was silent and unhappy. He looked at the author carefully and said, "What are you thinking about?" The author replied, "Is it not because the fugitive soldiers entered Wancheng and General Du did not punish them?" Duke Nanning said, "Yes." The author said, "This is not because of your lord's order; General Du would not dare to act alone. I will carry out the order." Riding a horse, he went into General Du's camp, executed several people, and thus settled the matter.

    Duke Nanning's staff was full of Confucian scholars, but the documents they wrote did not hit the mark. The author, who was not well-versed in books, could still make practical suggestions that were always appropriate. Duke Nanning rose from the ranks, was orphaned and poor as a child, and lost his mother. He requested posthumous honors for his mother but could not get her surname, and tears kept welling up in his eyes. The author said, "Have you not heard of the emperor granting a surname? This is an old story from my books." Duke Nanning was greatly pleased and immediately prepared a memorial. Being a military man, he immediately thought the author knew ancient and modern times and understood the big picture.

    58

    阮司马怀宁，生旧识也，与左郄而新用事。生还南中，请左曰：“见阮云何？”左无文书，即令口报阮，以捐弃故嫌，图国事于司马也。生归，对如宁南旨，且约结还报。及闻坂矶筑城，则顿足曰：“此示西备，疑必起矣！”后果如其虑焉。
    　　左丧过龙江关，生祠哭已，有迎且拜、拜不肯起者，则其爱将陈秀也。秀尝有急，生活之。具为予言救秀状。始左病恚怒，而秀所犯重，且必死。生莫得榰梧，乃设之以事曰：“今日饮酒不乐，君侯有奇物玩好，请一观可乎？”左曰：“甚善。”出所画己像二，其一“关陇破贼图”也，揽镜自照，叹曰：“良玉，天下健儿也，而今衰！”指其次曰：“吾破贼后，将入山，此图所以志也。”见衲而杖者数童子，从其负瓢笠，且近，则秀也。生佯不省而徐睨为谁，左语之，且告其罪。生曰：“若负恩当死，顾君侯以亲信，即入山且令相从，而杀之，即此图为不全矣！”左颔之。其善用权谲，为人排患解纷率类此。

    Explanation

    58

    General Yuan Huaining, a former acquaintance of the narrator, had a conflict with Zuo but recently came to power. The narrator returned to the south and asked Zuo, "What should I do when I meet Yuan?" Zuo, having no written documents, immediately ordered someone to verbally inform Yuan to set aside old grievances and plan state affairs with the general. The narrator returned and responded as instructed by Yuan, and also agreed to report back. Upon hearing about the construction of a city at Banji, he stamped his foot in frustration and said, "This shows preparation for the west, and suspicion will inevitably arise!" Indeed, his fears came true.

    After Zuo's death, his funeral procession passed through Longjiang Pass. The narrator cried at a shrine built in honor of Zuo. There was someone who greeted and bowed, but did not rise after the bow; this was his beloved general Chen Xiu. Chen Xiu had once been in a dire situation, and the narrator saved him. He detailed the story of saving Chen Xiu. Initially, Zuo was sick and angry, and Chen Xiu had committed a serious offense and was likely to be executed. The narrator could not find a way to defend or help. He then set up a pretext and said, "Today, drinking alcohol is not enjoyable. You have some rare and precious items. May I ask to see one of them?" Zuo replied, "Very good." He took out two paintings of himself: one was titled "The Map of Defeating the Rebels in Guanlong," and he looked into the mirror and reflected on himself, sighing, "Liangyu, a hero of the world, but now he is declining!" He pointed to the other painting and said, "After I defeat the rebels, I will retreat to the mountains. This picture is a record of my intentions." He saw several children wearing monk-like robes and holding staffs, following them and carrying gourds and straw hats. As they got closer, it was Chen Xiu. The narrator pretended not to recognize and slowly looked to see who it was. Zuo told him and also informed him of his crime. The narrator said, "If you betray me, you should die. But since you are a trusted person, even if I retreat to the mountains, I will still have you follow me. If you kill me, then this picture will be incomplete!" Zuo nodded in agreement. His skill in using cunning and deceit often helps others resolve problems and disputes in this manner.

    59

    初，生从武昌归，以客将新道军所来，朝贵皆倾动；顾自安旧节，起居故人无所改。逮江上之变，生所携及留军中者，亡散累千金，再贫困而意气自如。或问之，曰：“吾在盱眙市上时，夜寒藉束藁卧，屝履踵决，行雨雪中，窃不自料以至于此。今虽复落，尚足为生，且有吾技在，宁渠忧贫乎？”乃复来吴中，每被酒，常为人说故宁南时事，则欷歔洒泣。既在军中久，其所谈益习，而无聊不平之气无所用，益发之于书，故晚节尤进云o

    Explanation

    59

    At first, he returned from Wu Chang as a guest general, having traveled the new route to the army, and all the court nobles were greatly impressed. Yet, he remained content with his old habits and continued his daily routine and interactions with old friends without any change. When the significant event on the river occurred, the items he carried and left behind in the army were worth thousands of gold pieces but were lost, leaving him poor again. Despite this, his spirit remained undiminished. Someone asked him about it, and he replied, "When I was in the market of Xuyi, I slept on a pile of straw in the cold night, with old, worn-out shoes, walking in the rain and snow. I did not expect myself to reach this point. Now, though I am poor again, I still have enough means to live, and I still have my skills. Should I worry about poverty?" He then returned to Wu, and whenever he was drunk, he often told people about the events of the old Southern period, sighing and shedding tears. After spending a long time in the army, what he talked about became more familiar, but his feelings of boredom and dissatisfaction had no outlet. He increasingly expressed these feelings in his writing, and therefore, his later work was particularly advanced.

    60

    旧史氏曰：予从金陵识柳生。同时有杨生季蘅，故医也，亦客于左，奏摄武昌守，拜为真。左因强柳生以官，笑弗就也。杨今去官，仍故业，在南中亦纵横士，与予善。
    　　[张山来曰：戊申之冬，予于金陵友人席间与柳生同饮。予初不识柳生，询之同侪，或曰：“此即《梅村集》中所谓柳某者是也。”滑稽善谈，风生四座，惜未聆其说稗官家言为恨。今读此传，可以想见其掀髯鼓掌时也。] 汪十四传　钱塘徐士俊野君雁楼集
    　　汪十四者，新安人也，不详其名字。性慷慨激烈，善骑射，有燕赵之风。时游西蜀，蜀中山川险阻，多相聚为盗。凡经商往来于兹者，辄被劫掠。闻汪十四名，咸罗拜马前，愿作“护身符”。汪许之，遂与数百人俱，拥骑而行。闻山上嚆矢声，汪即弯弓相向，与箭锋相触，空中堕折。以故绿林甚畏之，秋毫不敢犯，商贾尽得数倍利。而白梃之徒日益贫困，心忮之，而莫可谁何也。

    Explanation

    60

    Translation

    The old historian said: I met Liu Sheng in Jinling. At the same time, there was Yang Sheng Ji Heng, an old doctor, who also lived in the left, was appointed to oversee Wu Chang, and was officially appointed. Zuo forced Liu Sheng to take office, but he laughed and did not accept. Yang has now left his office and still engages in his former profession. In the south, he is also a wandering scholar and is friendly with me.

    [ Zhang Shanlai said: In the winter of Wu Shen year, I met Liu Sheng while drinking with friends in Jinling. I did not know Liu Sheng at first, so I asked my peers, and one of them said, "This is the Liu mentioned in Meicun Collection." Liu was witty and good at talking, causing the atmosphere to become lively. I regretted not hearing his stories about minor officials. Now reading this biography, I can imagine him raising his beard and clapping. ]

    The Biography of Wang Shisi by Xuntang Xu Shijun's Wild Man's Collection of Poems

    Wang Shisi was from Xin'an, and his name is not known. He was generous and passionate, skilled in horseback riding and archery, with the spirit of Yan and Zhao. He traveled to western Sichuan, where the mountains and rivers were dangerous, and many gathered to become bandits. Merchants who traveled through this region were often robbed. Upon hearing of Wang Shisi's name, they all prostrated themselves before him, wishing to be his "protective charm." Wang agreed, and so he traveled with several hundred people, leading them on horseback. When they heard the sound of arrows being shot from the mountains, Wang immediately drew his bow and faced them, and the arrows fell to the ground. Therefore, the bandits greatly feared him, and they dared not commit any acts of violence. Merchants all made several times their profit. However, the bandits with white sticks became increasingly poor and resented him, but they could do nothing about it.

    61

    无几时，汪慨然曰：“吾老矣！不思归计，徒挟一弓一矢之勇，跋履山川，向猿猱豺虎之地以博名高，非丈夫之所贵也！”因决计归。归则以田园自娱，绝不问户外事。而曩时往来川中者，尽被剽掠，山径不通。乃踉跄走新安，罗拜于门外曰：“愿乞壮士重过西川，使我辈弱者可强，贫者可富，俾啸聚之徒大不得志于我旅人也。壮士其许之乎？”是时汪十四雄心不死，遂许之曰“诺！”大笑出门，挟弓矢连骑而去。于是重山叠岭之间，复有汪之马迹焉。
    　　绿林闻之咸惊悸，谋所以胜汪者；告诸山川雷雨之神，当以汪十四之头陈列鼎俎。乃以骁骑数人，如商客装，杂于诸商之队而行。近贼巢，箭声飒沓来。汪正弯弓发矣，而后有一人，持利刃向弦际一挥，弦断矢落。汪忙迫无计，遂就擒。擒入山寨中，见贼党咸持金称贺，然犹意在往劫汪之护行者。暂置汪于空室，絷其手足，不得动。俟日晡，取汪十四头，陈之鼎俎，酬山川雷雨之神。

    Explanation

    61

    After a short while, Wang sighed and said, "I am old! I no longer think about returning home, but only possess the bravery of a bow and arrow, traversing mountains and rivers, heading to dangerous places to gain fame, which is not what a true man values!" Thus, he decided to return home. Upon his return, he enjoyed himself in his fields and gardens, never concerned with matters outside the home. However, those who had previously traveled through the region were all plundered, and the mountain paths were impassable. They hurriedly went to Xin'an and knelt in a circle outside the door, saying, "We humbly request the brave warrior to return to the Western Sichuan region, so that the weak may become strong, the poor may become rich, and the bandits may not succeed against our travelers. Will you agree, brave warrior?" At that time, Wang Shisi's ambition was not yet extinguished, so he agreed and said, "Yes!" Laughing heartily, he left the house, taking his bow and arrows, and rode away with his companions. Thus, once again, there were traces of Wang's horse in the mountains and valleys.

    The bandits, upon hearing this, were all filled with fear and began to plot ways to defeat Wang. They appealed to the gods of mountains, rivers, thunder, and rain, promising to display Wang Shisi's head on a sacrificial table. They then sent a few swift riders, dressed as merchants, mixed among the other merchants' entourage, and approached the bandits' lair. As Wang was drawing his bow to shoot, a man suddenly stepped forward, holding a sharp blade and slashing the bowstring, causing the arrow to fall. Wang, in a panic, had no plan and was thus captured. He was taken to the bandits' stronghold, where they all held up gold and congratulated each other, but their true intention was still to attack Wang's entourage. For the time being, they placed Wang in an empty room, binding his hands and feet so that he could not move. At sunset, they took Wang Shisi's head, displayed it on a sacrificial table, and offered it to the gods of mountains, rivers, thunder, and rain.

    62

    汪忽瞪目，见一美人向汪笑曰：“汝诚豪杰，何就缚至此？”汪且愤且怜曰：“毋多言！汝能救我，则救之，娘子军不足为也！”美人曰：“我意如斯。但恐救汝之后，汝则如饥鹰怒龙，夭矫天外，而我凄然一身，徒婉转娇啼，作帐下之鬼，为之奈何？”汪曰：“不然。救其一，失其一，亦无策甚矣。吾行百万军中，空空如下天状，况区区贼奴，何足当吾前锋哉！”因相对慷慨激烈。美人即以佩刀断其缚而出之。汪不遑起谢，见舍旁有刀剑弓矢，悉挟以行。左挈美人，右持器械，间行数百步，遇一骑甚骏，遂并坐其上。贼人闻之，疾驱而前。汪厉声曰：“来，来！吾射汝！”应弦而倒。连发数十矢，应弦倒者凡数十人。贼人终已无可奈何，纵之去。

    Explanation

    62

    Wang suddenly opened his eyes wide and saw a beautiful woman smiling at him. She said, "You are truly a hero, why are you bound here?" Wang, filled with both anger and pity, replied, "Don't say more! If you can save me, then save me, but the women's army is not worth it!" The woman said, "I mean it. But I fear that after saving you, you will be like a hungry eagle or an angry dragon, twisting and turning in the sky, while I will be alone, pitifully weeping and wailing, becoming a ghost under the tent. What can I do?" Wang said, "Not so. To save one thing, lose another, there is no better plan. I move through a million soldiers as if descending from the heavens, let alone these petty bandits, how can they stand in my way?" They spoke passionately and intensely to each other. The woman immediately cut his bindings with her dagger and freed him. Wang had no time to thank her and saw a sword, spear, bow, and arrows beside the hut, all of which he took with him. He held the woman in his left hand and weapons in his right, moving stealthily for hundreds of steps. He encountered a very swift horse and climbed onto it with her. The bandits heard about it and rushed forward quickly. Wang shouted, "Come, come! I will shoot you!" With a twang of the bowstring, one fell. He fired dozens of arrows in succession, and dozens of people fell with each twang. The bandits were finally at a loss and let him go.

    63

    汪从马上问美人姓名。美人泣曰：“吾宦女也。父为兰省给事中，现居京国。今年携眷属至京，被劫，妾之老母及诸婢子尽杀，独留妾一人，凌逼蹂践，不堪言状。妾之所以不死者，必欲一见严君，可以无恨；又私念世间或有大豪杰能拔入虎穴者，故踌躇至今。今遇明公，得一拜严君，妾乃知死所矣！”汪曰：“某之重生，皆卿所赐，京华虽辽远，当担簦杖策卫汝以行。”于是陆行从车，水行从舟，奔走数千里，同起居饮食者非一日，略无相狎之意，竟以女归其尊人，即从京国返新安终老焉。老且死，里人壮其生平奇节，立庙以祀，称为“汪十四相公庙”。有祷辄应，春秋歌舞以乐之，血食至今不衰。
    　　[张山来曰：吾乡有此异人，大足为新安生色。而文之夭矫奇恣，尤堪与汪十四相副也。] 武风子传　桐城方亨咸邵村邵村杂记

    Explanation

    63

    Wang asked the beautiful woman her name while on horseback. The woman wept and said, "I am a servant girl. My father is a clerk in the Lánshěng, and he currently resides in the capital. This year, our family came to the capital, but we were attacked. My old mother and all the maids were killed, leaving only me. I was abused and trampled upon, and the suffering cannot be described. The reason I did not die is that I must see Master Yan so I can have no regrets. I also privately think there might be great heroes who can rescue me from the tiger's den, so I have hesitated until now. Now I meet a wise and upright person, and I can pay respects to Master Yan once. I will know where to die!" Wang replied, "My rebirth is all thanks to you. Even though the capital is far away, I will carry a parasol and a staff to protect you on the journey." Thus, they traveled by land in carriages and by water in boats, covering thousands of miles. They shared meals and living quarters for many days, with no intention of being intimate. Finally, Wang returned the woman to her father, and then returned to Xin'an to live out his life. When he was old and near death, the villagers admired his extraordinary deeds and built a temple to worship him, calling it the "Temple of Master Wang the Fourteenth." Prayers are always answered, and dances and songs are performed in his honor. The temple remains active and thriving to this day.

    [O Shanlai said: My hometown has such a remarkable person, greatly enhancing the prestige of Xin'an. And the style of the text, which is both graceful and bold, is particularly fitting for Master Wang the Fourteenth.] Wu Fengzi Biography by Fang Hengxian of Shao Village, Tongcheng.

    64

    武风子者，滇南之武定州人也，名恬。先世以军功官于卫。恬以胄子，少学书，已弃弗学。性好闲，不谋荣利。嗜酒，日唯谋醉，箪瓢屡空，晏如也。凡游艺杂技，过目即知之。
    　　滇多产细竹，坚实可为箸。武生以火绘其上，作禽鱼花鸟、山水人物、城门楼阁，精夺鬼工。人奇之，每得其双筹，争购钱数百。于是武生之交戚贫者，因以为利。生顾未尝售也，颇自矜重，一箸成，辄把玩不释，保护如头目。或醉后痛哭，悉焚之，醒复悔，悔而复作。然靳不轻与人。好事者每瞷其谋醉时，置酒招之，造必尽欢。酒酣，以火与箸杂陈于前而不言。生攘臂起，顷刻完数十筹，挥手不顾也。或于酒中以箸相属，则怒，拂衣出，终身不与之见。或遇贫士及释道者流，告以困穷，辄忻然为之，虽累百不倦。于是滇之士夫或相馈遗，皆以武生箸为重。王公大人游于滇者，不得武生箸即不光。

    Explanation

    64

    Wu Fengzi, a native of Wuding Prefecture in the southern region of Dian (modern-day Yunnan), was named Tian. His ancestors had served as officials due to military merit. As a descendant of these officials, Tian briefly studied calligraphy but soon abandoned it. He enjoyed leisure and did not pursue fame or profit. He loved drinking, thinking only about getting drunk every day, with his food and drink often running out, but he remained calm and unperturbed. He was skilled in various arts and techniques, knowing them instantly upon seeing them.

    Yunnan produces fine bamboo, which is sturdy enough to be made into chopsticks. Wu Fengzi used fire to paint on the chopsticks, creating intricate designs of birds, fish, flowers, birds, landscapes, people, city gates, and towers, with skill so precise it rivals divine craftsmanship. People found his work extraordinary, and they competed to buy his pairs of chopsticks for hundreds of coins. Thus, Wu Fengzi's relatives and poor friends benefited from his work. However, he never actually sold his chopsticks, but he was very proud of them and often held them, unwilling to part with them, treating them as if they were his head and eyes. Sometimes he cried bitterly and burned all his chopsticks after getting drunk, but upon waking, he regretted it and started making more. He was very stingy and did not easily give his chopsticks to others. Those who appreciated art often tried to get him drunk by setting up feasts, and when he came, he always enjoyed himself fully. When he was drunk, he arranged fire and chopsticks in front of him without saying anything. He quickly started painting dozens of chopsticks in a short time, then waved them away without a second thought. If someone asked for a chopstick while he was drinking, he got angry, left in a huff, and never met them again. If he met poor scholars or monks and nuns who asked for help, he happily helped them, even if it meant making hundreds of chopsticks without tiring.

    In Yunnan, officials and scholars often exchanged gifts, and Wu Fengzi's chopsticks were highly valued. Even high-ranking officials who visited Yunnan considered it incomplete without Wu Fengzi's chopsticks.

    65

    生固落落儒生耳，未尝以“风子”名。丁亥之岁，流贼从蜀败奔，假号于滇，滇士民慴于威，披靡以从。生独匿深菁中不出。贼于民间见其箸，异之，遍召不得，因悬赏索之。或告曰：“曷出以图富贵？”生大笑曰：“我岂作奇技淫巧以悦贼者耶？”侦者闻于贼，系以来。至则白眼仰天，喑无一语。贼命作箸，列金帛于前，设醇醪于右以诱之，不应；陈刀锯以恐之，亦不应。贼怒，挥斩之。缚至市曹，而神色自如，终无一语。时贼帅有侍侧者曰：“腐鼠何足膏斧钺？曷纵之？徐徐当自逞其技也。”释之，而生自此病矣。披发佯狂，垢形秽语，日歌哭行市中，夜逐犬豕与处，人遂皆呼“武风子、武风子”云。

    Explanation

    65

    I am indeed just a simple Confucian scholar, and I have never been called "Wind Child." In the year Dinghai, the roving bandits from Shu fled in defeat and claimed a title in Yunnan. The people of Yunnan were frightened by their might and followed them in disarray. I alone hid deep in the jungle and did not come out. The bandits saw my chopsticks in the village and found them strange, calling out everywhere but could not find me. They then offered a reward to seek me. Someone told me, "Why not come out to seek wealth and honor?" I laughed and said, "Am I going to perform extraordinary skills and vulgar tricks to please the bandits?" The spies reported this to the bandits, who tied me up and brought me in. When they brought me in, I looked up at the sky with white eyes and remained silent. The bandits ordered me to make chopsticks, placing gold and silk in front of me and setting strong liquor to the right to tempt me, but I did not respond; they threatened me with knives and saws, but I still did not respond. The bandits became angry and slashed me. They tied me to the marketplace, but I remained calm and said nothing. At that time, one of the bandit leader's attendants said, "A rotten mouse is not worth using to grease the axe and halberd. Why not let him go? He will gradually show his skills." They released me, and I became ill from then on. I let my hair down and pretended to be mad, with a dirty appearance and vulgar speech. I sang and cried in the marketplace every day and followed dogs and pigs around at night. People then all called me "Wu Fengzi."

    66

    及王师定滇，风子病少瘥，亦稍稍为人作箸以谋醉，人重之逾常时。安定守某者，受贵人属，召为之，不应。守怒，挞之于庭，血流体溃，终不应。自此风子之踪迹无定矣，或琳宫梵舍，或市肆田家，往必数日留，留必作数十箸以谋醉。然出入无时，于是其箸可得而不可得矣。

    Explanation

    66

    When the imperial army pacified Yunnan, Fengzi's illness slightly improved, and he gradually began to make chopsticks for others to drink to their hearts' content. People valued him even more than usual. The governor of Anding, at the request of a noble, summoned Fengzi to perform a task, but he refused. The governor became angry and beat him in the courtyard, causing blood to flow and his body to be torn apart, yet he still refused. From then on, Fengzi's whereabouts became uncertain; sometimes he was in Buddhist temples or monasteries, sometimes in marketplaces or rural homes. When he stayed, he would remain for several days and make dozens of chopsticks to drink to their hearts' content. However, his comings and goings were unpredictable, and thus his chopsticks could be obtained but could not be obtained.

    67

    余尝见其箸作“凌烟阁功臣图”者，箸粗仅及绳，而旌旗铠杖、侍从卫列，无不毕具；至褒公、鄂公，英姿毛发，道子传神，莫或过之。其画细如丝，深绀色，入竹分余如镂。武定太守顾舆山为余言：其作箸时，削炭如笔数十，置烈火中，酒满壶于旁，伺炭末红若锥，左执箸，右执炭，肃肃有声，如蚕食叶，快若风雨，且饮且作。壶干即止，益之复作。饮不用杯杓，以口就壶，不择酒。期醉，醉则伏火而卧，或哭或歌，或说《论语》经书，多奇解。及醒而问之，则他呓语以对。或正作时，酒未尽，忽不知其所往，逾数十日或数月复来，复卒成之。其状貌如中人，年近六十余，拜揖跪起无异，唯与之语，则风子矣。舆山曾作《武异人歌》赠之，故时往还也。但所绘故事，多稗官杂剧，有规以不雅驯者，笑而不答，亦终不易。或曰：“非病风者也，狂人也。”或曰：“共有道者欤？不然，何富贵不淫，威武不屈耶？”余于是作《武风子传》。

    Explanation

    67

    I have the pleasure of presenting to you the final translation of the source text, polished and ready for your perusal:

    I once saw his brushwork on the "Immortal Heroes of the Lingyan Pavilion," where the brush was so thin it was barely thicker than a string, yet flags, armor, halberds, attendants, and guards were all depicted in full detail; even the Duke of Praise and the Duke of E were portrayed with their heroic postures and hair, capturing the spirit like the hand of Wu Daozi, none surpassing them. His brushwork was as fine as silk, with a deep purple-red color, penetrating the bamboo as if carved. Governor Gu Yushan of Wuding told me: When he painted, he would shave charcoal into dozens of brushes, place them in a fierce fire, and have a full wine jar beside him. He would wait for the charcoal to turn red like a cone, holding the brush in his left hand and the charcoal in his right, making a rustling sound, like a silkworm eating leaves, as swift as wind and rain, drinking and painting at the same time. When the wine jar was empty, he would stop and refill it before continuing. He drank without a cup, directly from the jar, and drank whatever wine was available. His aim was to get drunk, and when drunk, he would lie down by the fire, sometimes crying, sometimes singing, sometimes reciting the Analects and other classics, often with unusual interpretations. When asked upon waking, he would answer with rambling nonsense. Sometimes, while painting, the wine was not yet finished, and suddenly he would disappear without a trace, returning after dozens of days or months to finish the painting. His appearance was like an ordinary person, near sixty years old, bowing, kneeling, and rising without any difference, but when spoken to, he was a madman. Gu Yushan once composed the Song of the Strange Man of Wu to give to him, so they would visit each other. However, his paintings often depicted stories from unofficial histories and plays, and there were those who criticized it for being unrefined, but he would laugh without answering, nor did he ever change. Some said, "He is not a lunatic, but a madman," while others said, "Could he be a sage? Otherwise, how could he remain unmoved by wealth and power, unyielding to force and might?" I then wrote the Biography of the Madman of Wu.

    68

    [张山来曰：武生岂真风子耶？不过如昔人饮醇近妇，以寄其牢骚抑郁之态，宜其箸之不轻作也。
    　　邵村先生与先君同年，余幼时曾一聆謦欬。癸亥冬，瓜洲梁子存斋以此传录寄。未几，而何省斋年伯又以刻本邮示。益信奇文欣赏，自有同心也。] 记老神仙事　方亨咸邵村邵村杂记

    Explanation

    68

    Zhang Shanlai said: Is the Wusheng truly a fengzi? He is merely like a person of old who drank pure wine and was close to women, to vent his complaints and melancholy, which is why his work is not lightly written.
    Mr. Shaocun was in the same class as my late father, and I once heard his voice when I was young. In the winter of 1673, Mr. Liang Zicun from Gua Zhou sent this manuscript by post. Not long afterward, Mr. He Shengzai sent a printed copy by post. Thus confirming that appreciation of extraordinary writing is shared by like-minded people.

    69

    蜀中刘文季为余言，昔献贼中有所谓“老神仙”者，事甚怪，能生已死之人，续已断之肢与骨，贼众敬如神明焉。其初被掳时，将杀之，——贼掳人，不即杀，审其人，凡一技一艺者皆得免。——神仙比能以泥塑像获免，贼中遂以“塑匠”呼之。
    　　一日，塑匠涤大釜沃水，析屋为薪燎之。水沸，沸凡数，以一榜左右搅成膏。贼众骇，争相传。献贼闻，谓妖人，又将杀之。塑匠曰：“愿一言以死：王不欲成大事耶？何故杀异士？”献贼异而问之，曰：“臣有异术，能生人。此膏乃仙授，或刀斧，或榜掠，受重创者，臣能顷刻完好。”献贼即榜一人，试之，立验。献贼残忍，日杀人，劓刖人，至笞掠无算。笞凡数百，血肉糜溃，气息仅属者，付塑匠，以白水膏傅之，无不生，且立刻杖而行。军中争趋之，馈遗饮食无虚日，以是衣食囊橐渐充矣。

    Explanation

    69

    During the reign of Zhang Xianzhong, Liu Wenji told me that there was a man known as the "Old Immortal" among the rebels. His abilities were extraordinary: he could bring back the dead and reattach severed limbs and bones. The rebels revered him as a deity. When he was first captured, they intended to kill him—rebels would not immediately execute their captives but would assess them, sparing anyone with a special skill or talent. The "Old Immortal" managed to escape death by creating a clay statue, and the rebels thereafter referred to him as the "Clay Sculptor."

    One day, the Clay Sculptor cleaned a large cauldron, poured in water, and burned down a house to use its wood as fuel. The water boiled several times, and he stirred it with a paddle to form a paste. The rebels were alarmed and spread the news. Zhang Xianzhong heard of this and called him a sorcerer, intending to kill him. The Clay Sculptor said, "I beg to speak before I die: Do you not wish to achieve great deeds? Why kill a person of extraordinary abilities?" Zhang Xianzhong was intrigued and asked him, "I have a unique skill that can bring people back to life. This paste was taught to me by a deity. Whether by sword or axe, or by beating, for those who have suffered severe injuries, I can restore them to health in an instant." Zhang Xianzhong immediately had a man beaten, and the Clay Sculptor applied the white water paste to him, and the man was instantly healed. Zhang Xianzhong was known for his cruelty, killing and mutilating people daily, often beating them mercilessly. For those beaten hundreds of times, their flesh torn and their breath barely remaining, he would give them to the Clay Sculptor, who would apply the white water paste, and they would all be restored to health and able to walk immediately. The rebels vied to seek him out, offering him food and drink every day, and as a result, his clothing and food supplies gradually became full.

    70

    献贼有爱将某者，攻城，为飞炮所中，去其颏，奄奄一息矣。塑匠曰：“易与耳！”即生割一人颏，按之，傅以膏，一日而苏，饮噉如未割也。时孙可望在贼为监军，夜被酒，杀一嬖妾。旦行三十里，醒而悔之。道遇塑匠，笑问曰：“监军夜来未醉耶？何有不豫色然？”可望告以故。塑匠曰：“监军果念其人乎？吾当回马觅之。”可望曰：“唉！起营时，尸不知何在，想为犬豕啖矣，何从觅？”塑匠曰：“监军若令我觅，何物犬豕，敢啖贵人乎？”可望曰：“鼠子绐我！汝欲逃耶？我当遣介士押汝觅！”塑匠笑曰：“何处觅？觅何能得？”可望怒曰：“汝何戏我？”塑匠指道旁舁一毡橐曰：“何需觅，即此是也！”可望曰：“已朽之骨，何舁之？”塑匠笑谓：“监军曷启之？”可望下马解毡，则星眸宛转，厌厌如带雨梨花，帐中之魂已返矣。

    Explanation

    70

    Zhang Xianzhong had a beloved general who, while attacking a city, was struck by a flying cannonball, losing his chin and barely surviving. A sculptor said, "This is easy to handle!" He then cut off the chin of another person, placed it on the general, applied some ointment, and the next day the general was revived, eating and drinking as if nothing had happened. At that time, Sun Kewang was serving as a monitor general in Zhang Xianzhong's army. One night, after drinking, he killed his favored concubine. The next morning, after traveling thirty miles, he woke up and regretted his actions. On the road, he met the sculptor and asked with a smile, "Monitor General, weren't you drunk last night? Why do you look so gloomy?" Sun Kewang explained the reason for his regret. The sculptor said, "If you truly miss that person, I will return to find her for you." Sun Kewang replied, "Alas! When we set up camp, I don't know where her body is; I suspect it was eaten by dogs and pigs. How can I find her?" The sculptor said, "If you order me to find her, what kind of dogs and pigs would dare to eat a noble person?" Sun Kewang said, "You rat, are you deceiving me? Are you trying to escape? I will send an envoy to force you to find her!" The sculptor smiled and said, "Where should I look? How can I possibly find her?" Sun Kewang became angry and said, "Why are you playing tricks on me?" The sculptor pointed to a bundle carried by someone on the side of the road and said, "Why look? This is her!" Sun Kewang said, "It's already a pile of bones, how can it be carried?" The sculptor laughed and said, "Monitor General, why not open it?" Sun Kewang dismounted, opened the bundle, and saw the concubine with bright eyes, her appearance delicate and beautiful like a rain-soaked pear blossom. The soul from the tent had returned.

    71

    可望喜噪，一军皆惊。闻于献贼，献曰：“此神仙也，当封之。”口封恐众未知，时营大泽中，下令军中人备一几，以次日集广原。是时贼数十万，令以数十万几累之，择累之最高者谓“拜仙台”。于是衣塑匠以深衣，巾以纶巾，方履丝绦。塑匠身高六尺，广颡阔面，大有须，望之如世所绘社神者然。命之升台，台高且危，塑匠怯不欲登。献贼令军士各持弓矢，引满以向之，曰：“不登，即射！”塑匠不得已，及其半，惴慄惶惧，而万矢拟之如的，不敢止，勉登其上。献贼令三军释弓矢，罗拜其下，呼“老神仙”者三。于时声震天地。自此不复呼“塑匠”，而皆曰“老神仙”矣。

    Explanation

    71

    When General Ke Wang was greeted with loud cheers, the entire army was startled. The event was heard by the rebel leader Xian, who declared, "This is a divine being, and he should be honored." To ensure the people believed, Xian ordered that a ceremony be held. At the time, the camp was located in a large marsh. Xian commanded all soldiers to prepare a table and gather the next day at a broad plain. There were tens of thousands of soldiers, and Xian ordered the tables to be piled up in tens of thousands, choosing the highest pile and calling it the "Altar for Worshiping the Immortal." The mason was dressed in a deep robe, wore a silk cap, and had square shoes tied with silk ribbons. The mason was tall, with a broad face and a full beard, resembling a village god as depicted in the world. Xian ordered him to ascend the platform, which was high and precarious, causing the mason to be afraid and unwilling to climb. Xian then ordered the soldiers to each hold a bow and arrow, draw them back, and point them at the mason, saying, "If you do not climb, we will shoot!" The mason, having no choice, climbed halfway up, trembling with fear and surrounded by arrows aimed at him like targets, unable to stop and forced to continue climbing. Once at the top, Xian ordered the three armies to lower their bows and arrows, surround him, and kneel down, calling out "Old Immortal" three times. The sound was so loud that it shook the heavens and the earth. From then on, they no longer called him "mason," but instead referred to him as "Old Immortal."

    72

    老神仙亦自此不轻试其术。有渠贼某者，战败伤足，胫骨已折，所不断者，皮仅寸耳。求老神仙治，辞以不易。某哀号宛转，盛陈金帛以请。老神仙挥之曰：“此身外物，吾无需。虽然，吾不忍将军之创也。吾无子，将军能养我乎？”某指天而誓，愿终身父事之。老神仙从容解所佩囊，出小锯，锯断其足上下各寸许，取生人胫，度其分寸以接之，傅以药，不数日而愈。自此贼中凡求其药者，皆不敢侈馈遗，争投身为养子矣。

    Explanation

    72

    The Old Immortal also decided not to lightly use his skills from then on. There was a certain bandit leader who, after being defeated in battle, had a severely injured foot with the shinbone broken. The only thing holding it together was a strip of skin, just an inch wide. He asked the Old Immortal to treat him, but the Old Immortal refused, saying it was not easy. The bandit leader cried out in distress and offered a large amount of gold and silk as a request. The Old Immortal waved it away and said, "These are external things, I do not need them. Although, I cannot bear to see the general's wound. I have no children, can the general take me in as a father?" The bandit leader swore by the sky, willing to serve him as a father for life. The Old Immortal calmly opened the bag he was carrying, took out a small saw, and sawed off the foot, one inch above and below. He then took a piece of a living person's shinbone, measured it, and attached it, applying medicine. In a few days, the injury healed. From then on, all those in the bandit camp who sought his medicine were too afraid to offer excessive gifts, and they all competed to become his adopted sons.

    73

    献贼有幸婢曰“老脚”者，美而慧，善书画，脚不甚纤，因名。凡贼中移会侦发文字皆所掌，献贼嬖之。燕处有所思，老脚见其独坐，私往侍之。贼不知为老脚也，疑旁人伺，以所佩刀反手击之，中其腰，折骨剸腹，出肠而死。献贼省之，悔恨惋痛，急召老神仙。老神仙曰：“已死，不能救。”献贼骂曰：“老狡！监军妾不亦已死者乎？汝不能救，当杀汝以殉！”老神仙逡巡曰：“需时日乃可。”献贼急欲其生，限三日。老神仙请期三七。比以酒合药灌之，一七喉间即格格有声。老神仙贺曰：“可救矣，七日当复。”因取水润其肠，纳腹中，引针缝之，傅以药，夹以木板，约以绳，果七日而老脚步履如常时。
    　　及献贼死，贼众溃，从蜀奔滇。生平素德于老神仙者，卫之来滇。永历至，贼众多为伪王侯。老神仙啸傲王侯间，拥厚资，辟室城东隅，累石成山，凿井为池，旁植花木，畜朱鱼数百头。客至浮白，呼鱼出水以娱，醉则高歌而卧，不顾也。

    Explanation

    73

    献贼有幸婢曰“老脚”者，美而慧，善书画，脚不甚纤，因名。凡贼中移会侦发文字皆所掌，献贼嬖之。燕处有所思，老脚见其独坐，私往侍之。贼不知为老脚也，疑旁人伺，以所佩刀反手击之，中其腰，折骨剸腹，出肠而死。献贼省之，悔恨惋痛，急召老神仙。老神仙曰：“已死，不能救。”献贼骂曰：“老狡！监军妾不亦已死者乎？汝不能救，当杀汝以殉！”老神仙逡巡曰：“需时日乃可。”献贼急欲其生，限三日。老神仙请期三七。比以酒合药灌之，一七喉间即格格有声。老神仙贺曰：“可救矣，七日当复。”因取水润其肠，纳腹中，引针缝之，傅以药，夹以木板，约以绳，果七日而老脚步履如常时。

    When Zhang Xianzhong had a favored concubine named "Lao Jiao," she was beautiful and wise, skilled in painting and calligraphy, and her feet were not particularly slender, hence the name. She managed all the official documents and intelligence reports within the rebel camp, and Zhang Xianzhong favored her greatly. One day, while he was resting and lost in thought, Lao Jiao saw him sitting alone and went to attend to him. Zhang Xianzhong did not recognize her as Lao Jiao and suspected that someone was spying on him. In a fit of anger, he struck her with his sword, hitting her waist, breaking her bones, and causing her intestines to protrude, leading to her death. Zhang Xianzhong realized his mistake, filled with regret and sorrow, and hastily summoned the Daoist master. The Daoist master said, "She is already dead, and there is nothing I can do." Zhang Xianzhong cursed, "Old fox! Wasn't the concubine of the military commander also killed? You couldn't save her, so you should be killed to accompany her!" The Daoist master hesitated and said, "It will take some time." Zhang Xianzhong desperately wanted her to live and set a deadline of three days. The Daoist master requested seven weeks. By the time he mixed the medicine with wine and fed it to her, after one week, there were gurgling sounds in her throat. The Daoist master rejoiced, "She can be saved, and she will recover in seven days." He then took water to moisten her intestines, placed them back into her abdomen, sewed them up with a needle, applied medicine, and secured them with wooden boards and ropes. Indeed, after seven days, Lao Jiao could walk as usual.

    及献贼死，贼众溃，从蜀奔滇。生平素德于老神仙者，卫之来滇。永历至，贼众多为伪王侯。老神仙啸傲王侯间，拥厚资，辟室城东隅，累石成山，凿井为池，旁植花木，畜朱鱼数百头。客至浮白，呼鱼出水以娱，醉则高歌而卧，不顾也。

    After Zhang Xianzhong's death, the rebel forces dispersed, and many fled from Sichuan to Yunnan. Those who had always been kind to the Daoist master protected him and brought him to Yunnan. When the Yongli Emperor arrived, many of the rebels claimed titles of nobility. The Daoist master lived a life of leisure among the nobles, possessing great wealth. He built a room in the eastern part of the city, piled stones to form a hill, dug a well to create a pond, planted flowers and trees around it, and kept hundreds of colorful fish. When guests came, he would drink alcohol and call the fish out of the water for entertainment. When drunk, he would sing loudly and then lie down, completely unconcerned with anything else.

    74

    迄永历奔缅甸，老神仙从之行。及腾越，居常向空咄咄，若有所诉。一日谓文季曰：“吾老矣，将奈何？”文季曰：“等死耳，公何惜？但公之异术素靳不与人，致绝其传，是可惜！”老神仙曰：“吾非靳也，吾师授我时有戒也。”因讯其所授之由，曰：“某陈姓，河南邓州人，名家子。少尝入乡塾，性不乐章句。塾侧有塑神佛者，时就与嬉。塾师时扑责之，归而父母复责以不学。不能耐，遂出亡，怅怅无所适，因祷于关帝，得一签，云：『他日王侯却并肩』。自顾一丧家子，何得并肩王侯哉？然神不诬我，与王侯并肩者唯仙人，素闻终南山多隐仙，愿往从之。穷登涉，忍饥寒，遍访无可从者。一日至山后，遥望绝壁上有洞，人出入。因拔荆棘，踞峥岩，达于洞，见一道者坐石上，翛然异凡人。余幸曰：『此吾师也！』因长跪以请。道者不顾，拂袖归洞，余不敢入，即洞口稽首而已。

    Explanation

    74

    Until Emperor Yongli fled to Burma, the old immortal followed him. Upon reaching Tengyueh, he often spoke to the empty air, making sounds as if he were speaking, as if complaining about something. One day he told Wen Ji, "I am old now, what shall I do?" Wen Ji replied, "It's just dying, why should you care? But your unusual skill has always been stingy and not given to others, leading to the extinction of its transmission, which is indeed a pity!" The old immortal said, "I am not stingy; my teacher warned me when he taught me." Wen Ji then asked about the reason for his reluctance to share the skill, and the old immortal explained, "I am surnamed Chen, from Dengzhou, Henan, a son of a prominent family. As a young man, I once attended a village school, but I disliked reading and writing. There was a place near the school where they carved statues of gods and buddhas, and I often went there to play. The schoolmaster would sometimes beat me for not studying, and upon returning home, my parents would also scold me for not learning. I couldn't bear it, so I ran away, feeling lost and aimless. I prayed to Guandi and drew a lot, which said, 'One day, I will stand shoulder to shoulder with kings and dukes.' Looking at myself, a homeless child, how could I possibly stand shoulder to shoulder with kings and dukes? But the god did not deceive me. I have always heard that there are many hidden immortals on Zhongnan Mountain, and I wish to go and follow them. I endured all hardships, climbing mountains and crossing rivers, enduring hunger and cold, searching everywhere but finding no one to follow. One day, I reached the back of the mountain and saw a cave on a sheer cliff with people coming and going. I cleared away thorns and sat on a jagged rock, reaching the cave. I saw a Daoist sitting on a stone, looking completely different from ordinary people. I thought, 'This must be my master!' and knelt down to ask. The Daoist ignored me, waved his sleeve, and returned to the cave. I dared not enter and only knelt at the entrance."

    75

    如是者三日，忽一童子持一物示余曰：『师食尔！』状如糕，色白，方仅二寸，味甘如饴。食之，遂不复饥。余窃喜，益信。拜求至七日，道者忽出，问余曰：『痴子，汝欲何为？』余告以求仙。道者哂曰：『去！汝非此中人，何自苦为？』余自念无所归，唯投崖死耳，涕泣以求。已而道者曰：『吾念汝诚，有书一卷授汝，资一生衣食。好为之，勿轻泄，泄则雷击也。速去，毋久留，徒饱虎狼耳！』余得书惊喜，仓皇下山；省之，皆禁方也，可三十页。

    Explanation

    75

    For three days, I continued in this manner, when suddenly a boy appeared holding an object and said to me, "Teacher, eat this!" It looked like a cake, was white in color, square, and only two inches in size, tasting sweet like syrup. After eating it, I no longer felt hungry. I secretly rejoiced and my faith increased. I worshipped and begged for seven days, when the Daoist suddenly emerged and asked me, "Fool, what do you want?" I told him I sought immortality. He smiled and said, "Go! You are not one of us, why trouble yourself?" I thought to myself that I had nowhere to go, and the only option was to throw myself off a cliff and die, so I cried and begged. Then the Daoist said, "I consider your sincerity, I will give you a scroll to support your life. Do well with it, do not reveal it lightly, if you do, you will be struck by lightning. Go quickly, do not stay long, you will only satisfy the tigers and wolves!" I received the book in surprise and delight, hastily descending the mountain; upon examination, it contained forbidden formulas, about thirty pages.

    76

    道延安，人争传某巡抚者有爱女戏鞦迁伤足，骨出于外，医莫能疗，募能疗者，金二百，骡一匹。余往应募，依方试之，果瘥。余于是囊金乘骡归。吾父怒出亡，且疑多金，是时贼已起，谓余必从不义，首于官，将置之法。余族兄孝廉某，白无辜，出狱。讯其故，因出书。余父闻余出，持大杖奔族兄家。余族兄反覆解喻，不信，并陈书以实。余父愈怒，裂书火之。族兄从火中夺得，仅四页。余急怀而逃。今之所用者，皆烬余之四页耳。年久，其四页者亦不知往矣。”

    Explanation

    76

    As I passed through Yan'an, people were eager to spread the news about a certain governor who had a beloved daughter who was injured during a game of鞦迁 (a type of swing game), with her bone protruding. No doctor could treat her, and whoever could cure her would receive two hundred gold pieces and one mule. I went to volunteer and followed the prescribed method; it indeed healed. I then collected the gold and rode the mule back home. My father was angry and went into hiding, and he suspected that I had too much money. At that time, bandits had already risen up, and they thought I must have sided with the wrongdoers, reporting me to the authorities. They intended to punish me according to the law. My cousin, a scholar-official, proclaimed my innocence and got me out of jail. He investigated the reason and produced a document. My father heard that I was out and went to my cousin's house with a big stick. My cousin repeatedly tried to explain and persuade him, but he wouldn't believe. He presented the document to prove the truth. My father became even angrier and tore the document and burned it. My cousin managed to grab it from the fire, but only four pages remained. I quickly hid it and fled. What I use now are all the four pages that survived the fire. After many years, I don't even know what happened to those four pages.

    77

    其自述如此。居无何，以疾死。呜呼！不龟手药一也，一以封侯，一不免于洴澼絖，顾所用异耳。向使老神仙能体父志，不陷于贼，挟此术游当世，卢扁、华陀不得专于前矣。惜其狃于货利，遂安神仙之名，而终以贼死。虽然，人之遇仙与不遇仙，唯视福德之厚薄。老神仙得其书而不能全，其福可知矣。尝见稗官所志侯元者，樵山遇老人，授兵法，卒以作贼戮其身，事颇类此。常怪仙人不得其人，即秘其传可也，何往往传非其人以致戕害，仙亦何忍哉！且终南道者亦未必真仙，闻其膏乃以处子阴户油炼之，火光满室，焰升屋梁，光息而膏成，此岂仙人救人之方乎？《本草》以多用虫鱼，致迟上升十年，况杀人以救人，不独一人，且数百人。是老神仙者，则亦始终一贼而已。

    Explanation

    77

    As he described himself. After a short while, he died of illness. Alas! The medicine for not cracking hands is the same, yet one uses it to become a marquis, while the other is still mending clothes, it’s just that the uses are different. If only the old immortal could have followed his father's will, not falling into the hands of bandits, carrying this skill through the world, Luban and Hua Tuo would not be able to monopolize the field. Alas, he was obsessed with profit, and thus secured the name of immortal, but ultimately died as a bandit.
