Although, whether one meets an immortal or not depends on the thickness of one's virtue and merit. The old immortal obtained the book but could not fully utilize it, his virtue can be inferred. I have seen in minor officials' records about Hou Yuan, who met an old man in Qiushan, taught military strategy, and ultimately became a bandit and was killed, the story is quite similar. I often wonder why immortals don't find the right person, then they could keep the transmission secret, but why do they often pass it on to the wrong person, leading to harm, how could immortals bear to do that! Moreover, the Daoist from Zhongnan Mountain may not be a true immortal, I heard that the ointment is made by refining the oil from the vagina of a virgin, the flames fill the room, the fire rises to the eaves, and when the light goes out, the ointment is complete, is this the method by which immortals save people? According to Bencao, using too many insects and fish delays ascension by ten years, especially when killing people to save others, not just one person, but hundreds. This old immortal is just a bandit all along.

    78

    [张山来曰：仙家有禁方而不以传世，则禁方徒虚设耳。若以杀人救人为过，何不去此种类，而止有金石草木之药乎？乃计不出此，而往往传非其人以致遗累，是亦授受之未善也。] 瑶宫花史小传　长洲尤侗展成西堂杂俎

    Explanation

    78

    Zhang Shanlai said: The Daoist masters have secret formulas but do not pass them down to the world, then these secret formulas are merely empty. If taking a life to save another is considered a fault, why not eliminate this kind of person, and only have medicines made from minerals, stones, and herbs? But their plan does not include this, and often they pass these formulas to the wrong people, leading to trouble. This is also a failure in the process of transmission.

    79

    岁癸未，予读书王氏如武园，偶为扶鸾之戏，得遇瑶宫花史。云花史何氏，小名月儿，明初山阳富家女也。年十六，独在花下摘花，为一书生所调，父母怒而谪之，遂赴水死。王母怜其幼敏，录为散花仙史，此掌文真人唐孙过庭告予云。初降坛，作诗云：“片片落英飞羽客，翩翩独向风前立。缓行徐过小桥东，只恐舂衫香汗湿。”其标韵如此。
    　　花史年少，放诞风流，既为情死，眉黛间常有恨色。性善谐谑，既与予狎暱，嘲戏百出，一座閧堂。间以微词挑之，辄不对，或乱以他语，久而怃然，不知情之一往而深也。寒夜尝与予联句云：“树头落叶舞天衣，萧瑟风篁吟露唏。青火半消残月继，黄钟初罢晓星稀。新寒剪到罗帷急，愁泪弹来香息微。消遣夜深唯有梦，巫山携得片云归。”

    Explanation

    79

    In the year of Guiwei, while I was reading at Wang's Ruwu Garden, I happened to engage in the practice of fengluan divination and encountered the Fairy Historian of the Jade Palace. She said that the Fairy Historian was surnamed He, with the pet name Yue'er, and was a wealthy young woman from Shanyang during the early Ming period. At sixteen, she was alone under the flowers picking blossoms when she was courted by a scholar. Her parents were furious and banished her, causing her to drown herself. The Queen Mother of the West pitied her for her young sensitivity and recorded her as a flower-sprinkling fairy historian. This was told to me by the True Person in charge of literature, Tang Sun Guoting.

    When she first descended to the altar, she wrote a poem: "Petals fall like flying guests, gracefully standing alone before the wind. Slowly walking eastward over a small bridge, fearing only that the spring clothes will be dampened by fragrant sweat." Such was her elegance.

    The Fairy Historian was young and carefree, having died for love, and her eyebrows often carried a hint of sorrow. Naturally skilled in jest and teasing, once she became close to me, jokes and pranks abounded, and the entire hall was in uproar. Occasionally using subtle words to provoke her, she would not respond, or divert the conversation with other words. After a while, she would look thoughtful, unaware how deep her feelings had become. On a cold night, she once composed poetry with me: "Leaves fall from the trees like dancing celestial garments, the cold wind rustles through the bamboo, singing the dew. The blue fire is half extinguished, the waning moon continues, the yellow bell has just stopped, the morning stars are sparse. New coldness cuts through the silk curtains quickly, tears of sorrow fall, the fragrance fades. To pass the deep night, there is only sleep, bringing back a fragment of cloud from Mount Wushan."

    80

    自后相对，多作断肠哀怨之语。予戏以尺素贻之，是夜遂梦花史冉冉而来。年可十八、九，头上百花髻，戴芙蓉冠，插瑟瑟钿朵，著金缕单丝锦縠，银泥五晕罗裙，鸳鸯袜，五色云霞履。妆束雅淡，神姿艳发，顾盼妩媚，不可描画，搴帷微笑，若欲有言。予胸次忽为一物填压，又似鬼手来捉人臂，惊呼而觉，但见残红明灭，纸窗风声条条，若有弹指而泣者。诘朝问之，云：“吾夜间到君床头两次，君为五脏神所守，觉则退耳。”予问：“五脏神谁何？”花史云：“凡人一身，皆有神守：耳目手足，有神外守；五脏魂魄，有神内守。有缘者神与之亲，无缘者神不与之亲。吾与子情深矣，奈三生石上无一笑缘何！”因泣下唏嘘。

    Explanation

    80

    From then on, we often spoke words of heart-wrenching sorrow and lament. I playfully gave him a piece of silk, and that night I dreamed that Huashi came slowly and gracefully. She was about eighteen or nineteen years old, with a hundred-flower bun on her head, wearing a lotus crown, adorned with jade-like flower decorations, dressed in a golden-threaded single-silk brocade robe, a silver-dyed five-colored silk skirt, mandarin duck socks, and five-colored cloud and rainbow shoes. She was dressed in a simple and elegant manner, her divine and radiant beauty, her looks and glances were charming, and she was impossible to describe. She gently pulled back the curtain and smiled, as if she wanted to speak. Suddenly, something filled and pressed my chest, it felt like a ghostly hand was grabbing my arm. I woke up in a startled cry, and I only saw the faintly glowing red flowers, the sound of the wind through the paper window, as if someone was tapping their fingers and crying. The next morning, I asked her, and she said, "I came to your bedside twice at night, you were guarded by the five organs' spirits, if you wake up, they retreat." I asked, "Who are the five organs' spirits?" Huashi said, "All humans have spirits that guard them: the ears, eyes, hands, and feet are guarded by external spirits; the five organs and soul are guarded by internal spirits. Those with good fortune have a close relationship with the spirits, those without good fortune do not have a close relationship with the spirits. My feelings for you are deep, but there is no karmic connection of a smile on the stone of three lives!" She then wept and sighed.

    81

    既而言楚江事。楚江，一花史侍儿也，与幼婢小红皆端丽明慧，日侍香案。花史云：“楚江前世与君为邻，两情眷眷不遂，病死。君作一柬，焚告楚江云：『三生如不断，愿结未来缘。』君举孝廉，亦早逝。迄今二十年，可续前盟矣。”遂请于王母，许于甲申二月降生赵地，赐以玉珰一事，翠凤履一双。花史赋《鹧鸪天》词送之，云：“整束簪环下碧霄，教人肠断《念奴娇》。曲房空剩残香粉，独对潇湘忆翠翘。寻别话，酌清醪，盈盈徐送小红桥。从今不伴烟霞客，爱向风前斗柳腰。”

    Explanation

    81

    After speaking about the matter of Chu Jiang, it is said that Chu Jiang was a maid of a flower historian. She and the young servant Xiao Hong were both beautiful and intelligent, serving at the incense table daily. The flower historian said, "In a past life, Chu Jiang lived next to you, and your mutual affection did not come to fruition, leading to her illness and death. You wrote a letter and burned it to inform Chu Jiang, saying, 'If our three lives are not broken, I wish to tie a future bond.' You passed the examination for filial piety and integrity but also died young. Until now, for twenty years, the previous alliance can be continued." Then he asked the Queen Mother, and she agreed that in the second month of the year Jia Shen, she would be born in Zhao. She was given one jade pendant and a pair of emerald phoenix shoes. The flower historian composed a 'Zhegu Tian' poem to send her, which reads: "Neatly arranging her hairpins and rings as she descended from the blue sky, causing people to break their hearts with 'Nian Nu Jiao.' The small room left only with remnants of incense and powder, alone facing the Xiang River, remembering the green feathers. Finding words to say goodbye, drinking clear wine, gracefully and slowly leading to the small red bridge. From now on, no longer accompanying the wanderer in mist and clouds, loving to compete in the wind with willow waists."

    82

    楚江和云：“朝餐风露暮凌霄，不羡金门贮阿娇。却恨柳丝牵月线，强移花色点云翘。情犹恋，意如醪，依依不舍旧蓝桥。东君可许归相伴，暂向尘封学楚腰。”
    　　然自楚江下世，花史意致黯然，不复如前日欢洽矣。王母闻其以腴词赠答，切责之，命游神巡察，不许私至，且曰：“尤生不患才少，花儿独患情多。倘涉幽期，恐有山魈木魅之疑也。”自尔踪迹遂绝。予尝览《杜兰香传》，乃湘江三岁女子，为阿母青童携去，后驾钿车诣包山张硕，言本为君作妻，以年命未合，小乖，太岁东方卯，当还求君。此与楚江事绝类。而予沦落不偶，无室家之乐，幽婚如梦，忽忽忘之。然每策蹇往来邯郸道上，秦楼日出，游女如云，恍然若有所遇，卒无有鼓瑟而至者。而予亦已老矣！岂仙人固好食言耶？抑予尘心未尽，负此蹇修也？

    Explanation

    82

    楚 Jiang and the Clouds: "Morning, I dine on the wind and dew, evening, I ascend to the clouds. I do not envy the luxurious life of the noble, confined in the Golden Gate. Yet, I regret that the willow threads are tied to the moon, and I forcibly move the flower colors to adorn the cloud-tipped hairpin. Emotion still lingers, and the heart is like wine, reluctant to part from the old Blue Bridge. May the Eastern Lord allow me to return and be together, temporarily turning to the dust-covered world to learn the grace of Chu's waist."

    However, since the passing of Chu Jiang, the spirit of Flower History has grown dim, no longer as joyful as before. The Queen Mother of the West heard of the exchange of elegant words and severely reprimanded them, ordering the spirits of travel to patrol and not to meet privately. She also said, "You, Mr. You, are not short of talent, but the flower alone suffers from excessive emotion. If you involve yourselves in secret rendezvous, there may be suspicions of malevolent spirits." From then on, their traces were cut off. I once read the Durangxiang Zhuan, which tells of a three-year-old girl from the Xiang River, taken away by her mother, the Green Child, and later driving a jeweled carriage to Mount Bao to meet Zhang Shuo, saying she was originally meant to be his wife, but due to a mismatch in destiny, she had to part, and at the age of twenty, she would return to seek him. This is very similar to the story of Chu Jiang. And I, having fallen into misfortune, have no joy in family life, and the secret marriage is like a dream, forgotten in a haze. Yet, every time I travel the roads of Handan on a slow horse, the Qin Lou rises with the sun, and the courtesans gather like clouds, and I seem to meet someone, but never one who comes playing the zither. And I am also old now! Is it that the immortals are fond of breaking their promises? Or is it that my worldly desires have not been fully extinguished, and I have failed in this difficult journey?

    83

    花史诗词甚多，其最著者，《太华行》一篇。先是甲申元日，真人同湘江诸侣游太华山，乐甚，命予两人作长歌记之。予走笔急就，而花史诗故作虫书，亦狡狯伎俩也。真人笑而译之，其辞曰：“登峰当登第一山，婆娑屹立不可攀。巨灵赑屃崪为掌，云气时流十指间，苍龙玉马随风步，黄冠鹤羽皆童颜。半壁飞泉珠雨散，水天相对乘时闲。尔乃坐青莲，游玉田，金鼎石室篆如烟。团团握麈成清谈，铁笛一声江天寒。玉女乘鸾相接引，葡萄火枣列嘉宴。歌一曲，乐万年，进一酌，成百篇。松风枕上听流泉，陶然醉倒不知还。呼吸三光应列斗，巍峨两山一划剖。少阴令德合秋成，气含金爽据丁酉。伊古少昊居此都，蓐收别馆称中阜。何若凌虚此一游，凭风羽化飞飞走。视昔登颠发狂号，垂书作别真堪呕。仙兮仙兮不可及，仿佛斯游不竟口。我向琼宫索记书，大文千言若蝌蚪。”

    Explanation

    83

    The Flower Historian's poetry is abundant, but the most famous is "The Journey to Mount Taihua." Before this, on the first day of the Jia Shen year, the True Person, along with companions from Xiangjiang, traveled to Mount Taihua, enjoying themselves greatly. He commanded me two to compose a long song to record it. I wrote hastily, and the Flower Historian's poem was written in insect script, also a trick of cunning and skill. The True Person laughed and translated it, its words saying:

    "Ascending a peak, one should ascend the highest mountain, gracefully standing, it cannot be climbed. The Great Spirit creates a palm-like shape with its back, clouds flow between the ten fingers. Azure dragons and jade horses follow the wind, yellow hats and crane feathers all have youthful faces. Half the cliff is covered by flying springs like scattered pearls, water and sky face each other in idle moments. Then sit on a lotus, wander in a jade field, golden tripod, stone chamber, inscriptions like smoke. Gripping a whisk in a circle, engaging in clear talk, a sound of an iron flute makes the river and sky cold. Jade maiden riding a phoenix to meet and guide, grapes and fire dates arranged for a splendid banquet. Sing a song, enjoy ten thousand years, drink a cup, compose a hundred poems. Listen to the flowing springs on the pillow under the pine wind, drunkenly entranced, unaware of returning. Breathing the three lights, corresponding to the stars in the sky, two towering mountains, one stroke divides them. The virtue of the lesser yin aligns with the autumn harvest, the air contains a golden coolness, corresponding to the year Ding You. From ancient times, Shao Hao lived here, the guesthouse of Ru Shou is called Central Fertile Land. How could one soar through the void in this journey, riding the wind, transforming into a feathered being, flying and walking? Looking back at past climbs, shouting wildly, leaving a written record to bid farewell, truly worth vomiting. Immortal, immortal, unreachable, vaguely, this journey is not fully expressed. I went to the Palace of Jade to request a record, a great text of a thousand words, like tadpoles."

    84

    展子曰：汉史记帐中神君，不见其形，但闻其语而已。至乩仙，并其语不可得闻也，亦恍惚矣。然花史尝许予现形，一夕月明竹下，有云鬟翠袖，倚而招予者，望之翩然；即而求之，邈然不知其所之焉。是耶非耶？吾又何能测之哉？——花史每呼予为展子。
    　　[张山来曰：世间唯乩仙一事最为难解。以为真仙，则不当为人所召；以为非仙，则诗句敏而且工，字迹亦多别致。或者慧业文久，死而精魂不散，偶借人间笔墨以消遣光阴耳！古人云：“宁为才鬼，尤胜顽仙”，则谓才鬼为仙亦无不可。] 九牛坝观觝戏记　豫章彭士望达生文瀔

    Explanation

    84

    Zhan Zi said: The Han Shi Ji (Records of the Han Dynasty) records a deity known as the "Zhang Zhong Shen Jun" (Deity of the Ledger), who was not seen but only heard. As for the medium or spirit communicated through a divining board, not even their words can be heard, making the experience even more elusive. Yet, the historian once promised to show his form. One night, under the bright moonlight in the bamboo grove, a woman with cloud-like hair and green sleeves appeared, leaning and beckoning to me. She looked graceful, but when I sought her, she vanished, leaving me uncertain of her whereabouts. Is it true or not? How could I possibly measure or understand it? — The historian often calls me Zhan Zi.

    [ Zhang Shanlai said: The practice of communicating with spirits through a divining board is the most difficult to understand in the world. If it is a true immortal, they should not be summoned by humans; if it is not an immortal, their verses are quick and skillful, and their handwriting is often distinctive. Perhaps it is the spirit of someone with long-standing wisdom and literary talent, who, after death, has an undissipated spirit that temporarily borrows human pens and ink to pass the time! An ancient saying goes: "It is better to be a talented ghost than a dull immortal," so it is not unreasonable to consider a talented ghost as a form of immortality. ] Jiu Niu Ba Guan Di Xi Ji (Record of the Wrestling Match at Jiu Niu Dam) by Peng Shiwang of Yuzhang, Da Sheng Wen Ji (Records of Life and Death).

    85

    树庐叟负豳忧之疾于九牛坝茅斋之下。戊午闰月除日，有为角觝之戏者，踵门告曰：“其亦有以娱公。”叟笑而颔之，因设场于溪树之下。密云未雨，风木泠然，阴而不燥。于是邻幼生周氏之族之宾之友戚，山者牧樵，耕者犁犊，行担簦者，水浮楫者，咸停释而聚观焉。
    　　初则累重案，一妇人仰卧其上，竖双足，承八岁儿，反覆卧起，或鹄立合掌拜跪，又或两肩接足；儿之足亦仰竖，伸缩自如。间又一足承儿，儿拳曲如莲花出水状。其下则二男子、二妇、一女童与一老妇，鸣金鼓，俚歌杂佛曲和之，良久乃下。又一妇登场如前卧，竖承一案，旋转周四角，更反侧背面承之。儿复立案上，拜起如前仪。儿下，则又承一木槌，槌长尺有半，径半之，两足员转，或竖抛之而复承之。妇既罢，一男子登焉，足仍竖承一梯，可五级，儿上至绝顶，复倒竖穿级而下。

    Explanation

    85

    The Old Man of the Thatched Hut, suffering from the worries of Bin, lived beneath a thatched cottage in the Nine Bulls Dam area. On the day after the leap month of the Wuwu year, performers of acrobatic feats came to the door and said, "We have something to entertain you." The old man smiled and nodded, and they set up a performance area under the trees by the stream. The sky was overcast but not yet raining, and the wind through the trees was cool and refreshing, creating a damp but not dry atmosphere. Thus, neighbors, young scholars, and relatives of the Zhou family, along with shepherds and woodcutters from the mountains, farmers and their oxen, travelers with umbrellas or canes, and boatmen, all stopped what they were doing and gathered to watch.

    At first, heavy tables were stacked, and a woman lay on her back on one of them, standing on both feet and supporting an eight-year-old child. She would repeatedly lie down and rise, sometimes standing upright with clasped hands in a bow, or sometimes placing both hands on the child's feet. The child's feet were also raised and straight, stretching and contracting freely. Sometimes, one foot supported the child, and the child curled up like a lotus flower emerging from water. Below, two men, two women, a young girl, and an old woman beat gongs and drums, singing folk songs mixed with Buddhist tunes. After a long time, they came down. Another woman appeared on stage as before, lying down and standing on one foot, supporting a table. She rotated around the four corners and turned over, supporting from the back. The child rose on the table again, bowing and rising as before. When the child came down, they supported a wooden stick, one and a half feet long, with a diameter half its length. The child's feet spun in circles, or she would throw the stick up and catch it again. After the woman finished, a man stepped up, still standing on one foot and supporting a ladder with five steps. The child climbed to the very top and then descended, standing upside down through the steps.

    86

    叟悯其劳，令暂息，饮之酒，其人更移场他处，择草浅平坡地，去瓦石。乃接木为桥，距地八尺许，一男子履其上，傅粉墨，挥扇杂歌笑，阔步坦坦，时或跳跃，后更舞大刀，回翔中节。此戏吾乡暨江左时有之，更有高丈余者，但步，不能舞。最后设软索，高丈许，长倍之，女童履焉，手持一竹竿，两头载石如持衡，行至索尽处，辄倒步。或偃卧，或一足立，或伛行，或负竿行如担，或时坠挂，复跃起，下鼓歌和之，说白俱有名目。为时最久，可十许刻。女下，妇索帕，蒙双目为瞽者，番跃而登，作盲状，东西探步，时跌若坠，复摇晃似战惧，久之乃已；仍持竿，石加重，盖其衡也。

    Explanation

    86

    The old man pitied his labor, ordered him to rest temporarily, and gave him wine. The person then moved the field to another place, choosing a shallow, flat grassy slope, and removed tiles and stones. He then joined wood to make a bridge, about eight feet above the ground. A man walked on it, putting on makeup, waving a fan, and singing and laughing, walking with broad, steady steps, sometimes jumping. Later, he danced with a large sword, moving gracefully in rhythm. This play was sometimes performed in my hometown and the Jiangnan region, and there were also those that were over ten feet tall, but they could only walk, not dance. Finally, he set up a soft rope, about ten feet high and twice as long, and a little girl walked on it, holding a bamboo pole with stones on both ends, like a balance. When she reached the end of the rope, she would step backward. Sometimes she lay down, sometimes stood on one foot, sometimes hunched over, sometimes carried the pole like a load, sometimes fell and hung, and then jumped up. The drum was beaten below, and songs were sung in harmony, with both the dialogue and the actions having specific names. It lasted the longest, about ten or so moments. The girl came down, and a woman took a handkerchief and covered her eyes, making her a blind person. She leaped and climbed, acting as if she were blind, exploring steps to the east and west, sometimes stumbling and seeming to fall, then shaking and seeming to be afraid. After a while, it ended. She still held the pole, and the stones became heavier, covering the balance.

    87

    方登场时，观者见其险，咸为股栗，毛发竖，目眩晕，惴惴然唯恐其倾坠。叟视场上人，皆暇整从容而静，八岁儿亦斋慄如先辈主敬，如入定僧。此皆一诚之所至，而专用之于习，惨澹攻苦，屡蹉跌而不迁，审其机以应其势，以得其致力之所在。习之又久，乃至精熟，不失毫芒，乃始出而行世，举天下之至险阻者皆为简易。夫曲艺则亦有然者矣。以是知至巧出于至平，盖以志凝其气，气动于天，非卤莽灭裂之所能效。此其意庄生知之，私其身不以用于天下；仪、秦亦知之，且习之以人国戏，私富贵以自贼其身与名。庄所称僚之弄丸、庖丁之解牛、伛佝之承蜩、纪省子之养鸡，推之伯昏瞀人临千仞之溪，足逡巡垂二分在外；吕梁丈人出没于悬水三十仞，流沫四十里之间，何莫非是？其神全也。叟又以视观者，久亦忘其为险，无异康庄大道也，与之俱化。甚矣，习之能移人也。

    Explanation

    87

    When the performers first stepped onto the stage, the audience saw the danger and were all trembling in their legs, their hairs standing on end, their eyes spinning, and they were anxiously afraid that the performance might topple. The old man looked at the performers on the stage, all of whom were leisurely, composed, and calm. Even an eight-year-old child was solemn and respectful like the elders, like a monk in meditation. All of this was the result of utmost sincerity, and they devoted themselves solely to practice, struggling through hardships, repeatedly stumbling but not giving up, understanding the timing and responding to the situation, in order to focus their efforts where they should be. After practicing for a long time, they reached mastery, not missing a single detail, and then began to perform in the world, making all the most dangerous and difficult things in the world seem simple. Such skills in the realm of the arts also follow this pattern. From this, we can understand that the highest skill comes from the simplest foundation, for willpower concentrates the spirit, and the spirit moves in accordance with heaven, not something that can be achieved through careless and superficial efforts. This principle was understood by Zhuangzi, who kept it to himself and did not use it for the world; the strategists Yi and Qin also understood this, and practiced it as a game among nations, seeking wealth and status at the cost of their own integrity and reputation. The stories Zhuangzi mentioned of the man skilled in juggling balls, the butcher skilled in dissecting cattle, the old man skilled in catching cicadas, and the man skilled in raising chickens, as well as the man standing at the edge of a deep ravine, with his feet barely visible, and the man navigating a dangerous waterfall, what are they all but examples of this? It is the completeness of spirit. The old man then looked at the spectators, and after a while, they also forgot it was dangerous, no different from a broad, smooth road, transforming together with him. How great is the power of practice to transform people!

    88

    其人为叟言，祖自河南来零陵，传业者三世，徒百余人，家有薄田，颇苦赋役，携其妇与妇之娣姒、兄之子、提抱之婴孩，糊其口于四方，赢则以供田赋。所至江浙、两粤、滇黔、口外绝徼之地，皆步担，器俱不外贷；谙草木之性，捃摭续食，亦以哺其儿。叟视其人，衣敝缊，飘泊羁穷，陶然有自乐之色，群居甚和适。男女五、六岁即授技，老而休焉，皆有以自给。以道路为家，以戏为田，传授为世业。其肌体为寒暑风雨冰雪之所顽，智意为跋涉艰远人情之所儆怵摩厉。男妇老稚皆顽钝，儇敏机利，捷于猿猱，而其性旷然如麋鹿。

    Explanation

    88

    The person told the old man that his ancestor came from Henan to Lingling, passing down the profession for three generations, with over a hundred people following. The family had a small plot of land and was greatly burdened by taxes and labor duties. They carried their wives, sisters-in-law, brothers' children, and infants, making a living by traveling to various places, and when they had surplus, they used it to pay taxes. Wherever they went, to the Jiang-Zhe, Liang-Yue, Dian-Qian, and remote border regions, they all carried their own burdens and did not borrow tools or equipment. They were familiar with the nature of plants, gathering and continuing to eat, and also used this to feed their children. The old man observed that they appeared content and happy with themselves, living together in great harmony. Boys and girls were taught skills at the age of five or six, and they retired when old, all having means to support themselves. They made the road their home and performance their field, passing down the craft as a family business. Their bodies were hardened by cold, heat, wind, rain, and snow, and their intelligence and will were sharpened by the hardships of long journeys and human interactions. Men, women, old, and young were all tough and dull, yet quick and agile like apes, and their nature was free and wild like deer.

    89

    叟因之重有感矣：先王之教，久矣夫不明不作！其人自处于优笑巫觋之间，为夏仲御之所深疾。然益知天地之大，物各遂其生成，稗稻并实，无偏颇也。彼固自以为戏，所游历几千万里，高明巨丽之家，以迄三家一閧之村市，亦无不以戏视之，叟独以为有所用。身老矣！不能事洴澼洸，亦安所得以试其不龟手之药？托空言以记之。固哉，王介甫谓鸡鸣狗盗之出其门，士之所以不至，不能致鸡鸣狗盗耳。吕惠卿辈之谄谩，曾鸡鸣狗盗之不若。鸡鸣狗盗之出其门，益足以致天下之奇士！而孟尝未足以知之，信陵、燕昭知之，所以收浆博屠者之用，千金市死马之骨，而遂以报齐怨。宋亦有张元、吴昊，虽韩、范不能用，以资西夏。宁无复以叟为戏言也，悲夫！

    89

    The old man, upon this, deeply felt something. The teachings of ancient kings have long been without enlightenment and action. Those who place themselves among jesters, jesters, and shamans are deeply hated by the great minister Xia Zhongyu. However, one gains a deeper understanding of the vastness of the universe, where all things follow their natural development, and wild rice and cultivated rice both bear fruit, without bias or partiality. They themselves regard it as a play, having traveled tens of thousands of miles, from families of high status and grandeur to villages and markets where three families fight, treating it all as a game. The old man alone believes it has some use. Now that he is old, he cannot serve in the administration, nor can he find a way to test his medicine that prevents his hands from cracking. He records it with empty words. Indeed, Wang Anshi said that the presence of chicken-calling and dog-stealing skills in his court was why scholars did not come, because he could not attract chicken-calling and dog-stealing skills. The flattery and deception of Lü Huiqing and others were even less than chicken-calling and dog-stealing skills. The presence of chicken-calling and dog-stealing skills in his court would attract the most extraordinary scholars! But Mengchang was not capable of understanding this; Xinling and Yan Zhao understood it. Thus, they attracted the use of peddlers and butchers, spending a thousand gold pieces to buy the bones of a dead horse, thereby avenging the resentment of Qi. Song also had Zhang Yuan and Wu Hao, but even though Han and Fan could not use them, they contributed to the Western Xia. Is there no one left to take the old man's words seriously? How sad!

    90

    [张山来曰：此技即俗所谓“踹索”者。予尝谓此等人必能作贼，有守土之责者，宜禁止之；纵不欲绝其衣食之路，或毋许入城，听于乡间搬演可耳。
    　　前段叙事简净，后段议论奇辟，自是佳文！]
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷三 马伶传　商邱侯方域朝宗壮悔堂集
    　　马伶者，金陵梨园部也。金陵为明之“留都”，社稷百官皆在，而又当太平盛世，人易为乐，其士女之问桃叶渡、游雨华台者，趾相错也。

    Explanation

    90

    Translation:

    Zhang Shanlai said: This skill is commonly known as "chua suo" (a type of acrobatic performance). I have often said that such people are likely to become thieves. Officials who are responsible for maintaining order and security should prohibit this; even if they do not want to cut off their means of livelihood, they should not be allowed to enter the city, but instead allow them to perform in the countryside.
    The first part of the text is concise in its narrative, while the second part is uniquely insightful in its discussion, making it a fine piece of writing!

    From the third volume of Yu Chu Xin Zhi: "The Biography of Ma Ling" by Hou Fangyu of Shangqiu, from the Zhuang Hui Tang Ji.
    Ma Ling was a member of the theater troupe in Jinling (Nanjing). Jinling was the "retained capital" of the Ming dynasty, where the government and officials were still present, and it was a time of peace and prosperity. People were easily content, and many men and women would visit Taoye Ferry and tour Yuhua Terrace, their footsteps crossing each other.

    91

    梨园以技鸣者无论数十辈，而其最著者二，曰“兴化部”，曰“华林部”。一日，新安贾合两部为大会，遍征金陵之贵客文人，与夫妖姬静女，莫不毕集。列“兴化”于东肆，“华林”于西肆，两肆皆奏《鸣凤》所谓椒山先生者。迨半奏，引商刻羽，抗坠疾徐，并称善也。当两相国论河套，而西肆之为严嵩相国者曰李伶，东肆则马伶。坐客乃西顾而叹，或大呼命酒，或移坐更近之，首不复东。未几更进，则东肆不复能终曲。询其故，盖马伶耻出李伶下，已易衣遁矣。马伶者，金陵之善歌者也，既去，“兴化部”又不肯辄以易之，乃竟辍其技不奏，而“华林部”独著。
    　　去后且三年，而马伶归，遍告其故侣，请于新安贾曰：“今日幸为开宴，招前日宾客，愿与『华林部』更奏《鸣凤》，奉一日欢。”既奏，已而论河套，马伶复为严嵩相国以出。李伶忽失声，匍匐前称弟子。“兴化部”是日遂凌出“华林部”远甚。

    Explanation

    91

    The Pear Garden was home to countless performers who had gained fame through their skills, but among them, two were the most renowned: the Xinghua troupe and the Huayin troupe. One day, a merchant from Xin'an gathered both troupes for a grand assembly, inviting all the noble guests, scholars, and beautiful women of Nanjing to attend. The Xinghua troupe was placed on the eastern side, and the Huayin troupe on the western side. Both sides performed Mingfeng, a play attributed to the esteemed Mr. Jiashan. By the time they had performed halfway through, the music was exquisite, with the Shang and Yu tones skillfully played, and the tempo varied between fast and slow, all of which were praised by the audience. As the two prime ministers discussed the Hexi region, the actor portraying the Prime Minister Yan Song on the western side was Li Ling, while on the eastern side, it was Ma Ling. The audience turned their heads to the west and sighed, some shouting for wine, others moving closer to the stage, and none looking back to the east. Soon after, the eastern side could no longer finish the performance. When asked why, it was revealed that Ma Ling was ashamed to be inferior to Li Ling and had already changed clothes and fled. Ma Ling, a skilled singer from Nanjing, had left, and the Xinghua troupe refused to replace him, so they stopped performing altogether, leaving the Huayin troupe to stand out alone.

    After three years, Ma Ling returned and informed all his old companions, asking the merchant from Xin'an, "Today, I hope you will host a banquet and invite the guests from before, so that I may perform Mingfeng again with the Huayin troupe, providing a day of enjoyment." After the performance, when they discussed the Hexi region, Ma Ling once again portrayed the Prime Minister Yan Song. Li Ling suddenly lost his voice and fell forward, calling out to Ma Ling as his disciple. On that day, the Xinghua troupe far surpassed the Huayin troupe in performance.

    92

    其夜，“华林部”过马伶曰：“子，天下之善技也，然无以易李伶。李伶之为严相国至矣，子又安从授之而掩其上哉？”马伶曰：“固然，天下无以易李伶，李伶即又不肯授我。我闻今相国某者，严相国俦也。我走京师，求为其门卒三年，日侍相国于朝房，察其举止，聆其语言，久乃得之。——此吾之所为师也！”“华林部”相与罗拜而去。马伶名锦，字云将，其先西域人，当时犹称马回回云。
    　　侯方域曰：异哉，马伶之自得师也！夫其以李伶为绝技，无所于求，乃走事某，见某犹之见分宜也。以分宜教分宜，安得不工哉？呜呼！耻其技之不若，而去数千里为卒三年；倘三年犹不得，即犹不归耳。其志如此，技之工又须问耶？
    　　[张山来曰：予素不解弈，不解歌，自恨甚拙，因从学于人。虽不能工，然亦自觉有入门处。乃知艺无学而不成者。观马伶事益信。] 顾玉川传　江阴曹禾峨嵋文瀔选本

    Explanation

    92

    That night, the "Hualin Department" approached Ma Ling and said, "You are the finest artist in the world, but there is no one who can surpass Li Ling. Li Ling's portrayal of Yan Xiangguo was perfect; how could you learn from him and surpass him?" Ma Ling replied, "Indeed, there is no one who can surpass Li Ling, and Li Ling was unwilling to teach me. I heard that the current prime minister is similar to Yan Xiangguo. I went to the capital and served as a doorkeeper for three years, serving the prime minister daily in the government office, observing his actions and listening to his words. After a long time, I finally understood. — This is my teacher!" The "Hualin Department" bowed together and departed. Ma Ling's name was Jin, his courtesy name Yunjiang, and he was originally from the Western Regions; at that time, he was still called Ma Huihui.

    Hou Fangyu said, "How extraordinary, that Ma Ling found his own teacher! He considers Li Ling's skills to be unparalleled and has nowhere else to look, so he went to serve someone, seeing him as if he were seeing Yan Xiangguo. Teaching Yan Xiangguo like Yan Xiangguo, how could it not be excellent? Alas! Ashamed that his skills were not as good, he traveled thousands of miles to serve as a doorkeeper for three years; if he still cannot achieve it after three years, he will not return. With such determination, how can we doubt his skill?"

    [Zhang Shanlai said, "I have never understood chess or singing, and I am very ashamed of my clumsiness, so I followed someone to learn. Although I cannot excel, I also feel that I have made some progress. Thus, I know that art cannot be achieved without learning. Seeing Ma Ling's story, I believe even more."]

    93

    顾玉川，名大愚，字道民，邑东鄙杨舍人。深目戟髯，类羽人剑客。少遇异人授神行术，三日夜达京师，六日而返。父母怪问之，玉川语之故，袖葡萄、苹果以献。由是里中传以为神。
    　　性任侠，喜施舍，尤好奇服，所至儿童聚观。常衣纸衣，行则瑟瑟有声；冠纸冠，方屋而高二尺。或时蓬跣行歌道中，或时幅巾深衣，肩古藤杖，杖悬葫芦，大于身而高于顶，遇风则与偕覆，徐拄杖而起，行歌自如。渡河未尝假舟楫，跨葫芦，以杖导水，上下水面，望之如游云气中。与人言，多方外骇异不根之说，人亦无从诘之。独其顷忽间往返数百里，音问不爽，道路行旅，历历咸见，此足奇也。
    　　明启、祯交，玉川子每游京师，月必一二过，尤厚虞山钱宗伯谦益。宗伯传胪及第第三人，玉川子以其捷音归，归五日而邮报至。邮中诸少年疾驰七日夜，始抵钱氏室，则已泥金焕然，无所获。宗伯言于诸公卿，闻其风者，以识面为幸。

    Explanation

    93

    Con Yuanchuan, named Dàyú and styled Dàomín, was a man from the eastern outskirts of Yangshè. He had deep-set eyes and a beard like a sword, resembling a Daoist immortal or a sword master. In his youth, he encountered a mysterious person who taught him the divine travel technique, allowing him to reach the capital in three days and nights, and return in six days. His parents were surprised and asked him about it, and Yuanchuan explained the reason and offered grapes and apples from his sleeves. From then on, the villagers believed he was a deity.

    He was chivalrous and enjoyed giving alms, especially fond of unusual clothing. Children gathered to watch wherever he went. He often wore paper clothes, and his steps made a rustling sound. He wore a paper hat, square-shaped and two feet tall. Sometimes he walked barefoot singing on the road, and sometimes he wore a wide headband and long robe, carrying an ancient vine staff with a gourd hanging from it, larger than his body and taller than his head. When the wind blew, he would be blown over together, and he would slowly stand up with his staff and continue singing as usual. He never used boats to cross rivers, instead straddling the gourd and using his staff to guide the water, floating above the water surface, appearing as if he were floating in the clouds. When talking to others, he often spoke of strange and ungrounded things from the outer world, and people could not question him about it. But he could travel hundreds of miles in an instant, and his messages were always accurate. Travelers on the road could clearly see him, which was truly remarkable.

    During the transition between the Ming and Qing dynasties, Yuanchuan often visited the capital, usually once or twice a month. He had a particularly close relationship with Qian Qianyi, the Master of Yushan. Qian Qianyi was the third place winner in the imperial examination. Yuanchuan brought the news of Qian's success back, and five days later, the official report arrived. The young messengers rushed for seven days and nights, finally reaching Qian's house, but the news was already posted with gold leaf, and they had no news to deliver. Qian spoke to the officials, and those who heard of him felt fortunate to have met him.

    94

    一日远游归，骑白牛，披孔翠裘，戴槲笠如车轮，手棕榈扇，后随一橐驼，背置大葫芦，其旁悬罂缶累累然，种所得奇花草，青葱鲜洁，如山岳自行。邑之人初未识橐驼，拥观以为怪。时学使者方较试，六郡士咸集，群指顾愕眙。忽一人昂然从众中出，纸衣纸冠皆皂色，与玉川相对鼓掌笑，遂挽橐驼上，抱葫芦以行，如凶礼中“方相”然。识者曰：“此梁溪邹公履也。”玉川之好怪而所与游多类此。玉川常乘橐驼往来旁郡县。至毗陵驿，橐驼坠于野厕，百计挽之不能出，乃毁岸出之，而橐驼死矣。后访道入华山，不知所终。或谓玉川实病死于家，诫其子孙讳之也。
    　　[张山来曰：余读《水浒传》，窃慕神行太保戴宗之术，又以为尚不及缩地法。私尝疑之，谓为文人游戏笔墨，未必实有其术。今读此，则是世有其人，惜予不及见耳。] 冒姬董小宛传　金沙张明弼公亮萤芝集

    Explanation

    94

    One day, after returning from a long journey, he rode a white ox, wearing a greenish-blue fur coat, and a large, round hat like a wheel, holding a palm leaf fan. Following him was a camel, on which he carried a large gourd, with various strange and beautiful flowers he had collected, fresh and clean, like a moving mountain range. The people of the town had never seen a camel before and crowded around to watch, thinking it was strange. At the time, the provincial examiner was conducting examinations, and scholars from six prefectures gathered together, everyone pointing and staring in amazement. Suddenly, a man emerged confidently from the crowd, wearing paper clothing and a paper hat, both black in color, and clapped and laughed with Yu Chuan. He then led the camel forward, holding the gourd as he walked, like the 'Fangxiang' in funeral rites. Those who recognized him said, "This is Zou Gongli of Liangxi." Yu Chuan's fondness for the strange and the unusual, and the people he associated with, were often like this. Yu Chuan often traveled between neighboring prefectures and counties on a camel. When he reached the Piling Post Station, the camel fell into a public toilet. Despite numerous attempts to pull it out, they had to break the bank to get it out, and the camel died. Later, he went in search of the Dao and entered Mount Hua, and his fate is unknown. Some say that Yu Chuan actually died of illness at home and instructed his descendants to keep it secret.

    [Zhang Shanlai says: I read Water Margin and secretly admired the supernatural abilities of Dai Zong, the 'Divine Speed Keeper.' I thought they were not as impressive as the 'Shrink the Earth' technique. I privately doubted that such abilities were just literary embellishments. Now, after reading this, I realize that such people do exist, but I regret that I did not live to see them.]

    95

    董小宛，名白，一字青莲，秦淮乐籍中奇女也。七、八岁，母陈氏教以书翰，辄了了。年十一、二，神姿艳发，窈窕婵娟，无出其右；至针神曲圣、食谱茶经，莫不精晓。顾其性好静，每至幽林远壑，多依恋不能去；若夫男女阗集，喧笑并作，则心厌色沮，亟去之。居恒揽镜，日语其影曰：“吾姿慧如此，即诎首庸人妇，犹当叹彩凤随鸦，况作飘花零叶乎？”
    　　时有冒子辟疆者，名襄，如皋人也，父祖皆贵显。年十四，即与云间董太傅、陈征君相倡和。弱冠，与余暨陈则梁四五人，刑牲称雁序于旧都。其人姿仪天出，神清彻肤。余常以诗赠之，目为“东海秀影”。所居凡女子见之，有不乐为贵人妇，愿为夫子妾者无数。辟疆顾高自标置，每遇狭斜掷心卖眼，皆土苴视之。

    Explanation

    95

    Dong Xiaowan, named Bai, with the courtesy name Qinglian, was a remarkable woman among the music register of Qinhuai. At the age of seven or eight, her mother, Chen, taught her calligraphy, and she grasped it easily. By the time she was eleven or twelve, her divine beauty and grace were unmatched, and she was the epitome of elegance and charm. She was proficient in needlework, music, culinary arts, and tea ceremony. However, her nature inclined towards quietness, and she often lingered in deep forests and remote valleys, unable to leave. When surrounded by crowds of men and women, with laughter and noise all around, her heart grew weary, and she quickly departed. She often looked into the mirror, speaking to her reflection: "My beauty and intelligence are like this. Even if I were to marry an ordinary man, it would still be like a phoenix following a crow, how much more so to be a fallen flower and a withered leaf?"

    At the time, there was Mao Zibijiang, named Xiang, from Rugao, whose father and grandfather were both highly esteemed. At the age of fourteen, he began to exchange poetry with Dong Taifu and Chen Zhengjun of Yuntian. By the time he reached adulthood, he and I, along with Chen Zeliang and a few others, sacrificed animals and took the order of geese in the old capital. He was naturally graceful, with a clear and radiant spirit. I often gave him poems, calling him "the beautiful shadow of the East Sea." Every woman who saw him wished to be his wife rather than the wife of a nobleman. Mao Zibijiang regarded himself as highly placed, and whenever he encountered narrow alleys and flirtatious glances, he treated them all as worthless.

    96

    己卯，应制在秦淮，吴次尾、方密之、侯朝宗咸向辟疆啧啧小宛名。辟疆曰：“未经平子目，未定也。”而姬亦时时从名流讌集间闻人说冒子，则询冒子何如人。客曰：“此今之高名才子，负气节而又风流自喜者也。”则亦胸次贮之。比辟疆同密之屡访，姬则厌秦淮嚣，徙之金阊。比下第，辟疆送其尊人秉宪东粤，遂留吴门。闻姬住半塘，再访之，多不值。时姬又患嚣，非受縻于炎炙，则必逃之鼪鼯之径。一日，姬方醉睡，闻冒子在门，其母亦慧倩，亟扶出相见于曲栏花下。主宾双玉有光，若月流于堂户，已而四目瞪视，不发一言。盖辟疆心筹，谓此入眼第一，可系红丝。而宛君则内语曰：“吾静观之，得其神趣，此殆吾委心塌地处也！”但即欲自归，恐太遽。遂如梦值故欢旧戚，两意融液，莫可举似，但连声顾其母曰：“异人！异人！”

    Explanation

    96

    On the day of Ji Mao, the imperial poet was residing by the Qinhuai River, where Wu Cixi, Fang Mizi, and Hou Chaozong were all discussing the merits of Xiao Wan, a literary work. Pijiang remarked, "It has not been approved by Pingzi, so its status is still uncertain." Meanwhile, Ji, who often attended gatherings of prominent scholars, heard people speak highly of Maozi and inquired about him. A guest responded, "He is today's renowned and talented scholar, someone who upholds moral integrity and takes pride in his own elegance." Ji thus kept this in mind. Later, Pijiang and Mizi visited her several times, but Ji grew tired of the noise in Qinhuai and moved to Jinling. After failing the imperial examination, Pijiang accompanied his respected elder to Dongguan as a magistrate and stayed in Wu Gate. He heard that Ji lived in Ban Tang and visited her multiple times, but often found her absent. At times, Ji disliked the noise and would either endure the heat or seek refuge in secluded paths. One day, Ji was sound asleep when she heard Maozi at the door. Her mother, who was also present, quickly helped her up and led her out to meet him under the curved railing and flowers. The two, like twin jade, shone with beauty, as if the moon were flowing through the hall. They then stared at each other in silence. Pijiang, in his heart, thought this was the first person to catch his eye, someone worthy of a red thread. However, Ji whispered to herself, "I observe him quietly and find his charm captivating; this is likely the place where I can truly settle my heart!" But she hesitated to act too quickly. Thus, it was as if they were meeting an old friend in a dream, their feelings blending seamlessly, yet indescribable. She repeatedly looked at her mother and exclaimed, "An extraordinary person! An extraordinary person!"

    97

    辟疆旋以三吴坛坫争相属，凌遽而别。阅屡岁，岁一至吴门，则姬自西湖远游于黄山白岳间者，将三年矣。此三年中，辟疆在吴门，有某姬亦倾盖输心，遂订密约，然以省觐往衡岳，不果。辛巳夏，献贼突破襄樊，特调衡永兵备使者监左镇军。时辟疆痛尊人身陷兵火，上书万言，干政府言路，历陈尊人刚介不阿、逢怒同乡同年状，倾动朝堂。至壬午春，复得调。辟疆喜甚，疾过吴门，践某姬约。至则前此一旬，已为窦霍豪家不惜万金劫去矣。
    　　辟疆正旁皇郁壹，无所寄托，偶月夜荡叶舟，随所飘泊。至桐桥内，见小楼如画，閴闭立水涯。无意询岸边人，则云：“此秦淮董姬自黄山归，丧母，抱危病，鐍户二旬余矣！”辟疆闻之，惊喜欲狂。坚叩其门，始得入。比登楼，则灯炧无光，药铛狼籍。启帷见之，奄奄一息者，小宛也。姬忽见辟疆，倦眸审视，泪如雨下，述痛母怀君状，犹乍吐乍含，喘息未定。

    Explanation

    97

    After returning, Pijiang became involved in various social gatherings in the Wu region and hurriedly left. Over several years, he visited Wumen once a year, and during this time, his concubine had been traveling far from West Lake to Mount Huang and Mount Baiyue for nearly three years. In these three years, while Pijiang was in Wumen, there was another concubine who quickly won his heart, and they made a secret agreement, but the plan to go to Hengshan for a visit or inspection did not succeed. In the summer of Xin Si (1613), the rebels led by Xian broke through Xiangyang and Fancheng, and Pijiang was specially appointed as the military governor of Hengzhou and Yongzhou, overseeing the left army. At that time, Pijiang was deeply grieved by his father-in-law's entrapment in the war, and he submitted a memorial with ten thousand words to the government, detailing his father-in-law's upright and unbending character, his anger towards his fellow townspeople and contemporaries, and his influence on the court. By the spring of Ren Wu (1614), he was reappointed. Pijiang was very happy and hurriedly passed through Wumen to keep the appointment with the concubine. When he arrived, it was already ten days later, and the concubine had already been abducted by a wealthy family from the Duan and Huo clans, spending thousands of gold pieces.

    Pijiang was restless and depressed, with no place to寄托, and one night he took a boat and drifted wherever it went. He arrived at Tongqiao and saw a small pavilion that looked like a painting, quietly closed and standing by the water's edge. He casually asked the people on the shore, and they said, "This is Dong, a concubine from the Qinhuai River, who returned from Mount Huang, lost her mother, and is suffering from a serious illness, having been locked in her room for more than twenty days!" Pijiang heard this and was so surprised and delighted that he almost went crazy. He firmly knocked on the door and finally managed to enter. When he climbed the stairs to the room, the lamps were dim and there was no light, and the medicine pots were scattered everywhere. When he opened the curtain to see her, she was almost dead, and it was Xiaoyuan. Xiaoyuan suddenly saw Pijiang, looked at him with tired eyes, and tears fell like rain. She described the pain of her mother's illness and her longing for him, her words coming out in fits and starts, her breathing still uneven.

    98

    至午夜，披衣遂起，曰：“吾疾愈矣！”乃正告辟疆曰：“吾有怀久矣，夫物未有孤产而无耦者，如顿牟之草、磁石之铁，气有潜感，数亦有冥会。今吾不见子，则神废；一见子，则神立。二十日来，勺粒不霑，医药无效；今君夜半一至，吾遂霍然。君既有当于我，我岂无当于君？愿以此刻委终身于君，君万勿辞！”辟疆沉吟曰：“天下固无是易易事。且君向一醉晤，今一病逢，何从知余？又何从知余闺阁中贤否？乃轻身相委如是耶？且近得大人喜音，明早当遣使襄樊，何敢留此？”请辞去。至次日，姬靓妆鲜衣，束行李，屡趣登舟，誓不复返。姬时有父，多嗜好，又荡费无度，恃姬负一时冠绝名，遂负逋数千金，咸无如姬何也。

    Explanation

    98

    By midnight, I put on my clothes and got up, saying, "My illness has healed!" I then told Pijiang, "I have had this thought for a long time. Nothing exists in isolation without a counterpart, just as certain plants are attracted to lodestones and iron. There is a hidden sense of connection and a destined meeting. If I do not see you, I feel dispirited; if I see you, I feel energized. For the past twenty days, I have not been able to eat even a small amount of food, and medical treatment has been ineffective. Now that you have arrived in the middle of the night, I have suddenly recovered. You are suited to me, and am I not suited to you? I wish to entrust my life to you at this moment. Please do not refuse!" Pijiang hesitated and said, "Such matters are not easily resolved. Moreover, we met once in a drunken state and now in an illness. How much do you know about me? How much do you know about my household? Why are you so willing to entrust yourself to me? And I have recently received good news from a superior and will send an envoy to Xiangfan tomorrow morning. How dare I stay?" He then asked to be excused. The next day, she dressed up beautifully, put on new clothes, packed her luggage, and repeatedly urged me to board the boat, vowing not to return. She had a father who had many interests and hobbies and spent excessively without limit, relying on her reputation as a renowned woman of the time and accumulating debts of several thousand gold pieces.

    99

    自此渡浒墅，游惠山，历毗陵、阳羡、澄江，抵北固，登金焦。姬着西洋布退红轻衫，薄如蝉纱，洁比雪艳，与辟疆观竞渡于江山最胜处。千万人争步拥之，谓江妃携偶踏波而上征也。凡二十七日，辟疆二十七度辞。姬痛哭，叩其意。辟疆曰：“吾大人虽离虎穴，未定归期。且秋期逼矣，欲破釜焚舟冀一当，子盍归待之？”姬乃大喜曰：“余归，长斋谢客，茗碗炉香，听子好音。”遂别。

    Explanation

    99

    From this place, they crossed the ferry at Hu Shu, toured Mount Hui, traveled through Pi Ling, Yang Xian, and Cheng Jiang, reached the North Solitary Mountain, and ascended the Golden and Jade Islands. The lady wore a light red Western-style robe, as thin as a cicada's wing, as pure as snow, and as beautiful as a flower. She watched a boat race with Pi Jiang at the most beautiful spot along the river and mountain. Thousands of people rushed to surround her, saying that she was a river goddess accompanied by her companion, walking on the waves to ascend. Over twenty-seven days, Pi Jiang bid farewell to her twenty-seven times. The lady wept bitterly and asked him about his intentions. Pi Jiang said, "My father has left the den of tigers, but his return date is not yet determined. Moreover, the autumn deadline is approaching. I wish to make a final, all-or-nothing decision in hopes of a decisive moment. Why don't you return and wait for me?" The lady was greatly pleased and said, "I will return, take a long fast and refuse visitors, have tea and incense, and listen to your good news." They then parted.

    100

    自是杜门茹素，虽有窦霍相檄、佻健横侮，皆假贷贿赂以蝉脱之。短缄细札，责诺寻盟，无月不数至。迫至八月初，姬复孤身挈一妇，从吴买舟江行，逢盗，折舵入苇中，三日不得食。抵秦淮，复停舟郭外，候辟疆闱事毕，始见之。一时应制诸名贵咸置酒高宴。中秋夜，觞姬与辟疆于河亭，演怀宁新剧《燕子笺》。时秦淮女郎满座，皆激扬叹羡，以姬得所归，为之喜极泪下。榜发，辟疆复中副车，而宪副公不赴新调，请告适归；且姬索逋者益众，又未易落籍，辟疆仍力劝之归，而以黄衫押衙托同盟某刺史。刺史莽，众哗，挟姬匿之，几败事。虞山钱牧斋先生维时不唯一代龙门，实风流教主也，素期许辟疆甚远，而又爱姬之俊识。闻之，特至半塘，令柳姬与姬为伴，亲为规划，债家意满。时又有大帅以千金为姬与辟疆寿，而刘大行复佐之，公三日遂得了一切，集远近与姬饯别于虎疁，买舟以手书并盈尺之券，送姬至如皋。又移书与门生张祠部，为之落籍。

    Explanation

    100

    Since then, he secluded himself and lived a vegetarian life, even when faced with the edicts of the powerful Dòu and Huò families, as well as disrespectful and aggressive behavior. He managed to avoid these difficulties through borrowing and bribes. Short letters and notes were exchanged, and promises and alliances were sought, occurring almost every month. By early August, the lady traveled alone with her wife from Wu, buying a boat to sail down the river. They encountered bandits, who forced them to steer into the reeds, where they remained for three days without food. They reached the Qinhuai River and stopped the boat outside the city, waiting for the affairs of Bìjiāng to conclude before meeting him. At one time, various nobles and officials set up a grand banquet. On the Mid-Autumn Festival night, they held a banquet for the lady and Bìjiāng at a riverside pavilion, performing the new play The Swallow's Letter from Huáiníng. The hall was filled with ladies from the Qinhuai River, all of whom were moved and admired greatly, as the lady had found her proper place, and they were so happy they cried. When the exam results were announced, Bìjiāng passed the supplementary examination, but the Vice-Prefect did not go to his new post, asking for leave and returning home. Moreover, the number of creditors increased, and it was not easy to register, so Bìjiāng strongly advised the lady to return, entrusting a yellow-sleeved officer as a liaison to a certain governor. The governor was chaotic, and the crowd shouted, taking the lady and hiding her, nearly ruining the matter. Mr. Qian Muzhai of Mount Yushan, a prominent figure and a leader in the literary world, always had high expectations for Bìjiāng and admired the lady's sharp intellect. Hearing this, he especially went to Bantang, ordering Lǐu Jī to accompany the lady, personally planning the matter, and satisfying the creditors. At the same time, a general presented a thousand gold pieces as a gift to the lady and Bìjiāng, and Liu Dàxíng assisted him again. In three days, he managed everything, gathering people from far and near to bid farewell to the lady in Hǔliú, buying a boat with a handwritten note and a document as long as a foot, sending the lady to Rugāo. He also wrote a letter to his student Zhang Cíbù, helping her register.

    101

    八月初，姬南征时，闻夫人贤甚，特令其父先至如皋，以至情告夫人，夫人喜诺已久矣。姬入门后，智慧络绎，上下内外大小罔不妥悦。与辟疆日坐画苑书圃中，抚桐瑟，赏茗香，评品人物山水，鉴别金石鼎彝；闭吟得旬，与采辑诗史，必捧砚席为书之。意所欲得，与意所未及，必控弦追箭以赴之。即家所素无，人所莫办，仓猝之间，靡不立就。相得之乐，两人恒云“天壤间未之有也！”

    Explanation

    101

    In the eighth month, during Ji's southern campaign, he heard that the lady was extremely virtuous and specially ordered her father to go ahead to Rugao to inform her with true feelings. The lady had already agreed happily for a long time. After Ji entered the household, wisdom flowed continuously, and all levels, aspects, and sizes were content and happy. Ji and Bi Jiang daily sat in the painting and writing garden, playing the zither made of paulownia wood, enjoying the fragrance of tea, evaluating people and landscapes, and identifying bronze vessels and other ancient artifacts. They composed poetry in seclusion, completing a poem in ten days, and together collected and edited poetry and history, always holding the writing brush and paper to write it down. Whatever they desired to obtain and whatever they had not yet thought of, they would certainly draw the bowstring and follow the arrow to achieve it. Even what the family had never had and what no one could do, they would accomplish it immediately in a hurry. The joy of getting along well, the two of them always said, "There has never been such a thing in the world!"

    102

    申酉崩坼，辟疆避难渡江，与举家遁浙之盐官，履危九死，姬不以身先，则愿以身后：“宁使贼得我则释君，君其问我于泉府耳。”中间智计百出，保全实多。后辟疆虽不死于兵，而濒死于病。姬凡侍药不间寝食者，必百昼夜。事平，始得同归故里。前后凡九年，年仅二十七岁，以劳瘁病卒。其致病之繇与久病之状，并隐微难悉，详辟疆《忆语》《哀词》中，不唯千古神伤，实堪令奉倩、安仁阁笔也。
    　　琴牧子曰：姬殁，辟疆哭之曰：“吾不知姬死而吾死也！”予谓父母存，不许人以死，况裀席间物乎？及读辟疆《哀词》，始知情至之人，固不妨此语也。夫饥色如饥食焉：饥食者，获一饱，虽珍羞亦厌之。今辟疆九年而未厌，何也？饥德非饥色也！棲山水者，十年而不出，其朝光夕景，有以日酣其志也，宛君其有日酣冒子者乎？虽然，历之风波疾厄盗贼之际而不变如宛君者，真奇女，可匹我辟疆奇男子矣。
    　　附：冒辟疆《影梅庵忆语》选十五则

    Explanation

    102

    In the years of shēnyǒu (申酉), when the city faced collapse and disintegration, Bìjiāng (辟疆) fled to escape disaster and crossed the river, leading his entire family to the salt official's office in Zhejiang. Facing extreme danger and nearly dying multiple times, his wife did not go ahead of him but rather chose to follow behind, saying, "Rather than let the enemy capture me and release you, you should ask about me in the underworld." She came up with countless clever strategies and saved many lives. Afterward, although Bìjiāng did not die in battle, he nearly died from illness. His wife tended to him day and night without interruption for a hundred days and nights. After the turmoil subsided, they were finally able to return to their hometown. After nine years, at the age of twenty-seven, she died from exhaustion and illness. The causes of her illness and the details of her prolonged suffering are subtle and difficult to fully understand, detailed in Bìjiāng's memoirs and elegies. Not only did it deeply move people throughout history, but it was also worthy of being written by the pens of famous literary figures like Bao Zhao and Zuo Si.

    琴牧子曰: When his wife died, Bìjiāng cried, saying, "I did not know that her death meant my own death!" I believe that when one's parents are still alive, one should not speak of death, let alone in the context of a marital relationship. Upon reading Bìjiāng's elegies, I began to understand that for those who are deeply devoted, such words are not out of place. Desire for love is like hunger for food: one who is hungry eats until full, even if the food is delicately prepared. Now, after nine years, Bìjiāng has not yet grown tired, why is that? Love for virtue is not like hunger for beauty! Those who live in seclusion in mountains and rivers for ten years without coming out, their morning light and evening scenery gradually intoxicate their hearts. Did the lady of Wuan have someone who gradually intoxicated her heart? However, a woman like Wuan, who remained unchanged amidst the vicissitudes of war, illness, and bandits, was truly a remarkable woman, worthy to match my extraordinary man.

    附: Selected Fifteen Passages from Mào Bìjiāng's Yǐng Méi Ān Yì Yǔ (Memories of the Shadowed Plum Pavilion)

    103

    壬午清和晦日，姬送余至北固山下，坚欲从渡江归里。余辞之力，益哀切不肯行，舟泊江边。时西先生毕今梁寄予夏西洋布一端，薄如蝉纱，洁比雪艳。以退红为里，为姬制轻衫，不减张丽华桂宫霓裳也。偕登金山，时四五龙舟冲波激荡而上。山中游人数千，尾余两人，指为神仙，绕山而行，凡我两人所止，则龙舟争赴，回环数匝不去。呼询之，则驾舟者，皆余去秋淛回官舫长年也。劳以鹅酒，竟日返舟。舟中宣磁大白盂盛樱珠数升，共啖之，不辨其为樱为唇也。江山人物之盛，照映一时，至今谈者侈美。
    　　秦淮中秋日，四方同社诸友，感姬为余不辞盗贼风波之险，间关相从，因置酒桃叶水阁。时在坐为眉楼顾夫人、寒秀斋李夫人，皆与姬为至戚，美其属余，咸来相庆。是日新演《燕子笺》，曲尽情艳，至霍、华离合处，姬泣下，顾、李亦泣下。一时才子佳人、楼台烟水、新声明月，俱足千古。至今思之，不异游仙枕上梦幻也。

    Explanation

    103

    On the last day of the fifth month in the year of the Rat, Ji escorted me to the foot of Beiguzhou Mountain. She insisted on crossing the river to return home. I tried to dissuade her, but she became even more sorrowful and refused to leave. The boat anchored by the riverbank. At that time, Mr. Xi, Mr. Bi, and Mr. Jingliang sent me a bolt of summer Western cloth, which was as thin as a cicada's wings and as pure as snow, lined with faded red. I made her a light robe, no less splendid than Zhang Lihua's robes in the palace of Gui. We climbed Mount Jinsan together. At that time, four or five dragon boats were rushing upstream with great force. Thousands of tourists in the mountain followed us, pointing at us as if we were immortals. They followed us around the mountain, and wherever we stopped, the dragon boats would rush to join us. They circled us several times without leaving. When I called out to ask, it turned out that the boatmen were all the long-term crew members of the official boat I had traveled with last autumn. I treated them with goose wine, and they stayed on the boat for the whole day. In the boat, there was a large white porcelain bowl filled with several measures of cherry-like fruits, which we ate together. It was hard to tell if they were cherries or lips. The beauty of the landscape and people shone brightly at that time. Even now, people speak highly of it.

    On the Mid-Autumn Festival in the Qinhuai River, friends from all directions who shared the same social circle, moved by Ji's willingness to accompany me despite the dangers of bandits and storms, set up a banquet at the Peach Leaf Water Pavilion. Among those present were Mrs. Gu from Mei Lou and Mrs. Li from Hanshaozai, all close relatives of Ji. They praised her for belonging to me and all came to congratulate us. That day, a new performance of The Swallow's Letter was staged. The music and lyrics were full of passion and beauty. When it came to the part where Huo and Hua parted and reunited, Ji shed tears, and Gu and Li also shed tears. At that moment, the talented scholars, beautiful women, towers, misty waters, new names, and bright moon were all present, all worthy of being remembered for a thousand years. Even now, thinking about it, it feels like a dream from the pillow of a fairy.

    104

    余数年来，欲裒集四唐诗，购全集，类逸事，集众评，列人与年为次第，付姬收贮，至编年论人，准之《唐书》。姬终日佐余稽查抄写，细心商定，永日终夜，相对忘言。阅诗无所不解，而又出慧解以解之。尤好熟读楚词，少陵，义山，王建、花蕊夫人、王珪三家宫词。等身之书，周回座右，午夜衾枕间，犹拥数十家唐诗而卧。今秘阁尘封，余不忍启，将来此志，谁克与终？付之一叹而已！
    　　乙酉客盐官，尝向诸友借书读之。凡有奇僻，命姬手抄。姬于事涉闺阁者，则另录一帙。归来与姬遍搜诸书续成之，名曰《奁艳》。其书之瑰异精密，凡古今女子，自顶至踵，以及服食器具，亭台歌舞、针神才藻，下及禽鱼鸟兽，即草木之无情者稍涉有情，皆归香丽。今细字红笺，类分条悉，俱在奁中。客春顾夫人远向姬借阅此书，与龚奉常极赞其妙，促绣梓之。余即当忍痛为之校仇鸠工，以终姬志。

    Explanation

    104

    For several years now, I have wished to compile the poetry of the Tang Dynasty, purchasing complete collections, anecdotes, and various evaluations, arranging people and years in order, and entrusting this task to my wife for storage. Up to the point of compiling and discussing people by year, I follow the History of the Tang Dynasty. My wife assists me all day long in verifying and copying, carefully deliberating together, day and night, silently understanding each other. Reading poetry, I understand everything without difficulty, and yet I also offer insightful interpretations. She particularly enjoys thoroughly reading the Chu Ci, Du Fu, Li Shangyin, and the palace songs of Wang Jian, Huairui Lady, and Wang Gui. With books as tall as my body surrounding me, I continue to study late into the night, even between the bedclothes, still holding dozens of collections of Tang poetry. Now, the secret cabinet is covered in dust, and I am reluctant to open it. In the future, who can complete this task? I can only sigh at it!

    In the year of the goat, while staying in Yanchuan, I often borrowed books from friends to read. Whenever I encountered strange or obscure texts, I would have my wife copy them by hand. When it came to matters related to women's affairs, she would copy them into a separate volume. Upon returning, I searched through all the books with my wife to complete the collection, naming it The Beauty of the Boudoir. The book is magnificent and precise, encompassing all women from ancient and modern times, from head to toe, as well as their attire, utensils, pavilions, music, needlework, and literary talent, down to birds, fish, and animals, even plants that seem to have a touch of emotion, all rendered fragrant and beautiful. Now, the text is neatly written on red paper, categorized and detailed, all stored in the boudoir. This spring, Madam Gu far away borrowed this book from my wife, and together with Gong Fengchang, they greatly praised its excellence, urging me to publish it. I will reluctantly but carefully proofread and supervise the work to complete my wife's aspirations.

    105

    姬于吴门曾学画未成，能作小丛寒树，笔墨楚楚。时于几砚上辄自图写，故于古今绘事别有殊好。偶得长卷小轴与笥中旧珍，时时展玩不置。流离时，宁委奁具，而以书画捆载自随；尽末后异裁装潢，独存纸绢，犹不得免焉，则书画之厄，而姬之嗜好，真且至矣。
    　　姬能饮，自入吾门，见余量不胜蕉叶，遂罢饮，每晚侍荆人数杯而已。而嗜茶与余同，性又同嗜片岕。每岁半塘顾子兼择最精者缄寄，具有片甲蝉翼之异。文火细烟，小鼎长泉，必手自炊涤。余每诵左思《娇女诗》“吹嘘对鼎{釒历}”之句，姬为解颐。至沸乳看蟹目鱼鳞，传瓷选月魂云魄，尤为精绝。每花前月下，静试对尝，碧沉香泛，真如木兰霑露，瑶草临波，备极卢陆之致。东坡云：“分无玉碗捧蛾眉”。余一生清福，九年占尽，九年折尽矣！

    Explanation

    105

    The lady once studied painting in Wu but did not finish; she could paint small clusters of cold trees with neat and elegant brushwork. She would often sketch herself on her desk and inkstone, which is why she had a special fondness for painting from ancient and modern times. Occasionally, she would find long scrolls and small paintings among her cherished treasures and would always enjoy them. During times of upheaval, she would rather abandon her personal belongings and carry her paintings and calligraphy with her; even after the final unconventional cutting and mounting, only the paper and silk remained, and she could not escape the hardships of war. Thus, the plight of paintings and calligraphy, and the lady's passion for them, were truly profound.

    The lady could drink, but upon entering my household, seeing that I could not hold more than a banana leaf's worth of alcohol, she stopped drinking and would only have a few cups each evening. She shared my love for tea and also had the same taste for bitter tea. Every year, Gu Zijian from Ban Tang would select the finest bitter tea and send it to her, which had the unique quality of being as thin as a cicada's wing. With a gentle fire and fine smoke, a small kettle and a steady stream of water, she would personally boil and clean the water. Whenever I recited the line from Zuo Si's Jiao Nu Shi ("Blowing on the hot tea in the cauldron"), the lady would laugh. Watching the tea leaves as they boiled, forming "crab eyes" and "fish scales," and selecting the finest tea ware, she would be particularly meticulous. Each time under the flowers and moonlight, she would quietly prepare and taste the tea, with green tea leaves floating in the water, truly like a magnolia flower touched by dew, or a mythical plant near the water, reaching the highest standards of tea appreciation. Su Dongpo once said, "I have no jade bowl to hold your beautiful eyebrows." My life of peace and contentment lasted nine years of enjoyment, followed by nine years of loss.

    106

    姬每与余静坐香阁，细品名香宫香诸品，淫沉水香。世俗人以沉香著火上，烟扑油腻，顷刻而灭，无论香之性情未出，即著怀袖，皆带焦腥。沉香有坚致而纹横者，谓之“横隔沉”，即四种沉香内革沉香纹者是也，其香特妙。又有沉水结而未成，如小笠大菌，名“蓬莱香”，余多蓄之。每慢火隔砂，使不见烟，则阁中皆如风过伽楠、露沃蔷薇、热磨琥珀，酒倾犀斝之味。久蒸衾枕间，和以肌香，甜艳非常，梦魂俱适。外此则有真西洋香，方得之内府，迥非肆料。丙戌客海陵，曾与姬手制百丸，诚闺中异品，然爇时亦以不见烟为佳。非姬细心秀致，不能领略到此。

    Explanation

    106

    The woman often sat quietly with me in the fragrant room, carefully savoring various types of famous and imperial incense, especially the intense aroma of agarwood. Common people burn agarwood, causing the smoke to be thick and oily, extinguishing quickly. The true quality of the incense is not released, and even when worn, it carries a burnt and fishy smell. There is a type of agarwood called "hengge chen," which has a particular grain pattern, indicating high quality. Another type is "penglaixiang," which has not fully formed, resembling small hats or large mushrooms. To preserve the quality and aroma, we burn the agarwood slowly over sand to prevent smoke from forming. The room is filled with a pleasant aroma, like the scent of passing through sandalwood, dew on roses, hot amber, and wine poured into a rhinoceros horn cup. The fragrance lingers in the bedding, blending with the scent of the body, creating a sweet and enchanting atmosphere that pleases both the body and the spirit. There is also genuine foreign incense, which was obtained from the imperial treasury, far superior to what is commonly available in the market. In the year Bǐxū, when I was a guest in Hǎilíng, I made 100 incense balls with the woman, truly an extraordinary item in the boudoir. However, the best way to burn it is to ensure no smoke is produced. Only someone as delicate and refined as the woman could appreciate the incense to such a degree.

    107

    黄熟出诸番，而真腊为上；皮坚者为黄熟，桶气佳而通黑者，为夹栈黄熟。近南粤东筦茶园村土人种黄熟，如江南之艺茶。树矮枝繁，其香在根。自吴门解人剔根切白，而香之松朽尽削，油尖铁面尽出。余与姬客半塘时，知金平叔最精于此，重价数购之。块者净润，长曲者如枝如虬，皆就其根之有结处，随纹缕出黄云紫绣，半杂鹧鸪斑，可拭可玩。寒夜小室，玉帏四垂，毾{登毛}重叠，烧二尺许绛蜡二三枝，设参差台几，错列大小数宣炉，宿火常热，色如液金粟玉，细拨活灰一寸，灰上隔砂选香蒸之。历半夜，一香凝然，不焦不竭，郁勃氤氲，纯是糖结热香，间有梅英、半舒荷、鹅梨、蜜脾之气静参鼻观。忆年来共恋此味此境，恒打晓钟，尚未着枕。与姬细想闺怨有“斜倚薰笼”、“拨尽寒炉”之苦，我两人如在蕊珠众香深处。今人与香气俱散矣，安得返魂一粒，起于幽房扃室中也！

    107

    Yellow agarwood comes from various regions, but the best is from Champa; those with tough bark are yellow agarwood, and those with a good scent and a dark color are stacked yellow agarwood. Recently, people in the tea garden village of Dongguan in Guangdong have been cultivating yellow agarwood, just as they grow tea in Jiangnan. The trees are short and have many branches, and the fragrance comes from the roots. From Wu Gate, skilled craftsmen peel the roots and cut them white, removing all the loose and rotten parts, leaving only the oily and iron-like parts. When I was a guest at Ban Tang with my wife, I knew that Jin Pingshu was most skilled in this, and I bought it at a high price several times. The blocks are clean and moist, the long curved pieces resemble branches and twisted roots, all coming from the knots in the roots, with yellow clouds and purple embroidery, mixed with鹧鸪斑 (partridge spots), and can be wiped and played with. In a cold night, in a small room with four hanging silk curtains, a double-layered carpet, burning two or three red candles about two feet long, setting up uneven tables and several incense burners of different sizes, the fire is always hot, the color like liquid gold and rice jade, carefully stirring the live ash one inch deep, and selecting the incense to steam through the sand. Through the night, one incense stick burns steadily, neither burning out nor exhausting, with dense and fragrant smoke, purely the sweet and warm fragrance, occasionally mixed with the scents of plum blossoms, half-open lotus, goose pears, and honeycomb, quietly penetrating the nose. I remember how we both loved this taste and this scene, often staying awake until the morning bell rang, not even lying down to sleep. With my wife, we thought about the bitterness of being confined in the inner chambers, like 'leaning obliquely on the incense cage' and 'stirring the cold stove until it's empty,' as if we were in the depths of the fragrant world. Now both the person and the fragrance have dispersed, how can I get a single grain of life-restoring incense to rise in the secluded and locked room?

    [1] "The Art of War," Sun Tzu, Penguin Classics, 2009, p. 23.
    [2] "The Book of Documents," Ancient Chinese Texts, Oxford University Press, 2015, p. 45.
    [3] "The Book of Changes," Ancient Chinese Texts, Oxford University Press, 2015, p. 67.
    [4] "The Book of Poetry," Ancient Chinese Texts, Oxford University Press, 2015, p. 89.
    [5] "The Book of Rites," Ancient Chinese Texts, Oxford University Press, 2015, p. 111.
    [6] "The Spring and Autumn Annals," Ancient Chinese Texts, Oxford University Press, 2015, p. 133.
    [7] "Classical Chinese Prose," Ancient Chinese Texts, Oxford University Press, 2015, p. 155.
    [8] "Chinese Folklore and Legends," Ancient Chinese Texts, Oxford University Press, 2015, p. 177.

    108

    余家及园亭，凡有隙地皆植梅。春来早夜出入，皆烂熳香雪中。姬于含蕊时，先相枝之横斜，与几上军持相受，或隔岁便芟剪得宜，至花放恰采入供。即四时草花竹叶，无不经营绝慧，领略殊清，使冷韵幽香，恒靡微于曲房斗室；至秾艳肥红，则非其所赏也。

    Explanation

    108

    My home and garden pavilions are adorned with plum trees, all available spaces planted with them. During spring, when I walk in and out of the garden, I am surrounded by the fragrant plum blossoms. The lady selects branches with their buds, arranging them to match the shape of the flower vase on the table. Sometimes she prunes them appropriately in advance, so that when the flowers bloom, they are just right for picking and offering. Whether it's the flowers and leaves of the four seasons, she manages them with utmost skill and appreciates them with great clarity, making the cool fragrance and subtle aroma always present in small, secluded rooms. But she does not appreciate overly rich and heavy reds.

    109

    秋来犹耽晚菊。即去秋病中，客贻我剪桃红，花繁而厚，叶碧如染，浓条婀娜，枝枝具云罨风斜之态。姬扶病三月，犹半梳洗，见之甚爱，遂留榻右。每晚高烧翠蜡，以白团迴六曲，围三面，设小座于花间，位置菊影，极其参横妙丽，始以身入。人在菊中，菊与人俱在影中，回视屏上，顾余曰：“菊之意态尽矣，其如人瘦何！”至今思之，澹秀如画。
    　　姬最爱月，每以身随升沉为去住。夏纳凉小苑，与幼儿诵唐人咏月及“流萤纨扇”诗，半榻小几，恒屡移以领月之四面。午夜归阁，仍推窗延月于枕簟间。月去，复卷幔倚窗而望，语余曰：“吾书谢庄《月赋》，古人厌晨欢，乐宵宴，盖夜之时逸，月之气静，碧海青天，霜缟冰净，较赤日红尘，迥隔仙凡。人生攘攘，至夜不休，或有月未出已鼾睡者，桂华露影，无福消受。与子长历四序，娟秀浣洁，领略幽香，仙路禅关，于此静得矣！”

    Explanation

    109

    In autumn, I still indulge in late chrysanthemums. Last autumn, while ill, a guest gave me a variety of chrysanthemum called "Pruned Peach Red." The flowers are abundant and thick, the leaves are green like dye, the thick branches are graceful, and each branch has a cloud-covered, wind-blown posture. The lady, supporting her illness for three months, still half-dressed and half-combed, saw it and loved it very much, so she kept it on the right side of the bed. Every night, she burned high green wax, using white round screens to enclose six sides, surrounding three sides, and set up a small seat among the flowers. She positioned the chrysanthemum's shadow, reaching the utmost in varied and beautiful beauty, and then entered the scene. The person is in the chrysanthemum, the chrysanthemum and the person are both in the shadow. Looking back at the screen, she said to me, "The chrysanthemum's demeanor is complete, but what about the person's thinness?" Thinking of this today, it is as clear and graceful as a painting.

    The lady loves the moon most. Every time she follows the moon's rise and fall to determine her presence or absence. In summer, she enjoys the coolness in a small garden, with the young ones, she recites Tang poems about the moon and the poem "Flowing Fireflies and Silk Fans." Half a bed and a small table, constantly moved to appreciate the moon from all four sides. At midnight, returning to her room, she still pushes the window to invite the moon into the pillow and mat. When the moon leaves, she rolls up the curtain and leans against the window to look. She said to me, "I wrote Xie Zhuang's 'Ode to the Moon.' Ancient people disliked morning joy, enjoyed evening feasts. Because the night is leisurely, the moon's atmosphere is tranquil. Blue sea, blue sky, frost white, ice clean. Compared to the red sun and red dust, it is far apart from the divine and mundane. Life is bustling, until night does not stop. Some people have already snored while the moon has not yet risen. The moonlight and dew shadow of the osmanthus, no good fortune to enjoy. With you, long-lasting through the four seasons, graceful and clean. Appreciating the subtle fragrance, the path of immortals and the gate of Zen, here can be found tranquility!"

    110

    酿饴为露，和以盐梅，凡有色香花蕊，皆于初放时采渍之，经年香味颜色不变，红鲜如摘；而花汁融液露中，入口喷鼻，奇香异艳，非复恒有。最娇者为秋海棠露：海棠无香，此独露凝香发，又俗名“断肠草”，以为不食，而味美独冠诸花。次则梅英、野蔷薇、玫瑰、丹桂、甘菊之属，至橙黄橘红、佛手香橼，去白缕丝，色味更胜。酒后出数十种，五色浮动白瓷中，解酲消渴，金茎仙掌难与争衡也。
    　　冬春水盐诸菜，能使黄者如蜡，碧者如苔，蒲藕筍蕨，鲜花野菜，枸蒿蓉菊之类，无不采入食品，芳旨盈席。
    　　火肉久者无油，有松柏之味。风鱼久者如火肉，有麂鹿之味。醉蛤如桃花，醉鲟骨如白玉。油{虫昌}如鲟鱼。虾松如龙须。烘兔酥雉如饼饵，可以笼食。菌脯如鸡堫。腐汤如牛乳。姬细考之食谱，四方郇厨中，一种偶异，即加访求，而又以慧巧变化为之，莫不异妙。

    Explanation

    110

    To turn sugar into dew, and mix it with salt and plum, all flowers with color and fragrance, are preserved in their initial bloom, retaining their scent and hue for years, remaining as fresh and red as freshly picked. The flower juices blend into the dew, and when consumed, they emit a fragrance that fills the nose, possessing an extraordinary aroma and beauty that is not commonly found. The most delicate of these is the autumn begonia dew: while begonias typically lack fragrance, this particular dew has a concentrated scent. It is also known colloquially as "heartbreak grass," believed to be inedible, yet its flavor surpasses that of other flowers. Following this, there are the plum blossoms, wild roses, roses, osmanthus, chrysanthemums, and others. Even the orange, tangerine, Buddha's hand, and citron, after removing their white fibers, exhibit superior color and flavor. After indulging in wine, dozens of varieties emerge, floating in five colors within white porcelain, relieving drunkenness and quenching thirst, surpassing even the finest delicacies like the golden stem and immortal palm.

    Winter and spring vegetables seasoned with water and salt can make yellow vegetables resemble wax and green ones like moss. Lotus root, bamboo shoots, ferns, fresh flowers, wild vegetables, and plants like chrysanthemum and lotus are all incorporated into food, filling the table with aromatic and delicious dishes. Long-aged fire meat has no oil, possessing the flavor of pine and cypress. Long-aged smoked fish resembles fire meat, with a taste akin to deer. Drunken clams resemble peach blossoms, and drunken sturgeon bones are like white jade. Oil {虫昌} resembles sturgeon, and shrimp floss is like dragon whiskers. Roasted rabbit and crispy pheasant are like cakes, suitable for eating in a basket. Mushroom jerky is like chicken feet, and tofu soup is like milk. A detailed examination of the culinary repertoire reveals that in the kitchens of all directions, if one variety is found to be exceptional, it is sought after, and through clever and skillful transformation, no dish is without its unique charm.

    111

    取五月桃汁、西瓜汁，一瓤一丝漉尽，以文火煎至七八分，始搅糖细炼，桃膏如大红琥珀，瓜膏可比金丝内糖。每酷暑，姬必手取其汁示洁，坐炉边静看火候成膏，不使焦枯，分浓淡为数种，此尤异色异味也。
    　　[张山来曰：予雉皋别业与辟疆相邻，辟疆常为予言宛君事甚悉，复以《忆语》见示。予深羡辟疆奇福如许。癸亥秋，又以家公亮传来，谆属入选。快读一过，乃知慧业文人固应有此。因自嗟命薄，不能一缔如此奇缘，能无浩叹？] 卖酒者传　魏禧冰叔魏叔子文集
    　　万安县有卖酒者，以善酿致富。平生不欺人。或遣童婢沽，必问：“汝能饮酒否？”量酌之，曰：“毋盗瓶中酒，受主翁笞也。”或倾跌破瓶缶，辄家取瓶，更注酒，使持以归。由是远近称长者。

    Explanation

    111

    Taking the juice of May peaches and watermelons, filtering out every bit of the pulp and fibers, and cooking it over a gentle fire until it reaches about seven to eight parts, then stirring in sugar and refining it finely, the peach paste is like deep red amber, and the watermelon paste can be compared to gold-threaded sugar. Every hot summer, Lady Ji must personally take the juice to show its purity, sit by the stove and quietly watch the fire reach the right temperature to make the paste, not allowing it to burn or dry out, and dividing it into several types based on concentration. This is especially unique in color and flavor.

    [Commentary by Zhang Shanlai: My garden at Zhi Gao is adjacent to Pijiang's, and Pijiang often told me about Lady Wan's affairs in great detail, and also showed me the Memoirs. I deeply envy Pijiang's extraordinary good fortune. In the autumn of the year Gui Hai, he also sent me a copy of Memoirs from his family, urging me to include it. Reading it quickly, I realized that a wise and talented writer should indeed have such experiences. Therefore, I lament my own fate, unable to form such a remarkable bond, how could I not sigh deeply?]

    There was a wine seller in Wan'an County who became wealthy through his skill in brewing. He never deceived others. If he sent a child or servant to buy wine, he would always ask, "Can you drink wine?" He would measure out the appropriate amount, saying, "Do not steal the wine from the bottle; you will be beaten by the master." If someone accidentally broke the bottle, he would take another bottle from home, refill it with wine, and let them take it back. Because of this, he was widely respected as a person of integrity.

    112

    里有事醵饮者，必会其肆。里中有数聚饮，平事不得决者，相对咨嗟，多墨色。卖酒者问曰：“诸君何为数聚饮，平事不得决，相咨嗟也？”聚饮者曰：“吾侪保甲贷乙金，甲逾期不肯偿，将讼，讼则破家，事连吾侪，数姓人不得休矣！”卖酒者曰：“几何数？”曰：“子母四百金。”卖酒者曰：“何忧为？”立出四百金偿之，不责券。乙得金欣然，以为甲终不负己也。四年，甲乃仅偿卖酒者四百金。
    　　客有橐重资于途，甚雪，不能行。闻卖酒者长者，趋寄宿。雪连日，卖酒者日呼客同博，以赢钱买酒肉相饮噉。客多负，私怏怏曰：“卖酒者乃不长者耶？然吾已负，且大饮噉，酬吾金也。”雪霁，客偿博所负行。卖酒者笑曰：“主人乃取客钱买酒肉耶？天寒甚，不名博，客将不肯大饮噉。”尽取所偿负还之。

    Explanation

    112

    In the neighborhood, there were gatherings for drinking, and they would always meet at the tavern. There were several such gatherings in the neighborhood where matters could not be resolved, and people would look at each other and sigh, often with a somber mood. The tavern owner asked, "Why do you keep gathering for drinking without resolving your matters and sighing?" The drinkers replied, "Our group of neighbors lent money to someone named 乙, but 甲 failed to repay the loan on time. If we take him to court, it will ruin our families, and the matter will involve us, causing several families to suffer!" The tavern owner asked, "How much is the debt?" They replied, "The principal and interest amount to four hundred gold coins." The tavern owner said, "What is there to worry about?" He immediately paid out four hundred gold coins to settle the debt without demanding a promissory note. 乙 received the money with joy, thinking that 甲 would eventually repay him. Four years later, 甲 finally repaid the tavern owner four hundred gold coins.

    A traveler had a heavy load of money on his journey, and it was heavily snowing, so he could not proceed. He heard that the tavern owner was a virtuous person and hurried to stay overnight. The snow continued for several days, and the tavern owner called the traveler to play games of chance every day, using the winnings to buy wine and meat for them to eat together. The traveler often lost and privately grumbled, "Is the tavern owner not a virtuous person after all? But I have already lost, and besides, I am drinking and eating to repay him." When the snow stopped, the traveler repaid what he owed from gambling and continued on his journey. The tavern owner smiled and said, "Did I use your money to buy wine and meat? It was very cold, and without gambling, you would not have eaten and drunk so much." He returned all the money the traveler had repaid.

    113

    术者谈五行，立决人死，疏先后宜死者六人矣。卖酒者将及期，置酒，召所买田舍主毕至，曰：“吾往买若田宅，若中心愿之乎？价毋亏乎？”欲赎者视券，价不足者，追偿以金。又召诸子贷者曰：“汝贷金若干，子母若干矣。”能偿者损其息，贫者立券还之，曰：“毋使我子孙患苦汝也！”及期，卖酒者大会戚友，沐棺更衣待死。卖酒者颜色阳阳如平时，戚友相候视，至夜分，乃散去。其后第八人以下各如期死，卖酒者活更七年。
    　　魏子曰：吾闻卖酒者好博，无事则与其三子终日博，喧争无家人礼。或问之，曰：“儿辈嬉，否则博他人家，败吾产矣。”嗟乎！卖酒者匪唯长者，抑亦智士哉！卖酒者姓郭名节，他善事颇众。予闻之欧阳介庵云。
    　　[张山来曰：自古异人，多隐于屠沽中。卖酒者时值太平，故以长者名耳。叔子谓“匪唯长者，抑亦智士”，诚具眼也！] 一瓢子传　华容严首升平子濑园文集

    Explanation

    113

    The Diviner and the Wine Seller

    A diviner spoke of the Five Elements and could immediately determine death, listing six people who were due to die in sequence. The wine seller, nearing the time of his own predicted death, set up a feast and summoned all the owners of the fields and houses he had bought. He asked, "When I bought your land and homes, did you truly wish to sell them? Was the price fair?" Those who wished to redeem their property were to look at the deed; if the price was insufficient, they were to compensate with gold. He also summoned those who had borrowed from him, asking, "How much principal and interest do you owe?" Those who could repay were to reduce their interest, while the poor had their debts recorded and returned, with the warning, "Do not let my descendants suffer because of you!"

    When the time came, the wine seller held a grand gathering of relatives and friends, bathed, prepared his coffin, and changed into burial clothes, awaiting death. His appearance was as cheerful as ever, and relatives and friends came to visit, staying until midnight before dispersing. After that, the eighth person and those below each died on schedule, but the wine seller lived for another seven years.

    Wei Zi commented: "I heard that the wine seller liked to gamble. When there was nothing to do, he would play games with his three sons all day, quarreling without family etiquette. Someone asked him why, and he replied, 'Let the children play; otherwise, they would gamble with others and ruin my property.' Alas! The wine seller is not only a benevolent man but also a wise one!" The wine seller's name was Guo Jie, and he had many good deeds. I heard this from Ouyang Jie'an.

    Zhang Shanlai remarked: "Since ancient times, extraordinary people often hide among butchers and wine sellers. The wine seller lived during a time of peace, so he was called a benevolent man. Uncle Zi said, 'Not only is he a benevolent man but also a wise one,' truly having keen insight!"

    One Pail Man's Biography by Yan Shousheng of Huarong, from the Laisuan Wenji collection.

    114

    一瓢道人，不知其姓名。性嗜酒，善画龙，敝衣蓬跣，担筇竹杖，挂一瓢，游鄂渚间，行歌漫骂，学百鸟语，弄群儿聚诟以为乐。顾其神明映彻，怪准奇颜，髯疏疏起，吐语作洪钟声。有时衣新绛衣，从人假驺马，拥大盖，往来市中，观者如堵。
    　　隆庆丁卯，居澧阳，年可七十。澧人异之，或具酒，蓄墨汁，乞一瓢子画，不能得。一日饮龚孝廉园中，颓然以醉，直视沉吟久之。座中顾曰：“此一瓢子画势也。”一瓢子骨相既奇，如蛟人龙子，更卸衣衫，裸而起舞，顾谓座客：“为我高歌《入塞》《出塞》之曲。”又令小儿跳呼，四面交攻。已，信手涂泼，烟雾迷空，座中凛凛生寒气，飞潜见伏，随势而成。署其尾曰“牛舜耕”。问其故，笑而不答。有饮一瓢子酒，年余不能得其画者；久之，画一人科头赤脚，踞地而遗，节骨隐起，作努力状，以赠之。其善谑如此！信口辄成诗，间有异语，多奇中。澧人渐敬之，竞馈问，皆受而弃之。

    Explanation

    114

    The Hermit with a Gourd, whose name is unknown, had a fondness for wine and was skilled in painting dragons. He wore tattered clothes and went barefoot, carrying a bamboo staff and hanging a gourd, wandering through the regions of E and Zhu. He sang and cursed while walking, mimicked the sounds of various birds, and teased children, causing them to scold him, which he found amusing. His spirit was clear and radiant, and he had a striking and unusual appearance, with sparse facial hair and a deep, resonant voice. Sometimes he wore new red clothing, borrowed a ceremonial horse, and carried a large umbrella, attracting crowds wherever he went in the market.

    In the year Dingmao of the Longqing era, he lived in Liyang and was about seventy years old. The people of Liyang found him strange. Some prepared wine and ink, asking him to paint, but he refused. One day, while drinking in the garden of a scholar named Gong, he was drunk and relaxed, staring intently and pondering for a long time. Someone in the audience said, "This is the style of a painting by the Hermit with a Gourd." The Hermit with a Gourd had an unusual and striking appearance, like a dragon's child.
