He took off his clothes, danced naked, and told the guests, "Sing for me the songs about entering and leaving the border." He also made children jump and shout, creating a chaotic scene. Then, he painted freely and spontaneously, filling the air with mist and fog. The audience felt a chill, and the painting depicted both visible and hidden elements, flowing with the scene. He signed his painting with the name "Niu Shungeng." When asked why, he smiled and did not answer. There was someone who had drunk the Hermit with a Gourd's wine but could not get a painting from him for over a year. After some time, the Hermit with a Gourd painted a person with a bald head and bare feet, sitting on the ground, with bones protruding and making an effortful pose, and gave it to him. He was so good at jesting! He could spontaneously compose poetry and occasionally said unusual things, often hitting the mark. The people of Liyang gradually respected him, competing to offer him gifts, but he accepted them and then discarded them.

    115

    华阳庄靖王请改馆，一瓢子不可。所居无定处。一日宿文昌祠中，礼文昌像，作梵咒；像落压其脑，乃遗书庄靖，请“速营黄肠，吾将老焉。”王如言为治木。木具，一瓢子坐其中，不覆，令人舁而过市，拱手大呼，与人言别。周遍街巷，迁郊外普贤庵，命众曰：“可覆我？”众不敢覆，视之，已去矣。遂覆而埋之，举之甚轻，如空棺然。澧人为题石于澧水桥头，署“画龙道人一瓢子之墓”，盖隆庆辛未七月也。
    　　或曰：一瓢子，少读书不得志，弃去走海上，从军征倭寇有功，至裨将。后失律，匿于群盗，出没吴楚间。乃以资市妓十余人，卖酒淮扬，所得市门资悉以自奉。诸妓更代侍之。日拥歌舞，具饮食以自豪。凡十余年，始亡去。乞食湖湘间，终于澧。

    Explanation

    115

    Prince of Huayang and Prince of Zhuangjing requested to change the residence, but One-Pot Man refused. He had no fixed place of residence. One day, he stayed at the temple of Wen Chang, worshiped the statue of Wen Chang, and performed Buddhist rituals; the statue fell and pressed on his head, so he left a letter to Prince Zhuangjing, asking, "Quickly prepare the yellow intestine, I will live out my days here." The prince followed his request and prepared the wood. When the wood was ready, One-Pot Man sat in it, not covered, and was carried through the market by people, bowing and shouting, saying goodbye to everyone. He went through the streets and alleys, then moved to the Puxian Temple outside the city. He ordered the crowd, "Can you cover me?" The crowd dared not cover him, and when they looked, he had already left. They then covered and buried him. When they lifted the coffin, it was very light, like an empty coffin. The people of Li Shi inscribed a stone at the bridgehead of the Li River, titled "The Grave of the Taoist Master Huaplong, One-Pot Man," which was in July of the year Xinhui during the Longqing era.
    Some say: One-Pot Man studied but did not achieve success and gave up, going to the sea. He served in the army and fought against the Japanese pirates with merit, becoming a deputy general. Later, he broke the law and hid among bandits, roaming between Wu and Chu. He then used his wealth to buy more than ten prostitutes, sold wine in Huaiyang, and used all the money earned from the market gate for himself. The prostitutes took turns serving him. Daily surrounded by music and dance, he provided food and drink to show off. For more than ten years, he finally disappeared. He begged for food in the Hu and Xiang regions and died in Li.

    116

    附：游一瓢传
    　　陈周二游
    　　启、祯之时，楚湖之南澧州，有游食道人，衣结履穿，臭秽不可迩，求乞市中，每日得酒一瓢。风雨中辄醉卧道上，其言在可解不可解之间，或验或不必验，无甚异于人，人亦不之异。以其游食，谓之“游道人”；以其喜酒一瓢，又谓之“游一瓢”也。尝醉中大言曰：“我善画龙。”人或以纸试之，磨墨满瓢，狂噀著纸，又以破袖渍尘浓涂，张纸空中，俟墨干时，烟云吞吐，鳞甲生动，有飞腾破壁之势，得者至今宝之。
    　　偶华阳王过市，前驱呵斥不起。王曰：“得全于酒者得全于天也。天全之人，自非凡品。”舆致宫中，供养致敬。一日，忽举手谢王曰：“吾禄食已尽，后事累王矣！”奄然长逝。王以两石缸函其尸，葬之。半载后，有自都门来者，见游在都，附书于王，果一瓢手迹。王异之，发其缸，空如也，因叹神仙之游戏人间，而人不之识也。

    Explanation

    116

    Appendix: The Biography of You Yipiao
    By Chen Zhou Er

    During the reigns of Emperors Chongzhen and Tianqi, in the southern part of Chu Lake, Li Prefecture, there was a wandering Taoist who wore patched clothes and worn-out shoes, stinking and filthy, not to be approached. He begged in the market, receiving one gourd of wine each day. In rain or wind, he would get drunk and sleep on the road. His words were sometimes understandable and sometimes not, and sometimes true, sometimes not. He was not much different from others, and people did not treat him differently. Because he wandered and begged, he was called "Wandering Taoist." Because he liked one gourd of wine, he was also called "Wandering Yipiao."

    Once, while drunk, he loudly said, "I am good at painting dragons." Some people tested him with paper. He ground ink until it filled the gourd, sprayed ink wildly onto the paper, and then used his torn sleeve to smear thick dust. He hung the paper in the air, and when the ink dried, the clouds and mist seemed to breathe, and the scales and armor appeared lifelike, with the appearance of flying and breaking through the wall. Those who obtained it treasure it to this day.

    Once, the Prince of Huayang passed through the market. His attendants shouted at him, but he did not move. The prince said, "Those who are complete in wine are complete in heaven. Those who are complete in heaven are not ordinary people." He was carried to the palace and treated with respect and offerings. One day, he suddenly raised his hand and thanked the prince, saying, "My share of sustenance is finished, and I leave my afterlife to you!" He died quietly. The prince placed his body in two stone jars and buried it.

    Six months later, someone from the capital came, saw You Yipiao in the capital, and sent a letter to the prince, which was indeed the handwriting of You Yipiao. The prince was surprised and opened the jars, which were empty. He sighed that immortals play games in the world, but people do not recognize them.

    117

    独拙和尚，澧州人，目击其异，并识其诗四绝。一曰：“磨快锄头挖苦参，不知山下白云深。多年寂寞无烟火，细嚼梅花当点心。”二曰：“游食多年不害羞，也来城市看妆楼。东风不管人贫贱，一样飞花到白头。”三曰：“破寺无僧好挂瓢，闲时歌舞醉吹箫。黄昏月落秋江里，没个人来问寂寥。”四曰：“门外何人唤老游？老游无事听溪流。而今世事多荆棘，黄叶飞来怕打头。”
    　　[张山来曰：予于《文瀔》中见严作，选后而濑江陈子二游，复以是作见寄。所纪事大同小异，因并录之，以彰瑜、亮云。] 宋连璧传　乐安李焕章象先爽韵居集

    Explanation

    117

    Duzhuo Monk, a native of Lixian, witnessed the unusual and recognized his four poems. The first poem reads: "Sharpen the hoe to dig bitter ginseng, not knowing how deep the white clouds are below the mountain. Years of loneliness without the smoke of fire, chewing the plum blossom as a snack." The second poem reads: "Wandered and begged for food for many years without shame, also came to the city to look at the makeup towers. The east wind does not care about people's poverty or lowly status, the same flying flowers reach the white hair." The third poem reads: "An abandoned temple without monks is good for hanging a gourd, in leisure, sing and dance while drunk and blow the flute. At dusk, the moon sets over the autumn river, no one comes to ask about loneliness." The fourth poem reads: "Who is calling the old wanderer outside the door? The old wanderer has nothing to do and listens to the stream. Nowadays, worldly affairs are full of thorns, yellow leaves fly and fear hitting the head."

    [Zhao Shanlai said: I saw Yan's work in the Wenju and later received this work from Chen Zi of Shaji River. The events recorded are largely the same but with slight differences, so I have included them both to highlight their merits and virtues.] Song Lianbi Zhuan by Li Huanchang Xiangxian of Le'an, Shuangyun Juji

    118

    宋连璧者，字玉梧，吾乘北郭人也。巨族，诸家率淳谨，璧独以侠行惊里中。性至孝，父鸿胪丞，晚得异疾，日脐出绿汁数合，医不治。有道士衣破絮，至其家，谓璧曰：“是非脔乳熊，莫能疗也。顾山左何从得？君其听之而已。”璧叱曰：“是岂天上物耶？”乃徒步入秦中，深山遇虎，几咥璧；会猎人大至，虎逸去。璧日伺幽箐伏莽，灌木丛祠，踪迹熊穴。窥熊出，潜刃其乳二，怀之出。熊至，璧仓皇惊堕崖谷下，伤两趾，病不能步，而持乳熊如故也。夜宿废庙中，疑户外有拖屐声至，璧曰：“援远人命！援远人命！”屐声入，取袖中草捏之，即爇。璧察之，乃曩所遇道人也。璧大骇：“师何至是？”道士曰：“待尔久矣！”乃以药傅璧足，辄能立。道士授一书，皆符咒，曰：“尔善用，后四十年，与尔会鸠兹之市。”璧遂至家，父吞乳熊肉，瘥。

    Explanation

    118

    Song Lianbi, courtesy name Yuwu, was a man from the northern outskirts of our city. He came from a prominent family, where all the members were generally upright and cautious, but Lianbi alone amazed the village with his chivalrous conduct. He was extremely filial; his father was an official in the Honglu Temple, who suffered from a strange illness in his later years, his navel would daily secrete green fluid in several measures, which could not be cured by doctors. There was a Taoist priest wearing ragged cloth who came to his home and said to Lianbi, "This can only be cured by cutting up a nursing bear; but where can such a bear be obtained in the eastern part of the country? You just listen to me and follow my instructions." Lianbi retorted, "Is this something from heaven?" He then walked alone into the Qin region, encountered a tiger in the deep mountains, nearly devoured him; just as hunters arrived in large numbers, the tiger fled. Lianbi daily waited in the dense undergrowth, in the thickets of shrubs and trees, tracking down bear dens. When he saw the bear emerge, he stealthily cut off two nursing cubs and carried them with him. The bear returned, Lianbi was so frightened that he fell into a ravine, injured his toes and became ill, unable to walk, but still carried the cubs as before. He spent the night in an abandoned temple, heard the sound of dragging shoes outside, shouted, "Help the distant person's life! Help the distant person's life!" The sound of shoes entered, and the priest took a herb from his sleeve, crushed it, and immediately set it alight. Lianbi observed, it was the Taoist priest he had met earlier. Lianbi was greatly startled, "Why are you here, master?" The priest replied, "I have been waiting for you for a long time!" He then applied medicine to Lianbi's feet, and he could stand immediately. The priest gave him a book filled with talismans and incantations, said, "Use it well, and in forty years, we will meet at the market of Jiuzi." Lianbi then returned home, his father ate the meat of the nursing cubs and recovered.

    119

    后数年，父以他病殁。璧愈厌弃世俗，欲为五岳游。乃稍稍理前道人所遗书，能隐形，驱风雷雨，又剪纸为人马甲盾器械。客侍御游公幕府。崔、魏忌侍御，祸家又以侍御匿妖妄报。缇骑至，缚侍御与璧。槛车至河西务，璧曰：“烦诸公致词中贵，我野人不习豪家，欲他往。”诸缇骑急视之，槛屋寂无人矣。璧与侍御亡之淮上，璧曰：“君可归楚中。”取一符付侍御：“急则焚之。”是时璧变姓名为张思任，于是朝廷捕亡者张思任，而璧之家人不知也。
    　　璧乃潜某宗伯家，遇之厚。时权要与宗伯隙，璧曰：“国贼也！”乃走长安上书劾权要险狠倾善类，为逆阉复仇，宜下司寇请室。上大怒，执之，就斩西市。桎梏忽脱地，寂无人矣。是时，璧又变姓名为李抱真，于是朝廷捕亡者李抱真，而璧之家人不知也。

    Explanation

    119

    After several years, his father died from another illness. Bai became even more disgusted with the mundane world and desired to travel the Five Great Mountains. He gradually organized the books left by the previous Daoist, gaining the ability to become invisible and summon wind, thunder, and rain, as well as cut paper into human and horse armor, shields, and weapons. He served as a guest in the military governor's office. Cui and Wei were jealous of the military governor, and the troublemakers accused him of hiding a false and supernatural figure. The imperial guards arrived, arresting the military governor and Bai. The iron cage vehicle arrived at Hexi, and Bai said, "Ask the gentlemen to convey a message to the noble: I am a rustic person unaccustomed to the wealthy, and I wish to go elsewhere." The guards quickly looked around, but the iron cage house was empty. Bai and the military governor fled to Huai, where Bai said, "You may return to Chu." He took a talisman and gave it to the military governor, saying, "In case of emergency, burn it." At that time, Bai changed his name to Zhang Siren, and the court was hunting for the fugitive Zhang Siren, while Bai's family remained unaware.

    Bai then hid in the house of a certain Grand Secretary, who treated him kindly. At that time, the powerful and influential were at odds with the Grand Secretary, and Bai said, "Traitors to the nation!" He went to Chang'an to submit a memorial denouncing the powerful and influential for their cruelty and treachery, claiming to avenge the evil eunuchs. He recommended that they be sent to the prison of the judicial official. The emperor was furious, arrested him, and ordered his execution in the Western Market. The shackles suddenly fell to the ground, and there was no one there. At that time, Bai changed his name to Li Baoren, and the court was hunting for the fugitive Li Baoren, while Bai's family remained unaware.

    120

    璧辄忆前道人约，至鸠兹市，僦居候道人，且三载。一日，人大呼墙外曰：“此中匿亡者三人，曰宋连璧、张思任、李抱真，可速出！”璧大骇无措，其人已排闼入，则昔所与别道人也。责之曰：“以尔夙有道契，故授之书，尔奈何与党锢事，为天下逋逃客耶？吾以此迟三年始至。”璧顿首谢，愿自此与师永绝世缘，不复恋妻孥矣。道人曰：“不可！尔还里，当再与家人见。”
    　　璧遂携药囊抵家。其妻已丧久，儿梦瑞，璧去方周岁，见不复认。则棲一庙中，曰：“我张思任，后改李抱真，与兹村有缘，故来。”璧同母弟珠，当捕张、李时，亦疑其为兄，终未敢以告人也。至是心动，趣之，急启扉，兄弟各相识，因抚其子，具告所以，留数日去。

    Explanation

    120

    Bì always remembered the previous agreement with the Taoist monk, arriving at the market town of Jiūzī, renting a dwelling to wait for the Taoist monk, and waiting for almost three years. One day, someone shouted loudly outside the wall, saying, "There are three fugitives hidden here, named Sòng Liánbì, Zhāng Sīrèn, and Lǐ Bào zhēn; you can come out quickly!" Bì was greatly startled and at a loss, and the person had already pushed open the door and entered. It turned out to be the Taoist monk he had parted ways with before. The monk reproached him, saying, "Because you had a spiritual pact with me long ago, I gave you the book. Why did you get involved in the Party Prohibitions, becoming a fugitive from justice? For this reason, I delayed for three years before coming." Bì bowed deeply and apologized, wishing to sever all worldly ties with the master from now on and no longer missing his wife and children. The Taoist monk said, "No! You should return home and meet your family again."

    Bì then carried a medicine bag back to his home. His wife had been dead for a long time, and his son, Mèng Ruì, was only one year old when Bì left. Upon seeing him, the child did not recognize him. He then took shelter in a temple, saying, "I am Zhāng Sīrèn, later changed my name to Lǐ Bào zhēn. I have a connection with this village, so I came." Bì's younger brother, Zhū, suspected that he was his brother during the capture of Zhāng and Lǐ but never dared to tell anyone. At this point, he felt moved, went to find him, and hurriedly opened the door. The brothers recognized each other, and Bì comforted his son, explaining everything, and stayed for several days before leaving.

    121

    [张山来曰：宋连璧虽不当误道人所期，然排解党锢处，亦足见其豪侠。]
    　　
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷四 义虎记　洪都王猷定于一文津选本
    　　辛丑春，余客会稽，集宋公荔裳之署斋。有客谈虎，公因言其同乡明经孙某，嘉靖时为山西孝义知县，见义虎甚奇，属余作记。
    　　县郭外高唐、孤岐诸山多虎。一樵者朝行丛箐中，忽失足堕虎穴。两小虎卧穴内。穴如覆釜，三面石齿廉利，前壁稍平，高丈许，藓落如溜，为虎径。樵踊而蹶者数，彷徨绕壁，泣待死。日落风生，虎啸逾壁入，口衔生麋，分饲两小虎。见樵蹲伏，张爪奋搏。俄巡视若有思者，反以残肉食樵，入抱小虎卧。樵私度虎饱，朝必及。昧爽，虎跃而出。停午，复衔一麂来，饲其子，仍投餕与樵。樵馁甚，取啖，渴自饮其溺。如是者弥月，浸与虎狎。

    121

    Zhang Shanlai said: Although Song Lianbi should not have mistaken what the Daoist expected, his efforts to resolve the party suppression also demonstrate his magnanimity and heroism.

    From the Yuchu Xinzhi, Volume 4, The Record of the Righteous Tiger by Wang Youding of Hongdu, selected by Yu Yiwenzhuan:

    In the spring of the Year of the Ox, I was a guest in Kuaiji and gathered at the office of Gong Lichang. A guest talked about tigers, and the host then mentioned his fellow townsman, Mingjing Sun, who served as the magistrate of Xiaoyi in Shanxi during the reign of Emperor Jiajing. He saw a righteous tiger and found it very remarkable, and commissioned me to write a record of it.

    Outside the walls of the county, the mountains of Gaotang and Guqi are filled with tigers. One woodcutter went through the dense bamboo forest in the morning and suddenly lost his footing and fell into a tiger's den. Two small tigers were lying in the den. The den was like an inverted pot, with sharp and jagged stone teeth on three sides. The front wall was slightly flat, about a zhang high, with moss falling like a stream, serving as the tiger's path. The woodcutter leaped and stumbled several times, wandered around the wall, and cried, waiting to die. As the sun set and the wind blew, the tiger roared and jumped over the wall. It had a live deer in its mouth and fed the two small tigers separately. Seeing the woodcutter crouching, it raised its claws and fiercely attacked. After a moment, it seemed to be thinking and then fed the woodcutter with the remaining meat, going back and lying down with the small tigers. The woodcutter privately thought the tiger was full and would definitely reach the morning. Just before dawn, the tiger leaped out. At noon, it again brought a young deer, fed its young, and still gave the leftovers to the woodcutter. The woodcutter was very hungry, took and ate, and thirsty, drank his own urine. Like this for a month, he gradually became familiar with the tiger.

    122

    一日，小虎渐壮，虎负之出。樵急仰天大号：“大王救我！”须臾，虎复入，拳双足俛首就樵。樵骑虎，腾壁上。虎置樵，携子行，阴崖灌莽，禽鸟声绝，风猎猎从黑林生。樵益急，呼“大王”，虎却顾，樵跽告曰：“蒙大王活我，今相失，惧不免他患，幸终活我，导我中衢，我死不忘报也。”虎颔之，遂前至中衢，反立视樵。樵复告曰：“小人西关穷民也，今去将不复见，归当畜一豚，候大王西关三里外邮亭之下，某日时过飨。无忘吾言。”虎点头。樵泣，虎亦泣。

    Explanation

    122

    One day, the tiger cub gradually grew strong, and the tiger carried it out. The woodcutter hurriedly cried out to the sky, "Your Majesty, save me!" Soon, the tiger returned, knelt on its forepaws, lowered its head, and approached the woodcutter. The woodcutter rode the tiger, leaping onto the wall. The tiger set down the woodcutter and carried the cub away. In the dark cliff with dense vegetation, the sounds of birds were silent, and the wind whistled through the dark forest. The woodcutter became even more urgent, calling out, "Your Majesty!" The tiger turned back, and the woodcutter knelt and said, "I was saved by Your Majesty. Now we have lost each other, and I fear I may fall into other misfortunes. May you keep me alive until the end, guide me to the main road. Even if I die, I will never forget to repay you." The tiger nodded, and they proceeded to the main road. The tiger stood facing the woodcutter. The woodcutter then promised, "I am a poor man from the western gate. Now that I am leaving, I may not see you again. When I return, I will raise a pig and await Your Majesty at the post station three miles west of the western gate on a certain day and time. I will offer you a feast. Do not forget my words." The tiger nodded. The woodcutter wept, and the tiger also wept.

    123

    迨归，家人惊讯。樵语故，共喜。至期具豚，方事宰割，虎先期至，不见樵，竟入西关。居民见之，呼猎者闭关栅，矛梃铳弩毕集，约生擒以献邑宰。樵奔救告众曰：“虎与我有大恩，愿公等勿伤。”众竟擒诣县，樵击鼓大呼，官怒诘，樵具告前事。不信。樵曰：“请验之，如诳，愿受笞！”官亲至虎所，樵抱虎痛哭曰：“救我者大王耶？”虎点头。“大王以赴约入关耶？”复点头。“我为大王请命，若不得，愿以死从大王。”言未讫，虎泪堕地如雨，观者数千人，莫不叹息。官大骇，趋释之，驱至亭下，投以豚，矫尾大嚼，顾樵而去。后名其亭曰“义虎亭”。

    Explanation

    123

    Upon returning, the family was startled and inquired. The woodcutter explained the story, and they all rejoiced. On the appointed day, they prepared a pig, in the process of slaughtering it, the tiger arrived ahead of schedule, not seeing the woodcutter, it entered the western gate. The residents saw it and called the hunters to close the gate and fence, gathering all spears, clubs, guns, and crossbows, agreeing to capture it alive and present it to the county magistrate. The woodcutter ran to save the tiger and told the crowd, "This tiger has done me a great favor; I beg you not to harm it." Despite his plea, the crowd finally captured the tiger and brought it to the county. The woodcutter beat the drum and shouted loudly. The official angrily questioned him, and the woodcutter told the previous events in detail. The official did not believe him. The woodcutter said, "Please verify it. If it is a lie, I am willing to accept the cane punishment!" The official personally went to where the tiger was, and the woodcutter embraced the tiger and wept bitterly, saying, "Is the one who saved me the Great King?" The tiger nodded. "Is the Great King coming to fulfill the appointment by entering the gate?" It nodded again. "I am pleading for the Great King's life. If I fail, I am willing to die with the Great King." Before he finished speaking, the tiger's tears fell to the ground like rain, and thousands of onlookers could not help but sigh. The official was greatly shocked and quickly released the tiger, driving it to the pavilion, where he threw a pig to it. The tiger raised its tail and devoured the pig, looking at the woodcutter and then leaving. Later, the pavilion was named "Loyal Tiger Pavilion."

    124

    王子曰：余闻唐时有邑人郑兴者，以孝义闻，遂以名其县。今亭复以虎名，然则山川之气，固独钟于此邑欤？世往往以杀人之事归狱猛兽，闻义虎之说，其亦知所愧哉？
    　　[张山来曰：人往往以虎为凶暴之兽，今观此记，乃知世间尚有义虎，人而不如，此余所以有《义虎行》之作也。] 丁药园外传　钱塘林璐鹿庵文瀔选本
    　　丁药园先生，名澎，杭之仁和人也。世奉天方教，戒饮酒，而药园顾嗜酒。饮至一石，貌益庄，言愈谨，人咸异之。诗赋古文辞，自少年未达时，即名播江左。其后仲弟景鸿，季弟潆，皆以诗名。世目之曰“三丁”。至香奁艳句，四方闺秀，尤喜诵药园诗。

    Explanation

    124

    The Prince said: I have heard that during the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Zheng Xing from a county, known for his filial piety and righteousness, and thus the county was named after him. Now, a pavilion is also named after a tiger. Is it then that the spirits of the mountains and rivers are uniquely concentrated in this county? The world often attributes acts of killing to fierce beasts. Hearing the story of the righteous tiger, one should feel ashamed.

    [Commentary by Zhang Shanlai: People often regard tigers as fierce and violent animals. Now, observing this account, one realizes that there are still righteous tigers in the world, and humans are not as virtuous. This is why I wrote the "Ode to the Righteous Tiger."]

    Mr. Ding Yaoyuan, whose given name was Peng, was from Renhe in Hangzhou. His family followed the teachings of Islam and abstained from drinking, yet Mr. Ding Yaoyuan had a fondness for alcohol. He could drink up to one dan (a unit of measurement) and his demeanor became more solemn, his speech more cautious, which was unusual and surprising to others. His poetry, prose, and classical writings were renowned even when he was young and not yet successful, spreading fame across the eastern regions. Later, his middle brother Jinghong and youngest brother Ying, all gained fame for their poetry. The world referred to them collectively as the "Three Dings." Especially his erotic and romantic verses were particularly cherished by women from all over, who especially enjoyed reciting Mr. Ding Yaoyuan's poetry.

    125

    家有揽云楼，三丁读书处也。客乍登楼，药园伏案上，疑昼寝。迫而视之，方观书，目去纸才一寸，骤昂首，又不辨某某。客嘲之曰：“卿去丁仪凡几辈？”药园戏持杖逐客，客匿屏后，误逐其仆。药园妇闻之大笑。
    　　一夕娶小妇，药园逼视光丽，心喜甚，出与客赋定情诗。夜半披帏，芗泽袭人，小妇卒无语。诘旦视之，爨下婢也。知为妇所绐，药园又大笑。
    　　延陵大姓遣一姬，能诗，久诵药园诗，誓曰：“主人令吾自择配，愿得如丁君足矣。”阳羡吴参军，与丁世讲也，诡以药园意请约姬，姬许之。丁有侍儿，小字冬青，主讴，善鼓琴。主妇不悦，将遣，府吏纳千金聘之。世方企羡两女子已得所。久之，延陵姬登舟，泣曰：“吾旦夕冀事丁郎，为幕府绐入掖庭，缘已矣！”方扣舷堕水，冬青忽至。延陵姬道故，冬青亦泣曰：“吾故主人翁。”相对泣不止。护骑以告药园，废寝食者累月。然药园数得孺子妾，犹鞅望。主妇贤，家人多不直丁君。

    Explanation

    125

    A family has a library called "Lan Cloud Tower," where three family members (the "three Dings") study. A guest suddenly ascends the tower and sees 药园伏案上,疑昼寝. Upon closer inspection, 药园 is found to be reading, with his eyes only an inch away from the paper. He suddenly lifts his head, unable to recognize something or someone. The guest teases him, saying, "How many generations have passed since you and Ding Yi?" 药园 playfully grabs a stick and chases the guest, who hides behind a screen, mistakingly chasing his servant. 药园's wife hears about it and laughs.

    One night, 药园 marries a young wife. He is captivated by her beauty and is very pleased. He writes a poem with his guests to express his feelings. At midnight, when he opens the curtain, the fragrance of her presence overwhelms him, but the young wife remains silent. The next morning, he realizes she is a kitchen maid. Knowing he has been deceived by his wife, 药园 laughs again.

    A prominent family from Yanling sends a concubine who is skilled in poetry. She has long admired 药园's poetry and vows, "If the master allows me to choose my own match, I would be content with someone like 丁君." 阳羡吴参军, who is acquainted with 丁, pretends to convey 药园's intentions to the concubine, and she agrees. 丁 has a maid named 冬青, who is the main singer and skilled in playing the zither. The mistress of the house is displeased and plans to dismiss her. A government official offers a thousand gold coins to hire her. The world envies these two women for their happy matches. After some time, the concubine from Yanling boards a boat, weeping, "I have always hoped to serve 丁郎, but the court deceived me into entering the imperial harem. My fate is sealed!" She jumps into the water, and 冬青 suddenly arrives. The concubine explains her story, and 冬青 also cries, saying, "I was my former master's servant." They weep together. A servant reports this to 药园, who neglects sleep and meals for months. Despite having several young concubines, 药园 still feels unfulfilled. The mistress is virtuous, but many family members do not respect or favor 丁君.

    126

    药园居法曹，无事，日作诗。与宋观察荔裳、施大参愚山、严黄门灏亭称“燕台七子”，诗名满京师。吏人窃其牍换鹅炙。灶下养思染指，不获，明日讼于庭，药园复赐吏人鹅炙。
    　　时药园官京师，犹守天方教，同官故以猪肝一片置匕箸。药园短视，吏人以告，获免。
    　　上方册立西宫，念无娴典礼者，调入东省兼主客。主客即古典属国也。贡使至，译问主客为谁？廉知公，持紫貂银鼠美玉象犀，从吏人易公诗归国。长安缙绅以为荣。晨入东省，侍郎李公奭棠从东出，药园从中入，瞠目相视。侍郎遣驺卒问讯，药园趋谢。侍郎笑曰：“是公耶？吾知公短视，奚谢为？”药园退而笑曰：“吾短视与诗名等。”

    Explanation

    126

    Yao Yuan, a legal official in the imperial court, had no official duties and spent his days writing poetry. He was part of a literary group known as the "Yantai Seven," which included Cao Cao (known as "Lichang"), Shi Dacheng (known as "Yushan"), and Yan Huangmen (known as "Haoting"). His poetry was renowned throughout the capital.

    One day, a servant stole his official documents to exchange for a roasted goose. Another servant, who worked in the kitchen, wanted to taste the dish but failed. The next day, he sued in court. Yao Yuan, however, gave the servant the roasted goose, sparing him from punishment.

    At the time, Yao Yuan was an official in the capital and still adhered to Islam. One of his colleagues placed a piece of pig liver on his chopsticks, knowing that he could not see clearly. The servant informed him, and Yao Yuan was spared from eating the pig liver.

    When the emperor established a new palace, he realized that there was no one skilled in rituals. Yao Yuan was transferred to the eastern office and appointed as the "host" for foreign envoys. The "host" was a position responsible for foreign affairs. When envoys arrived, they asked who the "host" was. Upon learning that it was Yao Yuan, they presented him with valuable gifts, including purple sable fur, silver mice, jade, and ivory. They exchanged these gifts for his poems and returned to their country. The scholarly elite in Chang'an considered this a great honor.

    One morning, Yao Yuan entered the eastern office. The Vice Minister Li Shitang (known as "Shitang") exited from the east, and Yao Yuan entered from the center. They stared at each other in astonishment. The Vice Minister sent a messenger to greet Yao Yuan, who hurried to thank him. The Vice Minister laughed and said, "Is it you? I knew you were short-sighted, so why thank me?" Yao Yuan smiled and replied, "My short-sightedness is equal to my poetic reputation."

    127

    谪居东，崎岖三千里，邮亭驿壁，读迁客诗，大喜。孺子妾问曰：“得非闻赐环诏耶？”药园曰：“上圣明，赐我游汤沐邑。出关迁客皆才子，此行不患无友。”久之，粮尽，馁而啼。孺子妾慰劳曰：“卿有友，必箪食迎若。”药园笑曰：“恐如卿言，当先以酒疗吾渴。”
    　　初至靖安，卜筑东冈，躬自饭牛，与牧竖同卧起。然暇辄为诗，诗益温厚，无迁谪态。国子藩公闻其名，欲枉见药园，迟不往。一日，乘牛车入城，药园车上执《周易》，骤遇藩公节，低头读《易》不及避。藩公归，语陆子渊曰：“吾今得遇药园先生矣！”子渊问故，藩公曰：“此间安有车上读书，傲然不顾若此人者乎？必药园无疑也。”嗣此西园飞盖，必延药园，饮酒赋诗，礼为上客。

    Explanation

    127

    The Exile in the East

    Exiled to the east, he traveled a rugged three thousand miles, reading poems left by exiled officials at the walls of post stations and inns, greatly pleased. His concubine asked, "Have you not heard the imperial decree allowing your return?" He replied, "The sovereign is wise and enlightened, granting me the privilege to travel to my fief. All exiled officials are talented individuals, so I have no fear of lacking friends." After a long time, his food ran out, and he cried from hunger. The concubine comforted him, saying, "If you have friends, they will certainly welcome you with food." He smiled and said, "If what you say is true, I will first drink alcohol to quench my thirst."

    Upon arriving in Jing'an, he built a dwelling on the eastern hill, personally feeding cattle, and sleeping and waking with the young herdsmen. When he had leisure, he would compose poetry, which became increasingly warm and profound, free from the demeanor of exile. The high-ranking official, Guozi Fan, heard of his name and wished to visit him, but was delayed. One day, riding a cart into the city, he held the Book of Changes on the cart, suddenly meeting Guozi Fan's procession. He lowered his head to read the Book of Changes and did not have time to avoid. Upon returning, Guozi Fan told Lu Ziyuan, "I have now met Master Yao Yuan!" Lu asked why, and Guozi Fan said, "How can there be someone reading on a cart, so proud and indifferent like this person? It must be Master Yao Yuan." From then on, at the grand gatherings at the West Garden, he would always invite Master Yao Yuan, drinking and composing poetry together, treating him as a guest of honor.

    128

    然药园亦困甚，塞上风刺入骨，秋即雨雪，山川林木尽白，河冰合，常不得汲。樵苏不至，五日不爨，取芦粟小米，和雪啮之。然孺子妾辄生子。当尔时，坐茅屋下，日照户，如渥醇酒，然畏风不能视日。日哺，山鬼夜啼，饥鼯声咽，忽闻叩门客，翩然有喜。从隙中窥之，虎方以尾击户。药园危坐自若。
    　　居东凡五迁，家日贫，诗日富。登临眺览，供其笔墨，作《归思轩记》以寓意。友人林璐闻之，曰：“卿归矣！曩者邯郸道上吕仙祠，即卢生受枕处也。仕宦过者，疾驱去以避不祥。卿衔命过其下，停车徐步入。道人方坐蒲团不起。卿异之，索笔题壁曰：『向翁乞取还乡梦，留得凌云化鹤飞』之句，得非诗谶耶？”贻书报药园，惘然悟。又一年始归，果如林生言。

    Explanation

    128

    But the medicine garden was also in dire straits. The cold wind on the frontier pierced to the bone; as autumn turned to winter, rain and snow soon followed, turning the mountains, rivers, and forests completely white. The river froze, and they often could not draw water. Firewood was scarce, and for five days they did not cook; they had to chew millet mixed with snow. Yet the young wives continued to bear children. At that time, sitting under a thatched hut, sunlight shone through the door, as if bathing in fine wine, but they feared the wind and could not look at the sun. In the late afternoon, the eerie sounds of the night echoed, and the hungry weasels wailed. Suddenly, they heard a knock at the door and felt a sudden joy. Peering through a crack, they saw a tiger hitting the door with its tail. The medicine garden sat upright and composed.

    In the east, he moved five times, the family became poorer day by day, but his poetry became richer. Climbing and looking around, he was provided with writing materials and wrote the "Return Thoughts Pavilion Record" to convey his thoughts. A friend named Lin Lu heard about it and said, "You are returning! Once, on the road to Handan, you passed by the Lü Xian Temple, where Lu Sheng received the magic pillow. Officials passing by hurry away to avoid misfortune. You received an order to pass by, stopped the carriage, and slowly walked in. The monk sat on a mat and did not get up. You were surprised and asked for a pen to write on the wall, writing the words, 'I ask the old man for a dream of returning home, leaving behind the clouds and flying like a crane.' Is this not a poetic omen?" Lin Lu sent a letter to Yeyuan, who was confused but understood. After another year, he returned, just as Lin Lu predicted.

    129

    [张山来曰：叙琐屑事，须眉活现，是颊上添毫手也。] 寄畅园闻歌记　莆田余怀澹心曼翁文集
    　　吴门徐生君见，以度曲名闻四方。与余善，著《南曲谱》，索余序。余为之序，有曰：南曲盖始于昆山魏良辅云。良辅初习北音，绌于北人王友山；退而镂心南曲，足迹不下楼十年。当是时，南曲率平直无意致，良辅转喉押调，度为新声，疾徐高下清浊之数，一依本宫，取字齿唇间，跌换巧掇，恒以深邈助其凄泪。吴中老曲师如袁髯、尤驼者，皆瞠乎自以为不及也。良辅之言曰：“学曲者移宫换吕，此熟后事也。初戒杂，毋务多，迎头拍字，彻板随腔，毋或后先之。长宜圆劲，短宜遒，然毋剽，五音依于四声，毋或矫也。毋艳。

    Explanation

    129

    Zhang Shanlai said: To narrate trivial matters vividly, making the characters come to life, is a masterful touch, adding a stroke to the cheek. Record of Hearing Songs at Jichang Garden by Yu Huai, a gentle and reflective man from Putian.

    Wumen Xu Junjian, known for his skill in composing music, is renowned throughout the land. He is a close friend of mine and has compiled The Southern Melody Spectrum, requesting me to write a preface. I wrote the preface, which states: The Southern Melody is said to have originated from Wei Liangfu of Kunshan. Liangfu initially studied Northern tunes and was inferior to the Northern musician Wang Youshan; he then devoted himself to the Southern Melody, rarely leaving his room for ten years. At that time, the Southern Melody was generally plain and lacked charm. Liangfu transformed the melody by adjusting the pitch and rhythm, creating new sounds. The tempo, pitch, and clarity of the melody were strictly adhered to the original scale, with each note carefully articulated, adding depth and melancholy to the music. Even the old masters of the Southern Melody in Wu, such as Yuan Ran and You Tao, were amazed and felt they could not match his skill. Liangfu said: "When learning the melody, changing the pitch and mode is something to be done after mastering the basics. At first, avoid complexity and do not try to learn too much. Focus on hitting the right notes and following the rhythm, without lagging or rushing. Long passages should be smooth and powerful, while short ones should be strong and vigorous, but avoid imitating others. The five tones should follow the four sounds, and do not force them. Avoid excessive ornamentation."

    130

    ”又曰：“开口难，出字难，过腔难，高不难低难，有腔不难无腔难。”又曰：“歇难阁难。”此不传之秘也，良辅尽泄之。而同时娄东人张小泉，海虞人周梦山，竞相附和。惟梁溪人潘荆南独精其技，至今云仍不绝于梁溪矣。今曲必用箫管，而吴人则有张梅谷，善吹洞箫，以箫从曲。毗陵人则有谢林泉，工擫管，以管从曲。皆与良辅游，而梁溪人陈梦萱、顾渭滨、吕起渭辈，并以箫管擅名。盖度曲之工，始于玉峰，盛于梁溪者，殆将百年矣。此道不绝如线，而徐生厥起吴门，搴魏赤帜易汉帜，恨良辅不见徐生，不恨徐生不见良辅也。

    Explanation

    130

    It is said, "Starting a song is difficult, pronouncing words is difficult, hitting the correct pitch is difficult, high notes are not difficult but low notes are, singing with a melody is not difficult but singing without one is." It is also said, "Pausing and sustaining a note are difficult." These are secrets not passed on, but Liangfu revealed them all. At the same time, Zhang Xiaquan from Loudong and Zhou Mengshan from Haishu vied with each other in agreement. Only Pan Jingnan from Liangxi alone excelled in his skill, and until today, the descendants still do not cease in Liangxi. Today, music must use flutes and pipes, and Zhang Meigu from Wu is skilled in playing the deep flute, following the melody with the flute. Xie Linquan from Piling is skilled in playing the pipe, following the melody with the pipe. All of them associated with Liangfu, and Chen Mengxuan, Gu Weibin, and Lu Qitui from Liangxi, and others, all became famous for their skill with flutes and pipes. The art of singing began in Yufeng and flourished in Liangxi, having been around for nearly a hundred years. This art continues like a thread, and Xu Sheng arose in Wu, raising the red flag of Wei and replacing it with the Han flag. I regret that Liangfu did not see Xu Sheng, but I do not regret that Xu Sheng did not see Liangfu.

    131

    徐生年六十余，而喉若雏莺静女，松间石上，按拍一歌，缥缈迟迴，吐纳浏亮，飞鸟遏音，游鱼出听，文人骚客，为之惝怳，为之神伤。妙哉技至此乎？一日徐生语余曰：“吾老矣！恐不能复作少年狡狯事。得吾之传者，乃在梁溪。今太史留仙秦公尊人以新公，所蓄歌者六七人是也。君倘游九龙二泉间，不可不见此人，闻此曲。”余心识之久矣！

    Explanation

    131

    Xu Sheng, aged over sixty, yet his voice was as soft and delicate as that of a young, quiet girl. On a rock in the pine forest, he sang, tapping his feet, his voice ethereal and lingering, clear and resonant. Birds stopped flying, and fish came to listen. The literati and poets were both entranced and moved to sadness by his singing. How wonderful is this skill! One day, Xu Sheng told me, "I am old! I fear I cannot do the tricks of youth anymore. The one who will inherit my skill is in Liangxi. The current official, Qin Gong, whose father has six or seven singers, is the one. If you happen to travel to Liangxi, you must see this person and hear this song." I have long known this in my heart!

    132

    庚戌九月，道经梁溪，适颖州刘考功公勇，拥大航西门外，留余方舟同游惠山。而吴明府伯成、秦宪使补念、顾孝廉修远及其子文学天石、朱公子子葆、刘处士震修皆在席。太史留仙则挟歌者六七人，乘画舫，抱乐器，凌波而至。会于寄畅之园。于是天际秋冬，木叶微脱，循长廊而观止水，倚峭壁以听响泉。而六七人者，衣青紵衣，躧五丝履，恂恂如书生，绰约若处子，列坐文石，或弹或吹。须臾歌喉乍转，累累如贯珠，行云不流，万籁俱寂。余乃狂叫曰：“徐生徐生，岂欺我哉！”六七人者，各道姓名，敛袖低眉，倾其座客。至于笙笛三弦，十翻箫鼓，则授之李生。李生亦吴人。是夕分韵赋诗，三更乃罢酒。次日复宴集宪使家，六七人又偕来各奏技。余作歌贻之，俾知徐生之言不谬。良辅之道，终盛于梁溪；而留仙父子，风流跌宕，照映九龙二泉间者，与山俱高，与水俱清也！是为记。

    Explanation

    132

    In the ninth month of the year Gengxu, I traveled through Liangxi and happened upon Liu Kaogong Gongyong from Yingzhou, who was holding a large ferry outside the west gate. He invited me to join him in a boat trip to Hui Mountain. Wu Mingfu, Qin Xianshi, Gu Xiaolian, and his son Gu Wenxue, Zhu Gongzi, Liu Chushi, and others were all present. Taishi Liu Xian brought with him six or seven singers, riding in a painted boat, carrying musical instruments, and arrived gracefully. We met at the Jichang Garden. At that time, the sky indicated the approach of autumn and winter, and the leaves were just beginning to fall. We walked along the long corridor to view the still water and leaned against the steep cliff to listen to the flowing spring. The six or seven singers, dressed in green hemp clothes and five-thread shoes, were modest like scholars and graceful like young girls. They sat in a row on patterned stones, some playing the lute and others blowing the flute. In a moment, their singing voices suddenly changed, like beads strung together, the clouds did not flow, and all sounds were silent. I shouted wildly, saying, "Xu, Xu, are you deceiving me!" The six or seven singers introduced themselves, folding their sleeves and lowering their eyebrows, captivating the audience. The flutes, flutes, and three-stringed instruments, ten times the sound of flutes and drums, were given to Li Sheng. Li Sheng is also from Wu. That evening, we divided the rhyme and composed poetry, and we stopped drinking until the third watch of the night. The next day, we had another banquet at the censor's house, and the six or seven singers came again and performed their skills. I wrote a song to give to them, so they would know that Xu's words were not false. The good and wise path, ultimately flourishes in Liangxi; and the father and son Liu Xian, with their graceful and unrestrained style, shining in the area between the Nine Dragons and Two Springs, as high as the mountains and as clear as the water. This is the record.

    133

    [张山来曰：吴俗于中秋夜，善歌者咸集虎丘石上，次第竞所长，唯最后一人为最善。听者止数人，不独忘言，并不容赞。予神往久矣！今读此记，益令我穆然以思，悠然以想也。] 陈小怜传　黄冈杜濬十泉变雅堂集
    　　陈小怜，郯城女子也。年十四，遭兵乱，失所，落狭斜。有贵公子昵之，购以千金，贮之别室，作小妻。相好者弥年，大妇知之，恚甚，磨砺白刃，欲得而甘心焉。公子不得已，召媒议遣。居间者以为奇货，遂将小怜入燕中，住西河沿。西河沿，亦斜狭也。

    Explanation

    133

    Translation:

    Zhang Shanlai said: In the Wu region, on Mid-Autumn Night, those skilled in singing all gather on the stones of Tiger Hill, competing in turn according to their strengths, with only the last person being considered the best. The listeners are only a few, not only forgetting to speak but also unable to offer praise. I have long been captivated by it! Now reading this record, it even more makes me solemnly reflect and leisurely imagine.

    Chen Xiaolian was a woman from Tancheng. At the age of fourteen, she encountered war and chaos, lost her place, and fell into the narrow alleys. A wealthy young man became infatuated with her and purchased her for a thousand gold pieces, keeping her in a separate room as his concubine. They enjoyed each other's company for a full year, but the main wife discovered this and was extremely angry, sharpening her blade, determined to get rid of her and take satisfaction. The young man had no choice but to call a matchmaker to discuss her departure. The intermediary regarded Chen Xiaolian as a valuable commodity and took her to Yan, where she settled in Xihéyán. Xihéyán is also a narrow and secluded place.

    134

    小怜姿慧不凡，遂倾动都人士，声价翔贵。虽达官富人，有华筵上客，欲得小怜一佐酒，必先致意，通殷勤，为期旬日之后，然后得其一至。时燕聚四方之士，座中往往多年少美姿容者，结束济楚，媚态百出，自谓必得当于小怜，小怜弗睇也。
    　　而钱唐知名士范性华者，老成人也，馆于燕。一日以赴某公宴，遘小怜，虽颇异其姿，然平澹遇之耳。范时年五十余，人地固自轩轩，顾貌已苍然，意不在佻达。而小怜一见，独为之心醉，注目视范，自入座以至酒阑，目不他视。凡范起则视其起，范步则视其步，范复就座，则视其就座。往则目送，旋则目迎。已或时起，数步之外，必回头视范，如恐失之。小怜固素谨，忽如此，举坐咸诧异。范反为之跼蹐不自得，笑而左右顾。而小怜自如也。将别，则详问范姓字，归而朝夕诵之。

    Explanation

    134

    Xiaolian's beauty and wisdom were extraordinary, and she captivated the capital's people, her reputation soaring in value. Even high-ranking officials and wealthy people, with guests at elegant banquets, who wished for Xiaolian to serve wine, must first express their intentions and show their politeness, waiting for a period of ten days, and then she would come. At that time, Yan gathered scholars from all over, and among the guests, there were often young men with handsome appearances, dressed elegantly, displaying various charms, believing they would surely please Xiaolian, but Xiaolian did not look at them.

    As for Fan Xinghua, a renowned scholar and elder statesman from Qiantang, who resided in Yan, one day he attended a banquet hosted by a certain official, where he met Xiaolian. Although he found her appearance somewhat unusual, he treated her calmly. Fan was over fifty years old, naturally dignified, but his appearance was already somewhat aged, and his intentions were not frivolous. However, upon seeing Xiaolian, he was uniquely captivated, focusing his gaze on her, from when she sat down until the end of the banquet, his eyes did not look elsewhere. Whenever Fan stood up, she watched him stand up; when Fan walked, she watched him walk; when Fan sat down again, she watched him sit down. She would watch him go and watch him return. Sometimes she would stand up, and even a few steps away, she would turn back to look at Fan, as if afraid of losing sight of him. Xiaolian was always cautious and reserved, yet she suddenly acted like this, and everyone at the table was astonished. Fan, on the other hand, felt uncomfortable and uneasy, laughed, and looked around. But Xiaolian remained composed. Before leaving, she carefully asked about Fan's name. After returning home, she recited it day and night.

    135

    有潘生者，往来于其家，又素识范，谓小怜曰：“尔念范君如此，盍往访之？”小怜正色曰：“吾既已心许范君终身矣，若猝往，是奔也。姑少待，范君相迎，斯可矣。”潘以其言白范，范犹恐其难致，试走伻探之。直小怜是日有巨公之约，肩舆在门矣，立改其所向，语其妪曰：“某公之约，一唯汝多方辞绝之。我赴范君召，不顾矣。”小怜至范所，语次，谓范君曰：“君知我日者席间注目视君之故乎？”范曰：“初不知。”小怜曰：“吾见君之酷似我故夫也。吾不能舍君矣！”是时小怜年始十七。范答曰：“以子之姿慧，从良固甚善，然当择年相若者，吾岂若偶耶？”小怜应曰：“君误矣！三十年以内所生之人，岂有可与论吾心者哉？”范大奇其言，叩之，知尝读书，粗通朱子《纲目》。范初无意，至是固已心动矣。因留连旬朔，相与定盟，然后去。

    Explanation

    135

    A man named Pan frequently visited her home and was also well-acquainted with Fan. He told Xiao Lian, "Given how you feel about Fan, why not go visit him?" Xiao Lian responded sternly, "I have already pledged my life to Fan. To visit him suddenly would be tantamount to running away. Please wait a little longer; if Fan comes to meet me, then it will be acceptable." Pan relayed her words to Fan, who was still hesitant and unsure if she could be won over. He sent a messenger to check on her. It turned out that Xiao Lian had an important appointment that day, and a sedan chair was already waiting at the door. She immediately changed her plans and told her maid, "Please find various ways to decline this appointment. I will go to meet Fan and will not hesitate." When Xiao Lian arrived at Fan's place, during their conversation, she said to Fan, "Do you know why I was staring at you during our last gathering?" Fan replied, "I did not notice." Xiao Lian explained, "I saw that you look very much like my late husband. I cannot abandon you!" At that time, Xiao Lian was just seventeen years old. Fan responded, "Given your beauty and wisdom, it would indeed be good for you to marry a good man, but you should choose someone of similar age. Am I a suitable match for you?" Xiao Lian replied, "You are mistaken! Among those born within the last thirty years, who could possibly understand my heart?" Fan was greatly impressed by her words and asked her about her background. He learned that she had read and had a basic understanding of Zhu Xi's Gangmu. Initially, Fan had no intention of pursuing her, but by this point, he was already moved. They stayed together for several weeks, made a formal agreement, and then parted ways.

    136

    而小怜所与一时宦方与范相忌，闻之，雅不能平，辄计致小怜曲室中，出而扃其户以困之。小怜顾室中，有髹几长丈余，遂泚笔于几上，书“范性华”三字，几千百满之。时宦归而睹几上字，色变不能言。燕中尝作盛会，广召宾友，及狎客妓女皆与。酒酣，客为觞政，下令人各饮满，既酌，自言其心上人为某，不实者，有如酒。次第至小怜，或戏之曰：“尔心上人多矣，莫适言谁也！”小怜嗔曰：“是何言？一人而已！”起持巨觥命满酌，一饮绝沥，覆觞大呼曰：“范性华！”举座相顾，以为此子无所引避矣。其笃挚至于此。

    Explanation

    136

    Xiao Lian was the object of jealousy among the eunuchs and Fan. Upon hearing this, they could not bear it calmly and would plan to bring Xiao Lian into a secluded room, locking the door to confine her. Xiao Lian, looking around the room, saw a lacquered table about a zhang (about 3 meters) long. She then dipped her pen on the table and wrote the three characters "Fan Xinghua" thousands of times. When the eunuch returned and saw the words on the table, his face turned pale and he could not speak. Once, a grand gathering was held in Yan, inviting many guests, companions, and courtesans. When the wine was flowing freely, the guests decided on a drinking game, ordering everyone to drink a full cup. After drinking, each person declared their beloved, and if it was not true, they would be punished by the wine. In turn, it came to Xiao Lian. Someone teased her, saying, "You have many beloveds, which one do you really like?" Xiao Lian angrily said, "What are you saying? Only one person!" She stood up, took a large cup, and drank it all in one go, then overturned the cup and loudly called out, "Fan Xinghua!" Everyone looked at each other, thinking that this person had no way to avoid the situation. Her loyalty and affection reached this point.

    137

    然久之无成事。范于是仰天叹曰：“醇政独非丈夫乎？何遂力不能举一女子，而忍负之也？且小怜与我约者，极不难耳。督过愆期，至于舌敝，金台之下，识范性华者多矣，而将伯之助寂然，又安事交游为？”乃为诗自伤云：“只愁世少黄衫客，李益终为薄倖人。”信乎其为薄倖人矣！小怜以河清难俟，竟为有势者强劫以去，犹留书与范云：“非妾负君，妾终不负君也。”噫，是可悲矣！
    　　先是小怜每数日不晤范，辄废眠食。及范至，则又庄语相勉以大义。且曰：“出处一不慎，则君之词翰，俱可惜矣。”闻者以为此非巷中人语。又力劝范迎其室人来燕中，曰：“小怜异日得事君子，固甘为之副。”范用其言。既而得与室人病诀，厚为之殡，祭吊成礼。小怜一言之力也，范尤感之云。

    Explanation

    137

    For a long time, there was no success. Fan then sighed to the heavens and said, "Is a true man not a man of virtue? Why should I be unable to lift a woman, and yet I bear to betray her? Moreover, Xiao Lian and I made an agreement, which is extremely easy. I have been urging her beyond the due date, to the point of exhausting my words. Under the Golden Terrace, many recognize Fan's refined nature, yet there is no help from friends. What is the use of making friends then?" He then composed a poem to lament, saying, "I only worry that the world lacks gallant men, Li Yi will ultimately be a faithless man." Indeed, he is a faithless man! Xiao Lian, unable to wait for the clear river, was ultimately forcibly taken away by someone powerful, yet she left a letter for Fan saying, "It is not I who betrayed you, I will never betray you." Alas, this is indeed pitiful!

    Previously, Xiao Lian would go several days without meeting Fan, and would neglect sleep and food. When Fan arrived, she would then speak seriously and encourage him with great principles. She also strongly urged Fan to bring his wife to Yan, saying, "Xiao Lian will one day serve a virtuous man, she would gladly be his deputy." Fan followed her advice. Later, he was able to say goodbye to his wife who was ill, gave her a proper burial, and performed the rituals of mourning and farewell. It was Xiao Lian's words that made this possible, and Fan was especially grateful and said, "It was Xiao Lian's words that made this possible."

    138

    徐无山人赞曰：昔晋羊皇后，丑诋故夫以媚刘聪。其死也化为千百亿男子，滔滔者皆是也。陈小怜何人，独不以故夫为讳，而吾友范性华，以似其故夫见许。岂羊皇后之教反不行于女子乎？噫！是为立传。
    　　[张山来曰：层次转折，无不入妙，尤妙在故夫一语。一见不复再见，是文之有品者。] 卖花老人传　江都宗元鼎定九新柳堂集
    　　卖花老人者，不知何许人。家住维扬琼花观后，茅屋三间，旁有小阁。室中茗碗丹灶，经案绳床，皆楚汉明洁。柴门内，方广二亩，以种草花为业。家尝有五色瓜，云即昔之广陵人邵平种也。所种芍药、玫瑰、虞美人、莺粟、洛阳、夜合、萱草、蝴蝶、夜落、金钱、剪春罗、剪秋罗、朱兰、蓝菊、白秋海棠、雁来红，共十数种。早晨担花向红桥坐卖，遇文人墨客，即赠花换诗而归。或遇俗子购之，必数倍其价，得钱沽酒痛醉。余者即散诸乞儿。市人笑为花颠。

    Explanation

    138

    Translation:

    A Praise for Mr. Xu Wu

    In ancient times, Empress Yang of Jin slandered her ex-husband to please Liu Cong, the Emperor of Han Zhao. After her death, she transformed into countless men, all of whom were like this. Who is Chen Xiaolian, that she alone does not hide her ex-husband, and my friend Fan Xinghua, who is praised for resembling his ex-husband. Is the teaching of Empress Yang not followed by women? Alas! This is the reason for writing a biography.

    [Comment by Zhang Shanlai: The layers and turns are all perfect, especially the use of the word "ex-husband." Once seen, never seen again, this is the hallmark of a fine piece of writing.]

    The Biography of the Flower-Selling Old Man, from The New Willow Pavilion Collection of Jiangdu Zong Yuanding

    The Flower-Selling Old Man, whose origins are unknown, lived in a thatched house behind the Jinhu Temple in Yangzhou. His home consisted of three small rooms, with a small pavilion beside them. Inside, there were tea bowls, alchemical furnaces, scripture tables, and rope beds, all clear and clean like those of Chu and Han. Inside the thatched door, there was a square plot of two mu, where he grew flowers for a living. His family once had colorful melons, said to be the same as those grown by Shao Ping of ancient Guangling. He cultivated various flowers, including peonies, roses, pinks, poppies, peonies, night-blooming jasmine, daylilies, butterflies, night-blooming cereus, money flowers, spring orchids, autumn orchids, red orchids, blue chrysanthemums, white begonias, and cockscomb. Early in the morning, he would carry flowers to sell at Hongqiao. When he met literati and artists, he would give away flowers in exchange for poems and return home. If he met a vulgar person who bought them, he would charge several times the price, using the money to buy wine and drink heavily. The rest he would give to beggars. The market people laughed at him as a madman for flowers.

    139

    尝九日渡江，经旬不归，人问之，答曰：“吾访故人殷七七于铁瓮城中耳。”袖中出杜鹃花一枝，红芬可爱。所往来者有笔道人、珏道人，围棋烹茗为乐。珏道人，疑即唐广陵人李珏，以贩籴为业成仙者。笔道人，疑即宋建炎中颜笔仙耳。昔琼花观中，有黄冠持画一轴献帅守，字皆云章鸟篆不可识。使人尾之，乃入观后井中玉勾洞天深处。相传老人或为童子，或为黄鹤，千年于兹矣。识者谓即黄冠后身云。

    Explanation

    139

    Once he crossed the river on the ninth day, he did not return for ten days. When people asked him about it, he replied, "I was visiting my old friend Yin Qiqi in the Iron Pot City." From his sleeve, he pulled out a branch of a cuckoo flower, red and fragrant and lovely. The people he interacted with included the Calligraphy Immortal and the Jade Immortal, and they enjoyed playing Go and brewing tea. The Jade Immortal is suspected to be the Tang Dynasty Guangling person Li Jue, who became an immortal through the trade of grain. The Calligraphy Immortal is suspected to be the Yan Calligraphy Immortal of the Song Dynasty Jianyan period. In the past, at the Peony Flower Temple, there was a Taoist monk who held a scroll of painting to offer to the military governor. The characters were all cloud patterns and bird seals, which could not be recognized. Someone followed him, and he went deep into the Jade Hook Cave Heaven behind the temple. It is said that the old man sometimes appears as a child, sometimes as a yellow crane, for a thousand years. The knowledgeable ones say it is the reincarnation of the Taoist monk.

    140

    [张山来曰：逸趣横溢，澹宕多姿。] 神钺记　旴江徐芳仲光诺皋广志
    　　庚辰夏，某乡有不孝子王某，父早丧，仅一老母，婢畜之。每晨拥妻酣睡，而役母使炊，俟熟方起，旦旦如是。小不如意，即恣口谇骂。
    　　生一子，甫数月，母抱之，视釜沸候，儿忽腾跳堕釜中，母知不救，即潜窜。不孝子闻儿叫，起视已死，乃大恨曰：“媪杀我子！”扪厨得刀，遂出。离家百武，有关帝庙。母见不孝子至，闪入庙，伏神座下。不孝子撚刀入，忽帝旁周将军像，从座跃下，提刀砍不孝子倒，正中其项。庙祝闻刀声铮然，趋出，则不孝子流血满地，而周将军一足尚在门限外未入。呼问老母，具述其事，盖几不免而神救之也。

    Explanation

    140

    Zhang Shanlai said: "Overflowing with unrestrained interest and grace, it is full of many forms." Shen Yue Ji by Xu Fangzhong of Xujiang, Guangzhao, and Guangzhi.

    In the summer of Gengchen, there was an unrighteous son named Wang in a certain village. His father had died early, leaving only an elderly mother whom he treated like a servant. Every morning, he would embrace his wife and sleep deeply while making his mother cook, only getting up when the food was ready, day after day. If anything was not to his liking, he would scold her harshly.

    He had a son who was just a few months old. One day, the mother was holding the child and watching the pot boil when the child suddenly jumped and fell into the pot. Knowing that she could not save him, the mother secretly fled. The unrighteous son heard the child crying, got up to see that the child was dead, and became greatly enraged, shouting, "The old woman killed my child!" He searched the kitchen and found a knife, then left the house. After walking a hundred steps, he came across a temple dedicated to Guan Yu. The mother saw the unrighteous son approaching and quickly hid in the temple, crouching under the deity's seat. The unrighteous son entered with the knife, and suddenly, a statue of Zhou General beside Guan Yu leaped down from the seat, wielding the knife and striking the unrighteous son, knocking him down and hitting him directly in the neck. The temple attendant heard the sharp sound of the knife and rushed out, finding the unrighteous son lying on the ground covered in blood, while Zhou General's foot was still outside the threshold. The attendant called out to the old mother and asked her about the incident, and she recounted what had happened, saying that it was almost unavoidable but that the deity had saved her.

    141

    自是远近喧传其庙周将军灵爽。庙以金重装其像，足仍门外如故。信州居民，近是乡者，日裹粮走谒。予过玉山，居停叶七十为道其异。
    　　夫帝庙，非西市也，神之刀，非斧钺也；木偶之将军，非有血气知觉指臂运动也。然异变所激，则金可使飞，土可使跃，块然之手足可使踰阈而搏。假令神不馘是子，其母且不免；神视子之剸刃其母而不之救，无为贵神矣。然必无是也。即使更入他庙，神之斧亦皆能跳而馘之也。苏子瞻云：“掘井得泉，水非专在于是。”而世不察，或疑为诞，或以为像之灵爽若是而奔走之，皆窥管刻剑而不达于感应之义者也。数十年前，吾郡有祖母抱孙堕池中死者，畏其子之怒，避去。子藏椎僻径石罅中，诱其母归过之，索椎，手既入，石辄合不可出。雷火下焚其面，乃自声罪，宛转石间，数日死。以理言，石岂开合啮人之物哉？罪逆之至，凡其所触皆为难矣。

    Explanation

    141

    Since then, the temple of Zhou General was widely known for its miraculous power. The temple adorned the image with gold, and the feet remained outside the door as before. Residents of Xinzhou, those living near the temple, daily wrapped food and went to pay homage. I passed through Yushan, and the host, Ye Seventy, described the unusual event.

    The imperial temple is not the Western Market, and the god's knife is not an axe or halberd; the wooden general is not a living being with blood, sensation, or movement. However, when an extraordinary change occurs, gold can be made to fly, and earth can be made to leap. The solid limbs can be made to leap over the threshold and strike. If the god did not decapitate the son, his mother would not escape; the god saw the son cutting his mother and did not save her. There would be no reason to honor the god. Yet, this must not be the case. Even if he entered another temple, the god's axe could also leap and decapitate him. Su Zizhan said, "Digging a well and finding water, the water is not exclusively there." Yet, the world does not understand, some doubt it is a fabrication, others believe that the image's miraculous power is so great that they rush to worship it, all of them looking through a tube or engraving a sword and failing to grasp the principle of mutual response. Several decades ago, in my county, there was a grandmother who died after falling into a pond while holding her grandson. Afraid of her son's anger, she fled. The son hid a mallet in a secluded path and a crevice in the rock. He lured his mother back, asked for the mallet, and when her hand went in, the rock closed and she could not get out. Lightning and fire burned her face, and she confessed her crimes, writhing in pain in the rock crevice, and died after several days. In terms of reason, a rock is not something that opens and bites people. The extreme wickedness made everything she touched difficult.

    142

    [张山来曰：阅至不孝子弑逆处，令人发指眦裂；读至神钺砍颈处，令人拍案称快！世之敢于悖逆者，皆以为未必即有报应耳。则曷不取是篇而读之也？
    　　又曰：吾乡有一人，负其至戚者，已非一端，而犹谓未足，又欲挟强而贷。至戚不能缄默，因诉其族人。此人遂大诟，遂逼其母死于至戚之家。其母固孀居而姑息者也，虽未如其言，而此言则亦难逭于神钺者矣。吾愿世之为母者，慎毋姑息而自贻伊戚也。] 焚琴子传　梁溪顾彩天石辟疆园文钞

    Explanation

    142

    张山来曰：读到不孝子弑逆之处，令人发指眦裂；读到神钺砍颈之处，令人拍案称快！世上敢于悖逆的人，都认为未必会有报应。那么为什么不读这篇呢？
    　　又说：我家乡有一个人，多次得罪他的至亲，仍认为不够，还想仗势逼债。至亲无法沉默，于是向他的族人投诉。此人于是大骂，并逼迫他的母亲死在至亲家中。他的母亲确实是孀居且纵容的人，虽然没有像他说的那样，但这句话也是难以逃避神的惩罚的。我希望世上的母亲们，谨慎不要纵容而给自己带来灾难。

    143

    焚琴子者，姓章氏，闽之诸生也。为人磊落不羁，伤心善哭，类古之唐衢、谢翱，而才情过之。为诗文，下笔累千言，皆感人心脾。
    　　庚子乡试，文已为主司所赏。及观五策，指陈时事太过，至斥耿氏以为包藏叛志。主司乃惧不敢录，遂下第。生遂弃诸生不为。登鼓山所谓天风海涛亭者，北望神京，痛哭失声曰：“今天下将有变，得如余者数辈，委以兵农财赋诸大政，犹可镇定。顾乃郁郁以青衿子困英雄，俾儿曹口臭者登廊庙而食肉，诚何为哉！诚何为哉！余且烧其诗书，绝笔不为文矣！”既而三藩继叛。闽亦疲于兵革，悉如生所料云。
    　　生既不得志，出游于潮，过潮刺史韩文公庙，读其《逐鳄文》，哭之。又历韶、惠、广、雷诸郡，悲岭海之烟瘴，思寇莱公谪雷时，枯竹生笋，蜡泪成堆，风流如在也，则又哭之哀。听鹧鸪作“行不得哥哥”声，则抗音而哭以乱其鸣。

    Explanation

    143

    Burner of the Zither was a person surnamed Zhang, a student from Fujian. He was free-spirited and unrestrained, good at crying when sad, similar to the ancient figures Tang Qu and Xie Ao, but surpassing them in talent and emotion. When he wrote poetry and prose, he could produce thousands of words in one go, all of which touched the heart and soul.

    During the Gengzi provincial examination, his writing was praised by the chief examiner. However, when the chief examiner read the five policy essays, he criticized current affairs too harshly, even accusing the Geng family of harboring rebellious intentions. The chief examiner was afraid and did not dare to select him, so he failed the examination. The student then abandoned his status as a student and stopped studying. He climbed Mount Gu to the pavilion known as the Pavilion of Heavenly Winds and Sea Waves, looked north toward the imperial capital, and cried out in pain, saying: "Now the world will soon change, and if a few people like me were entrusted with the major policies of military, agriculture, finance, and taxation, we could still stabilize the situation. But instead, they confine heroes in their youth, allowing mediocre people to occupy high positions and enjoy luxury. What is the point of it? What is the point of it! I will burn my poetry and books and stop writing altogether!"

    Soon after, the Revolt of the Three Feudatories began. Fujian was also exhausted by war, just as the student had predicted.

    The student, having no success, traveled to Chaozhou, passing by the temple dedicated to Han Yu, the governor of Chaozhou, and read his famous essay "驱鳄文" (驱鳄文), weeping over it. He then traveled through the prefectures of Zhao, Hui, Guang, and Lei, mourning the misty and damp climate of the southern regions, thinking of Kou Lei, who was exiled to Leizhou during his political downfall, when dried bamboo sprouted new shoots and wax tears formed piles, and the spirit of the exiled official still lingered. He wept bitterly over this. When he heard the call of the cuckoo bird, "go no further, brother," he cried out loudly to drown out its sound.

    144

    久之，学琴于惠州僧上振，得其音节之妙，遂归，变姓名，以琴游八闽。王公大人争延致而听其琴。有愿从而学者，虽善，然终莫能及也。久之，有将军自满洲来，驻防闽省，嗜琴，厚礼延生，使鼓琴于幕下。将军据上坐，而置一座于旁，命生坐。生怒目视将军曰：“吾博通万卷书，而明公唯知马上用剑槊，吾岂为若门下士耶？奈何不以宾礼见而屈于旁，吾不能鼓琴矣！”奋衣径出，不顾。将军惭，下与抗礼谢罪，强留之。乃踞上坐为一鼓琴。将军称善，左右无不竦听。然其声凄怆噍杀，有秦音焉。生曰：“琴者，天下之至和也。吾琴雝雝如鸾凤鸣。今枝上无螳螂捕蝉，而弦中忽变西北肃杀声，何也？岂军中殆将有警耶？”抚琴毕，三军之士皆为嗟叹，有流涕者。生尽醉，痛哭上马而去。将军赠之金，不受。后此军沦于海澄焉。

    Explanation

    144

    After a long time, he learned to play the qin from a monk named Shang Zhen in Huizhou, mastering the subtlety of its tones and rhythms. He then returned home, changed his name, and traveled the eight provinces of Fujian with his qin. Princes and high officials competed to invite him to play his qin. Those who wished to learn from him, even if they were skilled, could not match him. After a long time, a general from Manchuria came, stationed in Fujian Province, and loved the qin. He invited him with great respect and gifts, had him play the qin in his tent. The general sat in the upper seat and placed a seat beside it, ordering him to sit. The qin player glared at the general and said, "I am well-versed in ten thousand volumes of books, but you only know how to use a sword and spear on horseback. Am I to be your subordinate? Why are you not treating me with the courtesy due to a guest and seating me on the side? I cannot play the qin anymore!" He threw off his clothes and left without looking back. The general felt ashamed, descended to treat him with equal respect, apologizing, and forced him to stay. He then sat in the upper seat and played the qin once more. The general praised him, and those around him listened in awe. But the sound was mournful and harsh, with a Qin flavor. The qin player said, "The qin is the most harmonious instrument in the world. My qin sounds harmonious like the call of phoenixes. Now there are no cicadas being caught by mantises, but suddenly the strings produce a northwestern sound of desolation. Why is this? Is there perhaps a warning in the army?" After finishing playing the qin, all the soldiers sighed and some even shed tears. He became completely drunk, wept bitterly, mounted his horse, and left. The general offered him gold, but he refused it. Later, this army was defeated in Haicheng.

    145

    久之，闽人目生为琴师，虽江浙间，颇多闻其名者。然当道不以礼遇，招亦不往，往亦不为久留。常酒后耳热，摔琴于地，引满大卮，放言高论，惊其座宾。谈古今得失，虽老师宿儒，深通经济者，不能难也。

    Explanation

    145

    After a long time, the people of Fujian regarded him as a qin player. Even in Jiangsu and Zhejiang, many had heard of his name. However, those in power did not treat him with courtesy; he would not go when invited, and if he did go, he would not stay long. He often got heated after drinking, throwing his qin to the ground, pouring himself a large cup, speaking freely and loftily, startling the guests at the table. He would discuss the gains and losses of past and present, and even scholars and teachers well-versed in statecraft could not challenge him.

    146

    其最爱童子曰金兰，亦善琴，独得生传，常负奚囊从生游数千里外。生诗成，金兰辄缮录之盈帙。客访生不遇，金兰代为款接，以生惊人句示人。由是人颇异之，以为抱负非常之士，不得志而隐于琴。然当事卒莫有荐之者，竟佯狂以卒云。
    　　生笃于伉俪，妇陈氏，少生十岁，亦颇知书嗜音。生尝入为其妻鼓琴，茶香入牖，鬓影萧疏，顾而乐之，以为闺房清课，亦人生韵事。忽一日谓其妇曰：“吾向闻红颜薄命。卿才情如此，而推命者多言岁行在卯当死。岂汝亦天上人，不久当去耶？”因感慨悲伤，为弹《别鹄离鸾》之曲，曰：“琴音和，吾与汝尚无恙，然第七弦无故忽绝，少而慧者当之。”居数日，金兰死。生抚尸一哭，不胜其悲，吐血数斗，曰：“吾死后，《广陵散》绝矣。”遂焚其琴，不复鼓也。因自号“焚琴子”。生至康熙丁巳，年四十九，竟卒。闻其妇先亡一岁云。

    Explanation

    146

    The master's favorite young man was named Jinlan, who was also skilled in playing the qin. He alone received the master's transmission and often carried a small bag, traveling thousands of miles with him. When the master completed his poems, Jinlan would always carefully copy them into volumes. When guests visited the master and did not find him, Jinlan would entertain them and show them the master's astonishing lines. Because of this, people thought he was a man of extraordinary talent who, despite his ambitions, had not achieved success and thus retreated into music. However, in the end, no one recommended him, and he died pretending to be mad.

    The master was deeply devoted to his wife, Chen, who was ten years younger than him and also quite knowledgeable in books and fond of music. The master once played the qin for his wife. The fragrance of tea wafted through the window, and his sparse hair reflected in the light. He looked around and found joy in it, thinking that playing the qin in the boudoir was also a refined and elegant affair. One day, he told his wife, "I have heard that beautiful women have short lives. With your talent and emotions, many fortune-tellers say you will die when the year is in the year of the rabbit. Are you also a celestial being, about to depart soon?" He then felt sad and played the 'Farewell to the Swan and the Phoenix' tune, saying, "The qin sounds harmonious, and we are still well, but the seventh string suddenly broke without reason. It seems the young and wise one will be affected." A few days later, Jinlan died. The master cried over the body, unable to bear the sorrow, and spat out several dou of blood, saying, "After my death, the 'Guangling San' will be lost." He then burned his qin and stopped playing it. He then called himself 'Fen Qinzi'. The master died in the year Ding Si of the Kangxi era, at the age of forty-nine. It is said that his wife had died a year earlier.

    147

    顾子曰：焚琴子之事，余盖闻之漳州陈别驾云。别驾为余言最详，因嘱余亟为立传，殆古之有心人也。观生之少而肆于文，文不得志而游，一寄于琴，再寄于哭，卒之无有识生之才而用之者，宜其伤于情而碎于琴也。然生流风余韵，宛在丹山碧水之间，迄今登鼓山之亭，如闻其哭焉。生其化鹤而来归乎？松风夜弦，空林鬼哭，生何往而不在也？悲哉！
    　　[张山来曰：予尝观文人之不得志者，往往怨尤侘傺作不平之鸣。心窃议之，以为若辈即使得志，亦未必能有所树立，仅与肉食者等耳。今观焚琴子能预识耿氏于未叛之先，则其器识，诚有度越寻常者，未可谓此中无人也。] 四氏子传　金坛张明弼琴牧萤芝集

    Explanation

    147

    Gongzi said: The story of the "Burning the Zither" man, I have heard from the Deputy Magistrate of Zhangzhou. The Deputy Magistrate spoke to me in great detail about it, and so he urged me to quickly write a biography of him—he is indeed a person of great concern. Observing that he was young and indulged in writing, his writing did not achieve success, so he wandered. He first found solace in the zither, then in weeping. In the end, there was no one who recognized his talent and put it to use; it was fitting that he was hurt by his emotions and destroyed his zither. Yet his lingering influence and graceful demeanor seem to remain in the red mountains and blue waters. Even now, when one ascends the pavilion on Drum Mountain, one can still hear his weeping. Has he perhaps transformed into a crane and returned? The pine wind plays the night strings, and the ghosts weep in the empty forest—wherever he goes, he is always present. How sorrowful!

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: I have often observed that literati who are unfulfilled in their aspirations often express their grievances and discontent through lamentations. I secretly think that even if such people were to achieve success, they might not be able to accomplish anything significant, and they would still be no different from those who live off the fat of the land. But now, observing that the "Burning the Zither" man was able to foresee the rebellion of the Geng family before it happened, his wisdom and insight truly surpass the ordinary. It cannot be said that there are no talented people among them.]

    148

    四氏子，万历初吴人也。有姓名，四氏子者，人名之，因以为名焉。氏子家虽贫，亦产清门，凡缨緌之徒，初皆与游。
    　　顾其体中，痴黠各半，亦复各时。方其黠也，能作诗文，自作自书自讽，声满四邻，若出金石。及其痴也，天地变，黑白贸，亲疏怨德皆相反，妻孥无协志者。其父痛谕之，不从，则挝之，氏子亦报挝焉。久之，恒挝其父。既而著为论曰：“父子主亲，父若挞子，当其举手之时，亲谊已绝，子安得不报挞？又且君父一也，君有罪，汤武诛之，可以称圣；父有罪，子挞之，容得不号贤乎？”又立论：“古今无真名人，但能诃诋人则名归之。孟子诋杨、墨，庄周诋孔子，韩愈诋佛，岂好诋人哉？自为名焉耳！”故氏子遇当世大儒，其声名经旸谷、达濛汜者，皆极力訾诟之。且作嗔拳笑面曰：“是才不如我，而名居吾上，何也？”或相见至有受其大诟者。

    Explanation

    148

    Sìshìzǐ, a man from the Wu region during the early Wanli period of the Ming Dynasty. He was given the nickname "Sìshìzǐ" by others, and he adopted it as his own name. Despite his family's poverty, they were of a noble lineage, and initially, he associated with scholars and literati.

    However, Sìshìzǐ had a dual nature, being both foolish and clever, and his behavior varied depending on the situation. When he was clever, he could write poetry and prose, and he would write, copy, and recite his own works. His voice filled the neighborhood, as if it came from bells and drums. But when he was foolish, the world seemed to change, black and white were reversed, and the relationships of closeness and distance, resentment and virtue were all reversed. His wife and children had no common goals.

    His father sternly advised him, but he did not listen, and his father beat him. Sìshìzǐ also retaliated by beating his father. After a long time, he constantly beat his father. He then wrote an essay saying, "The father and son are bound by kinship. If the father beats the son, at the moment he raises his hand, the bond of kinship is severed. How can the son not retaliate? And the ruler and father are one. If the ruler commits a crime, Tang and Wu can punish him and be called sage kings. If the father commits a crime, the son can beat him and be called virtuous."

    He also wrote another essay: "There are no truly famous people in ancient and modern times. Only those who can criticize others gain fame. Mencius criticized Yang and Mo, Zhuangzi criticized Confucius, Han Yu criticized Buddhism. How could they enjoy criticizing others? They did it for their own fame!"

    Therefore, Sìshìzǐ encountered the great scholars of his time, those whose fame reached the eastern and western extremes, and he criticized them fiercely. He would say with a scowling face and a smiling mouth, "His talent is not as good as mine, but his fame is above mine. Why is that?" Sometimes when they met, they were severely criticized by him.

    149

    氏子既挝父母，詈兄嫂，诋諆当世之岳立者，国人皆鄙之，渐不与游。氏子游甚困，其兄割资食之。氏子未厌，有所如皆枳棘，则益卞急自恣，弃书不读，但好《世说》《水浒》。尝有人扣其门，氏子则怒曰：“谁敢扣若爷门耶？”曰：“我也！”曰：“谁为我？我为谁？”急取大棒击其胫。出行，见人有俯首者，曰：“避我耳！”詈之，答詈则相搏。见仰首者，曰：“骄我耶？”亦詈之，答詈亦相搏。故氏子有所之辄挂阂。既乃以所搏人自嫁于众曰：“彼为彼妻之厚我也，而仇我；虽然，岂予罪哉？”因出袖中一物曰：“此某妻之臂饰，誂我者也。”轻薄者竞传之，剧言苦语，各以加人，遂令邑少洁门。

    Explanation

    149

    The son of the family, having struck his parents, cursed his elder brother and sister-in-law, and slandered those who were respected and influential in the present time, was looked down upon by the people, and gradually no one wanted to associate with him. His brother cut off his own resources to support him, but the son was not satisfied. Wherever he went, he encountered thorns and brambles, and he became increasingly impulsive and unrestrained, abandoning his studies and only liking the Shi Shuo Xin Yu (A New Account of the Tales of the World) and Shui Hu Zhuan (Water Margin). Once, someone knocked on his door, and the son angrily said, "Who dares to knock on your father's door?" The person replied, "It's me!" The son said, "Who am I? For whom?" He quickly took a large stick and hit the person's shin. When he went out, if he saw someone bowing, he would say, "Avoid me!" and curse them; if they answered back, they would fight. If he saw someone looking up to him, he would say, "Are you showing off to me?" and curse them; if they answered back, they would fight. Therefore, wherever the son went, he encountered obstacles. He then boasted about the people he had fought to the crowd, saying, "They treated me well because they wanted to marry me, but now they hate me; although, is it my fault?" He took out an item from his sleeve and said, "This is the arm ornament of someone's wife, meant to entice me." The frivolous people spread it around, using harsh and bitter words to criticize each other, ultimately leading to a decline in the moral standards of the town.

    150

    其妻，中庸人也，稍劝之，氏子则手格之曰：“吾厚其妻，尔乃厚其夫乎？”其子年长，皆心诽之，不敢言。已而邑之人皆知其诡也，则家相告曰：“慎毋与四氏子游。有与立谈者，死期必至矣！”其怨家亦相告曰：“此秽豕也，昔有犬豕卧偃厕中，见狮子过，则负溲溺以侮之，狮子不敢近也。今氏子负秽来，谨避之而已，勿与角也。”于是氏子居都会中，若空庐；行巷市间，唯逢鸡犬草木，不能逢一人也。氏子游益困，则念《世说》中祖珽获髻上叵罗、袖中金叠，因遇物即怀之。人或率众追夺，指名于千百人之前，他人丑之，思入壁罅，氏子坦然徐步，不以屑意也。又欲作南塘夜出梁山筑栅之事，终岁召人，人无肯与同役者。

    Explanation

    150

    His wife was an ordinary, unremarkable person, and she tried to persuade him, but the Four Surnamed Man physically resisted and said, "I treat my wife well, and you treat your husband well?" As the children grew older, they all secretly criticized him, but dared not speak openly. Later, the people of the town all knew of his deceit, so families warned each other, "Be careful not to associate with the Four Surnamed Man. If anyone stands and talks with him, their death is certain!" His enemies also warned each other, "This is a filthy pig. Once there was a dog and a pig lying in a latrine, when they saw a lion passing by, they carried urine and feces to mock it, and the lion dared not approach. Now the Four Surnamed Man comes carrying filth, so be cautious and avoid him, do not engage with him." Thus, the Four Surnamed Man lived in the city as if it were an empty house; when he walked through the streets, he only met chickens, dogs, and plants, but no people. The Four Surnamed Man became increasingly isolated, and he thought of the Shi Shuo Xin Yu where Zu Ting obtained a comb on his head and gold in his sleeves, so whenever he met something, he would take it. Sometimes people would chase after him in groups to take back what he had stolen, naming him in front of hundreds of people, others would be ashamed of him, he would think of hiding in a crack in the wall, but the Four Surnamed Man would walk calmly and slowly, not caring at all. He also wanted to do something like the story of the Southern Pond at night, building a fence on Liang Mountain, calling people all year, but no one was willing to work with him.

    151

    如此十余年，颇自悔。其所亲因从容语之曰：“若为儒，而挝父母，何也？”曰：“吾与父母戏耳，何尝尽力挞之哉？且悔挝之，必沽酒以释之。”“若詈兄嫂，何也？”曰：“吾亦戏耳！且子视吾兄嫂之身，有吾詈迹者，吾当罪。”“子之尽绝六亲百朋，又何也？”曰：“吾初皆戏耳。乃吾六亲百朋，无一达人，见我辄物而不化。彼绝我，我宁绝彼耶？”其人曰：“子每诋通人达士，以为不如子，又奈何？”氏子曰：“尽戏也。吾戏言江水不如吾沼，江与沼不移位，岂非戏耶？”其人曰：“若子戏则尽然矣，今日者，名败身辱，父兄不以为子弟，交游不以为朋友，处环堵之室，上漏下湿，烟断粮绝。子何不尽以戏周旋之，顾怨尤侘傺乃尔耶？”氏子默然无以应。

    Explanation

    151

    For more than ten years, he began to regret his actions. His close associates gently told him, "You, as a scholar, struck your parents—why did you do that?" He replied, "I was just playing with my parents; I never hit them hard! Moreover, if I regret hitting them, I will buy wine to make amends." They asked, "Why did you curse your older brothers and sisters-in-law?" He responded, "I was just joking! Moreover, if you look at my older brothers and sisters-in-law, you won’t find any traces of my cursing; I would be guilty if there were." They then said, "Why did you completely cut off ties with your relatives and friends?" He answered, "I was just joking at first. But my relatives and friends are all ordinary people; they see me and treat me as an object, not transforming me. If they cut me off, why shouldn’t I cut them off?" One of them said, "You always criticize accomplished scholars and officials, thinking they are inferior to you. What should I do about that?" The man replied, "It’s all just joking. I jokingly said that the river is not as good as my pond. The river and the pond don’t change their positions, so isn’t that just a joke?" The other person said, "If you’re just joking, then why are you so resentful and dejected now? You’ve ruined your reputation and disgraced yourself. Your father and brothers no longer consider you a son, and your friends no longer regard you as a friend. You live in a cramped room with leaks above and dampness below, lacking food and firewood. Why can’t you just joke your way through it all? Why are you so resentful and dejected?" The man remained silent and had no response.

    152

    无何，其长子某，少亦韶令，将弱忽得狂疾，终日喃喃詈人。然听其所詈，则皆其父也。其父至，则枚数其罪而挞之。氏子号叫，不得免，或言惨于氏子父被挞时。氏子械子囚诸室，则以一木为其父，诘之曰：“父母可挝乎？”代应之曰：“不可！”曰：“是宜挞！”日挞至百数，其余罪皆然。数年，竟狂死。

    Explanation

    152

    Soon after, his eldest son, who was young and handsome, suddenly fell ill with a mad disease when he was young. All day long, he muttered curses at people. When he cursed, it was all directed at his father. When the father came, he would enumerate the son's offenses and beat him. The son would cry out, unable to escape, and some said it was more cruel than when the father was beaten. The father imprisoned him in a room and used a wooden figure representing his father to question him: "Can parents be beaten?" On his behalf, the son answered: "No!" The father said: "This should be beaten!" He beat him daily, up to a hundred times, and all other offenses were treated the same. After several years, the son eventually died from madness.

    153

    外史氏曰：吾犹及识四氏子，身短不盈四尺，其目莹然若攫食之鸱，颐颊矜长若索斗之鸡；其气如含瓦砾，抱荆棘，有触即摘射。邑人谓其顽嚚不友，似浑敦；不可教诲，不知话言，似梼杌；恶言诬善，贪冒货贿，又似穷奇、饕餮。以为兼有四氏之长，故目为“四氏子”。而四氏子不肯受也，曰：“凡吾所为皆戏耳！”虽然，四氏子戏，其子数木之罪而且挞之，岂亦戏狂耶？或以戏谏耶？今死矣！亦可云戏死耶？夫其父则狂，而反号其子为狂；其子父木而挞之则戏，而其父反以诸罪为戏，皆惑也。吾疑天公之愦愦久矣，今乃以其子之口与手，作天之口与手而日数之，日挞之，又酷巧。嗟乎！天公则诚戏耳，四氏子乌乎戏？
    　　[张山来曰：世岂真有若人耶？然观“吾犹及识之”云云，则是真有其人矣。乃知天生若人，诚近于戏，当亦未尝不悔之耳。后乃假手其子以巧报之，则彼苍之文过也。]
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷五 柳夫人小传　徐芳仲光藏山集

    Explanation

    153

    The Historian said: I still remember meeting the Four-Family Son, who was short, not exceeding four chi in height. His eyes shone brightly like those of a bird of prey about to seize its prey, and his face had prominent cheeks and a long jaw, resembling a chicken ready to fight. His temperament was sharp and aggressive, like holding gravel and thorns, ready to attack at any provocation. The townspeople called him stubborn and unfriendly, likening him to Hunden (a mythical figure representing chaos); they also said he was unteachable and did not understand speech, similar to Taowu (a mythical beast symbolizing disorder and wickedness); he spoke ill of good people and was greedy for wealth, further resembling the mythical beasts Qiongqi and Taotie. They believed he combined the characteristics of all four figures, hence calling him the "Four-Family Son." However, the Four-Family Son refused to accept this, saying, "Everything I do is just for fun!" Nevertheless, the Four-Family Son's fun led him to count his son's faults and beat him, was this also a form of playful madness? Or was it a playful reprimand? Now he is dead! Can this also be called a playful death? His father was mad, yet he labeled his son as mad; his son, when he counted his faults and beat him, was playing, but his father treated his various crimes as play, both were confused. I have long suspected that heaven has been confused for a long time, now using his son's mouth and hands to count and beat him every day, with a cruel precision. Alas! Heaven is indeed playing, but how can the Four-Family Son play?

    [Comment by Zhang Shanlai: Is there really such a person in the world? Yet, considering "I still remember meeting him," it seems there was indeed such a person. Thus, we know that such a person was born, truly close to play, and perhaps not without regret. Later, he used his son to cleverly repay him, which is heaven covering up its mistakes.]

    154

    柳夫人字某，虞山钱牧斋宗伯爱姬也。慧倩工词翰。在章台日，色艺冠绝一时。才隽奔走枇杷花下，车马如烟，以一厕扫眉才子列为重。或投竿衒饵，效玉皇书仙之句，纸啣尾属，柳视之蔑如也。即空吴越无当者，独心许虞山，曰：“隆准公即未夐绝今古，亦一代颠倒英雄手。”而宗伯公亦雅重之，曰：“昔人以游蓬岛、宴桃溪，不如一见温仲圭。吾可当世失此人乎？”遂因缘委币。
    　　柳既归宗伯，相得欢甚，题花咏柳，殆无虚日。每宗伯句就，遣鬟矜示柳，击钵之顷，蛮笺已至，风追电蹑，未尝肯地步让。或柳句先就，亦走鬟报赏，宗伯毕力尽气，经营惨淡，思压其上，比出相视，亦正得匹敌也。宗伯气骨苍峻，虬榕百尺，柳未能到；柳幽艳秀发，如芙蓉秋水，自然娟媚，宗伯公时亦逊之。于时旗鼓各建，闺阁之间，隐若敌国云。宗伯于柳不字，凡有题识，多署“柳君”。吴中人宠柳之遇，称之直曰“柳夫人”。

    Explanation

    154

    Lady Liu, whose name is unknown, was the beloved concubine of Master Qian of Yushan, a high-ranking official. She was both beautiful and talented in writing. During her time at Zhangtai, she was the most outstanding in both beauty and talent of her era. Talented scholars flocked to her like bees to blossoms, and her presence was as bustling as a cloud of vehicles. She was considered a prominent figure among the talented women of the time. Some would cast their fishing rods and display their bait, imitating the verses of the Immortal of Writing, but their attempts were continuous and endless. Lady Liu regarded them with contempt. Even in Wu and Yue, there was no one who could match her, except for Master Qian of Yushan, whom she held in high regard. She said, "Even if Master Qian is not the greatest of all time, he is still a significant figure who can change the course of events." Master Qian also highly regarded her, saying, "In ancient times, people would rather visit the fairyland of Penglai or enjoy a banquet in the peach blossom stream than meet someone like Wen Zhonggui. How could I let such a person slip away in this world?" Thus, he sought her hand through intermediaries.

    After Lady Liu returned to Master Qian, they were deeply happy together. They often wrote poems and verses, almost every day. Whenever Master Qian finished a verse, he would send a servant to show it to Lady Liu. Before the sound of a gong could even be heard, her response was already on the paper, swift as the wind and the lightning. She never hesitated to match his pace. Sometimes, Lady Liu would finish her verse first and send a servant to present it to Master Qian. Master Qian would then exert all his energy and effort, carefully crafting his response, striving to surpass hers. When they compared their verses, they found themselves equally matched. Master Qian's writing was strong and imposing, like a hundred-foot cypress, which Lady Liu could not match. Lady Liu's writing, on the other hand, was delicate and graceful, like a lotus in autumn waters, naturally charming. At times, Master Qian would also acknowledge her superiority. In the confines of their chamber, their rivalry was intense, like two nations at war. Master Qian did not formally marry Lady Liu, but he often signed his writings as "Liu Jun." The people of Wu admired her and referred to her simply as "Lady Liu."

    155
