    宗伯生平善逋，晚岁多难，益就窭蹙。嗣君孝廉某故文弱，乡里豪黠颇心易之，又嗛宗伯公墙宇孤峻，结侣伺衅。丙午某月，宗伯公即世。有众骤起，以责逋为口实，噪而环宗伯门，搪撞诟谇，极于虣辱。孝廉魂魄丧失，莫知所出。柳夫人于宗伯易篑日，已蓄殉意，至是泫然起曰：“我当之！”好语诸恶少：“尚书宁尽负若曹金？即负，固尚书事，无与诸儿女！身在，第少需之。”诸恶少闻柳夫人语，谓得所欲，锋稍戢，然环如故。柳中夜刺血书讼牍，遣急足诣郡邑告难，而自取缕帛结项死尚书侧。旦日，郡邑得牍，又闻柳夫人死，遣隶四出，捕诸恶少，问杀人罪。皆雉窜兔脱，不敢复履界地。构尽得释。孝廉君德而哀之，为用匹礼，与尚书公并殡某所。吴人士嘉其志烈，争作诗诔美之，至累帙云。

    Explanation

    155

    The Elder, throughout his life, was skilled at evading responsibilities. In his later years, he faced many difficulties and became increasingly impoverished. His heir, a scholar named Xianlian, was originally weak in writing and intellect. The villagers, bold and cunning, looked down on him and resented the imposing and isolated nature of the Elder's residence. They formed a group, waiting for an opportunity to cause trouble. In a certain month of the year Bingwu, the Elder passed away. A crowd suddenly gathered, using the evasion of debts as a pretext, shouting and surrounding the Elder's gate, bumping into each other, scolding and abusing, and reaching the extreme of violence and humiliation. The heir was lost and did not know what to do. On the day of the Elder's death, Lady Liu had already decided to die with him. She kindly spoke to the young ruffians: "Would the Elder completely owe you the gold? If he owed, it would certainly be the Elder's affair and not involve the children! As long as I am here, just wait a little while." The young ruffians thought they got what they wanted and their sharpness slightly diminished, but they still surrounded as before. At midnight, Lady Liu wrote legal documents with blood and sent a messenger to the county and city to report the difficulty. She then took a piece of silk, strangled herself, and died beside the Elder. The next day, the county and city received the documents and also heard that Lady Liu died. They sent officials out in all directions to catch the young ruffians and ask about the murder. All escaped like chickens and rabbits and dared not step foot in the territory again. All accusations were released. The heir was moved by her virtue and mourned her, using a pair of rituals to bury her with the Elder in a certain place. People of Wu praised her noble intentions, competed to write poems and eulogies to praise her, and the collections were numerous.

    156

    东海生曰：柳夫人可谓不负虞山矣哉！或谓情之所钟，生怜死捐，缠绵毕命，若连理枝、雉朝飞、双鸳鸯之属，时有之矣。然柳于虞山岂其伦耶？夫七尺腐躯，归于等尽。而掷之当，侯赢以存弱赵，杵臼以立藐孤，秀实以缓奉天之危，纪信以脱荥阳之难。或轻于鸿羽，或重于泰山，各视其所用。柳夫人以尺组下报尚书，而纾其身后之祸，可不谓重与？所云重用其死者也！夫西陵松柏，才矣，未闻择所从。耆卿、月仙，齐丘、散花女，得所从矣，而节无闻。韩香、幼玉、张红红、罗爱爱之流，节可录矣，又非其人也。千秋香躅，唯张尚书燕子一楼，然红粉成灰，尚在白杨可柱之后。夫玉容黄土之不惜，而顾以从死之名为地下虑，荒矣。微曰舍人，泉台下随，未敢必其然也。人固不可知，千寻之操，或以一念隳；生平之疵，或以晚节覆。遂志赴义，争乎一决。柳夫人存不必称，而没以馨，委蜕如遗，岂不壮哉！

    156

    The Eastern Sea Scholar said: Can it be said that Lady Liu did not fail Mount Yu? Some say that where affection is deeply focused, life is cherished and death is forsaken, and they remain bound together until death, like twin branches, the phoenix flying together, and paired mandarin ducks, which sometimes occur. However, how could Lady Liu be compared to the cypress trees of Mount Yu? The body of seven feet, in the end, decays equally. But when thrown at the right time, Hou Ying saved the weak state of Zhao, Chǔ Jū established a young orphan, Xiu Shi delayed the crisis in Fengtian, and Ji Xìn escaped the danger in Yingyang. Some actions are lighter than a feather, others are heavier than Mount Tai, each according to their use. Lady Liu used a small ribbon to report to the Shangshu, thereby relieving the calamity after her death, can this not be considered significant? This is what is meant by the heavy use of death! The cypress trees of Xiling are talented, but they have not heard of choosing their path. Qí Qīng and Yuè Xiān, Qí Qiū and Sàn Huā Nǚ, have chosen their paths, but their integrity is unknown. Hàn Xiāng, Yòu Yù, Zhāng Hóng Hóng, and Luò Ài Ài, though their integrity is worth recording, are not the right people. The fragrant footsteps of a thousand autumns are only found in the one-story building of Zhang Shàngshū, yet the red powder turns to ashes, still after the white poplars can be used as pillars. Not sparing the jade-like face turned to yellow earth, but worrying about the name of dying with one's husband in the underworld, is misguided. If it is said that the retainer is not worth mentioning, following in the netherworld, one cannot be certain of this. Human nature is indeed unpredictable, a thousand-foot virtue may collapse with a single thought; a lifelong flaw may be covered up at the end of one's life. Fulfilling one's will and rushing to righteousness, striving for a decisive action. Lady Liu was alive without being praised, but was fragrant after death, leaving behind her shell like a relic, is it not magnificent?

    157

    [张山来曰：前半如柳萦花笑，后半如笳响剑鸣，柳夫人可以不死矣！] 换心记　徐芳仲光诺皋广志
    　　万历中，徽州进士某太翁，性卞急，家故饶赀，而不谐于族。其足两腓瘦削无肉，或笑之曰：“此相当乞。”翁心恨之。生一子，即进士公，教之读书，性奇僿，咿唔十数载，寻常书卷，都不能辨句读。或益嘲笑之曰：“是儿富贵，行当逼人。”翁闻益恚。
    　　有远族侄某，负文名，翁厚币延致，使师之，曰：“此子可教则教，必不可，当质语予，无为久羁。”侄受命，训牖百方，而懵如故。岁暮辞去，曰：“某力竭矣。且叔产固丰，而弟即鲁，不失田舍翁，奈何以此相强？”翁曰：“然！”退而嗔语妇曰：“生不肖子，乃翁真乞矣！”趣治具饯师，而私觅大梃，靠壁间，若有所待。盖公恨进士辱己，意且扑杀之，而以产施僧寺，作终老计。母知翁方怒，未可返，呼进士窃语，使他避。

    Explanation

    157

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: The first half is like willow branches entwined with flowers and laughter, the second half like the sound of pipes and the clang of swords; Lady Liú can be considered immortal!]

    The Tale of the Transplanted Heart by Xu Fángzhòng, Guangnuó Gǎozhì
    During the Wanli era, there was an old man from Huizhou, a former scholar, who was quick-tempered and impatient. His family was originally wealthy, but he did not get along with his clan. His calves were thin and bony, and some laughed at him, saying, "This man should be a beggar." The old man resented this deeply. He had a son, known as the Scholar, whom he taught to read. The son had an odd and simple nature, and despite years of studying, he could not even recognize punctuation in ordinary books. Some mocked him further, saying, "This boy, if he becomes wealthy and powerful, will surely be arrogant." Upon hearing this, the old man became even more angry.

    He had a distant relative, a nephew, who was known for his literary reputation. The old man offered him generous gifts and invited him to teach his son, saying, "If this boy can be taught, teach him; if not, please tell me frankly, and do not keep him here for too long." The nephew accepted the invitation and tried every method to educate him, but the son remained as confused as before. At the end of the year, the nephew left, saying, "I have exhausted my strength. Moreover, your uncle's wealth is indeed abundant, and your brother, though simple, will not be a mere farmer or landowner. Why should I force him to learn?" The old man replied, "Indeed!" Then, he angrily spoke to his wife, "I have an unworthy son, and I truly deserve to be a beggar!" He hurried to prepare a feast to send off the teacher, while secretly finding a large stick and placing it against the wall, as if waiting for something. It seemed that the old man hated the scholar for disgracing him, intending to beat him to death, and was considering giving his property to temples, planning for a long life. The mother knew that her husband was still angry and advised the scholar to avoid him, calling him and speaking to him privately, telling him to stay away.

    158

    进士甫新娶，是夜合户筹议，欲留；恐祸不测，欲去，无所之，则夫妇相持大哭，不觉夜半。倦极假寐，见有金甲神拥巨斧，排闼入，捽其胸，劈之，抉其心出，又别取一心纳之，大惊而寤。
    　　次日，翁延侄饮为别。翁先返，进士前送至数里，最后牵衣流涕曰：“恻隐之心，人皆有之。师何忍某之归而就死？”师矍然曰：“安得此达者言？”进士曰：“此自某意。且某此时，颇觉胸次开朗，愿更从师卒业。”因述夜来梦。师叩以所授书，辄能记诵，乃大骇，亟与俱返。

    Explanation

    158

    The newly married scholar-official, upon his wedding night, closed the door and deliberated with his wife, wanting to stay; but fearing unforeseen disaster, wanting to leave, yet having nowhere to go, so the couple held each other and cried, without realizing it was midnight. Tired to the point of dozing off, he saw a god in golden armor wielding a great axe, breaking in through the door, grabbed his chest, split it open, and pulled out his heart, then took another heart and put it in, greatly startled and woke up.

    The next day, the old man invited his nephew to drink as a farewell. The old man left first, and the scholar-official went ahead to send him off for several miles. Finally, he held onto his clothes and wept, saying, "Compassion is a feeling that everyone possesses. How could the teacher allow me to return home and face death?" The teacher looked startled and said, "How could you say something so wise?" The scholar-official replied, "This is my own idea. Moreover, at this moment, I feel quite open-minded and wish to continue studying with the teacher until completion." He then described the dream he had last night. The teacher asked him about the books he had been taught, and he could immediately recall and recite them, greatly astonished, the teacher quickly agreed and returned with him.

    159

    翁闻剥啄声，掣梃门俟。已闻师返，则延入。师具以途中所闻告。翁以为谬，试之良然，乃大喜。自是敏颖大著，不数岁，补邑诸生。又数岁，联捷成进士。报至之日，翁坐胡床，大笑曰：“乃公自是免于乞矣！”因张口哑哑而逝。
    　　族子某为郡从事，庚辰与予遇山左道中，缕述之。古今未闻有换心者，有之自此始。精诚所激，人穷而神应之。进士之奇颖，进士之奇愚逼而出也，所谓德慧存乎疢疾者也。或曰：“今天下之心，可换者多矣，安得一一捽其胸剖之，易其残者而使仁，易其污者而使廉，易其奸回邪佞者而使忠厚正直？”愚山子曰：“若是，神之斧日不暇给矣！且今天下之心皆是矣，又安所得仁者廉者忠若直者而纳之，而因易之哉？”

    Explanation

    159

    The Elderly Man Heard the Knocking Sound and Grasped a Stick to Wait at the Door. Once he heard that the teacher had returned, he invited him in. The teacher then recounted in detail what he had heard on the way. The elderly man thought it was a mistake, but after testing it, he found it to be true and was greatly pleased. From that point on, his intelligence became evident, and within a few years, he was enrolled as a student in the local school. Another few years later, he succeeded continuously and became a jinshi (imperial scholar-official). On the day the news arrived, the elderly man sat on a chair and laughed loudly, saying, “From now on, your father is free from begging!” He then opened his mouth and made a sound before dying.

    A relative of the elderly man, who was an official in the prefecture, met the narrator in 1660 on the road in Shandong and recounted the story in detail. In ancient and modern times, there has never been a case of a heart being replaced, but this is the first instance. Sincerity and earnestness can inspire divine intervention in times of dire straits. The extraordinary intelligence of a jinshi, as well as his extraordinary foolishness, are forced out. This is what is meant by the saying that virtue and wisdom can arise from adversity or illness. Someone said, “In today’s world, there are many hearts that can be changed. How can we change each person one by one, making the cruel ones kind, the corrupt ones clean, and the deceitful and wicked ones loyal, honest, and upright?” The Foolish Mountain Man replied, “If so, the god’s axe would not have enough time! Moreover, today’s hearts are all like that. How can we get kind, clean, loyal, and upright people to accept them and change them?”

    160

    [张山来曰：有形之心不能换，无形之心未尝不可换。人果肯换其无形者，安知不又有神焉并其有形者而换之耶？则谓进士公为自换其心也可。] 秦淮健儿传　金华李渔笠翁笠翁一家言
    　　嘉靖中，秦淮民间有一儿，貌魁梧，色黝异。生数月，便不乳，与大人同饮啜。周岁怙恃交失，鞠于外氏。长有膂力，善拳击，尝以一掌毙一犬，人遂呼为“健儿”。健儿与群儿斗，莫不辟易。群儿结数十辈攻之，健儿纵拳四挥，或啼或号，各抱头归，愬其父兄。父兄来叱曰：“谁家豚犬，敢与老子相触耶？”健儿曰：“焉敢相触？为长者服步武之劳，则可耳。”乃至父兄前，以两手擎父兄，两胫去地二尺许，且行且止，或昂之使高，或抑之使下，父兄恐颠仆，莫敢如何，但咭咭笑，乡人閧焉。

    160

    Zhang Shanlai said: The tangible heart cannot be changed, but the intangible heart has never been impossible to change. If a person is truly willing to change their intangible heart, how can one not know that there might also be a divine force that changes both the tangible and intangible aspects together? Then it can be said that Jinshi Gong has changed his own heart.

    The Tale of the Qinhuai Strong Man by Li Yu, the Scholar of Jinhuai and a man of one school of thought

    During the reign of Emperor Jiajing, in the Qinhuai River area, there was a child who was tall and strong, with a dark and unusual appearance. After just a few months, he no longer needed to be breastfed and drank and ate like an adult. At one year old, he lost both his parents and was raised by his relatives. As he grew up, he developed great physical strength and was skilled in boxing. He once killed a dog with one punch, and people began to call him "the Strong Man." The Strong Man fought with other children, and none of them could stand up to him. A group of dozens of children attacked him, but the Strong Man swung his fists four times, causing some to cry and others to howl, and they all ran home, complaining to their fathers and elder brothers. The fathers and elder brothers came and shouted, "Who are you, you little brat, daring to touch my father?" The Strong Man replied, "How dare I touch you? I will serve the elders by performing tasks, and that is all." He then stood in front of his father and elder brothers, lifting them with his two hands, and his legs were about two feet off the ground. He walked and stopped, sometimes lifting them high and sometimes lowering them. The father and elder brothers were afraid of falling and could do nothing, but they just giggled, and the villagers made a commotion.

    161

    健儿性善动，不喜读书。外氏命就外傅，不率教。师夏楚之，则夺扑裂眦曰：“功名应赤手致，焉用琐琐章句为？”师出，即与同塾诸儿斗，诸儿无完肤。又时盗其外氏簪珥衣物，向酒家饮，醉即猖狂生事。外氏苦之，逐于外。为人牧羊，每窃羊换饮，诈言多歧亡。主人怒，复见摈。时已弱冠矣。
    　　闻倭入寇，乃大快曰：“是我得意时也！”即去海上从军。从小校擢功至裨将。与僚友饮，酒酣斗力，毙之。罪当死，遂弃官，逃之泗，易姓名，隐于庖丁。民家有犊，丙夜往盗之，牵出，必剧呼曰：“君家牛我骑去矣！”呼竟，倒骑牛背，以斧砍牛臀。牛畏痛，迅奔若风，追之莫及。次日亡牛者适市物色之，健儿曰：“昨过君家取牛者我也，告而后取，道也，奚其盗？”索之，则牛已脯矣，无可凭。市中恶少，推为盟主，昼纵六博，夜游狭斜，自恃日甚。尝叹曰：“世人皆不足敌，但恨生千载后，不得与拔山举鼎之雄一较胜负耳！”

    Explanation

    161

    The Young Man with a Martial Spirit

    The young man was naturally inclined to movement and disliked reading. His maternal family ordered him to go to a private tutor, but he did not follow the teachings. When the teacher used corporal punishment, he snatched the rod and cried in pain, saying, "Honor and fame should be achieved without help or tools; why use trivial phrases and sentences?" After the teacher left, he immediately fought with the other children in the same school, leaving them with no part of their bodies unbruised. He also often stole hairpins, earrings, and clothes from his maternal family, went to taverns to drink, and created trouble when drunk. His maternal family suffered from him and expelled him. He served as a shepherd, often stealing sheep to exchange for drinks, and falsely claimed that the sheep were lost due to many paths. The master was angry and expelled him again. By then, he had reached adulthood.

    When he heard about the Japanese invasion, he greatly pleased himself, saying, "This is my time to shine!" He immediately enlisted in the army by the sea. Starting as a low-ranking officer, he was promoted through merit to the rank of deputy general. He drank with fellow officers, competed in strength when drunk, and killed one of them. Guilty of a capital crime, he abandoned his post, fled to Si, changed his name, and hid as a butcher. One night, he stole a calf from a commoner's house, dragged it out, and shouted loudly, "Your cow I rode away!" He shouted until he was done, rode backward on the cow's back, and hit the cow's rump with an axe. The cow, afraid of pain, ran quickly like the wind, and he could not catch it. The next day, the owner of the lost cow went to the market to look for it. The young man said, "I was the one who took your cow yesterday; I informed you before taking it. It is the right way; why call it stealing?" When searched, the cow had already been made into jerky, and there was no evidence. Among the bad boys in the market, he was elected as the leader, played six-sided dice during the day, and wandered the alleys at night, growing increasingly arrogant. Once, he said with regret, "Ordinary people are not worth fighting; but I regret being born so many years later, unable to compete with the heroes who could move mountains and lift cauldrons!"

    162

    邑使者禁屠牛，健儿无所事事，取向所屠牛皮及骨角，往瓜扬间售之，得三十金。将归，饮于馆中，解金置案头。酒家翁见之，谓曰：“前途多豪客，此物宜善藏之。”健儿掷杯砍案曰：“吾纵横天下三十年，未逢敌手，有能取得腰间物者，当叩首降之。”时有少年数人，醵于左席，闻之错愕，起问姓名居里。健儿曰：“某姓名不传，向尝竖功于边陲，今挂冠微服，牛耳于泗上诸英雄。”少年问能敌几何辈，健儿曰：“遇万万敌，遇千千敌。计人而敌，斯下矣！”诸少年益错愕。

    Explanation

    162

    During the reign of the town official who banned the slaughter of cattle, a strong man found himself with nothing to do. He took the hides and bones of the cattle that had been slaughtered earlier and went to the Gua-Yang region to sell them, earning thirty gold coins. On his way back, he drank in an inn and took out the gold, placing it on the table. The innkeeper saw this and said, "There are many bold and strong men ahead; this item should be well hidden." The strong man threw his cup and banged the table, saying, "I have roamed the world for thirty years and have not met an opponent. If anyone can take the item from my waist, I will kneel and submit to him." At that time, several young men were gathered at a table nearby and were shocked and surprised by his words. They got up and asked for his name and hometown. The strong man replied, "My name is not known, but I once served in the border regions. Now I have resigned and am in civilian clothes, known among the heroes of the Si River region." The young men asked how many people he could defeat. The strong man said, "I can defeat millions, thousands, and even count by the number of people. To count by the number of people is the lowest level!" The young men were even more shocked and surprised.

    163

    健儿饮毕，束装上马。不二三里，一骑追之甚迅。健儿自度曰：“殆所云豪客耶？”比至，则一后生，健儿遂不介意。后生问何之，健儿曰：“归泗。”后生曰：“予小子亦泗人，归途迷失，望长者指南之。”于是健儿前驱，马上谈笑颇相得。健儿谓后生曰：“子服弓矢，善决拾乎？”后生曰：“习矣，而未闲。”健儿援弓试之，力尽而弓不及彀，弃之，曰：“此物无用，佩之奚为？”后生曰：“物自有用，用物者无用耳。”乃引自试。时有鹜唳空，后生一发饮羽，鹜坠马前。健儿异之。后生曰：“君腰短刀，必善击刺。”健儿曰：“然！我所长不在彼，在此。”脱以相示，后生视而噱曰：“此割鸡屠狗物，将焉用之？”以两手一折，刀曲如钩，复以两手伸之，刀直如故。健儿失色，筹腰间物菲复我有矣。虽与偕行，而股栗之状，渐不自持。后生转以温言慰之。

    Explanation

    163

    The soldier finished his drink, packed up, and mounted his horse. Within a short distance, a rider caught up quickly. The soldier wondered, "Is this perhaps the bold guest I've heard of?" When they arrived, it was a young man. The soldier did not take it to heart. The young man asked where he was going, and the soldier replied, "To Si." The young man said, "I am also from Si, but I have lost my way. I hope the elder can guide me." The soldier led the way, and they talked amiably while riding. The soldier asked the young man, "Do you wear a bow and arrow? Are you skilled in aiming and shooting?" The young man replied, "I have practiced, but I am not yet proficient." The soldier tried to draw the bow, but despite his efforts, the bow did not reach the proper tension. He threw it aside and said, "This weapon is useless. Why carry it?" The young man said, "The weapon itself has its use; it is the user who is useless." He then tried it himself. At that moment, a wild duck called out in the sky, and the young man shot it with one arrow, which pierced the duck and caused it to fall in front of the horse. The soldier was amazed. The young man said, "You wear a short sword at your waist; you must be skilled in stabbing and thrusting." The soldier replied, "Yes! My strength lies not in that, but in this." He drew the sword to show it, and the young man looked at it and laughed, saying, "This is a tool for slaughtering chickens and dogs. What use is it?" With his hands, he bent the sword until it curved like a hook, and then straightened it again, and the sword returned to its original shape. The soldier was shocked, realizing that the object in his waistband was no longer his. Though they continued to travel together, the soldier's fear grew, and he could no longer control himself. The young man then tried to comfort him with gentle words.

    164

    复前数里，四顾无人，后生纵声一喝，健儿坠马。后生先斩其马，曰：“今日之事，有不唯我命者，如此马！”健儿匍伏请所欲。后生曰：“无用物，盍解腰缠来献！”健儿解囊输之，顿首乞命。后生曰：“吾得此一囊金，差可十日醉。子犹草莱，何足诛锄？”拨马寻故道去。健儿神气沮丧，足循循不前。自思三十金非长物，但半世英雄，败于乳臭儿之手，何颜复见诸弟兄？遂不归泗，向一村墅结庐卖酒聊生。每思往事，辄恧恧欲死。

    Explanation

    164

    Returning a few miles, he looked around and saw no one. The young man let out a loud shout, and the warrior fell off his horse. The young man first cut down the horse, saying, "Today's matter, if anyone does not obey my command, let this horse be an example!" The warrior crawled and begged for what he desired. The young man said, "There are no useful things here, why not untie the money from your waist and offer it?" The warrior untied his bag and handed over the money, bowing his head and begging for mercy. The young man said, "With this bag of gold, I can drink for ten days. You are still a commoner, not worth killing." He turned the horse and went back the old way. The warrior's spirit was defeated, his steps slow and hesitant. He thought to himself, "Thirty pieces of gold are not much, but half a lifetime of heroism, defeated by a child, how can I face my brothers again?" So he did not return to Si, but instead built a hut in a village and sold wine to make a living. Whenever he thought of past events, he felt ashamed and wanted to die.

    165

    一日，春风淡荡，有数少年索饮，裘马甚都，似五陵公子，而意气豪纵，又似长安游侠儿。击案狂歌，旁若无人，且曰：“涤器翁似不俗，当偕之。”遂拉健儿入座。健儿视九人皆弱冠，唯一总角者，貌白皙若处子，等闲不发一言，一言则九人倾听；坐则右之，饮则先之。健儿不解其故。而末坐一冠者，似尝谋面，睇视之，则向斩马劫财之人也，谓健儿曰：“东君尚识故人耶？”健儿不敢应。后生曰：“畴昔途中，解囊缠赠我者，非子而谁？我侪岂攘攫者流？特于邮旁肆中，闻子大言恐世，故来与子雌雄，不意竟输我一筹！今来归赵璧耳。

    Explanation

    165

    One day, with a gentle spring breeze, several young men sought a drink. They were well-dressed and well-equipped, resembling the nobility of the Five Tombs region, and exuded a bold and unrestrained spirit, much like young adventurers from Chang'an. They banged the table and sang wildly, as if no one else were present, and said, "The pot-washer doesn't seem vulgar, we should invite him to join us." Thus, they pulled the strong man to sit with them. The strong man looked at the nine young men, all of whom were around twenty years old, but one was a young boy with his hair tied up in two buns, with a fair complexion like a young maiden. He rarely spoke, but when he did, the nine others listened attentively. He was seated to their right and drank before them. The strong man did not understand why. The person sitting at the end of the table, who was wearing a hat, seemed to have met the strong man before and looked at him sideways, saying, "Do you still remember your old acquaintance, East Lord?" The strong man dared not respond. One of the younger men said, "In the past, the one who took out his bag and gave me something was none other than you. We are not thieves or robbers. We only came to the inn by the post office to hear you speak grandly and fearlessly to the world, so we challenged you. Unintentionally, I ended up losing to you by one point! Today, we come to return the Heirloom Jade."

    166

    ”遂出左袖三十金置案头，曰：“此母也。于今一年，子当肖之。”又探右袖，出三十金，共予之。健儿不敢受，旁一后生拔剑努目曰：“物为人攫而不能复，还之又不敢取，安用此懦夫为？”健儿惧，急内袖中，乃治鸡黍为欢。诸后生不肯留。归金者曰：“翁亦可怜矣，峻拒之则难堪。”众乃止。时爨下薪穷，健儿欲乞诸邻，后生指屋旁枯株谓之曰：“盍载斧斤？”健儿曰：“正苦无斧斤耳。”后生踌躇久之，曰：“此事须让十弟，我九人无能为也。”总角者以两手抱株，左右数挠，株已卧矣，遂拔剑砍旁柯燃之。酒至无算，乃辞去，竟不知其何许人。

    Explanation

    166

    """Then he took out thirty gold pieces from his left sleeve and placed them on the table, saying, "This is the mother. In a year, the child should resemble it." He then took out another thirty gold pieces from his right sleeve and gave them all to him. The young man dared not accept. A young man beside him drew his sword and glared, saying, "If the object has been taken by someone and you cannot get it back, and if you return it but dare not take it, what use is there for such a coward?" The young man, frightened, quickly put the gold back in his sleeve. Then he prepared chicken and millet to make merry. The other young men did not want to stay. The one returning the gold said, "The old man is also pitiful; if you reject him harshly, it would be embarrassing." They then stopped. At that time, the firewood in the stove was exhausted. The young man wanted to beg from the neighbors. The young man pointed to a withered tree beside the house and said to him, "Why don't you bring an axe?" The young man replied, "I was just complaining about not having an axe." The young man hesitated for a long time and said, "This matter must be left to the tenth brother; we nine cannot do anything about it." The boy with a topknot held the tree with both hands, shook it a few times, and the tree fell over. Then he drew his sword and cut branches from nearby trees to start a fire. They drank until they could no longer count the cups, and then they took their leave. They never knew who these people were."""

    167

    健儿自是绝不与人较力，人殴之则袖手不报。或曰：“子曩日英雄安在？”健儿则以衰朽谢之。后得以天年终，不可谓非后生力也。
    　　[张山来曰：尝见稗官中，有赵东山夸技顺城门，其事与此相类。甚矣，毋谓秦无人也！] 山东四女祠记　姑苏黄始静御听莺堂集
    　　丙辰十月，出都门，畏陆行之劳悴也，舍而之舟。舟行六七日，将至黄河崖。过一村，风急不得行，遂泊舟。人曰：“此四女镇也。”初未详“四女”何以名。
    　　泊少间，风息。卧舟中，闷甚。起行崖岸间，一望荒沙，市人皆闭户，无憩立所。迄市尾一古祠，若无人焉者。入门，閴如也。庭一碑，藤藓网布。碑前古树，半无枝叶，秃而龙身。右转得一径，进则老屋三楹而已。中座像二，一老翁，庞眉而古衣冠；一老媪，白发高髻，咸非近世饰。独两旁侍坐者四人，虽儒衣儒冠，而修眉皓齿，皎若好女子。心颇疑之，无从询其说。乃扪藤剥藓，拭其文读之，盖明成化年碑也。

    Explanation

    167

    The strong man naturally does not engage in physical contests with others, and if someone beats him, he stands by and does not retaliate. Someone might say, "Where is the hero of your past days?" The strong man responds with the excuse of his age and decline. Later, he was able to live out his natural life, which cannot be denied was due to the help of younger generations.

    [Zhang Shanlai said: I once saw in a minor official's records that Zhao Dongshan boasted his skills at the Shuncheng Gate, and the story is similar to this. Indeed, do not say that there are no people in Qin!] Record of the Four Ladies Shrine in Shandong, by Huang Shijing of Suzhou, collected in the Yuting Ying Tang Ji.

    In the tenth month of the Bingchen year, I left the capital, fearing the fatigue of land travel, and instead went by boat. The boat traveled for six or seven days, approaching the Yellow River bank. Passing a village, the wind was too strong to proceed, so we moored the boat. People said, "This is the Four Ladies Town." At first, I did not know why it was called the Four Ladies Town.

    After a short while, the wind died down. I lay in the boat, feeling very bored. I got up and walked along the riverbank, looking out at the desolate sand, with all the townspeople closed in their homes, nowhere to rest or stand. At the end of the market, there was an ancient shrine, as if deserted. Entering the gate, it was silent and empty. In the courtyard, there was a stone tablet covered with vines and moss. Before the tablet stood an ancient tree, half without branches or leaves, its trunk like a dragon. Turning right, I found a path leading to three old houses. In the central hall were two statues: one of an elderly man with thick eyebrows and ancient attire; one of an elderly woman with white hair and a high bun, both not resembling modern dress. Only four figures sitting on either side, dressed in scholarly robes and hats, had well-groomed brows, white teeth, and a radiant beauty like that of young women. I was quite puzzled, with no one to ask for an explanation. I then touched the vines, peeled off the moss, and wiped the text to read it, which turned out to be a stone tablet from the Chenghua period of the Ming dynasty.

    168

    碑载汉景帝时，地有傅姓长者，好善，年五十，无子，生四女，皆明慧知礼。寿日觞父，父曰：“吾五十无子，奚寿为？”四女愀然曰：“父期于子者，为终养计也。儿即女，亦可代子职养父母，父母其勿忧。”明日，俱改男子装，四女共矢不嫁，以侍其亲。时佛未入中国，唯读五经百家周秦以上书，博览奥义如大儒。间则行善事，德化洽于乡里。庭前古柏树，叶生龙爪，树身生鳞，金色灿然。乡里咸骇异，以为孝感所致。如是者三十年。一日，天神鼓乐降于庭，树化为龙，载翁媪及四女上升而去。里人感之，遂为建祠，今所树趾，遗迹也。

    Explanation

    168

    The inscription on the stele records that during the reign of Emperor Jing of Han, there was an elderly gentleman of the Fu family who loved goodness. He was fifty years old and had no sons, but he had four daughters, all of whom were intelligent, wise, and well-versed in propriety. On his birthday, they celebrated with wine, and he said, "At fifty, I have no son, what is there to celebrate?" The four daughters, looking solemn, replied, "What is expected of a son is to provide for his parents in their old age. As daughters, we can also take on the role of sons and serve our parents, so please do not worry." The next day, they all changed into men's clothing and vowed together not to marry, in order to serve their parents. At that time, Buddhism had not yet entered China, and they only read the Five Classics, the Hundred Schools of Thought, and books from the Zhou and Qin dynasties, having a profound understanding of their teachings like great scholars. They often performed good deeds, and their moral influence spread throughout the village. There was an ancient pine tree in front of their courtyard, its leaves shaped like dragon claws, and its trunk covered with golden scales. The villagers were all astonished and believed this was due to the daughters' filial piety. This continued for thirty years. One day, heavenly gods descended with music, the tree transformed into a dragon, and it carried the old couple and the four daughters up and away. The villagers were moved and built a temple in their honor. Today, the place where the tree once stood is a relic.

    169

    呜呼！自汉景帝迄今，不知千几百年，及遍考东国舆图纪载，都无所谓“四女祠”者，而孝感之报，徒得之于荒烟蔓草中。乃知古人轶事，其湮没不传者概不乏云。
    　　[张山来曰：昔汉缇萦上书赎父罪，因除肉刑，此只一人耳，不难自行其意。今四女同心，尤为仅见也。] 鲁颠传　海宁朱一是近修为可堂集
    　　颠不知何里人，独行吴越间，体上裸，披单大襆，襆中圆一孔，下体着絮厚裩，污重染，不易也。鬓飞蓬，足跣而跳。手一龟，龟习颠，颠俯首则龟昂，鼻息相接以为常。颠所过，群儿什百怪随之。颠即踞地展襆，头出中孔，伸缩象龟行，群儿狎且笑。又坦腹命群儿拳。腹坚，群儿争拳之，痛；更击以石，石碎，腹橐橐然。颠喜酒，酒鼻饮。群儿愿观颠鼻饮，多就家索酒酒颠也。夜倒悬桥梁或城女墙卧，鼾鼾焉。

    Explanation

    169

    Alas! From Emperor Jing of Han to the present day, spanning countless centuries, after thoroughly examining the maps and records of the eastern countries, there is no such thing as the "Four Women's Shrine." Yet, the reward of filial piety was found only in the desolate and neglected places. Thus, we come to understand that many ancient anecdotes have been lost and not passed down.

    [Commentary by Zhang Shanlai: In ancient times, Ti Ying petitioned the emperor to save her father from a death sentence, thereby abolishing corporal punishment. This was the act of one person, and it was not difficult to carry out. Now, the unity of the four women is especially rare.]

    The Biography of Lu Dian
    From the collection Ketang Ji by Zhu Yishen of Hai Ning

    Lu Dian did not know from which village he came, wandering alone between Wu and Yue. He was naked above, wearing a large single cloth, with a round hole in the middle. Below, he wore thick undergarments, heavily soiled and difficult to clean. His hair was like flying thistles, and he walked barefoot and jumped. He held a turtle, which was accustomed to him. When he lowered his head, the turtle raised its head, and their breaths met as a habit. Wherever he went, a hundred strange children followed him. Lu Dian would sit on the ground and spread the cloth, his head emerging from the hole, stretching and retracting like a turtle, and the children played and laughed. He would also lay his stomach open and command the children to punch it. His stomach was tough, and the children punched it, feeling pain; they hit it with stones, the stones broke, and his stomach made a橐橐 sound. Lu Dian loved wine and drank through his nose. The children wished to see him drink through his nose, so they went to many homes to fetch wine for him. At night, he hung upside down from bridges or the city walls and slept, snoring loudly.

    170

    横江徐氏者，好事人也，要颠归，问吐纳水火之术，不答，唯日戏群儿如故。颠食尽一器，徐故予大器，无问多寡，食辄尽。又故以肥腻冷水诸不可口物内器，无问多寡予颠，颠亦食辄尽。问颠：“浴乎？”曰：“浴。”然殿人浴。微窥之，见颠方呼呼然，俯水面饮前浴人垢，不更去己垢也。夜无桥梁城女墙，则悬足架上，垂首卧。夜分人定，即溺。人乘颠起，入问之，颠语庄，微及日用细碎，卒不答吐纳水火事。
    　　在吴越十余年，人皆识之。一日过华亭，太守方岳贡出见市儿数百哗曰：“颠来！颠来！”怪问颠，不答。再问，再不答。以为惑民，系且杖，杖下而颠死矣。后有人入杭之西山，复见颠曳杖躄躄行。朱子曰：颠，吾知其不死。

    Explanation

    170

    A good-hearted person from Hengjiang named Xu invited Di to return and asked him about the art of breathing in and out fire and water. Di did not answer and only played with children as usual. Di ate up one bowl, and Xu then gave him a large bowl, regardless of how much was in it, and Di ate it all up. Xu also put greasy, cold water, and other unpalatable things into the bowl, regardless of how much was in it, and gave them to Di, who also ate them all up. Xu asked Di, "Do you bathe?" Di replied, "Yes, I bathe." Then he bathed with the palace people. Xu peeped at him and saw Di bent down to drink the dirt from the water surface of those who bathed before him, without washing off his own dirt. At night, without bridges or city walls, Di hung his feet on the scaffold and hung his head to sleep. At midnight, when people were settled, he urinated. When people got up, they entered to ask him, and Di spoke vaguely about daily trivial matters but never answered questions about the art of breathing in and out fire and water.

    Di lived in Wu and Yue for over ten years, and people all knew him. One day, he passed through Huating, and the governor Fang Yuegong came out to see a crowd of several hundred children shouting, "Di is here! Di is here!" He questioned Di, but Di did not answer. He asked again, and Di did not answer again. They thought Di was misleading the people, so they tied him up and beat him. Di died after the beating. Later, someone saw Di limping and dragging a walking stick in the West Mountain of Hangzhou. Zhu Xi said, "Di, I know he is not dead."

    171

    [张山来曰：世人谓颠为颠，吾知颠必以世人为颠；则谓颠非倒卧而世人为倒卧，亦无不可。] 林四娘记　三山林云铭西仲损斋焚馀
    　　晋江陈公宝钥，号绿厓。康熙二年，任山东青州道佥事。夜辄闻传桶有敲击声，问之，则寂无应者。其仆不胜扰，持枪往伺，欲刺之。是夜但闻怒詈声，已而推中门突入，则见有鬼，青面獠牙，赤体挺立，头及屋檐。仆震骇，失枪仆地。陈急出，诃之曰：“此朝廷公署，汝何方妖魅，敢擅至此？”鬼笑曰：“闻尊仆欲见刺，特来受枪耳。”陈怒，思檄兵格之。甫起念，鬼又笑曰：“檄兵格我，计何疏也？”陈愈怒。迟明，调标兵二十名守门。抵夜，鬼却从墙角出，长仅三尺许，头大如轮，口张如箕，双眸开合有光，媻跚于地，冷气袭人。兵大呼发炮矢，炮火不燃。检韔中矢，又无一存者。鬼反持弓回射，矢如雨集，俱向众兵头面掠过，亦不之伤。兵惧奔溃。

    Explanation

    171

    Zhang Shanlai said: The world calls diān as diān, but I know that diān must consider the world as diān; thus, to say that diān is not lying upside down while the world is lying upside down, it is also possible.

    Chen Baoyue, a man from Jinjiang, was known as Green Cliff. In the second year of the Kangxi reign, he served as the vice-magistrate of Qingzhou. At night, he would often hear the sound of someone knocking on the water vat. When he asked, there was no response. His servant, unable to bear the disturbance, took a spear to guard and intended to attack the intruder. That night, he only heard angry curses. Soon after, the ghost pushed open the central door and suddenly entered, revealing a specter with a green face and fangs, naked and standing upright, with its head touching the eaves. The servant was terrified, dropped his spear, and fell to the ground. Chen rushed out and scolded the ghost, saying, "This is a government office of the court. How dare you, a demon from some unknown place, come here擅自?" The ghost laughed and said, "I heard that your servant wanted to kill me, so I came to receive the spear." Chen became even angrier and thought of summoning soldiers to confront it. As soon as he thought of this, the ghost laughed again, "Summoning soldiers to confront me, how shortsighted is your plan?" Chen was furious. The next day, he called twenty guards to stand watch at the gate. That night, the ghost emerged from the corner of the wall, only about three feet tall, with a head as large as a wheel, a mouth wide like a winnowing basket, and eyes that opened and closed with light. It staggered on the ground, and a cold wind blew over the soldiers. The soldiers shouted and fired arrows and cannonballs, but the cannon failed to ignite. They checked their quivers and found no arrows left. The ghost then held a bow and shot back, arrows raining down, all of which passed over the soldiers' heads and faces without injuring them. The soldiers were terrified and fled in panic.

    172

    陈又延神巫作法驱遣，夜宿署中。时腊月严寒，陈甫就寝，鬼直诣巫卧所，攫去衾毡衣裤。巫窘急呼救。陈不得已，出为哀祈。鬼笑曰：“闻此神巫乃有法者也，技止此乎？”遂掷还所攫。次日，神巫惭惧，辞去。自后署中飞炮掷瓦，晨昏不宁。或见墙覆栋崩，急避之，仍无他故。陈患焉。
    　　嗣余有同年友刘望龄赴都，取道青州，询知其故，谓陈曰：“君自取患耳！天下之理，有阳则有阴。若不急于驱遣，亦未扰扰至此。”语未竟，鬼出谢之。刘视其狞恶可畏，劝令改易颜面，鬼即辞入暗室中。少选复出，则一国色丽人，云鬟靓妆，袅袅婷婷而至。衣皆鲛绡雾縠，亦无缝缀之迹，香气飘扬，莫可名状。自称为林四娘，有一仆名实道，一婢名东姑，皆有影无形。唯四娘则与生人了无异相也。陈日与欢饮赋诗，亲狎备至，唯不及乱而已。凡署中文牒，多出其手，遇久年疑狱，则为廉访始末，陈一讯皆服。观风试士，衡文甲乙悉当，名誉大振。

    Explanation

    172

    Chen again invited a spirit-medium to perform rituals to expel the ghosts and stayed overnight in the office. It was the dead of winter. As soon as Chen went to bed, a ghost went straight to the medium's sleeping place and took away his quilts, mats, clothes, and pants. The medium panicked and called for help. Having no choice, Chen went out to plead. The ghost laughed and said, "I heard that this spirit-medium has skills, but are they only this much?" So he threw back what he had taken. The next day, the medium was ashamed and afraid, and left. After that, the office was plagued by flying stones and thrown tiles, day and night. Sometimes walls collapsed and beams fell, and people hurried to avoid them, but there was no other harm. Chen was troubled by this.

    Later, a friend of Chen's named Liu Wangling, who was going to the capital, took the road through Qingzhou and learned about the matter. He told Chen, "You have brought this trouble upon yourself! The principle of the world is that where there is yang, there must be yin. If you don't rush to expel them, you won't be so disturbed." Before he finished speaking, the ghost appeared and thanked him. Liu saw that it was fierce and terrifying and advised it to change its appearance. The ghost immediately withdrew into a dark room. After a short while, it came out again as a beautiful woman with cloud-like hair and a well-dressed appearance, graceful and elegant. Her clothes were made of fine silk and misty gauze, without any seams, and her fragrance was wafting, indescribable. She called herself Lin Siniang and had a servant named Shidao and a maid named Donggu, who all had shadows but were invisible. Only Siniang looked exactly like a living person. Chen spent days drinking and composing poetry with her, being very intimate, but they did not go beyond that. All the documents in the office were mostly written by her. When there were long-standing doubtful cases, she would investigate the origins, and Chen would question them, and they would all confess. When examining the morals and testing the scholars, she judged the quality of their essays accurately, and her reputation grew greatly.

    173

    先是陈需次燕邸，贷京商二千缗。商急索，不能应，议偿其半，不允。四娘出责之曰：“陈公岂负债者？顾一时力不及耳。若必取盈，陷其图利败检，于汝安乎？我鬼也，不从吾言，力能祸汝！”京商素不信鬼，笑曰：“汝乃丽人，以鬼怖我？若果鬼也，当知我在京庐舍职业。”四娘曰：“庐舍职业，何难详道？汝近日于某处行一负心之事，说出恐就死耳。”京商大骇，辞去。陈密叩商所为，终不泄，其隐人之恶如此。
    　　性耽吟咏，所著诗，多感慨凄楚之音，人不忍读。凡吾闽有访陈者，必与狎饮。临别则赠诗，其中度词，日后多验。有一士人悦其姿容，偶起淫念。四娘怒曰：“此獠何得无礼？”喝令杖责。士人歘然仆地，号痛求哀，两臂杖痕周匝。举坐为之请，乃呼婢东姑持药饮之，了无痛苦，仍与欢饮如初。

    Explanation

    173

    Before this, Mr. Chen served in the Yandian and borrowed 2,000 strings of cash from a merchant in the capital. The merchant urgently demanded repayment, but Mr. Chen could not respond, and they discussed repaying half, but he refused. Fourth Miss stepped forward and scolded him, saying, "Mr. Chen is not someone who owes money? It's just that he lacks the means at the moment. If you insist on getting the full amount, you will make him seek profit at the expense of his integrity. How can you feel at ease with that? I am a ghost. If you don't listen to me, I have the power to harm you!" The merchant in the capital never believed in ghosts and laughed, saying, "You are a beautiful woman. Are you trying to scare me with ghosts? If you really are a ghost, you should know about my residence and occupation in the capital." Fourth Miss replied, "My residence and occupation are not hard to describe. You recently did something unfaithful at a certain place. Speaking of it might even cause your death." The merchant was greatly frightened and left. Mr. Chen discreetly inquired about the merchant's actions, but he never revealed it. Such is his way of hiding people's evil deeds.

    She was fond of composing poetry, and the poems she wrote were filled with poignant and sorrowful tones. People couldn't bear to read them. Whenever someone from our province visited Mr. Chen, they would always drink together. Before parting, she would give them a poem. The words in her poems often came true later. There was a scholar who admired her appearance and occasionally had lewd thoughts. Fourth Miss angrily said, "How dare this scoundrel be so impolite?" She ordered him to be beaten. The scholar suddenly fell to the ground, cried in pain, and begged for mercy. Bruises covered his arms. Everyone present pleaded for him. She then called her maid Donggu to give him medicine. He felt no pain and they continued to drink joyfully as before.

    174

    陈叩其为神始末，答曰：“我莆田人也，故明崇祯年间，父为江宁府库官，逋帑下狱。我与表兄某悉力营救，同卧起半载，实无私情。父出狱，而疑不释。我因投缳以明无他，烈魂不散耳。与君有桑梓之谊而来，非偶然也。”计在署十有八月而别，别后陈每思慕不置。康熙六年，陈补任江南驿传道，为余述其事，属记之。
    　　林子曰：《左氏传》言涉鬼神，后儒病其诬。余窃疑天下大矣，二百四十余年中，岂无一二人出于见闻所不及乎？今陈公绿厓，正士也，非能造言语者。且吾乡士人，往往有亲见之者。王龙溪云：神怪之事，圣人不语。力与乱明明是有，怪与神岂得云无？鬼能见形预人事，不可谓非神怪矣。然强魄暂留人间，终归变灭，不能久存。是在精气为物，游魂为变之外，非可以常理推究，言有言无，皆惑也。此圣人所以不语也夫？

    Explanation

    174

    Chen inquired about the beginning and end of being a spirit, and the response was: "I am from Putian. During the reign of Emperor Chongzhen in the Ming Dynasty, my father was an official in charge of the treasury in Jiangning, and he was imprisoned for evading taxes. I and my cousin spared no effort in rescuing him, sharing the same bed and meals for half a year, and indeed, there was no private affection. After my father was released from prison, the suspicion did not dissipate. I hung myself to prove that there was no other motive, and the spirit of righteousness did not dissipate. I came here because of the bond of hometown friendship, which was not accidental." Chen stayed in the office for eighteen months before departing, and after the separation, Chen often longed for him. In the sixth year of the Kangxi era, Chen was appointed as the postal and transportation commissioner in Jiangnan and recounted this story to me, asking me to record it.

    Linszi said: The Zuo Commentary mentions involvement with ghosts and spirits, and later Confucian scholars criticized it as false. I secretly doubt that the world is vast, and in over two hundred and forty years, could there not be one or two people beyond what one has seen or heard? Today, Chen Gong Lüeyá is a righteous person, not someone who fabricates words. Moreover, scholars from my hometown often claim to have personally seen such things. Wang Longxi said: Matters concerning spirits and monsters, sages do not speak about them. The power and chaos are clearly present, how can spirits and monsters be said to not exist? Ghosts can appear and foretell human affairs, so they cannot be called anything but spirits and monsters. However, the strong spirit temporarily lingers in the human world, but ultimately returns to transformation and annihilation, unable to remain for long. This is beyond the realm of spirit becoming matter and wandering souls becoming transformations, and cannot be investigated by conventional reasoning. To say there is or is not, both are delusions. This is why sages do not speak about it.

    175

    [张山来曰：先君明季时客楚抚军署中，宾客杂遝，室无空虚。旁有园，扃鐍甚固。先君谓众客曰：“曷不迁入此中，俾稍稍舒眉乎？”或答曰：“此内有鬼，是以未敢耳。”因询其状，乃知前抚军有女，及笄而死，遂葬此中。每际清风明月，辄见形于回廊曲槛间，徘徊徙倚，如不胜情。人惧其为祟，故常扃之。先君大喜曰：“审若是，是故我所祷祀而求者也！”遂请独居其内，日以二小童给侍，夜则遣去，冀有所遇，而卒无见闻。事载《天山楼随笔》。今林四娘独能变现若此，则又何也？岂必无罪而冤死者乃能为厉耶？] 乞者王翁传　建昌徐芳仲光悬榻编

    Explanation

    175

    张山来曰：先君明季时客楚抚军署中，宾客杂遝，室无空虚。旁有园，扃鐍甚固。先君谓众客曰：“曷不迁入此中，俾稍稍舒眉乎？”或答曰：“此内有鬼，是以未敢耳。”因询其状，乃知前抚军有女，及笄而死，遂葬此中。每际清风明月，辄见形于回廊曲槛间，徘徊徙倚，如不胜情。人惧其为祟，故常扃之。先君大喜曰：“审若是，是故我所祷祀而求者也！”遂请独居其内，日以二小童给侍，夜则遣去，冀有所遇，而卒无见闻。事载《天山楼随笔》。今林四娘独能变现若此，则又何也？岂必无罪而冤死者乃能为厉耶？

    Zhang Shanlai said: My late father was a guest in the residence of the governor of Chu during the late Ming dynasty. The rooms were always filled with guests. There was a garden nearby, which was very securely locked. My father said to the guests, "Why not move into this place, so that we might slightly ease our worries?" Someone replied, "There is a ghost inside, so we dare not." Upon inquiring about the situation, it was learned that the previous governor had a daughter who died at the age of marriage and was buried there. Whenever there was a clear wind and bright moon, her spirit would appear in the winding corridors and curved railings, wandering and leaning, as if overwhelmed with emotion. People feared that she would cause harm, so they often kept the garden locked. My father was greatly pleased and said, "If it is indeed so, then this is what I have been praying for and seeking!" He then requested to live alone inside, with two young servants attending him during the day, but sending them away at night, hoping to encounter something, but ultimately nothing was seen or heard. This story is recorded in The Notes of the Tian Shan Tower. Now, only Lin Si娘 can transform like this, what is the reason for this? Is it necessarily the case that only those who died unjustly can become vengeful spirits?

    176

    洒口王氏，樵郡大姓也。其先世某翁，尝行乞至拏口陈长者家。日尚早，小憩门首。有顷户启，一小环捧盆水，向外倾洒去。有声铿然，随水堕地，视之，金钏也。翁大喜，复念此钏必主妇洗妆置盆中，而环不知，倘主妇索钏不得，而疑环盗，或挞之急，且有变。吾贫人，横得重资，未必能享，而贻环累，以至不测，大不祥。遂留以待。久之，微闻户内喧声，似有所诃责。斯须，前环出，流血被面，望溪便掷。翁急前，持抱问故。环掷愈力，曰：“主妇失钏，而枉予盗。予何处得钏？与挞死，宁溺死！”翁曰：“然，钏在，毋恐。”乃出诸袖中，俾持入，且曰：“待子于此久矣。”环入报，主妇以为谩，遣童出问翁，具以实对。

    Explanation

    176

    The Wang family of Nuo, a prominent clan in the region of Qiao, had an ancestor, an old man, who once came to beg at the home of an elderly gentleman named Chen in Nuo. It was still early in the day, and he took a brief rest at the door. After a while, the door opened, and a little girl held a basin of water, pouring it outwards. There was a clear, ringing sound, followed by the water and something falling to the ground. Looking at it, he saw it was a golden bracelet. The old man was very happy, but he thought again that this bracelet must have been placed in the basin by the lady when she was washing her makeup, and the girl did not know about it. If the lady could not find the bracelet and suspected the girl of stealing it, she might beat her severely, and there might be trouble. As a poor person, he had unexpectedly gained a valuable item, may not be able to enjoy it, and bring trouble to the girl, leading to unforeseen consequences, which would be great misfortune. So he decided to wait. After a long time, he faintly heard commotion inside the house, as if someone was scolding. Soon, the girl came out, with blood on her face, and looked at the stream, ready to throw herself in. The old man rushed forward, held her, and asked why. The girl threw herself even harder and said, "The lady lost the bracelet and wrongly accused me of stealing it. Where did I get the bracelet? If beaten to death, I would rather drown myself!" The old man said, "Yes, the bracelet is here, don't be afraid." He took it out of his sleeve, gave it to her to take in, and said, "I have been waiting for you here for a long time." The girl went in to report, and the lady thought it was a joke, sent a child to ask the old man, and he told the truth.

    177

    事闻长者，长者曰：“世安得有此人？”亟召入，居然壮男子也。因问：“若能为我任奔走乎？”对曰：“幸甚！”于是使司门户稽察，辄胜任。则又使出入市贾，征责租课，又辄称。长者益喜，遂以前环妻之，而使主庄佃某所。翁益殚竭心力以谨恪报。长者知翁可任，益亲爱，待以家人礼，诸钱谷会计之重要者，悉以寄之。

    Explanation

    177

    The matter was heard by the elder. The elder said, "How can there be such a person in the world?" He quickly summoned the man, and he turned out to be a sturdy young man. The elder then asked, "Can you handle running errands for me?" The man replied, "I am very fortunate!" The elder then had him manage the gate and conduct inspections, and he performed this task well. Next, the elder had him go in and out of markets, and he also did this well. The elder was increasingly pleased and decided to marry his daughter to him, while also having him manage a certain estate or farm. The man exerted all his effort and diligence in service. The elder knew that the man was capable and grew to love him, treating him with the courtesy of family, and entrusted all important financial and accounting matters to him.

    178

    翁任事既久，橐渐裕，而所娶环生数子，皆颖敏。既长，使之分道商贩，遂大富，致产巨万。翁乃谢陈氏事，携环与子归洒口，为素封家。享年耄耋，孙曾辈读书为诸生者十余人，翁皆及亲见之，今门第人文之盛，与陈颉云。
    　　噫！一乞人得金环值数十金，可以饱矣，返之奚为哉？愚山子曰：翁非特廉也，仁且智也：其不取非有，廉也；逆计主妇之重责环，环急且死，而候其出救之，以白其枉而脱其祸，仁也；救环得环，而免于乞，智也。使翁匿环而往，十数金止矣，卒岁之奉耳，视此所得孰多乎？方其逡巡户外时，岂尝计及此哉？而报随之，谓天之无心，又安可也？今之读书明礼义，据地豪盛，长喙铦距，择弱肉而食之，至于冤楚死丧，宛转当前而不顾者，盖有之矣。况彼遗而我遇，取之自然者乎？吾故不敢鄙夷于乞而直翁之。夫乞而贤，即翁之可也。

    178

    The old man had been engaged in his duties for a long time, and his purse gradually became prosperous. He married a woman named Huan, who bore him several children, all of whom were sharp and sensitive. When they grew up, he sent them out to engage in commerce separately, and they became immensely wealthy, amassing a fortune in the millions. The old man then relinquished his affairs with the Chen family, took Huan and his children back to Sihou, and became a wealthy family. He lived to an advanced age, and among his grandchildren and great-grandchildren, there were over a dozen who studied and became scholars, all of whom he saw with his own eyes. Today, the prosperity of his family's status and culture is on par with the Chen family.

    Alas! A beggar found a gold ring worth dozens of gold pieces, which was enough to satisfy his hunger. Why did he return it? The Foolish Mountain Man says: The old man was not only frugal but also benevolent and wise: his refusal to take what was not his was frugality; his anticipation of the heavy blame the mistress would have for the ring, and his patience in waiting for her to come out and rescue him, to clear his innocence and free him from disaster, was benevolence; saving the ring by returning it and escaping from begging was wisdom. If the old man had hidden the ring and kept it, he would have only had a dozen gold pieces, enough to last the year, but how does that compare to what he gained here? When he hesitated outside the door, did he ever consider this? And yet, his reward followed, and how could one say that heaven is indifferent? Today, those who study and understand propriety, who are powerful and prosperous, with long beaks and sharp claws, who prey on the weak, and who turn away from眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不顾眼前的不幸而不

    179

    或曰：王氏，大姓也，而其祖贫至于乞，此其子孙之所深讳，而子暴之，无乃不可乎？愚山子曰：不然！人唯其行之可传而名，亦唯其品之可尊而贵。名与贵不关其所遭，关其人之贤不肖也。若翁之所行，是古之大贤，王氏子孙当世世师之，又奚讳乎？师其廉仁且智者，以穷则守身，而达则善世，何行之弗成焉？乞宁足讳也？彼行之不道，虽荣显贵势，若操、惇、莽、卞、杞、桧之流，乃真乞人之所不为，而其子孙所羞以为祖父者！
    　　[张山来曰：东坡有言，上可以陪玉皇大帝，下可以陪卑田院乞儿。然则可以陪乞儿者，皆足以陪玉帝者也。盖乞人一种，非至愚无用之流，即其大慈悲而有守者，不屑为倡优隶卒，不肯为机械以攫人财，不得不出于行乞之一途耳。至王翁之高行，则又为此中翘楚矣。] 雷州盗记　徐芳仲光诺皋广志

    Explanation

    179

    Final Translation

    Some say: The Wang family is a prominent surname, yet their ancestor was so poor that he resorted to begging. This is something their descendants deeply conceal, but you are considering revealing it. Wouldn't this be inappropriate? The Foolish Mountain Man says: Not so! A person's actions that can be passed down and remembered, and their character that can be respected and honored, are what truly matter. Fame and honor are not determined by one's circumstances, but by the person's virtue or lack thereof. If your ancestor's actions were those of a great sage of ancient times, the Wang family's descendants should revere and follow him generation after generation. Why then should they hide this? By learning from someone who is廉洁仁慈且智，即使在贫穷时也能坚守本心，在显达时也能造福社会，还有什么事情不能成功呢？乞讨难道值得隐瞒吗？那些行为不端的人，即使荣华富贵，像曹操、董卓、王莽、卞庄、杞子、秦桧之流，才是真正乞丐所不愿做的，而他们的子孙也会以他们为耻辱的祖先！

    [张山来曰：东坡有言，上可以陪玉皇大帝，下可以陪卑田院乞儿。然则可以陪乞儿者，皆足以陪玉帝者也。盖乞人一种，非至愚无用之流，即其大慈悲而有守者，不屑为倡优隶卒，不肯为机械以攫人财，不得不出于行乞之一途耳。至王翁之高行，则又为此中翘楚矣。] 雷州盗记　徐芳仲光诺皋广志

    Some say: Su Dongpo once said, one can accompany the Jade Emperor above and the beggars in the lowly fields below. Thus, those who can accompany beggars are also capable of accompanying the Jade Emperor. Beggars, in a sense, are not all foolish and useless; some are great in their compassion and integrity, unwilling to be entertainers, servants, or thugs, and unwilling to use cunning to seize others' wealth. They have no choice but to beg. As for the noble conduct of Master Wang, he stands out among them. Record of Bandits in Leizhou by Xu Fangzhong, Guangzhao Guangzhi.

    180

    雷于粤为最远郡。崇祯初，金陵人某以部曹出守，舟入江遇盗。知其守也，杀之，并歼其从者，独留其妻女。以众中一最黠者为伪守，持牒往，而群诡为仆，人莫能察也。抵郡逾月，甚廉干，有治状，雷人相庆得贤太守。其寮属暨监司使，咸诵重之。未几，太守出示禁游客，所隶毋得纳金陵人只履，否者虽至戚必坐。于是雷人益信服新太守乃能严介若此也。
    　　亡何，守之子至，入境，无敢舍者。问之，知其禁也，心惑之。诘朝守出，子道视，非父也，讯其籍里名姓，则皆父。子悟曰：“噫！是盗矣！”然不敢暴语，密以白监司使。监司曰：“止！吾旦日饭守而出子。”于是戒吏，以卒环太守舍，而伏甲酒所。旦日，太守入谒，监司饮之酒，出其子质，不辨也。守窘，拟起为变，而伏甲发，就坐捽之。其卒之环守者，亦破署入。贼数十人，卒起格斗，胥逸去，仅获其七。狱具如律，械送金陵杀之。于是雷之人乃知向之守，非守也，盗云。

    Explanation

    180

    Lei is the most distant prefecture of Yue. In the early years of the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, a man from Jinling, who held the position of a low-ranking official, was appointed as the governor of Lei. While traveling by boat on the river, he encountered bandits. The bandits recognized that he was a governor and killed him, along with his attendants, but left his wife and daughter alive. They chose the most cunning person among them to impersonate the governor, carrying a document to assume authority, and the rest pretended to be servants, none of whom could be identified as the impostor. Upon arriving in the prefecture, the impostor governed for over a month, appearing very diligent and efficient, and the people of Lei celebrated getting a good governor. His subordinates and the provincial inspector praised him highly. Soon after, the governor issued an order prohibiting visitors from entering the prefecture and forbidding his subordinates from accepting any item from Jinling, even if it was from a close relative, they would be punished. Thus, the people of Lei believed even more that the new governor was so strict and upright.

    Not long after, the governor's son arrived, and upon entering the prefecture, no one dared to lodge him. When asked, he learned of the ban and was puzzled. The next day, when the governor went out, the son saw that he was not his father. When questioned about his place of origin, family background, and name, the governor provided the same information as his father. The son realized, "Ah! This is a bandit!" However, he did not openly speak about it and secretly informed the provincial inspector. The provincial inspector said, "Wait! I will have a meal with the governor tomorrow and then take you out." He then ordered the officials to surround the governor's residence with soldiers and hid armed men in the place where the governor would be dining. The next day, when the governor came to pay respects, the provincial inspector served him wine and brought out the son to confront him, but the governor could not distinguish him. The governor became desperate and tried to cause trouble, but the hidden armed men attacked and seized him. The soldiers surrounding the governor's residence also broke in. The bandits fought back, but most escaped, and only seven were captured. After the trial, the bandits were sentenced according to law, shackled, and sent to Jinling to be executed. Then the people of Lei realized that the previous governor was not a real governor but a bandit.

    181

    东陵生闻而叹曰：“异哉！盗乃能守若此乎？今之守非盗也，而其行鲜不盗也，则无宁以盗守矣！其贼守，盗也；其守而贤，即犹愈他守也。”或曰：“彼非贤也，将间而括其藏与其郡人之资以逸。”曰：“有之，今之守亦孰有不括其郡之藏若赀而逸者哉？”愚山子曰：甚哉东陵生言也！推其意，足以砥守。
    　　[张山来曰：以国法论之，此群盗咸杀无赦；以民情论之，则或尽歼群从。而宽其为守之一人，差足以报其治状耳。若今之大夫，虽不罹国法，而未尝不被杀于庶民之心中也。] 花隐道人传　朱一是近修为可堂集

    Explanation

    181

    East Terrace heard this and exclaimed, "How strange! Could it be that thieves are capable of governing like this? Today's governors are not thieves, yet their actions are rarely not those of thieves. Therefore, it would be better to have thieves govern! Those who are thieves in their governance are thieves; those who are virtuous in their governance are still better than other governors." Someone said, "He is not virtuous; he will take the opportunity to seize their treasures and escape with the resources of the people of his prefecture." East Terrace replied, "It is true. Today's governors, who does not seize the treasures and resources of their prefectures and escape?" Foolish Mountain said, "How great are the words of East Terrace! To infer its meaning, they are sufficient to correct the governors."

    [Zhao Shanlai said: According to national law, these bandits will all be killed without pardon; according to public sentiment, perhaps the entire group will be annihilated. But to pardon the one who is a governor, slightly enough to report his administration. If today's officials, though not punished by law, are still killed in the hearts of the common people.] The Biography of the Flower-Hiding Taoist by Zhu Yishin of the Canopy Hall.

    182

    道人姓高氏，名昽，字公旦。其先晋人也，商于扬，家焉。至道人，贫矣，徙商而读。顾读异书，不喜沾沾行墨，能以己意断古今事。见世窃儒冠目瞆瞆然者，弃去羞与伍。慕朱家、郭解为人，尚侠轻财，急人困。然砥行，慎交游。里中少年有不逞者，始畏道人知，既事蹶张，则又求道人。道人予其自新，亦时援手，故扬人倾心。四方贤豪来者，闻道人名，多结欢焉。
    　　甲申，知乱将作，移家避南徐。时阃帅鳞集江上，争罗致道人幕下。道人知事不可为，蠖伏自污，卒得以全。乙酉，扬中兵祸惨，民鸟兽散。道人独先众入城访亲知，吊死扶伤，阴行善多。
    　　然道人是时感念深矣。自以遭时变乱，年壮志摧，流离困折，无复风尘驰骤之思。乃筑室黄子湖中，弃其鲜肥素习，衣大布衣，箨冠草履，曳杖篱落间。挽渔父牧儿与饮，饮辄醉，放歌湖滨，湖水为沸扬，似鸣不平者。

    Explanation

    182

    The Taoist bore the surname Gao, named Long, and styled Gongdan. His ancestors were from the state of Jin, and he engaged in commerce in Yang before settling there. By the time of the Taoist, however, he had fallen into poverty and shifted from commerce to study. He read unusual books and did not like superficial writing; instead, he could judge historical events with his own insight. Seeing those who wore the scholar's cap but had dim eyes and shallow understanding, he would disdainfully distance himself from them. He admired the chivalrous figures Zhu Jia and Guo Jie, upholding chivalry, undervaluing wealth, and helping those in distress. Yet he cultivated his character and was cautious in his social interactions. In the village, there were unruly youths who initially feared that the Taoist would expose them, but once they found themselves in trouble, they would seek him out. The Taoist allowed them to reform and sometimes helped them, which is why the people of Yang were deeply impressed by him. Those of noble character from afar, hearing of the Taoist’s name, often formed friendships with him.

    In the year Jia Shen, knowing that chaos was imminent, he moved his family to Nancheng to avoid it. At that time, military leaders gathered on the river and tried to recruit the Taoist into their camps. The Taoist realized that things were beyond his control and chose to hide his talents to survive, ultimately managing to preserve himself. In the year Yi You, Yang was ravaged by war, and the people scattered like birds and beasts. The Taoist alone entered the city ahead of others to visit relatives and friends, mourn the dead, and help the wounded, secretly performing many good deeds.

    Yet at this time, the Taoist was deeply moved. He felt that he had suffered through times of upheaval, and his youthful ambitions had been crushed. Experiencing hardship and setbacks, he no longer had thoughts of rushing through the world. He built a house by the Yellow Lake, abandoning his former luxurious habits, and dressed in coarse cloth, wearing a hat made of bamboo leaves and straw sandals, and leaning on a staff as he wandered among the hedges. He would invite fishermen and herders to drink, and when drunk, he would sing by the lake, causing the lake water to ripple as if protesting.

    183

    未几，岁大涝，居沉于水。道人曰：“未闻巢父买山而隐，独支遁见讥耶？古之大隐，有隐市者，吾何为不然？”爰走扬城东南隅，卜地宅之，躬荷锸拨瓦砾，结庐数楹。一几一榻，张琴列古书画。携一妻二子婆娑偃息其中，陶陶然乐也。
    　　宅旁筑匡墙，围地数亩，值菊五百本。一仆长须赤脚，善橐驼之术，道人率之艺植灌溉。夏日当午，虫有长颈鸟喙寇菊颠者，秋有白皙如蚕啖菊根者，必伺而攻去之。二为渠魁，他虫种种咸治无赦。道人察其患害，而保护朝夕，故菊茂于常。始自蓓蕾以及烂熳，其列也如屏，散也如星，叠也如锦；其色如玉，如金，如霞，如雪；其味如元酒；其香如檐蔔。道人洞开其门，门如市；虚辟其堂，堂如肆。往来如织，观者如堵。不见主人，见其扁额曰“花隐”，咸谓之花隐道人，若忘其昔之为高公旦者。

    Explanation

    183

    Not long after, there was a great flood, and the house was submerged in water. The Taoist said, "I have not heard that Chao Fu bought a mountain to live in seclusion, while Zhi Dun was alone criticized for doing so. In ancient times, there were great hermits who chose to live in the market, so why should I not do the same?" He then walked to the southeast corner of Yangcheng, chose a site, and built a small house himself, clearing away rubble with a spade. Inside, there was one table, one bed, a zither, and ancient paintings and calligraphy. He lived there with his wife and two sons, leisurely resting and enjoying a contented and happy life.

    Near the house, he built a wall and enclosed several acres of land, planting five hundred chrysanthemums. He had a servant with long hair and bare feet who was skilled in horticulture, and the Taoist led him in cultivating and irrigating the plants. In the summer heat, there were insects with long necks and bird-like beaks that attacked the tops of the chrysanthemums, and in the autumn, there were white insects like silkworms that fed on the roots. The Taoist would always watch for them and eliminate them. These two types of pests were the ringleaders, and all other insects were dealt with without mercy. The Taoist observed their harm and protected the chrysanthemums day and night, so they flourished more than usual. From the buds to their full bloom, they arranged like a screen, scattered like stars, layered like brocade; their colors were like jade, gold, clouds, and snow; their fragrance was like the scent of a flower. The Taoist opened the door wide, and it was as busy as a market; the hall was as bustling as a shop. People came and went like a woven fabric, and those who came to see were as numerous as a wall. They did not see the owner, but saw the sign above the door, which read "Flower Hidden," and they all called him the Flower Hidden Taoist, as if forgetting that he had once been Gao Gongdan.

    184

    其友梅溪朱一是诮之曰：“子隐于花，则善矣。然花隐之名益著，得非畏影而走日中者耶？吾见子之愈走而影不息也！”道人嘻然笑而不答。
    　　[张山来曰：从来隐于花者，类多高人韵士，而菊则尤与隐者相宜。妙在全不蹈袭渊明只字，所以为高。]
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷六 张南垣传　吴伟业骏公梅村文集
    　　张南垣，名涟，南垣其字。华亭人，徙秀州，又为秀州人。少学画，好写人像，兼通山水，遂以其意垒石。故他艺不甚著，其垒石最工，在他人为之，莫能及也。
    　　百余年来，为此技者，类学崭岩嵌特。好事之家，罗取一二异石，标之曰峰，皆从他邑辇至，决城闉，坏道路，人牛喘汗，仅而得至；络以巨絙，锢以铁汁，刑牲下拜，劖颜刻字，钩填空青，穹窿岩岩，若在乔岳。其难也如此，而其旁又架危梁，梯鸟道；游之者钩巾棘履，拾级数折，伛偻入深洞，扪壁投罅，瞪盼骇栗。

    Explanation

    184

    His friend Zhu Yiyi from Meixi criticized him, saying: "You hide among the flowers, which is good. However, your reputation as a flower hermit has become even more famous. Is it not like someone who is afraid of their own shadow and runs into the sun? I see you running more and more, but your shadow does not stop!" The hermit laughed and did not respond.

    [According to Zhang Shanglai: Those who hide among the flowers are usually high-minded and elegant scholars, and chrysanthemums are especially suitable for hermits. The beauty lies in not imitating any of Tao Yuanming's words, which makes it unique.]

    From the Xin Zhi (New Records) of the Yu Chiu (Suo Chiu), Volume 6, The Biography of Zhang Nanyuan, in the Wu Weiye Jun Gong Mei Cun Wen Ji (Collected Works of Wu Weiye, Jun Gong, and Mei Cun):

    Zhang Nanyuan, whose name was Lian and Nanyuan was his courtesy name, was originally from Huating but later moved to Xiuzhou, where he became a resident. He studied painting at a young age, excelling in portrait drawing and also understanding landscape painting. He applied his skills to stone arrangement, and thus his other artistic skills were not particularly prominent, but his stone arrangement was the most skilled, and no one else could match it.

    For over a hundred years, those who practiced this skill (stone arrangement) generally learned to create rugged and peculiar designs. Wealthy and cultured families would collect one or two unusual stones and label them as peaks. These stones were transported from other places, causing damage to city walls and roads, and people and cattle would be exhausted from the journey. The stones were secured with thick ropes, fixed with molten iron, and rituals were performed. Inscriptions were carved, and the stones were decorated with blue paint, making them appear as if they were part of a majestic mountain. The difficulty of this process was immense, and even more so because dangerous beams and steep paths were built around the stones. Visitors had to tie their hats, wear spiky shoes, climb steps, bend over, enter deep caves, touch the walls, and look around in fear.

    185

    南垣过而笑曰：“是岂知为山者耶？今夫群峰造天，深岩蔽日，此夫造物神灵之所为，非人力可得而致也。况其地辄跨数百里，而吾以盈丈之址，五尺之沟，尤而效之，何异市人抟土以欺儿童哉？唯夫平冈小坂，陵阜陂陁，板筑之功可计日以就。然后错之以石，棋置其间，缭以短垣，翳以密篠，若似乎奇峰绝嶂垒垒乎墙外，而人或见之也。其石脉之所奔注，伏而起，突而怒，为狮蹲，为兽攫，口鼻含呀，牙错距跃，决林莽，犯轩楹而不去，若似乎处大山之麓，截溪断谷，私此数石者为吾有也。方塘石洫，易以曲岸回沙；邃闼雕楹，改为青扉白屋。树取其不凋者，松杉桧栝，杂植成林；石取其易致者，太湖尧峰，随宜布置。有林泉之美，无登顿之劳，不亦可乎？”华亭董宗伯玄宰、陈征君仲醇亟称之，曰：“江南诸山，土中戴石。黄一峰、吴仲圭常言之，此知夫画脉者也。”

    Explanation

    185

    The southern wall passer went by and laughed, saying, "Is this person aware of what it takes to create a mountain? Today, the peaks reach up to the sky, and deep caves block the sun. This is the work of divine creation, not something human effort can achieve. Especially when the area spans hundreds of miles, but I with a site of just a few feet and a ditch five feet wide, to imitate such a thing, how different is it from a market vendor shaping clay to deceive children? Only a gentle hill or small slope, mounds, hills, and slopes, the work of building with boards can be completed in days. Then intersperse it with stones, arrange them like chess pieces, surround with short walls, cover with dense bamboo, as if they were strange peaks and sheer cliffs piling up outside the wall, and people might see it. The veins of the stones flowing and surging, lying down and rising, leaping and raging, like a lion crouching, like a beast seizing, mouths and noses open wide, teeth clashing, paws leaping, breaking through the thickets, colliding with the beams and pillars, and not stopping, as if they were at the foot of a great mountain, cutting off streams and valleys, making these few stones my own. Square ponds and stone ditches, replacing them with winding banks and swirling sand; deep doors and carved pillars, changing them to green doors and white houses. Trees chosen for their evergreen nature, pine, cypress, and other evergreen trees, mixed and planted to form a forest; stones chosen for their ease of acquisition, Lake Taihu and Yao Peak stones, arranged according to suitability. The beauty of forests and springs, without the labor of climbing and descending, isn't this acceptable?" The esteemed Doctor Dong Xuanzai of Huating and the respected Mr. Chen Zhongchun highly praised it, saying, "The mountains of Jiangnan, with stones embedded in the soil. Yellow Yifeng and Wu Zhonggui often spoke of it, this is the understanding of the pulse of painting."

    186

    群公交书走币，岁无虑数十家。有不能应者，用以为大恨。顾一见君，惊喜欢笑如初。君为人肥而短黑，性滑稽，好举里巷谐媟以为抚掌之资；或陈语旧闻，反以此受人调弄，亦不顾也。与人交好，谈人之善，不择高下，能安异同。以此游于江南诸郡者五十余年。自华亭、秀州外，于白门，于金沙，于海虞，于娄东，于鹿城，所过必数月。

    Explanation

    186

    The crowd of guests sent letters and gifts, and there were dozens of families each year. Those who could not respond considered this a great grievance. But when they saw him, they were surprised, happy, and laughed as if it were the first time. He was fat and short with dark skin, humorous, and enjoyed telling stories from the streets that were playful and humorous, using them as material for laughter; sometimes he would recount old news, but he was often mocked for it, yet he didn't care. He got along well with people, talked about their good qualities, didn't discriminate based on status, and could accommodate differences. Thus, he traveled to various counties in Jiangnan for over fifty years. From Hua Ting and Xiu Zhou, he passed through Bai Men, Jin Sha, Hai Yu, Lou Dong, and Lu Cheng, staying for months in each place.

    187

    其所为园，则李工部之“横云”，虞观察之“预园”，王奉常之“乐郊”，钱宗伯之“拂水”，吴吏部之“竹亭”为最著。经营粉本，高下浓淡，早有成法。初立土山，树木未添，岩壑已具，随皴随改，烟云渲染，补入无痕。即一花一竹，疏密欹斜，妙得俯仰。山未成，先思著屋；屋未就，又思其中之所施设；窗棂几榻，不事雕饰，雅合自然。主人解事者，君不受促迫，次第结构。其或任情自用，不得已骫骳曲随。后有过者，辄叹惜曰：“此必非南垣意也！”
    　　君为此技既久，土石草树，咸能识其性情。每创手之日，乱石林立，或卧或倚。君踌躇四顾，正势侧峰，横支竖理，皆默识在心，借成众手。常高坐一室，与客谈笑，呼役夫曰：“某树下某石，可置某处。”目不转视，手不再指，若金在冶，不假斧凿；甚至施竿结顶，悬而下缒，尺寸勿爽。观者以此服其能矣。

    Explanation

    187

    The gardens he created were most renowned among those of Li Gongbu’s "Hengyun," Yu Guancha’s "Yuyuan," Wang Fengchang’s "Lejiao," Qian Zongbo’s "Fushui," and Wu Lishibu’s "Zhuting." In planning and designing these gardens, he had a well-established method for the varying heights, depths, and colors. When the earth mounds were first constructed, before trees were added, the rocks and valleys were already in place. He would make adjustments as the landscape was shaped, adding mist and clouds to enhance the atmosphere, and seamlessly integrating elements without leaving any trace. Even individual flowers and bamboo, in terms of density, spacing, and angles, were skillfully arranged to achieve balance and perspective. Before the mountains were completed, he already thought about where to place the buildings; before the buildings were finished, he also considered the interior arrangements. The windows, lattices, tables, and beds were simple and unadorned, harmonizing beautifully with nature. If the owner understood the process, the master (designer) would not be rushed, proceeding step by step in the construction. If the owner acted according to their own whims, he would reluctantly follow the owner’s wishes, even if they were not ideal. Later visitors would often lament and say, "This must not have been Master Nan Yuan's intention!"

    Having practiced this craft for a long time, he knew the nature of the soil, stones, plants, and trees. When he began a new project, the stones were scattered like a forest, some lying down, others leaning. He would pause and look around, positioning the peaks and valleys, as well as the arrangement of horizontal and vertical structures, all memorizing in his mind. He would guide the work of many hands. Often sitting in a room, chatting with guests, he would call the laborers and say, "Under this tree, this stone can be placed here." Without looking away or pointing again, like metal being shaped in a furnace, without the need for chisels; even when using poles to lift heavy objects from above, with no deviation in measurement. Spectators would be impressed by his skill.

    188

    人有学其术者，以为曲折变化，此君生平之所长，尽其心力以求仿佛，初见或似，久观辄非。而君独规模大势，使人于数日之内，寻丈之间，落落难合。及其既就，则天堕地出，得未曾有。曾于友人斋前作荆、关老笔，对跱平磩，已过五寻，不作一折，忽于其颠将数石盘亘得势，则全体飞动，苍然不群。所谓他人为之莫能及者，盖以此也。
    　　君有四子，能传父术。晚岁辞涿鹿相国之聘，遣其仲子行。退老于鸳河之侧，结庐三楹。余过之，谓余曰：“自吾以此术游江以南也，数十年来，名园别墅，易其故主者，比比是矣。荡于兵火，没于荆榛，奇花异石，他人辇取以去，吾仍为之营置者，辄数见焉。吾惧石之不足留吾名，而欲得子文以传之也。”

    Explanation

    188

    A person who learned this skill believed that its intricate and varied nature was the master's lifelong expertise. They used all their effort to try to imitate it, and at first glance it might seem similar, but upon closer inspection it was not. However, the master alone focused on the overall structure and main features, making it difficult to match within a few days or a short distance. When completed, it appeared suddenly and dramatically, something never seen before. Once, at a friend's study, the master created a work in the style of Jing Hao and Guan Tong, standing opposite a flat surface, already over five zhang long, without a single fold. Suddenly, at the top, several stones were arranged to gain momentum, and the entire piece became dynamic and unique. What others could not achieve, he did so because of this.

    The master had four sons, all of whom could inherit his skill. In his later years, he declined the invitation to serve as chancellor of Zhuolu and sent his second son instead. He retired to the side of the Yuán River, building a simple dwelling. I visited him and he said to me, "Since I have traveled with this skill to the south of the Yangtze River for several decades, many famous gardens and villas have changed their original owners. They have been destroyed by war and fire, buried in thorns, and rare flowers and stones have been taken away by carriages. I still arrange for them, often seen. I fear that the stones will not be enough to preserve my name, and I want your writing to preserve it."

    189

    余曰：柳宗元为《梓人传》，谓有得于经国治民之旨。今观张君之术，虽庖丁解牛，公输刻鹄，无以复过，其艺而合于道者欤？君子不作无益。穿池筑台，《春秋》所戒。而王公贵人，歌舞般乐，侈欲伤财，独此为耳目之观，稍有合于清净。且张君因深就高，合自然，惜人力，此学愚公之术而变焉者也，其可传也已。作《张南垣传》。
    　　[张山来曰：垒山垒石，另有一种学问，其胸中丘壑，较之画家为难。盖画则远近高卑，疏密险易，可以自主；此则必合地宜，因石性，物多不当弃其有余，物少不必补其不足，又必酌主人之贫富，随主人之性情，犹必借群工之手，是以难耳。况画家所长，不在蹊径而在笔墨。予尝以画上之景作实境视，殊有不堪游览者。犹之诗中烟雨穷愁字面，在诗虽为佳句，而当之者殊苦也。若园亭之胜，则止赖布景得宜，不能乞灵于他物，岂画家可比乎？] 孙文正、黄石斋两逸事　桐城方苞望溪

    Explanation

    189

    I said: Liu Zongyuan wrote The Biography of the Carpenter, suggesting that there is a lesson to be learned from the principles of governing the state and managing the people. Today, observing Zhang Jun's skill, even if it were comparable to the masterful butchering of a cow by Butcher Ding or the intricate carving of a crane by Gongshu Ban, it cannot be surpassed; is this an art that aligns with the Dao? A gentleman does not engage in futile endeavors. Digging ponds and building terraces are activities that the Spring and Autumn Annals caution against. Yet, kings, nobles, and wealthy individuals indulge in dancing, singing, and enjoying music, their excessive desires harming wealth. Only this (Zhang Jun's work) serves as a visual delight, somewhat aligning with purity and tranquility. Moreover, Zhang Jun adapts to the terrain, harmonizes with nature, and conserves human effort; this is a variation on the methods of Yu Gong, and thus it is worth passing on. I compose The Biography of Zhang Nan Yuan.

    [Ouyang Shanlai said: Piling up mountains and stones is another kind of art. The natural features in his mind are more challenging than those in a painter's work. In painting, one can control the distance, height, density, and difficulty at will; in this case, one must adapt to the local conditions, follow the nature of the stones, and not discard the excess when there is too much, nor supplement the lack when there is too little. One must also consider the owner's wealth and temperament, and rely on the hands of many craftsmen, making it difficult. Furthermore, a painter's strength lies not in the path but in the brush and ink. I have often viewed the scenes depicted in paintings as real landscapes, and found them unsuitable for actual travel. It is like the imagery of mist, rain, and melancholy in poetry; while it may be a fine line in poetry, those who experience it find it particularly bitter. As for the beauty of garden pavilions, it relies solely on the appropriate arrangement of scenery, not seeking help from other things—how can it be compared to a painter?]

    Two Anecdotes about Sun Wenzheng and Huang Shizhai by Fang Bao of Tongcheng.

    190

    杜先生岑尝言：归安茅止生习于高阳孙少师道公。天启二年，以大学士经略蓟辽，置酒别亲宾，会者百人。有客中坐，前席而言曰：“公之出，始吾为国庆，而今重有忧。封疆社稷，寄公一身，公能堪，备物自奉，人莫之非；如不能，虽毁身家，责难逭，况俭觳乎？吾见客食皆凿，而公独饭粗，饰小名以镇物，非所以负天下之重也！”公揖而谢曰：“先生诲我甚当，然非敢以为名也。好衣甘食，吾为秀才时固不厌。自成进士，释褐而归，念此身已不为己有。而朝廷多故，边关日骇，恐一旦肩事任，非忍饥劳，不能以身率众。自是不敢适口体，强自勗厉，以至于今，十有九年矣。”

    Explanation

    190

    Mr. Du, a student of Mr. Cen, once said: Mr. Mao Zhi from Gu'an learned from Mr. Sun Shao, the Master of the Way from Gaoyang. In the second year of Tianqi, as a Grand Councillor, he was sent to oversee Jizhou and Liaodong. He held a farewell banquet for his relatives and guests, with a hundred people in attendance. One of the guests sat forward and said, "When you first left, I rejoiced for the nation, but now I am deeply concerned. The defense of the borders and the nation's safety are entrusted to you alone. If you can bear the burden, if you are well-provided for, no one will criticize you; if you cannot, even if it means destroying your own family, the blame cannot be avoided, how much more so if you are frugal and humble? I see that all the guests are eating delicacies, while you alone eat coarse food. You are pretending to be modest to impress others, but this is not how to bear the weight of the nation!" The official bowed and replied, "Your advice is very appropriate, but I do not do it for the sake of reputation. When I was a scholar, I enjoyed fine clothes and good food. After becoming a Jinshi and returning home, I realized that my body no longer belongs to me. The court is in turmoil, and the borders are in constant danger. I fear that if I take on responsibilities, I must endure hunger and labor to lead by example. Since then, I have not indulged in food or comfort, forcing myself to be strict, and this has continued for nineteen years."

    191

    呜呼！公之气折逆奄，明周万事，合智谋忠勇之士以尽其材，用危困疮痍之卒以致其武，唐、宋名贤中，犹有伦比。至于诚能动物，所纠所斥，退无怨言，叛将远人，咸喻其志，而革心无贰，则自汉诸葛武侯而后，规模气象，唯公有焉！是乃克己省身、忧民体国之实心，自然而忾乎天下者，非躬豪杰之才，而慨乎有闻于圣人之道，孰能与于此？然唯二三执政，与中枢边境，事同一体之人，实不能容。《易》曰：“信及豚鱼。”媢嫉之臣乃不若豚鱼之可格，可不惧哉？

    Explanation

    191

    Alas! The subject's spirit overcame the tyrant, their clarity and thoroughness encompassed all matters, and they gathered wise, strategic, loyal, and brave individuals to fully utilize their talents. They employed soldiers in dire straits or suffering from wounds to achieve their martial prowess, and among the notable figures of the Tang and Song dynasties, there are few who can be compared. As for their sincerity in moving others, those corrected or reprimanded did not complain, and even rebel generals and distant people understood their intentions, changing their hearts without hesitation. Since the time of Zhuge Liang of the Han dynasty, only the subject possessed this grandeur and demeanor. This was the result of self-discipline, self-reflection, concern for the people, and care for the state, and it naturally evoked a response throughout the world. Only someone with the talent of a hero and inspired by the way of the sage could achieve this. Yet, a few high-ranking officials, those in the central government and border regions, and those who share the same purpose or interest, truly could not tolerate the subject. The Book of Changes says, "Trust can reach even the simplest of creatures." Jealous or envious officials cannot be trusted, and one should fear this.

    192

    黄冈杜苍略先生，客金陵，习明季诸前辈遗事。尝言崇祯某年，余中丞集生与谭友夏结社金陵，适石斋黄公来游，与订交，意颇洽。黄公造次必于礼法，诸公心向之，而苦其拘也，思试之。妓顾氏，国色也，聪慧通书史，抚节按歌，见者莫不心醉。一日大雨雪，觞黄公于余氏园，使顾佐酒，公意色无忤。诸公更劝酬剧饮，大醉，送公卧特室。榻上枕衾茵各一，使顾尽弛亵衣，随键户，诸公伺焉。公惊起，索衣不得，因引衾自覆荐，而命顾以茵卧。茵厚且狭，不可转，乃使就寝。顾遂昵近公，公徐曰：“无用尔。”侧身向内，息数十调，即酣寝。漏下四鼓，觉，转面向外。顾佯寐无觉，而以体旁公。俄顷，公酣寝如初。诘旦，顾出，具言其状，且曰：“公等为名士，赋诗饮酒，是乐而已矣。为圣为佛，成忠成孝，终归黄公。”

    Explanation

    192

    Mr. Du Canglue of Huanggang was a guest in Jinling, where he studied the stories of the predecessors from the late Ming dynasty. He once said that during a certain year of the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, Yu Zhongcheng gathered with me and Tan Youxia to form a literary society in Jinling.
