    Alas, is it divine? Or is it not divine? Based on what I have witnessed, it is indeed divine. Yet the virtue of the divine is so great that it fills the heavens and earth, runs through ancient and modern times, and is nowhere absent. However, it appears only in the Yao River, the eastern suburbs, the fourth and third months, and just before the rain during the hot season. Is this truly divine? Scholars, in their pursuit of the profound and the hidden, gather hearsay and examine strange records, and thus their doubts and disputes are understandable. What my eyes have witnessed, and what I have observed by standing for a long time with fixed gaze, I do not know why it happens. To seek to be a learned gentleman, is it not difficult? Or is there truly something unknown? If it is unknown, then it is divine. Therefore, I have detailed this account to present it to the world's many-knowing ones. The year was Bǐngxū, the month was Guǐsì, and the day was Jǐmǎo. Those who traveled with me were my fellow-year friend Zhan Hou Zijun Jin, and the three gentlemen Mi, Shen, and Ye, as well as Yu Xiucai Ziyi, a scholar in my school.

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: In my hometown, there is Lingjin Mountain. Every year on the eighteenth day of the sixth month, an altar is established to distribute food and summon various spirits. The ghostly lights rise in groups, coming together and separating rapidly. The text for this ritual was written by Li Shanchang, the Duke of Korea, when he was studying in the mountain. After a long time, the boards became so blurred that they were no longer legible. The Taoist priests carved a new board, burned it, but the spirits did not appear. They then used the old boards to re-read the text, and the phosphorescent lights reignited. To this day, whenever the altar is set up, both the new and old texts are burned. This shows that the ways of ghosts and spirits interact with humans. The divine lights of the Yao River are not mere idle talk.]

    234

    有穿窬之盗，有豪侠之盗，有斩关辟门、贪婪无厌、冒死不顾之盗，从未有从容坐论、杯酒欢笑如名士之盗者。盖盗者，迫于饥寒，或为仇恶报怨，不得已而为之。盗而名士，盗亦奇矣。
    　　南城萧明彝先生，家世为显官，厚其赀，庾于田。时当秋获，挈其爱妾，刈于乡之别墅。有少年三人，自屋而下，启其户，连进十数辈，曰：“萧先生睡耶？”就榻促之起，为先生着衣裳，进冠履，若执僮仆役，甚谨，曰：“先生有如君，男女之际，不可使窥外事，请键其室。”迎先生至外厅，设坐，面南向，爇烛其下，曰：“某读先生今古文，可一一为先生诵之，最佳者无如某篇。某篇之中，有某转某句，非巧思不能道。尝于某显曹处私伺先生宴，连饮十五犀觥，诸公不及也。江南藩司碑记，唯先生文为绝笔。”

    Explanation

    234

    There are thieves who dig through walls, thieves who are chivalrous, and thieves who boldly break through gates, greedy and insatiable, recklessly disregarding death. There has never been a thief who calmly discusses while seated, drinks and laughs with others, and behaves like a scholar of high moral and cultural standing. Thieves are driven by hunger and cold, or seek revenge for grudges, and act out of necessity. A thief who is also a scholar of high moral and cultural standing is indeed a rare and unusual thing.

    Mr. Xiao Mingyi of Nancheng, born into a family of high-ranking officials, has a substantial fortune and is engaged in agriculture. When it was time for the autumn harvest, he took his beloved concubine to reap at a country villa. Three young men descended from the house, opened the door, and a dozen or so people entered in succession. They asked, "Is Mr. Xiao asleep?" They urged him to get up from the bed, dressed him in clothes, and put on his hat and shoes, acting as if performing the duties of a servant, very carefully. They said, "Mr. Xiao has a companion. Between men and women, it is not appropriate for you to see external matters, so please lock your room." They led Mr. Xiao to the outer hall, set up a seat facing south, and lit a candle below. They said, "I have read Mr. Xiao's modern and ancient writings. I can recite them for you one by one. The best is without a doubt a certain piece. In a certain piece, there is a certain turn of phrase, only with clever thought can it be expressed. I once secretly observed Mr. Xiao's banquet at a certain high-ranking official's residence, drinking fifteen cups of wine in succession, the others could not match him. The memorial inscriptions of the Jiangnan provincial administration, only Mr. Xiao's writing is unparalleled."

    235

    左右有恐吓先生者，其盗魁力止之，曰：“此萧先生，不可以常态惊也。”索酒肴相啖食。先生为之陈庖厨。饮酣，曰：“某等闻先生名久矣！不惜千金路费至此，可出其囊橐以偿吾愿。”先生曰：“昨有四百金稻谷价，惜来迟耳，今早已送之城中。此所留者，仅羹酒之需，不过二十七金，人参八两，玉带一围而已，愿持赠诸豪士。”左右疑有埋藏者，盗魁曰：“此先生真实语也，不须疑。”启其箧，如数。
    　　夜将半，先生倦，且恐。盗魁曰：“先生倦乎？我为先生起舞。”解长服，甲铠绣鲜，金光灿耀夺人目。拔双剑，起舞厅中，往来近先生鼻端，迹其状，如项庄鸿门意在沛公时也。良久乃止。先生待益恭，盗益重先生。自启户论文，始终敬礼先生，卒不敢犯如此。

    Explanation

    235

    There were those in attendance who threatened the Master, but the leader of the bandits forcefully stopped them, saying, "This is Master Xiao, who cannot be startled by ordinary means." They then asked for wine and dishes to share together. The Master prepared a feast for them. After drinking heartily, they said, "We have long heard of Master's name! We spared no expense to travel here; could you reveal what you have in your bags to fulfill our wishes?" The Master replied, "Yesterday, I had four hundred taels of silver for rice, but it was too late to bring it. I have already sent it to the city. What remains is merely enough for a meal, no more than twenty-seven taels, eight ounces of ginseng, and a waist sash of jade. I wish to present these to the gentlemen." Those around him suspected there might be hidden treasures, but the bandit leader said, "These are Master's honest words; there is no need to doubt." They opened his chest and found everything as he had said.

    By midnight, the Master was weary and fearful. The bandit leader asked, "Are you tired, Master? Let me dance for you." He removed his long robe, revealing armor adorned with bright, fresh embroidery that shone with golden light, dazzling the eyes. He drew two swords and danced in the hall, moving so close to the Master that his blades nearly touched his nose. Watching him, one could see that his movements were like those of Xiang Zhuang at the Hongmen Feast, with intent on Liu Bang. After a long while, he stopped. The Master treated him with even greater respect, and the bandits came to revere him more. From the moment they opened the door to discuss literature, they always showed him utmost respect and never dared to offend him in such a manner.

    236

    先生房委曲，四顾夜黑，持灯周书幌曰：“此窗棂宜向某处上下，此楼宜对某方，所惜鸠工时少经营耳。”登楼，窥先生藏书，见《名臣奏议》《忠臣谱》二集，曰：“吾愿得此。”笔筒中旧置网巾二副，纳之袖中。字画多时贤为者，曰：“乌用此玷辱书斋？”择其不佳者毁裂之。有美人一幅，乃名笔，曰：“此不可多觏者。”罗君某写有小楷扇一柄，藏笔床侧。曰：“吾与此公有旧好，宜珍之。”亦携之去。
    　　将出门，邀先生送。先生强留曰：“若辈皆少年豪侠，待至明日归取四百金相遗何如？”盗魁曰：“世从无其事，余何能待？”请姓名，不答，曰：“后会有期。惜先生老，若少壮，当与之同往。”先生出走里许，见木舟二，泊溪日，尽登，摇橹而去。语作吴下音。
    　　嗟乎！盗而如是，可以常盗目之哉？吾恐盗虚声者，灭礼义，弃《诗》《书》，反不若是之深于文也！谓之曰“名士之盗”。

    Explanation

    236

    The room was cramped and dark, with the surroundings shrouded in night. Holding a lamp, he examined the window curtains and said, "This window frame should be adjusted up and down, this tower should face a certain direction. What is regrettable is that the craftsmen were not well managed during construction." He climbed the tower and looked into Mr. X's library, where he saw two collections of memorials by famous ministers and a collection of loyal ministers. He said, "I wish to have these." In the pen holder, there were two old headbands, which he put into his sleeve. There were many paintings and calligraphy works by contemporary scholars, and he said, "Why would you use this to defile the study?" He chose the poor ones and tore them up. There was a painting of a beautiful woman, which was a famous work, and he said, "This is not something one can often see." Mr. Luo had a small calligraphy fan, which was kept beside the writing desk. He said, "I have an old friendship with this gentleman, so it should be treasured." He also took it with him.

    As he was about to leave, he invited Mr. X to see him off. Mr. X tried to keep him, saying, "You all are young heroes, why don't you wait until tomorrow to take 400 gold pieces as a gift?" The leader of the thieves said, "Such things never happen in the world, how can I wait?" He asked for his name, but he did not respond, and said, "We will meet again. I am sorry, Mr. X, you are old, if you were young, I would go with you." Mr. X walked out about a li and saw two wooden boats moored in the stream. He climbed aboard both and rowed away, speaking in Wu dialect.

    Alas! A thief like this cannot be considered a common thief. I fear that those who pretend to be thieves, who destroy rites and propriety, discard the Shijing and Shujing, are not as deeply versed in literature as this! Let us call him a "scholarly thief."

    237

    [张山来曰：有盗如此，即开门揖之，似亦无不可者。虽然，天下岂少此辈哉？独恨蹈其实而讳其名，且所欲无餍，固不若此辈之直而且廉耳。] 化虎记　徐芳仲光诺皋广志
    　　年来予乡多虎，啮人甚众，及行脚历闽、楚、晋、豫皆然。或曰：“是帝所役，以襄戈镝所不及。”或曰：“为在猛鬼厉魄激郁而化。”是二者，疑皆有之，而无如危子允臧所述黄翁事尤异。
    　　黄翁者，密溪人，去樵城十余里。生三子，俱壮矣。乙未春，使耕田山中，晨出酉返，如是数日。一夕，邻子谓翁曰：“田芜弗治，倘无意乎？”翁曰：“儿曹日躬耒耜，奚芜也？”邻子曰：“未也。”翁心怪。诘旦，三子出，翁密尾，侦其所往。则见入山林中，祛衣挂树，随变为虎，哮跃四出。翁大恐，奔归，窃告邻子，拒户匿处。迨夜，三子归，呼门良久，不应。邻子谕之曰：“若翁不尔子矣！”问其故，以所见告。三子曰：“有之，帝命所驱，不自由也。

    Explanation

    237

    Translation:

    Zhang Shanlai said: "If there is a thief like this, to open the door and bow to him, it seems not entirely unreasonable. However, how many of this sort are there in the world? The only regret is that they step on the truth but avoid its name. Moreover, their desires are insatiable, which is certainly not as straightforward and honest as this kind."

    Ji Hu Ji by Xu Fangzhong Guangnuo

    In recent years, my hometown has seen many tigers, biting many people. When traveling to Fujian, Hunan, Shanxi, and Henan, the same was true. Some said, "These tigers are commanded by the emperor, to be in places where arrows and spears cannot reach." Others said, "They are transformed by the intense anger and resentment of fierce spirits." Both of these explanations may be true, but none is as strange as the story told by Master Wei Yuzang about Master Huang.

    Master Huang was a man from Mìxī, about ten miles from Qiaocheng. He had three sons, all grown. In the spring of 1655, he sent them to farm in the mountains. They left in the morning and returned in the evening, as usual. One night, a neighbor told Master Huang, "Your fields are overgrown and not being cultivated, do you not care?" Master Huang replied, "My sons work with plows every day, why would they let the fields grow wild?" The neighbor said, "No, it's not that." Master Huang was puzzled. The next morning, the three sons went out, and Master Huang secretly followed them to see where they went. He saw them enter the forest, take off their clothes, hang them on a tree, and then transform into tigers, roaring and jumping around. Master Huang was very afraid, ran back home, and secretly told the neighbor. They locked the door and hid. That night, the three sons returned, called for the door for a long time, but no one answered. The neighbor told them, "Your father no longer recognizes you!" They asked why, and the neighbor told them what he had seen. The three sons said, "Yes, it is true. It is commanded by the emperor, not out of our own will."

    238

    ”因呜咽呼翁曰：“罔极之恩，宁不思报？无如父名早在劫中，儿辈数日远出，正求其人可以代者。既尔逗露，不可复止。然某所衣领中，有小册，幸为简付。不然，父固不利，儿皆坐是死矣。”翁因取烛觅衣领中，果得小册，皆是樵郡应伤虎者，而翁名在第二。翁曰：“奈何？”三子曰：“第开门，当自有策。”翁勉听，三子受册泣拜，因告翁曰：“此俱帝命。父当蒙厚衣数重，勿结带，加黄纸其上，匍伏虔祷，儿自有救父法。”翁如言，三子次第从后跃过，各啣一衣，虎吼而出，遂不复返。翁至今犹在。

    Explanation

    238

    He then wept and called out to his father, saying, "Your boundless kindness, how could I not wish to repay it? But since my father's name was destined for disaster long ago, my brothers and I have been away for several days, looking for someone who could take his place. Now that this has been revealed, it cannot be stopped. But there is a small book in my collar, please give it to him carefully. Otherwise, my father would be at risk, and we would all die because of it." The father then took a candle to search in the collar and indeed found a small book, which listed those from the county of Qiao who were to be injured by a tiger, with his father's name second on the list. The father said, "What shall we do?" The three sons said, "Just open the door, and I will have a plan." The father reluctantly agreed, and the three sons, after receiving the book, wept and bowed. They then told their father, "All this is by imperial decree. My father should wear several layers of thick clothing, without tying the sash, and place yellow paper on top, kneeling humbly and praying devoutly. I will have a way to save him." The father followed their instructions, and the three sons, one by one, jumped over him, each holding a piece of clothing. The tiger roared and came out, but did not return. The father is still alive to this day.

    239

    自昔以人化虎，多有之矣，如封邰、李微辈，即皆易皮换面而去，未有溷处人中若三子者。且帝既以伤人役之，而又列其父册中，尤极难处之事。而三子求代不得，又曲尽以全之，可谓形易而心不易者矣。天下固有五官四体居然皆人，而君父当前，竟不相识者。岂既已虎矣，而犹有恩之不可负哉？虽然，三子既虎矣，奈何列翁名册中，岂司此者偶忘之乎？又岂年来气数之变，虽负恩之大，至于戕贼其父，帝亦恣其所为而不甚问也？则非予之所敢知也。
    　　[张山来曰：三子求可以代父者，其计甚拙。设代者当死于虎，则仅足蔽其本辜，未可以代其父罪。设彼不当死于虎，而三子枉法以杀之，则是父罪未免，而己先罹于法矣，将若之何？] 义犬记　徐芳仲光诺皋广志

    Explanation

    239

    From ancient times, there have been many instances of people transforming into tigers, such as Feng Tai and Li Wei, who all changed their skin and face and then left, but none have lived among people like these three sons. Moreover, the emperor, having injured them and put them to work, also listed their father in the records, making this an especially difficult situation to handle. The three sons sought to take their father's place but could not, and they exhausted all means to preserve him, truly showing that while their form may have changed, their hearts remained the same. There are indeed cases where the five senses and four limbs are still those of a human, yet in the presence of their father, they fail to recognize him. How can one, having transformed into a tiger, still bear the debt of gratitude? However, the three sons, having become tigers, why are they listed in their father's records? Could those in charge have forgotten this? Or could it be that changes in fate over the years, despite the great ingratitude, have led to the emperor allowing them to harm their father and acting as they please without much inquiry? This is beyond what I dare to know.
    [Comment by Zhang Shanlai: The three sons sought to take their father's place, a plan that was quite foolish. If the substitute were to die at the hands of the tiger, it would only cover their own guilt, not their father's. If the substitute should not die at the hands of the tiger, and the three sons wrongfully kill him, then their father's crime would go unpunished, and they themselves would first face legal consequences. What then would they do?]
    Record of the Righteous Dog by Xu Fangzhong, Guang Nuogao, and Guangzhi.

    240

    丙申秋，有太原客南贾还，策一卫，橐金可五六百。偶过中牟县境，憩道左。有少年人，以梃荷犬至，亦偕憩。犬向客咿哑，若望救者。客买放之。少年窥客装重，潜蹑至僻处，以梃搏杀之，曳至小桥水中，盖以沙苇，负橐去。
    　　犬见客死，阴尾少年至其家，识之，却诣县中。适县令升座，衙班甚肃，犬直前据地叫号，若哭若诉，驱之不去。令曰：“尔何冤？吾遣吏随尔。”犬导隶出，至客死所，向水而吠。隶掀苇得尸，还报，顾无从得贼。
    　　犬亦复至，号掷如故。令曰：“若能知贼乎？我且遣隶随尔。”犬又出，令又遣数隶尾去。行二十余里，至一僻村人家，犬竟入，逢一少年，跳而啮其臂，衣碎血濡。隶因絏之到县，具供杀客状。问其金，尚在，就家取之。因于橐中得小籍，知其邑里姓字。令乃抵少年辟，而籍其橐归库。

    Explanation

    240

    In the autumn of the Bing Shen year, a guest from Taiyuan, returning from a southern trading trip, rode a single horse and carried gold worth several hundred units. He happened to pass through the territory of Zhongmou County and rested by the roadside. A young man came with a stick and a dog, also resting there. The dog barked at the guest as if seeking help. The guest bought and released the dog. The young man, seeing the guest's heavy load, secretly followed him to a secluded place, struck and killed him with the stick, dragged him to a small bridge over water, covered him with sand and reeds, and carried away the bag.

    The dog, seeing the guest dead, secretly followed the young man to his home, recognized him, and then went to the county. Just as the county magistrate was seated, the court was very solemn. The dog stood before the ground and cried out, as if crying or pleading, and could not be driven away. The magistrate said, "What is your grievance? I will send officials to follow you." The dog led the officials out to the place where the guest was killed, barked at the water. The officials pulled back the reeds and found the body, reported back, but there was no way to catch the thief.

    The dog continued to cry out and throw itself around. The magistrate said, "Can you identify the thief? I will send officials to follow you." The dog went out again, and the magistrate sent several officials to follow it. They traveled more than twenty miles to a secluded village, where the dog entered a house and met a young man, jumping and biting his arm, tearing his clothes and staining them with blood. The officials led him to the county, where he gave a full confession of killing the guest. The magistrate asked about the gold, which was still there, and went to his home to retrieve it. They found a small register in the bag, knowing his hometown, surname, and name. The magistrate then punished the young man and seized his bag, returning it to the treasury.

    241

    犬复至令前吠不已，令因思曰：“客死，其家固在，此橐金安属？犬吠，将无是乎？”乃复遣隶直往太原，此犬亦随去。既至，其家方知客死，又知橐金无恙，大感恸。客有子，束装偕隶至，贼已瘐死狱中。令乃取橐验而付之。其犬仍尾其子至，扶榇偕返，还往数千里，旅食肆宿，与人无异。
    　　论曰：夫人赴几在智，观变在忍。祸起仓卒，张皇震慑而不知所出，智不足也；不忍忿忿之心，蹈义赴难，而规画疏略，志虽诚而谋卒无济，忍不足也。故曰成事难。使犬当少年戕客之时，奋其牙齿以与贼角，糜身巨梃而不之避，烈矣，然于客无补。啣哀茹痛，疾走控吁，而于贼之窟宅未能晓识，纵令当事怜而听我，荒畦漫野，于何索之？冤难达，贼不可得也。唯明有报贼之心，而不骤起以骇之。

    Explanation

    241

    A dog repeatedly came to the magistrate's presence and barked incessantly. The magistrate then thought and said, "The guest died, but his family still exists. To whom does this bag of money belong? The dog barking, could it be this?" He then sent a subordinate to go directly to Taiyuan, and the dog also followed. Once they arrived, the family only then knew the guest had died, and also knew that the bag of money was unharmed. They were greatly moved to tears. The guest had a son who, dressed and accompanied by the subordinate, arrived, and the thief had already died in prison. The magistrate then took the bag, verified it, and handed it over. The dog still followed the son, helped carry the coffin back, and traveled back and forth thousands of miles, eating and lodging in inns, no different from humans.

    The commentator says: A person's response to danger lies in wisdom, and observing changes lies in patience. Disasters arise suddenly, and one panics and is terrified, not knowing what to do, showing a lack of wisdom. Unable to bear anger, daring to face danger for righteousness, but planning is rough and inadequate, though the intention is sincere, the plan ultimately fails, showing a lack of patience. Therefore, it is said that success is difficult. If the dog had been present when the young man killed the guest, it would have fought with its teeth against the thief, facing a large stick without flinching, it would have been heroic, but it would not have helped the guest. Enduring grief and pain, running quickly to appeal, but it did not know the thief's hideout, even if the authorities pitied and listened to me, desolate fields and wilderness, where would one look for it? The injustice is hard to resolve, the thief cannot be found. Only with a clear intention to avenge the thief, but not suddenly to startle them.

    242

    知县之可诉，而姑忍以候，逡巡追蹑以识其处，贼已在吾目中，而后走诉之。已落吾彀中，而后奋怒于一啮，而仇可得，金可还，太原之问可通，而客之榇可以归矣。其经营细稳，不必痛之遽伸，而务其忠之克济，是荆轲、聂政之所不能全，子房、豫让诸人所不得遂，而竟遂之者也。岂独狺讼公庭，旅走数千里外之奇且壮哉？夫人孰不怀忠，而遇变则渝；孰不负才，而应猝则乱。智取其深，勇取其沉，以此临天下事，何弗办焉？予既悲客，又甚羡客之有是犬也而胜人也。
    　　[张山来曰：义犬事不一而足，特录此篇者，以其事为尤奇也。
    　　又曰：犬固义矣，而此令亦有良心。设墨吏当之，此金尚能归客之子乎？] 奇女子传　建昌徐芳仲光悬榻编

    Explanation

    242

    Knowing that the county magistrate can be appealed to, but for the time being, endure and wait, cautiously follow and track to identify the location. Once the thief is within my sight, I then go to report him. Once he is within my grasp, I will then unleash my anger with a single bite, and thus the enemy can be defeated, the money can be recovered, the question from Taiyuan can be resolved, and the guest's coffin can be returned. His careful and steady planning does not require immediate action out of anger, but rather focuses on achieving loyalty and success. This is something that even Jing Ke, Nie Zheng, Zhang Liang, and Yu Rang could not accomplish, but he has achieved it. Is it not extraordinary and heroic to not only bark in court and travel thousands of miles, but also to achieve such a feat? Who does not harbor loyalty, but when faced with change, they waver; who does not have talent, but when faced with sudden events, they become disorganized. Wisdom lies in depth, and courage lies in steadiness. With such qualities, what in the world cannot be accomplished? I grieve for the guest, and I greatly envy the guest who has such a dog that surpasses humans.

    [Ou Shanlai said: Stories of loyal dogs are numerous, but this particular account is recorded because it is especially extraordinary.
    Furthermore, the dog is indeed loyal, and this magistrate also has a sense of conscience. If a corrupt official were in charge, could this money still be returned to the guest's son?] The Strange Tale of a Loyal Woman, compiled by Xu Fangzhong of Jianning.

    243

    奇女子者，丰城杨氏女，归李氏子为妇。谭兵围南昌，游骑四出，掠丁男实军。妇为小校王某所得。校山东人，故有妻；妇曲意事之，甚见昵，已生一子矣。
    　　亡何，校家渐落，从军去。妇诡语妻曰：“生事萧条，恨不身生羽翼。”妻曰：“何也？”妇曰：“妾故夫本大家，先世遗资良厚，当播越时，曾以金珠数斛，潜瘗密室。今夫死妾掳，栋宇皆烬，此中重宝，瓦石同没。使得徙而之此，妾与夫人，何患不富乎？”妻艳之曰：“果尔，盍遣人发之？”妇曰：“此妾手营，无人识也。”嗟惜而罢。他日妻又问，妇曰：“妾固筹之，欲得此金，非妾行不可。妾妇人，安能远出？必易服，往还且数月，而此呱呱，何堪久掷？”妻大喜曰：“第行耳，若子吾自抚之。”妇故绻恋不肯，妻恿愈力，乃择日释笄薙辫，鞾袴腰弓刀，从两健儿，跃马而南。

    Explanation

    243

    A Remarkable Woman

    She was a woman from the Yang family of Fengcheng, who married into the Li family as a wife. During the time when Tan's troops surrounded Nanchang and roamed cavalry spread out, raiding men to fill the army, she was captured by a low-ranking officer named Wang. The officer was from Shandong and already had a wife; the woman served him with great care and was greatly favored, having already given birth to a son.

    Not long after, the officer's family gradually declined, and he went to join the army. The woman deceitfully said to his wife, "Our lives are so desolate, I regret not having wings." The wife asked, "Why is that?" The woman replied, "My former husband was from a wealthy family, and the wealth left by previous generations was substantial. During the time of dispersion and chaos, I once hid several measures of gold and jewels in a secret room. Now that my husband is dead and I am a captive, this treasure is buried along with the bricks and stones. If I could move here, my husband and I would have no fear of poverty!" The wife was intrigued and said, "If it is true, why not send someone to dig it up?" The woman replied, "This was my own doing, and no one else knows about it." They sighed and gave up. Another day, the wife asked again, and the woman said, "I have already thought about it. To get this treasure, I must go myself. As a woman, how can I travel far? I must change my clothes, and it will take several months to go and return. But this baby, how can it bear to be left behind for so long?" The wife was delighted and said, "Just go ahead, your son I will take care of myself." The woman hesitated, reluctant to leave, but the wife urged her more strongly. So, she chose a day to cut her hair and braid, wore boots, trousers, and girded herself with a bow and sword, accompanied by two strong men, she leapt on horseback and rode south.

    244

    渡章江，去家数十里，止逆旅。以醇酒饮两健儿，皆醉，夜潜起骈馘之。驰骑至里，以马策挝家门大叫。夫从牖罅瞷视，见是少年将军，不敢出。里老数辈，稍前谒问。妇曰：“别有勾当，不关公等。”门启，妇歇马中堂，踞坐索故夫，呼叱甚厉。里中疑有他故，恐相累，共促夫出。夫伛偻前谒，伏地不敢起。妇曰：“颇识吾否？”夫对曰：“万死不能识将军。”妇曰：“试认之。”夫谢不敢，侧目微睇，惘然失措。妇叹曰：“真不识矣！”于是推几前抱夫起，痛哭曰：“妾非他，妾，君被掠杨氏妇也。”具述其易装巧脱状，一时喧动里中。亲识更阗门，贺李氏子再得妇。
    　　事闻邑令，为给牒奖许。绅士之贤者，多妇义略，相率为诗歌美之，皆曰：“奇女子！奇女子！”云。此甲午年事。

    Explanation

    244

    Crossing the Zhang River, he traveled dozens of miles from home and stopped at an inn. He drank strong wine with two strong men, and they all became drunk. At night, he secretly got up and killed them. He rode his horse to the village, beating the door with his horsewhip and shouting loudly. The husband peered through a crack in the window and saw that it was a young general, daring not to come out. Several elders from the village approached to pay respects and ask. The woman said, "There is another matter at hand, which does not concern you all." The door opened, and the woman dismounted her horse in the middle of the hall, sitting upright and demanding her former husband, calling out to him very sternly. The villagers suspected there was another matter and feared it might implicate them, so they all urged the husband to come out. The husband, bowing low, approached to pay respects, and fell to the ground, unable to stand up. The woman asked, "Do you recognize me?" The husband replied, "Even if I die a thousand deaths, I cannot recognize the general." The woman said, "Try to recognize me." The husband refused, saying he dared not, and peered sideways with a slight glance, feeling lost and at a loss. The woman sighed and said, "Truly, you do not recognize me!" She then pushed the table aside and embraced the husband, crying bitterly and saying, "I am not someone else; I am the Yang woman who was abducted by you." She detailed her story of easily changing clothes and skillfully escaping, causing a commotion in the village. Relatives and acquaintances came to fill the door, congratulating Li's son on getting a wife again. The matter was reported to the county magistrate, who issued a document to commend and approve. The virtuous gentry spoke highly of the woman's virtue, writing poems to praise her, all saying, "A remarkable woman! A remarkable woman!" This happened in the year Jia Wu.

    245

    论日：《易》有之：“妇人之义，从一而终”。邮亭之妇，以引腕小嫌，举刀自断其臂；其肯隐忍驱掠，为厮养生子乎？女行如此，节不足称矣。然人之情，于近则昵之，所远则益疏而掷之。妇巾帼婉弱，异地飘堕，以数千里雨绝星分，势无回合；乃能谲谋幻出，弭耳豢槛之中，飏翮绦笼之外，弄愚妇如转丸，剪凶雏若折朽，其深智沉勇，有壮男子不办者矣！彼台柳之假手虞候，乐昌之乞怜半镜，奄奄气色，视此孰多乎？女子如此，不谓之奇不可也。往盱郡之变，里中有长年，为卒絷驾一舟，舟所载掠得妇十数人，膏首袨服，笑语吃吃，无有几微惨悴见颜面者。长年退而叹息。而某村少妇归一弁，夫闻，百计营入，以重金求赎。妇见夫，瞠目曰：“此非吾夫！”夫骇走，几于不免。盖情迁腹变，其甚者又如此矣！且天下之得新捐故，仇其夫不肯一顾者岂少乎？抑如柳先生所传河间妇者，自昔已如是耶？

    Explanation

    245

    Translation

    In discussing this matter, the Yijing states: "The duty of a woman is to be faithful to one husband until death." The woman at the posthouse, due to a minor grievance over her wrist, took a knife and severed her own arm; would she endure being driven and mistreated, and serve as a slave to bear a child? Her conduct as a woman is such that her moral integrity is not worthy of praise. However, human nature tends to be close to those nearby and distant to those far away. A woman, delicate and weak, stranded in a foreign land, separated by thousands of miles, with no hope of reunion; yet she was able to devise cunning plans, tame and confine herself within the cage, and soar beyond the bounds of her imprisonment, treating foolish women like rolling balls, destroying vicious children as easily as breaking rotten wood—her deep wisdom and steadfast courage are something even a strong man could not achieve! Compared to the woman Tai Liu, who used the servant to act on her behalf, or Lady Luo of Lechang, who sought pity through a broken mirror, appearing lifeless and weak, who among them is more remarkable? Such a woman, one cannot call her extraordinary. During the upheaval in Xuyu, there was an elderly man in the village who was captured by soldiers and made to row a boat. The boat carried several women who had been forcibly taken, all dressed in luxurious and ornate clothing, laughing and chatting, with no signs of sadness or distress on their faces. The elderly man, after returning, sighed deeply. In another village, a young woman returned to a man in a hat. When her husband heard of this, he tried every means to enter and paid a large sum of money to redeem her. When the woman saw her husband, she stared in disbelief and said, "This is not my husband!" Her husband was so startled that he ran away, nearly escaping unharmed. It seems that feelings and loyalties can change, and in extreme cases, it is like this! Moreover, in the world, there are not few women who discard their former husbands for new ones and harbor resentment toward their old partners. Is it not the case that such women, as recounted by Mr. Liu from Hejian, have been like this since ancient times?

    246

    或曰：“女子不忘夫，是矣。而舍其子，无乃忍乎？”东海生日：此所以奇也。非是子无以信其妻，而故夫不可见矣。厮养之子，奚子也。世之不能为女子者，皆其不能舍者也。女子之以金珠艳其妻，想奇；巾帼而介胄，胆奇；夜醉馘两健儿，手奇；抵家不遽识夫，踞而骇之，而后哭之，始终结撰，亦无不奇。然尤更奇于舍其子。夫唯其能舍，斯所以能取也欤？

    Explanation

    246

    Some say, "A woman does not forget her husband, that is true. But to abandon her son, is that not cruel?" Donghai Sheng replied: This is why it is extraordinary. Without this son, she cannot trust her wife, and the former husband cannot be seen. A servant's son, what kind of son is he? Those who cannot be good wives are all those who cannot let go. A woman uses gold and jewels to adorn her wife, thinking it extraordinary; wearing a headscarf and armor, daring to be extraordinary; killing two strong men while drunk at night, extraordinary by hand; upon returning home, she does not immediately recognize her husband, sits down in shock, and then cries, throughout the entire process, there is nothing not extraordinary. But it is even more extraordinary to abandon her son. Only because she can let go, can she gain something in return.

    247

    [张山来曰：拙庵之论备矣。尤妙在小校从军去后，始露其谋。设非然者，则小校必偕之而行矣。] 曲全节义疏　阿毕阮 邸报
    　　巡视南城监察御史阿口口、毕口口、阮尔询等，题为曲全节义，以敦风化事。
    　　该臣等看得王知礼，即正法牵连叛犯李范同之子李殿机也。其母张氏，给配象房校尉王伏。殿机年甫三岁，随母抚养，因入后父王姓。后充校尉，以私回原籍，曾经銮仪卫革退。于廿三年，将身卖与镶红旗佛尔海佐领下厄尔库家。
    　　据幼聘王氏供称：年三十四岁，伊叔伊兄逼嫁，决志不从，探得伊夫尚存，不忍即死，守妇人从一之义，匍匐千余里外，以图完聚。是女子真有丈夫行也。
    　　据厄尔库之供：我虽一穷巴牙拉，无人供役，价卖李殿机。因只身不便使唤，复买婢萧氏，配为夫妇。今重王氏节义，不取伊仆身价，情愿断出，不忍拆李殿机已配之妇，并许与萧氏同归。前后二婚，悉候发落。轻财好义，此巴牙拉真有义士风也。

    Explanation

    247

    Zhang Shanlai said: Zhuo'an's argument is comprehensive. Especially clever is that the plot is revealed only after the minor officer leaves for the army; if it were not so, the minor officer would have accompanied him.

    A Memorandum on Ingeniously Preserving Loyalty and Righteousness by A, B, and Ruan Erxun, in the Official Gazette

    The inspectors of the Southern City, A, B, and Ruan Erxun, et al., submit a memorandum on ingeniously preserving loyalty and righteousness to encourage proper customs and morals.

    We, the officials, observe that Wang Zhili is the son of Li Fantong, a convict implicated in a rebellion. His mother, Zhang, was assigned to the Elephant House as a guard named Wang Fu. When Wang Dianji was just three years old, he was raised by his mother and took the surname of his stepfather, Wang. Later, he served as a guard but privately returned to his hometown and was once dismissed by the Imperial Ceremonial Guard. In the twenty-third year, he sold himself to the household of Erku under the banner of the Bordered Red Banner.

    According to Wang, who was betrothed at a young age and is now thirty-four years old, she was forced into marriage by her uncle and brother, but she was determined not to comply. Upon finding out that her husband was still alive, she could not bear to die immediately and adhered to the principle of a woman being faithful to one husband. She crawled over a thousand miles to reunite with her husband. This woman truly acted with the courage of a man.

    According to Erku's statement: Although I am a poor B. Y. L., I have no one to serve me. I sold Wang Dianji for a price. Because he was alone and it was inconvenient to use him, I again bought a maid named Xiao and married her to him. Now, I value Wang's loyalty and righteousness and do not take the price of his servant. I willingly let him go and cannot bear to separate Wang Dianji's already married wife. I also allow Xiao to return with them. The two previous marriages await final decision. I am light on wealth and fond of righteousness; this B. Y. L. truly has the spirit of a righteous man.

    248

    据范一魁虽供年六十二岁，但以异姓人，携一女子远行，迹涉嫌疑，事干非分，因唤稳婆更番验过，已得真实。据女子之供，是范一魁怜王氏立志寻夫，不顾是非成败，护持完节，似亦人情所难得者。
    　　此皆我皇上至德深仁，恩濡化洽，人心风俗，直接唐虞。是以女人怀贞，匹夫向义，共成一段奇缘，播之海内，传之千万世，见贞节之风，超出于寻常事外者。臣等查在官人与旗人原有定例，何敢于例外妄奏？但王氏贞心守节，冒死寻夫，若竟不准其完聚，王氏无从着落，情似可悯。虽据厄尔库之供，情愿断出听其完聚，然又非现行之例。臣等再四踌躇，因事关风化，仰体我皇上尧、舜，不忍一夫一妇不得其所至意，故备述其情事本末，合词上闻。格外之仁，均候圣断，非臣等所敢置喙也。伏乞敕部议覆施行。

    248

    Though he claimed to be sixty-two years old, as an outsider carrying a woman far away, the situation raised suspicions of improper conduct. Midwives were called to examine her, and the examination confirmed the truth. According to the woman's testimony, Fan Yikui pitied Wang's determination to find her husband, regardless of right or wrong, success or failure, and upheld her chastity, which seems to be a rare quality of human nature. All this is due to the virtue and benevolence of our emperor, whose grace and influence have shaped the people's hearts and customs, directly influenced by the ideals of Yao and Shun. Women uphold chastity, and men uphold righteousness, together forming a remarkable bond, spread throughout the land and passed down for countless generations, to see a virtue of chastity that transcends ordinary affairs. Officials and Manchu people have established rules, how dare we make an exception and report this? If we do not allow them to reunite, Wang will have no place to go, the situation seems pitiable. Though according to Erku's report, he is willing to sever ties and allow them to reunite, yet it is not a current precedent. We have hesitated repeatedly, because it concerns moral conduct, in accordance with the emperor's virtue, like Yao and Shun, cannot bear that one man and one woman should not be together. Therefore, we fully describe the details of the situation and jointly report to the emperor. Extraordinary benevolence, awaiting the emperor's decision, not something we dare to comment on. We humbly request the department to deliberate and implement.

    249

    [张山来曰：此事已经部覆，如其所请矣。王氏守志寻夫，固为难得，而巴牙拉厄君听其与萧氏同归，不索身价，尤属义举。予故亟表而出之。
    　　按唐诗中，有闺秀三人联句，前列名处，合称“光威裒”。今此疏三君联名，因仿其例称“阿毕阮”云。]
    　　
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷八 江石芸传　豫章吴良枢璇在强意堂稿
    　　江石芸，吴山桃花崖女子也。幼习经史，穷元会运世之数。及长，好兵法，铸剑诛妖，摄人万里外。一日过小孤山，遇白衣道士，授以书，尽通其义；人读之，莫能晓也。以时无知者，遂隐于吴山。种桃花，无根，花四时常开，名其地曰桃花崖。

    Explanation

    249

    Zhang Shanlai said: "This matter has already been reviewed by the department, as requested. The woman Wang remained steadfast in her resolve to find her husband, which is indeed rare. And the gentleman Ba Yala'er allowed her to return with Xiao without demanding a price, which is particularly a noble deed. Therefore, I quickly bring it to light."

    According to the Tang poetry, there were three female poets who collaborated on verses, and their names were combined into a collective title called "Guang Weipou." Now, this petition by three gentlemen is also named collectively as "Abi Ruan" following the same example.

    From the Yuchu Xinzhi Volume 8, The Biography of Jiang Shiyun, by Wu Liangshuxuan of Yuzhang in Qiangyi Tanggao:

    Jiang Shiyun was a woman from Taohuaya on Wushan Mountain. As a child, she studied the classics and histories, mastering the principles of the universe, the seasons, and the cycles of time. When she grew up, she took an interest in military strategy, forging swords to slay demons, and could summon people from thousands of miles away. One day, as she passed by Xiaogushan, she encountered a white-robed Taoist who gave her a book. She understood all its meanings; when others read it, they could not comprehend it. At the time, there were no others who understood it, so she retreated to Wushan. She planted peach blossoms, which had no roots but bloomed all four seasons, and she named the place Taohuaya.

    250

    崖下月，当日午而明。或曰：“此龙宫女子也。有宝珠，其光夺日入月。”因聚群盗劫之，其珠不可见。石芸曰：“珠固在，若乌能得也？舍若珠，劫吾珠，若将失其珠，乌能得我珠？唯自宝其珠以无失其珠可耳。”
    　　崖之中有黄夫人者，与之善。黄夫人家有虎，名白公，出入常骑之，能陟山渡水。石芸家有白牛一头，卧桃花下，鼻无绳，常出入自如。人以为黄夫人虎，不敢近。久之，石芸与夫人亦不知也。于时构茅屋崖下，读《易》终日，不为人所知。所著有《悟真注》。有为之序者，曰：“不知何许人也。”
    　　予尝见石芸，观其所著书，其女子邪？其非女子邪？天乎，其不知我也！宜其不知何许人也！
    　　[张山来曰：补天立极，应归女娲氏。其光夺日入月，则丹成矣；驱烟染墨，设想着语，皆不在人间，宜世人之不知也。

    Explanation

    250

    A moon-like phenomenon in the cliff is bright at noon. Some say, "This is a girl from the dragon palace. She has a pearl, whose light outshines both the sun and the moon." Because a group of bandits gathered to rob it, the pearl cannot be seen. Shi Yun said, "The pearl is still there; how can you get it? Give up your pearl, and rob my pearl. If you lose your pearl, how can you get my pearl? Only by valuing your own pearl can you avoid losing it."

    In the cliff, there is a lady named Huang, who is kind to her. Lady Huang's family has a tiger, named Bai Gong, which she often rides. The tiger can climb mountains and cross rivers. Shi Yun's family has a white cow, lying under peach blossoms, with no rope, and it can move freely. People think it is Lady Huang's tiger and dare not approach. After a long time, Shi Yun and Lady Huang also did not know. At that time, she built a thatched cottage at the cliff and read the I Ching all day, and no one knows her. She wrote Wuzhen Zhu. There is a preface for it, which says, "I do not know who she is."

    I once met Shi Yun and read her writings. Is she a woman? Or is she not a woman? Heaven knows, she does not know me! It is appropriate that the world does not know her.

    [Comment: Zhang Shanlai said, "To mend the sky and establish the pole, it should be attributed to Nuwa. If its light outshines the sun and the moon, then the elixir is complete; driving away smoke and dyeing ink, imagining words, all of these are not in the human world. It is appropriate that the world does not know her."]

    251

    又曰：洪子去芜，授我《强意堂稿》，美不胜收。仅登其一，余者自当借光梓入《阐幽集》中，以成大观也。] 耕云子传　汇邨洪嘉植去芜大荫堂稿
    　　耕云子，秦人也，隐于楚江之西。尝有人见其登匡庐顶，携一竹杖，衣葛藟衣，不冠，冬夏不易；见月出，则抚掌大叫啸，糜鹿不辟，从之行，见之者皆谓神仙人也。身长七尺，长髯而修下，双瞳子炯炯如流电光。人问其姓字，不答。性嗜酒，有饷，则大笑尽饮，去亦不谢。卒有人终饷之不懈。人疾病过其前者，则止之，语其故，治以药草，遂愈。酬以钱，不受，曰：“吾非医者，恶用此？”其行事多如此类。然其不能与人以可见者，人遂不能知也。尝入市，众哗之，谓其异人。趋而前，则不为礼；各相视无语，则又两手爬搔，眼顾五老峰云起，移时去。
    　　或曰：“耕云子，非秦人也。”耕云子曰：“秦无人也。”或曰：“耕云子，有道人也，龙蛇其身者也。人莫知其所自来，其隐君子邪？”

    Explanation

    251

    Gengyunzi, a native of Qin, hid in the west of Chu River. Once, someone saw him climb to the top of Mount Lu, carrying a bamboo staff, wearing a geltui robe, without a hat, unchanged through winter and summer; when he saw the moon rise, he would clap his hands and shout loudly, and deer would not avoid him, following him, and those who saw him all called him a god or immortal. He was seven feet tall, with long beard and long lower body, his double pupils shining like flowing lightning. When people asked his name, he did not answer. He loved wine, if offered, he would laugh and drink it all, leaving without thanking. Eventually, there was someone who continued to offer him wine without tiring. If someone who was ill passed by him, he would stop them, tell them the reason, and treat them with medicinal herbs, and they would recover. When offered money, he would not accept, saying, "I am not a doctor, what use would I have for this?" His actions were mostly like this. However, because he could not show himself to people, people could not understand him. He once went to the market, where the crowd hailed him as a strange person. When they approached him, he did not show respect; when they looked at each other without speaking, he would scratch his hands and look at the rising clouds on Wulao Peak, and leave after a while.

    Some said, "Gengyunzi is not from Qin." Gengyunzi said, "There are no people in Qin." Others said, "Gengyunzi is a person of the Way, one whose body is like a dragon or snake. No one knows where he comes from, perhaps he is a hidden sage?"

    252

    洪子日：古无神仙，无异人。天下有道，将安其身于烟霞泉石之中乎？夫何皇皇如也欲与天下之士日相见哉？顾天有不可逆者，而终皭然长往矣！凤集于棘，鷃雀调之；神龙潜乎深渊，终能雨此九土也。
    　　[张山来曰：古无神仙，非无神仙也。耕田凿井，含哺鼓腹，夫人而神仙也。古无异人，何以异于人哉？尧、舜与人同耳。然则神仙、异人之有，其于中古乎？读此可以知世变矣。] 吴孝子传　魏禧冰叔魏叔子文集
    　　孝子姓吴，名绍宗，字二璧，建昌新城县人，世居梅溪里。性聪敏，幼善属文。万历丙午，督学骆公日升，拔置诸生第一，时年二十，屡试辄高等。
    　　孝子父道隆，善病，久之，痹不能起，前后血并下，医药十余年，无效者。戊午正月病甚，孝子惶恐无所出，乃斋戒沐浴，焚香告天地，刺肘上血书，将谒太华山，自投舍身崖下代父死。

    Explanation

    252

    Final Translation

    Gongzi said: In ancient times, there were no immortals, nor were there extraordinary beings. If the world was governed by virtue, would one seek refuge in the natural beauty of mist, clouds, springs, and stones? Why then the anxious desire to meet scholars every day? Despite the unchangeable nature of heaven, one remains pure and continues on their path! The phoenix gathers on thorns, and small birds disturb it; the divine dragon hides in deep waters, yet it can still bring rain to all nine lands.

    [Zh Zhang Shanlai said: In ancient times, there were no immortals, but there were immortals. Tilling fields, digging wells, and living a simple life, everyone could achieve immortality. In ancient times, there were no extraordinary individuals, how could they be different from ordinary people? Yao and Shun were no different from ordinary people. Thus, the existence of immortals and extraordinary individuals, does it belong to the middle ages? Reading this can provide insight into the changes in society.]

    Biography of Wu Xiaozhi by Wei Xi Bingshu and Wei Shuzi

    Wu Xiaozhi, whose courtesy name was Erbi, was from Jianchang Xincheng County, and his family had long resided in Meixi Village. He was intelligent and showed a talent for literature from a young age. In the year Bingwu of the Wanli era, the supervisor of education, Lao Gong Risheng, placed him first among the students, and at the age of twenty, he consistently achieved high ranks in exams.

    Wu Xiaozhi's father, Dao Long, was prone to illness. After a long time, he became paralyzed and could not move, and he experienced bleeding from both ends. He sought medical treatment for over ten years, but it was ineffective. In the first month of the Wuxu year, his illness worsened, and Wu Xiaozhi, in a state of panic and unable to find a solution, fasted, bathed, burned incense, and prayed to heaven and earth. He wrote a message in blood on his elbow and intended to visit Mount Taihua, throwing himself off a cliff to die in his father's place.

    253

    太华山者，抚州崇仁县之名山也，距新城三百里，相传神最灵异。诸来谒者，有罪辄被祸不得上，甚则有灵官击杀之，同行人闻鞭声铮然；或忽狂病，自道生平隐恶事。而神殿左有悬崖陡绝，曰“舍身崖”。人情极不欲有生者，则掷身投之，头足尽破折死。
    　　孝子既告天作疏，明晨独身行。二日，至山上，宿道士管逊吾寮。同寮宿者，南昌乡先生二人，同郡邑诸生三人。十八日，孝子升殿，默祷焚疏既，同寮人相邀游著棋峰，路经舍身崖。孝子于是越次前行，至崖所歘然投身下。同行人惊绝，不知所为，一时传骇，聚观者千人。道士使人买棺往就殡，自山顶至崖下，路迂折四十里。而殿上道士急奔崖所，呼众人曰：“谁言吴秀才投崖死也？今方在神座下叩头，方巾道服如故。”众群走殿上视之，果然。

    Explanation

    253

    The Great Hua Mountain is a famous mountain in Chongren County, Fuzhou Prefecture, located 300 li away from Xincheng. It is said that the deity here is the most miraculous. Those who come to pay homage, if they have committed a sin, will be punished and cannot ascend. In severe cases, the divine officials will kill them, and fellow travelers will hear the sound of whips. Sometimes, they will suddenly fall ill with madness, confessing their hidden evil deeds. There is a cliff called the "Cliff of Self-Sacrifice" on the left side of the temple. Those who are extremely unwilling to live will throw themselves down, breaking their heads and feet to death.

    The filial son had already reported to heaven and made a memorial. The next morning, he set out alone. After two days, he arrived at the mountain and stayed at the lodging of Taoist Priest Guan Xunwu. Among the fellow lodgers were two scholars from Nanchang and three from the same prefecture and county. On the eighteenth day, the filial son ascended the temple, silently prayed, and burned the memorial. The fellow lodgers then invited him to visit the Chess Peak. On the way, they passed by the Cliff of Self-Sacrifice. The filial son suddenly went ahead and threw himself down. The fellow travelers were shocked and did not know what to do. The news spread quickly, and a crowd of a thousand people gathered to watch. The Taoist priest bought a coffin and went to retrieve his body, which took a winding path of forty li from the top of the mountain to the bottom. The Taoist priest on the temple steps quickly ran to the cliff and called out to the crowd, saying, "Who says that Scholar Wu threw himself off the cliff and died? Now he is kneeling before the deity, wearing his scholar's cap and Taoist robe as usual." The crowd rushed to the temple to see him, and indeed, he was there.

    254

    方孝子之自投崖也，立空中不坠，开目视，足下有白云起；又遥望见石门，门上一大“孝”字。俄而见三神人命之曰：“孝子，吾左侧石有仙篆九十二画，汝谨记之，归书纸食汝父，不独却疾，且延年矣。”更授催生、治痢疟、驱瘟咒并诸篆。孝子叩头谢毕，身已在殿上。孝子乃言：“吾如梦中也。”
    　　孝子既定，疾走归，一日有半而至家，至则父垂绝，不能言。孝子急书九十二画篆焚服之，室中人皆闻香气。甫入口，父即言曰：“是何药耶？”明日起坐啜粥，旬日疾大愈。孝子徒步反复六百里，不饮食者五日。而父乃益康强善饭，以诗酒自娱，年九十二，耳目清明，无疾终焉。

    Explanation

    254

    When the filial son threw himself off the cliff, he stood in mid-air without falling. Opening his eyes, he saw white clouds rising beneath him; he also saw a stone gate in the distance, with a large "孝" character on it. Soon after, he saw three divine beings who commanded him, saying, "Filial son, on the stone to our left, there are 92 characters of divine script. Remember them well. When you return home, write them on paper and feed them to your father. Not only will it cure his illness, but it will also prolong his life." They then taught him spells for childbirth, treating diarrhea and malaria, warding off epidemics, and other characters. The filial son bowed and thanked them, and found himself already on a platform. He then said, "It feels like a dream to me."

    After calming down, the filial son hurriedly returned home, arriving in half a day. When he got there, his father was near death and could not speak. The filial son quickly wrote the 92-character script, burned it, and fed it to his father. Everyone in the room smelled a fragrant aroma. As soon as it was taken, his father spoke, asking, "What kind of medicine is this?" The next morning, he sat up and ate porridge. After ten days, his illness greatly improved. The filial son walked back and forth 600 li without food or drink for five days. His father then became even healthier, strong, and good at eating, enjoying poetry and wine, lived to be 92 years old, with clear vision and hearing, and died peacefully.

    255

    由是孝子名闻远近。邑大冢宰涂公国鼎与为同道友，进士黄端伯、过周谋，举人黄名卿、涂伯昌，贡士璩光孚，皆拜为弟子。孝子当国变时，避乱泰宁，以病卒诸生廖愈达家。愈达，予所传三烈妇夫也。愈达来新城，主孝子子吴长祚，予故并得交。一日而见孝子之子，烈妇之夫，为荣幸焉。愈达言：“孝子生平好名义，轻财，往往出钱物为人解讼斗。既感神应，益自修。人病苦者，恒用符篆救之，以施药为名。”
    　　魏禧论曰：闻孝子常诣太华山，登座附神耳语，为人祈祷，颇不经。然邑君子往往道其事甚悉。梅溪东出四十里，为南丰县，县贡士赵希乾者，与禧交。母尝病甚，割心以食母；即剖胸，心不可得，则叩肠而截之。母子俱无恙。其后胸肉合，肠不得入，粪秽从胸间出，而谷道遂闭，饮食男女如平人。假谓非有神助，其谁然哉？其谁然哉？

    Explanation

    255

    Therefore, the filial son's fame spread far and wide. The Grand Minister of Works, Tu Gong Guoding, became his fellow in virtue, and scholars such as Jinshi Huang Duanbo, Guo Zhoumou, Ju Ren Huang Mingqing, Tu Bochang, and Gongshi Qu Guangfu all paid him homage as their teacher. During times of national upheaval, the filial son sought refuge in Taiping, where he eventually passed away due to illness at the home of a scholar named Liao Yuda. Liao Yuda, whom I have heard tell of three virtuous women, came to Xin Cheng, where he became the host of the filial son's son, Wu Changzuo. Thus, I also had the opportunity to meet him. It was an honor to meet both the son of the filial son and the husband of the virtuous women in one day. Liao Yuda said, "The filial son valued righteousness above all, was generous with his wealth, and often used his money to mediate disputes. After experiencing divine intervention, he became even more self-disciplined. He would often use talismans to heal the sick, claiming to distribute medicine."

    Hui Xi commented: I have heard that the filial son often visited Taihua Mountain, where he would sit and whisper to a deity, praying on behalf of others. This seems quite extraordinary. However, the gentlemen of the county often spoke of his deeds in great detail. Forty li east of Meixi lies Nanfeng County, where a scholar named Zhao Xiqian, who is acquainted with Xi, once lived. His mother was once gravely ill, and he cut out his own heart to feed her; when he could not find his heart, he cut into his intestines and severed them. Both mother and son survived unharmed. Later, the chest wound healed, but the intestines could not be reinserted, causing waste to seep from the chest and blocking the anus. Despite this, they lived like ordinary people. If it were not for divine intervention, who would believe such a story? Who would believe such a story?

    256

    [张山来曰：古有以祝由治病者，今“九十二画篆”，以及痢疟诸篆，殆即其道耶？然吾以为必孝子行之，乃能有验；若人人可行，斯又理之所难信者矣！] 李一足传　王猷定于一四照堂集
    　　李一足，名夔，未详其家世。有母及姐与弟。貌甚癯，方瞳微髭，生平不近妇人。好读书，尤精于《易》，旁及星历医卜之术。出常驾牛车，车中置一柜，藏所著诸书，逍遥山水间。所至人争异之。
    　　天启丁卯，至大梁，与鄢陵韩叔夜智度交。自言其父为诸生，贫甚，称贷于里豪；及期无以偿，致被殴死。时一足尚幼。其母啣冤十余年。姐适人，一足亦婚。母召其兄弟告之。一足长号，以头抢柱大呼。母急掩其口。不顾，奋身而出，断一梃为二，与弟各持，伺仇于市，不得；往其家，又不得；走郭外，得之，兄弟奋击碎其首。仇眇一目，抉其一，祭父墓前。归告其母，母曰：“仇报，祸将及！”乃命弟奉母他徙，遂别去。

    Explanation

    256

    Zhang Shanlai said: In ancient times, there were those who treated illnesses through the practice of Zhuoyou; today, the "92-stroke seal script" and other scripts associated with dysentery and malaria—could these be their methods? However, I believe it can only work if a filial son performs it; if everyone could do it, then it would be hard to believe. Li Yizhu's Biography by Wang Youding in Yu Yizhao Tang Ji

    Li Yizhu, whose given name was Kui, had an unknown family background. He had a mother, a sister, and a younger brother. He was very thin, with square pupils and a slight mustache, and throughout his life he did not associate with women. He loved reading, especially the I Ching, and also had expertise in astrology, calendrical calculations, and medical divination. He often traveled in a cart drawn by an ox, carrying a cabinet in the cart that housed his various writings, and he roamed freely through mountains and waters. Wherever he went, people marveled at him.

    In the year Ding Mao of the Tianqi era, he arrived in Daliang and formed a friendship with Yanling's Han Shuyue Zhide. He recounted that his father was a scholar, extremely poor, and had borrowed money from a local tyrant; when the repayment period arrived, he could not pay it back and was beaten to death. At that time, Li Yizhu was still young. His mother carried the grudge for over a decade. His sister married, and Li Yizhu also got married. The mother summoned her sons and told them. Li Yizhu let out a long cry, banging his head against a pillar and shouting loudly. His mother quickly covered his mouth. He ignored her, leaped up, broke a stick in two, and each took one, waiting for their enemy in the marketplace but could not find him; they went to his house, but still could not find him; they went outside the city walls and finally found him. The brothers attacked fiercely, breaking his skull. The enemy lost one eye, and they plucked out the other, offering it as a sacrifice at their father's tomb. Returning home, they told their mother, who said, "Now that the enemy has been avenged, disaster will soon befall us!" She then ordered her younger brother to take her and move to another place, and they parted ways.

    257

    时姐夫为令于兖，往从之。会姐夫出，姐见之，惊曰：“闻汝击仇，仇复活，今遍迹汝，其远避之！”为治装，赠以马。一足益恚恨，乃镌其梃曰：“没棱难砍仇人头。”遂单骑走青齐海上。见渔舟数百泊市米，一足求载以济，遂舍骑登舟。渡海，至一岛，名高家沟，其地延袤数十里，五谷鲜少。居民数百户，皆蛋籍，风土淳朴，喜文字，无从得师。见一足至，各率其子弟往学焉。
    　　其地不立塾，晨令童子持一钱诣师，师书一字于掌教之，则童子揖而退，明日复来。居数年，积钱盈室。辞去，附舟还青州，走狭邪。不数日，钱尽散，终不及私。由辽西过三关，越晋，历甘凉，登华岳，入于楚，抵黔、桂，复历闽海、吴、越间，各为诗文纪游。二十载，乃反其家。仇死，所坐皆赦。母亦没，登其墓大哭，数日不休。自以足迹遍天下，恨未入蜀。会鄢陵刘观文除夔守，招之同下三峡，游白帝、绵、梓诸山，著《依刘集》一卷。

    Explanation

    257

    At that time, my sister's husband was the magistrate of Yan, and I went to follow him. Just as my sister-in-law was about to leave, she saw me and was startled, saying, "I heard that you attacked your enemy, and the enemy has revived; now they are searching for you everywhere, so you should flee far away!" She prepared my luggage and gave me a horse. I became even more angry and resentful, and I carved on the staff, saying, "Without a sharp edge, it is hard to cut off the enemy's head." So I rode alone to the sea area of Qing and Qi. I saw hundreds of fishing boats docked to sell rice, and I asked for a ride to cross the sea. I left my horse and boarded the boat. I crossed the sea and arrived at an island called Gaojia Gou. The land extends for dozens of miles, but there is little grain. There are hundreds of households, all registered as fishermen; the local customs are simple and honest, they love literature, but have no teacher. When they saw me, they each took their children to learn from me. There is no school in this place. Every morning, the children would bring a coin to the teacher, who would write a character on their palm and teach them; the children would bow and leave, returning the next day. After several years, I had accumulated a lot of money. I bid farewell and boarded a boat back to Qingzhou, wandering through the streets. In a few days, all the money was spent, and I never touched any of it. I traveled from Liaoxi through the Three Passes, across Shanxi, through Gansu and Lanzhou, climbed Mount Hua, entered Chu, reached Guizhou and Guangxi, and then traveled through Fujian, Wu, and Yue. I wrote poems and essays to record my travels. After twenty years, I returned home. The enemy died, and all my crimes were forgiven. My mother also passed away, and I wept at her grave for several days. I thought I had traveled all over the world, but regretted not having visited Sichuan. Coincidentally, Liu Guanwen of Yanling was appointed governor of Kuizhou and invited me to travel down the Three Gorges with him, visiting White Emperor Mountain, Mian Mountain, and Zizhou Mountain, and I wrote a collection of poems called Yi Liu Ji.

    258

    其弟自母丧，不知所在。一日欲寄弟以书，属韩氏兄弟投汴之通衢。韩如其言，俄一客衣白袷，幅巾草屦，貌与一足相似，近前揖曰：“我张太羹也，兄书已得达。”言讫不见。辛巳，李自成陷中州诸郡，韩氏兄弟避乱至泗上，见一足于途，短褐敝屣，须眉皆白。同至玻璃泉，谈笑竟日，数言天下事不可为。问所之，曰：“往劳山访徐元直。”韩笑之。一足正色曰：“此山一洞，风雨时披发鼓琴，人时见之，此三国时徐庶也。”约诘朝复来，竟不果。

    Explanation

    258

    His younger brother, after his mother's death, was unknown. One day, he wanted to send a letter to his younger brother and entrusted the Han brothers to deliver it to the bustling streets of Bian. The Han brothers did as he asked, and soon a guest appeared wearing white robes, a wide-brimmed hat, and straw sandals. His appearance was similar to that of the one-footed man. He approached and bowed, saying, "I am Zhang Taigeng. Your letter has been delivered." After speaking, he disappeared. In the year Xin Si (1644), Li Zicheng captured the various counties of Zhongzhou. The Han brothers fled to the area around Si and saw the one-footed man on the road. He was wearing a coarse robe and tattered shoes, with white beard and eyebrows. They went together to the Glass Spring, talked and laughed all day, and he repeatedly said that the world was not worth doing anything about. When asked where he was going, he said, "I am going to Laoshan to visit Xu Yuanzhi." The Han brothers laughed at him. The one-footed man said seriously, "There is a cave in this mountain where he sometimes lets his hair down and plays the qin. People sometimes see him. He is Xu Shu from the Three Kingdoms period." He promised to return the next day, but he did not.

    259

    甲申后，闻一足化去。先一日，遍辞戚友，告以远行。是日，鼻垂玉筋尺许，端坐而逝，袖中有《周易全书》一部。后数月，济人有在京师者，见之正阳门外。又有见于赵州桥下，持梃观水，伫立若有思者。韩子智度，不妄言人也，述其事如此。
    　　[张山来曰：观一足行事，亦孝子，亦侠客，亦文人，亦隐者，亦术士，亦仙人，吾不得而名之矣。] 孝贼传　王猷定于一
    　　贼不详其姓名，相传为如皋人，贫不能养母，遂作贼。久之，为捕者所获，数受笞有司。贼号曰：“小人有母无食，以至此也！”人且恨且怜之。一日母死，先三日廉知邻寺一棺寄庑下。是日，召党具酒食，邀寺中老阇黎痛饮。伺其醉，舁棺中野，负其母尸葬焉。比反，阇黎尚酣卧也。贼大叫叩头乞免，阇黎惊，不知所谓，起视庑下物，亡矣！亡何，强释之。厥后不复作贼。

    Explanation

    259

    After the year of Jia Shen (1644), it was heard that One Foot had passed away. The day before, he bid farewell to all his relatives and friends, telling them of his departure. On that day, tears streamed down his nose, about a foot long, and he sat upright and passed away, with a copy of the Complete Book of Changes in his sleeve. Several months later, someone from Jizhou who was in the capital city saw him at Zhengyangmen Gate. Another person saw him at the Zhaozhou Bridge, holding a stick and looking at the water, standing still as if in deep thought. Han Zhi Du, who does not speak lightly of others, describes the matter in this way.

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: Observing the actions of One Foot, he was also a filial son, a chivalrous knight, a scholar, a hermit, a magician, and a sage. I cannot name him.]

    The Story of the Filial Bandit by Wang Youding

    The bandit's name is unknown; he was said to be from Rugao, where poverty prevented him from supporting his mother, and thus he became a bandit. After some time, he was captured by the authorities and repeatedly whipped. The bandit cried out, "I am a poor man with a mother who has no food, and that is why I have come to this!" People both hated and pitied him. One day, his mother died. Three days before, he learned that there was a coffin in the temple's porch. On that day, he summoned his accomplices to prepare food and wine, inviting the old abbot of the temple to drink heavily. When the abbot was drunk, he carried the coffin to the wilds and buried his mother's body there. When he returned, the abbot was still sound asleep. The bandit cried out and begged for mercy, and the abbot was startled, not understanding what had happened. He looked down at the object in the porch and found it was gone! Shortly after, the bandit was forced to be released. Afterwards, he did not become a bandit again.

    260

    [张山来曰：有孝子如此，而听其贫，至于作贼，是谁之过欤？] 王翠翘传　余怀澹心手授钞本
    　　余读《吴越春秋》，观西施沼吴，而又从范蠡以归于湖，窃谓妇人受人之托，以艳色亡人之国，而不以死殉之，虽不负心，亦负恩矣。若王翠翘之于徐海，则公私兼尽，亦异于西施者哉。嗟乎！翠翘故娼家，辱人贱行，而所为耿耿若此。须眉男子，愧之多矣！余故悲其志，缀次其行事，以为之传。传曰：
    　　王翠翘，临淄人，幼鬻于倡，冒姓马，假母呼为翘儿。美姿首，性聪慧，携来江南。教之吴歈歌，则善吴歈歌；教之弹胡琵琶，则善弹胡琵琶。吹箫度曲，音吐清越，执板扬声，往往倾其座客。平康里中，翘儿名藉甚。然翘儿雅淡，顾沾沾自喜，颇不工涂抹倚门术。遇大腹贾及伧父之多金者，则目笑之，不予一盼睐温语。以是假母日忿而笞骂。会有少年私翘儿金者，以计脱假母，而自徙居嘉兴，更名王翠翘云。

    Explanation

    260

    张山来曰：有孝子如此，而听其贫，至于作贼，是谁之过欤？

    王翠翘传　余怀澹心手授钞本

    余读《吴越春秋》，观西施沼吴，而又从范蠡以归于湖，窃谓妇人受人之托，以艳色亡人之国，而不以死殉之，虽不负心，亦负恩矣。若王翠翘之于徐海，则公私兼尽，亦异于西施者哉。嗟乎！翠翘故娼家，辱人贱行，而所为耿耿若此。须眉男子，愧之多矣！余故悲其志，缀次其行事，以为之传。传曰：

    王翠翘，临淄人，幼鬻于倡，冒姓马，假母呼为翘儿。美姿首，性聪慧，携来江南。教之吴歈歌，则善吴歈歌；教之弹胡琵琶，则善弹胡琵琶。吹箫度曲，音吐清越，执板扬声，往往倾其座客。平康里中，翘儿名藉甚。然翘儿雅淡，顾沾沾自喜，颇不工涂抹倚门术。遇大腹贾及伧父之多金者，则目笑之，不予一盼睐温语。以是假母日忿而笞骂。会有少年私翘儿金者，以计脱假母，而自徙居嘉兴，更名王翠翘云。

    261

    当是时，歙人罗龙文，饶于财，侠游结宾客，与翠翘交欢最久，兼昵小妓绿珠。而越人徐海者，狡佻，贫无赖，方为博徒所窘，独身跳翠翘家，伏匿不敢昼见人。龙文习其壮士，倾身结友，接臂痛饮，推所昵绿珠与之荐寝。海亦不辞，酒酣耳热，攘袂持杯，附龙文耳语曰：“此一片土非吾辈得意场，丈夫安能郁郁久居人下乎？公宜努力，吾亦从此逝矣！他日富贵，毋相忘！”因慷慨悲歌，居数日别去。徐海者，杭之虎跑寺僧，所谓“明山和尚”者是也。

    Explanation

    261

    During that time, Luo Longwen from She County was wealthy and influential, known for his bravery and for associating with guests. He had the longest relationship with Cui Qiao, a woman of high social standing, and also favored the courtesan Green Pearl. Meanwhile, Xu Hai, a cunning and mischievous man from Yue, was poor and shiftless, having just been humiliated by gamblers. He alone jumped into the house of Cui Qiao, hid himself, and dared not appear during the day. Luo Longwen befriended him, sharing a deep bond and drinking heavily together. He offered the beloved Green Pearl to Xu Hai for intimate relations. Xu Hai accepted without hesitation. When they were drunk and flushed, Xu Hai rolled up his sleeves and held a cup, whispering into Luo Longwen's ear, "This land is not our place to achieve success. How can a man remain depressed and stay below others for long? You should strive hard, and I will also leave from here! In the future when we are wealthy and honored, do not forget each other!" He then sang a sorrowful song with great emotion and stayed for several days before departing. Xu Hai was a monk from the Hupao Temple in Hangzhou, known as Master Ming Shan.

    262

    居无何，海入倭，为舶主，拥雄兵海上，数侵江南。嘉靖三十五年，围巡抚阮鹗于桐乡，翠翘、绿珠皆被掳。海一见惊喜，命翠翘弹胡琵琶以佐酒，日益宠幸，号为夫人，斥诸姬罗拜。翠翘既已骄爱无比，凡军机密画，唯翠翘与闻。乃翠翘阳为亲昵，阴实幸其覆败，冀归国以老，泪渍渍常承睫洗面也。
    　　会总督胡宗宪开府浙江，善用兵，多计策，欲召致徐海，自戕麻叶、陈东，而离散王直之党，乃遣华老人赍檄招降。海怒，缚华老人，将斩之。翠翘语海曰：“今日之事，生杀在君，降不降何与来使？”海乃释其缚，畀金而遣之。老人归，告宗宪曰：“贼气方锐，未可图也。然臣睨海所幸王夫人者，左右视，有外心，或可借以歼贼耳。”

    Explanation

    262

    Soon after, Hai entered the ranks of the Japanese pirates, becoming their ship captain and possessing a powerful navy that repeatedly invaded the Jiangnan region. In the 35th year of the Jiajing era (1556), he surrounded Governor-General Ruan E in Tongxiang, capturing both Cui Qiao and Lu Zhu. Upon seeing them, Hai was delighted and ordered Cui Qiao to play the Huqin (a type of lute) to accompany the wine, increasingly favoring and pampering her, calling her "Lady" and dismissing all his other concubines, making them bow before her. Cui Qiao, already immensely proud and loved, was privy to all military strategies and plans. Yet, she outwardly pretended to be close to Hai, secretly hoping for his downfall, wishing to return to her homeland to grow old, with tears constantly welled up in her eyes and running down her face.

    Just then, Governor-General Hu Zongxian established his headquarters in Zhejiang, skilled in warfare and having many strategies. He wanted to summon and bring over Xu Hai, to kill Ma Ye and Chen Dong himself, and to disband Wang Zhi's followers. So he sent the old man Hua with a proclamation to persuade them to surrender. Hai was angry and tied up the old man Hua, about to behead him. Cui Qiao spoke to Hai, saying, "Today's matter is in your hands; whether to surrender or not has nothing to do with the messenger." Hai then released his bonds, gave him money, and sent him away. The old man returned and told Hu Zongxian, "The enemy's morale is high, and it cannot be plotted against. However, I noticed the woman Hai favored, who looked around and seemed to have ulterior motives. Perhaps we can use this to eliminate the enemy."

    263

    而罗龙文者微闻是语，自喜与翠翘旧好，乃因幕府上客山阴徐渭以见于宗宪。宗宪以乡曲故，降阶迎揖曰：“生亦有意功名富贵乎？吾今用君矣！”与语大说。遂受指诣海营，摄旧日任侠衣冠，投刺谒海。海亟延入，坐上座，置酒握龙文手曰：“足下远涉江湖，为胡公作说客耶？”龙文笑曰：“非为胡公作说客，乃为故人作忠臣耳。王直已遣子纳款，故人不乘此时解甲释兵，他日必且为虏。”海愕然曰：“姑置之，且与故人饮酒。”锦绣音乐，备极豪侈，僩然自以为大丈夫得志于时之所为也。酒半，出王夫人及绿珠者见龙文。龙文改容礼之，极宴语不及私。翠翘素习龙文豪侠，则劝海遣人同诣督府输款，解桐乡围。

    Explanation

    263

    Ruo Longwen secretly heard this news and was pleased that he had an old friendship with Cui Qiao. He went through the upper guest of the military camp, Xu Wei from Shanyin, to meet Zongxian. Because they were from the same village, Zongxian descended the steps to greet him with a bow and said, "Do you also have aspirations for fame, wealth, and honor? I will now employ you!" They spoke and were greatly pleased. So he accepted the instructions and went to the sea camp, putting on the clothes of a former roguish hero, and presented his card to pay a visit to Hai. Hai quickly invited him in, seating him in the upper seat, setting up wine, and taking Ruo Longwen's hand, he said, "Are you far-traveling across the rivers and lakes to act as a mediator for Hu Gong?" Ruo Longwen smiled and said, "I am not acting as a mediator for Hu Gong, but rather as a loyal minister for my old friend. Wang Zhi has already sent his son to surrender. If my old friend does not take this opportunity to lay down his weapons and disband his troops, he will surely become a captive in the future." Hai was startled and said, "Let's put it aside for now, and let's drink with our old friend." The embroidered music and luxurious preparations were fully prepared, and he felt proud, believing that he was a great man who had achieved success in his time. Halfway through the wine, he brought out Wang's wife and the Green Pearl to see Ruo Longwen. Ruo Longwen changed his expression and treated them with respect, and during the feast, they did not discuss private matters. Cui Qiao, who was accustomed to Ruo Longwen's heroic and chivalrous nature, encouraged Hai to send someone to the governor's office to surrender and lift the siege of Tongxiang.

    264

    宗宪喜，从龙文计，益市金珠宝玉，阴赂翠翘。翠翘益心动，日夜说海降矣。海信之，于是定计，缚麻叶，缚陈东，约降于宗宪。至桐乡城，甲胄而入。是时赵文华、阮鹗与宗宪列坐堂皇，海叩首谢罪，又谢宗宪。宗宪下堂摩其顶曰：“朝廷今赦汝，汝勿复反。”厚劳而出。海既出，见官兵大集，颇自疑。宗宪犹怜海，不欲杀降，而文华迫之。宗宪乃下令，命总兵俞大猷整师而进。会大风，纵火，诸军鼓噪乘之，贼大溃，歼焉。海仓皇投水，引出，斩其首，而生致翠翘于军门。

    Explanation

    264

    Zong Xian was pleased, following Long Wen's plan, and increased the purchase of gold, jewels, and precious stones, secretly bribing Cui Qiao. Cui Qiao became increasingly moved, day and night, persuading Hai to surrender. Hai believed it, and thus decided on a plan, tying up Ma Ye and Chen Dong, agreeing to surrender to Zong Xian. He arrived at Tongxiang City, entering in armor. At that time, Zhao Wenhua, Ruan E, and Zong Xian sat in a row in the main hall. Hai knelt and apologized, and also thanked Zong Xian. Zong Xian left the hall, touched his head, and said, "The court now forgives you, do not rebel again." He was treated with great care and then left. Hai had already left, and saw a large gathering of soldiers, becoming quite suspicious. Zong Xian still pitied Hai, and did not want to kill the surrendering, but Wenhua pressured him. Zong Xian then issued an order, ordering General Yu Dayou to prepare the army and advance. A strong wind arose, and they set fire. The troops shouted and attacked, routing and annihilating the rebels. Hai panicked and jumped into the water, was pulled out, had his head cut off, and Cui Qiao was captured alive and brought to the military camp.

    265

    宗宪大飨参佐，命翠翘歌吴歈歌，遍行酒。诸参佐或膝席，或起舞捧觞，为宗宪寿。宗宪被酒大醉，瞀乱，亦横槊障袖，与翠儿戏。席乱，罢酒。次日，宗宪颇愧悔醉时事，而以翠翘赐所调永顺酋长。翠翘既随永顺酋长，去之钱塘江中，恒悒悒捶床叹曰：“明山遇我厚，我以国事诱杀之。毙一酋又属一酋，吾何面目生乎？”向江潮长号大恸，投水死。
    　　外史氏日：嗟乎！翠翘以一死报徐海，其志亦可哀也！罗龙文者，世称小华道人，善制烟墨者也。始以游说阴赂翠翘，诱致徐海休兵，可谓智士。然其后依附权势，与严世蕃同斩西市，则视翠翘之死，犹鸿毛之于泰山也。人当自重其死，彼倡且知之，况士大夫乎？乃倡且知之，而士大夫反不知者，何也？悲夫！

    Explanation

    265

    Zongxian grandly entertained his aides, ordering Cuiqiao to sing a Wu Yu song and passing around the wine. The aides knelt on the mat or stood up to dance and hold the cup, toasting Zongxian. Zongxian, drunk from drinking heavily, became confused and disoriented, holding a spear and covering his sleeves, playing with Cuiqiao. The seating arrangement became chaotic, and the drinking stopped. The next day, Zongxian felt quite guilty and regretful about his drunken behavior and gave Cuiqiao as a gift to the leader of Yongshun. Cuiqiao, having followed the leader of Yongshun, traveled to Qiantang River, where she often sighed and pounded the bed, saying, "Mingshan treated me kindly, and I lured him to his death for the sake of the country. Killing one chief and handing over another, how can I face life?" Facing the tide, she wailed loudly and threw herself into the water, dying.

    The historian said: "Alas! Cuiqiao died to repay Xu Hai, and her resolve is also pitiable! Luo Longwen, known as the Little Hua Daoist, was a skilled maker of ink. Initially, he used persuasion and bribed Cuiqiao secretly, luring Xu Hai to stop fighting, which can be called a wise move. However, later on, he relied on power and authority, was executed with Yan Shifan at the Western Market, making his death seem insignificant compared to Cuiqiao's. People should value their own lives; even a prostitute knows this, let alone scholars and officials? Yet, while a prostitute knows it, scholars and officials do not. Why is that? Sad indeed!"

    266

    [张山来曰：胡公之于翠翘，不以赐小华，而以赐酋长，诚何必乎？观翠翘生致之后，不能即死，居然行酒于诸参佐前，则其意有所属从可知已。其投江潮以死，当非报明山也。] 戴文进传画苑三高士传之一　钱塘毛先舒稚黄东苑文钞
    　　明画手以戴进为第一，进字文进，钱唐人也。宣宗喜绘事，御制天纵，一时待诏有谢廷循、倪端、石锐、李在，皆有名。进入京，众工妒之。一日在仁智殿呈画，进进《秋江独钓图》，画人红袍垂钓水次。画唯红不易著，进独得古法入妙。宣宗阅之，廷循从旁跪曰：“进画极佳，但赤是朝廷品服，奈何著此钓鱼？”宣宗颔之。遂麾去余幅不视。故进住京师，颇穷乏。

    Explanation

    266

    Zhang Shanlai said: Regarding Hu Gong's treatment of Cui Qiao, he did not bestow her on Xiao Hua but instead bestowed her on the chieftain. Is it really necessary? After Cui Qiao was brought to life, she did not immediately die, and she unexpectedly served wine to the various attendants. Thus, it can be known that her intentions were directed towards someone. Her death by drowning in the river was likely not in retaliation against Ming Shan.

    As for the painting, the first of the Ming dynasty's painters was Dai Jin, whose courtesy name was Wenjin, and he was from Qiantang. The Xuande Emperor was fond of painting, and his skill was divinely inspired. At that time, there were court painters such as Xie Tingxun, Ni Duan, Shi Rui, and Li Zai, all of whom were renowned. When Dai Jin entered the capital, the other painters envied him. One day, at the Renzhi Palace, he presented a painting called "Autumn River Fishing Alone," depicting a man in a red robe fishing by the water. Painting red is difficult, but Dai Jin alone mastered the ancient technique to perfection. The Xuande Emperor examined it, and Xie Tingxun knelt beside him and said, "Dai's painting is extremely excellent, but red is the color of imperial attire. How can it be used for fishing?" The emperor nodded in agreement. He then dismissed the other paintings without further examination. Therefore, Dai Jin lived in the capital and was quite impoverished.

    267

    先是进锻工也，为人物花鸟，肖状精奇，直倍常工。进亦自得，以为人且宝贵传之。一日于市，见熔金者，观之，即进所造，怃然自失。归语人曰：“吾瘁吾心力为此，岂徒得糈，意将托此不朽吾名耳！今人烁吾所造，亡所爱，此技不足为也；将安托吾指而后可？”人曰：“子巧托诸金，金饰能为俗习玩爱，乃儿妇人御耳。彼唯煌煌是耽，安知工苦？能徙智于缣素，斯必传矣。”进喜，遂学画，名高一时。然进数奇，虽得待诏，亦轗轲亡大遇。其画疏而能密，著笔澹远，其画人尤佳，其真亦罕遇云。予钦进锻工耳，而命意不朽，卒成其名。

    Explanation

    267

    Before this, Jin was a metalworker, crafting figures of people, flowers, and birds that were exquisitely lifelike, priced double that of ordinary craftsmen. Jin took great pride in his work, believing that people would value and pass it down. One day in the marketplace, he saw someone melting gold and recognized it as his own creation. Feeling disheartened and lost, he returned home and told others, "I have exhausted my heart and effort on this, not just for the sake of earning a living, but to entrust it to immortalize my name! Now, people melt my creations, no longer caring, and this skill is not worth pursuing. Where can I entrust my fingers to achieve this?" Someone replied, "Your skill is tied to gold, but gold ornaments are only valued by children and women. They are only interested in the brilliance, not the effort. If you transfer your skill to silk and paper, your work will surely be passed down." Jin was pleased and decided to learn painting, achieving fame for a time. However, Jin's fate remained unlucky, and despite his success, he did not receive great recognition. His paintings were sparse yet detailed, with strokes that were subtle and distant. His portraits of people were especially good, and his real-life portraits were also rare. I admire Jin as a metalworker, but his intention to immortalize his name ultimately led to his success.

    268

    赞日：立志探悬，鬼神所赞。孰是殚精，而屑近玩？戴君操捶，锻金为生。感慨徙业，卒成高名。盖人极而天呈矣夫！
    　　[张山来曰：明画史又有仇十洲者，其初为漆工，兼为人彩绘栋宇，后徙而业画，工人物楼阁。予独嫌其略带匠气，顾不若戴文进为佳耳。且戴兼工山水，则尤不可及也。] 髯樵传　锡山顾彩天石手授钞本
    　　明季吴县洞庭山，乡有樵子者，貌髯而伟，姓名不著，绝有力。每暮夜樵采，独行山中，不避蛇虎。所得薪，人负百斤而止，髯独负二百四十斤，然鬻于人，止取百斤价。人或讶问之，髯曰：“薪取之山，人各自食其力耳。彼非不欲多负，力不赡也。吾力倍蓰而食不兼人，故贱其值。且值贱，则吾薪易售，不庸有利乎？”由是人颇异之，加刮目焉。

    Explanation

    268

    Translation:

    It is said: To set one's mind to explore the profound, praised by the gods and spirits. Who exhausts their energy, yet deems it trivial or unworthy? Master Dai wields the hammer, smelts gold for a living. Moved to change careers, finally achieved great fame. When human effort reaches its limit, heaven manifests itself!

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: The History of Ming Dynasty Painting also mentions Qiu Shizhou, who initially worked as a lacquerer, decorating buildings for others, and later changed his career to painting, specializing in figures and buildings. I alone find his work slightly tainted with a craftsman's style, though it is not as good as Master Dai. Moreover, Dai also excels in landscape painting, which is particularly unparalleled.]

    The Biography of the Bearded Woodcutter
    A handwritten copy given by Gu Cai Tianshi of Xishan

    During the late Ming dynasty, in the Dongting Mountains of Wuxian County, there was a woodcutter who had a beard and a great appearance. His name is unknown, but he was extraordinarily strong. Every evening, he would go into the mountains to gather firewood alone, unafraid of snakes and tigers. While others could carry only a hundred pounds of wood, he alone could carry two hundred and forty pounds. However, when he sold it, he charged only the price for a hundred pounds. People were surprised and asked him about this. The woodcutter replied, "The firewood comes from the mountain, and each person eats according to their own labor. They are not unwilling to carry more, but their strength is insufficient. My strength is five times greater, yet I consume no more than one person, so I lower the price. And by lowering the price, my firewood sells easily. Isn’t it still profitable?" Because of this, people began to look at him with new respect.

    269

    髯目不知书，然好听人谈古今事，常激于义，出言辩是非，儒者无以难。尝荷薪至演剧所，观《精忠传》所谓秦桧者出，髯怒，飞跃上台，捽桧殴，流血几毙。众咸惊救，髯曰：“若为丞相，奸似此，不殴杀何待？”众曰：“此戏也，非真桧。”髯曰：“吾亦知戏，故殴；若真，膏吾斧矣！”其性刚疾恶类如此。
    　　髯有兄进香茅山，堕崖折胸死。或传其暮夜饮酒不诚，被王灵官鞭杀者。髯怒，走一日夜，诣茅山，饮大醉，数王灵官曰：“汝有罪三！人敬祖师，来进香，固有善心，饮酒小过，无死状，汝辄杀之，不仁，罪一。祖师以慈庇下土，量甚宏大，汝居位下，行残忍，不遵祖师意，不恭，罪二。吾兄，小人也，酬香而来，小被酒，汝辄杀之；吾来不酬香，昨实大饮，今日詈汝，汝反不能杀，无勇，罪三。汝宜毁撤，曷为横鞭嗔目，坐踞于此？”欲夺鞭碎象，众譬遣之，乃止，负兄骨归葬焉。

    Explanation

    269

    A Bearded Man Who Knew Little of Books, Yet Enjoys Listening to People Talk About Ancient and Modern Affairs

    The bearded man, though not well-versed in books, enjoys listening to people discuss ancient and modern events. He is often moved by righteousness and speaks up to distinguish right from wrong, a task that even Confucian scholars cannot challenge. Once, while carrying firewood, he went to a theater to watch the play The Legend of Loyalty, where the character of Qin Hui appeared. The bearded man became angry, leapt onto the stage, grabbed Qin Hui, and beat him until he bled almost to death. Everyone was shocked and tried to save him. The bearded man said, "If you were the Chancellor, so corrupt, why not beat him to death?" The audience replied, "It's just a play, not the real Qin Hui." The bearded man responded, "I know it's a play, so I beat him; if it were real, I would have chopped him up!" His nature is stern and hating evil, just like this.

    The bearded man had a brother who went to Mount Mao to burn incense but fell off a cliff and died with a broken chest. Some said that his brother drank at night without sincerity and was beaten to death by the God of Wonders. The bearded man became angry, walked for a day and night to reach Mount Mao, drank heavily, and rebuked the God of Wonders, saying, "You have three sins! People respect the ancestral teachers and come to burn incense, naturally having good intentions. Drinking is a minor fault, not deserving of death. You killed him, unjust, first sin. The ancestral teachers protect the world with kindness and great tolerance. You in your position, act cruelly, do not follow the ancestral teachers' will, disrespectful, second sin. My brother, a commoner, came to burn incense, slightly drunk, you killed him; I came without burning incense, yesterday I drank heavily, today I insult you, you can't even kill me, lack of courage, third sin. You should be destroyed and removed, why brandish your whip and glare, sit here?" He wanted to take the whip and destroy the image, but the crowd persuaded him to stop, and he stopped, carrying his brother's bones back to bury them.

    270

    洞庭有孤子陈学奇，聘邹氏女为室，婚有期矣。女兄忽夺妹志，献苏宦某为妾。学奇泣诉于官，官畏宦势，无如何也。学奇讼女兄，宦并庇兄不得伸，学奇窘甚。一日，值髯于途，告之故，且曰：“若素义激，能为我筹此乎？”髯许诺：“然需时日以待之，毋迫我也。”学奇感泣。髯去，鬻身为显者舆仆。显者以其多力而勤，甚信爱之，得出入内闼。邹女果为其第三妾。髯得间，以陈情告，女泣如雨，诉失身状，愿公为昆仑。髯曰：“毋迫。

    Explanation

    270

    In the Dongting Lake region, there was an orphan named Chen Xueqi who had arranged to marry a woman from the Zou family. Their wedding was set for a specific date. However, her elder sister suddenly changed her mind and presented the woman as a concubine to a high-ranking official named Su. Chen Xueqi wept and pleaded with the authorities, but the officials feared the power of the official and could do nothing to help him. Chen Xueqi then filed a lawsuit against his sister-in-law, but the official protected his elder brother, and the case could not be resolved. Chen Xueqi was in a very difficult situation.

    One day, he met a man with a beard on the road and told him the reason for his distress, saying, "If you are usually moved by righteousness, can you help me with this?" The bearded man agreed and said, "However, it will require time to wait, so do not rush me." Chen Xueqi was deeply moved and wept.

    The bearded man left and sold himself as a carriage driver for a prominent person. The prominent person, impressed by his strength and diligence, trusted and loved him greatly, allowing him to enter and exit the inner chambers. Indeed, the woman became his third concubine. The bearded man found an opportunity to reveal his feelings to her, and she wept like rain, telling him about her state of being forced into concubinage and wishing to be treated as a noble person. The bearded man said, "Do not rush."

    271

    ”一日，显者夫人率群媵游天平山，显者不能禁。髯嘿贺曰：“计行矣！”于是密具舟河干。众妾登舆，髯舁第三舆，乃邹氏也。出门，绐其副迂道，疾行，至河干，谓女曰：“登舟！”舟遽开，帆疾如驶。群仆骇变，号呼来追。髯拳三人仆地，不能出声。徐去，则女舟已至陈门矣。学奇得室忻感，谓古押衙不是过也。髯谓学奇，亟宜鸣之官以得妻状。官始不直显者，至是称快，询知义由于髯，赐帛酒花彩以荣之。显者惭，杜门若不闻者。自是“义樵”名益著，年五十余矣。
    　　甲申，闯贼破京城，崇祯帝凶问至。或传于市中曰：“李自成坐却龙廷矣！”髯不信，历问三四人，言如一口。髯大愤曰：“吾生年七八岁时，即知皇帝姓朱，今李贼何为者耶？故君安往耶？何文武满朝，无一人出力救耶？吾年老，不能复为贼百姓也！”乃大呼天者三，投具区以死。死之日，义声振吴下云。

    Explanation

    271

    One day, the noblewoman led her entourage to visit Mount Tianping, and the nobleman could not stop her. The bearded man silently rejoiced and said, "The plan is in motion!" He then secretly prepared a boat by the riverbank. The concubines boarded the sedan chairs, and the bearded man carried the third one, which was Zou's. Upon leaving, he deceived her attendant into taking a detour, hurriedly went, reached the riverbank, and told the girl, "Board the boat!" The boat quickly set sail, its sails moving swiftly. The servants were shocked and changed their behavior, shouting and chasing after. The bearded man punched three of them to the ground, unable to make a sound. After a while, the girl's boat had already arrived at Chen's door. Xue Qi felt happy and grateful to have found a wife, saying that the ancient guard was not overdoing it. The bearded man told Xue Qi that he should immediately report to the authorities to get the marriage certificate. The official initially did not side with the nobleman, but now he was pleased, inquiring about the righteousness due to the bearded man, and rewarded him with silk, wine, flowers, and colorful decorations. The nobleman was ashamed and closed his door as if he had not heard anything. From then on, the name "Righteous Woodcutter" became more famous, and he was over fifty years old.

    In the year Jia Shen, the rebel Li Zicheng broke through the capital city, and the news of the death of Emperor Chongzhen came. Someone in the market said, "Li Zicheng has taken the throne of the dragon!" The bearded man did not believe it, asked several people, and they all said the same thing. The bearded man became very angry and said, "When I was seven or eight years old, I knew the emperor's surname was Zhu. Now, what is Li Zicheng doing? Where is the old emperor? Why are there no officials in the court who are willing to save him? I am old, and I cannot be a subject of the rebels anymore!" He cried out to heaven three times and jumped into the lake to die. On the day he died, his name for righteousness spread throughout Wu.

    272

    顾子日：义哉髯也！见义必为，矢志不屈，求之士人中，亦戛戛难之，况樵子乎？髯无姓名，吾师吴颂筠，曾为立传，传未悉。予又询之朱子僧臣，所言如此，良不妄矣。彼附势利、忘君亲者，观髯梗概，亦可以知所儆乎？
    　　[张山来曰：观剧忿怒杀人，所闻者非止一事。此樵奇处，在后数段，劫邹女尤见作用。至自投具区以死，真可谓得其所矣。] 赵希乾传　南丰甘表中素手授钞本
    　　赵希乾，南丰东门人，幼丧父，以织布为业。年十七，母抱病月余，日夜祈祷身代，不少愈。往问吉凶于日者，日者推测素验，言母命无生理。又往卜于市，占者复言不吉。希乾踟蹰不去，曰：“何以救母病？”占者恶其烦数，曰：“汝母病必不治，若欲求愈，无乃割心救之耶？”希乾归，侍母左右，见病益危笃。时日光斜射床席，形影孑立，寂寂旁无一人。希乾忽起去，笥中得薙发小刀，立于窗外，剖胸，深寸许，以手入取其心，不可得。

    Explanation

    272

    Conzi said: How righteous the bearded man is!
