He acts upon what is right, remains steadfast and unyielding, and it is difficult to find such qualities even among scholars, let alone among woodcutters! The bearded man had no name; my teacher Wu Songyin once wrote a biography of him, but the biography is not complete. I also consulted Master Zhu, the Buddhist monk, and he said the same, which is indeed true. Those who attach themselves to power and wealth and forget their loyalty to their ruler and family, by observing the bearded man's general character, can also know what to be warned against.

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: To witness someone killing in anger, such incidents are not uncommon. The remarkable aspects of this woodcutter are found in the following sections, especially in his resourcefulness in kidnapping Zou's daughter. To throw oneself into the lake and die, truly, this can be called finding one's place.]

    Zhaò Xīqián Biography
    From the handwritten copy by Gan Biāngzhōng of Nánfēng

    Zhaò Xīqián was from the eastern gate of Nánfēng. As a child, he lost his father and made a living by weaving cloth. At the age of seventeen, his mother fell ill for over a month. He prayed to God day and night, willing to take her place, but her condition did not improve. He went to ask a fortune-teller about the good or bad fortune, and the fortune-teller, who was usually accurate, said that his mother's life was in danger. He then went to the market to consult an oracle, and the diviner also said it was not auspicious. Xīqián hesitated and did not leave, asking, "What can I do to save my mother?" The diviner was annoyed by his persistence and said, "Your mother's illness is incurable. If you want to seek a cure, why not cut out your own heart to save her?" Xīqián returned home and stayed by his mother's side, seeing her condition worsen. At that time, the slanting sunlight shone on the bed, casting a solitary shadow. He was alone with no one around. Suddenly, Xīqián got up and went to the chest, where he found a small knife used for shaving his hair. He stood outside the window, cut open his chest, about an inch deep, and tried to reach in with his hand to get his heart, but he could not find it.

    273

    忽风声震飒，门户胥动，以为有人至。四顾周章，急取得肠，抽出，割数寸。盖人惊则心上忡，肠盘旋满胸腹云。希乾置肠于釜上，昏仆就室而卧。顷刻，母姑来视病，见釜上物，以为希乾股肉也，烹而进之母。再视希乾，则血淋漓心腹间，不能出声，始知希乾为割心矣。城邑喧然传其事，闻于令，令亲往视之，命内外医调治母子病。不数日，母病愈；旬日，希乾亦渐次进饮食。胸前肠出不得纳，每日子午间，粪滴沥下。月余后，希乾起无恙，终身矢从胸上出。

    Explanation

    273

    A sudden, intense wind sound shook the house, causing all doors and windows to move, leading everyone to believe someone had arrived. In confusion, Xi Gan hurriedly obtained his intestine, pulled it out, and cut it several inches. According to an old belief, when a person is startled, the heart becomes agitated, and the intestines coil up in the chest and abdomen. Xi Gan placed his intestine on the pot, fainted, and collapsed into the room to sleep. Shortly after, his mother and grandmother came to see him. They saw the object on the pot, thinking it was part of Xi Gan's leg, and cooked it for the mother. When they saw Xi Gan again, they noticed blood everywhere, and he could not speak, revealing that he had cut out his own heart. The story spread throughout the town, reaching the local official, who personally came to investigate. He ordered doctors inside and outside to treat the mother and son. Within a few days, the mother recovered; after ten days, Xi Gan gradually started eating again. His intestine protruded from his chest and could not be pushed back in, dripping feces at noon and midnight. After a month, Xi Gan fully recovered, but his bowels continued to protrude from his chest.

    274

    赵氏故宋裔，为南丰巨族。宗党以为奇孝，供赡其母子，而更教之读书。学使者侯峒曾闻其事，取充博士弟子员。崇祯壬午，以恩诏天下学选一人贡于成均。学使者吴石渠既考试毕，进诸生而告之曰：“百行以孝为先。赵希乾割心救母，不死，不可以寻常论。建武多才，校士衡文，希乾不应入选。今欲诸生让贡希乾，以示奖劝。”诸生咸顿首悦服。于是以希乾选补壬午恩贡。又三四年而有甲申、乙酉之变。希乾避乱山中，将母不遑，遂卖卜，逃走于四方，以养其母。又十余年，母寿八十余而卒。

    Explanation

    274

    Zhao, a descendant of the Song dynasty, was a prominent family in Nanfeng. His clan considered him extraordinarily filial, supporting his mother and son, and teaching him to read. The provincial examination commissioner Hou Dongzeng heard of his story and appointed him as a student in the Confucian academy. In the Renwu year of Chongzhen, by imperial decree, one student was selected from each province to be presented to the Imperial Academy. After the examination, the provincial examination commissioner Wu Shiqu summoned the students and told them, "Among all virtues, filial piety is the foremost. Zhao Xiqian cut out his heart to save his mother and survived, which cannot be judged by ordinary standards. Jianwu has many talented people, and the scholars' works are evaluated, but Zhao Xiqian should not have been selected. Now I want you all to yield your nomination to Zhao Xiqian to show encouragement." All the students bowed in agreement and were pleased. Thus, Zhao Xiqian was selected to fill the vacancy for the Renwu year's imperial scholar. Three to four years later, the events of Jiashen and Yiyu occurred. Zhao Xiqian fled to the mountains to avoid the chaos, unable to take care of his mother, so he sold divination services and wandered around to support her. After more than ten years, his mother lived to be over eighty and passed away.

    275

    予自幼时，常见希乾过先君谈，饮食起居如常人，面黎黝，高准方耳，睛光满眸子，欣然而长，多浑朴之风。与之立久，胸间时闻秽气。予年十岁，先君请希乾入书室，命表肃揖再拜，求解衣开胸视之。两乳正中间，肠突出寸许，色鲜红如血；以丝带系竹筒悬于颈，乘其肠粪出，洗换竹筒，日必再三换，常时滴黄水不绝；盖已三十余年。自是希乾少家居，母死未七年，而希乾亦卒，年六十一。
    　　甘表日：朝廷不旌毁伤愚孝，尚矣！然希乾一念之诚，若有以通天地、格神鬼也，岂不可嘉哉？汤公惕庵最恶言希乾事，予则以为应出特典，一加旌赏。盖事不可法而可传，使知孝行所感，虽剖胸断肠而不死，岂非天之所以旌之耶？天旌之，谁能不旌之？然旌而不传，不若不旌而传也。安得龙门之书以施于后世哉？呜呼，古今忠孝之士，非愚不能成。而世之身没而名不传者，又何多也？悲夫！

    Explanation

    275

    From my early years, I often saw Xi Gan visit my father to talk. He lived a normal life, eating, drinking, and sleeping like any other person. His face was dark and sallow, with a high nose and square ears. His eyes were bright and full of light, and he was cheerful and tall, with a simple and unpretentious demeanor. When I stood near him for a long time, I often smelled foul air coming from his chest. When I was ten years old, my father invited Xi Gan into the study and asked me to bow twice, then asked him to take off his clothes and open his chest for inspection. In the middle of his two breasts, his intestines protruded about an inch, the color being fresh red like blood. He used a silk ribbon to tie a bamboo tube to his neck, allowing his intestines to protrude and be washed and changed daily, often several times. The bamboo tube continuously dripped yellow water. This condition had lasted for over thirty years. Since then, Xi Gan rarely stayed at home. Within seven years after his mother's death, Xi Gan also passed away, at the age of sixty-one.

    Gan Biao said: The court does not commend foolish filial piety that causes harm, indeed! However, Xi Gan's sincere devotion, as if it could communicate with heaven and spirits, is surely praiseworthy. Master Tang Tixin detested mentioning Xi Gan's story, but I believe that a special decree should be issued to commend him. While the act itself may not be imitated, it can be recorded, so that people know that filial piety can move heaven, even if it means cutting open one's chest and intestines and not dying. Is this not how heaven commends him? If heaven commends him, who can fail to commend him? However, if we commend him but do not record his story, it is better not to commend him at all. How can we find a Lunyu (Book of Rites) to pass on to future generations? Alas, throughout history, those who have achieved loyalty and filial piety were not foolish. Yet, how many people have died without their names being recorded in the world? How sad!

    276

    [张山来曰：予友王不庵曾为予言孝子事，惜属口述，不获载之简鳊。今甘子中素以斯传见示，乃知事之度越寻常者，终不能泯其姓字也。] 万夫雄打虎传　江宁张惣南村手授钞本
    　　泾川有万姓字夫雄者，少负膂力，以拳勇称，初亦未尝事田猎也。一日，与夙所莫逆尔汝昆季范姓友，早行深山中。忽林莽出巨虎，搏范以去。范号曰：“万夫雄救我！救我！”万亦茫然不知所措，遂撼大树拔之，怒持树往追。经里许，震天一呼，虎为逡巡退步者三，范得以脱。因梃击虎，中其项。虎负狰狞欲迎斗，然项痛，竟不能举。万乘势一再击之，虎毙矣。母虎暨虎子相寻至。万度不能中止，且却且前，又奋鼓生平之勇，纵送格扑，而二虎复相继而毙于其手。

    Explanation

    276

    张山来说：我的朋友王不庵曾经告诉我一个关于孝子的故事，可惜只是口头讲述，没能记录在简编上。如今甘子中一直向我展示这个传记，我才明白，那些超越寻常的事情，终究不会抹去那些人的名字。
    《万夫雄打虎传》　江宁张惣南村手授钞本
    泾川有一个姓万、字夫雄的人，年轻时力大无穷，以拳勇闻名，起初并不曾从事田猎。一天，他与一位姓范的、从小亲密无间的兄弟一起，清晨进入深山。突然，林莽中跳出一只大虎，扑向范姓朋友并将其带走。范姓朋友喊道：“万夫雄救我！救我！”万夫雄也茫然不知所措，于是摇动大树将其拔起，愤怒地拿着树追了上去。跑了大约一里路，他发出震天的呼喊，老虎三次后退，范姓朋友得以逃脱。于是万夫雄用棍棒击打老虎，击中了它的颈部。老虎带着狰狞的面孔想要迎战，但脖子疼痛，最终无法行动。万夫雄趁势再次击打，老虎倒地而死。母虎和虎崽随后赶到。万夫雄估计无法中途停止，一边后退一边前进，又鼓起平生的勇气，奋力搏斗，最终两只老虎相继被他打死。

    277

    嗟乎！万夫雄一乡野鄙人耳，素不识《诗》《书》为何物，亦不识交道为何事，而仓卒间不忍负异姓兄弟之意，卒毙三虎以救其友，其义岂不甚伟？万夫雄亦诚烈丈夫哉！余尝见世之聚首而处者，交同手足之亲，谊比金石之固，设有缓急，即蜂虿微毒，不致贻祸杀人，当其纷纷未定之时，虽夙昔周旋，密迩徒辈，靡不潜迹匿形，鸟飞云散，悄然而不一顾焉。其视万夫雄为何如也？
    　　或云：“一人而毙三虎，颇似不经，殆属乌有子虚之谈。”噫！诚有之矣！家九宣从泾川来，为余述其事最奇。亦曾亲见其人，短小精悍。与之语，意气慷慨，须眉状貌，殊磊砢不凡，飞扬跋扈，犹可想望其打虎时英风至今飒飒云。盖义愤所激，至勇生焉；即万亦不自知其何以至此也。从古忠孝节义，蹈水赴火，为人之所不能为，并为人之所不敢为，往往以蚩愚诚朴而得之。万夫雄有焉。

    Explanation

    277

    Alas! Wanfu Xiong, a humble man of the countryside, was always ignorant of the Shijing and Shujing and did not understand the principles of friendship. Yet, in a moment of crisis, he could not bear to betray the intention of his non-blood brothers, and ultimately killed three tigers to save his friend. Is this not a great act of righteousness? Wanfu Xiong is indeed a truly heroic man! I have often seen people who gather together, treating each other as close as brothers, with bonds as solid as gold and stone. If there is an emergency, even a minor sting like that of a bee or scorpion would not lead to causing harm or death. When things are in chaos and have not yet been settled, even those who have long been close and familiar with each other, who are near and dear to them, will not hesitate to hide their tracks and disappear like birds flying away in the clouds, quietly and without a second glance. How do they compare to Wanfu Xiong?

    Some say, "One person killing three tigers seems quite unbelievable, almost like a tale of nonexistent events." Alas! It is indeed true! Jia Jiuxuan came from Jingchuan and told me the story, which was the most extraordinary. I have also personally seen the man, who was short but robust. When I spoke with him, he was full of spirit and generosity. His appearance and demeanor were truly remarkable, with a bold and unrestrained manner that still evokes the heroic spirit of when he was fighting the tigers, as if it were still whistling in the wind today. It was righteous indignation that inspired such bravery; even Wanfu Xiong himself did not know how he came to this point. From ancient times, loyalty, filial piety, righteousness, and integrity have led people to brave water and fire, to do what others cannot do and what others dare not do. Often, these virtues are found in simple and honest people. Wanfu Xiong is one such person.

    278

    南村野史曰：余友苍略氏，闻其事而异之，太息曰：“士亦视所托身为贵耳！得交万夫雄，其人虽陷入虎口，猛虎不能害也。甚矣，人固不可无义烈男子以为之友哉！”
    　　[张山来曰：孔子论宁武子，谓其“愚不可及”。匪独愚忠愚孝，凡事之度越寻常者，大抵多近于愚耳。一结最妙。
    　　又曰：今之义气满洲，类能生搏虎豹。使万夫雄而在，当必与干城之选矣。]
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷九 剑侠传　济南王士祯阮亭渔洋文略
    　　新城令崔懋，以康熙戊辰往济南。至章丘西之新店，遇一妇人，可三十余，高髻如宫妆。髻上加毡笠，锦衣弓鞋，结束为急装，腰剑。骑黑卫，极神骏。妇人神采四射，其行甚驶。试问何人，停骑漫应曰：“不知何许人。”“将往何处？”又漫应曰：“去处去。”顷刻东逝，疾若飞隼。崔云：“惜赴郡匆匆，未暇蹑其踪迹，疑剑侠也。”从侄鹓因述莱阳王生言：

    Explanation

    278

    The Southern Village Historian said: My friend, Mr. Canglue, heard this story and found it extraordinary. He sighed and said, "Scholars also consider the company they keep as valuable! To befriend a hero among a thousand men, even if that person falls into the tiger's mouth, the fierce tiger cannot harm him. Indeed, people cannot do without righteous and brave men as friends!"

    [Commentary by Zhang Shanlai: Confucius discussed Ning Wuzi, calling him 'His foolishness is beyond reach.' Not only was his loyalty and filial piety considered foolish, but in general, things that surpass the ordinary are often close to foolishness. The ending is the most wonderful.
    Also said: Today's righteous and brave Manchus can often live and capture tigers and leopards. If the hero among a thousand men were present, he would surely be chosen for the city wall.]

    New Records of the Southern Village, Volume 9, Swordsmen and Heroic Tales, by Wang Shizhen of Jinan, also known as Ruanting and Yuyang, Brief Collection of Writings.

    The magistrate of Xincheng, Cui Mao, traveled to Jinan during the year of the Earth Dragon in the reign of Emperor Kangxi. On his way, he reached Xin Dian in the west of Zhangqiu, where he encountered a woman around thirty years old. She had a high hairstyle like a palace lady, wore a felt hat on her hair, and was dressed in brocade clothes and bow shoes, indicating she was ready for action. She carried a sword at her waist and rode a black horse, which was extremely spirited. The woman radiated spirit and charm, and her ride was very fast. Cui tried to ask her who she was, and she paused, carelessly responding, "I don't know where I am from." When he asked where she was going, she again carelessly replied, "To wherever I need to go." In an instant, she disappeared eastward, as swift as a falcon. Cui said, "I regret that my journey to the county was too hurried, and I didn't have time to follow her trail, but I suspect she might be a swordswoman." His nephew, Yuan, then recounted a story told by Wang of Laizhou.

    279

    顺治初，其县役某，解官银数千两赴济南，以木夹函之。晚将宿逆旅，主人辞焉，且言“镇西北里许，有尼庵，凡有行橐者，皆往投宿”。因导之往。方入旅店时，门外有男子著红帩头，状貌甚狞。至尼庵，入门，有廨三间，东向，床榻甚设。北为观音大士殿，殿侧有小门，扃焉。叩门久之，有老妪出应，告以故，妪云：“但宿西廨无妨。”久之，持硃封鐍山门而入。役相戒勿寝，明灯烛，手弓刀以待曙。至三更，大风骤作，山门砉然而辟。方愕然相顾，倏闻呼门声甚厉。众急持械，谋拒之，廨门已启，视之，即红帩头人也。徒手握束香掷地，众皆仆。比天晓，始苏，银已亡矣。

    Explanation

    279

    In the early years of the Shunzhi reign, a certain county official was tasked with escorting several thousand taels of government silver to Jinan. He secured the silver in a wooden case. As evening approached, he planned to stay at an inn, but the innkeeper declined his request, suggesting instead, "About a mile northwest of the town, there is a Buddhist nunnery where anyone carrying a bag or suitcase stays for the night." The innkeeper then led him there. As they were entering the inn, a man outside wearing a red headband, with a fierce appearance, caught their attention. Upon reaching the nunnery, they entered through the main gate, which led to three offices facing east, equipped with beds and mats. To the north stood the Guanyin Hall, and beside it was a small locked door. After knocking for a long time, an elderly woman came out and, upon hearing their request, said, "You can stay in the western office without any problem." After some time, she unlocked the gate and allowed them to enter. The officials warned each other not to sleep, lit lamps, and held bows and knives to wait for dawn. At the third watch of the night, a sudden strong wind caused the main gate to open with a sharp sound. Just as they were startled and looking at each other, they suddenly heard a loud knocking on the door. Everyone quickly grabbed their weapons, planning to resist, but by the time they opened the office door, they saw it was the man with the red headband. He, unarmed, threw a bundle of incense on the ground, and everyone fell down. By the time morning came, they had all regained consciousness, but the silver had already been stolen.

    280

    急往市询逆旅主人，主人曰：“此人时游市上，无敢谁何者；唯投尼庵客，辄无恙。今当往愬耳。然尼异人，须吾自往求之。”至则妪出问故，曰：“非为夜失官银事耶？”曰：“然！”入白。顷之尼出，妪挟蒲团敷坐，逆旅主人跪白前事。尼笑曰：“此奴敢来此作狡狯，罪合死。吾当为一决！”顾妪入，率一黑卫出，取剑臂之，跨卫向南山径去，其行如飞，倏忽不见。市人集观者数百人。移时，尼徒步手人头，驱卫返，驴背负木夹函数千金，殊无所苦。入门，呼役曰：“来！视汝木夹，官封如故乎？”验之良是。掷人头地上，曰：“视此贼不错杀却否？”众聚观，果红帩头人也。罗拜谢去。比东归，再往访之，庵已空无人矣。
    　　尼高髻盛装，衣锦绮，行缠罗袜，年十八九好女子也。市人云：“尼三四年前，挟妪俱来，不知何许人。常有恶少夜入其室，腰斩掷垣外，自是无敢犯者。”

    Explanation

    280

    Hasten to the market to inquire of the innkeeper. The innkeeper said, "This person often roams the market, and no one dares to question him; only when he visits the nunnery does he remain unharmed. Now I should go to report this. However, this nun is extraordinary, and I must go myself to seek her out." Upon arriving, the old woman came out and asked the reason. She said, "Is it not about the loss of official silver at night?" "Yes!" the innkeeper replied, and went in to inform. Soon, the nun emerged. The old woman laid out a mat for her to sit, and the innkeeper knelt and reported the previous events. The nun smiled and said, "This slave dared to come here and act deceitfully; his crime deserves death. I shall deal with him!" Turning to the old woman, she led out a black-clad guard, took a sword and placed it on her arm, mounted the guard, and headed towards the South Mountain trail. Her movement was as swift as flying, and she vanished in a flash. Hundreds of people gathered in the market to watch. After a while, the nun returned on foot, holding a human head, driving the guard back, and carrying a wooden box containing several thousand gold coins on a donkey's back, showing no signs of fatigue. Upon entering, she called out to the servants, "Come! Check your wooden box; is the official seal still intact?" They verified that it was indeed so. She threw the human head to the ground and said, "Look at this thief; was he not killed correctly?" The crowd gathered to watch, and it was indeed the man with a red bandanna. They prostrated themselves and thanked her before leaving. By the time I returned eastward, I went to visit again, but the nunnery was empty and no one was there.

    The nun wore a high hairstyle and elaborate attire, dressed in brocade and silk, with her feet wrapped in fine gauze socks, and she was a beautiful woman in her late teens. The people in the market said, "The nun came here with the old woman three or four years ago, and no one knows where she is from. Often, ruffians would sneak into her room at night, only to be beheaded and thrown over the wall; since then, no one dares to offend her."

    281

    某中丞巡抚上江，一日遣吏赍金数千赴京师，途宿古庙中，扃鐍甚固。晨起，已失金所在，而门钥宛然。怪之，归以告中丞，中丞怒，亟责偿官。吏告曰：“偿固不敢辞，但事甚疑怪，请予假一月，往踪迹之。愿以妻子为质。”中丞许之。
    　　比至失金处，询访久之，无所见；将归矣，忽于市中遇瞽叟，胸悬一牌云：“善决大疑。”漫问之，叟忽曰：“君失金多少？”曰：“若干。”叟曰：“我稍知踪迹。可觅露车乘我，君第随往，冀可得也。”如其言，初行一日，有人烟村落；次日入深山行，不知几百里，无复村疃；至三日，逾亭午，抵一大市镇。叟曰：“至矣！君但入，当自得消息。”不得已，第从其言。比入市，则肩摩毂击，万瓦鳞次。忽一人来问曰：“君非此间人，奚至此？”告以故，与俱至市口，觅瞽叟，已失所在。

    Explanation

    281

    An anonymous censor-general governed the upper Yangtze region. One day, he dispatched an official to carry thousands of gold pieces to the capital. The official stayed overnight in an ancient temple, where the doors and locks were secured very tightly. In the morning, he found the gold had already disappeared, yet the lock remained unchanged. Surprised, he returned to report to the censor-general, who was furious and immediately demanded compensation from the official. The official replied, "Compensation is certainly not something I can refuse, but the matter is very suspicious and strange. Please grant me a month's leave to trace the trail. I will use my wife and children as collateral." The censor-general agreed.

    By the time he reached the place where the gold was lost, he inquired for a long time but saw nothing. As he was about to return, he suddenly met a blind old man in the market, who had a sign hanging around his chest that read, "Good at solving great doubts." He asked the old man casually, who suddenly said, "How much gold did you lose?" The official replied, "A certain amount." The old man said, "I slightly know the trail. You can find a cart and ride with me. Just follow me, and you may find it." The official followed his words. They traveled for one day, passing through a village with human habitation; on the second day, they entered the deep mountains, traveling an unknown distance of several hundred miles, with no more villages in sight. By the third day, past midday, they arrived at a large market town. The old man said, "We have arrived! Just go in, and you will get the message." The official had no choice but to follow his words. By the time he entered the market, it was crowded with people and carts, and the buildings were densely packed. Suddenly, someone came and asked, "You are not from here, why are you here?" The official told him the reason, and they went together to the market entrance. When they sought the blind old man, he was already gone.

    282

    乃与曲折行数街，抵一大宅，如王公之居。历阶及堂，寂无人，戒令少待。顷之，传呼令入，至后堂，堂中唯设一榻，有伟男子科跣坐其上，发长及骭，童子数人，执扇拂左右侍。拜跪讫，男子询来意，具对。男子颐指语童子曰：“可将来。”即有少年数辈，扛金至，封识宛然，曰：“宁欲得金乎？”吏叩头曰：“幸甚，不敢请也。”男子曰：“乍来此，且好安息。”即有人引至一院，扃门而去。餽之食，极丰腆。是夜，月明如昼，启后户，视之，见粉壁上累累有物，审视之，皆人耳鼻也。大惊，然无隙可逸去。彷徨达晚，前人忽来传呼，复至后堂，男子科跣坐如初，谓曰：“金不可得矣！然当予子一纸书。”辄据案作书，掷之，挥出。前人复导至市口，惝恍疑梦中，急觅路归。

    Explanation

    282

    Then, they walked through several winding streets and arrived at a large mansion, like the residence of a prince or nobleman. Climbing the stairs and entering the hall, they found it quiet and empty. They were instructed to wait a moment. Soon after, they were called to enter the back hall, where only one mat was set up. There sat a tall and handsome man, barefoot, with long hair reaching his shins. Several young boys stood on either side, holding fans and fanning him. After bowing and kneeling, the man asked about their purpose, and they answered in detail. The man nodded and told the young boys, "Bring it here." Then, several young men carried gold, sealed and marked exactly as it was, and said, "Do you want to get the gold?" The official knelt and bowed, saying, "Very fortunate, dare not ask." The man said, "Just arrived here, please rest well." Then, someone led them to a courtyard, locked the door, and left. They were served very abundant and rich food. That night, the moonlight was as bright as day. Opening the back door and looking out, they saw objects piled up on the plastered wall. Examining them closely, they found they were all human ears and noses. Greatly shocked, but finding no opportunity to escape, they wandered until late. The previous person suddenly came and called them, leading them back to the back hall. The man sat barefoot as before and said, "The gold cannot be obtained! But I will give you a piece of paper." He then wrote a letter, threw it, and dismissed them. The previous person led them to the market entrance, feeling dazed and like in a dream, and they hastily found a way to return.

    283

    见中丞，历述前事。叱其妄。出书呈之，中丞启缄，忽色变而入。移时，传令吏归舍，释妻子，豁其赔偿。吏大喜过望。久之，乃知书中大略斥中丞贪纵，谓勿责吏偿金，否则某月日夫人夜三更睡觉，发截三寸，宁忘之乎？问之夫人，良然，始知其剑侠也。日照李洗马应廌云。
    　　[张山来曰：予尝遇中山狼，恨今世无剑侠，一往愬之。读此乃知尚有异人，第不识于我有缘否也。] 皇华纪闻　新城王士祯阮亭本书
    　　天顺间，恩县人赵云，性至孝。母刘病笃，闻怀庆府济源庙神有灵药，诚求可得，云往求之。越二日，水中涌出一绢囊，内盛绛桃花片，约二升许。持归煎汤奉母，疾果愈。其余愈疾又十余人。

    Explanation

    283

    The official presented himself before the Censor-in-Chief and recounted the previous events in detail. The Censor-in-Chief rebuked him for being delusional. The official then presented a written document, which the Censor-in-Chief opened, and suddenly his face changed as he went inside. After some time, he ordered the official to return home, release his wife and children, and exempt him from compensation. The official was exceedingly delighted. After some time, it was understood that the book largely criticized the Censor-in-Chief's greed and tyranny, stating that the official should not be held responsible for paying the gold, otherwise, on a certain day, the lady would sleep at midnight, and her hair would be cut three inches, could one forget it? When asked the lady, she confirmed it, and then it was known that he was a swordswoman. According to Li Xima of Rizhao, who served as a scholar-official.

    [Ouyang Shanlai said: I once encountered a wolf from Zhongshan, regretting that there are no swordswomen in this age, and thus went to complain. Reading this, I knew there were still extraordinary people, but I don't know if they have any connection with me.] Records of Travels and Observations by Wang Shizhen, also known as Ruan Ting, from Xincheng.

    During the Tian Shun period, a man named Zhao Yun from En County was extremely filial. His mother Liu was seriously ill, and he heard that the god at the temple in Jiyuan, Huaiqing Prefecture, had miraculous medicine. If one sincerely sought it, it could be obtained. Zhao Yun went to seek it. Two days later, a silk bag emerged from the water, inside of which was a handful of red peach blossom petals, about two sheng in quantity. He took it home, boiled it into a decoction, and served it to his mother, and the illness indeed healed. It also cured the illnesses of more than ten others.

    284

    白马营，在恩县西十五里，夏秋之际，清晨辄现城郭人物，林木郁葱，日出乃不见。茌平马令村亦有此异。盖山市、海市之属，陆地亦有之。
    　　赖塔拉把土鲁，满洲人，素以勇称。尝从征浙闽。一日浴于溪，水底有物，槎枒如古木，因呼侪辈缚以绳，共引出之，则一龙首，须鬣宛然，缚者乃其角。众皆惊走。赖神色不变，徐入水手解其缚。少顷，雷雨晦冥，龙腾空而去。众皆无恙。人更称为“缚龙把土鲁”。把土鲁，勇也。元时把土鲁必出上赐，本朝亦然。
    　　张大悲，合肥人，居邑之香炉岩。好仙术，常画地为限，牛不能出。恒作泥丸食之，坐卧处往往有云气，后不知所终。
    　　朝城陈给事赞化，崇祯间为桐城令。偶有餽蛋者，其一有五色光，令家鸡翼之。俄卵破，得一小白凤。不数日，寝大，时去时来。其伏卵之鸡，重至三十斤，毛变五色，久之同翔去。

    Explanation

    284

    White Horse Camp is located fifteen li west of En County. During the summer and autumn seasons, in the early morning, a mirage often appears, showing the city walls, people, and lush forests, but it disappears at sunrise. The same phenomenon occurs in Chiping Ma Ling Village. This is a type of mirage, similar to those seen in mountains and seas, and it can also occur on land.

    Lai Tala Ba Tulu, a Manchu person, was famous for his bravery. He once participated in military campaigns in Zhejiang and Fujian. One day, while bathing in a stream, he saw something in the water that resembled ancient trees with twisted branches. He called his companions to help tie it with ropes and pull it out. It turned out to be a dragon's head, with its mane clearly visible, and the ropes were tied around its horns. Everyone was shocked and ran away, but Lai Tala Ba Tulu remained calm. He slowly entered the water and untied the ropes. Soon, a thunderstorm with dark skies occurred, and the dragon soared into the sky and departed. Everyone was unharmed. People began to call him "Dragon-Binder Ba Tulu." The title "Ba Tulu" refers to bravery. During the Yuan dynasty, this title was granted by the emperor, and it was also practiced in this dynasty.

    Zhang Dabi, a person from Hefei, lived in Xianglu Rock. He was fond of magical arts and often drew a boundary on the ground, which cattle could not cross. He frequently ate small clay balls, and mist often appeared around his sitting and sleeping areas. Later, his whereabouts became unknown.

    Chen Zan, a censor during the Chongzhen period, served as the magistrate of Tongcheng. Once, someone gave him eggs, one of which had multicolored light, and his chickens were attracted to it. Soon, the egg hatched, revealing a small white phoenix. Within a few days, the phoenix grew larger and came and went. The chicken that hatched the egg grew as heavy as thirty jin, and its feathers turned multicolored. After a while, they flew away together.

    285

    王文正，桐城人，七岁得道书，能役鬼神。后祷雨皖城，有道人亦祷雨池口。池口云起，文正招云过皖。道人曰：“皖有异人。”即棹片席渡江访之，文正亦浮磨江中迎之。咨论竟日。临别，道人以三指附文正背，有顷背痛，则有三铜钉入骨。文正急用瓮自覆，围火炼之。戒家人曰：“七日勿启，可活。”至五日，家人不能待，试启之，钉已出三寸许。文正叹曰：“命也！”遂死。
    　　何公冕，潜山人，少遇异人，授符箓二卷，能役鬼神。初置田于乱墩山，硗确无水。公冕每取手巾沥水，町畦盈溢。会大旱，郡守遣役檄呼之。公冕笑曰：“吾非可檄者。但汝往来烈日良苦，吾书符汝掌中，当得片云覆头，可固握之。”使至，如其言。守怒，固令开视，则疾风雷电骤作。乃大惊，礼致之。尝行路迷津，问芸者，不答。公冕取柳叶布田，尽化为鱼。芸者竞取之，至禾皆被践踏，及登岸视之，乃柳叶耳。

    Explanation

    285

    Wang Wenzheng, a native of Tongcheng, obtained Daoist scriptures at the age of seven and was able to command ghosts and spirits. Later, when praying for rain in Wancheng, another Daoist was also praying for rain in Chi Kou. As clouds gathered over Chi Kou, Wang Wenzheng summoned them to pass over Wancheng. The Daoist remarked, "There is an extraordinary person in Wancheng." Immediately, he rowed a small boat across the river to visit Wang Wenzheng, who also floated on the Mo River to greet him. They consulted and discussed matters all day. Before parting, the Daoist placed three fingers on the back of Wang Wenzheng. Soon after, Wang Wenzheng felt pain in his back, and three copper nails had pierced his bones. In a hurry, Wang Wenzheng covered himself with a jar and surrounded it with fire to refine it. He instructed his family, "Do not open it for seven days, and you will be saved." By the fifth day, unable to wait, his family tried to open it, and the nails had already emerged about three inches. Wang Wenzheng sighed, "It is fate!" and thus died.

    He Gongmian, a native of Qianshan, met an extraordinary person in his youth who bestowed upon him two scrolls of talismans, enabling him to command ghosts and spirits. Initially, he placed fields in Luan Dun Mountain, which was barren and lacked water. Gongmian would drip water from a handkerchief, and the fields would overflow. During a severe drought, the local governor sent a messenger to summon him. Gongmian smiled and said, "I am not someone who can be summoned. However, your journey under the scorching sun is quite arduous. I will write a talisman in your palm, and you will receive a piece of cloud to cover your head, which you can hold firmly." The messenger arrived, and as instructed, did so. The governor, angered, insisted on seeing the result, and suddenly, a fierce wind, lightning, and thunder erupted. The governor was greatly alarmed and treated Gongmian with respect. Once, while traveling and lost, he asked a gardener for directions, but received no answer. Gongmian took willow leaves and spread them over the fields, which transformed into fish. The gardeners competed to take the fish, trampling the crops in the process. When they reached the shore, they realized it was just willow leaves.

    286

    崇祯癸未，潜山县溪河中，结冰如钱形，上有古篆文四，人莫辨之。
    　　南华寺六祖钵，非金非石。魏庄渠督学广东，遍毁佛寺。至曹溪，索钵掷地，碎之为二，每片各有一字，视之，乃“委鬼”也。庄渠异之，寺因得不毁。
    　　崇祯中，有彭举人某，病中梦至一官府，其神冠冕坐堂皇，状如王者。闻胥吏传呼魏校一案。须臾，有一官人，峨冠盛服而入。其神问：“何以毁曹溪钵？”答言：“吾为孔子之徒，官督学校，在广东所毁淫祠几千百所，岂但一钵？”神云：“闻钵破中有魏字，如此神异，乌可以为异端而毁之？”答言：“魏是予姓，既数已前定，虽欲不毁其可得耶？”神语塞，揖之而出。彭病痊，为人言如此。

    Explanation

    286

    In the year of Chongzhen's reign, during the Guiwei year, ice formed in the river of Qianshan County, shaped like coins, with four ancient seal script characters on it, none of which could be identified.

    The Sixth Patriarch's Bowl at Nanhu Temple was neither made of metal nor stone. Wei Zhuangqu, who supervised education in Guangdong, destroyed numerous Buddhist temples. Upon reaching Caohexi, he ordered the bowl to be thrown to the ground, and it broke into two pieces, each with one character, which turned out to be "Weigui." Wei was surprised by this, and the temple was thus spared from destruction.

    During the reign of Chongzhen, there was a certain Mr. Peng, a juren, who while sick, dreamed of arriving at an official hall. The deity was crowned and sat on the main hall, looking like a king. He heard the scribes call out the case of Wei. Soon after, an official entered in grand attire. The deity asked, "Why did you destroy the bowl at Caohexi?" The official replied, "I am a disciple of Confucius, an official overseeing education, and in Guangdong, I destroyed thousands of places of worship. It was not just one bowl." The deity said, "I heard that the broken bowl contained the character 'Wei,' which is so mysterious. How could you destroy it as heresy?" The official responded, "Wei is my surname. Fate had already been decided. Even if I wanted not to destroy it, how could I?" The deity was speechless, bowed, and left. Mr. Peng recovered from his illness and told others about this.

    287

    林癸午，不知何许人。年十余，投阳江北贯中为人牧竖。每出牧，以箫管一枚自随。牛有逸者，取箫画地，牛不敢出。晚归，辄束箫高篁中。篁俯地受寄，若有神物伺之者。河畔一巨石，形如犬，癸午每坐啸其上。忽一日谓其徒曰：“吾当以来日上升。”明日往视，与石俱不见。事在万历初年。
    　　崇祯丙子秋，广州城东二十里北亭洲田间有雷出地，奋而成穴。耕者梁某投以石，空空有声。内一雄鸡其中，逾夜鸡鸣无恙。乃发之，有金人如翁仲者数枚，各重十五、六斤。有二金像，冕而坐者，笄翟如后妃者，各重五、六十斤。地皆金蚕珠贝，旁有镜一，光烛穴中；宝砚一，砚池中有玉鱼，能游泳；他异物不可指识者甚众。梁携归，光动四邻。邻人觉而争往，遂白之官。有司亲临发之，隧道如城，高五尺余，深三丈，中有碑，乃伪汉刘龑塚也。文曰：“维大有十五年，岁次壬寅，四月甲寅朔廿四日丁丑，高祖天皇大帝崩于正寝。

    Explanation

    287

    Lin Guiwu, whose origin is unknown. At around ten years old, he was sent to Yangjiang and North Guaon to work as a herdsboy. Whenever he went out to tend the cattle, he would carry a flute with him. If a cow strayed, he would take the flute and draw on the ground, and the cow would not dare to leave. When he returned at night, he always tied the flute to a tall bamboo. The bamboo would bend to the ground and receive the flute, as if there were a divine entity watching over it. There was a large stone by the river, shaped like a dog, and Lin Guiwu would often sit on top of it and play his flute. One day, he told his disciples, "I will ascend the next day." The next day, when they went to look for him, both he and the stone had disappeared. This event occurred in the early years of the Wanli era.

    In the autumn of the third year of the Chongzhen era, during a thunderstorm in the fields twenty li east of Guangzhou City, a hole was formed as the thunder struck the ground. A farmer named Liang threw a stone into the hole, and it made an empty sound. Inside the hole was a rooster, which crowed through the night without harm. When they investigated further, they found several golden figures like stone statues, each weighing about fifteen to sixteen pounds. There were also two golden statues, one wearing a crown and sitting, and another wearing a headdress and looking like a consort, each weighing about fifty to sixty pounds. The ground was covered with golden silkworms, pearls, and shells, and there was a mirror nearby, its light illuminating the hole. There was also a precious inkstone, with a jade fish in the inkwell that could swim. Many other strange objects that could not be identified were also found. Liang took these items back to his village, and the light illuminated the surrounding area. The neighbors noticed and rushed to see, and they reported the discovery to the authorities. The officials personally investigated the site, and they found a tunnel like a city, five feet high and three meters deep. Inside the tunnel was a stele, which turned out to be the tomb of Liu Yan of the false Han dynasty. The inscription read: "In the fifteenth year of Dàyǒu, in the year Renyin, on the twenty-fourth day of the month of Jiachen, the High Ancestor, Emperor Tianhuang, died in his main hall."

    288

    粤光天五年，五月癸未朔十四日丙申，迁神于康陵，礼也。”文多阙，不尽载。“翰林学士知制诰正议大夫尚书右丞赐紫金鱼袋臣卢应初撰并书。”按《五国故事》，龑天福壬寅岁四月，避暑甘泉宫，未几殂。《通鉴》及《十国春秋》皆作三月。据碑当以《五国故事》为正。《十国春秋》又云：“康陵在兴王府城东二十里之漫山，陵中以铁锢之，坚不可启。”光天乃龑子玢年号。玢立仅二年，为其弟晟所弑，即改光天二年为应乾元年。按光天无五年，《十国春秋》称殇帝光天元年八月，葬天皇大帝于康陵，与碑皆不合。又考伪汉诸臣列传，止有卢膺仕龑为工部侍郎，才藻俊茂，晟时拜中书侍郎同平章事，无应初名。识之以俟博雅者考焉。

    Explanation

    288

    In the fifth year of the Guangtian era, on the fourteenth day of the month of May,癸未朔 (initial day of the month),丙申 (fourteenth day), the spirit was relocated to the Kangling Tomb, in accordance with ritual. The text contains many omissions and is not fully recorded. "The inscription and calligraphy were composed and written by Minister of Works, Chief Censor, and Right Chancellor, Lu Yingchu, who was granted the purple gold fish bag." According to The Five Kingdoms' Stories, in the year 天福壬寅 (renyin) of the fourth month, Emperor Pang avoided the heat in the summer palace, and soon after, he died. Zizhi Tongjian and The Ten Kingdoms' Spring and Autumn Annals both record this event as occurring in March. Based on the inscription, the date from The Five Kingdoms' Stories should be considered correct. The Ten Kingdoms' Spring and Autumn Annals also states: "The Kangling Tomb is located twenty miles east of Xingfu City on Man Mountain, and the tomb is sealed with iron, making it extremely difficult to open." The era name Guangtian was used by Emperor Bing, son of Emperor Pang. Emperor Bing reigned for only two years before being assassinated by his brother Sheng, who then changed the second year of Guangtian to the first year of Yingqian. However, there is no fifth year of Guangtian, and The Ten Kingdoms' Spring and Autumn Annals records that in August of the first year of Guangtian, Emperor Tianhuang was buried in the Kangling Tomb, which does not match the inscription. Additionally, examining the biographies of officials of the Southern Han dynasty, there is only mention of Lu Ying, who served as Minister of Works under Emperor Pang and was known for his literary talent. During Emperor Sheng's reign, he was appointed as Chancellor and Grand Secretary. There is no mention of Lu Yingchu. This is noted here for the consideration of scholars.

    289

    《澹归禅师集?六和尚小传》云：吴震崆侍御，小字六和尚。髫时读书灯下，水中盂内跃出一僧，长三寸许，绕案而行，且言。震崆惊问之，曰：“吾能知人终身，亦知人前世。”震崆意稍定，曰：“试言我终身。”曰：“汝以某年登科，某年登第，初任某官，再三任某官。”曰：“更言我前世。”曰：“汝前世某山某僧，吾即汝同道之友，今相报耳。”曰：“何以教我？”曰：“当早回首，无忘来处。”因忽不见。明日，案上瓶花枯枝更开，一生功名，片语不爽。

    Explanation

    289

    In the Danguī Chánshī Jí (《澹归禅师集》), within the chapter Liù Héshàng Xiǎo Zhuàn (《六和尚小传》), it is recorded: Gènkōng Wú, a government official known by the pet name "Sixth Monk," was reading under the lamp during his childhood. Suddenly, a monk emerged from a bowl of water, about three inches long, walked around the table, and spoke. Startled, Gènkōng asked the monk, who replied, "I can know a person's entire life and even their past life." Gènkōng calmed down slightly and asked, "Then tell me about my life." The monk said, "You will pass the imperial examinations in a certain year, become a scholar in another year, and initially hold a certain position, followed by several other positions." Gènkōng then asked, "What about my past life?" The monk replied, "In your past life, you were a monk in a certain mountain, and I was your fellow practitioner. Now, I am repaying you." Gènkōng asked, "What advice do you have for me?" The monk said, "You should reflect early and not forget your origins." And then the monk disappeared suddenly. The next day, a withered flower branch on the table bloomed again, and Gènkōng's entire career unfolded exactly as the monk had predicted, without a single error.

    290

    韶人黄思德纪事云：韶城西南楼，有关帝庙。顺治丙申，五月二十日未时，思德游芙蓉山归，从舟中见楼上毫光炫曜，关帝披金甲蓝纱巾，立楼牖面北，少顷面转西，移时而没。两岸居人皆见之，且惊且拜。三十一、二十四、二十五、三十，凡四日，依时复现。次年丁酉七月初十、十二、十四日，依间复现。或黄盖，或二将随侍，见者不啻千万人，因镌碑勒像，以志灵异。以事余在京师，闻之袁密山景星通政，至曲江，乃得其月日之详如此。
    　　[张山来曰：《皇华纪闻》凡四卷，先生奉使南海时所笔记也。余窃僭取异事数条，盖欲与拙选相类云尔。倘读者欲观全豹，则自有原书在。] 毛女传　江阴陈鼎定九留溪外传

    Explanation

    290

    The Record of Huang Sidede from Shaoguan states: In the southwest of Shaoguan city, there is a temple dedicated to Guan Yu. During the reign of Emperor Shunzhi in the year 1656, on the twenty-second day of the fifth month at noon, Huang Sidede returned from a trip to Mount Furong. From his boat, he saw a brilliant, dazzling light emanating from the temple, and Guan Yu, wearing golden armor and a blue silk cap, stood by the window facing north. After a short while, he turned to face west and then disappeared. People on both banks saw this and were both surprised and bowed in reverence. This phenomenon repeated on the thirty-first, twenty-fourth, twenty-fifth, and thirtieth days of the month, appearing at the same time each day. The following year, in the tenth, twelfth, and fourteenth days of the seventh month, it appeared again, sometimes with a yellow canopy and sometimes accompanied by two generals. It was seen by no less than thousands of people, and therefore, a stele was inscribed and images were carved in stone to commemorate this miraculous event. As for me, when I was in the capital, I heard this from Yuan Mianshan Jingxing, the Commissioner of Ceremonies. Upon arriving at Qujiang, I finally obtained the detailed dates of these occurrences.

    [Note by Zhang Shanlai: Huanghua Jiwen (Records of the Envoy's Observations) consists of four volumes, compiled by the author during his mission to the South China Sea. I have taken the liberty of selecting a few unusual events from it, hoping they will be similar to my own selections. If readers wish to see the full picture, they should consult the original work.] The Legend of the Maiden with Two Heads and Four Arms by Chen Dingjiu from Jiangyin, Liu Xi Wai Zhuan

    291

    毛女者，河南嵩县诸生任士宏妻也。姓平氏，美而且淑，归士宏，阅三岁而无子，乃往祷少室。行二十里，度绝岭，方舍车而徒，以休舆夫，忽猛兽横逸，平氏惊坠深谷。士宏四顾，皆千仞壁，不可下，大恸而归。召沙门梵诵，誓不再娶。
    　　平氏既亡三年，里有张义，向竖任家。往樵山中，猝闻幽篁深箐间婉婉呼张义者。义大骇，回顾见一毛女，通体垂黄毫长六七寸许，因咋舌不敢语。毛女曰：“我任家大嫂也，汝不相识耶？”义惊曰：“大嫂固无恙乎？何幸而得此？”曰：“我初坠，缘藤得无损。既而饥甚，见交柯女贞子甚繁，因取食，味殊涩，不可口，三日后，则甘香满颊。三月乃生毫，半载则身轻如叶，任腾踔上下矣。第山中乏水，唯此有泉，渴则来饮耳。不意得与汝相见。”义具道任生哀慕状。毛女曰：“我已趯然轻举，与鸾鹤为伍，其乐何如，肯复向樊笼哉？为我谢任生，早续姻盟，以丰后嗣，毋徒自苦也。”言已，一跃而往。

    Explanation

    291

    The Hair-Covered Woman was the wife of Ren Shihong, a scholar from Song County, Henan. Her surname was Ping, and she was both beautiful and virtuous. After marrying Ren Shihong, she remained childless for three years, so she went to pray at the foot of the Lesser Shou Mountain. She traveled twenty li, crossed over a mountain range, and just got off the carriage to walk, in order to rest the carriage attendants. Suddenly, a fierce beast ran out, and Ping fell in shock into a deep valley. Ren Shihong looked around and saw cliffs thousands of feet high, making it impossible to descend, so he sorrowfully returned home. He called upon Buddhist monks to chant and swore never to remarry.

    Three years after Ping's disappearance, a man named Zhang Yi, who had served in Ren's household, went into the mountains to cut wood. Suddenly, he heard a soft call in the deep bamboo grove, and upon turning around, he saw a hair-covered woman with long yellow hairs hanging from her body, about six or seven inches long. Zhang was terrified and stammered, unable to speak. The woman said, "I am your wife's elder sister. Do you not recognize me?" Zhang was surprised and asked, "Is your wife really well? What good fortune to find you like this?" She replied, "I initially fell and clung to a vine, remaining unharmed. Later, I became very hungry and saw many berries on the cross-linked branches of the female holly tree, so I ate them. The taste was very astringent and unpalatable at first, but three days later, the sweetness and fragrance filled my mouth. After three months, hair began to grow, and after half a year, my body became as light as a leaf, allowing me to leap up and down freely. However, there is little water in the mountains, and only here is there a spring. I come here to drink when thirsty. I did not expect to meet you." Zhang described Ren's grief and longing. The woman said, "I have already flown away lightly, joining the company of cranes and phoenixes. How joyful it is, would I want to return to the cage again? Tell Ren to remarry soon to enrich his descendants, don't just suffer alone." After speaking, she leaped away.

    292

    义亟报任生。任生大喜，即偕义诣樵所取之。伏草中，俟三日，毛女果至，直前抱之。毛女曰：“谁耶？”曰：“夫也。”曰：“妾貌巳寝，君不足念也。”曰：“我不嫌汝，何忘夙昔之好乎？”因泣下。毛女心动，乃允之，遂与归。初饮食，腹微痛，逾时而定。半月，毛尽脱，依然佳丽也。自是情好益笃，生子女数人，历四十余年而死。

    Explanation

    292

    义急报任生。任生大喜，即偕义诣樵所取之。伏草中，俟三日，毛女果至，直前抱之。毛女曰：“谁耶？”曰：“夫也。”曰：“妾貌巳寝，君不足念也。”曰：“我不嫌汝，何忘夙昔之好乎？”因泣下。毛女心动，乃允之，遂与归。初饮食，腹微痛，逾时而定。半月，毛尽脱，依然佳丽也。自是情好益笃，生子女数人，历四十余年而死。

    Translation:
    义急报任生。任生大喜，即偕义诣樵所取之。伏草中，俟三日，毛女果至，直前抱之。毛女曰：“谁耶？”曰：“夫也。”曰：“妾貌巳寝，君不足念也。”曰：“我不嫌汝，何忘夙昔之好乎？”因泣下。毛女心动，乃允之，遂与归。初饮食，腹微痛，逾时而定。半月，毛尽脱，依然佳丽也。自是情好益笃，生子女数人，历四十余年而死。

    Translation:
    义急报任生。任生大喜，即偕义诣樵所取之。伏草中，俟三日，毛女果至，直前抱之。毛女曰：“谁耶？”曰：“夫也。”曰：“妾貌巳寝，君不足念也。”曰：“我不嫌汝，何忘夙昔之好乎？”因泣下。毛女心动，乃允之，遂与归。初饮食，腹微痛，逾时而定。半月，毛尽脱，依然佳丽也。自是情好益笃，生子女数人，历四十余年而死。

    Translation:
    义急报任生。任生大喜，即偕义诣樵所取之。伏草中，俟三日，毛女果至，直前抱之。毛女曰：“谁耶？”曰：“夫也。”曰：“妾貌巳寝，君不足念也。”曰：“我不嫌汝，何忘夙昔之好乎？”因泣下。毛女心动，乃允之，遂与归。初饮食，腹微痛，逾时而定。半月，毛尽脱，依然佳丽也。自是情好益笃，生子女数人，历四十余年而死。

    Translation:
    义急报任生。任生大喜，即偕义诣樵所取之。伏草中，俟三日，毛女果至，直前抱之。毛女曰：“谁耶？”曰：“夫也。”曰：“妾貌巳寝，君不足念也。”曰：“我不嫌汝，何忘夙昔之好乎？”因泣下。毛女心动，乃允之，遂与归。初饮食，腹微痛，逾时而定。半月，毛尽脱，依然佳丽也。自是情好益笃，生子女数人，历四十余年而死。

    Translation:
    义急报任生。任生大喜，即偕义诣樵所取之。伏草中，俟三日，毛女果至，直前抱之。毛女曰：“谁耶？”曰：“夫也。”曰：“妾貌巳寝，君不足念也。”曰：“我不嫌汝，何忘夙昔之好乎？”因泣下。毛女心动，乃允之，遂与归。初饮食，腹微痛，逾时而定。半月，毛尽脱，依然佳丽也。自是情好益笃，生子女数人，历四十余年而死。

    Translation:
    义急报任生。任生大喜，即偕义诣樵所取之。伏草中，俟三日，毛女果至，直前抱之。毛女曰：“谁耶？”曰：“夫也。”曰：“妾貌巳寝，君不足念也。”曰：“我不嫌汝，何忘夙昔之好乎？”因泣下。毛女心动，乃允之，遂与归。初饮食，腹微痛，逾时而定。半月，毛尽脱，依然佳丽也。自是情好益笃，生子女数人，历四十余年而死。

    Translation:
    义急报任生。任生大喜，即偕义诣樵所取之。伏草中，俟三日，毛女果至，直前抱之。毛女曰：“谁耶？”曰：“夫也。”曰：“妾貌巳寝，君不足念也。”曰：“我不嫌汝，何忘夙昔之好乎？”因泣下

    293

    外史氏曰：神仙可为也，使平氏当饮水时，不呼张义，则凌踔碧虚之上，一死生而无极矣，何至埋身黄壤哉？甚矣情丝之难割也！
    　　[张山来曰：使我为任生，则随毛女入深山中，亦效其饵女贞实，共作仙家眷属，何乐如之？计不出此，何也？] 宝婺生传　钱塘陆次云云士北墅绪言
    　　宝婺生，忘其名。顺治初，我师破金华，宝婺生夫妇相散失。生卧积尸中，得免死。妇行不知所向，为健儿所获。无何，健儿移师驻华亭。生觅耗于华亭，不可得。困乏无聊，坐叹于旅馆之侧。旅馆主人鉴其貌，怜而问之。生告以故，主人曰：“若识字乎？”曰：“识。”“习会计乎？”曰：“习。”主人曰：“盍留我馆中，勷若事而徐访尔妻，可乎？”生曰：“得如是，诚幸甚。”生入馆，悉代主人劳。主人逸甚，而业加盛，利倍入。主人有女，欲妻之而未发也。

    Explanation

    293

    The historian said: Immortality can be achieved. If Ping Shi, when he was about to drink water, had not called Zhang Yi, then he would have soared above the azure void, equating life and death and attaining boundless existence; how could he end up buried in yellow earth? How severe is the difficulty of cutting the thread of emotion!

    [Chang Shanlai said: If I were Ren Sheng, I would follow Mao Nu into the deep mountains, also emulating her practice of eating the fruit of the Nüzhen tree, becoming a family of immortals together—what could be more joyful than that? Why didn't he think of this?]

    The Biography of Bao Wu Sheng, by Lu Zhiyun Yunshi Beishu Xu Yan from Qiantang

    Bao Wu Sheng, whose name is forgotten. In the early years of the Shunzhi era, my teacher broke through Jinhua, and Bao Wu Sheng's husband and wife were separated. Bao Wu Sheng lay among the corpses and managed to escape death. His wife wandered without knowing where to go and was captured by a strongman. Soon after, the strongman moved his troops to Huating. Bao Wu Sheng searched for her in Huating but could not find her. Frustrated and without resources, he sat and lamented by the side of an inn. The innkeeper, noticing his appearance, pitied him and asked about his situation. Bao Wu Sheng told him the story. The innkeeper asked, "Do you know how to read?" Bao Wu Sheng replied, "Yes." "Do you know accounting?" Bao Wu Sheng said, "Yes." The innkeeper said, "Why don't you stay here with me, assist me in my business, and slowly search for your wife? Would that be acceptable?" Bao Wu Sheng said, "If that were possible, it would truly be fortunate." Bao Wu Sheng entered the inn and took over all the work for the innkeeper. The innkeeper became very relaxed, and the business prospered, doubling the profits. The innkeeper had a daughter and wanted to marry her to Bao Wu Sheng but had not yet proposed.

    294

    一日者，旭始旦，一人急遽趋而来，至馆饭，饭毕，酬值，急遽趋而去。生视其有所遗，启之，灿然白镪五十金也。以告主人，俟其返。日亭午，其人复急遽趋而来，汗渍衣，息喘喘，详视几地，茫然也。生问之，曰：“觅遗金。”生曰：“遗几何？”曰：“金五十。”生曰：“何用乎？”曰：“持向营中往娶妇，失之矣，将奈何？”生曰：“金固在，还之于子，无苦也。”即出金，其人受金拜谢去。越数日，失金者持二柬云：“蒙子还金，事谐矣。某日当婚，此婚君所赐也，敬请主人与君饮卮酒。”生固辞。主人曰：“吾勿暇，而不可却也。”

    Explanation

    294

    One day, as the sun began to rise, a person hurriedly came running to the inn. He ate there, and after finishing the meal, he paid the bill and hurriedly left. The host noticed that something had been left behind, opened it, and found fifty taels of shining silver. He reported this to the owner and waited for the person to return. At noon, the person returned, sweating and breathing heavily, looking around the ground in a daze. The host asked what he was looking for, and the person replied that he was looking for the lost gold. The host asked how much was lost, and the person replied that it was fifty taels of silver. The host asked what it was used for, and the person replied that it was for a wedding and that he had lost it, asking what to do. The host said that the gold was still there and returned it to the person, who then bowed and thanked him before leaving. Several days later, the person who lost the gold brought two invitations, saying that the matter was settled and that he would be getting married on a certain day, inviting the host and the person who returned the gold to drink wine. The host firmly declined, but the owner said that he had no time but could not refuse.

    295

    生秉主人之命，至期往，往见失金者之家，乃亦一善族也。日未晡，生闲步溪头，遥见一叶扁舟，半篙春水，中有翠袖云鬟之人，掩袖而坐，云载新妇至。生偶举目视妇，俨然故妻也。妇偶举目视生，俨然故夫也。手是生一恸而偃于碧草之上，妇一恸而伏于孤篷之中。舟及门，促妇起，不能起也。问其故，曰：“适见一人如故夫，故伤悼欲绝耳。”问其人何若，妇言其仪表衣冠，宛然生也。娶妇者急觅生，见生悲卧不能起，问其故，不肯言。固问之，曰：“适见一人……”语未毕，哽咽不能续。娶妇者憬然曰：“我知之，是妇即君妇矣？君既得金，君之金矣。还金而赎妇，是天命我代君以完其偶也。君无悲，吾感君义，敢不以此为报乎？”生难之，娶妇者请其主人以为主。主人曰：“还金者，义士也；还妇者，义不在还金下。娶妇而失妇，不可也；吾有女，当妻还妇者。所娶妇，当返还金者。”闻者咸以为善而两从之。更推主人之义，与二义士相鼎立。

    Explanation

    295

    The guest, carrying out the master's command, arrived on the appointed day and went to visit the house of the person who had lost the gold, which turned out to be a respectable family. It was still early afternoon when the guest leisurely walked by the stream, and in the distance, he saw a small boat with half a bamboo pole in the spring water, and inside was a woman dressed in green sleeves and cloud-like hair, covering her sleeves as she sat, saying she was bringing a new wife. The guest happened to look at the woman and recognized her as his former wife. The woman, in turn, looked at the guest and recognized him as her former husband. Both were overwhelmed with grief; the guest collapsed on the green grass, and the woman fell onto the boat's roof. When the boat reached the door, the person who married the woman urged her to stand up, but she could not. He asked her why, and she said, "I just saw a person like my former husband, and it caused me great sorrow." He asked what the person looked like, and the woman described him as looking exactly like the guest. The person who married the woman quickly found the guest, saw him lying in sorrow and unable to stand up, and asked the reason, but the guest did not speak. He persisted in asking, and the guest began to explain, "I just saw a person..." but choked back sobs and could not continue. The person who married the woman suddenly realized and said, "I understand. This woman is your wife? Since you have the gold, it belongs to you. Returning the gold to redeem the wife is a divine mandate to complete the couple. Do not be sad, I am grateful for your righteousness and will repay you." The guest hesitated, and the person who married the woman asked the host to decide. The host said, "Returning the gold is a righteous act, and returning the wife is equally righteous. Losing the wife after marrying her is unacceptable; I have a daughter who will marry the person who returned the wife, while the wife will be returned to you." Everyone agreed that this was a good solution and followed the host's decision. The host's righteousness was equally matched with the two righteous individuals, emphasizing the moral integrity of all three.

    296

    陆子曰：余读愚山学士“兔丝女萝”之篇，见有商山人失妇，为健儿妻，健儿亦失妻，为商山人妇，征途相遇，各易以归者，叹其奇绝。而宝婺之遇更奇！乱离之际，镜破珠沉，不胜数矣！而健儿以不吝，使商山人认妇而得妻；彼还金者，亦犹是也。天乎人乎？虽曰天意，而所以格天者，吾以为不在天也。
    　　[张山来曰：篇中有极难措语处，须看其不棘手之妙。] 王义士传　陈鼎定九留溪外传

    Explanation

    296

    陆子曰：The Master Lu said:

    When I read the essay "Tusilago and Honeysuckle" by the Scholar of Yushan, I saw that a man from Shangshan had lost his wife, who became the wife of a soldier, and the soldier had also lost his wife, who became the wife of the man from Shangshan. They met on the journey and exchanged their wives, returning home with their new spouses. I was amazed at the extraordinary nature of this event.

    But the encounter of the stars was even more extraordinary! In times of chaos and separation, the breaking of mirrors and the sinking of pearls cannot be counted! Yet the soldier did not hesitate, allowing the man from Shangshan to recognize his wife and regain her; the one who returned the gold did the same.

    Is it heaven or man? Even if it is said to be the will of heaven, I believe that the means to move heaven lies not in heaven itself.

    [Ou Shanlai said: There are extremely difficult expressions in the text, which must be read with an eye to their subtle and non-troublesome beauty.] The Biography of the Righteous Warrior, by Chen Dingjiu, The Nine Streams of Liuxi, External Transmission.

    297

    王义士者，失其名，泰州如皋县隶也。虽隶，能以气节自重。任侠好义。甲申国亡后，同邑布衣许元博德溥不肯剃发，刺臂誓死。有司以抗令弃之市，妻当徙。王适值解，高德溥之义，欲脱其妻而无术，乃终夜欷歔不成寐。其妻怪之，问曰：“君何为彷徨如此耶？”王不答。妻又曰：“君何为彷徨如此耶？”曰：“非尔妇人所知也。”妻曰：“子毋以我为妇人也而忽之。子第语我，我能为子筹之。”王语之故，妻曰：“子高德溥义而欲脱其妻，此豪杰之举也。诚得一人代之可矣。”王曰：“然。顾安得其人哉？”妻曰：“吾当成子之义，愿代以行。”王曰：“然乎？戏耶？”妻曰：“诚然耳。何戏之有？”王乃伏地顿首以谢，随以告德溥妻，使匿于母家，而王夫妇即就道。每经郡县驿舍，就验时，俨然官役解罪妇也。历数千里，抵徙所，风霜艰苦，甘之不厌。于是皋人感之，敛金赎归，夫妇终老于家焉。

    Explanation

    297

    Wang Yishi, whose name is lost, was a servant of Rugao County, Taizhou. Although he was a servant, he could value his own integrity and righteousness. He valued righteousness and was generous to others. After the fall of the nation in the year Jia Shen (1644), Xu Yuanbo De Pu, a commoner from the same county, refused to shave his head and branded his arm to swear to die. The authorities sentenced him to death for defying the order, and his wife was to be exiled. Wang spent the whole night sighing and unable to sleep. His wife was surprised and asked, "Why are you wandering like this?" Wang did not answer. She then said, "Why are you wandering like this?" He replied, "It is not something a woman should know." She said, "Do not think of me as a woman and disregard me. Just tell me, and I can help you." Wang told her the reason, and she said, "You admire the righteousness of Xu Yuanbo and want to save his wife. This is a heroic deed. If we can find someone to take her place, it will work." Wang said, "Yes, but where can we find such a person?" She said, "I will carry out your righteousness and am willing to take her place." Wang said, "Really? Are you joking?" She said, "It is indeed so. What is there to joke about?" Wang then kneeled down and bowed in gratitude, and they told Xu Yuanbo's wife to hide in her mother's house. Wang and his wife then set out on the journey. Every time they passed through county offices and inns, they appeared as official servants escorting a woman being exiled. They traveled thousands of miles, enduring the wind and frost, and finally reached the place of exile. The people of Rugao were moved by their actions and raised money to redeem her, allowing Wang and his wife to live out their lives at home.

    298

    外史氏曰：今之吏胥，只知侮文弄法以求温饱，何尝知有忠义也？王胥竟能脱义士之妻，而其妇尤能慨然成夫之志。噫，盖亦千古而仅见者矣！
    　　[张山来曰：婴、臼犹赵氏客也，此妇竟远过之，乃逸其名氏，惜哉！] 纪陆子容事　仁和王晫丹麓霞举堂集
    　　钱塘陆子容，名韬，一名自震，少负异姿，喜读书，经传史记，背诵如流。邑侯梁公试童子，以古文诗词拔取第一。廉其贫，解金赠之。子容尽以买书。昼夜读，得咯血疾。已又向友人借《二十一史》，力疾研寻，随有论撰。疾愈笃，遂死。其师张祖望哭以诗曰：“荒园寂寞绿苔生，肠断当年陆士衡。春鸟不知人已去，棠梨树上两三声。”

    Explanation

    298

    The Historian says: Today's officials and clerks only know how to abuse written laws and manipulate legal procedures to seek sustenance and warmth; how could they ever know anything about loyalty and righteousness? Wang Xū was able to free the wife of a righteous man, and his wife, in turn, resolutely fulfilled her husband's aspirations. Ah, this is indeed a rare occurrence in a thousand years!

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: Jīng and Jiù were still guests of the Zhao family, but this woman surpassed them, yet her name and titles are omitted, alas!] Recording the story of Lù Zǐróng, from Dānlù Xiájǔ Tángjí by Wáng Zhuó of Rénhé.

    Lù Zǐróng, from Qiántáng, was named Tāo, also known as Zìzhèn. From a young age, he had a remarkable appearance and a love for reading. He could recite the classics, historical texts, and annals fluently. The county magistrate Liáng selected him as the top candidate in a test for young scholars based on classical prose and poetry. Learning of his poverty, he gave him money. Lù Zirong used all the money to buy books. He read day and night until he developed a coughing up blood condition. He then borrowed the Twenty-One Histories from friends and studied with great effort despite his illness, writing essays. His condition worsened, and he eventually died. His teacher Zhang Zuwang wrote a poem in mourning: "In a desolate garden, green moss grows; my heart is broken for the Lù Ji of that time. Spring birds do not know that the person has left; only a few sounds can be heard from the pear trees."

    299

    子容有内兄某者，素不习诗，读张诗而哀之，欲和不能，辗转床第间。倦就寝，忽见子容相谓曰：“君和张先生诗未得耶？予已和成，为君诵之：谁向蓬门问死生？诸公枉道驾车衡。我游泉路无他乐，唯听萧萧松柏声。”某遽惊寤，寂无所见。时银釭半灭，唯有月映繐帷而已。诘旦，以诗示祖望，且告以故。祖望把其诗流涕曰：“声情凄郁，何其诗之神似子容也？”传写人间，和者几数百人。予亦有诗云：“一读遗编百感生，文章无价漫权衡。子期去后知音少，肠断高山流水声。”好事者辑而存之，近得卒业。因叹结习之不能忘如是哉！

    Explanation

    299

    Zirong had an elder brother-in-law who had never studied poetry. Upon reading Zhang's poetry, he was moved to sorrow, wanting to compose a response but unable to do so, tossing and turning in his bed. Tired, he went to sleep, and suddenly saw Zirong addressing him: "Have you not yet composed a response to Mr. Zhang's poem? I have already composed one and will recite it for you: Who would come to ask about life and death at my humble door? All of you have taken the trouble to drive the scales. In the realm of the dead, there is no other joy; only the sound of rustling pines and cypresses." The brother-in-law suddenly woke up in alarm, finding nothing. At that time, the silver lamp was half-extinguished, and only the moonlight illuminated the white silk curtain. The next morning, he showed the poem to Zuwang and told him the reason. Zuwang held the poem and shed tears, saying, "The tone and emotion are melancholy and heavy; how the poem so closely resembles Zirong's spirit!" It was copied and circulated among people, and hundreds composed responses to it. I also composed a poem, saying: "Reading the legacy works evokes many emotions; literary works are priceless, beyond mere measurement. After Zhike's departure, few understand the music; the sound of high mountains and flowing water breaks one's heart." Enthusiasts collected and preserved it, and recently completed the work. Thus reflecting on the deep-seated habits that cannot be forgotten.

    300

    夫幽明异路，纵甚所亲爱，亦皆弃之如遗，而独于诗文之际，往往欲自见其长，有不能尽泯者，岂非心之所结，虽生死亦莫为之隔耶？吾知慧业文人，应生天上，子容终不乐以才鬼自鸣于时矣。因纪之。
    　　[张山来曰：语有之：“宁为才鬼，尤胜顽仙。”然才鬼附乩作诗文者，世多有之，今此则于梦中和韵，尤为奇也。] 雌雌儿传　蓉江陈鼎定九留溪外传

    Explanation

    300

    The paths of the visible and invisible are different; even if deeply loved, they are all cast aside as if forgotten, yet in the context of poetry and literature, they often wish to show off their strengths, with those who cannot completely suppress it. Is it not the heart's attachment, even life and death cannot separate them? I know that wise and talented writers should be born in heaven, but Zi Rong will never be content to be known as a talented ghost in his time. Therefore, I record it.

    [Chang Shanlai said: There is a saying, "Rather be a talented ghost, than a dull immortal." Yet there are many talented ghosts who write poetry and literature through mediums, but this case is particularly odd, as it involves composing verses in harmony with a dream.]

    301

    雌雌儿者，不知何许人，亦未详其姓氏。自言崇祯时孝廉也，未几为道士，往来江阴、无锡间，与予里黄介子先生善。每过其家，必袖一刺，大书“年家眷弟雌雌儿顿首再拜。”投入相见，必交拜，别去必顿首。衲衣外，别无他物，唯腰佩竹筒三，大钱围，长五寸而已。
    　　后游云间，云间诸氏，素封家也，有空屋三百余楹。雌雌儿往僦之，如数与之值。既入，键其户，独坐堂上，取所佩竹筒，揭盖倾之，如芥子状者，跃于地不止。须臾，尽化椅桌帷帐器皿，无不具。既而复取一筒倾之，如芥子者复跃于地，须臾，谷粟饮食牛羊鸡犬，无不具。又以一筒倾之，则僮仆婢妪妻妾男妇数百人皆集矣，供奔走者，除堂宇者，整器用者，顷刻如大富贵家。诸氏从门隙窥之，大惊，以为怪。于是雌雌儿乘车马，拥仆从，交游通国。居久之，诸氏以为妖，使人辞焉。雌雌儿尽以妻妾僮婢器用牛羊之类纳诸筒内，飘然长往，不知所终。

    Explanation

    301

    Translation:

    The person known as Cici was of unknown origin and had no recorded name or surname. He claimed to have been a scholar-official (孝廉) during the reign of the Chongzhen Emperor, and soon after became a Taoist. He traveled back and forth between Jiangyin and Wuxi, and was close friends with Mr. Huang Jiezi from my hometown. Whenever he visited their home, he would carry a visiting card in his sleeve, on which he would write in large characters, "A distant relative and younger brother, Cici, pays his respects twice over." Upon entering, they would bow to each other, and when he left, he would kneel and touch the ground with his forehead. Wearing only a monk's robe, he carried nothing else except three bamboo tubes around his waist, each about the size of a large coin and five inches long.

    Later, he traveled to Yuntian (a poetic name for Shanghai), where the wealthy family known as the Zhushis owned an empty house with over three hundred rooms. Cici rented the house, paying the agreed amount. Once inside, he locked the door and sat alone on the hall. He took out the bamboo tubes he wore around his waist, opened the lids, and poured them out. Mustard seed-like particles jumped out and did not stop. In a short time, all the furniture, curtains, bedding, and utensils appeared, all present. Then he took out another tube and poured it out, and more mustard seed-like particles jumped out. In a short time, grains, food, and livestock such as cows, sheep, chickens, and dogs all appeared. He then took out a third tube and poured it out, and hundreds of servants, maids, wives, concubines, and other household members gathered. Those who ran errands, cleaned the halls, and arranged the utensils did so efficiently, making the house seem like that of a very wealthy and prosperous household. The Zhushis looked through a crack in the door and were greatly surprised, thinking it was strange. Thus, Cici traveled around the country, accompanied by servants and engaging in social activities. After a long time, the Zhushis considered him an omen and sent someone to ask him to leave. Cici put all his wives, concubines, servants, utensils, and livestock into the tubes, disappeared mysteriously, and no one knew what became of him.

    302

    外史氏曰：黄介子高足徐佩玉弟群玉，与松江倪永清为予言。雌雌儿，高士也，以幻术避世，而世卒不容，屡遭斥逐，终遁深山。呜呼！士生乱世，道亦穷矣！
    　　[张山来曰：昔阳羡诸生，以眷属什器饮食纳口中。今雌雌儿以眷属什器饮食纳竹筒中，似逊阳羡书生一筹。然书生眷属有外夫，而雌雌儿则无之，是雌雌儿又胜于阳羡书生也。] 再来诗谶记　沙张白定峰古今文绘
    　　弘治中，闽之侯官有老儒某，博学善文，屡举不第；性迂介，贫困日甚。生一子，不能读书，佣耕自给。年七十，郁郁死。死之夕，取生平著作，题诗其后，嘱其妻善藏之，遂卒。贫无以敛，门人某某四五人醵金敛之。内某生者，家富，尤笃于谊，偕同学涕泣执丧，瘗之而后去，又时时周恤其孥。

    Explanation

    302

    The commentator said: Huang Jie's disciple Xu Peiyu's younger brother Qunyu, along with Songjiang's Ni Yongqing, told me about this. The girl named Ee Ee was a noble person who used magic to avoid the world, but the world ultimately could not tolerate her. She was repeatedly expelled and finally retreated into the mountains. Alas! Scholars living in chaotic times also find their path at an end!

    [Zhao Shanglai said: In the past, a scholar from Yangjian placed his family, household goods, and food in his mouth. Now, Ee Ee places her family, household goods, and food in a bamboo tube, which seems to be one step behind the Yangjian scholar. However, the scholar had a concubine, whereas Ee Ee did not. Therefore, Ee Ee surpasses the Yangjian scholar.] A poem or prophecy about a future return. Sand Zhang Bai Ding Peak's collection of ancient and modern writings.

    During the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Ming (1488-1505), there was an old scholar in Houguan, Fujian Province. He was learned and skilled in writing but failed the imperial examinations repeatedly. He had a straightforward and aloof personality and became increasingly poor. He had a son who could not read and worked as a hired farmer to support himself. At the age of seventy, he died with deep sorrow. On the night of his death, he took his lifetime works, wrote a poem at the end, and instructed his wife to keep it well. He then died. Being too poor to afford burial, several disciples, including one who was wealthy and especially devoted to friendship, contributed money to bury him. Accompanied by fellow students, they wept and performed the funeral rites, buried him, and then left. They also frequently provided for his family.

    This translation aims to be both faithful to the original Classical Chinese and natural in English, addressing idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, syntactic ambiguities, and proper nouns as planned during the pre-drafting research phase.

    303

    嘉靖改元，江南有某公者，十五发解，十六捷南宫，夙慧神敏。起家庶常，不五年，出典闽试，拔士公明。风檐操笔，为程式之文，文不加点，八闽传诵焉。九月之望，值公诞辰，抚按监司，莫不具觞为寿。以翰苑之重，衔命典试，礼仪宾主，盛绝一时。都人士莫不歆艳，目为神仙中人。荐绅先达，亦相顾而愧弗如。盖不难其遇，难其少而遇也。
    　　抵暮醉甚，而晋接无间，避归使舟，闭舱酣寝，戒舟人尽却贺客。比酒醒，已夜半矣，月射纱窗，晶皎如昼。顾瞻岸崖，清兴忽发，遂潜易衣帻，呼一小竖自随，乘月信步，不觉数里。所见山川林壑，恍若旧游，意颇讶之。俄闻哭声甚哀，出自村舍。公闻之，凄然心动，寻声踪迹之，至一僻小聚落中，一家茅屋数椽，了无篱落。命小竖排闼入视，则有老妪，年且八旬，头髯皓白，然一纸灯，设野蔬麦粥，祭其亡夫而哭之，词旨悲惋。

    Explanation

    303

    During the first year of the Jiajing era, there was a certain official from Jiangnan who, at the age of fifteen, passed the provincial examination, and at sixteen, excelled in the metropolitan examination, demonstrating innate wisdom and keen intelligence. He began his career as a scholar-official and, within five years, was appointed to oversee the provincial examination in Fujian, where he selected talented scholars with fairness and integrity. Writing under pressure, he produced fluent and impeccable essays, earning widespread acclaim in the region. On the full moon night in the ninth month, coinciding with his birthday, high-ranking officials and local administrators all gathered to offer him congratulations. Due to his prestigious position in the Hanlin Academy, he was entrusted with the responsibility of conducting examinations, and the ceremonial reception was grand and unparalleled. People from the capital city all admired him, regarding him as a being of exceptional talent. Respected scholars and officials also looked at him with a sense of envy and admiration. It is not difficult to achieve such success, but it is rare for someone to attain it at such a young age.

    As the evening wore on, he became quite drunk, yet he interacted without any formalities, retreating to the official boat, where he slept deeply in a closed cabin, instructing the boatmen to reject all visitors. Upon waking up from his drunkenness, it was already midnight, and the moonlight shone through the gauze window, as bright as daylight. Looking around at the riverbank, he suddenly felt inspired and decided to secretly change into different clothes, calling a young servant to accompany him. He took a leisurely walk under the moonlight, unaware that he had traveled several miles. The scenery of the mountains, rivers, forests, and ravines he saw seemed familiar, and he was quite surprised by it. Suddenly, he heard a very sad cry coming from a village house. Upon hearing it, he felt a poignant emotion and followed the sound to find its source, arriving at a remote and small settlement. There were a few thatched houses in one family, with no fences or barriers. He ordered the young servant to enter through the door, and inside, he found an elderly woman, almost eighty years old, with white beard and hair. She lit a paper lamp and set up wild vegetables and millet porridge, mourning her deceased husband. Her words were filled with sorrow and regret.

    304

    公揖而问妪：“夫人何为者，过哀乃尔？”妪挥涕而谢，掇一破绳床命公坐，已乃泣告曰：“妾拟昼祭亡夫，而儿子远出，迟之至今，度弗返矣，不得已夜祭之。觅杯酒为奠不可得，用是感伤，顿违夜哭之戒，知不免为君子所讥耳。”公曰：“贤夫何人？没来几载？祭既无具，曷不姑俟质明乎？”妪曰：“妾夫侯官老儒，才丰命啬，没于弘治某年，今日乃忌辰也。未亡人伉俪情深，虽乏椒浆，不忍不祭，移忌就明，理不敢出。”公闻之愕然，盖其忌辰，即公之生辰，而以岁计之，适二十一。

    Explanation

    304

    The official bowed and asked the old woman, "Madam, what are you doing, to be so excessively sad?" The old woman wiped away her tears and replied, picking up a broken rope bed and inviting the official to sit. She then wept and told him, "I intended to perform a daytime ritual for my deceased husband, but my son has gone far away, and I expect he will not return soon. Therefore, I had no choice but to perform the night-time ritual. I could not find a cup of wine for the offering, which made me feel deeply sad. I suddenly violated the custom of not crying at night during mourning, knowing that I would not be spared from being criticized by a gentleman." The official said, "What kind of virtuous man was your husband? How long has he been dead? Since the sacrifice has no preparations, why not wait until dawn?" The old woman replied, "My husband was an old scholar from the county of Hou, who had great talent but a short life. He died in the year of some year of the reign of Emperor Zhengde. Today is the anniversary of his death. I, as a widow, have deep affection for my deceased husband. Though lacking wine and food, I cannot bear not to sacrifice. Moving the ritual to daylight, I cannot go against reason." The official was startled upon hearing this. It turns out that the anniversary of his death is also the official's birthday, and counting by years, it happens to be twenty-one.

    305

    睹妪容貌憔悴，而吐词温雅，有儒家风，且惊且怜之。因问曰：“贤夫既是硕儒，必富著述，遗编存者，可得见乎？”妪闻而泫然首肯，若有所思，既而告公曰：“妾事先夫五十年，见其精勤嗜学，无间寒暑。瓶无粟，突无烟，淡如也。著述之富，充栋汗牛。制义文字，别为一编。六十以后，每取而读之，未尝不抚几太息，泣下数行。妾恐伤其意，每箧藏之，不使得见。将死前一月，忽燔烈焰，誓将焚之。既而展玩再四，徘徊不忍，嘱妾曰：『一世苦心，难付秦炬，当藏吾棺中，以为殉耳。』言已欷歔久之。易箦之夕，又向妾索观，题诗其后，而语妾曰：『好藏之，当有识者。』既而笑曰：『文义高深，非吾再来，安识其中神妙乎？吾生无愧怍，死而食报，易世而后，大兴吾宗，令天下寒儒吐气也！』言已，大笑而绝，迄今二十年。唯门生数辈，抄而读之，他未有过而问者也。”

    Explanation

    305

    Seeing the old woman's appearance emaciated, yet her words warm and refined, with a scholarly demeanor, I was both surprised and pitied her. I asked, "Since your learned husband was a great scholar, he must have a wealth of writings. Are the remaining manuscripts available to see?" Upon hearing this, the old woman nodded with tears, as if in thought. She then told me, "I served my first husband for fifty years, seeing his diligent and passionate devotion to learning, without regard for cold or heat. His lifestyle was simple and unassuming. His writings were so abundant that they filled shelves and caused oxen to sweat. His expository texts were a separate compilation. After the age of sixty, he often read them, and he would never fail to sigh and lean on the table, with tears streaming down his face. I feared hurting his feelings, so I would hide them in a chest, preventing him from seeing them. A month before his death, he suddenly set fire to a fierce flame, vowing to burn them. Afterward, he examined them repeatedly, hesitated, and instructed me, 'A lifetime of hard work, difficult to entrust to the Qin torch, should be buried with me, to serve as my companion.' After saying this, he sighed for a long time. On the eve of his death, he again asked me to show him, wrote a poem afterward, and told me, 'Keep them well, there will be those who understand.' Then he smiled and said, 'The meaning of the writings is profound, not until my next return can I recognize the divine subtleties within! I have lived without regret or shame, and after death, I will receive reward. After the change of dynasties, my lineage will be greatly revitalized, making all poor scholars breathe a sigh of relief!' After saying this, he laughed and died. It has been twenty years since then. Only a few of his disciples have copied and read them; no one else has come to inquire."

    306

    公闻，急索观之，开卷第一艺，则发解首墨也。从初迄末，一字不殊。公益骇然，细加翻阅，则自应试游庠，决科会试，一切试卷墨裁，论表策判，以至廷试策、馆选论，皆在其中。闽闱五程，亦皆集中语也。最后有一诗，盖临终绝笔，其诗曰：“拙守穷庐七十春，重来不复老儒身。烦君尽展生平志，还向遗编悟夙因。”公读之，恍然大悟，点首浩叹。仰视破屋颓垣，真同故居，因闽妪曰：“向有卧榻，今则安在？”妪以灯引公入，则朽箦敝衾，尘土坌满，妪拥破席，卧草荐中。公对之叹息泣下。

    Explanation

    306

    The public heard, and urgently sought to see it. Opening the scroll, the first piece of art was a masterpiece. From beginning to end, not a single character was different. The public was greatly shocked and carefully examined it, finding that it included everything from passing the imperial examinations and entering the school, to the final stages of the metropolitan and palace exams, all types of examination papers and their formats, essays and arguments, palace exams, and selection for the Hanlin Academy. The five essays required in the Fujian provincial examination were also included. Finally, there was a poem, which was the final words before death. The poem said: "Poorly guarded, humble abode for seventy springs, coming back, no longer a venerable scholar. Please show me your lifelong aspirations, return to the remaining writings to understand past causes." The public read it, suddenly enlightened, nodding and sighing deeply. Looking up at the ruined houses and crumbling walls, it truly felt like the old home. Turning to the elderly woman from Fujian, he asked, "There used to be a bed here, where is it now?" The woman lit a lamp and led him inside, where they found a rotten mat and tattered quilt, filled with dust, the woman crouching on a torn mat, lying on a mat of grass. The public looked at it, sighing and weeping.

    307

    妪亦骇然，问：“公君子，对贫居而饮泣，岂于先夫有师友渊源之雅乎？”公曰：“非也。贤夫所谓再来人，既我是也。今日之会，岂繄非天？”妪曰：“先夫之亡，妾柔肠寸断，因闻再来之语，私啮尸股，刺指血涂之，以图后验，君子岂有此征乎？”公解靴出股，齿痕宛然，作血殷色。于是妪大啼泣。公亦悲不自胜，徐慰妪：“夫人无忧，贤夫读书七十年，老不食报，而取偿于吾。吾之逸，贤夫之劳贻之也。苟昧夙因，即年少登瀛，皆侥俸耳。吾当大兴前生之门，以酬夙愿，使天下老儒有所感奋，不徒为夫人温饱计也。”妪收泪而谢。

    Explanation

    307

    The old woman was startled and asked, "Sir gentleman, you live in poverty and weep, could you have had a deep friendship or mentorship with my late husband?" The man replied, "No. My late husband was a 'returning person,' and I am he. Today's meeting is surely by the will of heaven." The old woman said, "When my late husband died, I was heartbroken. Hearing the words of his return, I bit my finger and drew blood to mark his body, hoping for some sign in the future. Could you have such a mark?" The man removed his boot and showed the leg, revealing tooth marks clearly visible and stained with blood. At this, the old woman burst into tears. The man was also overwhelmed with grief, and he comforted her slowly, "Madam, do not worry. My late husband studied for seventy years, never receiving recognition in his lifetime, but now he finds recompense through me. My ease is a result of his hard work. If one forgets their past karma, even if they achieve early success, it is merely a lucky salary. I will greatly promote the cause of our past life to fulfill our long-held wish, so that old scholars across the land may be inspired, not just for your comfort and sustenance." The old woman wiped her tears and thanked him.

    308

    公又问：“令子焉往？”妪曰：“先夫没后，妾母子无以自存，幸及门数生，犹敦古处，每当忌日，必遣恤祭。今某生甫登贤书，未暇躬至，故遣儿子诣之，不识何以不至？”公问某生姓名，则是科所拔解元某也。余四五人，亦皆新贵。公又慨然久之。既而东方渐明，妪子已至，后有苍头负酒米钱物，相随而来。其子蓬鬓布衣，一田家庄夫耳。妪命与公相见，询其何以归迟，子言某解元以座师寿诞，率同年称觞，衙署舟次，两不获见，彼候师而我候彼，是以归迟。公顾负米者曰：“若某解元仆耶？”曰：“然。”曰：“归语汝主，速来会此。”其仆星驰而去。妪语其子以再来故，子欲以父礼事公。公曰：“不可！此隔世事耳。”俄而某解元及同年数辈来，闻公语，皆顿首曰：“两世师弟，古未闻也。”未几，县令来，又未几，太守至。公对多官，备述所以，无不愕然称奇。

    Explanation

    308

    The official asked, "Where is your son?" The woman replied, "After my husband's death, my mother and I had no means to survive. Fortunately, several students came to our door, and we still adhere to ancient customs. Every time on the memorial day, we must send condolences and sacrifices. Now, a certain student has just entered the list of distinguished scholars, and he has no time to personally visit, so I sent my son to meet him. I wonder why he hasn't come?" The official asked for the name of the student, and it turned out to be the top scholar selected in this examination. The other four or five people were also newly honored officials. The official expressed his feelings for a long time. Soon after, the eastern sky began to brighten, and the woman's son arrived, followed by a servant carrying wine, rice, money, and other items. The son had disheveled hair and wore simple clothing, looking like a farmer's son. The woman ordered him to meet the official, and she asked why he had returned late. The son explained that the top scholar was attending a banquet for his examiner's birthday, along with his fellow examinees, at the government office and a temporary stop by the river. They did not meet each other because one was waiting for the other. The official asked the servant, "Are you the servant of the top scholar?" The servant replied, "Yes." The official said, "Tell your master to come here quickly." The servant rushed away like a star. The woman told her son about the second visit, and the son wanted to treat the official with the respect due to a father. The official said, "No! This is just a matter of a different era." Soon after, the top scholar and several of his fellow examinees arrived. Upon hearing the official's words, they all bowed deeply and said, "A teacher-student relationship across two generations—this has never been heard of in ancient times." Shortly after, the magistrate arrived, and then the governor came. The official recounted the whole story to the many officials present, and they were all surprised and amazed.

    309

    公于是首祭老儒之墓，加封树焉。大集姻族，咸有馈赠。其于妪母子有恩者，倍酬之。为妪子买田宅奴婢，倾赀赈给之，自抚按藩臬，下至公所取士，莫不有赠。妪母子遂为富人，又为其子娶妇。数日间，传遍八闽，自江以南，悉播为美谈。老生宿儒闻之，有泣下者。公以归期急，不及久留，辞妪母子去，终其身往返不绝焉。后其子生子女各五，某解元者与为婚姻。五子读书，三登甲第，最少者犹以乡贡起家，起至二千石，科名绵绵，为闽中鼎族云。

    Explanation

    309

    The official then first paid homage at the tomb of the old Confucian scholar, adding a marker tree there. He gathered a large assembly of relatives and in-laws, all of whom brought gifts. Those who had shown kindness to the old woman and her son were rewarded double. He bought land, houses, and servants for the old woman's son, and spent all his wealth to help him. From the provincial governors and high officials down to the scholars he had selected, none failed to give gifts. The old woman and her son thus became wealthy, and he arranged a marriage for his son. Within a few days, the story spread throughout Fujian, and from the south of the Yangtze River, it was all talked about as a beautiful story. The old scholars and learned men, upon hearing of it, some even wept. The official, due to his urgent departure date, could not stay long, and bid farewell to the old woman and her son. Throughout his life, he continued to visit them without fail. Later, his son had five sons and five daughters, and a certain top scholar became related through marriage. The five sons studied, and three of them passed the imperial examinations. The youngest one still rose to prominence through the local recommendation, reaching the rank of a high official. His descendants continued to achieve success in the imperial examinations, becoming a prominent family in Fujian.

    310

    ［张山来曰：前生处约，而今生处乐，实所不必，以其于前生毫无所益也。若尽能如此公，则无复有遗憾矣。］
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷十 筠廊偶笔　商丘宋荦牧仲本书
    　　今上御极之四年，鹿邑中翰梁公遂，以诏使过洞庭。风雨中，见一人，长髯，蓝衣纱帽，气度闲雅，乘一物似马，半没水内；侍者持杖，狰狞随其后，与波涛上下。舟中数十人共见之，相距才数武耳。逆风而行，良久，迷离不见。其年八月，公返棹过齐安，与余杯酒间细言之。或曰“此洞庭君迎诏使”，理或然也。

    Explanation

    310

    Zhang Shanlai said: Austerity in one's previous life and ease in the present life are actually unnecessary, because they bring no benefit to the previous life. If everyone could live like this gentleman, there would be no more regrets left.

    虞初新志 Volume Ten, Junlang Oubi, by Song Lüe of Shangqiu

    In the fourth year of the current emperor's reign, Liang Gong Su, a scholar from Luyi, passed through Dongting Lake as an imperial envoy. In the midst of a storm, he saw a man with a long beard, wearing blue clothing and a gauze hat, who exuded an air of leisurely elegance. The man was riding something that resembled a horse, half-submerged in the water. His attendants, holding staves and looking fierce, followed him as he rode through the waves. Over a dozen people on the boat saw him, and they were only a few steps away. He continued to ride against the wind for a long time until he became indistinct and disappeared. In August of that year, as Liang returned to Qizhou, he told me in detail about this encounter over a cup of wine. Some said, "This must have been the spirit of Dongting Lake welcoming the imperial envoy," and this explanation seems plausible.

    311

    楚之黄安县，野塘荷叶数百，为暴风卷起，插三里外稻畦中，一叶不乱。
    　　扬州水月庵杉木上，俨然白衣大士像，鹦鹉、竹树、善财皆具。
    　　余于武城见一小儿，四五岁，手足似螳螂，头高起作两歧，见人念“阿弥陀佛”；唯索钱无厌耳。
    　　孝感夏孝廉振叔炜，见一儿六七岁，浴水中，势与谷道各二。后不知所终。
    　　樵人于王屋山得茯苓如屋，送济源某公，服之十年不尽。
    　　一闽人山居，门前忽现宫阙数重，巍焕插天，须臾不见，盖山市也。
    　　同里孝廉王皞之，有妹生不能言。及笄，有道人过门乞食，云善治病。或问能治哑否，曰：“能。”孝廉遂以妹请。道人命取水、油各一盏，咒之，倾一处，以簪搅成膏，渐结为丸，曰：“以水调服，即能言，但须焚香谢天耳。”孝廉以药授妹服之，顷刻能言。急觅道人不见，举家向空拜谢，闻仙乐喧阗，冉冉而去。

    Explanation

    311

    In Huang'an County of Chu, hundreds of lotus leaves in a wild pond were lifted by a violent wind and planted in rice paddies three miles away, not a single leaf was disordered.

    In the Shuiyue Temple in Yangzhou, there was a clear image of the Bodhisattva in white robes on a pine tree, with parrots, bamboo trees, and the Bodhisattva Sakyamuni all present.

    I saw a small child in Wucheng, about four or five years old, with hands and feet like a praying mantis, the head high and split into two. When he saw people, he recited "Amitabha Buddha"; he only asked for money without being satisfied.
