    Xia Shoulin Zhen Shuwei from Xiaoguan saw a child about six or seven years old bathing in water, with the body and the valley each having two parts. Later, it is unknown where he went.

    A woodcutter found a fungus as big as a house on Wutai Mountain, sent it to a certain official in Jiyuan, and he took it for ten years and it was not exhausted.

    A person from Fujian lived in the mountains, and suddenly, several palaces appeared in front of his door, majestic and towering into the sky, soon disappeared. It was a mountain market.

    Wang Haozhi, a scholar from the same village, had a sister who was born unable to speak. When she came of age, a Taoist monk passed by the door asking for food, saying he was good at treating diseases. Someone asked if he could treat muteness, and he said, "Yes." The scholar then brought his sister to ask. The monk ordered to take one cup of water and one cup of oil, chanted over them, mixed them together, and stirred them into a paste with a hairpin, gradually forming into pills. He said, "Take with water, and she will be able to speak, but you must burn incense and thank heaven." The scholar gave the medicine to his sister to take, and she spoke immediately. They hurriedly looked for the monk but could not find him. The whole family bowed to the sky in gratitude, hearing heavenly music and seeing him slowly disappear.

    312

    闽中洛阳桥圮，有石刻云：“石头若开，蔡公再来。”鄞人蔡锡，中明永乐癸卯乡试，仁庙授兵科给事中，升泉州太守。锡至，欲修桥，桥跨海，工难施。锡以文檄海神，忽一醉卒趋而前曰：“我能赍檄往。”乞酒饮大醉，自没于海，若有神人扶掖之者。俄而以“醋”字出。锡意必八月廿一日也，遂以是日兴工。潮旬余不至，工遂成。语载锡本传中，乃实事也。人不知而以其事附蔡端明，且以为传奇中妄语矣。锡官至都御史，以才廉闻。
    　　[张山来曰：宋先生，予父执也，抚吴时，以大集暨此帙见赠，获之不啻拱璧。敬采异事数条载入选中，盖仿前人节录《搜神记》《续齐谐记》之例，非敢有所去取也。] 金忠洁公传　毗陵董以宁文友国仪集

    Explanation

    312

    The Luoyang Bridge in Fujian collapsed, and there was an inscription on the stone that read: "If the stone opens, P. will return." Yin native P. Cai passed the provincial examination during the Ming Dynasty in the Yongle period and was appointed by Emperor Yongle as a military censor. He was later promoted to the position of governor of Quanzhou. Upon his arrival, P. Cai wished to repair the bridge, but since it spanned the sea, the work was difficult to undertake. P. Cai issued a written proclamation to the sea god, and suddenly a drunken soldier approached and said, "I can deliver the proclamation." He asked for wine, drank it, became drunk, and then submerged himself in the sea as if being carried by a divine being. Soon after, the character "醋" emerged. P. Cai interpreted this to mean August 21st, so he began the construction on that day. The tides did not arrive for over ten days, and the work was completed. This account is recorded in P. Cai's biography and is a true story. People were unaware of this and mistakenly attributed the event to P. Zhenming, even considering it a fabrication from legendary tales. P. Cai eventually reached the position of chief inspector and was renowned for his talent and integrity.

    [Comment by Zhang Shanlai: Mr. Song was my father's friend. When I governed Wu, he presented me with the Da Ji and this volume, which I valued as highly as a precious jade. I carefully selected a few strange events to include in the selection, imitating the precedent of excerpting from Shou Shen Ji and Xu Qi Xie Ji. I did not dare to exclude or include anything.] The Biography of Jin Zhongjie by Dong Yining of Piling, The Collection of Literary Friends and National Instruments.

    313

    金铉，字伯玉，武进之剡村人也。因殉节，谥“忠洁”，人称金忠洁云。初以顺天籍领解，成进士，时年十九；不习吏，请改教授。其大父户部主事汝升，旧多藏书，乃与弟錝日夜读之。继擢国子监博士，迁工部主事。
    　　先是时，明怀宗已诛魏忠贤，而太监张彝宪等旋用事。至是而贼李自成兵始炽；添内饷，命彝宪总理户工钱粮，建别署。忠洁曰：“此天下存亡之机也，奈何诛忠贤，复任一忠贤？且我为工曹，必将属视我矣。”乃抗疏言，先言彝宪既有独踞之庭，必强二部郎官匍匐进谒，挫士节，辱朝廷。疏上不报，而总理已建署，果檄郎官以谒尚书仪注见。复上疏固争之，旨谕职事相关，自当礼见，余不必通谒，金铉亦不得激陈。彝宪意甚得，与其党议接侍郎官礼。或曰：“视尚书当稍倨。”宪曰：“我当稍恭，而待金铉倨耳。”

    Explanation

    313

    Jin Xuan, courtesy name Boyu, was from Tian Village in Wujin. Posthumously titled "Zhongjie" for his loyalty and integrity, he is commonly referred to as Jin Zhongjie. Initially, he was selected through the provincial examination under the Shuntian register and became a Jinshi at the age of nineteen. Unfamiliar with bureaucratic affairs, he requested a change to a teaching position. His paternal grandfather, Yu Sheng, served as a Shangshu in the Ministry of Revenue and had a large collection of books, which Jin Xuan and his younger brother Zhen read day and night. He was later promoted to a position as a Doctor at the Imperial College and then to a Shangshu in the Ministry of Works.

    At that time, Emperor Chongzhen had already executed Wei Zhongxian, but eunuchs like Zhang Yixian soon took control of affairs. By this time, the rebel forces led by Li Zicheng were growing stronger. To increase internal revenue, the government ordered Zhang Yixian to oversee the finances and construction-related revenues and to build a separate office. Jin Xuan remarked, "This is the critical moment for the survival or destruction of the nation. How could we execute Wei Zhongxian and then appoint another Wei Zhongxian? Moreover, as a Shangshu in the Ministry of Works, I will inevitably be forced to comply with these policies." He submitted a memorial opposing the decision, stating that Zhang Yixian, having a separate office, would compel officials from the Ministry of Revenue and the Ministry of Works to kowtow to him, undermining the dignity of scholars and disgracing the court. The memorial was not acknowledged, and the separate office was already established. As a result, officials were ordered to follow the protocol for visiting the Minister of Works. Jin Xuan submitted another memorial to firmly contest the decision, and the emperor's edict stated that since the matter concerned his duties, it was appropriate to pay respects, but no further visits were necessary, and Jin Xuan was not allowed to make a passionate appeal. Zhang Yixian was very pleased and discussed the reception of officials of the second rank with his associates. Someone said, "We should be slightly arrogant when receiving the Minister of Works." Zhang Yixian replied, "I will be slightly respectful, but I will treat Jin Xuan with arrogance."

    314

    金遂集诸郎官倡议曰：“职事可令椽吏移之，我曹有一人登彝宪堂，即属彝宪假子，毋许入孔子庙。当提我靴掷肿其面，辱之朝堂。”于是诸郎官诣尚书，各请以公事出。至期，彝宪坐堂皇，黄衫缇衣，倡赞毕，但见吏，不见郎官。曰：“谐尚书始来乎？待午乎？”久之，又不至，乃恚曰：“避金铉，不即来，待晚乎？”命小竖窃伺门外，望扇导来即报。已而马蹄前后过之，无一人入者，乃大惭愤。借验放十六门火器，诬指十八位无火门，劾以故误军机，曰：“必杀铉。”会尚书争之力，仅削籍归。

    Explanation

    314

    Gold gathered the officials of the various departments to propose: "Official duties can be delegated to lower-ranking clerks. If any one of us enters the Yiyuan Hall, we would then become his adopted sons and would not be allowed to enter the Temple of Confucius. We would pick up my boot and throw it at his face, humiliating him in the court." Thus, the officials went to the Ministry of Works, each requesting leave for official business. On the appointed day, Yiyuan sat in the hall, wearing ceremonial attire, and after the ceremonial opening, they only saw clerks, not the officials. He asked, "Did you come to the Ministry of Works at the beginning? Or are you waiting until noon?" After a long time, they still did not arrive. He angrily said, "Avoiding Jin Xuan, you are not coming. Are you waiting until evening?" He ordered a young servant to secretly watch outside the door and report immediately when someone with a fan arrives. Soon after, the sound of horses passing before and behind, not a single person entered. He was greatly ashamed and angry. He tested the firing of sixteen cannons, falsely accusing the eighteen positions of lacking firing mechanisms, impeaching for causing a military mistake, saying, "To definitely kill Jin Xuan." Fortunately, the Ministry of Works strongly opposed it, and he was only stripped of his rank and dismissed.

    315

    家居益与弟錝尽读所藏书，尤善《易》学。而父汀州太守显、母恭人章，更时时慰勉之。至父死，服阙，复起为兵部车驾司主事，分守皇城，益修城守火器。时崇祯十七年二月也。李自成已陷大同，而宣府镇方有太监杜勋监视。又上疏曰：“宣府京城之蔽，宣府不救，虑在京城。抚臣朱之冯忠勇足恃，恐受内臣之掣，请亟撤之，并撤居庸关监视。”不听。至三月，果闻杜勋以宣府迎贼，朱死之。因哭语弟錝：“目今我哭朱公，数日后汝曹旋哭我也。”

    Explanation

    315

    At home, he and his brother Zhen read all the books they had collected, with the elder brother being particularly skilled in the study of the Yi Jing (Book of Changes). His father, who was the governor of Tingzhou, and his mother, Lady Zhang, often comforted and encouraged him. When his father died, he completed his mourning period and was then appointed as a clerk in the Vehicle Department of the Ministry of War, responsible for guarding the imperial city. He further improved the city's defenses and weapons. It was February of the seventeenth year of the Chongzhen era. Li Zicheng had already captured Datong, and at the same time, the eunuch Du Xun was overseeing the Xuanfu garrison. He also submitted a memorial to the emperor saying, "Xuanfu is the shield of the capital city; if Xuanfu is not saved, the danger will be in the capital. The governor, Zhu Zhi Feng, is loyal and brave enough to rely on, but I fear he will be hindered by the eunuchs. I request that he be immediately recalled, along with the supervisor at Juyong Pass." The request was not heeded. By March, it was indeed reported that Du Xun had welcomed the rebels into Xuanfu, and Zhu Zhi Feng died as a result. He then wept and told his brother Zhen, "Now I am weeping for Zhu Gong, but in a few days, you will be weeping for me."

    316

    及贼至居庸关，太监杜之秩果复迎降，遂进薄彰义门城下。杜勋缒城上，入见大内，唯张皇贼势以逼帝，遍语诸珰，谓我党富贵自在云。忠洁则仓皇点禁兵，归谋匿母，因哭告母曰：“铉守皇城，城亡当与偕亡。今日从母乞此身殉王事。”母曰：“噫！久谓汝读书知大义，乃今始向我乞身哉？且我命妇，与汝偕勉之。汝魂归，可会我于井矣！”趣之出，又命仆追往，以朝衣随之。
    　　见贼入京城，杀监察御史王章于城上，王章亦武进人，字芳洲，与忠洁素厚。方为之欷歔数声，见市中宫人遍至，言贼入皇城，帝后已死社稷。欲趋入宫，又传闻提督京城太监王承恩从死，曰：“微独吾乡王御史也，若辈中尚有一人知大义者，我乃后之，不已为若笑耶？”遂衣朝衣，投御河死，死时有吕胖者，亦内监也，傫然而至，两手反接而睨视之，曰：“是金兵部耶？是人素不居我辈于人面，岂渠能死，吾独不能死哉？渠生欲远我，我偏近之！”亦自沉于此。

    Explanation

    316

    When the rebels reached the Juyong Pass, the eunuch Du Zhizhi indeed welcomed them and surrendered. They then advanced and encamped near the city walls of Changyi Gate. Du Xun climbed the city wall by rope, entered, and saw the imperial palace. He only exaggerated the power of the rebels to pressure the emperor, speaking to all the eunuchs and telling them that their party would have wealth and honor in the future. Zhongjie, in a state of panic, mobilized the palace guards and returned home to plan hiding his mother. He then cried and told her, "Xuan, who guards the imperial city, should die with it if it falls. Today, I ask you to allow me to sacrifice myself for the king's cause." His mother said, "Ah! I have long believed that you would understand the great principles after reading. Only now do you ask me for your life? And I, as a lady-in-waiting, will also strive with you. When your soul returns, you can meet me by the well!" She urged him to go out and ordered a servant to follow him, dressing in court robes.

    Seeing the rebels enter the capital, they killed the censor Wang Zhang on the city walls. Wang Zhang was also from Wujin, with the courtesy name Fangzhou, and was close friends with Zhongjie. He was sobbing several times when he saw palace women spread throughout the city, saying the rebels had entered the imperial city and the emperor and empress had already died for the state. He wanted to rush into the palace but also heard that the eunuch Wang Cheng'en, who supervised the capital, had followed the emperor in death. He said, "Not only is there a censor from my hometown, there is still one person among you who understands the great principles. I will follow after them, wouldn't you laugh at me?" He then dressed in his court robes and drowned in the imperial river. At the time of his death, there was a eunuch named Lü Pàng, who came limping with his hands tied behind his back, looking at him and saying, "Is he the Minister of War Jin? This person has always held us in contempt. If he can die, why can't I? If he wants to distance himself from me while alive, I will be close to him in death!" He also drowned himself there.

    317

    仆以奔告其母。母曰：“孝哉铉也，既信于王公，又能激吕监死，吾安可以诳铉？”急正冠帔，投井中，妾王氏随之下，遂与俱死。錝归，收葬毕，焚其书而长恸曰：“吾母乎！吾兄乎！此时会相见而相依乎！”哀号数日，又死井中。其后清兵至，家人请入皇城，求得忠洁尸，已与吕监骨相杂，不可分敛。而皇城又不得入榇。竟合两骸藁葬御河堤，而王御史之丧归里。
    　　［张山来曰：明末死于忠义者，较前代为独盛。特存此一编，以当清夜闻钟，发人深省。］ 核舟记　嘉善魏学洢子敬茅檐集
    　　明有奇巧人曰王叔远，能以径寸之木，为宫室器皿人物，以至鸟兽木石，罔不因势象形，各具情态。
    　　尝贻余核舟一，盖大苏泛赤壁云。舟首尾长约八分有奇，高可二黍许。中轩敞者为舱，篛篷覆之。旁开小窗，左右各四，共八扇。启窗而观，雕栏相望焉。闭之，则右刻“山高月小，水落石出”，左刻“清风徐来，水波不兴”；石青糁之。

    Explanation

    317

    Translation

    I rushed to inform my mother. She said, "How virtuous you are, Xuan! Having been trusted by the king, you could also incite the death of the censor, how can I deceive you?" She quickly adjusted her hat and robe, then threw herself into the well. My wife, Lady Wang, followed her down, and thus they both perished. When I returned, I completed the burial rites and burned his books, weeping bitterly and for a long time, saying, "My mother! My brother! Will we meet and rely on each other now?" I mourned for several days and then also threw myself into the well. Later, when the Qing forces arrived, my family asked to enter the imperial city to search for the body of the loyal and clean official. The body had already been mixed with the bones of the censor, making it impossible to separate them. Moreover, the imperial city did not allow the coffin to be brought in. In the end, the two bodies were buried together on the embankment of the Imperial River, while the funeral rites for the magistrate Wang were held in his hometown.

    [Ou Shanglai said: During the late Ming period, those who died for loyalty and righteousness were more numerous than in previous dynasties. This account is preserved to serve as a wake-up call, provoking deep reflection.]

    The Inscribed Boat on a Nut
    By Wei Xueyi of Jiashan, courtesy name Zijing, from the collection Moya Collection

    There was a skilled craftsman in the Ming Dynasty named Wang Shuyuan who could carve palaces, utensils, figures, and people on a piece of wood no more than an inch in diameter, as well as birds, beasts, trees, and stones, all shaped according to their forms, each with its own expression and posture.

    Once he gave me a wooden boat carved on a nut, which is said to be a boat of Su Shi traveling on the Red Cliff. The boat is about eight fen long and slightly more, and about two grains of millet high. The central part is open and spacious, serving as the cabin, covered with a bamboo mat. Small windows are opened on the sides, four on each side, totaling eight panels. To open the windows and look, carved railings face each other. To close them, the right panel is inscribed with "the mountain is high and the moon is small, the water has receded and the rocks are exposed," and the left panel with "the gentle breeze comes, and the water ripples do not rise." The stone blue pigment is sprinkled on it.

    318

    船头坐三人，中峨冠而多髯者为东坡，佛印居右，鲁直居左。苏、黄共阅一手卷，东坡右手执卷端，左手抚鲁直背；鲁直左手执卷末，右手指卷，如有所语。东坡现右足，鲁直现左足，各微侧。其两膝相比者，各隐卷底衣褶中。佛印绝类弥勒，袒胸露乳，矫首昂视，神情与苏、黄不属。卧右膝，诎右臂支船，而竖其左膝；左臂挂念珠倚之，珠可历历数也。舟尾横卧一楫，楫左右舟子各一人。居右者椎髻仰面，左手倚一衡木，右手攀右趾，若啸呼状。居左者右手执蒲葵扇，左手抚炉，炉上有壶，其人视端容寂，若听茶声然。
    　　其船背稍夷，则题名其上，文曰：“天启壬戌秋日，虞山王毅叔远甫刻。”细若蚊足，钩画了了，其色墨。又用篆章一，文曰：“初平山人”，其色丹。
    　　通计一舟，为人五，为窗八，为篛篷，为楫、为炉、为壶、为手卷、为念珠各一；对联题名并篆文，为字共三十有四；而计其长，曾不盈寸。盖简桃核修狭者为之。

    Explanation

    318

    At the bow of the boat sat three figures: the one in the middle, with a tall hat and many beards, was Su Dongpo; Buddha Print (Foyin) sat to his right, and Lu Zhi (Lu Ji) to his left. Su Dongpo and Lu Zhi were jointly viewing a handscroll. Su Dongpo held the end of the scroll with his right hand and touched Lu Zhi's back with his left hand; Lu Zhi held the end of the scroll with his left hand and pointed at it with his right finger, as if saying something. Su Dongpo showed his right foot, and Lu Zhi showed his left foot, each slightly turned. Their knees were close together, hidden in the folds of their clothes at the bottom of the scroll. Buddha Print resembled the Bodhisattva Maitreya very much, exposing his chest and breasts, with his head raised and looking up. His expression did not match that of Su Dongpo and Lu Zhi. He lay on his right knee, with his right arm bent to support the boat, and his left knee raised; his left arm hung with prayer beads, which could be counted clearly. At the stern of the boat, a paddle lay horizontally, with two boatmen on either side. The one on the right had a topknot and looked up, his left hand leaning on a horizontal stick, and his right hand grasping his right foot, as if shouting. The one on the left held a fan made of palm leaves with his right hand and touched a brazier with his left hand, with a pot on the brazier. The person looked straight ahead and appeared calm, as if listening to the sound of tea brewing.

    The back of the boat was slightly flat, and on it were inscribed the names: "In the autumn of Renxu in the Tianqi era, by Wang Yizhi of Yushan, carved." The writing was fine as mosquito legs, with clear strokes, and the color was black. Another seal was used, inscribed with "Chuping Mountain Man," and the color was red.

    In total, the boat included five figures, eight windows, a bamboo mat, a paddle, a brazier, a pot, a handscroll, and a string of prayer beads. The couplet, the inscription, and the seal text together made up thirty-four characters. But when measured, it was less than an inch long. It was carved from a narrow peach pit.

    319

    魏子详瞩既毕，诧曰：“嘻，技亦灵怪矣哉！《庄》《列》所载，称惊犹鬼神者良多，然谁有游削于不寸之质，而须麋瞭然者？假有人焉，举我言以复于我，亦必疑其诳，乃今亲睹之。由斯以观，棘刺之端，未必不可为母猴也。嘻！技亦灵怪矣哉！”
    　　［张山来曰：眼镜中有所谓显微镜者，一虱之细，视之大如枣栗。由此推之，则一核未尝不可视为东瓜矣。］ 沈孚中传　武林陆次云云士北墅绪言
    　　沈嵊，字孚中，居武林北墅。不修小节，越礼惊众。作填词，夺元人席。好纵酒，日走马苏、白两堤。髯如戟，衿未青，不屑意也。
    　　崇祯末年，当九日，携酒持螯，独上巾子蜂头，高吟浮白。有僧濡笔窃记其一联云：“有情花笑无情客，得意山看失意人。”为之叫绝。拉归精舍，痛饮达旦。

    Explanation

    319

    Hui Zi, having finished his careful observation, exclaimed in astonishment: "Indeed, the technique is truly wondrous and miraculous! As recorded in the works of Zhuangzi and Liezi, there are many things said to be as astonishing as ghosts and gods. Yet who has ever carved such intricate details on a piece of wood no thicker than an inch, with such clarity? If someone were to repeat what I say back to me, I would certainly doubt it. But now I have seen it with my own eyes. From this, we can see that it might be possible to carve a monkey from the tip of a thorn. Indeed, the technique is truly wondrous and miraculous!"

    [According to Zhang Shanlai: There is such a thing as a microscope in eyeglasses. A louse, when viewed through the microscope, appears as large as a date or chestnut. By this logic, it is not impossible to consider a seed as a melon.]

    Biography of Shen Fuzhong
    Lu Ciyun from Wulin (Hangzhou), North Garden

    Shen Sheng, known as Fuzhong, lived in North Garden, Wulin. He did not care about minor details and often shocked people by breaking etiquette. He wrote lyrics that surpassed those of the Yuan dynasty poets. He enjoyed drinking and riding horses along the Su and Bai dikes every day. With a beard like a spear, he did not bother to wear a scholar's robe, showing his disdain for conventional norms.

    In the late years of the Chongzhen era, on the ninth day of the ninth month, he carried wine and crabs, climbed alone to the top of the Jinzi Fengtou Hill, and recited poetry while drinking. A monk secretly wrote down one of his couplets: "Flowers smile at the heartless guest, those who are successful look at those who are unsuccessful." Shen Fuzhong exclaimed in admiration. He was taken back to the study and drank heavily until dawn.

    320

    家人觅至，曰：“今邑试，郎君何不介意耶？”嵊方醉睐未开，履无详步，扶入试院，则已几席纵横，置足无地。嵊乃积墨广砚，立身高级，大书《登高词》于粉壁之上。其首阕曰：“万峰顶上，险韵独拈糕。撑傲骨，与秋鏖，天涯谁是酒同僚？面皮虽老，尽生平受不起青山笑。难道他辟英雄一纸贤书，到做了禁登高三寸封条？”题毕而下，有拍其肩狂叫者曰：“我得一贤契矣！”嵊视之，则令也，潜视其后良久矣。令宋姓，兆和名，字禧公，云间名士，不屑为俗吏态者。把嵊臂曰：“昔贺监遇李白，为解金龟当酒。我虽远逊知章，君才何异太白？此日之事，今古攸同，盍拈是题，与君共填散曲，志奇遇乎？”嵊曰：“善！”令未成而嵊脱稿，更复击节，擢之冠军。荐之学使者，补弟子员，声誉大起。

    Explanation

    320

    Family members came to look for him, saying, "Today is the county examination. Why aren't you concerned, Young Master?" Sheng was still half-asleep, his steps unsteady. He was helped into the examination hall, where tables and chairs were scattered everywhere, leaving no place to stand. Sheng then gathered ink and spread out a large writing surface, standing in a prominent position, and wrote a large poem titled "Ascending Heights" on the whitewashed wall. The first stanza read: "At the summit of ten thousand peaks, I boldly choose a risky rhyme. With a proud spirit, I battle autumn. Who in the world is a drinking companion? Though my face is old, I have endured the laughter of green mountains throughout my life. How can a piece of paper praising heroes turn into a three-inch ban on ascending heights?" After finishing the poem, he stepped down. Someone slapped his shoulder and shouted wildly, "I have found a worthy disciple!" Sheng looked up and saw it was the magistrate, who had been observing him secretly for a long time. The magistrate's name was Song, with the courtesy name Zhaohe and the style name Xigong, a famous scholar from Yunjian who despised the ways of ordinary officials. Grasping Sheng's arm, he said, "In the past, when the magistrate met Li Bai, he offered him a precious object to buy wine. I may be far inferior to Zhang Ji, but your talent is no different from Li Bai. This day's events are as rare as they come, why don't we take this topic and fill a free-form poem together, commemorating this strange encounter?" Sheng replied, "Good!" Before the magistrate could finish his own poem, Sheng had already completed his, and the magistrate was so impressed that he awarded him the top prize. He recommended Sheng to the provincial examiner, who admitted him as a student, and Sheng's reputation soared.

    321

    嗣是非令醉嵊，即嵊醉令，交谊既狎，略师生而尔汝，更冠易服，戏乐不羁。嵊弟有讼，对簿于令，令佯为研鞫。嵊跃出厅事，大呼曰：“错矣！错矣！”令拂袖起。事闻直指，以白简斥令，令恬然勿怨也。
    　　明鼎既移，阁部马士英卷其残旅，遁迹西陵。嵊往谈兵，士英伪为壮语云：“当背城决胜。”嵊驰归语里人曰：“此地顷为战场矣。”里人群哗曰：“丞相宵奔，将军夜遁，谁能任战，欲殃吾民？”争击毙嵊，烧其著书，所存者，独《息宰河》《绾春园》传奇二种。《绾春园》尤为词场称艳云。
    　　陆次云曰：余童子时，尝从道中见孚中策骑过，有河朔少年风。及长，读其词而叹其死。语云：“凡人之死，有重于泰山，轻于鸿毛者。”孚中之死，鸿毛耶？泰山耶？吾乌能论定之？
    　　［张山来曰：文人不谙世务，是以为世所轻，稍不得意，辄作不平鸣。若止观其文，诚足令人敬之重之。甚矣全才之难也！］ 爱铁道人传　古黔陈鼎定九留溪外传

    Explanation

    321

    Final Translation

    If it was the magistrate who was drunk and scolding Shen, or Shen who was drunk and scolding the magistrate, their friendship was already intimate, and they disregarded the teacher-student relationship, calling each other by their names. They changed hats and clothes, playing and enjoying themselves without restraint. When Shen's younger brother had a lawsuit, he faced the magistrate in court, and the magistrate pretended to investigate. Shen leaped out of the hall, shouting, "Wrong! Wrong!" The magistrate rose up and left in anger. The matter was reported to the censor, who then criticized the magistrate with a white cane, but the magistrate remained calm and did not complain.

    After the fall of the Ming dynasty, Grand Secretary Ma Shiying gathered his remaining troops and fled to Xiling. Shen went to discuss military matters, and Ma Shiying feigned bold words, saying, "We should fight a decisive battle behind the city walls." Shen rushed back and told the villagers, "This place will soon become a battlefield." The villagers shouted in unison, "The chancellor fled at night, and the general retreated in the dark. Who can take responsibility for the battle? They want to harm our people!" They attacked and killed Shen, burned his writings, and what remained were only two types of legends: Xizaihe and Wanchun Yuan. Wanchun Yuan was especially praised in the literary world.

    Lu Ciyun said: When I was a child, I once saw Fu Zhong riding a horse on the road, with the demeanor of a young man from the northern regions. When I grew up, I read his writings and lamented his death. It is said, "The death of a person can be as heavy as Mount Tai or as light as a feather." Was Fu Zhong's death as light as a feather or as heavy as Mount Tai? How can I decide on this?

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: Scholars who do not understand worldly affairs are therefore looked down upon by the world. If they are slightly dissatisfied, they immediately express their discontent. If one only looks at their writings, they are indeed worthy of respect. How difficult it is to be a complete talent!] Aite Dao Ren Zhuan by Gu Qian Chen Dingjiu, Liu Xi Wai Zhuan.

    322

    爱铁道人，逸其姓名，云南人也。少时曾为郡诸生。明亡，即弃家为道士。冬夏无衣裈，唯以尺布掩下体。不火食，所食者，瓜蓏蔬果。滇中四时皆暖，虽腊月有鳞物，故道人竟辟谷。性爱铁，见铁辄喜，必膜拜，向人乞之。头项肩臂以至胸背腰足，皆悬败铁，行路则铮铮然如披铠，自号曰“爱铁道人”。久之，言人祸福多奇中，愚男女皆以神仙奉之。而道人亦遂以神仙自居，更号曰“爱铁神仙”。
    　　嗜饮，市人争醉以酒。妇人持酒与，则倾泼不饮。或诘之，则厉声曰：“若不闻孟圣人云：男女不亲授受乎？”于是神仙之名四走。有不远数十百里，来问吉凶。时道人寄迹破庙，日环绕门者数百人。道人大怒，骂曰：“我何神仙，我贪酒花子耳，知底吉凶？汝辈来问我？”即擎秽撒之，众乃散。

    Explanation

    322

    The Taoist Who Loves Iron

    The Taoist who loves iron, whose name is unknown, was from Yunnan. In his youth, he was a student at a local academy. When the Ming dynasty fell, he abandoned his family to become a Taoist priest. He went without clothes in winter and summer, only covering his lower body with a piece of cloth. He did not eat cooked food, subsisting on fruits, vegetables, and other plant-based foods. In Yunnan, the four seasons are warm, even in the coldest month of December, so the Taoist practiced辟谷. He loved iron and was delighted whenever he saw it, always worshipping it and begging for it from others. From his head to his shoulders, arms, and down to his chest, back, waist, and feet, he hung broken pieces of iron, making a clinking sound like armor as he walked. He called himself "the Taoist who loves iron."

    Over time, he often accurately predicted people's fortunes and misfortunes, and foolish men and women all revered him as a god. The Taoist also considered himself a god and took on the title "the Taoist god." He loved drinking, and people in the market competed to get him drunk. If a woman offered him wine, he would pour it out and not drink. If someone questioned him about this, he would sternly say, "Have you not heard Mencius say: 'Men and women should not directly hand things to each other?'"

    Thus, the name of the Taoist god spread far and wide. People traveled from far and wide, sometimes hundreds of miles, to ask him about good or bad luck. At the time, the Taoist was living in a dilapidated temple, and every day, hundreds of people would gather around the temple gate. The Taoist became very angry and shouted, "What god am I? I am just a beggar who loves drinking. How should I know about good or bad luck? You all come to ask me?" He then threw filth at them, and the crowd dispersed.

    323

    与蜀中铜袍道人张闲善。铜袍者，联铜片为衣而服之者也，故号曰“铜袍道人”。常携杖头钱，与爱铁饮与市，醉则歌呜呜，大恸而后休。甲寅乱，二人不知所往。
    　　外史氏曰：以铁为衣，以铜为袍，岂炫异以骇人耳目耶？抑道家别有所属，而寓意于铜铁耶？皆不可得而解也。
    　　［张山来曰：既有铁，便应有铜。爱金银者为贪夫，则爱铜铁者自是异人矣。］ 北墅奇书　陆次云云士大有奇书
    　　顺治时，山左有李神仙，游行京邸。庚子北直乡试，有两生密询试题。李笑曰：“公皆道德仁艺中人也，无庸卜。”题出，乃“志于道”全章，二人皆中式。辛丑会试，又有以场题问者，李曰：“五后四可。”场中首题，乃“知止而后有定”一节，果五“后”字；二题乃“夫子之文章”一章，三题乃“易其田畴”二节，果四“可”字。灵异最多，此特其一事耳。

    Explanation

    323

    A close friend of mine in Sichuan was a Taoist named Zhang Xian, known as the "Copper Robe Taoist" because he wore a robe made of copper plates. The copper robe was constructed by linking copper plates together to form a garment, hence his nickname. He often carried small change on the handle of his staff and would drink with a man named Ai Tie in the market. When he was drunk, he would sing mournfully, weep greatly, and then rest. During the Jia Yin upheaval, the two of them disappeared, and no one knew where they went.

    The historian commented: "Wearing iron as clothing and copper as a robe, is this to show off strangeness and shock people's eyes and ears? Or do the Taoists have a different affiliation, and do they convey meaning through copper and iron? All of this remains a mystery."

    [Commentary by Zhang Shanlai: If there is iron, there should be copper. Those who love gold and silver are greedy, while those who love copper and iron are extraordinary people.]

    The Strange Tales of the North Villa by Lu Ci Yun
    Lu Ci Yun's Great Collection of Strange Tales
    During the Shunzhi era, there was a man named Li, a so-called immortal, who traveled in the capital. In the year of Gengzi, during the provincial examination in the northern region, two candidates secretly asked him about the exam questions. Li laughed and said, "You are all people of virtue, morality, benevolence, and artistry; there is no need to guess." When the questions were revealed, they were the entire chapter on "Aspiring to the Way," and both candidates passed. In the following year, during the metropolitan examination, someone else asked him about the exam questions, and Li replied, "Five 'after' and four 'can'." The first question in the exam was indeed "Knowing when to stop, one can find stability," which contained five instances of the character "after"; the second question was "The articles of the Master," and the third question was "Changing the fields," which contained four instances of the character "can." Li had many miraculous abilities, and this was just one example.

    324

    ［张山来曰：先君视学山左时，李神仙来谒，自署曰“治仙”。先君延入署中，仙命人于架上随手取书一册，复令信手揭开，随于袖中取出字纸一条，乃其首行也。又云：“明日有贵人送礼至。”及次日，衍圣公以叵罗见赠。后不知所之矣。］
    　　陈我白瞽目，善揣骨。居扬州，吴江相国金岂凡召之。先令遍相诸人，多验。后及公，陈遍摸之，云：“此穷相，不足道。”公不语，傍人曰：“子无误言！”陈复遍摸，辄摇首曰：“不差！”公复不语。陈摸至公眼，遽跪曰：“此龙眼，当大贵！”众愕然。公笑曰：“果神相也。”重赠以金，复为延誉。盖公未生时，父翁祷于神庙，甚虔，夜梦神许赐以一子，视之，即寺傍丐者。私念有子如此，不如无矣。神复曰：“汝勿虑，当易其眼。”取殿庑龙眼纳之，未几生公。故公以为神也。
    　　［张山来曰：审若是，则富贵之后身，仍为富贵；乞丐之后身，仍不免贫贱耶？真不可解！］

    Explanation

    324

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: When my father was overseeing education in Shandong, the Daoist master Lǐ Shénxiān came to pay respects, and he signed himself as "Zhìxiān." My father invited him into the office, and the master instructed someone to randomly pick a book from the shelf, then to open it randomly. He then took out a piece of paper from his sleeve, which matched the first line of the book. He also said, "Tomorrow, a noble person will send a gift." On the next day, the Duke Yansheng presented a gift in the form of a "pǒluó." Afterward, no one knew what happened to the master.

    Chén Wǒbái, a blind man skilled in diagnosing by feeling bones, lived in Yangzhou. The high-ranking official from Wujiang, Jīn Qífán, summoned him. First, the official had Chén examine several people, and most of the diagnoses were accurate. Later, when Chén examined the official, he said, "This is a poor fortune, not worth mentioning." The official remained silent, and a bystander said, "You are not mistaken!" Chén repeated the examination and shook his head, saying, "It is correct!" The official remained silent again. When Chén examined the official's eyes, he suddenly knelt and said, "These are dragon eyes, you will be greatly honored!" The people were surprised. The official smiled and said, "Indeed, you are a divine fortune-teller." He then gave Chén a generous gift and further enhanced his reputation.

    The official's father had prayed at a temple with great sincerity and dreamed that a god promised him a son, who turned out to be a beggar boy near the temple. The father privately thought, "If I have a son like this, it would be better to have no son at all." The god replied, "Do not worry, I will change his eyes." The god took the dragon eyes from the temple and placed them in the beggar boy, who later became the official. Therefore, the official believed in the power of the god.

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: If this is true, then the descendants of the rich and noble will remain rich and noble, while the descendants of the poor and beggars will remain poor and lowly. This is truly incomprehensible!]

    325

    余卜居维扬时，陈我白已大富，不复为人揣骨，故无从一询休咎，闻其颇精于弈，目虽瞽，人不能欺之，尤为奇也。
    　　河南刘理顺，乡荐久不第。读书二郎庙中，闻哭声甚哀。问之，乃妇人也：其夫出外，七年不归，母贫且老，欲嫁媳以图两活，得远商银十二两，将携去。姑媳不忍别，故悲耳。刘闻之，急呼其仆曰：“取家中银十二两来。”仆曰：“家中乏用，止有纳粮银在，明早当投柜矣。”刘曰：“汝且取来，官银再设处可也。”因代为其子作一书，称离家七年，已获五百余金，十日后便归矣，先寄银十二两等语。觅人送其家，姑媳得银及书，以告商。商知其子在，取银去。越十日，其子果归，所得之银及所行之事，与书中适符。母以问子，子骇甚，但曰：“此神人怜我也！”唯每日拜谢天地而已。刘公是年会试，庙祝见二郎神亲送之，中崇祯甲戌状元。其子后于庙中见公题咏，乃知书银出自公手，举家往谢，公竟不认，尤不可及也。

    Explanation

    325

    When I chose to live in Weiyang, Chen I-po had already become very wealthy and no longer practiced fortune-telling, so I had no way to ask him about good or bad omens. However, I heard that he was quite skilled at chess, and despite being blind, he could not be deceived by others, which was particularly remarkable.

    Liu Lishun, from Henan, had been recommended for the imperial examination for a long time but had not yet passed. He was studying in the temple of Er Lang when he heard a woman's voice crying bitterly. He asked her, and she explained that her husband had been away for seven years and had not returned. Her mother was poor and old and wanted to marry off her daughter to support both of them. They had received twelve taels of silver from a distant merchant and were about to take it away. The mother and daughter were reluctant to part, which was the cause of their sorrow.

    Liu, upon hearing this, quickly called his servant and said, "Fetch twelve taels of silver from home." The servant replied, "We are short of funds at home; we only have the silver for taxes, which we will deposit in the treasury tomorrow." Liu said, "You can take it now; we can arrange for the official silver later." He then wrote a letter on behalf of his son, stating that he had been away for seven years, had earned more than five hundred taels of silver, and would return in ten days. He first sent twelve taels of silver. He found someone to deliver the letter and the silver to the woman's home. The mother and daughter received the silver and the letter and told the merchant. The merchant, knowing that his son was still alive, took the silver.

    Ten days later, his son indeed returned, and the events and the amount of money he had earned matched those described in the letter. The mother asked her son about the letter, and he was amazed, saying, "This must be a divine person who has shown mercy to us!" He only thanked heaven and earth every day. That year, Liu passed the imperial examination. The temple attendant saw Er Lang personally escorting him and he became the top-ranked candidate (zhuangyuan) in the year 1634 (Chongzhen Jia Xu). Later, his son saw Liu's inscription in the temple and realized that the letter was written by Liu himself. The entire family went to thank Liu, but he refused to acknowledge his role, which was especially admirable.

    326

    蓟门有人，新置茧袍一领，衣之过芦沟桥。值推车者碎其右袂，其人自顾，绝无一语。推车者跪而请曰：“小人误碎君服，贫不能偿，乞赐痛责以惩过。”衣者曰：“衣已碎矣，责尔何为？”拂袖竟去。推车者归，忽颠狂曰：“吾冤不能报矣！”邻人聚观，诘问其故。曰：“衣茧袍者为某，与我仇积前生。今日我数当尽，碎其衣，欲致其击我，我则随击而毙，使彼受法抵偿。而无如其不较也，吾如彼何哉？其量若此，吾怨已解。然彼于前世，尚负我五金。乞邻翁为我语彼，持此金来，资我殡事，我则与彼释此冤矣。”邻人走访，详语其人。其人大惊，拜推车汉于破坑之下。推车汉历叙前因，碎衣者浃汗，叩求上五金偿夙负。复上五金，曰：“以此为君祈福，修佛事。”推车汉曰：“如是，吾不唯不汝冤，且汝德矣！”一笑而逝。

    Explanation

    326

    A Man in Jixian Gate

    In Jixian Gate, there was a man who had recently acquired a silk robe. Wearing it, he crossed the Lugou Bridge. Just then, a cart-pusher accidentally broke the right sleeve of his robe. The man glanced at it without saying a word. The cart-pusher knelt down and asked, "I mistakenly broke your clothes, but being poor, I cannot compensate. Please punish me severely to make amends for my mistake." The man replied, "The clothes are already broken; what good will punishing you do?" He waved his sleeves and left immediately. The cart-pusher returned home and suddenly went mad, saying, "My grudge cannot be avenged!" Neighbors gathered to watch and questioned him about the reason. He explained, "The man wearing the silk robe is someone, and we have a grudge accumulated from a past life. Today, my time to die has come. By breaking his clothes, I hoped to provoke him to hit me, and I would then hit back and die, so that he would suffer legal consequences. But since he does not seek retribution, what can I do to him? Such is his capacity, and my grudge is resolved. However, in a past life, he still owes me five taels of gold. I beg the old neighbor to speak for me and ask him to bring this gold to fund my funeral. Then I will resolve this grudge with him." The neighbors went to visit the man and explained the situation in detail. The man was greatly surprised and bowed to the cart-pusher at the bottom of the pit. The cart-pusher then detailed the cause, and the man who broke the clothes sweated profusely, kneeling and asking for the gold to repay the old debt. The man gave him five taels of gold and said, "Use this to pray for your good fortune and perform Buddhist rituals." The cart-pusher replied, "In this way, not only will I not hold a grudge against you, but I will also be grateful to you!" He smiled and passed away.

    327

    顺治戊戌进士汤聘，为诸生时，家贫奉母。忽病死，鬼卒拘至东岳。聘哀吁曰：“老母在堂，无人侍养，望帝怜之！”岳帝曰：“汝命止此，冥法森严，难徇汝意。”聘扳案哀号。帝曰：“既是儒家弟子，送孔圣人裁夺。”鬼卒押至宣圣处，曰：“生死隶东岳，功名隶文昌，我不与焉。”回遇大士，哀诉求生。大士曰：“孝思也，盍允之以警世？”鬼卒曰：“彼死数日，尸腐奈何？”大士命善财取牟尼泥完其尸。善财取泥，若旃檀香，同至其家，尸果腐烂，一灯荧然，老母垂涕，死七日，尚无以殓。善财以泥围尸，臭秽顿息，遂有生气。魂归其中，身即蠕动。张目见母，呜咽不禁。母惊狂叫，邻人咸集。聘曰：“母勿怖，男再生矣！”备言再生之故，曰：“男本无功名，命限已尽，求报亲恩，大士命男持戒，许男成进士，但命无禄位，戒以勿仕。”后聘及第，长斋绣佛，事母而已。迨母死，就真定令，卒于官，岂违勿仕之戒欤？

    Explanation

    327

    During the Shunzhi era, in the year Wu Xu, Tang Ping passed the imperial examination as a Jinshi. When he was a student, he was poor and supported his mother. Suddenly, he fell ill and died. Ghost officials detained him and took him to the Eastern Peak. Tang Ping pleaded in grief, saying, "My old mother is still alive, with no one to take care of her. I hope the emperor will have pity on me!" The god of the Eastern Peak replied, "Your life ends here. The strict laws of the underworld make it difficult to follow your wish." Tang Ping clutched the table and wailed. The god said, "Since you are a disciple of Confucianism, send you to Confucius for judgment." The ghost officials took him to the Confucius temple, saying, "Life and death are under the jurisdiction of Dongyue, and official career is under the jurisdiction of Wen Chang. It is not within my jurisdiction." On the way back, he met the Great Bodhisattva, who pleaded for a chance to live. The Bodhisattva said, "It is an act of filial piety. Why not allow it to warn the world?" The ghost official said, "He has been dead for several days. What about his decaying body?" The Bodhisattva ordered Shanci to fetch the clay of Buddha to restore his body. Shanci fetched the clay, which smelled like sandalwood. Together they went to his home, where his body was indeed decaying. A dim light was flickering, and the old mother was weeping. He had been dead for seven days, and still no one could bury him. Shanci surrounded his body with the clay, the stench disappeared, and he regained vitality. His soul returned to his body, and he began to move. He opened his eyes and saw his mother, sobbing uncontrollably. The mother was shocked and screamed, and neighbors gathered. Tang Ping said, "Mother, do not be afraid. I have been reborn!" He explained the reason for his rebirth, saying, "I originally had no official career, and my fate was already determined. To repay my parents' kindness, the Bodhisattva ordered me to observe precepts and allowed me to become a Jinshi. But my fate does not include a salary or position, and I was warned not to serve in office." Later, he passed the imperial examination, observed long-term vegetarianism, made embroidered Buddhas, and took care of his mother. Until his mother died, he became the magistrate of Zhen Ding and died in office. Did he violate the warning not to serve in office?

    328

    ［张山来曰：大士慨发慈悲，吾夫子独不为裁夺者，以死数日而复生，是为索隐行怪，非中庸之道，故不为耳。］

    Explanation

    328

    The Great Bodhisattva was deeply moved by compassion. Why did our Master, Confucius, not make a judgment on this matter? The person died for several days and then came back to life. This is considered seeking obscure and strange things, which is not in line with the Way of the Mean. Therefore, Confucius did not judge it.

    329

    顺天江霞子云：其母舅汪公，于崇祯十三年任四川巡道。经略到省，单骑往谒，中途所乘马无病而死。蜀道难行，计无所出。忽有少年对马言曰：“我当变马与公乘之。”左右以为奸人，拥至公前。公云：“此狂人也。”释之。少年出门去，而马忽活。公喜甚，乘之，至辕门，甫下马而复倒矣。公入谒，事毕，乘肩舆归。方行，见一老者牵一人至，喊云：“救命！”视其人，即少年也。老者云：“适见公乘马死，小人随藏身山穴，变马负公。出马腹而寻身，不意宅舍竟为此人所占，伏乞敕彼更换，各还故有。”公语少年，少年云：“此系难得之物，愿受官刑，断不还矣。”公欲绳之以法，而无法可加。老者知不可强，拳詈交及，少年唯有笑受。公劝老者：“尔有此手段，不若另觅好舍何如？”老者曰：“公肯为某留心，某当从命。”少年拜谢去，老者亦随公回署。

    Explanation

    329

    Shuntian Jiang Xiazi said: His maternal uncle, Mr. Wang, was appointed as the governor of Sichuan in the 13th year of the Chongzhen era. When the military commissioner arrived in the province, Mr. Wang rode alone to pay respects. On the way, the horse he was riding died without any apparent illness. The journey through the treacherous Sichuan roads left him with no solution. Suddenly, a youth spoke to the dead horse, saying, "I will transform into a horse for you to ride." Those around thought him a rogue and brought him before Mr. Wang. Mr. Wang said, "This man is mad," and had him released. The youth left, and the horse suddenly came back to life. Mr. Wang was overjoyed and rode it. Upon reaching the gate, as soon as he dismounted, the horse collapsed again. Mr. Wang entered to pay respects, completed his business, and returned home in a sedan chair. As he was traveling, he saw an elderly man dragging someone and shouting, "Save me!" Looking at the person, it was the same youth. The elderly man said, "Just now I saw the public official's horse die. I hid in a cave and transformed into a horse to carry the official. After leaving the horse's body, I found my own, but unexpectedly, the residence was taken by this person. I beg you to command him to exchange, and each can return to what is originally his." Mr. Wang spoke to the youth, who replied, "This is a rare thing, and I am willing to accept official punishment, but I will not give it back." Mr. Wang wanted to punish him by law, but found no way to do so. The elderly man knew he could not force him and alternately beat and cursed him, but the youth only laughed. Mr. Wang advised the elderly man, "You have this ability; why not find another good place instead?" The elderly man said, "If you will take care of me, I will obey." The youth bowed and thanked before leaving, and the elderly man also followed Mr. Wang back to the office.

    330

    越半载，一日向公云：“公书吏之子，今夜暴亡，明晨弗令掩盖，使移置郊外，当拜公佳舍之惠。”公许之。明早升堂，问某吏：“可有子昨夜死否？”吏曰：“有之。”公云：“汝欲令其重生否？”吏曰：“安能得之？”公云：“汝命无子，虽生必命出家，不则生而复死。”吏曰：“与其死隔，宁使生离。”公令其舁之郊外，吏泣谢去。公归语老者，老者求一新衣，随公出郭。吏夫妇已先迎候，观者万众。见老者扶尸起，脱其衣，以己衣衣其身，随脱己衣，以其衣衣自身。老者忽卧地，棺中人突然起矣，拜谢汪公。吏夫妇呼之，绝不应，亦唯有向之拜谢而已。吏夫妇痛哭去。是人遂作道人妆，虽若舞勺之年，而所出者尽神仙之语。谓公云：“时事不可问，宜急隐。”答曰：“君父事了却，稍俟之。”后再促公，公言如故。因叹云：“固有定数，不可强也！”遂辞去。明年寇大警，公卒于官。裘武宋口述。

    Explanation

    330

    After half a year, one day, the old man said to the official, "Your son, who is a clerk, died suddenly tonight. Tomorrow morning, do not allow him to be covered, but move him to the outskirts, and I will thank you for the kindness of your good house." The official agreed. The next morning, he ascended the hall and asked a certain clerk, "Did you have a son die last night?" The clerk replied, "Yes, he did." The official said, "Do you want to make him live again?" The clerk said, "How can I achieve it?" The official said, "Your fate has no son. Even if he is born, he will be destined to become a monk, or he will be born and then die." The clerk said, "Rather than being separated by death, I would prefer him to live apart." The official ordered him to carry the body to the outskirts, and the clerk wept and thanked him before leaving. The official returned home and told the old man, who asked for a new set of clothes and followed the official out of the city. The clerk's husband and wife had already been waiting to greet them, and there were thousands of spectators. They saw the old man lift the body up, take off its clothes, and dress it with his own clothes. Then he took off his own clothes and dressed himself in the clothes of the deceased. Suddenly, the old man lay down, and the person in the coffin suddenly stood up, bowing and thanking Mr. Wang. The clerk's husband and wife called to him, but he did not respond, only bowing and thanking them. The clerk's husband and wife wept and left. The person then became a Taoist, though he appeared to be in his youth, and all he said were words of immortals. He told the official, "Current affairs cannot be inquired about; it is advisable to hide quickly." The official replied, "Your father's affairs are settled; wait for a little while." The person urged the official again, but the official remained the same. The official sighed and said, "There is a fixed destiny; it cannot be forced!" Then he left. The next year, there was a great alarm from the enemy, and the official died in office. This story was narrated orally by Qiu Wu Song.

    331

    明末，关东有为玉器之工李宛者，白皙无髭之人也。其里中有张远者，长髯倾黑之人也。宛、远俱抱病，宛先三日死，远后三日死。宛至冥，冥官曰：“张合死，李犹未也，放转生。”鬼卒曰：“李舍坏矣！”冥官曰：“即借张舍舍之。”鬼卒送宛魂附远体而去。尸忽起，远之父惊喜曰：“儿生矣！”妻曰：“夫活矣！”子曰：“父能动矣！”宛张目曰：“我李宛也。此何地，尔何人，而子我夫我父我耶？”竟趋李宅，李阖家怪而逐之。

    Explanation

    331

    During the late Ming dynasty, in the eastern region of China, there was a craftsman named Li Ruan who made jade objects; he was a fair-skinned man without a beard. In his village, there was another person named Zhang Yuan, a man with a long black beard. Both Ruan and Yuan fell ill, and Ruan died three days before Yuan. After Ruan's death, his soul arrived in the underworld, where an underworld official said, "Zhang has died, but Li has not yet; allow him to be reborn." A ghost soldier replied, "Li's body is damaged!" The underworld official then said, "Use Zhang's body instead." The ghost soldier sent Ruan's soul to attach to Zhang's body and left. Zhang's body suddenly rose, and his father, surprised and happy, exclaimed, "My son is alive!" His wife said, "My husband is alive!" His son said, "My father can move!" Ruan opened his eyes and said, "I am Li Ruan. Where am I? Who are you? And why do you call me your son, your husband, and your father?" He then went to Li's house, but Li's entire family was surprised and chased him away.

    332

    宛曰：“我李宛也，父何以不我子？妻何以不我夫？子何以不我父耶？”其父曰：“我子死且腐，我子无髭，而尔多髯，大异矣！何诡说耶？”宛曰：“此张远之躯，冥曹判而假我生者也，盍辨我之声乎？”其家人曰：“声果宛声也。”张之父子追至，亦曰：“声诚非远声也。”而李之家究不敢纳也。宛曰：“不信，试取我器具来。”须臾，剖玉磨滤，为璧为珪，事事俱宛之素艺，远所不能者。于是信其果为宛也。张不能强之归，李不复驱之去。此王艾衲游边，云亲见其事者。

    Explanation

    332

    "宛 said, 'I am Li Wan, why does my father not recognize me as his son? Why does my wife not recognize me as her husband? Why does my son not recognize me as his father?' His father replied, 'My son has died and decayed, he had no beard, but you have a lot of facial hair, it is very strange! What kind of deceptive talk is this?' Wan said, 'This is Zhang Yuan's body, it was given to me by the officials of the underworld, let us verify my voice!' His family said, 'His voice is indeed Wan's voice.' Zhang's father and son arrived, and also said, 'His voice is not Zhang Yuan's voice.' But Li's family still dared not accept him. Wan said, 'If you don't believe me, try to get my tools here.' In a moment, he cut and polished jade, making it into a jade disc and a jade tablet, everything he did was something Wan had always been skilled at, which Zhang Yuan could not do. Therefore, they believed he was indeed Wan. Zhang could not force him to leave, and Li's family no longer tried to drive him away. This was told by Wang Ainuo, who claimed to have personally witnessed the events."

    333

    ［张山来曰：冥官亦舞文如此耶？虽与受贿者不同，然亦恐宜挂弹章也。
    　　不识李宛之妻肯与之同宿否？以白皙无髭之婿，而忽易以长髯倾黑之夫，能无怏怏？即张远之妇，见其夫复生，而为李宛之妻所踞，心能甘乎？俱不可解。］ 鬼母传　兴化李清映碧古今文绘
    　　鬼母者，某贾人妻也。同贾人客某所，既妊暴殒，以长路迢远，暂瘗隙地，未迎归。适肆有鬻饼者，每闻鸡起，即见一妇人把钱俟，轻步纤音，意态皇皇，盖无日不与星月侔者。店人问故，妇人怆然曰：“吾夫去身单，又无乳，每饥儿啼，夜辄中心如剜。母子恩深，故不避行露，急持啖儿耳。”

    Explanation

    333

    [Zhang Shanlai said: Do the underworld officials also manipulate the law in this way? Although it is different from bribery, it still warrants a formal complaint.
    I wonder if Li Wan's wife would be willing to share a bed with someone else. To suddenly replace a pale, clean-shaven husband with a bearded, dark-haired one, would she not feel discontent? If Zhang Yuan's wife sees her husband come back to life but is replaced by Li Wan's wife, could she be content? Both situations are incomprehensible.]
    Ghost Mother's Tale
    Xinghua Li Qingyinbing, Ancient and Modern Writings

    The Ghost Mother was the wife of a certain merchant. While traveling with her husband, she suddenly died in childbirth. Due to the long and distant journey, she was temporarily buried in a nearby field, and her body was not yet brought back home. Coincidentally, there was a woman selling pies at a stall. Every morning, as soon as the rooster crowed, she would see a woman holding money waiting in line. The woman walked lightly and spoke softly, her demeanor anxious and distressed, as if she were always competing with the stars and moon. The shop owner asked her why she was so distressed. The woman replied with sorrow, "My husband has left me alone, and I have no milk for our child. Whenever the child cries from hunger, I feel as though my heart is being torn apart at night. Out of deep maternal love, I cannot avoid the dangers of the road to quickly bring food to my child."

    334

    店中初聆言，亦不甚疑，但昼投钱于笥，暮必获纸钱一，疑焉。或曰：“是鬼物无疑。夫纸爇于火者，入水必浮，其体轻也。明旦盍取所持钱，悉面投水瓮，伺其浮者物色之。”店人如言，独妇钱浮耳。怪而踪迹其后，飘飘飏飏，迅若飞鸟，忽近小冢数十步，奄然没。
    　　店人毛发森竖，喘不续吁，亟走鸣之官。起柩视，衣骨烬矣，独见儿生。儿初见人时，犹手持饼啖，了无怖畏。及观者蝟集，语嘈嘈然，方惊啼。或左顾作投怀状，或右顾作攀衣势，盖犹认死母为生母，而呱呱若觅所依也。伤哉儿乎！人苦别生，儿苦别死！官怜之，急觅乳母饲，驰召其父。父到，抚儿哭曰：“似而母。”是夜儿梦中趯趯咿喔不成寐，若有人呜呜抱持者。明旦视儿衣半濡，宛然未燥，诀痕也。父伤感不已，携儿归。

    Explanation

    334

    At first hearing the words, they were not very suspicious, but during the day when they put money in the chest, at night they would always find a paper money. They found this strange.
    Someone said, "It is undoubtedly a ghostly thing. Paper burned by fire, when put in water, will surely float because its body is light. Why not take the money you carry, and throw all of it into the water jar, wait and see which one floats, and then observe it."
    The shop owner followed the advice, and only the wife's money floated. Finding it strange, they followed it. It floated and flew, swift like a bird, suddenly approaching a small tomb by several dozen steps, and suddenly disappeared.
    The shop owner's hair stood on end, breathing uneven, and they quickly ran to report to the officials. When the officials opened the coffin, clothes and bones were burned to ashes, but only seeing the child alive. The child, when first seeing people, was still holding a piece of cake to eat, completely without fear. When the onlookers gathered like hedgehogs, voices were noisy, then he cried in fright. Sometimes looking left, making a gesture to be held, sometimes looking right, making a gesture to grab clothes, as if he was mistaking the dead mother for the living one, crying as if seeking support. Alas, the child! People suffer from separation from the living, the child suffers from separation from the dead!
    The officials pitied him, quickly found a wet nurse to feed him, and hurriedly summoned his father. When the father arrived, embracing the child and crying, saying, "It looks like your mother." That night, in his dream, he kicked and made sounds, unable to sleep, as if someone was holding him gently. The next morning, seeing the child's clothes half wet, still not dried, it was the mark of a parting. The father was deeply moved and couldn't stop feeling sad, took the child back home.

    335

    后儿长，贸易江湖间，言笑饮食，与人不异。唯性轻跳，能于平地跃起，若凌虚然。说者犹谓得幽气云。儿孝，或询幽产始末，则走号旷野，目尽肿。
    　　［张山来曰：余向讶既已为鬼，亦安事楮镪为？今观此母，则其有需于此，无足怪矣。］ 狗皮道士传　黔中陈鼎定九留溪外传
    　　狗皮道士者，不知何许人，亦未详其姓氏。明末，尝冠道冠，蹑赤舄，披狗皮，乞食成都市。每至人家乞食，辄作犬吠声，酷相类。家犬闻之，以为真犬也，突出吠之。道士辄与对吠不休。邻犬闻之，亦以为真犬也，辄群集绕吠之。道士怒，忽作虎啸声，群犬皆辟易。每独居破庙，至深夜，辄作一犬吠形声，少顷，作众犬吠声，俨然百十犬相吠也。久之，通国之犬皆吠，而达乎四境矣。

    Explanation

    335

    The child grew up, trading in the world, speaking, laughing, eating, and drinking, no different from others. Only nature was light and agile, could jump up from the ground, as if flying through the air. Those who say still claim it gained an ethereal quality. The child was filial, and sometimes asked about the beginning and end of the ethereal, then ran and cried in the open field, with eyes swollen.

    [Zhang Shanlai said: I was previously surprised that already being a ghost, also what use for paper money? Now observing this mother, then there is a need here, nothing to be surprised about.] The Account of the Dog-Skin Taoist, External Transmission of Liu Xi by Chen Dingjiu from Qianzhong

    The dog-skin Taoist, whose origin is unknown, and whose surname and given name are also not known. At the end of the Ming dynasty, he often wore a Taoist hat, stepped on red shoes, wore a dog skin, and begged for food in Chengdu city. Whenever he went to people's homes to beg for food, he would make a dog barking sound, which was very similar. Household dogs hearing this, thought it was a real dog, and rushed out to bark at him. The Taoist would then bark back without stopping. Neighboring dogs hearing this, also thought it was a real dog, and would gather around to bark at him. The Taoist became angry and suddenly made a tiger's roar, causing all the dogs to retreat. Whenever he lived alone in a ruined temple, at midnight, he would make the sound and appearance of a single dog barking, and after a while, the sound of many dogs barking, as if a hundred or more dogs were barking at each other. After a long time, all the dogs in the country would bark, and the sound reached the four borders.

    336

    岁余，献贼入寇，道士突至贼马前数十步，大作犬吠声。献贼怒，令群贼策马逐杀之。道士故徐徐行，贼数策马，马不前。献賊益怒，令飞矢射之，如雨，皆不中。献贼益大怒，以为妖，亲策马射之，中其首不入，矢还中贼马，马毙。献贼大骇，乃已。
    　　他日献贼僭尊号，元旦朝贼百官，忽见道士披狗皮，列班行，执笏作犬吠声。献贼大怒，令群贼缚之。道士乃大作犬吠声，盈庭如数千百犬争吠状，声彻四外。合城之犬，闻声从而和吠之，声震天地。献贼大声呼，众皆不闻，为犬声乱也。献贼大惊而退。既退，犬声息，道士亦不知何往。

    Explanation

    336

    After more than a year, the Xianbei invaders invaded. Suddenly, the Taoist priest appeared before the Xianbei horses, several dozen steps away, making a loud barking sound. The Xianbei leader became angry and ordered his men to whip their horses to chase and kill the priest. The priest deliberately walked slowly, and the Xianbei repeatedly whipped their horses, but the horses did not move forward. The Xianbei leader became even more angry and ordered arrows to be shot, like rain, but all missed. The Xianbei leader became even more furious, considering the priest a demon, and personally whipped his horse to shoot. The arrow hit the priest's head but did not penetrate, and it rebounded, hitting the Xianbei horse, which died. The Xianbei leader was greatly frightened and stopped.

    Another day, the Xianbei leader usurped the imperial title. On New Year's Day, the Xianbei officials paid homage. Suddenly, the Taoist priest appeared, wearing a dog skin, lined up in ranks, holding a tablet and making a barking sound. The Xianbei leader became angry and ordered his men to bind the priest. The priest then made a loud barking sound, filling the courtyard as if thousands of dogs were barking in competition, and the sound reached all around. All the dogs in the city heard the sound and joined in barking, and the sound shook heaven and earth. The Xianbei leader shouted loudly, but no one heard him, as the noise of the dogs was too great. The Xianbei leader was greatly frightened and retreated. After retreating, the dog sounds ceased, and the Taoist priest was nowhere to be seen.

    337

    外史氏曰：世之言神仙者比比，余则疑信相半。今观狗皮道士之所为，岂非神仙哉？不然，何侮弄献贼如襁褓小儿哉？
    　　［张山来曰：人皮者不能吠贼，狗皮者反能之，可以人而不如狗乎？］ 烈狐传　贵州陈鼎定九留溪外传
    　　明末有狐，幻老人状，年可六、七十，诣昆山葛氏，欲僦其荒圃以居。葛谢以无屋，老人曰：“君第诺我，勿论屋有无也。”葛异而诺之。老人即与葛约曰：“我异类也，与君家有夙世缘，故相依耳。徙来，请戒从者勿相扰，则佩君高谊矣。”葛曰：“谨奉教。”乃去。
    　　越数日，老人投刺进谒曰：“徙来矣！”既至，从者数十人，皆衣裳楚楚，陈币悉珠玉锦绣，值数千缗。葛辞之，老人固让，葛然后纳其币。及去，达圃扉，即不见。葛愈异之，使人私瞷之，见圃内皆高堂大厦，画栋雕题，俨然缙绅家也。他日治酒招葛，樽俎之盛，帷幄之富，极人间之异。

    Explanation

    337

    The Historian says: In the world, those who speak of immortals are numerous, and I am equally divided between doubt and belief. Now, observing what the dog-skin Taoist priest does, could it not be the work of an immortal? Otherwise, how could he mock and play with the traitor as if he were a helpless child in swaddling clothes?

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: Those with human skin cannot bark at the traitor, but those with dog skin can, can humans be less effective than dogs?]

    The Legend of the Fiery Fox
    By Chén Dǐngdìng from Guìzhōu, Líu Xī Wàichuán

    In the late Ming dynasty, there was a fox that appeared as an old man, aged about sixty or seventy, and visited the family of Ge in Kunshan, wanting to rent a deserted garden to live in. Ge refused because he had no house, and the old man said, "Just agree to me, do not consider whether there is a house or not." Ge found it strange but agreed. The old man then told Ge, "I am a different kind, and I have a predestined connection with your family, so we rely on each other. When I move in, please instruct your servants not to disturb me, then I will appreciate your high regard." Ge said, "I will follow your instructions," and the old man left.

    After several days, the old man presented a card and paid a visit, saying, "I have moved in!" Upon arriving, he was accompanied by several dozen followers, all dressed neatly, and presented gifts of precious jewels and fine silk, worth several thousand strings of coins. Ge refused them, but the old man insisted on offering, and Ge then accepted the gifts. When they left, they reached the garden gate and then disappeared. Ge found it even more strange and sent someone to secretly observe, seeing inside the garden were grand halls and large buildings, with elaborately painted beams and carved decorations, looking like a family of scholars and officials. Another day, they prepared wine and invited Ge, with the abundance of food and drink, the richness of the tent and curtains, reaching the extreme of human marvels.

    338

    葛有子方弱冠，风流都雅倾一邑。偶过其居，见一丽人，年可十五、六，如海棠一枝，轻盈欲语。归而思之不置。久之，遂成病，且欲死。父知其情，走告老人以姻请。老人曰：“恐吾辈异类，不足以辱君子耳！”葛固请之，乃许。择吉迎之，奁赠以万计。既归，夫妇笃好，事舅姑甚孝。未几，国变，乱兵入其家，见妇艳，欲污之。妇大骂，夺刀自刭而死；乃一九尾狐也。
    　　外史氏曰：狐淫兽也，以淫媚人，死于狐者，不知其几矣。乃是狐竟能以节死，呜呼！可与贞白女子争烈矣！
    　　[张山来曰：曩于友人处，见小书一帙，皆纪妖狐故事。狐之多情者固不乏，南烈者则未之前闻。今得此文，可为淫兽增光矣！

    Explanation

    338

    Ge You, when in his early twenties, was a man of elegance and grace who captivated the entire county. Once, passing by her residence, he saw a beautiful woman, around fifteen or sixteen years old, like a branch of the hawthorn tree, graceful and poised as if about to speak. After returning, he couldn't stop thinking about her. After a long time, he fell ill and nearly died. His father, knowing his feelings, went to ask the old man for permission to marry her. The old man said, "I fear that we, being different beings, are not worthy of disgracing a gentleman!" Ge You insisted, and the old man finally agreed. They chose a good day to welcome her, and gifts worth tens of thousands were given. After they married, they were deeply affectionate and very filial to their in-laws. Not long after, there was a change in the country, and rebels entered their home. Seeing the woman's beauty, they wanted to defile her. She fiercely cursed them and took a knife, killing herself; it turned out she was a nine-tailed fox.

    The historian said: Foxes are lustful beasts, using their lust to seduce people, and many have died due to foxes, but it is hard to count. It turns out that even a fox can die for chastity! Alas, she can compete with chaste women in terms of purity!

    [Zhao Shanglai said: A while ago, I saw a small book at a friend's place, which recorded stories of magical foxes. There are many stories of foxes being emotionally involved, but stories of them being chaste are rarely heard of. Now that I have found this text, it can add luster to the reputation of these lustful beasts!]

    339

    葛翁肯与联姻，亦非寻常可及。狐之以烈报之固宜。]
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷十一
    　　过百龄传　锡山秦松龄留仙古今文绘
    　　　　锡固多佳山水，间生瑰闳奇特之士，常以道艺为世称述。若倪征君云林以画，华学士鸿山以诗，王佥事仲山以书，乃今过处士百龄者，则以弈。其为道不同，而其声称足以动当世则一也。
    　　　　百龄名文年，为邑名家子。生而颖慧，好读书。十一岁时，见人弈，则知虚实先后、进击退守之法。曰：“是无难也。”与人弈，弈辄胜。于是闾党间无不奇百龄者。时福唐叶阁学台山先生，弈品居第二。过锡山，求可与敌者，诸乡先生以百龄应召。至则尚童子也，叶公已奇之。及与弈，叶公辄负。诸乡先生耳语百龄曰：“叶公显者，若当阳负，何屡胜？”百龄色然曰：“弈固小技，然枉道媚人，吾耻焉；况叶公贤者也，岂以此罪童子耶？”叶公果益器之，欲与俱北，以学未竟辞。自是百龄之名，噪江以南。

    Explanation

    339

    Old Master Ge agrees to a marriage alliance, which is also not something ordinary can achieve. It is indeed fitting for the fox to report it with severity.

    The New Collection of Yuchu, Volume Eleven
    Biography of Guo Bailin by Qin Songling from Xishan, Collection of Ancient and Modern Writings

    Wuxi is indeed a place rich in beautiful landscapes, and it occasionally produces extraordinary and unique talents, often praised for their arts and skills. If Ni Zhengjun Yunlin for painting, Hua Xueshi Hongshan for poetry, Wang Qian Shi Zhongshan for calligraphy, and now Guo Bailin, then for chess. Although the methods are different, their reputation is sufficient to move the world, and they are all the same.

    Guo Bailin, whose name is Wen Nian, is a son of a prominent family in the county. He was born with exceptional intelligence and a love for reading. At the age of eleven, he saw people playing chess and understood the principles of virtual and real, advance and retreat, attack and defense. He said, "This is not difficult." When he played chess, he always won. Therefore, no one in the neighborhood was surprised by Guo Bailin's talent. At that time, Fu Tang Ye Taishan, a high-ranking official, was second in the chess world. Passing through Xishan, he sought someone to challenge him. The local scholars recommended Guo Bailin. When he arrived, he was still a child, but Ye Taishan was already impressed. When they played chess, Ye Taishan always lost. The local scholars whispered to Guo Bailin, "Ye Taishan is a prominent person. If you lose, why do you always win?" Guo Bailin replied, "Chess is a small skill, but if I compromise my principles to please others, I am ashamed. Besides, Ye Taishan is a virtuous person; how could he blame a child for this?" Ye Taishan was even more impressed and wanted to take him north with him, but Guo Bailin declined, saying he had not finished his studies. From then on, Guo Bailin's name spread throughout southern Jiang.

    340

    遂益殚精于弈。不几年，学成，曰：“可以应当世矣！”会京师诸公卿闻其名，有以书邀致者，遂至京师。有国手曰林符卿，老游公卿间，见百龄年少，意轻之。一日，诸公卿会饮，林君谓百龄曰：“吾与若同游京师，未尝一争道角技，即诸先生何所用吾与若耶？今愿毕其所长，博诸先生欢。”诸公卿皆曰：“诺！”遂争出注，约百缗。百龄固谢不敢。林君益骄，益強之，遂对弈。枰未半，林君面颊发赤热，而百龄信手以应，旁若无人。凡三战，林君三北。诸公卿哗然，曰：“林君向固称霸，今得过生，乃夺之矣！”复皆大笑。于是百龄棋品遂第一，名噪京师。

    Explanation

    340

    Therefore, he devoted all his efforts to chess. After a few years, he had mastered it, saying, "I am now worthy to compete in the world!" By chance, the high officials in the capital heard of his name, and there were those who invited him by letter. Therefore, he arrived in the capital. There was a national master named Lin Fuqing, who roamed among the high officials. Seeing Bai Ling, who was young, he thought little of him. One day, the high officials gathered for a drink, and Mr. Lin said to Bai Ling, "I and you have traveled together in the capital, but we have never once competed in skill. Then what use are we, me and you? Now I wish to fully demonstrate my talents, to win the joy of all the sirs." All the high officials said, "Yes!" Therefore, they competed to bet with a bet of a hundred strings of cash. Bai Ling firmly declined, saying he dared not. Mr. Lin became increasingly arrogant and forced him. Therefore, they played chess. Not halfway through the game, Mr. Lin's cheeks turned red and hot, but Bai Ling responded casually, as if no one else were there. In total three battles, Mr. Lin suffered three defeats. The high officials were in an uproar, saying, "Formerly, Mr. Lin firmly claimed dominance, but now encountering Bai Ling, he has now taken it away!" Again, they all laughed loudly. Therefore, Bai Ling's chess skill became first, and his name became famous in the capital.

    341

    当是时，居停主某锦衣者，以事系狱，或谓百龄曰：“君为锦衣客，须谨避，不然，祸将及。”百龄毅然曰：“锦衣遇我厚，今有难而去之，不义。且吾与之交，未尝干以私，祸必不及。”时同客锦衣者悉被系，百龄竟免。
    　　　　已天下多故，百龄不欲久留，遂归隐锡山。日与一二酒徒狂啸纵饮，不屑屑与人弈，独征逐角戏以为乐。百龄素贫，出游辄得数百金，辄尽之博簺。其戚党谯呵百龄，百龄曰：“吾向者家徒壁立，今所得资，俱以弈耳。得之弈，失之博，夫复何憾？且人生贵适志，区区逐利者何为？”噫，若百龄者，可谓奇矣！以相国之招而不去，以金吾之祸而不避，至知国家之倾覆而急归；为公卿门下客者垂四十年，而未尝有干请。若百龄者，仅谓之弈人乎哉！

    Explanation

    341

    At that time, a host who was a member of the Jinyiwei (Imperial Bodyguards) was imprisoned due to an incident. Someone warned Bailing, saying, "You are a guest of the Jinyiwei; you must be cautious and avoid them, otherwise, misfortune will befall you." Bailing firmly replied, "The Jinyiwei treated me well; to leave them in their time of trouble would be unjust. Moreover, I have never sought personal gain in our association, so misfortune will not reach me." At that time, all his fellow guests of the Jinyiwei were imprisoned, but Bailing finally escaped.

    Later, as the world was in turmoil, Bailing did not wish to stay long and thus returned to seclusion in Xishan. He would daily drink with a few wine companions, shouting and carousing, and did not bother to play chess with others. Instead, he only sought out and played dice games for amusement. Bailing had always been poor; whenever he traveled, he would earn several hundred gold coins, which he would immediately spend all on gambling. His relatives and friends scolded him, but Bailing said, "In the past, I was as poor as a man with nothing but the walls of his house. Now, all the wealth I have gained came from chess. I gained from chess, lost to gambling; what else is there to regret? Besides, life is about following one's heart; why pursue petty profits?" Ah, such as Bailing, can be called extraordinary! He refused the invitation of the Chancellor, did not avoid the disaster of the Jinyiwei, and hurriedly returned upon learning of the state's downfall. He served as a guest of high officials for nearly forty years, and never made any requests. Can such a person be merely called a chess player?

    342

    [张山来曰：善弈者多在垂髫，然其人往往嗇于寿。今过君独历四十余载，岂其命名为之兆耶？]
    　　八大山人传　江阴陈鼎定九留溪外传
    　　　　八大山人，明宁藩宗室，号人屋。“人屋”者，“广厦万间”之意也。性孤介，颖异绝伦。八岁即能诗，善书法，工篆刻，尤精绘事。尝写菡萏一枝，半开池中，败叶离披，横斜水面，生意勃然；张堂中，如清风徐来，香气常满室。又画龙，丈幅间蜿蜒升降，欲飞欲动；若使叶公见之，亦必大叫惊走也。善诙谐，喜议论，娓娓不倦，常倾倒四座。父某，亦工书画，名噪江右，然喑哑不能言。

    Explanation

    342

    Zhang Shanlai said: Those who excel at chess often do so at a young age, yet such individuals often have short lifespans. Now, Guo Jun has lived for over forty years. Is it not a sign of his name?

    Translation:

    Zhang Shanlai said: Those who excel at chess often do so at a young age, yet such individuals often have short lifespans. Now, Guo Jun has lived for over forty years. Is it not a sign of his name?

    八大山人传 江阴陈鼎定九留溪外传

    八大山人传

    八大山人, a member of the royal family of the Ning王府 during the Ming dynasty, was known by the pseudonym Renwu. "Renwu" means "a mansion with ten thousand rooms." He was known for his solitary and aloof nature, with exceptional talent that surpassed his peers. At the age of eight, he could already compose poetry, was skilled in calligraphy and seal carving, and particularly excelled in painting. Once, he painted a single branch of lotus, half-open in a pond, with scattered and fallen leaves, lying horizontally across the water, full of vitality. When hung in a hall, it seemed as if a gentle breeze was blowing, and the fragrance of the lotus filled the room. He also painted dragons, which wound and ascended across a large scroll, as if they were about to fly or move. If Ye Gong were to see them, he would surely scream and run away. He was good at humor and enjoyed discussing various topics, speaking eloquently without tiring, often leaving his audience in awe. His father, whose name is not mentioned, was also skilled in painting and calligraphy, famous in Jiangxi, but was mute and could not speak.

    343

    甲申国亡，父随卒。人屋承父志，亦喑哑。左右承事者，皆语以目：合则颔之，否则摇头。对宾客寒暄以手，听人言古今事，心会处，则哑然笑。如是十余年，遂弃家为僧，自号曰“雪个”。未几病颠，初则伏地呜咽，已而仰天大笑，笑巳，忽跿跔踊跃，叫号痛哭。或鼓腹高歌，或混舞于市，一日之间，颠态百出。市人恶其扰，醉之酒，则颠止。岁余，病间，更号曰“个山”。既而自摩其顶曰：“吾为僧矣，何可不以驴名？”遂更号曰“个山驴”。数年，妻子俱死。或谓之曰：“斩先人祀，非所以为人后也，子无畏乎？”个山驴遂慨然蓄发谋妻子，号“八大山人”。其言曰：“八大者，四方四隅，皆我为大，而无大于我也。”

    Explanation

    343

    In the year of Geng Shen, the nation perished, and his father died shortly after. Rén Wū, carrying on his father's will, also became mute. Those who served him on the left and right all communicated with him through gestures: if he agreed, he would nod; if not, he would shake his head. He greeted guests with gestures, and when he heard people talk about ancient and modern events, if he understood them, he would laugh silently. In this way, for more than ten years, he then abandoned his family and became a monk, giving himself the name 'Xue Ge' (Snow Piece). Not long after, he fell ill and became deranged. At first, he would crouch on the ground and weep, and then he would laugh loudly at the sky. After laughing, he would suddenly leap and dance, shouting and weeping bitterly. Sometimes he would sing loudly with his belly, sometimes he would dance wildly in the market. Within a day, he would display a hundred different deranged states. The people in the market disliked being disturbed by him, so they would get him drunk, and his derangement would stop. After more than a year, his illness improved, and he changed his name to 'Ge Shan' (Piece Mountain). He then rubbed his head and said, "Since I am a monk, how can I not name myself after a donkey?" He then changed his name to 'Ge Shan Lu' (Piece Mountain Donkey). Several years later, his wife and children all died. Someone told him, "Cutting off the ancestral worship is not what it means to be a descendant. Are you not afraid?" Ge Shan Lu then resolutely grew his hair and planned to marry a wife, and he gave himself the name 'Ba Da Shan Ren' (Great Mountain Man). He said, "Eight Great means that in all directions and corners, I am the greatest, and there is no one greater than me."

    344

    山人既嗜酒，无他好。人爱其笔墨，多置酒招之，预设墨汁数升、纸若干幅于座右。醉后见之，则欣然泼墨广幅间，或洒以敝帚，涂以败冠，盈纸肮脏，不可以目。然后捉笔渲染，或成山林，或成丘壑，花鸟竹石，无不入妙。如爱书，则攘臂搦管，狂叫大呼，洋洋洒洒，数十幅立就。醒时，欲求其片纸只字不可得，虽陈黄金百镒于前，勿顾也。其颠如此。
    　　　　外史氏曰：山人果颠也乎哉？何其笔墨雄豪也？余尝阅山人诗画，大有唐宋人气魄，至于书法，则胎骨于晋魏矣。问其乡人，皆曰得之醉后。呜呼！其醉可及也，其颠不可及也！

    Explanation

    344

    The recluse loves wine but has no other hobbies. People admire his calligraphy and paintings, often inviting him with wine and setting out several liters of ink and several sheets of paper on the right side of the seat. After drinking, seeing them, he joyfully splashes ink across broad sheets, sometimes splashing with old brooms and smearing with broken hats, making the paper filthy, unfit for viewing. Then he takes up the brush and paints, sometimes creating mountains and forests, sometimes valleys and ravines, flowers, birds, bamboo, and stones, all rendered masterfully. If he loves writing, he waves his arms and holds the brush, shouting wildly, writing expansively, completing dozens of sheets in an instant. When sober, one cannot find a single sheet or word of his, even if a hundred ingots of gold were offered, he would not look back. Such is his madness.

    The historian says: Is the recluse truly mad? How bold and powerful his brushwork is! I have often read the recluse's poems and paintings, which have the grandeur of Tang and Song masters. As for calligraphy, it is rooted in the Jin and Wei dynasties. Asking his fellow villagers, they all say it comes from after he drinks. Alas! His drunkenness can be imitated, but his madness cannot be.

    345

    [张山来曰：予闻山人在江右，往往为武人招入室中作画，或二三日不放归。山人辄遗矢堂中，武人不能耐，纵之归。后某抚军驰柬相邀，固辞不往。或问之，答曰：“彼武人何足较？遗矢得归可矣。今某公固风雅者也，不就见而召我，我岂可往见哉？”又闻其于便面上，大书一“哑”字，或其人不可与语，则举“哑”字示之。其画上所钤印，状如屐。予最爱其画，恨相去远，不能得也。]
    　　圆圆传　陆次云云士北墅绪言
    　　　　圆圆，陈姓，玉峰歌妓也，声甲天下之声，色甲天下之色。崇祯癸未岁，总兵吴三桂慕其名，赍千金往聘之，已先为田畹所得。时圆圆以不得事吴怏怏也，而吴更甚。田畹者，怀宗妃之父也，年耄矣。圆圆度《流水高山》之曲以歌之，畹每击节，不知其悼知音之希也。

    Explanation

    345

    Translation:

    Zhang Shanlai said: I have heard that the recluse in Jiangxi is often invited by military men into their rooms to paint, sometimes not allowing him to return for two or three days. The recluse would often defecate in the hall, and the military men could not bear it, so they let him go. Later, a certain military governor sent a letter inviting him, but he firmly declined to go. When asked about it, he replied: "Those military men are not worth comparing to. Being able to defecate and return is enough. Now, this gentleman is indeed a cultured person, but if he did not come to see me but summoned me, how could I go to see him?" I also heard that he would write a large 'mute' character on a small tablet, and if someone was not worth talking to, he would show them the 'mute' character. The seal on his paintings looks like a clog. I love his paintings the most, but I regret that we are far apart and unable to obtain them.

    Yuan Yuan's Biography by Lu Ci Yun Yunshi Beishu Xuyan

    Yuan Yuan, surnamed Chen, was a courtesan from Yufeng, known for her voice being the best in the world and her beauty unparalleled. In the year 1643 during the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, General Wu Sangui admired her name and took a thousand gold pieces to hire her, but she had already been obtained by Tian Wan. At the time, Yuan Yuan was unhappy not being able to serve Wu, and Wu was even more so. Tian Wan was the father of the concubine of Emperor Huizong (also known as Emperor Chongzhen) and was very old. Yuan Yuan sang the melody of 'Flowing Water and High Mountains,' and Tian Wan clapped in appreciation, unaware that it was mourning the rarity of true appreciation.

    346

    甲申春，流氛大炽，怀宗宵旰忧之，废寝食。妃谋所以解帝忧者于父，畹进圆圆。圆圆扫眉而入，冀邀一顾，帝穆然也。旋命之归畹第。
    　　　　时闯师将迫畿辅矣，帝急召三桂对平台，锡蟒玉，赐上方，托重寄命，守山海关。三桂亦慷慨受命，以忠贞自许也。而寇深矣！长安富贵家胥皇皇，畹忧甚，语圆圆。圆圆曰：“当世乱而公无所依，祸必至，曷不缔交于吴将军，庶缓急有借乎？”畹曰：“斯何时，吾欲与之缱绻，不暇也。”圆圆曰：“吴慕公家歌舞有时矣，公鉴于石尉，不借人看，设玉石焚时，能坚闭金谷耶？盍以此请？当必来，无却顾。”畹然之，遂躬迓吴观家乐。吴欲之而固却也，强而可。至则戎服临宴，俨然有不可犯之色。畹陈列益盛，礼益恭。

    Explanation

    346

    In the spring of the Jia Shen year, the rebel tide was raging, and the emperor, deeply concerned, neglected his sleep and meals. The concubine, planning with her father how to relieve the emperor's worries, had Wan present Yuan Yuan. Yuan Yuan swept her brows and entered, hoping to gain the emperor's favor, but he remained silent and solemn. Soon, he ordered her to return to Wan's residence.

    At that time, the rebel forces were about to besiege the capital region. The emperor urgently summoned Wu Sangui to the platform, awarded him the dragon robe and jade seal, granted him imperial favor, entrusted him with a heavy responsibility, and sent him to guard the Shanhai Pass. Wu Sangui also accepted the command with great enthusiasm, vowing to uphold loyalty and integrity. But the rebels were already deeply entrenched! The wealthy families in Chang'an were all in a state of panic. Wan was very worried and spoke to Yuan Yuan. Yuan Yuan said, "In times of chaos, if you have no support, disaster will surely come. Why don't you form an alliance with General Wu? Perhaps in times of need, you can rely on him." Wan replied, "Now is not the time for me to indulge in romantic entanglements." Yuan Yuan said, "General Wu has admired your family's music and dance for some time. You should learn from Shi Wei, who did not allow others to see his jade. If the jade and gold are burned, can you still keep them tightly shut in the Golden Valley? Why not use this as a reason to invite him? He will surely come, and you won't have to worry about anything." Wan agreed, and so he personally welcomed General Wu to see his family's entertainers. General Wu wanted to see her, but she firmly refused. Eventually, he was persuaded and came. When he arrived, he wore his military attire and appeared at the banquet with an imposing and unapproachable demeanor. Wan arranged an even more splendid display and showed even greater respect.

    347

    酒甫行，吴即欲去。畹屡易席，至邃室，出群姬调丝竹，皆殊秀。一淡妆者，统诸美而先众音，情艳意娇，三桂不觉其神移心荡也，遽命解戎服，易轻裘，顾谓畹曰：“此非所谓圆圆耶？洵足倾人城矣！公宁勿畏而拥此耶？”畹不知所答，命圆圆行酒。圆圆至席，吴语曰：“卿乐甚？”圆圆小语曰：“红拂尚不乐越公，矧不迨越公者耶？”吴颔之。酣饮间，警报踵至，吴似不欲行者，而不得不行。畹前席曰：“设寇至，将奈何？”吴遽曰：“能以圆圆见赠，吾当保公家，先于保国也。”畹勉许之。吴即命圆圆拜辞畹，择细马驮之去。畹爽然，无如何也。

    Explanation

    347

    The wine had just been served when Wu immediately wanted to leave. Wan repeatedly changed seats and eventually went to a secluded room, where he brought out a group of concubines to play musical instruments. All were exceptionally beautiful, but one, lightly dressed, led all the beauties and preceded all the sounds. She was lovely and charming, and Wu was unaware that his spirit was moved and his heart was agitated. He immediately ordered to remove his military attire and put on light clothing, then turned to Wan and said, "Is this not what is called Yuan Yuan? Indeed, it is enough to topple a city! Are you not afraid to possess such beauty?" Wan did not know how to respond and ordered Yuan Yuan to serve the wine. Yuan Yuan came to the seat, and Wu spoke in Wu dialect, "Are you enjoying yourself?" Yuan Yuan replied softly, "Red Brush still did not enjoy herself with Yue Gong, let alone those who are not as good as Yue Gong?" Wu nodded. During the merry drinking, warnings came one after another. Wu seemed reluctant to leave but had to leave. Wan moved forward and said, "If the enemy comes, what will we do?" Wu immediately said, "If you can give me Yuan Yuan, I will protect your family first, before protecting the country." Wan reluctantly agreed. Wu immediately ordered Yuan Yuan to bow and bid farewell to Wan, and chose a fine horse to carry her away. Wan was greatly disappointed and could do nothing about it.

    348

    帝促三桂出关，三桂父督理御营名骧者，恐帝闻其子载圓圆事，留府第，勿令往。三桂去，而闯贼旋拔城矣。怀宗死社稷。李自成据宫掖，宫人死者半，逸者半。自成询内监曰：“上苑三千，何无一国色耶？”内监曰：“先帝屏声色，鲜佳丽。有一圆圆者，绝世所希，田畹进帝，而帝却之。今闻畹赠三桂，三桂留之其父吴骧第中矣。”是时骧方降闯，闯即向骧索圆圆，且籍其家，而命其作书以招子也。骧俱从命，进圆圆。自成惊且喜，遽命歌。奏吴歈，自成蹙额曰：“何貌甚佳而音殊不可耐也！”即命群姬唱西调，操阮、筝、琥珀，己拍掌以和之，繁音激楚，热耳酸心。顾圆圆曰：“此乐何如？”圆圆曰：“此曲只应天上有，非南鄙之人所能及也！”自成甚嬖之，随遣使以银四万两犒三桂军。

    Explanation

    348

    The emperor urged Prince Wu Sangui to leave the pass. Prince Wu Sangui's father, named Xiang, who was in charge of the imperial camp, was afraid that the emperor would hear about his son's involvement with Yuan Yuan. He stayed in his mansion and did not allow Prince Wu to go. Prince Wu left, and soon the rebel bandits captured the city. Emperor Chongzhen died for the state. Li Zicheng occupied the palace, and half of the palace women died, while the other half escaped. Li Zicheng asked the eunuch, "There are three thousand women in the harem, why isn't there a single beauty?" The eunuch replied, "The late emperor avoided music and women, and there were few beautiful ones. There was one named Yuan Yuan, who was a beauty of unparalleled rarity. Tian Wan presented her to the emperor, but the emperor rejected her. Now I hear that Tian Wan gave her to Prince Wu, and Prince Wu kept her in his father's mansion." At that time, Xiang had just surrendered to Li Zicheng. Li Zicheng immediately demanded Yuan Yuan from Xiang, and also confiscated his property, ordering him to write a letter to summon his son. Xiang complied with all orders and presented Yuan Yuan. Li Zicheng was both surprised and delighted, and immediately ordered her to sing. She sang a Wu song, and Li Zicheng frowned and said, "Why is her appearance so beautiful but her voice so unbearable?" He then ordered the other concubines to sing Western tunes, playing the lute, zither, and huqin, while he clapped along. The complex and intense music was so loud and piercing that it made the ears hot and the heart ache. He looked at Yuan Yuan and said, "How is this music?" Yuan Yuan replied, "This music can only be found in heaven, not by people from the southern barbarians!" Li Zicheng greatly favored her and sent an envoy with 40,000 taels of silver to reward Prince Wu's army.

    349
