    三桂得父书，欣然受命矣，而一侦者至，询之曰：“我家无恙耶？”曰：“为闯籍矣！”曰：“吾至当自还也。”又一侦者至，曰：“吾父无恙耶？”曰：“为闯拘絷矣！”曰：“吾至当即释也。”又一侦者至，曰：“陈夫人无恙耶？”曰：“为闯得之矣！”三桂拔剑砍案曰：“果有是，吾从若耶？”因作书答父，略曰：“儿以父荫，待罪戎行，以为李贼猖狂，不久即当扑灭。不意我国无人，望风而靡。侧闻圣主晏驾，不胜眦裂！犹意吾父奋椎一击，誓不俱生，不则刎颈以殉国难。何乃隐忍偷生，训以非义？既无孝宽御寇之才，复愧平原骂贼之勇。父既不能为忠臣，儿安能为孝子乎？儿与父决，不早图贼，虽置父鼎俎旁以诱三桂，不顾也。”随效秦庭之泣，乞王师以剿巨寇，先败之于一片石。

    Explanation

    349

    Wu Sangui received his father's letter and gladly accepted his command. However, a scout arrived and asked him, "Is our family safe?" Wu Sangui replied, "It has been registered by Chong!" The scout said, "I will return when I arrive." Another scout arrived and asked, "Is my father safe?" Wu Sangui replied, "He has been detained by Chong!" The scout said, "I will be released when I arrive." A third scout arrived and asked, "Is Madam Chen safe?" Wu Sangui replied, "She has been taken by Chong!" Wu Sangui drew his sword and chopped the table, saying, "If it is true, will I follow you?" He then wrote a letter to his father, roughly stating: "I have been serving in the military under my father's protection, awaiting punishment. I believed that the bandit Li Zicheng would soon be defeated. I did not expect our country to have no one to stand up, but everyone surrendered at the sight of the enemy. I have heard that the Holy Emperor has passed away, and I cannot bear the tears in my eyes! I still hoped that my father would strike with a hammer and vow not to live together, or cut his own throat to die for the country's troubles. Why did he endure in silence and live secretly, teaching me something that is not right? Neither does he have the talent of Xiao Kuang to defend against the enemy, nor the bravery of Pingyuan to curse the bandits. If my father cannot be a loyal minister, how can I be a filial son? I make a final decision with my father: if we do not act against the bandits soon, even if my father is placed beside the cauldron and chopping block to lure me, I will not hesitate." He then mimicked the weeping of the Qin court, asking the imperial army to eliminate the great bandits, and planned to defeat them first at Yipianshi.

    350

    自成怒，戮吴骧并其家人三十余口，欲杀圆圆，圆圆曰：“闻吴将军卷甲来归矣，徒以妾故，又复兴兵。杀妾何足惜，恐其为王死敌不利也！”自成欲挈圆圆去。圆圆曰：“妾既事大王矣，岂不欲从大王行？恐吴将军以妾故而穷追不已也。王图之，度能敌彼，妾即褰裳跨征骑。”自成乃凝思。圆圆曰：“妾为大王计，宜留妾缓敌，当说彼不追，以报王之恩遇也。”自成然之，于是弃圆圆，载辎重，狼狈西行。是时也，闯胆已落，一鼓可灭。三桂复京师，急觅圆圆。既得，相与抱持，喜泣交集，不待圆圆为闯致说，自以为法戒追穷，听其纵逸而不复问矣。

    Explanation

    350

    Li Zicheng, enraged, executed Wu Xiang and over thirty members of his family. He intended to kill Duan Yanran, but she said, "I have heard that General Wu has rolled up his armor and returned. It is only because of me that he has once again raised arms. What is there to regret in killing me? I fear that if he dies fighting for the king's enemy, it will be detrimental!" Li Zicheng wanted to take Duan Yanran with him. She said, "I have already served the great king. How could I not wish to follow him? I fear that General Wu will relentlessly pursue us because of me. Your Majesty, consider this: if you can defeat him, I will lift my skirt and mount a warhorse to fight." Li Zicheng pondered her words. Duan Yanran said, "For the great king's sake, it would be best to leave me behind to delay the enemy. I will persuade them not to pursue, thereby repaying the king's kindness." Li Zicheng agreed, and so he abandoned Duan Yanran, loaded his supplies, and retreated in disarray to the west. At that time, Li Zicheng's courage had waned, and he could be defeated with a single attack. Wu Sangui recaptured Beijing and urgently sought Duan Yanran. Upon finding her, they embraced, their joy and tears mingling. Without waiting for Duan Yanran to explain, Wu Sangui decided to follow the law and stop the pursuit, allowing them to go free without further questioning.

    351

    旋受王封，建苏台、营郿邬于滇南，而时命圆圆歌。圆圆每歌《大风》之章以媚之。吴酒酣，恒拔剑起舞，作发扬蹈厉之容，圆圆即捧觞为寿，以为其神武不可一世也。吴益爱之，故专房之宠，数十年如一日。其蓄异志，作谦恭，阴结天下士，相传曰“多出于同梦之谋”。而世之不知者，以三桂能学申胥以复君父大仇，忠孝人也。曷知其乞师之故，盖在此而不在彼哉？厥后尊荣南面三十余年，又复浪沸潢池，致劳挞伐，跋扈艳妻，同归歼灭，何足以偿不子不臣之罪也哉？

    Explanation

    351

    Immediately after receiving the royal title, Wu built Su Terrace and constructed Mie Pavilion in the south of Dian (Yunnan). He often commanded Yuan Yuan to sing, and Yuan Yuan would often sing the chapter "Great Wind" to flatter him. When Wu was drunk, he would often draw his sword and dance, displaying a vigorous and fierce appearance. Yuan Yuan would then present a cup to toast him, thinking that his martial prowess was unmatched in the world. Wu's exclusive favor towards Yuan Yuan lasted for many years, as if it were just one day. He harbored secret ambitions, acted modestly, and secretly gathered scholars from all over the world. It is said that much of this was due to the same dream. Some people claimed that Wu could learn from Sun Shuao to avenge his lord and father, making him a person of loyalty and filial piety. However, the true reason for seeking aid was to cause trouble and rebellion, not to avenge his lord and father. Later, Wu enjoyed a life of honor and respect for over thirty years, but he again caused trouble and rebellion, leading to his destruction. His domineering and beautiful wife also met the same fate. How could this atone for the crimes of being neither a son nor a minister?

    352

    陆次云曰：语云“无征不信”，圆圆之说有征乎？曰：有。征诸吴梅村祭酒伟业之诗矣。梅村效《琵琶》《长恨》体，作《圆圓曲》以刺三桂曰：“冲冠一怒为红颜。”盖实录也。三桂赍重币，求去此诗，吴勿许。当其盛时，祭酒能显斥其非，却其赂遗而不顾，于甲寅之乱，似早有以见其微者。呜呼！梅村非诗史之董狐也哉！
    　　　　[张山来曰：吴三桂未叛时，予读祭酒《圆圆曲》，不解所谓。甲寅后，友人因为予言其故，深服先生先见之明。今读此传，益知《圆圆曲》之妙也。
    　　　　又曰：唐陈鸿作《长恨传》，白居易因谱为歌。今云士乃因歌作传，详略之际，较之前人稍难，诚足辉映后先矣。]
    　　啸翁传　陈鼎定九留溪外传
    　　　　啸翁者，歙州长啸老人汪京，字紫庭，善啸，而年又最高，故人皆呼为“啸翁”也。

    Explanation

    352

    Lu Ci Yun said: It is said that "without evidence, it is not credible." Is there evidence for the story of Yuan Yuan? The answer is yes. The evidence can be found in the poetry of Wu Mei Cun, a scholar and poet named Wei Ye. Wu Mei Cun imitated the style of the "Pipa" and "Long Sorrow" and composed the "Yuan Yuan Qu" to criticize Wu San Gui, saying: "A furious head for a beautiful face." This is a factual record. Wu San Gui brought heavy bribes, requesting the removal of this poem, but Wu did not agree. When Wu San Gui was at the height of his power, the scholar openly criticized his faults, refused the bribes, and paid no heed to them. During the Jia Yin Rebellion, it seems that he had already seen the signs of his downfall. Alas! Wu Mei Cun is not the Dong Hu of poetry and history!

    [Chang Shan Lai said: When Wu San Gui had not yet rebelled, I read the "Yuan Yuan Qu" by the scholar and did not understand its meaning. After the Jia Yin Rebellion, a friend explained its significance to me, and I deeply admired the scholar's foresight. Now, reading this biography, I better understand the brilliance of the "Yuan Yuan Qu."

    He also said: In the Tang Dynasty, Chen Hong wrote the "Long Sorrow," and Bai Juyi composed a song based on it. Today, Yun Shi wrote a biography based on the song, which is more difficult to handle in terms of detail and brevity compared to previous works. Truly, it shines equally in both past and present.]

    The Biography of Xiao翁 by Chen Ding Jiu, a Retired Scholar from Liu Xi

    Xiao翁 was a long whistling elder from Shezhou, named Wang Jing, with the courtesy name Zi Ting. He was skilled in whistling and was the oldest in age, so people called him "Xiao翁."

    Translation by:

    Assistant: Lu Ci Yun said: It is said that "without evidence, it is not credible." Is there evidence for the story of Yuan Yuan? The answer is yes. The evidence can be found in the poetry of Wu Mei Cun, a scholar and poet named Wei Ye. Wu Mei Cun imitated the style of the "Pipa" and "Long Sorrow" and composed the "Yuan Yuan Qu" to criticize Wu San Gui, saying: "A furious head for a beautiful face." This is a factual record. Wu San Gui brought heavy bribes, requesting the removal of this poem, but Wu did not agree. When Wu San Gui was at the height of his power, the scholar openly criticized his faults, refused the bribes, and paid no heed to them. During the Jia Yin Rebellion, it seems that he had already seen the signs of his downfall. Alas! Wu Mei Cun is not the Dong Hu of poetry and history!

    [Chang Shan Lai said: When Wu San Gui had not yet rebelled, I read the "Yuan Yuan Qu" by the scholar and did not understand its meaning. After the Jia Yin Rebellion, a friend explained its significance to me, and I deeply admired the scholar's foresight. Now, reading this biography, I better understand the brilliance of the "Yuan Yuan Qu."

    He also said: In the Tang Dynasty, Chen Hong wrote the "Long Sorrow," and Bai Juyi composed a song based on it. Today, Yun Shi wrote a biography based on the song, which is more difficult to handle in terms of detail and brevity compared to previous works. Truly, it shines equally in both past and present.]

    The Biography of Xiao翁 by Chen Ding Jiu, a Retired Scholar from Liu Xi

    Xiao翁 was a long whistling elder from Shezhou, named Wang Jing, with the courtesy name Zi Ting. He was skilled in whistling and was the oldest in age, so people called him "Xiao翁."

    353

    啸翁尝于清夜独登高峰颠，豁然长啸，山鸣谷应，林木震动。禽鸟惊飞，虎豹骇走，山中人已寐者，梦陡然醒；未寐者，心悚然惧，疑为山崩地震，皆彷徨罔敢寝。达旦，群相惊问，乃知为啸翁发啸也。啸翁之啸，幼传自“啸仙”。能作鸾鹤凤凰鸣，每一发声，则百鸟回翔，鸡鹜皆舞。又善作者龙吟，醉卧大江滨，长吟数声，鱼虾皆破浪来朝，鼋鼍多迎涛以拜。
    　　　　他日，与黄鹤山樵、天都瞎汉，潇湘渔夫、虎头将军十数辈，登平山六一楼，拉啸翁啸。啸翁以齿落固辞，强而后可。初发声，如空山铁笛，音韵悠扬。既而如鹤唳长天，声彻霄汉。少顷，移声向东，则风从西来，蒿莱尽伏，排闼击户，危楼欲动。再而移声向西，则风从东至，訚然荡然，如千军万马，驰骤于前，又若两军相角，短兵长剑紧接之势。久之，则屋瓦欲飞，林木将拔也。于时炷香烬，而啸翁气竭，昏仆于地。众客大惊，亟呼山僧，灌以沸水，半晌乃苏。——归而月印前溪矣。

    Explanation

    353

    The Howler's howl once occurred on a clear night when he ascended alone to the peak of a high mountain. Suddenly, he let out a loud howl, and the mountains echoed, the valleys responded, and the trees trembled. Birds took flight in fright, tigers and leopards fled in terror. Those in the mountains who were already asleep suddenly woke from their dreams; those who were not asleep felt a sudden fear, suspecting it to be an earthquake or landslide, and all hesitated and dared not sleep. Until dawn, they all asked in alarm, and then realized it was the howling of the howler. The howler's howl, learned from a young age from the "Howling Immortal," could imitate the calls of phoenixes and cranes. Every time he made a sound, hundreds of birds circled, and chickens and ducks danced. He was also skilled in imitating the roar of a dragon. Lying drunk by the great river, after a few long howls, fish and shrimp broke the waves to pay homage, and turtles and alligators met the tide to bow.

    One day, with the Yellow Crane Mountain Hermit, the Blind Man of Tiandu, the Fisher of Xiaoxiang, and the Tiger-Headed General and others, they climbed the Six-First Floor of Ping Mountain and pulled the howler to howl. The howler firmly refused due to tooth loss, but was forced to agree. At first, the sound was like a bamboo flute in an empty mountain, melodious and resonant. Then it was like the cry of a crane in the vast sky, echoing through the heavens. A moment later, shifting the sound eastward, the wind came from the west, flattening all vegetation, pushing doors and windows, and nearly shaking the tall building. Then shifting the sound westward, the wind came from the east, creating a calm and gentle effect, like a thousand troops and horses galloping, and as if two armies were clashing with short and long weapons. After a while, the tiles seemed to fly off, and the trees were about to be uprooted. At that time, the incense burned out, and the howler fainted from exhaustion. The guests were greatly alarmed, quickly calling a mountain monk, who gave him boiling water, and he revived after a while. They returned, and the moon reflected on the front stream.

    354

    啸翁能医，工画，善歌；垂八十，声犹绕梁云。
    　　　　外史氏曰：古善啸者称孙登，嗣后寥寥，不见书传。迨至我朝，称善啸者，洛下王、昭阳李而已。然予尝一闻之矣。第未知与苏门同一音响否？昨闻啸翁之啸，则有变风云、动山岳之势，大非洛下者可几及也。岂啸翁之啸，直接苏门者耶？
    　　　　[张山来曰：予遇啸翁，欲闻其啸，翁以齿豁辞，不意其在平山发如许高兴。惜予不及知也！]
    　　客窗涉笔　伕名 大有奇书选本

    Explanation

    354

    The Whistling Master is skilled in medicine, painting, and singing. Approaching eighty, his voice still lingers like a lingering echo.

    The outer historian says: In ancient times, those skilled in whistling were known as Sun Deng. After that, there are few, and they are not recorded in books. Until our dynasty, those known for their whistling skills are only Wang of Lu and Li of Zhaoyang. Yet, I have once heard it. But it is unknown if it is the same sound as Su Men. Yesterday, I heard the Whistling Master's whistling, which has the power to change the weather, move mountains. Far surpasses what the Lu people can achieve. Is it that the Whistling Master's whistling directly inherits from Su Men?

    [O Zhang Shanlai says: I met the Whistling Master, but he claimed to be toothless and thus unable to whistle. Unsurprisingly, I did not get to know it!]

    Guest Window Note by the Master
    The Great Book of Oddities Selection

    355

    康熙间，天津城外有旅店。其后一室，夜多鬼，店主键其门。时有优人至其家，人无宿处，欲入此室。店主告以故。其扮净者云：“无惧，吾能服之。”众饮酒，半醉，各归寝。扮净者取笔涂赤面，着袍靴，装关公。丑涂墨面，持刀装周仓；小生白面，持印作关平，左右立。关正坐，点烛若看兵书状。顷之，炕后一少妇出，前跪呼冤。装关公者，心慑不能言。扮周仓者，厉声问：“有何冤？可诉上！”妇指炕者再，周又厉声云：“汝且去，明日当伸若冤。”妇拜谢，忽隐去。至明日，三人启炕砖视之，下果有一尸。询店主，云：“此屋本一富家者，前年迁去，某赁之，其邻右云，屋主向有一妾，后不复见，殆冤死耶？”众云：“今夜必复至，当细询之。”至夜，三人仍装像于室，众伏户外伺之。初更，妇人又自炕后出，怒指三人云：“吾以汝为真关君，特与诉冤。汝辈何能了吾事？”乃披发吐舌灭灯而去。众大惊，三人不敢复入室。

    Explanation

    355

    During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, there was an inn outside the city of Tianjin. In one of the rooms, ghosts were often seen at night, so the innkeeper locked it. One day, some performers came to the inn and had no place to stay, so they wanted to enter this room. The innkeeper told them why the room was locked. The actor playing the comedic role said, "Do not be afraid, I can subdue them." They drank together, got slightly drunk, and went to bed. The actor playing the comedic role took a brush and painted his face red, put on a robe and boots, and dressed up as Guan Yu. Another actor painted his face black, held a knife, and dressed up as Zhou Cang; a third actor wore a white face, held a seal, and dressed up as Guan Ping, standing on either side. Guan Yu sat in the center, lit a candle, and pretended to read military books. Soon after, a young woman appeared from behind the kang, knelt down, and cried out her grievances. The actor playing Guan Yu was so frightened that he could not speak. The actor playing Zhou Cang shouted sternly, "What is your grievance? You may tell me!" The woman pointed to the kang twice, and Zhou Cang shouted again, "You may go now, but I will clear your grievances tomorrow." The woman bowed in thanks and suddenly disappeared. The next day, the three actors removed the bricks from the kang and found a body underneath. They asked the innkeeper, who said, "This room used to belong to a wealthy family who moved out last year, and I rented it. My neighbor said that the owner used to have a concubine who disappeared later, possibly dying a tragic death." The group said, "She will definitely come back tonight, and we should ask her more detailed questions." That night, the three actors dressed up again in the room, and the others hid outside to wait. At the first watch, the woman appeared again from behind the kang, angrily pointing at the three actors and saying, "I thought you were the real Guan Yu, so I came to tell you my grievances. What can you do about my situation?" She then pulled her hair loose, stuck out her tongue, and extinguished the light before leaving. Everyone was greatly alarmed, and the three actors were too scared to enter the room again.

    356

    [张山来曰：此鬼谬矣！即非真关君，宁不可借其力以鸣于官而究其冤耶？]
    　　　　康小范言其伯父讳元积者，顺治辛丑进士，自幼能知前事。方诞生时，与同辈三人，皆沙门中道履坚粹者。冥主赐以进贤冠，绣紫衣，礼而遣之。至一桥，有以杯茗进，同辈饮之，某独疑而置之，遂别去。某困诸生久，每思及此，曰：“吾既紫绣来，阎老非谑我者！”后登进士，谢恩之日，班次中遇两同年，面目宛然当日两僧与偕来者。询之两君，则皆惘然，想即桥上杯茗为之蔽也。

    Explanation

    356

    Zhang Shanlai said, "This ghost is mistaken! Even if it is not the real Lord Guan, why can't we use its power to appeal to the authorities and clear the injustice?"

    Kang Xiaofan spoke of his uncle, whose name was Yuan Ji, who passed the imperial examination as a Jinshi in the year 1661 (the year of Xin Chou during the Shunzhi era). From a young age, he could recall past events. At the time of his birth, he was with three peers, all of whom were among the most steadfast and pure in the Buddhist community. The ruler of the underworld bestowed upon them hats of learning and embroidered purple robes, sending them off with respect. They came to a bridge where someone offered cups of tea. The others drank it, but I alone doubted and put it aside, then left separately. I have been struggling as a scholar for a long time, and whenever I think of this, I say, "I came with the purple robes; Lord Yan is not joking with me!" Later, I passed the imperial examination, and on the day of thanking the emperor, I met two of the same year in the ranks. Their faces were exactly like those of the two monks who came with me. I asked the two gentlemen, and they were all bewildered. I think it was the tea on the bridge that caused this.

    357

    崇祯末，张献忠屠戮楚中。麻城人为贼所杀，魂走川中，不自知其死也，急欲东归。每至途中，辄为风吹转。夜行三载，终不得归。于是闻风声，即伏地握草木根，乃不复回。将至故邑，城门尚闭，于岳庙后少憩。见有一神，奉簿登殿，向岳帝云：“与麻城梅某一子。”帝云：“此人孽重，不得有子。”神又云：“天曹所命，不敢违。”判官持一簿向帝云：“梅某于某日，见一冻人，买一草束烘之得活，是当得子。”帝云：“可将坐庙旁人与之。”四五人拽是人行。是人呼云：“我人也，何投胎之有？”众笑曰：“汝是人，何畏风夜行耶？”是人始悟已为鬼。至殿上，又云：“某即投胎，不愿之梅某家，向识其人，何可为若儿？”判官云：“但往为若儿，有好处。”是人记所言，数人押至梅某家。梅某妇产一儿，即能言，家人以为怪，欲杀之。儿述前生，并托生事，梅惊异。于是力行善，抚子成人，今尚在也。康熙丙辰二月，施溥霖言之。

    Explanation

    357

    At the end of the Chongzhen era, Zhang Xianzhong massacred the people of Hubei. A man from Macheng was killed by the rebels, and his soul wandered to Sichuan, unaware that he was dead, eager to return eastward. Whenever he reached a path, he was blown back by the wind. He traveled at night for three years, never able to return. When he heard the wind, he would crouch on the ground, grasping the roots of grass and trees, and would no longer be blown away. As he approached his hometown, the city gates were still closed, and he rested briefly behind the temple of the God of Mount Heng. He saw a god who held a register and ascended to the hall, saying to the God of Mount Heng, "Grant a child to Mei from Macheng." The god replied, "This man has heavy sins and should not have a child." The god then said, "It is ordered by the Heavenly Court; I dare not disobey." The judge held a register and said to the god, "On a certain day, Mei saw a frozen person, bought a bundle of straw to warm him, and saved his life. He should be granted a child." The god said, "You may grant the child to the person sitting beside the temple." Four or five people dragged the person away. The person cried out, "I am a human, how can I reincarnate?" The crowd laughed and said, "You are a human, why do you fear the wind at night?" The person then realized he was a ghost. Upon reaching the hall, he said, "If I am to reincarnate, I do not want to be reborn into Mei's family. I know him, how can I be his son?" The judge said, "Just go and be his son, it will be beneficial." The person remembered what was said, and several people escorted him to Mei's family. Mei's wife gave birth to a child who could speak immediately, and the family thought it was strange and wanted to kill him. The child recounted his past life and the details of his rebirth, and Mei was astonished. Thereafter, he practiced virtue, raised the child to adulthood, and the child is still alive today. In the third month of the year 1676, Shi Pulin recounted this story.

    358

    [张山来曰：方岳帝未奉天曹命时，梅某妇已有孕矣，岂预知有投胎者耶？此与回生者胸前微温，同不可解也。]

    Explanation

    358

    Zhang Shanlai said: When the Mountain God had not yet received the command from Heaven, a woman surnamed Mei was already pregnant. How could one have known in advance about someone being reborn? This is equally inexplicable as the slight warmth on the chest of someone who has been revived.

    359

    闻见卮言　嘉善顾珵美辉六大有奇书选本
    　　　　顺治甲午正月，四明一士人金良者召仙。仙大书乩云：“解元金良”。士人大喜，及开榜，解元乃钟朗也。盖“钟”字旁有“金”字，“朗”字字旁有“良”字，神仙之游戏耳。然金君于次科亦即中式。
    　　　　晋时义兴善权寺，雷震其柱，题字凡三：一曰“诗米汉”，一曰“射钩记”，一曰“谢君之”；皆大书，可径尺，非篆非隶，深入木理。正统间，周文襄公命试削之，字随削而入。乡人摹搨，云佩之可以愈瘧。宋祥符间，岳州玉贞观，雷书一柱曰“谢大仙人”。问乩仙，曰：“雷神之名也。”本朝顺治间，福州饥，昼锦坊有卖米者，雷震死其三人，有字大书尸上，其文曰“兴口月癶康口月癶昼”。无人识者。人题之于万寿塔壁。夜有蜘蛛垂丝于字之中，直贯而下，视之，乃“米中用水，康中用木查”九字也。询知其人平日果然，天诛不爽矣。

    Explanation

    359

    Random Words from Hearsay and Observation: A Collection of Extraordinary Books by Gu Chengmei Hui of Jiaxing

    In the first month of the Jia Wu year of the Shunzhi era (1644), a scholar from Ningbo named Jin Liang summoned a spirit. The spirit wrote in large characters on the spirit tablet, "Jin Liang, the top scorer." The scholar was overjoyed, but when the results were announced, the top scorer was actually Zhong Lang. It turned out that the character "Zhong" contains the character "jin" (gold), and the character "Lang" contains the character "liang" (good), which was a playful trick by the gods. However, Jin Liang also passed the next examination.

    During the Jin dynasty, in the Shanyuan Temple of Yixing, lightning struck one of its pillars, and three inscriptions were left: one read "Poetry Rice Han," another "Shoot Hook Record," and the third "Thank You, Sir." All were written in large characters, about a foot in size, and were neither seal script nor clerical script, deeply carved into the wood. During the Zhengtong era, Zhou Wenxiang, the Duke of Wen, ordered an attempt to scrape them off, but the characters simply sank deeper into the wood. Locals made copies of them, claiming that wearing them could cure malaria.

    During the Xiangfu era of the Song dynasty, at the Yuzhen Temple in Yuezhou, lightning wrote on a pillar, "Thank the Great Immortal." When asked by a spirit medium, it was replied, "It is the name of the thunder god." During the Shunzhi era of the Qing dynasty, there was a famine in Fuzhou. In the Dayujin Lane, three people selling rice were struck by lightning and killed. On their bodies were written large characters, reading "Xing Kou Yue Bai Kang Kou Yue Bai Zheu." No one could decipher the meaning. Someone wrote the inscription on the wall of the Wanshou Pagoda. At night, a spider hung a thread through the middle of the characters, descending straight down. Upon closer inspection, the characters read "Use water in the rice, use wood in the medicine." It was found that the people indeed practiced this method in their daily lives, and the divine punishment was just.

    360

    [张山来曰：予曩在鸠兹市上，曾见破书一帙，所记皆雷事。其中雷书甚多，以其近于荒唐，未之购也。由今思之，仍当以数十文买之。今亦不知在否矣。]
    　　樵书　萧山来集之元成大有奇书选本
    　　　　樵川吴生善请仙。顺治丁酉，督学岁试将及，数子问场中题，书曰：“尹字带儿孙，一旦不离心。”复问：“次题出经题否？”曰：“否否否！”比入试，首题是“得见君子者斯可矣。”至“得见有恒者斯可矣”，乃知“尹字儿孙”，“君子”也；“一旦心”，“恒”字也。次题“乐正子强乎”三段，三“否”字也。同时有武学生，亦问试题，书四语曰：“二人并肩，不缺一边。立见其可，十字撤添。”及入试，论题乃“天下奇才”四字。始悟“二人并肩”，“天”也；“不缺一边”，“下”也；“立见其可”，“奇”也；“十字撤添”，才也。拆字巧妙如此，非仙语不能到也。

    Explanation

    360

    Zhang Shanlai said: "In the past, while I was in Juzi Market, I once saw a tattered book, the contents of which were all about thunder-related matters. There were many such books, but because they were close to being absurd, I did not buy it. Reflecting on it now, I should have bought it for several coins. I don’t know if it’s still there now."

    From the Qiao Shu (Woodcutter’s Books), collected by Xiao Shanlai and selected by Yuan Chengda.

    A woodcutter from Qiao Prefecture, Wu Sheng, was skilled in summoning spirits. In the Dingyou year of the Shunzhi era, the annual examination by the provincial supervisor was approaching. Several students asked about the exam questions, and the writing said, "The character '尹' (Yin) carries its descendants; never separate from the heart." They asked again, "Will the next question be from the classics?" The writing replied, "No, no, no!" When they entered the exam, the first question was, "To see a gentleman is sufficient." Later, they realized that "尹字儿孙" referred to "gentlemen," and "一旦心" referred to the character "恒" (constancy). The next question, "Is Le Zhengzi strong?" in three sections, contained three "no" characters. At the same time, a student of martial arts also asked about the exam questions, and the writing said four words: "Two people standing side by side, not missing one side. Immediately see its possibility, ten characters added or removed." When they entered the exam, the essay topic was "Extraordinary talent in the world." They then understood that "二人并肩" referred to "天" (heaven), "不缺一边" referred to "下" (below), "立见其可" referred to "奇" (extraordinary), and "十字撤添" referred to "才" (talent). The art of character decomposition is so clever that it could only be achieved through the words of a spirit.

    361

    康熙己酉科，山阴袁显襄，叩乩仙，问场中题目。批云：“不可语。”曰：“然则终无一言耶？”曰：“题目即在不可语上。”曰：“乞明示之。”批一“署”字。出题乃“知之者”一节，有四“者”字，且在“不可语上”一章之上。袁遂获隽。
    　　　　贵州番民杂处，多閟术，能以木易人之足。有郡丞某过其地，记室二人从游其地，寓于客邸。一人与妇人淫，其夫怨之，易其一足。一人不与妇淫，其妻怨之，易其一足。明日踯躅于庭。丞知，逮其人，始邀归作法，而足如故。

    Explanation

    361

    During the Kangxi 6th year (1667), Yuan Xianxiang from Shanyin consulted a medium, asking about the examination questions. The medium replied, "It cannot be spoken." Yuan asked, "Then is there no word at all?" The medium responded, "The question is contained within the phrase 'It cannot be spoken.'" Yuan then requested, "Please clearly indicate it." The medium wrote the character "署." The question turned out to be the passage "Those who know" from the Analects, which contains four instances of the character "者," and it was also found in the chapter titled "It cannot be spoken." Yuan subsequently passed the examination.

    In Guizhou, where ethnic minorities live together, there are many secret arts, including the practice of replacing a person's foot with wood. A certain county magistrate traveled through the region, accompanied by two secretaries who stayed at a guesthouse. One of the secretaries engaged in sexual relations with a woman, and her husband, resentful, replaced one of his foot with wood. Another secretary did not engage in such relations, but his wife, resentful, replaced one of his foot with wood. The next day, he limped in the courtyard. The county magistrate learned of this and arrested the person, inviting them back to perform a ritual, but the foot remained as it was.

    362

    [张山来曰：淫其妇而仅易其足，可谓罪重而罚轻矣。]
    　　钱塘于生三世事记　毗陵陈玉璂椒峰学文堂集
    　　　　钱塘于生某，忠肃公裔孙也。笃行，不妄言。虽盛暑不解衣带。每沐浴，必深自蔽匿。人怪之。一日浴昭庆寺僧寮，同学蔡生者，排户逼视，见其两腋间，肌寸许，左豕右蛇，豕鬣而蚴，蛇麟麟然。
    　　　　生泣下，已乃曰：“此予三生业也，于今犹不忘。予初为豕，甚憎其生，既就死，极梃刃汤火，神识终不去。已为蟒蛇，在岩穴下，自顾狞恶，时掩藏则口苦饥；百虫啐腥，附于甲，立啖尽。已念业益重，间日食一大禽。又念杀生无已时，誓日饮水。又念毒涎入水杀鱼蚌，误饮人杀人，慨然曰：『生而害生，曷不死？』遂引首于山，曝烈日中以死。见冥官，曰：『汝有人性，重生命，舍生。当拔汝为人。』”言罢，生又泣曰：“予未尝以告妻子，今亦无用自匿矣。”蔡闻言悚然，因语于李九来，笔之书。

    Explanation

    362

    [张山来曰：淫其妇而仅易其足，可谓罪重而罚轻矣。]
    　　钱塘于生三世事记　毗陵陈玉璂椒峰学文堂集
    　　　　钱塘于生某，忠肃公裔孙也。笃行，不妄言。虽盛暑不解衣带。每沐浴，必深自蔽匿。人怪之。一日浴昭庆寺僧寮，同学蔡生者，排户逼视，见其两腋间，肌寸许，左豕右蛇，豕鬣而蚴，蛇麟麟然。
    　　　　生泣下，已乃曰：“此予三生业也，于今犹不忘。予初为豕，甚憎其生，既就死，极梃刃汤火，神识终不去。已为蟒蛇，在岩穴下，自顾狞恶，时掩藏则口苦饥；百虫啐腥，附于甲，立啖尽。已念业益重，间日食一大禽。又念杀生无已时，誓日饮水。又念毒涎入水杀鱼蚌，误饮人杀人，慨然曰：『生而害生，曷不死？』遂引首于山，曝烈日中以死。见冥官，曰：『汝有人性，重生命，舍生。当拔汝为人。』”言罢，生又泣曰：“予未尝以告妻子，今亦无用自匿矣。”蔡闻言悚然，因语于李九来，笔之书。

    Final Translation

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: "To indulge in sexual relations with one's wife and merely change her feet can be considered a severe crime with a light punishment."]

    The Three Lives of Yu from Qiantang
    Collected in Jiaofeng Xuewen Tang Ji by Chen Yuqi of Piling

    Yu from Qiantang, a descendant of the loyal and upright official, was known for his steadfast actions and truthful speech. Even in the height of summer, he would not untie his belt. Whenever he bathed, he would hide himself deeply, which puzzled others. One day, while bathing in a temple hall at Zhaoguang Temple, a fellow student named Cai forced open the door and looked inside, seeing that between his armpits, a small patch of flesh on the left resembled a pig and on the right a snake, with pig bristles writhing and snake scales shimmering.

    Yu wept, then said, "This is my karma from three lives, and I still remember it today. I was first a pig, and I deeply hated being alive. After death, I endured extreme torture with weapons, fire, and water, but my spirit never departed. Then I became a python, living in a cave, looking at myself and seeing how fierce and ugly I was. When I hid, I felt hungry; all insects were repelled by the smell, clinging to my scales and being eaten immediately. I realized my karma had grown heavier, so I ate a large bird every few days. Then I thought about killing living things endlessly, and I swore to drink only water. But I also thought that my poisonous saliva in the water could kill fish and clams, and if people drank it, they would die. I said to myself, 'Living and harming life, why not die?' So I pulled my head out into the mountains, exposed to the scorching sun, and died. I met the officials of the underworld, who said, 'You have human nature, value life, and give up your life. You should be reborn as a human.'" After speaking, Yu wept again and said, "I never told my wife and children about this, but now there is no need to hide it anymore." Cai, upon hearing this, was shocked and told Li Jiulai about it, who wrote it down.

    363

    陈子曰：轮回果报，为浮屠家说，予不乐道。阅《太平广记》诸书，载此类甚多，亦不之信。今九来亲得之其友，可无疑。嗟乎！物类以不嗜杀而得为人，人嗜杀将不得复为人，亦理有必然者。金坛某巨公死，距百里许，农家适产牛，见腹下殊毛，若书某公姓名。众骇语，闻其子，鬻归，闭之别室，以终其年。予闻之巨公姻党，亦无足疑。夫天下之为乱臣贼子者多矣，岂能尽执其人而刀锯鼎烹之？故往往有逃于法者。苟非有冥报，使计穷力竭，贿赂无所施，干请无所用，人亦何惮而不为乱臣贼子？故冥报者，所以济国法之穷也。吾友魏冰叔作《地狱论》，其说实有稗于世道人心，当书此文质之。

    Explanation

    363

    Tianzi said: Reincarnation and its karmic results, these are teachings of Buddhism, and I am unwilling to discuss them. I have read various books from the Taiping Guangji, which contain many such stories, but I do not believe them. Now, through a friend, I have personally encountered this phenomenon, so there is no doubt. Alas! Certain creatures attain human form by avoiding killing, while those who indulge in killing will not be able to return to human form—it is a matter of natural consequence. A prominent official from Jintan passed away, and about a hundred miles away, a farmer’s family gave birth to a cow. It was observed that the cow had unusual hair on its belly, which seemed to spell out the name of the deceased official. The villagers were shocked and informed the official’s son, who bought the cow and confined it in a separate room, where it spent the rest of its life. I heard this from relatives of the deceased official, and there is no reason to doubt it. There are many traitors and rebels in the world; how could we capture and punish all of them with extreme measures? Therefore, there are often those who evade the law. If there were no supernatural retribution, those who plot and exhaust their resources, bribe without success, and make futile appeals would have no fear of becoming traitors or rebels. Thus, supernatural retribution serves to supplement the inadequacies of state law. My friend Wei Bingshu wrote On Hell, and its teachings truly benefit the moral and social order; this text should be consulted for verification.

    364

    [张山来曰：余曾作《轮回说》，谓人为异类，世苟不知，便不足以为戒，故必毛上成字方可耳。]
    　　活死人传　陈鼎定九留溪外传
    　　　　活死人，姓江，四川人，名本实。家素封。明亡，散家财，弃妻子，入终南学仙。十年得其道，遂遨游四海。既而止妙高峰，从阎老人结庐炼金丹。又十年，丹成。

    Explanation

    364

    Zhang Shanlai said: "I once wrote The Theory of Reincarnation, in which I argued that humans are a different kind, and if the world does not know this, it is not enough to serve as a warning. Therefore, it must be inscribed on fur to be effective."

    The Biography of the Living Dead
    Chen Ding's Ninth Record of Liuxi's Outer Biography
    The Living Dead, surname Jiang, from Sichuan, named Ben Shi. Originally wealthy. After the fall of the Ming dynasty, he dispersed his family wealth, abandoned his wife and children, and went to the Zhongnan Mountains to learn Daoism. After ten years, he attained the Dao and then roamed the four seas. Later, he settled at the Wudao High Peak, built a hut with the old man Yan, and refined elixirs. After another ten years, the elixir was completed.

    365

    座下弟子百余人，推荆溪陈留王为首，能驾云往来，能水面上立，能峭壁间行。尝缚虎为骑，出入市中。活死人怒，呼而责之曰：“所贵乎道者，清净无为也。无为而至于无声，方臻众妙之门。故曰『有声之声，延及百里；无声之声，延及四海。』今汝所行，皆有为也。有为则骇世惑俗，岂清净道哉？”于是陈留王乃尽弃其术，掩关息坐。三年，然后请见。活死人大悦，曰：“子可以授吾大道矣！”
    　　　　既授，乃集群弟子告曰：“吾闻『成功者退』。今吾道既已得人，吾将隐矣。”乃命掘一土穴山半，仅可容身。活死人入居之，命以土掩，毋使有隙，但朝夕来呼吾可耳。既埋，群弟子如命，朝夕往呼之。活死人在土中，必大声应。三年，呼之不应矣。群弟子乃树以碣，曰“活死人之墓”。

    Explanation

    365

    Under the seat of the master, there were more than a hundred disciples, among whom Jingxi Chenliu Wang was elected as the leader. He could ride clouds, stand on water, and walk on steep cliffs. He once tied a tiger as a mount and rode through the marketplace. The living dead became angry and called out to rebuke him, saying, "What is valued in the Way is purity and non-action. Through non-action, one reaches a state of no sound, thus entering the gate of all wonders. It is said, 'The sound that can be heard extends for a hundred miles; the sound that cannot be heard extends for the entire world.' Now everything you do is full of action. Action causes the world to be startled and the common people to be confused; how is this the Way of purity?" Thus, Chenliu Wang completely abandoned his techniques, closed the door, and sat in meditation. After three years, he then asked to be seen. The living dead was greatly pleased and said, "You can now teach me the great Way!"

    After receiving the transmission, the living dead gathered all the disciples and said, "I have heard that 'those who succeed must withdraw.' Now that my Way has found a successor, I will withdraw." He ordered a hole to be dug in the middle of the mountain, just large enough to fit one person. The living dead entered and lived there, and the earth was ordered to cover it, leaving no gaps, but allowing them to call him in the morning and evening. After it was covered, the disciples followed orders, calling him in the morning and evening. The living dead inside the earth would definitely respond loudly. After three years, calling him no longer elicited a response. The disciples then set up a stele, saying "The Tomb of the Living Dead."

    366

    外史氏曰：神仙多为骇世惑俗之事，活死人既怪其弟子骇世惑俗，何为活埋土穴，而使呼之应之三年之久耶？岂夫子所谓索隐行怪者，即世之所谓神仙耶？
    　　　　[张山来曰：活埋土穴中，令人呼之而应，此当是其弟子辈故为此言以骇世耳，未必果有其事也。]
    　　义牛传　陈鼎定九留溪外传
    　　　　义牛者，宜兴桐棺山农人吴孝先家水牯牛也。力而有德，日耕山田二十亩，虽饥甚，不食田中苗。吴宝之，令其十三岁子希年牧之。希年跨牛背，随牛所之。牛方食草涧边，忽一虎从牛后林中出，意欲攫希年。牛知之，即旋身转向虎，徐行啮草。希年惧，伏牛背不敢动。虎见牛来，且踞以俟，意相近即攫牛背儿也。牛将迫虎，即遽奔以前，猛力触虎。虎方垂涎牛背儿，不及避，蹼而仰偃隘涧中，不能辗。水壅浸虎首，虎毙。希年驱牛返，白父，集众舁虎归，烹之。

    Explanation

    366

    The Outer History says: Immortals often engage in actions that shock or confuse the world. The master of the dead man finds it strange that his disciple also engages in such actions. Why was the disciple buried alive in a cave for three years, and why could he still be called upon to respond? Is what the master referred to as "seeking out the obscure and doing strange things" indeed what the world calls immortals?

    [Chang Shanlai said: Being buried alive in a cave, and still being able to respond to calls, this must be a fabricated story by the disciples to shock the world. It may not have actually happened.]

    The Story of the Righteous Cow, from Chen Dingding's Collection of Outer Histories from the Nine Streams

    The Righteous Cow was a water buffalo belonging to a farmer named Wu Xiaoxian from Tongguan Mountain in Yixing. It was strong and virtuous, plowing twenty acres of mountain fields daily. Even when very hungry, it did not eat the crops in the fields. Wu treasured the cow and let his thirteen-year-old son, Xi Nian, tend to it. Xi Nian rode on the cow's back, following wherever the cow went. One day, while the cow was grazing by a stream, a tiger suddenly emerged from the forest behind it, intending to seize Xi Nian. The cow sensed the danger and turned to face the tiger, slowly grazing as if unaware. Xi Nian, frightened, crouched on the cow's back and dared not move. The tiger, seeing the cow approach, sat down and waited, planning to grab Xi Nian from the cow's back. As the cow got close to the tiger, it suddenly charged forward and forcefully collided with the tiger. The tiger, still salivating over the prospect of grabbing Xi Nian, failed to dodge in time and was knocked onto its back in a narrow stream, unable to move. The water rushed in, drowns the tiger, and killed it. Xi Nian drove the cow back home, informed his father, gathered the villagers, carried the tiger back, and cooked it.

    367

    他日孝先与邻人王佛生争水。佛生富而暴，素为乡里所怨，皆不直之，而袒孝先。佛生益怒，率其子殴死孝先。希年讼于官。佛生重赂邑令，反杖希年。希年毙杖下，无他昆季可白冤者。孝先妻周氏，日号哭于牛之前，且告牛曰：“曩幸借汝，吾儿得免果虎腹。今且父子俱死于仇人矣！皇天后土，谁为我雪恨耶？”牛闻之，大怒，抖搜长鸣，飞奔至佛生家。佛生父子三人，方延客欢饮，牛直登其堂，竟觝佛生，佛生毙；复觝二子，二子毙。客有持杆与牛斗者，皆伤。邻里趋白令，令闻之，怖死。
    　　　　外史氏曰：世之人子不肖，父仇不能报者比比矣。乃是牛竟能为吴氏报两世杀身仇。噫，牛亦勇矣哉！宜乎令闻之怖死也。

    Explanation

    367

    One day, Xiao Xian had a dispute over water with his neighbor Wang Fusheng. Wang Fusheng was wealthy and tyrannical, and had long been resented by the villagers, who all sympathized with Xiao Xian. Wang Fusheng became even angrier and, with his sons, beat Xiao Xian to death. Xiao Xian's son, Xi Nian, took the matter to the authorities. Wang Fusheng heavily bribed the county magistrate, who instead beat Xi Nian to death with a rod. Xi Nian died under the rod, leaving no other brothers or sons to clear his name. Xiao Xian's wife, Zhou, daily wailed before the ox and said to it, "Formerly, I was fortunate to borrow you, and my son was saved from being eaten by a tiger. Now, both father and son have been killed by our enemy! Heaven and Earth, who will avenge my hatred?" The ox, hearing this, greatly enraged, shook and bellowed, and rushed to Wang Fusheng's house. Wang Fusheng and his two sons were just entertaining guests with drinking when the ox directly entered the hall, ramming Wang Fusheng, who died; then it rammed the two sons, who also died. Some guests tried to stop the ox with poles, but all were injured. The neighbors rushed to report to the magistrate, who, upon hearing the news, was frightened to death.

    The historian commented: The sons of the world are unworthy, and those who cannot avenge their father's death are numerous. It was the ox that could avenge the two generations of the Wu family. Ah, the ox is also brave indeed! It is fitting that the magistrate was frightened to death upon hearing the news.

    368

    [张山来曰：牛之为物，虽巍然一躯，然观其状，大抵顽而不灵。今此牛独能为主报两世之仇，复怖死一贪墨吏，殆所谓“犁牛之子骍且角”者也。]
    　　
    　　
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷十二
    　　邵士梅传　上海陆鸣珂次山古今文绘
    　　　　邵士梅，号峄晖，山东济宁州人也。其前身为高小槐，本高家庄人，向充里正，急公守法，不苛索民间一钱。病革时，见二青衣人，如公差状，令谨闭其目，挟与俱行。行甚捷，唯闻耳边风涛声。少顷，至一室，青衣已去，目顿开，第见二妪侍房帷间，则已托生在邵门矣。口不能言，心辄自念，觉目中所见，栋宇器物，骤然改观。即手足发肤，何似非故我也？至二三岁能言时，辄云“欲上高家庄高家庄”云。父母怪而叱之曰：“儿妄矣！高家庄安在？”及出就外傅，间以语傅。傅曰：“此子前身事，宜秘之。”遂不复言。

    Explanation

    368

    Zhang Shanlai said: The ox, as a creature, though majestically single-bodied, appears stupid and not intelligent when observed. This particular ox alone could avenge two generations of grievances for its owner and scare to death a corrupt official, which is perhaps what is meant by the phrase "the son of an ox that plows is both red and horned."

    From the New Collection of Strange Tales, Volume Twelve,
    The Biography of Shao Shimei, compiled by Lu Mingke of Shanghai, Ancient and Modern Writings

    Shao Shimei, also known as Yihui, was from Jinan Prefecture, Shandong Province. His previous life was that of Gao Xiaohuai, originally from Gaojiazhuang. He served as a village head, diligently fulfilling his duties and adhering to the law without ever demanding a single coin from the people. At the time of his death, he saw two blue-clad officials, who ordered him to carefully close his eyes and carried him along. The journey was very swift, and he only heard the sound of wind and waves in his ears. Shortly afterward, he arrived in a room, where the blue-clad officials had departed. His eyes suddenly opened, and he saw two women attending to the room. He realized he had been reborn into the Shao family. Unable to speak, he thought to himself, noting how the buildings and objects he saw had suddenly changed appearance. His hands, feet, and skin seemed so different from his old self. By the time he was two or three years old and could speak, he would say, "I want to go to Gaojiazhuang, Gaojiazhuang." His parents found this strange and scolded him, saying, "You are being foolish! Where is Gaojiazhuang?" When he went out to a private tutor, he occasionally told the tutor about it. The tutor said, "This is the past life of this child, and it should be kept secret." And so, he stopped talking about it.

    369

    己亥成进士，改授登州郡博。适奉台檄，署篆栖霞，道经高家庄，市井室庐，宛然如昨。因集土人而问之曰：“此地曾有高小槐乎？”曰：“有之，去世已历年所矣。”及询其殁时月日，与士梅生辰无异。遂告之故。觅其子，一物故，一他出，唯一女适人，相距里许。呼与语，语及少时膝下事，甚了了。并访里中诸故老，其一尚存，皤皤黄发，年九十余矣。相见道故旧，欢若平生。士梅因恍然有得，半生疑案，从此冰消。乃赋诗云：“两世顿开生死路，一身会作古今人。”遂捐资置产，厚恤其家。

    Explanation

    369

    In the year of Ji Hai, I became a Jinshi and was subsequently appointed as the prefect of Dengzhou. Just happened to receive an imperial edict, I acted as the official in charge of Qixia and passed through Gaogang Village. The streets and houses were exactly as they were yesterday. Therefore, I gathered the local people to ask them, "Has there ever been a person named Gao Xiaohuai here?" They replied, "Yes, but he has passed away for many years now." When I inquired about the exact date of his death, it turned out to be the same as my own birthdate. I then told them the reason for my inquiry. I sought out his sons, but one had passed away and the other was away. Only one daughter had married, living about a mile away. I called out to her and spoke with her, and we talked about the things of his youth and family, which she remembered very clearly. I also visited the old men of the village, and one of them was still alive, with white hair and a full head of hair, over ninety years old. We met and talked about old times, and it was as if we had known each other all our lives. Suddenly, I had an epiphany, and the half-life mystery was thus completely resolved. I then wrote a poem saying, "Two lives suddenly opened the path of life and death, one person could become both ancient and modern." I donated money to establish property and provided generous support to his family.

    370

    后俸满量移，作令吴江。吴中人士，盛传其事。余初未之信也。适登州明经李曰白为余同年曰桂胞弟，便道过访。余偶言及，曰白曰：“得非我登州邵峄晖先生乎？其事甚真，余所稔闻。”因述邵在登时，尝以语同官李簠，簠以语曰白者，缕悉如此。余稍铨次其语，为立小传。夫高小槐一里正耳，片善之积，尚能死无宿孽，生得成名，况其他哉？云间野史陆鸣珂撰。时康熙七年五月晦日也。

    Explanation

    370

    After serving a full term and being transferred, he served as magistrate in Wujiang. The story was widely circulated among the people of Wujiang. I did not initially believe it. Zhang Yibai, a scholar from Dengzhou, who was my fellow examinee's younger brother, stopped by on the way. I happened to mention it, and Yibai said, "Could it be Mr. Shao Yihui from Dengzhou? The story is very true, what I have heard repeatedly." Because he recounted what Shao did in Dengzhou, and Fu told Yibai in such detail. I slightly organized his words to write a short biography. Gao Xiaohuai was just a village head, but accumulated good deeds still allowed him to die without any lingering sins and live to achieve fame, let alone others? Compiled by Lu Mingke, a wild historian from Yuntian, on the last day of May in the seventh year of the Kangxi era.

    371

    [张山来曰：观里正之善者，其福报如此，其恶者，来生从可知矣！]
    　　彭望祖传　陈鼎定九留溪外传
    　　　　彭望祖，名远，江西人。幼端方沉静，寡言笑。弱冠举诸生，从师读书西山草庵中。冬月，有道士衣单麻衣，冒大雪来求宿，忽病足不能起。望祖怜之，日分饮食奉之。三年，道士足愈，起谢曰：“吾受郎君惠厚矣！无以报。”出丹书三卷授之，曰：“读之可证飞仙。”遂去，不复见。
    　　　　望祖得其书，熟读之。明亡，弃举子业，来游江南。顺治中，京口明经张行贞延为孺子句读师，宾主甚相欢。他日饮青梅下，行贞盛言闽粵鲜荔之美，恨不得啖。望祖曰：“是固无难致也。”行贞曰：“噫！先生何云不难哉？固无论山川险阻，第相去数千里，即使策骏马乘传，日夜兼程，行至此，亦槁矣！”望祖唯唯。

    Explanation

    371

    Zhang Shanlai said: Observe the good ones among the village officials; their blessings are like this, and for those who are evil, the afterlife can be known from this!

    Peng Wangzu Biography, by Chen Dingjiu, from Liuxi Externally Transmitted

    Peng Wangzu, whose courtesy name was Yuan, was from Jiangxi. As a young adult, he passed the preliminary examination and became a student, studying under a teacher in a thatched hut on West Mountain. One winter month, a Taoist priest in a single layer of hemp clothing came in the snow to seek lodging, suddenly fell ill with his feet and could not stand up. Wang Zu pitied him and shared his daily meals with him. After three years, the Taoist priest's feet healed, and he stood up to thank him, saying, "I have received great kindness from you, but I have nothing to repay you." He took out three volumes of alchemical books and gave them to him, saying, "Reading them can lead to becoming a flying immortal." He then left and was never seen again.

    Wang Zu obtained the books and read them thoroughly. When the Ming dynasty fell, he abandoned his scholarly pursuits and came to travel in southern Jiangnan. During the Shunzhi era, Zhang Xingzhen, a scholar from Jingkou, invited him to be a teacher for his child. The host and guest were very happy together. One day, they drank under an apricot tree. Xingzhen spoke highly of the beauty of fresh lychees from Fujian and Guangdong, saying, "I can't even taste them." Wang Zu said, "It is indeed not difficult to obtain them." Xingzhen replied, "Ah! Why do you say it's not difficult? Regardless of the dangers of the mountains and rivers, just the distance of thousands of miles. Even if you ride a swift horse and travel day and night, by the time you get here, you would be exhausted!" Wang Zu nodded in agreement.

    372

    抵暮，行贞入，望祖命童子洒扫书舍，庀香具法坛，戒童子先寝。童子慧，怪之，假寐，窃起窥。望祖于箧中取草龙一具，祭于坛。须臾，龙忽蠕然，鳞甲爪牙皆动。望祖乘之腾去，不半夜归矣。龙两角挂累累，皆鲜荔也。乃撤坛，收草龙置箧中，而东方已白。呼童子起，进之。行贞大骇，诘童子，童子具以告。于是行贞知望祖有神术，谨事之。岁余，望祖忽于午夜出草龙，收行旅琴剑书箧挂于上，乘之而去，不知所终。
    　　　　外史氏曰：神仙固多幻术也，往往以幻术游戏人间，第无缘值之耳。或曰：“望祖特术士耳，非神仙也。”虽然，数千里不半夜而往还，即谓之神仙也亦宜。

    Explanation

    372

    By dusk, Xing Zhen returned home. Wang Zu instructed the child to sweep and tidy the study, prepare incense, and set up the altar, then told the child to go to bed first. The child, being clever, found this strange and pretended to sleep, secretly getting up to peek. Wang Zu took a grass dragon from a chest, offered it on the altar. In an instant, the dragon began to wriggle, its scales, armor, claws, and teeth all moving. Wang Zu rode it and flew away, returning before midnight. The dragon's two horns were laden with fresh litchis. Then, Wang Zu dismantled the altar, gathered the grass dragon and placed it back in the chest, and the east had already turned white. He called the child to wake up and brought him in. Xing Zhen was greatly startled and questioned the child, who told him everything. Thus, Xing Zhen knew that Wang Zu possessed supernatural powers and treated him with reverence. After a year, Wang Zu suddenly, at midnight, brought out the grass dragon, gathered the traveler's lute, sword, and book chest and hung them on it, then rode it away, disappearing without a trace.

    The historian said: Immortals indeed possess many magical arts, and they often play these tricks among humans, but one just doesn't have the chance to encounter them. Someone said, "Wang Zu is merely a magician, not an immortal." Although this may be true, traveling thousands of miles and returning before midnight, it is fitting to call him an immortal as well.

    373

    [张山来曰：余尝羡左慈于盂中钓松江四腮鲈鱼，今望祖尚有借于草龙，犹觉逊一筹也！]
    　　程弱文传　会稽罗坤宏载古今文绘
    　　　　弱文程氏，名璋，歙人程某之女也，其母梦吞花叶而生。幼极颖慧，九岁即好弄翰墨，工诗文，日摹《曹娥》《麻姑》诸帖，书法尤称精楷。性复喜植花，更爱花叶，能于如钱莲叶，熨制为笺，书《心经》一卷。
    　　　　及笄，适里人方元白，伉俪甚欢。元白偕友人吴某，作客广陵。弱文忧形颜色，不能自已。尝作诗文，缄寄元白，元白开缄，辄闭户欷歔，怅惋累日。一日平头复持缄至。友人伺其出，私启视之。乃制新柳叶二片，翠碧如生，各书绝句一首。其一曰：“杨柳叶青青，上有相思纹。与君隔千里，因风犹见君。”其二曰：“柳叶青复黃，君子重颜色。一朝风露寒，弃捐安可测？”又有《染说》一编、《原愁》一则寄元白，文情绵恻，媚楚动人。

    Explanation

    373

    Zhang Shanlai said: "I have often envied Zhang Shanlai's experience of catching four-lipped perch from Songjiang River in a basin, now looking at my ancestors, they have a connection to a mythical creature (grass dragon), still, I feel a step behind!"

    Cheng Ruo Wen's Biography, compiled by Luo Kunhong of Kuaiji in the collection of ancient and modern writings.

    Cheng Ruo Wen, a woman named Zhang, was the daughter of Cheng from She County. Her mother dreamed of swallowing flower leaves before giving birth to her. She was extremely intelligent from a young age, starting to write and draw at the age of nine. She excelled in poetry and prose, and practiced calligraphy daily, particularly the styles of Cao E and Magu, which were highly regarded for their precision. She also enjoyed planting flowers and loved flower leaves. She could press lotus leaves into sheets and write a copy of the Heart Sutra on them.

    When she came of age, she married a local man named Fang Yuanbai, and they were very happy together. Fang Yuanbai went with his friend Wu to Guangling (Yangzhou). Cheng Ruo Wen showed signs of worry and could not help but be concerned. She wrote poems and letters and sent them to Fang Yuanbai. When he opened them, he would close the door and sigh, feeling regretful for several days. One day, a servant brought another letter to Fang Yuanbai. His friend waited until he left and secretly opened it. It contained two new willow leaves, green and lifelike, each with a short poem written on it. One poem said: "The willow leaves are green, with patterns of longing. Though we are thousands of miles apart, the wind still brings you to me." The other poem said: "The willow leaves turn green and yellow, the gentleman values their beauty. One day, when the wind and dew grow cold, who knows if they will be abandoned?" She also sent him a compilation titled "On Dyeing" and an essay titled "The Origin of Sorrow," both of which were emotionally touching and captivating.

    374

    年二十一而卒。著有文集数卷，歙人有传之者。元白伤悼过情，终不复娶，亦不复作客，遂入天台山为名僧焉。
    　　　　[张山来曰：吾邑有此闺秀，当访购其集而表章之。]
    　　薜衣道人传　陈鼎定九留溪外传
    　　　　薜衣道人祝巢夫，名尧民，洛阳诸生也，少以文名。明亡，遂弃制艺，为医，自号薜衣道人。得仙传疡医，凡诸恶疮，傅其药少许，即愈。人或有断胫折臂者，请治之，无不完。若刳腹洗肠，破脑濯髓，则如华陀之神。

    Explanation

    374

    At the age of twenty-one, he passed away. He had composed several volumes of collected works, which were transmitted by someone from She County. Yuan and Bai were excessively grieved, and he never remarried. He also ceased traveling and eventually entered TianTai Mountain to become a renowned monk.

    [Ouyang Shanlai said: Our county has this talented woman; we should seek out her works and honor them.]

    The Biography of Xie Yi Daoist, by Chen Ding, The Additional Biography of Liuxi

    Xie Yi Daoist, courtesy name Zhuo Fu, named Yao Min, was a scholar from Luoyang. He was young and known for his literary talent. After the fall of the Ming Dynasty, he abandoned the literary style of the imperial examinations and became a physician, giving himself the name Xie Yi Daoist. He learned healing techniques from a legendary source and could treat various severe skin diseases with just a small amount of his medicine. People with broken legs or arms would ask him for treatment, and all were fully healed. If he performed extreme medical procedures such as abdominal surgery or brain operations, his skill was as divine as that of Hua Tuo.

    375

    里有被贼断头者，头已殊，其子知其神，谓家人曰：“祝巢夫，仙人也，速为我请来。”家人曰：“郎君何妄也？颈不连项矣，彼即有返魂丹，乌能合即离之形骸哉？”其子固强之而后行。既至，尧民抚其胸曰：“头虽断，身尚有暖气。暖气者，生气也；有生气，则尚可以治。”急以银针纫其头于项，既合，涂以末药一刀圭，熨以炭火。少顷，煎人参汤，杂他药，启其齿灌之。须臾，则鼻微有息矣。复以热酒灌之，逾一昼夜，则出声矣。又一昼夜，则呼其子而语矣。乃进以糜粥，又一昼夜，则可举手足矣。七日而创合，半月而如故。举家拜谢，愿以产之半酬之。尧民不受。后入终南山修道，不知所终。无子，其术不传。
    　　　　外史氏曰：世称华佗为神医，能破脑剜臂，然未闻其能活既杀之人也。乃尧民能之，不几远过于佗耶？孰谓后世无畸人哉！

    Explanation

    375

    In a village, there was a man whose head had been severed by a thief. The head lay apart from the body, and his son, sensing that his father's spirit was still present, told the family, "Zhuo Fu is a fairy, a divine being. Quickly go and fetch him for me." The family replied, "Young master, what foolishness is this? The neck is not connected to the head anymore. Even if he has the pill of returning life, how can it possibly combine what is already separated?" The son insisted strongly and then proceeded. Once Zhuo Fu arrived, he patted the man's chest and said, "Though the head is severed, the body still has warmth. This warmth is the life force; as long as there is life force, it can still be treated." He quickly used silver needles to sew the head back onto the neck. Once reattached, he applied a small amount of powder medicine and ironed the area with charcoal fire. After a short while, he boiled ginseng soup mixed with other medicines and administered it by opening the man's mouth. In a moment, there was a slight breath through the nose. He then administered hot wine, and after more than one day and night, the man made a sound. Another day and night passed, and he called out to his son and spoke to him. Then, he fed him porridge, and after another day and night, he could move his hands and feet. After seven days, the wound healed, and after half a month, he was as good as new. The whole family bowed and thanked him, willing to pay half of their property as a reward. Zhuo Fu did not accept. Later, he entered Zhongnan Mountain to cultivate Dao and was never seen again. Having no son, his skills were not passed on.

    The historian commented, "The world calls Hua Tuo a divine physician, one who can cut open the brain and remove the arm. Yet, it has not been heard that he can revive those who have already been killed. But Zhuo Fu can do it, is he not far superior to Tuo? Who would say there are no extraordinary people in later generations?"

    376

    [张山来曰：理之所必无，事之所或有。存此以广异闻可耳。
    　　　　又曰：使我得遇此公，便当以师事之。]
    　　刘医记　晋陵陈玉璂椒峰学文堂集
    　　　　刘云山，万历间医也，然当时其术未行，身死三十七年，而名始著。陈子闻之曰：“异哉！理可信哉？”客曰：“杭州巨室某者，子患恶疾，垂毙，其家已环而哭之。有一医突至，曰：『我刘云山也。』视毕而病者愈。赠以金，不受去，曰：『他日晤我于毗陵城之司徒庙巷。』逾月，巨室子果至，觅云山，巷之老人曰：『子谬矣！云山死且三十七年矣。然云山生时信鬼神，曾梦授斯庙之神，募钱尚书地以广其祠宇，因自为像于神旁，其形容尚可识也。』巨室子跃入，惊顾骇愕，抱其像哭泣而去。由是吾郡之人，观者，拜者，祭祷者，奔走无虚日，亦复有验。”

    Explanation

    376

    Zhang Shanlai said, "What is logically impossible may still occur in practice. It is worth preserving this for the sake of unusual news."
    He also said, "If I were to meet this person, I would treat him as a teacher."
    Recorded in the Medical Annals of Liu Yunshan, compiled by Chen Yüqi of Jinling in the Peacock Peak Literary Hall.

    Liu Yunshan was a physician during the Wanli period, but his skills were not widely recognized at the time. He died thirty-seven years ago, yet his fame only began to spread then. Chen Yüqi heard this and said, "Strange indeed! Can this be true?" A guest replied, "There was a wealthy family in Hangzhou whose son suffered from a severe illness and was on the brink of death. His family had gathered around him to mourn. Suddenly, a physician arrived and said, 'I am Liu Yunshan.' After examining the patient, the illness was cured. The family offered him gold, but he refused and left, saying, 'I will meet you again in the Si Tu Temple Lane of Pi Ling City.' A month later, the son of the wealthy family indeed arrived at the lane to look for Liu Yunshan. An old man in the lane said, 'You are mistaken! Liu Yunshan has been dead for over thirty-seven years. However, Liu Yunshan believed in ghosts and spirits when he was alive. He once dreamed that the god of this temple instructed him to raise funds from the land of the Shangshu Qian to expand the temple, and he carved his own image beside the god, which can still be recognized.' The son of the wealthy family rushed in, astonished and horrified, hugged the image, and cried before leaving. From then on, people in our county came to worship, pay homage, and pray, and the temple was never empty. There were also cases where the prayers were answered."

    377

    陈子闻之曰：异哉！理可信哉？虽然，使云山之术，得展于生时，吾固知云山之志可毕也。乃负其术而不遇其时，此云山之所以至死而犹不肯泯没者乎？虽其事近于荒唐怪异，君子亦当悯其志而姑信之也。康熙四年三月某日记。
    　　　　[张山来曰：艺术果精，其为神也固宜。]
    　　湖堧杂记　陆次云云士本书
    　　　　净慈寺罗汉，其始止十八尊：吴越王梦十八巨人，而范其像。南宋时，僧道容增塑至五百尊，覆之以田字殿，殊容异态，无一雷同。焚香者按己年齿，随意数之，遇愁者愁，遇喜者喜。按罗汉之异，不止一端。烟霞洞后石壁，有石罗汉六尊，亦见梦于吴越王，乞完聚同气。王为补刻其一十二。又《愿云现果录》载：明时休宁赵贾，出海病疽，同舟者弃之穷岛。赵苏，匍匐至一大寺，见有异僧，问彼沙弥，知为罗汉。

    Explanation

    377

    Chen Zi heard it and said: "Strange indeed! Is it credible?" Although, if Yunshan's skills could have been practiced during his lifetime, I would know that Yunshan's aspirations could have been fulfilled. However, carrying his skills but not meeting the right time, this is why Yunshan still would not allow himself to fade away until death? Although the matter seems somewhat absurd and strange, gentlemen should also pity his aspirations and provisionally believe in them. Diary of a certain month in March of the fourth year of the Kangxi era.

    [Zh Zhang Shanlai said: If the art is indeed refined, it is natural for it to become divine.]

    Miscellaneous Records of Lake and Marshes by Lu Ci Yun, pen name Yunshi.

    The Arhats of Jingci Temple originally numbered only eighteen: The King of Wu Yue dreamt of eighteen giants and had their images cast. During the Southern Song dynasty, the monk Dao Rong increased the number to five hundred, covering them with a tianzi hall, each with unique appearances and no two alike. Those who burn incense count according to their own age, randomly, and find themselves either sad or happy. The wonders of the Arhats are not limited to one aspect. Behind the Yanyu Cave, there are six stone Arhats on the rock wall, also appearing in a dream to the King of Wu Yue, asking to be completed and united with their kind. The king carved twelve more. The Wish Cloud Manifestation Record also states: In the Ming dynasty, Zhao Jia from Xiuning fell ill with a boil while sailing at sea, and his shipmates abandoned him on a desolate island. Zhao recovered and crawled to a large temple, where he saw a strange monk. He asked the young novice, who told him it was an Arhat.

    378

    贾向一僧求其送归，僧曰：“可入袖中。”即越海掷贾室中，飘然竟去。贾还，捐资造建初寺，画神僧之事于壁，以彰佛力。又明季太仓有一巨姓，老年无子，斋十万八千僧讫，有十八异僧，复来求食，家僮拒之。一僧竟入堂中，以指濡唾作行书书其几曰：“十八高人特地来，谓言斋罢莫徘徊。善根虽种无余泽，连理枝头花未开。”随书随成金字。家僮惊报，主人急出，僧已逝矣。巨姓顶礼诗几，积诚一载，忽见“未”字转动，自下而上，竟成“半”字，遂得一女。

    Explanation

    378

    Jia asked a monk to send him back, and the monk said, "You can put me in your sleeve." The monk then crossed the sea and threw Jia into his room, departing mysteriously. After being returned, Jia donated funds to build Jianchu Temple, painted the story of the divine monk on the wall to glorify the power of the Buddha. In the late Ming dynasty, in Taicang, there was a wealthy family whose elderly head had no children. After feasting ten thousand eight thousand monks, eighteen strange monks came to beg for food, and the family's servant rejected them. One monk entered the hall, wrote a poem with his finger dipped in saliva, and the poem read: "Eighteen high men have come specially, saying not to linger after the feast. Though the good roots are planted, there is no further benefit; the flowers on the twin branches have not yet bloomed." As he wrote, the words turned to gold. The servant reported this in shock, and the master rushed out, but the monk had already passed away. The wealthy family knelt and worshiped the poem, accumulating sincerity for a year. Suddenly, the "未" character rotated from bottom to top, ultimately becoming the "半" character, and they eventually gave birth to a daughter.

    379

    明末，净寺一僧尝昼寝，梦伽蓝语之曰：“有张姓新贵人至矣，急迎之！”僧惊寤，旋往山门物色，见一书生倚松太息。僧询之曰：“君得无张姓某名乎？”书生曰：“然！”僧急拉之曰：“新贵人盍过我？”书生急谢曰：“公勿误！我乃不取科举秀士也。今八月初六日矣，诸试俱毕，无计观场，过此排闷，安得为新贵人耶？”僧曰：“君之为新贵人，神告之矣。未录科，易事耳，吾为尔续取！”书生曰：“续取须金。

    Explanation

    379

    A Monk and a Scholar in Late Ming Dynasty

    In the late Ming dynasty, a monk at a Buddhist temple took a nap during the day and had a dream in which the temple guardian told him, "A new noble person with the surname Zhang has arrived. Hurry and welcome him!" The monk woke up startled and immediately went to the temple gate to look for the person. He saw a scholar leaning against a pine tree and sighing. The monk approached and asked, "Are you not Zhang, surname [name]?" The scholar replied, "Yes!" The monk quickly pulled him and said, "New noble person, why don't you come to me?" The scholar politely declined, saying, "Please don't misunderstand! I did not take the imperial exams. Today is August 6th, and all the exams are over. I have no way to participate, so I am here to relieve my boredom. How could I be a new noble person?" The monk insisted, "You are a new noble person, as the god has told me. Not being recorded in the list is an easy matter; I will continue to select you!" The scholar responded, "Continuing to be selected requires money."

    380

    ”僧曰：“吾为若输金？”书生曰：“吾观场无费，不如休也。”僧曰：“吾为若措费，第得科名后无相忘足矣。”书生曰：“斯何敢？”僧续名为投卷、市参、授餐、僦寓。场事毕，又为卜筊于伽蓝，得大吉，益喜跃。榜将发，拉书生曰：“君候放榜，当必在我舍。”书生曰：“公无虑，我舍公，将安归？”于是轰饮彻夜，将旦，僧先入城观揭榜，果见姓名高列矣。驰归拉生赴宴。至则再视，视上名虽是而籍则非，相顾错愕。生甚惭而僧甚悔，各不复顾，分道叹息而去。

    Explanation

    380

    The monk said, "Will I pay the gold for you?" The scholar replied, "I see there is no expense in the field, so it's better to stop." The monk said, "I will arrange the expenses for you, but it's enough if you don't forget me after you pass the exams." The scholar said, "How dare I?" The monk continued with the names for submitting scrolls, buying references, providing meals, and renting accommodation. After the examination was over, he also divined using a yarrow stalk at the temple and got a very auspicious result, which made him even more joyful. When the list was about to be posted, he pulled the scholar and said, "You are waiting for the list to be posted, and it will certainly be at my place." The scholar said, "You have no worries, where will I go if I leave you?" They then drank heartily all night, and as dawn approached, the monk first went into the city to see the list being posted, and indeed, his name was highly ranked. He rushed back and pulled the scholar to the feast. When they arrived, they looked again, and although the name on the list was correct, the registration was wrong, and they looked at each other in confusion. The scholar felt very ashamed, and the monk deeply regretted, and they each went their separate ways, sighing.

    381

    [张山来曰：此当是寺僧平时势利炎凉，故伽蓝恶而戏之耳。]
    　　　　高丽寺者，高丽国王为某世子所建也。宋神宗时，国王尝祈嗣于佛，得一子，昼夜啼，唯闻木鱼声则暂止。有声自空中来，或远或迩，王命寻声所自起，愈寻愈遥。渡海而南，倾耳清听，得之于武林镜湖之畔。一僧端坐招提，静宣贝叶，击鱼按节，梵韵清扬。使者敬礼僧前，请涉朝鲜以疗世子。僧曰：“世子云何？”使告以故，且曰：“其臂间湛然有『佛无灵』字，佛之所賜，而题识谓之无灵，此何说欤？”僧曰：“异哉！为尔往视。”渡海见王。王出世子，僧合掌作礼，世子笑而颔之。

    Explanation

    381

    Zhang Shanlai said: This is likely due to the temple monk's usual favoritism and coldness towards others, hence the Buddha is mocked and played with.

    The Goryeo Temple was built by the King of Goryeo for his son, the Crown Prince. During the reign of Emperor Shenzong of Song, the king once prayed to the Buddha for an heir and was granted a son. However, the crown prince cried incessantly day and night, only stopping briefly when the sound of the wooden fish was heard. One day, a strange sound came from the air, sometimes far, sometimes near. The king ordered his men to trace the source of the sound, but the more they searched, the farther away it seemed to be. They crossed the sea to the south, listening intently, and eventually found the sound coming from the shore of Mirror Lake in West Lake, Hangzhou. There, a monk was sitting upright in a temple, quietly proclaiming the scriptures written on palm leaves, striking the wooden fish in rhythm, and reciting the Buddha's teachings in a clear and uplifting voice. An envoy paid respects before the monk and requested that he cross to Korea to heal the crown prince. The monk asked, "What about the crown prince?" The envoy explained the situation and added, "There is a clear inscription on the crown prince's arm that reads 'The Buddha is not powerful.' It is said to be a gift from the Buddha, but the inscription claims that the Buddha is not powerful. What does this mean?" The monk replied, "Strange indeed! I will go and see for you." They crossed the sea to meet the king. The king brought out the crown prince, and the monk clasped his hands in a bow. The crown prince smiled and nodded in agreement.

    382

    王异之，问何故，僧曰：“王之世子，吾师也。吾师曾为比丘矣。其先盖舆夫也。肩舆得金，自给之外，每以余资投井底，积既久，金益多，出金建剎于湖上，遂为释。吾钦其德为之徒。乃师一年跛，明年盲，三年为雷击以死。吾深不平，因濡笔题“佛无灵”字于其臂。孰意其生于此欤？”王曰：“审如是乎？佛有灵矣！彼种种者，安知非夙生之孽，并报一世，而后偿其善果乎？”因为建寺于其旧地，颜曰“高丽”，且进金塔以表奇。因志失载，碑不存矣，余纪其略以貽主僧。今寺唯无梁殿尚在，人比之鲁灵光云。
    　　　　[张山来曰：使其徒不于臂间书“佛无灵”三字，則佛竟无灵矣。]

    Explanation

    382

    Wang was surprised and asked why. The monk replied, "Wang's heir, my teacher, once was a monk. His ancestors were probably porters. He carried a sedan chair and earned money, using it to support himself and occasionally throwing the surplus into a well. After a long time, the money accumulated, and he used it to build a temple by the lake, thus becoming a monk. I admired his virtue and became his disciple. My teacher became lame in the first year, blind in the second, and was struck by lightning and died in the third. I deeply resented this and wrote the words 'Buddha is not powerful' on his arm with a wet brush. Who would have thought he was reborn here?" Wang said, "Is it truly like this? Then Buddha has power! How can we know that these misfortunes are not the result of past lives' sins, all to be repaid in this life, and then the good fruit will be reaped?" Therefore, a temple was built on the old site, named 'Goryeo,' and a golden stupa was erected to commemorate the unusual event. The records were lost, and the stele no longer exists, so I recorded the brief account to give to the abbot. Now only the beamless hall remains, and it is compared to the Lu Lingguang Hall.

    Zhang Shanlai said: "If my disciple had not written the words 'Buddha is not powerful' on his arm, then Buddha would indeed be powerless."

    383

    三茅观，踞吴山之最胜。按《茅山志》记茅君示现，以云气为衣服，而不辨眉目。一道士曾于观前见一幻影，与此说符，是灵奇不独茅山矣。观中张三丰曾来寄迹，故于其左肖三丰像，建三仙阁。中坐仙，平平耳；左立仙，首戴笠，玉质亭亭，扶杖欲出；右睡仙，侧卧覆衾，曲肱加枕，如得五龙蛰法，而呼吸有声也。其境不凡，故仙踪恒集。万历时，有凌姓医者，事仙最虔，每以针术施人，而不孳孳于利者。过观中，见群乞儿席地会饮，候值隆冬，同云欲雪，丐者且袒臂裸襟，握拳射覆。凌异而视之。丐者授以一脔。凌曰：“吾不茹。”酌以一盏。凌曰：“吾不饮。”问何故，曰：“以奉仙故。”一丐曰：“勿强之。我辈醉，宜归矣！”飘然而散。所遗在地数荷叶，鲜翠如盘，似倾露珠而新出水者。凌思木叶尽脱时焉得有此？丐者殆真仙，而以此貽我也？拜而收之，珍藏什袭。每行针，先以针针叶上，疗疾即愈；人拟之徐秋夫。至今其裔以针名世。

    Explanation

    383

    Final Translation

    The Three Masters' Temple, perched on the most magnificent peak of Wu Mountain, is a place of great spiritual significance. According to the Mao Shan Records, the Three Masters appeared in a cloud of mist, wearing the clouds as their garments, yet their features were indistinct. Once, a Daoist monk saw a phantom before the temple, which matched this description, proving that the wonders of the place are not limited to Mount Mao alone. Within the temple, Zhang Sanfeng once stayed briefly, and in his honor, a statue of him was sculpted and a Three Immortals Pavilion was built. The central figure is a seated immortal, rather ordinary; the one on the left stands tall, wearing a bamboo hat, with a jade-like, graceful demeanor, holding a staff as if about to step out; the one on the right lies on his side, covered by a quilt, bending his arm and resting it as a pillow, as if practicing the Taoist method of deep meditation, and breathing audibly. The scenery here is extraordinary, and thus the traces of immortals often gather. During the Wanli era, there was a physician named Ling, who was devout in his worship of the immortals, often using his needle technique to heal others without seeking profit. Passing through the temple, he saw a group of beggars sitting on the ground, drinking together. It was a severe winter, with clouds gathering and snow about to fall, and the beggars were unbuttoning their sleeves and exposing their chests, playing a guessing game with their fists. Ling found this strange and watched them. One beggar offered him a piece of food. Ling said, "I do not eat." Another beggar poured him a cup of wine. Ling said, "I do not drink." When asked why, the beggar replied, "Because it is for the immortals." Another beggar said, "Do not force him. We are drunk and should go home!" They scattered away gracefully. On the ground were several fresh, green lotus leaves, as if freshly plucked from the water, still glistening with dew. Ling wondered how such leaves could exist in the dead of winter. Perhaps the beggars were true immortals, and they had given him these leaves as a gift? He bowed and collected them, treasuring them as if they were precious relics. Whenever he used his needle technique, he first pricked the leaves, and his patients were cured immediately. People compared him to Xu Qiufu. To this day, his descendants are renowned for their needlework.

    384

    一亩田，在武林门内。有谁庵者，僧静然主之。静然晨夕焚修，诵经不怠。于顺治戊子元旦，方宣梵呗，有鼠窥于梁。嗣后每叩鱼声，其鼠即至；渐乃由梁及户，由户及几。僧呼：“鼠子，尔来听经耶？”鼠即点头，蹲伏金经之右，经止，乃徐徐去。率以为常，如是逾年。一日者，复来听经。经毕，向僧如作礼状，礼毕，寂然不动。僧抚之曰：“尔圆寂耶？”已涅槃矣。越数日，体坚如石，有旃檀香。僧为制一小龛，塔而瘗之，如浮屠礼。
    　　　　[张山来曰：余亦曾于讲院听经，竟不解所谓，而妇人女子，见其作点首会意状，殊不可解。然异类往往能之，则妇人女子听经会意，又不足奇矣。]

    Explanation

    384

    A mu of land lies inside the Wulin Gate. There is a temple called Who's Temple, overseen by a monk named Jingran. Jingran burns incense and meditates morning and evening, reciting scriptures without slackening. On New Year's Day of the year 1658 during the Shunzhi era, as he was just chanting Buddhist hymns, a mouse peered from the beam. Thereafter, every time the sound of the fish-shaped bell was heard, the mouse would appear; gradually, it moved from the beam to the door, and from the door to the table. The monk called out, "Mouse, have you come to listen to the sutras?" The mouse would nod and sit down on the right side of the golden sutra. When the recitation ended, it would then slowly leave. This became a regular occurrence, lasting for over a year. One day, the mouse came again to listen to the sutras. After the recitation ended, it performed a bowing gesture towards the monk, and after the bow, it remained still. The monk patted it and said, "Are you entering nirvana?" It had already attained nirvana. After several days, its body became as hard as stone, with the fragrance of sandalwood. The monk made a small niche, built a stupa, and buried it, following the Buddhist ritual.

    [Comment by Zhang Shanlai: I have also listened to the sutras in a lecture hall, and still do not understand what it means. Seeing women and girls nodding in understanding is particularly incomprehensible. Yet strange creatures often do this, so it is not so surprising anymore for women and girls to listen to the sutras and understand them.]

    385

    吴山之最胜者，曰紫阳山。径曲奥，石玲珑，洞幽閴，水潺湲，岩秀刻，故米芾书其石曰“吴山第一峰”。仙境也，真仙出焉。宋嘉定间，有丁野鹤者全真其处。山麓有善姓，恒斋丁。一日丁受斋，不即去。忽有无赖子数辈，掖一垂毙乞儿，投其家，众急走。无何乞儿毙矣，善姓遽急，丁曰：“无恐，盍闭我于静室，闻弹指声，方出。”俄而无赖之众复轰然集矣，声以毙命，裂眦攘臂，正欲劫其资，而毙者倏然自地起，趋出户。众呼之不应，拉之不止，追之不可及也；归于无赖之家，复告毙。众错愕，急散去。而丁弹指出室中，谢善姓，不复至矣。人由是知丁之奇。未几，召其妻王守素，付偈与别曰：“懒散六十年，妙用无人识。顺逆两俱忘，虚空镇长寂。”抱膝而逝。守素遂漆其尸，遗蜕尚在，不异生平。其妻后亦证道云。

    Explanation

    385

    The most beautiful place of Wu Mountain is called Ziyang Mountain. Its winding paths are intricate, its stones are delicate, its caves are secluded and quiet, its streams babble, and its cliffs are carved with beauty. Therefore, Mi Fu inscribed the stone with the words "First Peak of Wu Mountain." It is a fairyland, where true immortals emerge. During the Jia Ding period of the Song Dynasty, there was a person named Ding, who practiced Daoism in this place. At the foot of the mountain, there was a good-hearted family that practiced vegetarianism. One day, when Ding was receiving his vegetarian meal, he did not leave immediately. Suddenly, a group of ruffians appeared, carrying a beggar who was on the verge of death, and brought him to the family's home. The crowd panicked and ran away. Soon, the beggar died, and the good-hearted family was alarmed. Ding said, "Do not be afraid. Why not lock me in a quiet room? When you hear the sound of finger snapping, I will come out." Soon after, the ruffians gathered again, shouting threats of death, their eyes wide with anger, and their arms raised in preparation to rob the family. However, the dead beggar suddenly rose from the ground, rushed out of the door. The crowd called out to him, but he did not respond, and they could not stop him. They tried to chase him, but they could not catch up. When they returned to the ruffians' home, they reported that he had died again. The crowd was shocked and quickly dispersed. Meanwhile, Ding emerged from the room, snapped his fingers, thanked the good-hearted family, and never returned. People thus knew of Ding's extraordinary abilities. Shortly after, he summoned his wife, Wang Shousu, gave her a verse and said farewell: "Lazy and carefree for sixty years, my wonderful abilities were unrecognized by others. I forgot both success and failure, remaining in a state of emptiness and tranquility." He passed away while holding his knees. Wang Shousu then preserved his body in lacquer, and his remains remained unchanged, just as they were in life. Later, his wife also attained enlightenment.

    386

    [张山来曰：此日假人命最多，安得丁仙遍满人间也？]
    　　　　崇祯末年，有江右客，寓珠宝巷。携一硃盒，中藏碧草一本，上有生就小龙，其大如指，长逾三寸，光似淡金，鳞角爪牙，无一不备，循枝盘绕，气色如新。博物者不知其所从出。时潞王播越在浙，售其府中。按潞王名敬一，精通释典，名潞佛子。工书善画，尤精于兰，至今有石刻留虎跑寺。制为“潞琴”，前委两角，材最精良。其府中颇蓄异物。有拂水石，有竹节盆，其大如轮；有纯阳像，乃仙笔也，风右则须飘而左，风左则须飘而右；有舍利一颗，晦夜放光，视其燥湿，可占晴雨；有四面观音一尊，得之大鳖腹中者，王之绣佛长斋，从剖鳖得佛像始。而后陵谷变迁，不知其乌有矣。

    Explanation

    386

    Zhang Shanlai said: "On this day, the most lives are taken, how can the immortal Ding be everywhere in the world?" In the final years of the Chongzhen era, there was a guest from Jiangxi who lived in the jewelry lane. He carried a red box containing a book of green grass, with a living little dragon on it, as big as a finger, over three inches long, shining like pale gold, with scales, horns, claws, and teeth, none missing, circling the branch, looking as fresh as new. The knowledgeable ones do not know where it came from. At that time, the Prince of Lu was exiled in Zhejiang, and it was sold in his mansion. According to, the Prince of Lu's name was Jingyi, proficient in Buddhist scriptures, known as the Prince of Lu, the Son of Buddha. Skilled in calligraphy and painting, especially skilled in orchids, to this day, there are stone carvings left in Huapao Temple. Made into a "Lu qin," with two horns at the front, the material is the finest. His mansion housed many strange objects. There were water-wiping stones, bamboo-joint pots, as big as a wheel; there was a Pure Yang statue, painted by a divine hand, when the wind blows from the right, the beard moves to the left, and vice versa; there was a single relic, which emits light on dark nights, by observing its dryness or moisture, one can predict the weather; there was a four-faced Guanyin, obtained from the belly of a large turtle, the Prince of Lu's embroidered Buddha and long fast began with the dissection of the turtle to obtain the Buddha image. Afterwards, mountains and valleys changed, and it is unknown whether it still exists.

    387

    藩司治前有百狮池。甚深广。顺治八年季冬，群儿绕栏嬉戏，忽见赤蟹浮于池上。共讶严寒焉得有此？遂钩取之。有囊吞钩而起，举之甚重，视之，一肢解人也。急报藩伯，藩伯陈姓，曰：“蟹具八足，此间岂有行八之人，与名八之地乎？”一卒曰：“去司不远，八足子巷中有丁八。”藩伯曰：“速捕之！”至则遁矣。廉得巷中有皮匠妇，与丁八有私，而匠复数日不见，邻人疑而举之。捕匠妇，一讯而伏，诚与丁八成谋，以皮刀磔匠而沉之池，将偕奔而未迨也。狱成，究不得八。藩伯旋开府粵西，偶至一山寺，寺僧具迎。随开府者一童子，忽执一僧曰：“杀人丁八在是矣！”僧失色。开府曰：“若安识之？”童子曰：“余邻也，虽变服而貌不可变。”童子盖浙人，而挈之以适粤者也。既得八，械送之浙，同伏法。穷凶冤债，虽髡发万里之外，其能避乎？

    Explanation

    387

    Before the office of the provincial governor, there was a pool called the Hundred Lions Pool. It was very deep and wide. In the winter of the eighth year of Shunzhi, a group of children were playing around the railing when they suddenly saw a red crab floating on the surface of the pool. Everyone was surprised that such a thing could exist in such cold weather. They then hooked it up. There was a bag that swallowed the hook and rose up, and when they lifted it, it was very heavy. When they looked at it, it was a dismembered person. They immediately reported it to the provincial governor, who was surnamed Chen. The governor said, "Crabs have eight legs. Could there be someone named Eighth in this place, or a place called Eighth?" One soldier replied, "Not far from the office, in Alley of Eight Feet Son, there is a person named Ding Eight." The governor said, "Capture him quickly!" When they arrived, he had already fled. They investigated and found that in the alley, there was a tanner's wife. She had a private affair with Ding Eight, and the tanner had been missing for several days. The neighbors suspected and reported her. They captured the tanner's wife, and after one interrogation, she confessed. She truly conspired with Ding Eight, using a leather knife to dismember the tanner and sink him in the pool. They were about to flee together but did not manage to do so. The case was resolved, but they could not find Eighth. The provincial governor soon became the governor of Guangxi. He happened to visit a mountain temple, and the monks of the temple prepared to welcome him. A young boy who accompanied the governor suddenly grabbed a monk and said, "The murderer, Ding Eight, is here!" The monk turned pale. The governor said, "How do you know?" The young boy said, "I am his neighbor; even if he changed his clothes, his appearance did not change." The young boy was from Zhejiang and had brought him to Guangxi. Once they found Ding Eight, they shackled him and sent him back to Zhejiang. He was executed along with the tanner's wife. Extreme evil and karmic debt— even if one shaves their head and travels a thousand miles away, can they avoid their fate?

    388

    武林山之最高者，独推五云。唯高斯寒，故宋时山僧，每在腊前进雪。崇祯癸未，时当重九，有数书生，约登此山，以作龙山之会。贾勇而上，休息庙中，为时正早。庙祀五通之神。一生戏拈神筄卜曰：“我辈今日得入城否？”筄语答以“不能”。书生睨视阶晷，大笑曰：“何神之有灵，劾尚未午，而云我辈不得归家耶？”随步下。至一溪头，见双鲫游泳，迥异凡鱼，书生共下捕之。或远或近，或潜或跃，或入手中，泼剌又去。书生以必得为期，脱衣作网，濡手沾足，良久得之，贯以柳枝。携出山麓，至南屏酒家，而月上东山，禁门扃钥矣。因命童子烹鱼取醉，遣此良夜。童子谓鱼游釜中，久之不熟，命童子添薪益火，而其游如故。又加踊跃，有碎釜声。书生急往视之，俨然鱼也，取出乃木筄耳。因共惊悔。翌日归筄庙中，以牲醴祷神而去。

    Explanation

    388

    Among the mountains of Wulin, the highest is uniquely praised as Wuyun. Due to its height, it is cold, and thus during the Song Dynasty, mountain monks would each year before the New Year send reports about the snow. In the year of the Gengshen (1633), on the Double Ninth Festival, several scholars agreed to climb this mountain to hold a gathering similar to the one on Long Mountain. They bravely ascended, rested in the temple, and it was still early. The temple worships the Five Tong gods. One of the scholars playfully drew the oracle sticks and asked, "Can we enter the city today?" The oracle sticks answered with "no." The scholars glanced at the sundial and laughed, saying, "What kind of god is so powerful, it hasn't even reached noon, and says we can't go home?" They then walked down. Upon reaching a stream, they saw two carp swimming, very different from ordinary fish. The scholars all tried to catch them. Some were far, some were near; some hid, some jumped; some would enter their hands but then swim away. The scholars set their goal to definitely catch them, took off their clothes and made a net, wet their hands and feet, and after a long time, they caught them. They tied them together with willow branches and carried them out to the foot of the mountain, going to the Nanping wine shop. The moon rose over the eastern mountain, and the palace gates were locked. They then ordered the servant to cook the fish and get drunk, to enjoy this good night. The servant said the fish was swimming in the pot, it didn't cook for a long time, and they ordered the servant to add more wood and increase the fire, but it continued to swim as before. It swam even more vigorously, and there was a sound of the pot breaking. The scholars rushed to see it, and it was clearly a fish. They took it out and found it was just a wooden oracle stick. They were all shocked and regretful. The next day, they returned the oracle sticks to the temple, offered sacrifices and prayed to the gods, and left.

    389

    超山在皋亭山北，山不深而穴虎。顺治十八年冬月，有僧闻虎啸，欲拽杖往伏之，竟为所噬。其徒延虎师捕虎。师江右人，捕虎有年矣。初造井，即知当获七虎。每获一虎，乡人赠之以金。其法以羊置井中，鸣以相诱，煮青螺斗许，遍撒山隅。虎至，伥鬼导之。伥见螺，贪剔螺肉，忘为虎护。虎遂孤行，即误入井，虎师遂束之以归。盖僧之徒，隔山遥望，所见如此。越月师云：“今日当获第七虎矣！”乡人益以金为赠。师怀金纵步往视。虎在井中大吼一声，猛如霹雳。忽井外二伏虎，自草中起，各衔师一足，中裂其体而去。夫擒虎乃祛害也，虎宜不能与师仇。而卒为之害者，意者有祛害之心，而因之以为利欤？吁嗟！虎师知虎之死于井中，不知己亦殉于阱外也！

    Explanation

    389
