Then he jumped into the Sword Pool (剑池). The crowd rushed to save him. His face and forehead were all injured, and his thigh was also injured. He couldn't walk. People sent him back home. From then on, he lay weak in bed, every day in a drunken dream.

    434

    至是忽闻六如至，乃从榻间跃起，急叩豫章佳人状。六如出所摹“素琼图”示之。灵一见，诧为天人，急捧至案间，顶礼跪拜。自陈“才子张灵拜谒”云云。已闻莹已入宫，乃抚图痛哭。六如复出莹所题《行乞图》示之。灵读罢，益痛哭，大呼：“佳人崔素琼！”随踣地呕血不止。家人拥至榻间，病愈甚。三日后，邀六如与诀曰：“已矣唐君！吾今真死矣！死后，乞以此图殉葬。”索笔书片纸云：“张灵，字梦晋，风流放诞人也。以情死。”遂掷笔而逝。六如哭之恸，乃葬灵于玄墓山之麓，而以图殉焉。检其生平文章，先已自焚，唯收其诗草及《行乞图》以归。

    Explanation

    434

    At this time, Liu Ling suddenly heard that Liu Sixu had arrived, and he leaped up from his bed, urgently asking about the appearance of the beauty from Yuzhang. Liu Sixu presented the copy of the "Su Qiong Diagram" to him. Upon seeing it, Liu Ling was amazed by its heavenly beauty and quickly held it to the table, paying homage with a kneeling bow. He introduced himself as "the talented scholar Zhang Ling paying his respects." Having heard that Ying had already entered the palace, he then clutched the diagram and wept bitterly. Liu Sixu then presented the "Begging Diagram" inscribed by Ying to him. After reading it, Liu Ling wept even more and shouted loudly, "The beautiful woman Cui Suqiong!" He then fell to the ground, vomiting blood uncontrollably. His family members carried him back to his bed, and his illness worsened. Three days later, he invited Liu Sixu to bid farewell and said, "It is over, Master Tang! I am truly dying now! After my death, I ask that this diagram be buried with me." He took a pen and wrote on a small piece of paper, saying, "Zhang Ling, style name Mengjin, a man of free and unrestrained spirit. He died of love." He then threw down the pen and passed away. Liu Sixu wept bitterly and buried Liu Ling at the foot of Xuanmu Mountain, with the diagram accompanying him in death. When they checked his lifetime writings, they had already been burned by him, leaving only his poetry collection and the "Begging Diagram" to be taken back.

    435

    时莹已率“十美”抵都，因驾幸榆林，久之未得进御。而宸濠已举兵反，为王守仁所败，旋即就擒。驾还时，以“十美”为逆藩所献，悉遣归母家，听其适人。于是莹仍得返豫章。值崔翁已捐馆舍，有老仆崔恩殡之。莹哀痛至甚，然茕孑无依；葬父已毕，遂挈装径抵吴门，命崔恩邀六如相见于舟次。莹首讯张灵近状，六如怆然收涕曰：“辱姐钟情远顾，奈此君福薄，今已为情鬼矣！”莹闻之，呜咽失声。询知灵葬于玄墓，约明日同往祭之。六如明果携灵诗草及《行乞图》至，与莹各拏舟抵灵墓所。莹衣缞絰，伏地拜哭甚哀。已乃悬《行乞图》于墓前，陈设祭仪；坐石台上，徐取灵诗草读之。

    Explanation

    435

    At the time, Ying had already led the "ten beauties" to the capital. Due to the emperor's visit to Yulin, it took a long time before she could gain access to him. Meanwhile, Zhu Chenhao had raised an army in rebellion, but was defeated by Wang Yangming and soon captured. Upon his return, the emperor, considering the "ten beauties" as gifts from the rebellious prince, sent them all back to their mothers' homes, allowing them to remarry. Thus, Ying was able to return to Nanchang. Unfortunately, Cui翁 had passed away, and an elderly servant named Cui En performed the burial rites. Ying was overwhelmed with grief, feeling utterly alone and without support. After completing the burial of her father, she gathered her belongings and traveled directly to Wuxi, instructing Cui En to invite Tang Yin to meet her on a boat. Ying first inquired about Zhang Ling's recent condition, and Tang Yin, with a sorrowful expression, replied, "Your sister's affection for him was so deep, but alas, this man had little fortune and has now become a ghost of love!" Upon hearing this, Ying wept uncontrollably. She learned that Zhang Ling was buried at Xuanmu Tomb and agreed to visit the grave the next day. The following morning, Tang Yin indeed brought Zhang Ling's poetry manuscript and the "Begging Portrait" with him, and they each took a boat to the tomb. Ying, dressed in mourning attire, prostrated herself before the grave and wept bitterly. She then hung the "Begging Portrait" in front of the tomb, set up the offerings, sat on a stone platform, and slowly took out Zhang Ling's poetry manuscript to read it.

    436

    每读一章，辄酹酒一卮，大呼“张灵才子！”一呼一哭，哭罢又读，往复不休。六如不忍闻，掩泪归舟。而崔恩伫立已久，劝慰无从，亦起去，徘徊丘垄间。及返，则莹已自经于台畔。恩大惊，走告六如。六如趋视，见莹已死，叹息跪拜曰：“大难大难！我唐寅今日得见奇人奇事矣！”遂具棺衾，将易服敛之。而莹通体衫襦，皆细缀严密无少隙，知其矢死已久。六如因取诗草及《行乞图》并置棺中为殉，启灵圹与莹同穴，而植碑题其上云：“明才子张梦晋佳人崔素琼合葬之墓”。时倾城士人閧传感叹，无贵贱贤愚，争来吊诔，络绎喧豗，云蒸雨集，哀声动地，殆莫知其由也。六如既合葬灵、莹，检莹所遗橐中装，为置墓田，营丙舍，命崔恩居之，以供春秋奠扫之役。

    Explanation

    436

    Every time he read a chapter, he would pour a cup of wine as an offering and shout loudly, "Zhang Ling, a talented scholar!" He would shout and cry, cry and then read again, repeating this back and forth without ceasing. Liu Sixu (Liu Gongquan) could not bear to hear this and wiped away his tears as he returned to his boat. Meanwhile, Cui En had stood waiting for a long time, unable to comfort him, and also left, lingering around the graves. When he returned, he found that Ying had already hanged herself by the side of the platform. Cui En was greatly shocked and hurried to tell Liu Sixu. Liu Sixu quickly went to see and found that Ying was already dead. He sighed and knelt down, saying, "Great difficulty! Today, I, Tang Yin, have witnessed extraordinary people and events!" He then prepared a coffin and shroud, intending to change her clothes and wrap her. However, Ying's entire body was covered in finely stitched, tightly woven clothing without any gaps, indicating that she had been determined to die for a long time. Liu Sixu then took her poetry drafts and the "Alms Begging Figure" and placed them in the coffin as offerings. He opened the tomb of Ling and buried Ying with him in the same grave, erecting a stele and inscribing on it: "The tomb of the Ming Dynasty's talented scholar Zhang Mengjin and the beautiful woman Cui Suqiong, buried together." At that time, the entire city's scholars were moved and sighed in unison, regardless of their social status or intelligence. They all came to mourn and lament, forming a continuous noisy crowd, like clouds rising and rain gathering. The sorrowful sounds shook the ground, and no one knew the reason for this. After Liu Sixu had buried Ling and Ying together, he checked the contents of Ying's bag and set aside land for the tomb, built a small house, and ordered Cui En to live there, to provide sacrifices and sweep during the spring and autumn seasons.

    437

    呜呼！才子佳人，一旦至此，庶乎灵、莹之事毕，而六如之事亦毕矣。而六如于明年仲春，躬诣墓所拜奠。夜宿丙舍旁，辗转不寐。启窗纵目，则万树梅花，一天明月，不知身在人世。六如怅然叹曰：“梦晋一生狂放，沦落不偶，今得与崔美人合葬此间，消受香光，亦差可不负矣！但将来未知谁葬我唐寅耳！”不觉欷歔泣下。忽遥闻有人朗吟云：“花满山中高士卧，月明林下美人来。”六如急起入林迎揖，则张灵也。六如讶曰：“君死已久，安得来此吟高季迪诗？”灵笑曰：“君以我为真死耶？死者形，不死者性。

    Explanation

    437

    Ah! The talented man and the beautiful woman, once they have reached this point, perhaps the story of Ling and Ying is complete, and the story of Liu Ru is also complete. And Liu Ru, in the following spring, personally visited the tomb to pay respects. He stayed the night beside the third dwelling and tossed and turned, unable to sleep. Opening the window and looking out, he saw ten thousand trees of plum blossoms and a full moon in the sky, not knowing if he was still in the world. Liu Ru sighed and said, "Meng Jin, who lived a wildly unrestrained life, was a man of misfortune, but now he has been buried with the beautiful Cui here, enjoying the fragrance and light, which can be somewhat justified! But I wonder who will bury me, Tang Yin, in the future!" He couldn't help but sob and weep. Suddenly, he heard someone reciting a poem loudly: "Flowers fill the mountains where a noble recluse lies, and under the bright moon, a beautiful woman comes to the woods." Liu Ru hurriedly got up and went into the woods to greet him, and it was Zhang Ling. Liu Ru asked, "You have been dead for a long time, how can you come here to recite the poem of Gao Jidi?" Zhang Ling replied, "Do you think I am truly dead? The body dies, but the spirit does not."

    438

    吾既为一世才子，死后岂若他人泯没耶？今乘此花满山中，高士偃卧，特来造访耳。”复举手前指曰：“此非『月明林下美人来』乎？”六如回顾，有美人姗姗来前，则崔莹也。于是两人携手整襟，向六如拜谢合葬之德。六如方扶掖之，忽又闻有人大呼曰：“我高季迪梅花诗，乃千古绝唱，何物张灵，妄称才子，改雪为花？定须饱我老拳！”六如转瞬之间，灵、莹俱失所在。其人直前呼曰：“当捶此改诗之贼才子！”捽六如欲殴之。六如惊寐，则半窗明月，阒其无人。六如怃然，始信真才子与真佳人，盖死而不死也。因匡坐梅窗下，作《张灵、崔莹合传》，以纪其事。然今日《六如集》中，固未尝见此传也，余又安得而不亟补之哉？

    Explanation

    438

    I, having been a talented scholar of my time, after death, how could I be like others who are forgotten? Now, taking advantage of this time when flowers cover the mountains, the recluses are lying down, I have come specifically to visit. Is this not the 'beauty comes under the moonlight in the forest'? Six-As looked back, and there was a woman coming gracefully forward, it was Cui Ying. Therefore, the two of them held hands and straightened their clothes, bowed to Six-As in gratitude for his kindness in burying them together. Six-As was just supporting her, suddenly, someone shouted loudly: "My poem 'Plum Blossoms' by Gao Jidi, is a timeless masterpiece. What sort of Zhang Ling, unjustly claims to be a talented scholar, changed snow to flowers? Must surely be beaten by my fist!" In the blink of an eye, Zhang Ling and Cui Ying disappeared. Came forward and shouted, "Should beat this thief who altered the poem." Grabbed Six-As and wanted to beat him. Six-As woke up in shock, then half the window was lit by moonlight, and the room was empty. Six-As was filled with a sense of loss, began to believe that true talented scholars and true beauties are immortal. Therefore, sat upright under the plum window, wrote the 'Combined Biography of Zhang Ling and Cui Ying,' to record the event. However, in today's 'Six-As Collection,' it has never been seen before. How could I not urgently add it?

    439

    畸史氏曰：嗟乎！盖吾阅《十美图编》，而后知世间真有才子佳人也。从来稗官家言，大抵真赝参半。若梦晋之名，既章章于《六如集》中；但素琼之事，无从考证。虽然，有其事何必无其人？且安知非作者有为而发乎？独怪梦晋之才，目空千古，而其尚论才子佳人，则耑以太白与莺莺当之。夫太白诚天上仙才，不可有二。若千古佳人，自当以文君为第一。而梦晋顾舍彼取此，厥后果遇素琼，毋乃思崔得崔，适符其谶耶？至于张以情死，崔以情殉，初非有一词半缕之成约，而慷慨从容，等泰山于鸿毛，徒以才色相怜之故。推此志也，凛凛生气，日月争光，又远出琴心犊鼻之上矣！而或者犹追恨于梦晋之蚤死，以为梦晋若不死，则素琼遣归之日，正崔、张好合之年，后此或白头唱和，兰玉盈阶，未可知也。噫！此固庸庸蚩蚩者之厚福也，何有于才子佳人哉！

    Explanation

    439

    畸史氏曰：嗟乎！盖吾阅《十美图编》，而后知世间真有才子佳人也。从来稗官家言，大抵真赝参半。若梦晋之名，既章章于《六如集》中；但素琼之事，无从考证。虽然，有其事何必无其人？且安知非作者有为而发乎？独怪梦晋之才，目空千古，而其尚论才子佳人，则耑以太白与莺莺当之。夫太白诚天上仙才，不可有二。若千古佳人，自当以文君为第一。而梦晋顾舍彼取此，厥后果遇素琼，毋乃思崔得崔，适符其谶耶？至于张以情死，崔以情殉，初非有一词半缕之成约，而慷慨从容，等泰山于鸿毛，徒以才色相怜之故。推此志也，凛凛生气，日月争光，又远出琴心犊鼻之上矣！而或者犹追恨于梦晋之蚤死，以为梦晋若不死，则素琼遣归之日，正崔、张好合之年，后此或白头唱和，兰玉盈阶，未可知也。噫！此固庸庸蚩蚩者之厚福也，何有于才子佳人哉！

    畸史氏说：唉！我读了《十美图编》之后，才知道世间确实有才子佳人。历来那些民间传说，大多是真假参半。像梦晋的名字，已经在《六如集》中明确记载；但关于素琼的事情，却无法考证。然而，既然有其事，为什么没有其人呢？况且，又怎能知道这不是作者有意为之呢？只是奇怪梦晋的才华，竟然目空一切，而他在评价才子佳人时，却专门以太白和莺莺为例。太白确实是天上仙才，无可匹敌。至于千古佳人，自然应该以卓文君为第一。而梦晋却舍弃她而选择了素琼，结果后来果然遇到了素琼，难道不是心想事成，正好应验了预言吗？至于张因为爱情而死，崔因为爱情而殉情，他们最初并没有任何约定，但他们的慷慨从容，却把生死看得像泰山与鸿毛一样轻重，只是因为彼此才华和容貌的相互吸引。由此可以看出他们的志向，凛然的生命力，与日月争辉，又远远超越了琴心与犊鼻的境界！而有人仍然为梦晋的早逝感到遗憾，认为如果梦晋不死，那么素琼被送回的那一天，正是崔和张喜结良缘之年，之后或许会白头偕老，子孙满堂，这也不可得知。唉！这确实是普通人的厚福，与才子佳人又有什么关系呢！

    440

    [张山来曰：梦晋若不蚤死，无以成素琼殉命之奇。此正崔、张得意处也。]
    　　陈老莲别传　毛奇龄大可西河文选
    　　　　洪绶，好画莲，自称老莲。数岁，见李公麟画《孔门弟子》勒本，能指其误处。十四岁，悬其画市中，立致金钱。初法传染时，钱塘蓝瑛工写生，莲请瑛法传染，已而轻瑛。瑛亦自以不逮莲，终其身不写生，曰：“此天授也！”

    Explanation

    440

    Zhang Shanlai said: If Duke Jin had not died early in his dream, there would be no way to accomplish the extraordinary act of Su Qiong sacrificing her life. This is precisely where Cui and Zhang found their satisfaction.

    In the Biography of Chen Laolian from The Collection of Mao Qilin's Works from the Western River, it is recorded:

    Hong Shou had a fondness for painting lotus flowers and referred to himself as Laolian. At a young age, he saw Li Gonglin's painting of The Disciples of Confucius and was able to point out its inaccuracies. At the age of fourteen, he hung his paintings in the marketplace and immediately earned money. When he first studied painting techniques, the Qiantang painter Lan Ying was skilled in capturing the vitality of living subjects. Hong Shou asked Lan Ying to teach him these techniques, but later came to look down on Lan Ying. Lan Ying also believed that he could not match Hong Shou and spent the rest of his life not painting living subjects, saying, "This is a gift from heaven!"

    441

    莲游于酒人，所致金钱随手尽。尤喜为窭儒画，窭儒借莲画给空。豪家索之，千缗勿得也。尝为诸生，督学使索之，亦勿得。顾生平好妇人，非妇人在坐不饮，夕寝非妇人不得寐；有携妇人乞画，辄应云。崇祯末，愍皇帝命供奉，不拜，寻以兵罢；监国中，待诏。王师下浙东，大将军抚军固山，从围城中搜得莲，大喜，急令画，不画；刃迫之，不画；以酒与妇人诱之画。久之，请汇所为画署名，且有粉本。渲染已，大饮，夜抱画寝。及伺之，遁矣！
    　　　　朝鲜、兀良哈、日本、撤马儿罕、乌思藏购莲画，重其直。海内传模为生者数千家。甬东袁鹍贫，为洋船典簿记，藏莲画两幅截竹中，将归，贻日本主。主大喜，重予宴，酬以囊珠，亦传模笔也。

    Explanation

    441

    Lotus wanders among wine drinkers, and the money he receives is spent immediately. He especially likes to paint for poor scholars, who borrow his paintings to fill their homes. Wealthy families demand them, but even a thousand strings of cash cannot obtain them. He once was a student, and the supervisor of education demands them, but they too cannot get them. Throughout his life, he favored women, and he would not drink unless women were present. He could not sleep without a woman by his side at night. When someone brought a woman to ask for a painting, he would agree. At the end of the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, the Emperor of Mercy ordered him to serve, but he refused, and soon after, he was dismissed due to military affairs. During the regency, he was appointed as a court painter. When the royal army marched into eastern Zhejiang, the great general, the pacifier of the army, and the governor of Guishan, searched for Lotus within the besieged city, greatly pleased, urgently ordered him to paint, but he refused; they threatened him with swords, but he still refused; they offered wine and women to entice him to paint. After some time, he asked to compile all his paintings, sign them, and also have a model for the paintings. After finishing the painting, he had a big drink, and at night, he held the painting while sleeping. When they watched him, he had already fled!

    Korea, Wulanh, Japan, Sarmarhan, and U-Tsang purchased Lotus's paintings, valuing them highly. Within the country, thousands of families made a living by copying his paintings. In Yongdong, Yuan Kun, who was poor, worked as a clerk on a foreign ship, hiding two of Lotus's paintings in bamboo sections, intending to return them to the Japanese lord. The lord was greatly pleased, hosting a grand banquet, rewarding him with a bag of pearls, and it was also a copy of the brushwork.

    442

    莲尝模周长史画，至再三，犹不欲已。人指所模画谓之曰：“此画已过周，而犹嗛嗛，何也？”曰：“此所以不及者也，吾画易见好，则能事未尽也。长史本至能，而若无能，此难能也。吾试以为文言之：今夫为文者，非持论，即摭事耳。以议属文，以文属事，虽备经营，亦安容有作者之意存其中耶？自作家者出，而作法秩然，每一文至，必衔毫吮墨，一若有作者之意先于行间，舍夫论与事而就我之法，曰如是则当，如是则不当，而文亡矣！故夫画，气韵兼力，沨沨容容，周、秦之文也。勾绰捉勒，随境堑错，汉、魏文也。驱遣于法度之中，钉前燕后，陵轹矜轶，抟裂顿斫，作气满前，八家也。故画有入神家，有名家，有当家，有作家，有匠者家，吾唯不离乎作家，以负此嗛也。”其论如此。

    Explanation

    442

    Lian once copied the painting of Zhou, the Chief Secretary, up to three times, still not wanting to stop. Someone pointed to the copied painting and said to him, "This painting has surpassed Zhou, yet still feels unsatisfied. Why is that?" Lian replied, "This is why it falls short. My painting is easily liked, so the artist's skill is not fully expressed. The Chief Secretary is originally very skilled, yet appears as if he has no skill. This is difficult to achieve. I will try to explain this through writing: Nowadays, writing is either argumentative or factual. Writing is either argumentative or factual. Even if it is fully planned, how can the author's intent be present in it? Since the original creators emerged, the methods of creation became orderly. Every time a text is written, it must be filled with the author's intent, as if it were present in the lines themselves. By focusing on my own methods rather than argument or fact, I say that this is right, and that is wrong, and the text is lost! Therefore, in painting, combining spirit and power, flowing and graceful, is the style of the Zhou and Qin dynasties. Drawing and capturing, following the environment and creating intricate patterns, is the style of the Han and Wei dynasties. Driving within the bounds of rules, pinning before and after, overwhelming and surpassing, crushing and cutting, creating a full presence, is the style of the eight great masters. Therefore, in painting, there are those who enter the realm of the divine, those who are famous, those who are master artists, those who create, and those who are craftsmen. I only adhere to being an artist who creates, to bear this dissatisfaction." His argument was as such.

    443

    莲画以天胜，然各有法：骨法法吴生，用笔法郑法士，墨法荆浩，疏渲传染法管仲姬，古皇圣贤孔门弟子法李公麟，观音疏笔法吴生，细公麟，诸天、罗汉、菩萨、神馗、鬼、{者鬼}法张骠骑，衣冠士法阎右相，士女法周长史昉，几幛、尊卣、瓶罂、什器、戎衣、穹庐、番马、骆驼、羊犬法赵承旨，钩勒竹法刘泾，折枝桃、牡丹、梅、水仙、草花法黄检校、钱选，鸟睛、花须、点漆、凸厚法宣和，蜂蝉、蛱蝶、蛴螬、螳螂、蛛蛗法宣和，亦杂法崔、徐、黄父子，莲法于莲。于青年以莲称

    Explanation

    443

    Lotus paintings excel in their depiction of heaven, yet each has its own method: the structure follows Wu Daozi's method, the brushwork follows Zheng Fashi's method, the ink follows Jing Hao's method, the light and shade and color blending follow Guan Zhongji's method, the depiction of ancient emperors, sages, and Confucian disciples follows Li Gonglin's method, the depiction of Guanyin with sparse strokes follows Wu Daozi's method, the fine details follow Li Gonglin's method, the depiction of celestial beings, arhats, bodhisattvas, gods, demons, and ghosts follows Zhang Biaoyi's method, the depiction of scholars in official robes follows Yan Youxiang's method, the depiction of ladies follows Zhou Changshi Fang's method, the depiction of screens, vases, bottles, utensils, military clothing, yurts, horses, camels, sheep, and dogs follows Zhao Chengzhi's method, the depiction of bamboo with hook and outline follows Liu Jing's method, the depiction of peach branches, peonies, plum blossoms, water lilies, and wildflowers follows Huang Jianxiao and Qian Xuan's methods, the depiction of bird eyes, flower stamens, painted lacquer, and thick layers follows Huizong (Emperor Huizong)'s method, the depiction of bees, cicadas, butterflies, larvae, mantises, and spiders follows Huizong (Emperor Huizong)'s method, and it also incorporates the methods of Cui, Xu, and the Huang family, while the lotus follows the lotus itself. In his youth, he was called "Lotus."

    444

    章侯《博古牌》，为新安黄子立摩刻，其人能手也。章侯死后，子立昼见章侯至，遂命妻子办衣敛，曰：“陈公画地狱变相成，呼我摩刻。”然则莲画之贵，岂独人间邪？原评
    　　　　[张山来曰：陈章侯《水浒牌》，近年如画灯，如席上小屏风，皆取为稿本。其为益于世者甚多，则其食报于将来者，所必然耳。]
    　　桑山人传　毛奇龄大可西河文选
    　　　　山人许氏，汴人，少举茂才。崇祯中，尝献剿贼三策于阁部督师杨君，不用。既而为东平侯刘泽清幕客。与泽清语不合，辞去。乡人怨家发其隐事于清师之镇汴者，走匿桑下，因姓桑，号桑山人。山人乃与嵩阳曹道士游。夜坐耳鸣，丝竹徐发，若有物拔其顶，耸身丈余，骨节皆通。尝卖药嵩山庙市，以水酌喑者，能言；许州小男为狐所苦，呼狐斩之。既还汴，怨家见曰：“此许澄茂才也。”帅捕十许人迹至，山人乃独身指挥，尽缚诸捕者，揖怨家去谢之，而身游衡阳不返云。

    Explanation

    444

    Translation:

    Chen Hongshou's Bo Gu Pai was engraved by Huang Zili of Xin'an, a master of his craft. After Chen's death, Huang Zili saw Chen during the day and was instructed by him to prepare his burial clothes, saying, "Master Chen has completed his painting of the transformations in Hell; call me to engrave it." Thus, the value of Chen's paintings extends beyond the human realm, does it not? Original commentary:

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: Chen Hongshou's Shui Hu Pai has, in recent times, been used as a model for painted lanterns and small folding screens. Its benefits to the world are numerous, and thus the retribution he will receive in the future is inevitable.]

    Biography of the Mulberry Mountain Man by Mao Qilin, Dake Xihe, from Xihe Wenxuan

    The Mulberry Mountain Man, surnamed Xu, was from Kaifeng. He was young and talented. During the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, he once offered three strategies for suppressing bandits to Yang, the high-ranking military commander, but they were not adopted. Later, he became a counselor in the entourage of Liu Zequan, Duke of Dongping. Disagreeing with Liu, he left his service. His enemies revealed his secrets to the Qing forces stationed in Kaifeng, and he fled to hide under mulberry trees, adopting the surname Sang and calling himself the Mulberry Mountain Man. The Mulberry Mountain Man then associated with a Taoist priest named Cao in Songyang. One night, while sitting, he heard ringing in his ears, and soft music began to play. It seemed as if something was pulling his head, and he rose several feet, with all his joints connected. He once sold medicine in the temple market of Songshan, and with water, he could make the mute speak. In Xu Prefecture, a young boy was troubled by a fox, and the Mulberry Mountain Man called it and killed it. After returning to Kaifeng, his enemies recognized him and said, "This is Xu, the talented young man." A group of ten or so people were sent to capture him, but the Mulberry Mountain Man, alone, directed them, tying up all the pursuers, bowing to his enemies, thanking them, and then traveling to Hengyang, never to return.

    445

    [张山来曰：此等道士，我恨不得遇之。]
    　　李姬传　侯方域朝宗壮悔堂集
    　　　　李姬者，名香，母曰贞丽。贞丽有侠气，尝一夜博，输千金立尽；所交接皆当世豪杰，尤与阳羡陈贞慧善也。姬为其养女，亦侠而慧，略知书，能辨别士大夫贤否。张学士溥、夏吏部允彝亟称之。少风调皎爽不群。十三岁，从吴人周如松受歌。玉茗堂四传奇，皆能尽其音节，尤工《琵琶词》，然不轻发也。雪苑侯生己卯来金陵，与相识。姬尝邀侯生为诗，而自歌以偿之。

    Explanation

    445

    Zhang Shanlai said: "I cannot help but wish to meet such Daoists."
    Li Ji, whose name was Xiang, had a mother named Zhenli. Zhenli had a chivalrous spirit and once gambled all night, losing a thousand gold coins in one go. Those she associated with were all the great men of the time, especially close friends with Chen Zhenhui from Yangxian. Li Ji was adopted by her mother and had a unique charm, being bright and refreshing. She had a basic knowledge of literature and could distinguish between good and bad officials. Mr. Zhang Xueshi Pu and Mr. Xia Lishi Yunyi highly praised her. At the age of thirteen, she learned singing from Zhou Rusong, a person from Wu. She could fully capture the rhythm and melody of the four legendary plays from the Yuming Hall, especially excelling in Pipa poetry, though she did not easily perform it. In the year of Gui Mao, Mr. Hou from Xueyuan came to Jinling and became acquainted with her. Li Ji once invited Mr. Hou to compose poetry and sang to repay him.

    446

    初皖人阮大铖者，以阿附魏忠贤论城旦，屏居金陵，为清议所斥。阳羡陈贞慧、贵池吴应箕实首其事，持之力。大铖不得已，欲侯生为解之，乃假所善王将军，日载酒食与侯生游。姬曰：“王将军贫，非结客者。公子盍叩之？”侯生三问，将军乃屏人述大铖意。姬私语侯生曰：“妾少从假母识阳羡君，其人有高义，闻吴君尤铮铮。今皆与公子善，奈何以阮公负至交乎？且以公子之世望，安事阮公？公子读万卷书，所见岂后于贱妾耶？”侯生大呼称善。醉而卧，王将军者殊怏怏，因辞去，不复通。
    　　　　未几，侯生下第，姬置酒桃叶渡，歌《琵琶词》以送之，曰：“公子才名文藻，雅不减中郎。中郎学不补行，今《琵琶》所传词固妄，然尝昵董卓，不可掩也。公子豪迈不羁，又失意，此去相见未可期，愿终自爱，无忘妾所歌《琵琶词》也。妾亦不复歌矣！”

    Explanation

    446

    At the beginning, there was a man from Wan named Ruan Dacheng, who was condemned to the punishment of Chengdan for flattery of Wei Zhongxian and lived in seclusion in Jinling (Nanjing), criticized by the public opinion. Yangxian Chen Zhenhui and Guichi Wu Yingji were the first to initiate this matter and persistently upheld it. Out of necessity, Ruan Dacheng wanted Hou Sheng to mediate for him and relied on his good friend General Wang, daily bringing wine and food to entertain Hou Sheng. The concubine said, "General Wang is poor and not someone who can form connections. Why not ask him?" Hou Sheng asked three times, and General Wang privately told Hou Sheng Ruan Dacheng's intention. The concubine privately spoke to Hou Sheng, saying, "I was young and knew Yangxian Master through my foster mother. He is a man of high virtue, and I heard that Wu Master is particularly upright. Now they are all friends with you. Why should you betray your closest friends for Ruan Gong? And considering your noble family background, why serve Ruan Gong? You have read ten thousand scrolls of books. What you see is certainly not inferior to me." Hou Sheng loudly praised it, drunk and lay down. General Wang was very disappointed and therefore took his leave, never communicating again.

    Shortly after, Hou Sheng failed the imperial examination. The concubine set up a banquet at Taoye Ferry and sang the 'Pipa Poem' to bid him farewell, saying, "Your talent and literary style are not inferior to Zhonglang. Zhonglang's learning did not compensate for his behavior. Now the words of the 'Pipa' poem are indeed false, but he once befriended Dong Zhuo, which cannot be hidden. You are bold and unrestrained, and have failed. We may not meet again. I hope you will always take care of yourself, do not forget the 'Pipa Poem' I sang. I will no longer sing it!"

    447

    侯生去后，而故开府田仰者，以金三百锾邀姬一见。姬固却之。开府惭且怒，且有以中伤姬。姬叹曰：“田公宁异于阮公乎？吾向之所赞于侯公子者谓何？今乃利其金而赴之，是妾卖公子矣！”卒不往。
    　　　　[张山来曰：吾友岸堂主人作《桃花扇》传奇，谱此事，惜未及《琵琶词》。岂以其词不雅驯故略之耶？]
    　　记缢鬼　泰邮王明德今樵读律佩觿
    　　　　凡系有人缢死，其宅内及缢死之处，往往有相从而缢，及缢之非一人者，俗谓之“讨替身”，谓已死之鬼，求以自代。此种渺茫幻妄、惑世诬民之谈，岂君子所乐闻？然书谓“子不语怪”，夫于怪仅曰“不语”，则是怪亦世所尝有，非云世绝无怪也。

    Explanation

    447

    After "侯生" left, the former official "田仰" offered "姬" three hundred units of gold to meet with him. "姬" firmly refused the offer. "田仰" felt both ashamed and angry, and he had ways to slander "姬." "姬" sighed and said, "How could '田公' be different from '阮公'? What did I praise about '侯公子' before? Now I am seeking profit from the gold and going to see him, which is like selling myself to '公子'!" In the end, she did not go.

    [According to Zhang Shanlai: My friend "岸堂主人" wrote the legendary play "The Peony Pavilion," which depicts this matter, but it did not include "The Pipa Ode." Could it be that he omitted it because the words were not elegant or refined?]

    Record of a Hanged Ghost — Taizhou Wang Mingde, a contemporary scholar of law

    In cases where someone has hanged themselves, often there are others who follow suit in the same house and at the same place where the first person hanged themselves, sometimes more than one person. This is commonly called "seeking a substitute," meaning that the dead ghost seeks to replace itself. Such vague and fantastical, misleading and deceiving people's talk, is it something a gentleman would enjoy hearing? However, the book says, "Confucius does not talk about strange things," and as for strange things, he only says "does not talk about." Therefore, strange things are also what the world has experienced, not saying that the world has no strange things at all.

    448

    吾乡有张姓者，其家仅足自食。夫先卧，妇则仍工女红。偷儿乘夜逾垣往窃，未敢竟入，伺于窗外。见床侧一鬼妇，向本妇先嬉后泣，拜跪再三。本妇睨视数次，忽长叹，潸然泪下。偷儿心惊，专心伺之。妇即自理绢帛，仍有不忍即行之状。鬼妇更复再拜祈求，本妇方行自缢。偷儿急甚，大声疾呼，其夫鼾呼若不闻。偷儿无法以救，适檐下有竹竿，取从窗棂中撺击鬼妇，其夫方觉。偷儿呼令急为开门，相助解救。在此妇固不自解觅死为何事，其夫亦不问呼门为何人，而偷儿亦自忘乎其为偷儿矣。事后，各道其详，因发床侧之壁视之，其中梁畔实有先年自缢绳头尚存，虽云朽烂非真，而其形其迹，则仍宛然。由此以观，则凡世俗所传，亦未尽属无根之谈、荒唐之论矣。

    Explanation

    448

    In my village, there lived a man surnamed Zhang, whose family barely supported itself. The husband went to bed first, while the wife continued her needlework. Thieves took advantage of the night to climb over the wall and steal, but they were not bold enough to enter completely, so they waited outside the window. They saw a ghostly woman beside the bed, who first played with the real woman and then wept, kneeling and bowing repeatedly. The wife glanced several times, suddenly sighed, and shed tears. The thieves were startled and watched intently. The wife then handled her silk fabrics, still showing reluctance to leave. The ghostly woman knelt and prayed again, and the wife finally hanged herself. The thieves were very anxious and shouted loudly, but the husband snored as if he didn't hear. The thieves had no way to save her, so just then, there was a bamboo pole under the eaves, which they grabbed and thrust through the window to strike the ghostly woman. The husband finally woke up. The thieves shouted for him to quickly open the door and help rescue her. Afterward, they each recounted the details, and they then uncovered the wall beside the bed to examine it. There was indeed a rope used for hanging that had been there for years, although it was said to be decayed and not real, but its shape and traces were still clearly visible. From this perspective, all the stories passed down in the common folk are not entirely baseless or absurd.

    449

    据故老所示辟除秘法，不知出自何典，颇有行之而验者。法于自缢之人，尚在悬挂未解时，即于所悬身下，暗为记明。于方行解下时，或即用铁器，或即用大石，镇而压之。然后于所镇四面，深为挖取，将所镇土中，层层拨视，或三五寸，或尺许，或二三尺，于中定有如鸡骨、及如各骨之物在内，取而或弃或焚，则可辟除将来，不致有再缢之事。实为屡试屡验，其理殊不可解。但及时即挖则得之浅而易，迟则深而难，然亦不出八、九尺外也。虽云幻妄无稽，不知何以行之实有可据？得毋如圣哲所云“天地之大，何所不有？”“心知理之所必无，安知非情之所必有？”其殆是欤？愚故从而笔之。即或行之未验，聊以解愚夫愚妇之疑，亦未必非拯救自缢之一预道也。

    Explanation

    449

    According to the secret method shown by the elderly, it is unknown from which classic it originates, but there are cases where it has been tried and proven effective. The method involves, when a person is hanging and has not yet been untied, marking the spot beneath where they are suspended. When the person is being untied, one may use an iron tool or a large stone to press and weigh them down. Then, around the area where the person was pressed, one should dig deeply, layer by layer, examining the soil that was pressed down, perhaps three or five inches, or a foot, or two or three feet deep. Within this, there will certainly be bone-like objects or other such items. These should be either discarded or burned, and this can prevent future occurrences of hanging. It has indeed been repeatedly tested and found effective, though the reasoning behind it is particularly difficult to understand. However, if dug up promptly, the objects will be found shallow and easily accessible; if delayed, they will be deeper and harder to find, but they will not exceed a depth of eight or nine feet. Although it may seem like a fanciful and baseless tale, there must be some practical basis for its effectiveness. Could it be as the sages have said, "The world is vast, and nothing is impossible?" "Though one may know that something is logically impossible, how can one be sure it is not emotionally or experientially real?" Is this perhaps the case? I therefore write this down. Even if it has not been proven effective, it may still help to alleviate the doubts of the ignorant and uneducated, and it may not be without merit as a preventive measure for saving someone from hanging.

    450

    [张山来曰：世间自尽之鬼，如投河、自缢、自刎之类，俗谓其必讨替身。予素不之信。审若此，则此等鬼必有定额，不容增减耶？真不可解。]
    　　
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷十四
    　　平苗神异记　永平王谦撝斋邮寄钞本
    　　　　城步，非邑也，故属湖广宝庆之武冈州，设官城步巡检司。苗民杂处，民不及什一，数岁辄窃发，守土将吏不能胜，恒被害。有明弘治甲子，峒苗李再万倡乱，巡抚阎公讨平之，疏请建县治，用资弹压；爰割武冈之绥宁二里半隶焉。城于巫水之上，凡五峒十八寨环其外。为宰者闻父老谈旧事，目瞪股栗，若不终日。城雉不盈百，东、西、南列三门。北门故有汉前将军关帝祠岿然踞城上。邑人敬事之，祷求必应，然未尝现身示异也。

    Explanation

    450

    Zhang Shanlai said: "Ghosts who have committed suicide, such as those who drown themselves, hang themselves, or cut their throats, are said to seek a replacement. I have never believed this. If indeed it is so, then these ghosts must have a fixed quota, and cannot be increased or decreased? Truly inexplicable."

    虞初新志 Volume 14
    平苗神异记 - Copied by Wang Qianhui during the Yongping period
    Chengbu, originally a district, belonged to the Baoqing Prefecture of Wugang State. An official position of City Inspection was established there. The Miao people lived there, and the Han population was less than one-tenth. Every few years, they would secretly rebel, and the local officials could not control them, often suffering losses. In the year 1504 during the Ming dynasty, Li Zaiman of the Dong Miao tribe led a rebellion, which was quelled by Governor Yan Gong. He then requested to establish a county administration to maintain control, and two and a half li from Wugang were divided to form the new county. The city was built on the Wushui River, with five Dong and eighteen Miao villages surrounding it. The local officials, when hearing old stories from the elders, would be so shocked that they would be unable to finish the day. The city walls were less than a hundred feet high, with three gates on the east, west, and south sides. On the north gate, there was an ancient temple of Guan Yu, the revered general and deity, standing majestically on the city wall. The people of the town revered the temple, and their prayers were always answered, but the temple had never shown any supernatural phenomena.

    451

    余以康熙庚申谒选，得是邑宰，亲故饯别者，为余危。余笑谢之。初莅治，苗不敢猖獗。迨癸亥七月朔，粵西全州西延峒苗杨应龙，啸聚苗瑶一千七百余党，将侵城步。杀人祭旗，誓以七夕决胜，谓孤城无备，可谈笑取。先是余逆揣变作，阴募敢死士三百人，练习有法。及侦得实，单骑相地势，秘授计。七日阅，贼直薄城下，望见旌旗刀戟皆严整，相顾错愕，如出神算，不复有斗志。余属典史徐士奇、把总王明守北面，练总杨应和守南城，抚苗陈天武守西城。余独当东面，扼其冲，率精锐出城，乘贼暮气，深入其阻。应龙仓猝失措，有左道用符演咒法，无一效，皆手戮之。余党胆落奔溃，不二里，伏兵四起，除被刀箭中火器死者，生擒五百余人。渠魁应龙，故马宝部下裨将。助贼为妖者，黄羊山道士周大圣也。

    Explanation

    451

    I was appointed as the magistrate of this county in the year of Gengshen during the Kangxi era. My relatives and friends who saw me off were worried for me. I smiled and thanked them. At first, the bandits did not dare to act recklessly. By the first day of July in the year of Guihai, Yang Yinglong, a bandit leader from Xiyantong in Quanzhou, Guangxi, gathered over 1,700 bandits and Miao and Yao tribesmen, planning to invade Chengbu. They killed people and sacrificed them to a flag, vowing to win on the Qixi Festival, believing that the isolated city was unprepared and could be easily captured. Before this, I had anticipated the uprising and secretly recruited 300 brave soldiers, training them according to a plan. When I confirmed the situation, I rode alone to inspect the terrain and secretly gave them instructions. Seven days later, the bandits directly attacked the city gates, seeing the banners and weapons all neatly arranged. They looked at each other in confusion, as if they were outsmarted, and lost their fighting spirit. I assigned the county magistrate Xu Shiqi and the captain Wang Ming to guard the north, the drillmaster Yang Yinghe to guard the south, and the Miao pacifier Chen Tianwu to guard the west. I alone guarded the east, controlling the key position, leading the elite forces out of the city, taking advantage of the bandits' low morale, and penetrating their defenses. Yang Yinglong was caught off guard and disoriented, using strange methods and spells, none of which worked, and were all put to death by hand. The remaining bandits lost their courage and fled, within two li, the ambush troops rose up. Except for those killed by swords, arrows, and firearms, more than 500 were captured alive. The leader Yang Yinglong was a subordinate general of Ma Bao. The one who helped the bandits was the Taoist Zhou Dasheng from Huangyang Mountain.

    452

    及讯贼“曷不奔窜，而屈首受擒？”佥曰：“方将遁，恍惚有赤面长髯大将，乘白马自天而下，指挥神兵，八面旋绕不得脱。”余始惊异，旋问我军，所见无异辞。日既晡，振旅归，亟登城谒帝，仰见帝面汗浃如雨，如甫释甲状，益加悚惕，叩首谢。
    　　　　自惟凉德，何敢辱帝力？或者正可胜邪，诚可回天？今兹平苗斩妖，不请一兵，不伤一民者，真神助，非人力也！余何人斯，敢妄据天功哉？爰是新庙貌，肃几宴，远近奔走者日盛。邑人士作《平妖传》，及诗歌传奇纪事，谓百年来所未有。苗患遂不复作，今又二十余稔矣。每岁七夕，余必斋肃祀帝，无忘厥功。独怪帝乘马故赤色，此独白。或疑马援尝伏五溪蛮，得毋伏波将军来耶？余谓不然，神像既汗浃示灵爽矣。余非疑乘马者非帝也，疑帝之马何以白也，姑阙疑以俟考。

    Explanation

    452

    When I questioned the captives, "Why did you not flee, but instead surrender?" they all replied in unison: "We were just about to escape when we suddenly saw a red-faced, bearded general riding a white horse descending from the heavens, commanding divine soldiers who surrounded us from all sides, leaving us unable to escape." I was initially surprised and then asked my own troops, who confirmed seeing the same thing. By evening, I gathered my troops and returned to the city to report to the emperor. Upon seeing him, I noticed that his face was drenched in sweat, as if he had just been released from a state of tension, which made me even more respectful and fearful, prompting me to prostrate myself in gratitude.

    Reflecting on my own limited virtue, how dare I burden the emperor's efforts? Perhaps this victory was indeed due to the emperor's power, or could it have changed the course of events? This time, in suppressing the rebels and defeating the supernatural threat, we did not request a single soldier and did not harm a single civilian—this was truly divine assistance, not human effort! What am I, to claim such a divine victory? Since then, a new temple was constructed, and solemn rituals were set up, with an increasing number of people from far and near coming to pay respects. Local scholars and poets wrote The Tale of Suppressing the Supernatural Threat and composed poems and legends to commemorate the event, calling it unprecedented in a century. The threat from the rebels was permanently eliminated and has not reappeared for over twenty years. Every year on the seventh day of the seventh lunar month, I must fast and solemnly worship the emperor, never forgetting his achievements. I find it peculiar that the emperor's horse, which is usually red, is white in this case. Some speculate that it might be a reference to General Ma Yuan, who once subdued the Five Streams barbarians. I do not believe this, as the divine image's sweating already indicates its spiritual presence. I am not questioning whether the rider is the emperor, but I am curious about why the emperor's horse is white, and I will leave this as a point for further investigation.

    453

    附：吴宝崖曰：拎明初某勋戚家畜一白马，肥且健。一夕关帝梦示云：某省寇乱，欲假而马助兵。旦起视厩中马，僵卧不起，盖摄其神往矣。迨奏凯，勋戚益敬服。京师人异之，因建白马庙奉帝。自是帝现身显灵，捍倭破贼，辄骑白马以为常。今大司马遂宁张公尝云尔。则城步平苗神异，信哉为帝无疑也。特旧传帝驭赤兔马，一日千里，岂一蹶不复振耶？抑久用而瘏，用人间马协力耶？附识以资传闻之异云。

    Explanation

    453

    Appendix: Wu Baoyi Said

    During the early Ming dynasty, a noble family kept a white horse that was both fat and strong. One night, Guan Di appeared in a dream and said, "There is a rebellion in a certain province, and I wish to borrow your horse to aid the army." The next morning, when they looked at the horse in the stable, it was found lying motionless and unable to rise, likely because its spirit had been taken away. When the campaign was victorious, the noble family became even more reverent. The people of the capital found it strange and built a White Horse Temple to honor the deity. From then on, the deity appeared and showed divine power, defending against the Japanese pirates and defeating the rebels, always riding the white horse. Now, General Zhang of Suining has said so. Thus, the miraculous event in Chengbu, where the Miao were pacified, confirms that it was indeed the deity without doubt. However, according to old legends, the deity rode a red rabbit horse that could travel a thousand miles in a day, could it not recover from a single stumble? Or perhaps it was exhausted from prolonged use, and needed the help of a human horse? This is recorded as an addition to provide variety to the stories.

    454

    附：纪香木作像
    　　　　吴陈琰宝崖
    　　　　观察永年王公，初仕城步，平峒苗之乱，感关帝神兵之助，将特立帝像以祀。一日巫水暴涨，浮一香木于张家冲殊胜庵前。僧法彻见而异之，谓若有神运，当留镇山门。士民请于公，作像奉之，公为碑文以纪。愚按先辈黄贞父云：“江南文德桥，有香楠木一株，长五丈许，浮秦淮而下。诸生徐嘉宾梦神告曰：是乃聚宝门外关庙物也。于是收而斫之，作三义像。二事何后先合符也？大抵神物不世出，有主则灵。巫水之木，安知非感王公正气，为弹压溪蛮百世不复萌乱之兆耶？江南之木感于梦，则一介不可妄取，天下事类然矣！矧倚恃权要，窃据神物，如周宣王鼎为严嵩祟者，可胜道哉？
    　　　　[张山来曰：今壬午岁，苗民投诚剃发，慑伏于圣天子之威灵，直当与虞帝之舞干羽而格有苗者辉映后先。读此记而益信。]

    Explanation

    454

    Appendix: Record of the Carved Image from Perfumed Wood

    Wu Chen Yan Bao Ya

    Governor Wang of Yongnian, when he first served in Chengbu, quelled the rebellion of the Dong Miao and was grateful for the assistance of Guan Di's divine soldiers. He intended to erect a statue of Guan Di for worship. One day, the Wushui River swelled, and a piece of perfumed wood floated to the front of Zhangjia Stream's Shusheng Temple. The monk Fachè saw it and found it extraordinary, saying that if it seemed to be divinely moved, it should be left to guard the mountain gate. The officials and common people requested the governor to carve an image from it, which he did, and he wrote an inscription on a stele to commemorate the event.

    I, a humble person, follow the words of my predecessor Huang Zhenfu: "In the southern region, on Wende Bridge, there is a perfumed nanmu tree, about five zhang long, which floated downstream on the Qinhuai River. A student named Xu Jiajun dreamed that a spirit told him: 'This is the material for the Guan Temple outside the Jubaomen Gate.' Thus, he collected and cut it to make an image of the Three Heroes. How do these two events correspond in time? Generally, divine objects do not appear in every generation, but they become effective when they have a master. How can we know that the wood from Wushui is not a sign of the righteous spirit of Governor Wang, which will suppress the Ximan people for a hundred generations and prevent further rebellion? The wood from Jiangnan, which was felt in a dream, should not be taken lightly. How much more so for those who rely on power and seize divine objects, like the tripod of King Xuan of Zhou, which became a demon for Yan Song. How can it be described?"

    [Zhang Shanglai said: In the year Renwu, the Miao people surrendered and shaved their heads, submitting to the awe-inspiring power of the Holy Emperor. It should be compared to the dance of the dry feathers and the ritual to pacify the Miao people by Emperor Yu, shining forth in both past and present. Reading this record will increase one's faith.]

    455

    纪老生妄讼　钱塘吴陈琰宝崖手授钞本
    　　　　永年马兆煃，中崇祯庚辰进士，癸未殿试本朝，由行人考选巡按湖北。有郧阳老生某投牒云：“运将鼎革，不闻汉寿关公扶我国祚，请下令讯之。”马可其请，遽发郧阳司理某亲鞫。司理奉令唯谨，委胥役往招之。役亦莫知所从，谒关庙叩首谢过。起，见香炉侧白镪一锭，始未尝见也，乃悟神亦如人世赏劳然者。旋复司理，悬牌某日听鞫。届期，老生果至，空际忽有旋风自城南来，突现帝像，衣冠皆与今世同，隐示气数难回，帝亦从时制也。现身未久，驾空而去。司理及胥吏惊怖欲绝，老生已昏仆，七窍流血死。
    　　　　愚哉老生！懵天运而咎神，神其能宥乎？若巡方贸然许，司理贸然行，胥役贸然往，皆愚之愚者。而帝必现身说法，所以儆愚者至矣哉！冒渎者可鉴矣！马氏尚存案卷，永年王观察公犹及见之。

    Explanation

    455

    A Foolish Scholar's False Lawsuit — Manuscript Provided by Wu Chenyan Baoyai of Qiantang

    Mr. Ma Zhaokui of Yongnian passed the imperial examinations in the year Gengchen of the Chongzhen era and was selected as an imperial censor in the year Guiwei, serving as the provincial inspector of Hubei. An old scholar from Yunyang submitted a petition stating, "The times are about to undergo a great change, yet I have not heard of the Duke of Hanshou, Guan Gong, supporting our dynasty. Please issue an order to investigate this." Mr. Ma agreed to his request and immediately ordered the Prefect of Yunyang to personally interrogate the matter. The Prefect obeyed the order strictly and entrusted the clerks and servants to go and summon the old scholar. The clerks and servants did not know what to do and went to the temple of Guan Gong, knelt down, and apologized for their mistake. Upon rising, they saw a piece of silver coin beside the incense burner, which they had never seen before. They then realized that the deity rewards and punishes just like in the human world. Soon returning to the Prefect, they posted a notice to hear the case on a certain day. On the appointed day, the old scholar indeed arrived. Suddenly, a whirlwind appeared in the air from the south of the city, and a divine image appeared. The attire was the same as in the current world, implying that the fate is hard to reverse, and the deity also follows the current system. The image appeared briefly and then disappeared into the air. The Prefect and the clerks were terrified to the point of death, and the old scholar fainted and died, with blood flowing from his seven orifices.

    How foolish the old scholar was! Ignorant of the heavenly will and blaming the deity, how could the deity forgive such ignorance? If the provincial inspector rashly agreed, the Prefect rashly acted, and the clerks and servants rashly went, all were foolish in their foolishness. Yet the deity must have appeared to teach a lesson, to warn the foolish to the utmost! Those who dare to be disrespectful should take this as a warning! Mr. Ma still keeps the case files, and Mr. Wang, the Observer of Yongnian, still saw them.

    456

    [张山来曰：若巡方不贸然许，司理不贸然行，胥役不贸然往，亦不能显此灵异。]
    　　会仙记　宜兴徐喈凤竹逸愿息斋文集
    　　　　会仙者，非真仙也，有似乎仙则仙之矣；非会其面也，闻其言如会其面矣。曷言乎有似乎仙也？知人心中之事，知人未来之祸福，非仙而能之乎？曷言乎如会其面也？不见其形，得闻其声，有问必答，语皆切中，非如会其面乎？
    　　　　壬戌春正月，扶风桥许生，名丹，字若夔，同其父玉卿入城探亲。去城二里许，遇两美女视之而笑。许生素谨朴，不动念。是夕宿亲袁氏家，卧小楼上。灯灭，忽闻剥啄声，问之，则称“奴家。”许生父子怪之，急叩主人门，大呼有鬼。主人率僮婢秉烛出，一无所见。坐逾时许，辞主人。主人退，复作声，述许家平日事详而确。且说：“奴与生有夫妇缘，故来相访。”许益疑而畏之，假寐不与言。遂倚楼唱时曲数阕，达旦而去。

    Explanation

    456

    Zhang Shanlai said: If the inspector does not rashly agree, if the magistrate does not rashly act, and if the clerks do not rashly go, this miraculous event would not be revealed.

    A Record of Meeting a Fairy by Xu Jie Feng of Yixing, from the Zhuyi Yuanxi Zhai Wenji

    One who meets a fairy is not a true fairy but seems like one and thus is called a fairy; it is not a face-to-face meeting but hearing his words is like meeting him face-to-face. Why is it said that he seems like a fairy? Because he knows the thoughts in people's hearts and their future misfortunes and blessings. Can anyone other than a fairy do this? Why is it said that hearing his words is like meeting him face-to-face? Although his form is not seen, his voice is heard. Any question asked receives an answer, and all his words are accurate. Is this not like meeting him face-to-face?

    In the first month of spring in the year Renxu, Xu Sheng, a man from Fufeng Bridge, whose name was Dan and courtesy name Ruokui, went into the city with his father, Yujing, to visit relatives. About two miles from the city, they met two beautiful women who looked at them and smiled. Xu Sheng, who was naturally cautious and simple-minded, did not have any thoughts. That night, he stayed at the home of his relative Yuan, sleeping in a small upstairs room. When the lamp went out, he suddenly heard a knocking sound. He asked, and they replied, "We are maidservants." Xu Sheng and his father were surprised and urgently knocked on the master's door, shouting loudly that there was a ghost. The master came out with the maids and pages, holding candles, but saw nothing. They sat for over an hour before bidding farewell to the master. After the master left, they made another sound, describing in detail and accurately the daily affairs of the Xu family. They also said, "I have a marital destiny with the young master, so I have come to visit." Xu Sheng became even more suspicious and afraid, pretending to sleep and not speaking to them. They then leaned against the wall and sang several popular songs of the time, leaving at dawn.

    457

    阅十日，生自外入卧室，见前途遇美女，艳服坐其床，旁一美婢侍。许生怪之，细询其来历，自言：“姓胡，字淑贞。五百年前，在宋真宗宫，生寺人，奴采女，意甚相悦，订来世为夫妇。不意奴堕狐胎，生转数世不相值。今奴修炼将成，乘生娘子归宁，了此夙缘。毋疑我也。”生以告其祖汉昭。汉昭故明秀才，年已七十余，闻而怪之。急入室，无所见，但闻妇人声，以太公呼之：“请坐，受奴家拜。”汉昭心知是妖，而无法祛之。

    Explanation

    457

    After ten days, Xu Sheng entered the bedroom from the outside, where he encountered a beautiful woman ahead of him. She was sitting on her bed in splendid attire, with a beautiful maid serving beside her. Xu Sheng found it strange and asked her in detail about her origin. She said, "My surname is Hu, and my courtesy name is Shuzhen. Five hundred years ago, in the palace of Emperor Zhenzong of Song, I was born as a eunuch, and she was a concubine. We were very fond of each other and agreed to be husband and wife in the next life. Unfortunately, she fell into a fox's womb, and I was reborn in several lifetimes without meeting her. Now she is nearly finished with her cultivation, and I took advantage of her daughter returning to her parents' home to fulfill this old destiny. Do not doubt me." Xu Sheng told his grandfather Han Zhao about this. Han Zhao was an old scholar, already in his seventies. He heard and found it strange. He rushed into the room, saw nothing, but heard a woman's voice. He called her "Grandfather" and said, "Please sit, I will pay my respects." Han Zhao knew it was an evil spirit, but he had no way to dispel it.

    458

    夜伴生寝，淑贞执妇道甚谨。与汉昭叙谈，引经据古，无一俚语。以汉昭在，未尝与生狎。比晓，里人知之，竟来讯诘。淑贞因人而语，与子言孝，与弟言悌，与姑言慈，与妇言顺，一如大儒之言。间有以故事相难者，淑贞悉其原委，出人意表，往往难者反为所穷。于是汉昭信其妖而不邪，故出以成其夫妇缘。
    　　　　其初至也有诗，定情也有词，风流芳艳，允为情种。乃许氏戚族，咸为生虑。或叱之，或怒詈之，甚或持刀向空挥之，或掖生匿避之。淑贞曰：“吾为情来，诸人不以情待我，盍去诸？”吟怨别诗而去。去遂不复来。
    　　　　然侍女素娥时通音问，取履式制履，精致胜于常妇。口诵淑贞《相思曲》，情甚殷。一日生涎其美，以手戏之，素娥严词拒，不似人间婢子之易挑者。自后素娥来，必偕秋鸿。有时偕数婢来，曰春燕，曰一枝红，曰青青柳，皆古美人之名，使人闻之而魄动。

    Explanation

    458

    At night, she accompanied him to bed, adhering strictly to the duties of a wife. In conversation with Han Zhao, she cited classical texts and ancient precedents, never uttering a single vulgar word. Because Han Zhao was present, she never engaged in intimacy with him. By dawn, the villagers had learned of this, and they came to question and interrogate her. Shu Zhen spoke differently according to the audience, advising the son on filial piety, the brother on fraternal respect, the aunt on kindness, and the wife on obedience, all as if she were a great Confucian scholar. Sometimes, those who challenged her with stories were left without answers, for she knew their origins and details, surpassing expectations. Often, those who questioned her were left speechless. Thus, Han Zhao believed her to be supernatural but not evil, and so he allowed her to continue their relationship.

    When she first arrived, she composed a poem; when they confirmed their relationship, there was a song of love. She was graceful and beautiful, truly a person deeply in love. However, the relatives of the Xu family were all concerned for her. Some scolded her, others cursed her, some even brandished knives in the air, and others hid him away. Shu Zhen said, "I came for love, but none of you treat me with love. Why not leave?" She recited a poem of farewell and departed. After that, she never returned.

    However, her maid Su'e occasionally communicated with him, taking measurements to make shoes, which were more exquisite than those of ordinary women. She recited Shu Zhen's "Song of Longing," her emotions deeply felt. One day, he was attracted to her beauty and tried to playfully touch her, but Su'e firmly refused, unlike a common servant who is easily tempted. Afterward, whenever Su'e came, she was always accompanied by Qiu Hong. Sometimes, several maids came together, named Chunyan, Yizhi Hong, and Qingqing Liu, all ancient names of beautiful women, evoking a sense of awe in those who heard them.

    459

    癸亥五月，淑贞遣秋鸿迎生去，生难之。秋鸿曰：“闭目附吾肩，可顷刻至。”生如其言，耳闻风浪声，目不敢开。少顷，秋鸿曰：“至矣！”生开眼视，石壁削立。秋鸿以扇拂壁，豁大门，肃生入，内皆精舍。女乐两行，鼓吹音妙不可状。淑贞一姐一妹，俱出见，分主客坐。素娥抱一女孩，曰：“此小姐所产，十阅月矣。以其生绿阴下，因名绿阴。”生接置膝上，女即以爹呼之。留生宿，其供具鲜华，都非尘世所有。淑贞随其姐若妹，早暮焚香诵佛，与生并坐而不与同寝。留四日，淑贞曰：“官人宜归矣。家中娘子欲投河，倘不测，奈何？”即遣秋鸿送生归。归而妇已泣河干矣。临別，手制葛衣葛裤赠生，归而视之，颇与闽葛类。是年冬，又遣婢迎去，其路较前略近。生问何地，素娥曰：“前黄山，今铜峰也。”素娥、秋鸿辈时到生家，为之理家事，虽琐屑必当。

    Explanation

    459

    In the fifth month of the year of the Metal Horse, Shu Zhen sent Autumn Crane to escort the scholar away. The scholar hesitated, but Autumn Crane said, "Close your eyes and lean on my shoulder, and you can arrive in an instant." The scholar followed her instructions, hearing the sound of wind and waves, but dared not open his eyes. After a short while, Autumn Crane said, "We have arrived!" The scholar opened his eyes and saw a sheer stone wall. Autumn Crane waved her fan against the wall, and it parted to reveal a grand gate. She solemnly led the scholar inside, where he found exquisite halls. Two rows of female musicians played, their melodies wondrous beyond description. Shu Zhen’s sister and younger sister both came out to greet, seating themselves as host and guest. Su E held a little girl, saying, "This is the lady's child, born ten months ago. Because she was born under the green shade, she was named Green Shade." The scholar took her and placed her on his knees, and the girl immediately called him "Daddy." They kept the scholar for the night, and the food and drink were fresh and splendid, all unlike anything in the mortal world. Shu Zhen followed her sister or younger sister, burning incense and reciting the Buddha in the morning and evening, sitting with the scholar but not sleeping with him. After four days, Shu Zhen said, "Sir, you should return. Your wife at home wants to throw herself into the river; if anything happens, what will we do?" She immediately sent Autumn Crane to escort the scholar back. Upon returning, his wife was already weeping by the riverbank. At parting, she made a hemp robe and pants by hand and gave them to the scholar. Upon returning home, he saw that they were quite similar to the hemp from Fujian. That winter, she sent a maid to escort him again, and the path was slightly shorter than before. The scholar asked where they were going, and Su E replied, "Previously it was Mount Huang, now it is Copper Peak." Su E, Autumn Crane, and others would often come to the scholar's home, managing household affairs, even the most trivial matters.

    460

    许生，余之内甥也，向余述其详。余疑之而亦羡之，属生致素娥，求一会以问休咎。生果以余意致之，素娥曰：“诺。当以甲子正月十二日为期。”届期，余放小舠往。生设酒馔。畅饮毕，余曰：“仙莫爽约乎？”汉昭曰：“必不爽，请安枕以待之。”漏未二下，忽榻前呼曰：“老相公，丫环来矣！”“老相公”，称汉昭也。余披衣起，问之曰：“来者素娥姐乎？”应曰：“是。徐相公请安卧，不消起来。我小姐有诗赠徐相公、周夫人。”诵诗云云。初闻不尽晓，问之，又诵一遍，曰：“小姐更有诗，专赠徐相公的。”诵诗云云。余曰：“亦未尽晓。”又诵一遍，尚有未晓处，问之，一一说明。既而曰：“相公寿有九旬，晚景都佳。

    Explanation

    460

    Xu, my nephew, told me in detail about the matter. I doubted it but also envied it, so I instructed Xu to bring Suhuo, and asked for a meeting to inquire about fortune and misfortune. Xu indeed brought Suhuo as I requested, and Suhuo said, "Yes. We will set the date for the first day of the twelfth month of the year 甲子." On the appointed day, I set out in a small boat. Xu prepared a banquet with wine and food. After drinking freely and to our heart's content, I said, "Will the fairy not break the appointment?" Han Zhao replied, "I will not break the appointment. Please lie down peacefully and wait for her." Before two hours had passed, suddenly someone called from the bed, "Old Master, the maid has arrived!" "Old Master" is a respectful term for an older man, referring to Han Zhao. I got up and asked, "Is the person who arrived Suhuo Sister?" The maid replied, "Yes. Mr. Xu, please lie down peacefully, there's no need to get up. Our young lady has a poem to give to Mr. Xu and Mrs. Zhou." She recited the poem. Initially, I did not fully understand it, so I asked her to recite it again. She said, "The young lady has another poem, specifically for Mr. Xu." She recited the poem again. I said, "I still do not fully understand." She recited it once more, and there were still parts I did not understand, so I asked her to explain them point by point. She then said, "Mr. Xu will live to be ninety, and his later years will be good."

    461

    ”余问曰：“我前世是何等人？”曰：“相公前世是医生，误用药伤人之子。夫人前世是堪舆，误看地，绝人之嗣。是以今世生而不育。然相公忠厚正直，暮年必得一子，只是积德要紧！”时同候会者，周子云槎、仇子长文、陆子求声，各有所问，皆就事直答，不作影响语。语久辞去，濒行，曰：“吾妹秋鸿，即送香水来饮。”顷之，空中忽报曰：“秋鸿送香水在此！”移灯照之，果有一壶在几。手抚壶，壶热如新瀹茶。秋鸿自言，须请许二官来斟。呼许生出，取香水分酌之，气香味甘，仙家所谓琼浆者非乎？闻有步履声，推门入，口唱曲，嫋嫋不绝，出即告去。余留之曰：“秋鸿姐何不歌一曲，使我辈共听好音乎？”秋鸿应声而唱，虽不辨其为何曲，而曼声缥缈，闻者莫不神飞。曲终，飘然去。余录其诗示同人，同人属而和，得诗词若干首，汇录之，颜曰《仙音集》。

    Explanation

    461

    I asked, "What kind of person was I in my past life?" The response was, "In your past life, you were a doctor who mistakenly prescribed medicine that harmed someone's child. Your wife in her past life was a geomancer who misread the land, thus cutting off the descendants of others. That is why in this life you have no children. However, since you are loyal, kind, and upright, you will surely have a son in your old age; but accumulating virtue is crucial!" At that time, those who were present and gathered together were Zhou Ziyuncha, Qiu Zizhangwen, and Lu Ziqiusheng, each of whom had their own questions, and they were all answered directly without any vague or ambiguous language. After talking for a long time, they bid farewell, and as I was about to leave, they said, "My sister, Autumn Swallow, will come to offer you fragrant water to drink." Soon after, there was a sudden announcement from the air, "Autumn Swallow has brought fragrant water here!" I moved the lamp to see, and indeed, there was a pot on the table. I touched the pot, and it was as warm as freshly boiled tea. Autumn Swallow herself said that she needed to invite Second Official Xu to pour the drink. I called Xu out, and he took the fragrant water and poured it for us. The aroma was sweet and delightful, surely what the immortals call nectar? I heard footsteps, and the door was pushed open, and someone began to sing a song, the melody continuous and unending. When the song ended, she left. I tried to keep her, saying, "Sister Autumn Swallow, why don't you sing a song for us to enjoy together?" Autumn Swallow sang in response, though I couldn't tell what the song was, but the sound was melodious and ethereal, and all who heard it felt their spirits soar. When the song ended, she disappeared. I recorded her poem and showed it to my companions, who then composed poems in response, resulting in several poems that we compiled into a collection titled The Collection of Immortal Sounds.

    462

    噫嘻！子不语怪，恐惑人也。若淑贞之事，怪耶非耶？其形但与许生见，他人未有见者。来也无影，去也无迹，窗户不启，倏而坐人之床。以为怪，则真怪也。然始以情，继以义，所言者中庸之道，所习者人事之常；投以诗词，辄次韵和答。以为非怪，则真非怪也。盖胡者，狐也；美姿容，笃因缘者，淑也；匿其貌，不与他人见者，贞也。狐而近于仙也！夫古人登岳涉海，以求仙而仙未易得会，今余于咫尺间亲为问答，饮香水，聆妙曲，直以为会仙可矣。第其女绿阴，许生所生，非狐矣，后必有出世之时。余果寿，尚得见之否乎？

    Explanation

    462

    Ah! Confucius did not speak of strange things, for fear of confusing people. Is the story of Shu and Zhen strange or not? Their appearance is only seen by Xu Sheng, and no one else has seen them. They come without a trace, and they leave without a sign, passing through closed windows and suddenly sitting on a bed. If one considers this to be strange, then it truly is strange. However, initially based on emotion, followed by righteousness, what they say aligns with the principle of the Mean, and what they learn is the common way of human affairs; when given poems, they respond with verses of the same rhyme scheme. If one considers this not to be strange, then it truly is not strange. It is said that "hu" refers to foxes; those who are beautiful and have close relationships are called Shu; those who hide their appearance and do not show themselves to others are called Zhen. Foxes are close to being divine beings! In ancient times, people climbed mountains and crossed seas to seek immortals, but it was not easy to meet them. Now I have personally engaged in conversation with them at close range, drinking fragrant water and listening to beautiful music, which is truly like meeting an immortal. However, her daughter Green Shade, born to Xu Sheng, is not a fox, and she will surely have a time of emergence into the world. If I live long enough, will I still be able to see her?

    463

    [张山来曰：狐而贞且淑者，其性也；淹博而知礼义者，则其学也。吾不知其以谁氏为师。]
    　　太恨生传　荆溪徐瑶天璧
    　　　　太恨生，东海佳公子也。与余形影周旋，神魂冥合，因熟悉生情事。生父司李公，望重一世。生承家学，折节读书，当代名流，咸倾其才调。丰神俊迈，性孤洁寡欲，未尝渔非礼色。
    　　　　娶元女夫人，婉嫕贞淑，生相敬如宾。夫人尝谓生曰：“吾夙耽清净，苦厌凡缘。膝下芝兰，幸蚤林立，生平志愿已足。当觅一窈窕，备君小星，吾即守木叉戒，绣佛长斋，不复烦君画眉矣。”生曰：“自卿为余家妇，门庭雍睦。方期百年偕老，岂忍令卿诵《白头吟》耶？虽然，卿业有命，余宁矫情？第选妾须德才色皆备乃善。正恐书生命薄，难获奇缘，有辜卿意耳。”

    Explanation

    463

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: "A fox that is chaste and virtuous is by nature; one that is learned and knowledgeable in rites and justice is through its studies. I do not know who its teacher might be."]

    The Biography of Tai Hen Sheng
    By Jingxi Xu Yao Tianbi

    Tai Hen Sheng is a fine young man from the East Sea region. He and I have been inseparable in spirit and soul, so I am familiar with his personal affairs. His father, Sir Sīlǐ, was renowned throughout the world. Tai Hen Sheng inherited his family's learning and humbled himself to study, admired by contemporary celebrities for his talent and demeanor. He is handsome and vigorous in spirit, with a solitary and pure nature, and has never pursued improper relationships.

    He married a woman from the Yuán family, who is gentle, virtuous, and chaste. They respect each other like guests. The wife once told Tai Hen Sheng: "I have always preferred a quiet life and have been weary of mundane affairs. Our children are flourishing, and my life goals are fulfilled. I should find a beautiful and virtuous woman to serve as your concubine. Then I will follow Buddhist precepts and practice vegetarianism, no longer troubling you with my appearance." Tai Hen Sheng replied: "Since you became part of our family, our household has been harmonious. We hope to live together for a long time. How could I let you recite 'The Whitehead Ode'? Although your fate is predetermined, I am willing to adapt. If we choose a concubine, she should be virtuous, talented, and beautiful. I fear that my fate may not allow me to find such a concubine, which would disappoint you."

    464

    先是太原某，世为洞庭山人，以贫故，赁其妻为生子保媪。未几，某死，遗一女无依，寄养豪右某家。某家妇悍，名曰养女，实婢畜之。女受困百端，无生理。媪恚甚，往争曰：“向固以吾女为若女，而女困辱至此，于义已绝，吾挈女去矣！”某家咸憎女，听媪挈归生家，年十六矣。女虽支离憔悴，而柔婉之态，楚楚动人。夫人一见绝怜之，亲为熏沐。教以女红，无不精致。时戊辰冬，生自茂苑归，问所从来。夫人语之故，因谓生曰：“曩欲为君置妾，而难其选。今此女明慧端懿，乃天赐也。亦有意乎？”生昵而笑曰：“唯卿所命。”生母亦见女贤，密谕媪，欲为生成之。会生仍往茂苑，寻丁外艰，事遂寝。

    Explanation

    464

    Previously, a man from Taiyuan, whose family had lived in Dongting Mountain for generations, due to poverty, hired his wife to help with childcare. Shortly after, the man died, leaving behind a daughter who was fatherless. She was then raised by a wealthy family. The wife of this family was harsh, and though she called the girl a foster daughter, she treated her like a servant. The girl suffered in many ways and had no means of survival. The aunt, greatly angered, went to argue and said, "Previously, I certainly considered your daughter as mine, but now she is suffering and humiliated to this extent. Our bond of righteousness has been severed, and I will take my daughter away!" The family all hated the girl and allowed the aunt to take her back to her family. By then, the girl was sixteen years old. Though she was tattered and worn-out, her gentle and yielding demeanor was charming and captivating. The lady, upon seeing her, felt extremely pitied her and personally bathed and perfumed her. She taught her women's work, and everything she did was exquisite. It was during the winter of the year of Wuchen, when the young man returned from Maoyuan. He asked where she came from. The lady told him the story and then said to him, "I had previously wanted to arrange a concubine for you, but found it difficult to choose. Now this girl is bright, wise, upright, and virtuous, a gift from heaven. Do you have any intention?" The young man smiled and said, "Do as you wish." The young man's mother also saw the girl as virtuous and secretly instructed the aunt, wanting to make her the young man's concubine. However, the young man still went to Maoyuan, and soon after, he encountered the bereavement of his father. The matter was then put aside.

    465

    居半载，夫人乘间谓女曰：“吾视汝德性贞醇，体度庄雅，虽名闺淑媛，无以过之，岂宜为庸人妇？吾郎君才品风流，真堪婿汝，当以赤绳系汝两人。幸事获济，即妹视汝，汝盍早自决计？”女沉吟未答，既而泣拜曰：“妾惸惸母子，困苦伶仃，来托宇下。夫人遇妾，谊逾所生，常恨碎骨粉身，不足为报；生死祸福，敢不唯命？今所以不轻一诺者，诚虑人心叵测，事变难知；三生缘浅，好事多磨折耳。

    Explanation

    465

    After half a year, the lady took the opportunity to speak to the girl and said, "I see your virtue and character as pure and sincere, and your demeanor as dignified and graceful. Even if called a virtuous lady of the inner chambers, there is no one who surpasses you. How could you be suitable as the wife of an ordinary man? My husband's talents and character are so charming and refined, he is truly worthy to be your husband. We should tie you two together with a red string. If this fortunate matter comes to fruition, even if I, your sister, see you, why don't you make a decision soon?" The girl pondered but did not answer. She then wept and knelt down, saying, "My mother and I are poor and destitute, coming to rely on your protection. The lady has treated me with affection that exceeds that of a biological mother. I often feel that even if I were to break bones and grind to dust, it would not be enough to repay her kindness. Whether it is life, death, or fortune, I dare not disobey her commands. The reason why I do not lightly make a promise now is that I truly fear that human hearts are unpredictable, and it is difficult to predict changes in circumstances. Our karmic connections are shallow, and good things often face many obstacles."

    466

    幸辱夫人与郎君约，郎君家世清华，先业未竟，当勉图光大，努力青云，慎无以儿女情长，令英雄气短。且太夫人春秋高，承欢养志，端在郎君。讵可牵惹闲情，致乖色养？一也。郎君与夫人，鸡鸣戒旦，鸿案相庄，万一割爱分宠，遗刺《绿衣》，妾罪大矣！二也。郎君外服未阕，大节攸关，妾当珍此女儿身，俟除服后，上启高堂，明成嘉礼。倘稍逞情缘，冒嫌涉疑，妾不足惜，人其谓郎君何？三也。诚如妾言，妾无悔矣。”夫人笑曰：“固知汝有心人也。好自爱。”因具以告生。生惊喜曰：“安得此大学问语！谨受教。”自是生必欲得女，女一意以身委生。而夫人亦唯恐不得当也。

    Explanation

    466

    I am honored to have made an agreement with Madam and the Gentleman. The Gentleman's family is of noble lineage, and the ancestral business has not been completed. He should strive to make it glorious and prosperous, and work hard to achieve high positions. Be cautious not to be overly entangled in romantic feelings, which would make a hero's spirit wane. Moreover, the Elder Lady is of advanced age, and it is important to please and support her in her wishes and aspirations, for this all depends on the Gentleman. How can one indulge in idle pleasures, which would lead to neglecting filial duties? That is one point. The Gentleman and Madam, who rise early like roosters, live in harmony and respect, like the famous couple who shared one bed. If there were to be any division of affection, it would be akin to lamenting the loss of a loved one, and I would be greatly at fault! That is another point. The Gentleman's mourning period has not yet ended, and it concerns a major principle. I should cherish my maidenhood, and await the completion of the mourning period to inform the elders and formally complete the marriage ceremony. If one were to indulge in romantic feelings, risking suspicion and doubt, I would not mind, but what would people say about the Gentleman? That is a third point. If indeed what I say is true, I will have no regrets. Madam smiled and said, "I knew you were a considerate person. Take good care of yourself." She then told him in full detail. The Gentleman was surprised and said, "How could such profound words come from you? I will take your advice seriously." From then on, the Gentleman was determined to marry her, and she was fully committed to him. And Madam was also afraid that it might not be appropriate.

    467

    大率女之为人，性殊灵警，而严于举止；情极肫恻，而简于言笑。居常女伴相征逐，女独靓妆凝神，萧然自远。终日坐阁中，专理刺绣，影匿形藏，非媪呼，不入中堂。间遇生，辄遥引，以故终岁同处室中，绝未通一言。生情不自禁，欲得女一晤语，倩夫人为介。女难之。夫人固请曰：“郎君无他意，第欲共汝作良友相酬对耳。”至则俨容端坐，双目瞪视而已。然生亦以远嫌，不敢数请相见。即女见生，必邀夫人与俱，乍语乍默，若近若远。间或并坐月中，偕行花下，各陈慰勉之辞，半吐愁思之句。虽情好愈挚，而燕昵俱忘，历三年不及于乱。夫人每从旁戏曰：“汝两人内密外疏，何乃无风月情？”

    Explanation

    467

    Generally speaking, this woman's character is unusually alert and perceptive, yet strict in her behavior; her emotions are deeply tender and compassionate, yet reserved in speech and laughter. While her female companions often interact and socialize, she alone adorns herself with care and focuses her gaze, appearing detached and aloof. She spends her entire day sitting in the pavilion, devoted to embroidery, concealing her presence, and only entering the main hall when called by the old woman. When she occasionally encounters the male character, she always distances herself, which is why, despite living in the same room for an entire year, they never speak a word to each other. The male character, unable to control his feelings, wishes to have a conversation with her and asks the wife to mediate. The woman finds this difficult. The wife persists, saying, "The young master has no ulterior motives; he simply wishes to be friends with you and engage in mutual respect." When they meet, she sits formally and silently, her eyes fixed intently. However, the male character also avoids any appearance of impropriety and does not frequently ask for meetings. Whenever the woman sees him, she always invites the wife to accompany her, alternating between speaking and silence, being both close and distant. Occasionally, they sit together under the moon or walk together in the garden, exchanging words of comfort and encouragement, and occasionally expressing feelings of sorrow or melancholy. Although their affection grows deeper, they avoid intimate or overly familiar behavior, and over three years, they do not engage in inappropriate or scandalous conduct. The wife often teases them, saying, "The two of you are close inside but distant outside—how is it that you lack romantic feelings?"

    468

    生卧室与女妆阁虽隔绝，而室密迩。生中夜朗吟，与女刀尺声，时相答也。女尝谓生：“郎君惊才逸韵，妾如获侍巾帻，永伴文人，素愿已惬。第自恨未娴翰墨，他日香奁中，弗克供捧砚役，奈何？”生笑曰：“以汝夙慧，奚患不识字耶？结褵之后，汝备弟子礼奉余为师，灯前月下，授汝《女论语》、《孝经》及古诗词，何如？”女点首曰：“尚须教我《法华》《多心》诸经也。”随口授《关睢》数章，并解说意义。女微笑覆之，不失一字。生出外，女随夫人过书斋。视几砚上尘，拂拭之；图籍纵横者，整齐之；庭花色悴，则汲水灌之。

    Explanation

    468

    The man’s bedroom and the woman’s dressing room, though separated, were close to each other. In the middle of the night, the man would recite poetry, and the sound of the woman sewing would echo in response. The woman once told the man, “My lord, your extraordinary talent and graceful manner are truly astonishing. If I could serve you as a wife and support your scholarly pursuits, I would be content. However, I regret that I am not skilled in writing. In the future, I will not be able to hold the inkstone for you, which is a pity.” The man smiled and said, “With your innate wisdom, why should you worry about not knowing how to read? After we are married, you will treat me as your teacher, and we will study together by the light of the lamp and under the moon. I will teach you the Nü Lunyu (On the Conduct of Women), the Xiao Jing (Classic of Filial Piety), and classical poetry. How does that sound?” The woman nodded and said, “I would also like to learn the Lotus Sutra and the Heart Sutra.” The man then recited several chapters of the Guān Jū poem and explained their meanings. The woman listened attentively and repeated the verses without missing a single word. When the man left, the woman followed the lady of the house to the study. She wiped the dust from the desk and inkstone, arranged the scattered books, and watered the withered flowers in the courtyard.

    469

    性爱焚香，竟体芬郁袭人。雅好淡素妆，荆钗裙布，必整必洁，泊如也。生每遗以香钿诸物，必坚却之，或以夫人命始受。又常倩制一锦囊，不可。强之，则云：“俟两年后为郎制之。”其谨慎识大体如此。
    　　　　始女寄养某家时，嫉女殊甚，至是闻女美且贤，乃大悔。遂改养女为养媳，诱媪兄及侄，坐侄主婚，而以媒氏属媪甥，更为流言以捍生曰：“女固某家妇也，而生实图之。”生有忤奴利其金，因挟为奇货，于媪前作楚歌，而阴告某家，且授之计。生素以名义自持，又见肘腋间多媒孳之者，犹豫未决。会以事远出，某家闻之，疾令媪甥持五十金为聘，给媪兄劫媪使受，约某日来娶。

    Explanation

    469

    She has a fondness for burning incense, and the fragrance permeates her entire body, touching those around her. She prefers simple and plain makeup, wearing humble clothing, and always keeps herself neat and clean, remaining calm and unperturbed. Her husband often gives her fragrant jewelry and other items, but she firmly refuses them unless his name is mentioned. She once asked to make a silk bag, but he refused. When pressed, she said, "I will wait two years to make it for you." Such is her prudence and understanding of the big picture.

    When the girl was first entrusted to a certain family, she was intensely disliked by them. By then, they heard that the girl was beautiful and virtuous, and she deeply regretted her previous attitude. She then changed from adopting the girl as a daughter to adopting her as a daughter-in-law, and coaxed the old woman's brother and nephew, having the nephew act as the matchmaker, and had the matchmaker be the old woman's nephew. She spread rumors to protect the husband, saying, "The girl is already the wife of a certain family, but the husband actually wanted her." The husband had a servant who benefited from the money and used it as a valuable commodity, singing a song in the old woman's presence and secretly informing the certain family, giving them a plan. The husband always upheld his reputation, but saw that there were many people around him spreading rumors, and hesitated and did not decide. Just happened to be away on a trip, and the certain family heard about it and quickly ordered the old woman's nephew to bring fifty taels of gold as a dowry, giving the old woman's brother money to force the old woman to accept, and arranged for a certain day to come and marry.

    470

    生归，益错愕，不知所为，夜同夫人谓女曰：“吾向以汝为囊中物，今变起不测，势难复挽，奈何？”女曰：“妾计决矣！倘事势穷促，以死继之；否则祝发空门耳。外此非妾所知。”生曰：“汝奈何轻言死哉？余与汝缠绵情境，三载于兹，居恒晤对，俨若宾师，情固难拋，义则可判。今奸人逐影寻声，将甘心于汝。万一以余故轻生，外间耳食，其以汝为何如人？杀身不足以雪恨，只增余悲耳！且汝纵弗自惜，独不念汝母乎？唯向空王乞命，于计较可。辦香供佛，佘当一以资汝。然汝凄凉禅榻，断送青春，余又不忍令汝出此也。”女欷歔久之，曰：“嗟乎郎君！今生已矣！”面壁长号。生频呼之，不复应。时壬申正月十二夜也。

    Explanation

    470

    Upon returning home, the man became increasingly bewildered, not knowing what to do. At night, he spoke to his wife about the girl, saying, "I once thought of you as a treasure in my pocket. Now, unexpected changes have occurred, and the situation is hard to reverse. What shall we do?" The girl replied, "I have made up my mind! If the situation becomes dire, I will follow it with my life; otherwise, I will become a nun. Beyond this, I do not know." The man said, "Why do you speak lightly of death? I have been with you in intimate circumstances for three years. We always met and interacted, as if I were your teacher. Our feelings are hard to let go, but our duty can be decided. Now, evil people are following your shadow and seeking your sound, intending to harm you. If you were to take your own life for my sake, people outside will only hear secondhand information. What will they think of you? Even sacrificing your life would not be enough to avenge the wrong. It would only increase my sorrow! Moreover, even if you do not care for yourself, do you not think of your mother? Only by seeking life from the Buddha is viable. I will prepare incense and offerings for the Buddha, and I will support you with all my resources. But you will be alone in your cold Buddhist bed, wasting your youth. I cannot bear to see you go this way." The girl sighed for a long time and said, "Ah, my lord! This life is over!" She faced the wall and cried out loudly. The man called out to her repeatedly, but she did not respond. It was the twelfth night of the first month in the year Jia Shen.

    471

    先是女密藏酖与剪于衽，为女伴所觉，搜去之。至是乃手制女僧冠服，促媪于试灯夕，偕入尼庵。临行，夫人持女痛哭，不忍舍。左右皆掩泣，莫能仰视。生但目送而已，虞辞楚帐，嫱离汉庭，不足喻其悲也。庵内老尼诘其事，不肯为女剃度；哀恳再三，终不许。而某家侦知之，惧有变，急倩媪妯娌趋庵中，防护甚严。女自度不免，中夜起，呼媪哭曰：“母乎！儿至此命也夫！为传语……”语未毕，气结不能出声。

    Explanation

    471

    Previously, the girl had secretly hidden poison and scissors in her sleeve, which were discovered by her companions and taken away. At this point, she made the headgear and clothing for a female nun by hand and urged the old woman to enter the nunnery on the Lantern Festival together. As they were about to depart, the mother held the girl and cried bitterly, unable to bear parting. Those around her covered their faces and wept, unable to look up. The son could only watch silently, the sorrow of Yu leaving the Chu court and Qiang departing the Han palace could not adequately describe the depth of their grief. Inside the nunnery, the old nun questioned the matter and refused to let the girl be tonsured; despite her repeated pleas, she was ultimately not allowed. The family, fearing a change of heart, quickly asked the old woman's sisters to go to the nunnery and protect her strictly. The girl, knowing she could not escape, got up at midnight and called out to the old woman, crying, "Mother! My fate is sealed here! Please convey my message..." Her words were cut off as she choked and could not speak.

    472

    媪急抱持之曰：“儿欲何言？”女欲言，复大哭晕绝，如是三。良久始曰：“儿与郎君，迹若路人，分喻知己，生平志念，皎如日星。本期办一死以报郎君，今流离转辗，计无复之。求死不得，求为尼又不得，命之穷也，一至于斯！天实为之，其又何尤？儿为郎君，涩眼全枯，惊魂久散，顾念死出无名，徒令枉死城中，增一业案耳。今与郎君恩断义绝矣！天荒地老，永无见期！好谢夫人，善慰郎君，勿复以儿为念，即视儿作已死观可耳。”言讫，母子相抱大恸，仆佛前。而某家人舟适至，蜂拥入庵，挟女而去。

    Explanation

    472

    The old woman hurriedly held her and said, "What do you want to say?" The girl wanted to speak, but then she burst into a great cry and fainted. This happened three times. After a long time, she began to say, "My child and the young man are as distant as strangers. We understand each other as if we were friends, and our lifelong aspirations are as clear as the sun and stars. We originally planned to die to repay the young man. Now, wandering and turning around, there is no way out. Unable to die, unable to become a nun. Our fate is so poor, it has come to this. It is truly the will of heaven, what more can we blame? For the young man, my child's eyes have become dry, and her soul has been scattered for a long time. Considering that dying would be meaningless, it would only add one more case of wrongful death in the city. Now, the bond of love and righteousness between my child and the young man is broken. Until the sky turns to earth and the earth to sky, there will never be a time to meet again. Please convey my thanks to the lady, and comfort the young man. Do not worry about my child anymore, consider her as already dead." After she finished speaking, the mother and daughter embraced each other and wept bitterly, falling to the ground before the Buddha. And just then, the family's boat arrived, and they rushed into the temple, taking the girl away.

    473

    生自与女诀别后，心摇意乱，忽忽如有失。及媪归述女言，益狂惑失志，触目神伤。夫人忧之，且慰且让曰：“吾本欲为君缔此良因，不图变出非常，累君至是。虽然，君自与女无缘耳。君向不早为之所，因循蹉跌，坐失事机。迨奸人计赚时，以君之力，犹足与争，挺身而前，未必无济。乃袖手任其鼓弄。今大事已去，悔恨何及？且天下岂少良女子，而独沾沾于是为！”生仰天太息曰：“夫人休矣！余非登徒子，誓不效杂情奴态，暮翠朝红。自见女后，毕世悃忱，无端倾倒。

    Explanation

    473

    After the protagonist parted from the girl, his heart was agitated and his mind confused, as if something precious had been lost. When the old woman returned and recounted the girl's words, he became even more bewildered and lost his resolve, feeling deep sorrow at every sight. The wife, concerned for him, consoled and advised him, saying, "I originally intended to arrange this good match for you, but did not anticipate such an extraordinary turn of events that have brought you to this state. Although, it seems that you and the girl were not meant to be together. You failed to act sooner, and as a result, missed the opportunity. When the villain's scheme was in place, with your strength, you could have still contested it, stepped forward bravely, and it might not have been without benefit. Instead, you stood by and watched as he manipulated the situation. Now that the matter is irreparable, what good is regretting it? Moreover, there are many good women in the world, and you should not be overly attached to just one!" The protagonist sighed to the heavens and said, "Lady, please stop! I am not a man who seeks fleeting pleasures and indulges in promiscuity. Since seeing the girl, I have been devoted with all my sincerity, and unexpectedly, I have been overwhelmed by my feelings."

    474

    试问遇合之奇，有如此女者乎？我见犹怜，有如此女者乎？两心相得，有如此女者乎？乃婉娈一室之中，荏苒三年之久，余亦非鲁男子也。所以禁欲窒私、坐怀不乱者，亦冀正始要终，各明本怀耳。事幸垂成，一朝云散，若以丹诚所感，虽灭顶捐躯，亦复奚恤！顾乃咽泪吞声，甘为奸人所卖，诚欲以礼相终始也。鼠牙雀角，适足增羞，抑岂令卖菜佣持我短长乎？今而后，余终当以情死耳！血殷肠裂，骨化形销，此恨绵绵，宁有穷极！卿勿复生别念，纵使贤如络秀，丽若绿珠，不能易此恨矣！”自是益不自聊赖，或竟日枯坐，或彻夜悲歌，积久遂成心疾。

    Explanation

    474

    To ask if the meeting and union were extraordinary, such as this woman? Even when I see her, I pity her, such as this woman? Two hearts are in harmony, such as this woman? In a cozy and intimate setting, three years have passed gradually, and I am not a man from Lu who is immune to temptation. The reasons for self-restraint and suppressing selfishness, to remain calm and composed even in tempting situations, are also to start and end with integrity and to clarify one's true intentions. The matter was almost successful, but one day everything dissolved like clouds. If moved by sincere feelings, even if one sacrifices oneself completely, what is there to regret! Yet I hold back tears and suppress my voice, willingly to be deceived by a villain, truly desiring to maintain propriety from beginning to end. Trivial disputes and petty conflicts only add to the shame, how can I let a lowly vegetable seller judge my strengths and weaknesses? From now on, I will die out of deep emotional attachment! Blood-stained intestines torn apart, bones turned to ashes, body disintegrated, this hatred is endless, how can it ever end? Do not think of separation, even if virtuous as Luo Xiu, beautiful as Lu Zhu, it cannot change this hatred! From then on, I increasingly find no solace, sometimes sitting idle all day, sometimes singing mournfully all night, after a long time, it developed into a mental illness.

    475

    余见且伤之，为作《咄咄吟》一卷，《情忏词》一卷，以广其意。且生与女相爱怜若此，而卒不相遇，真堪遗恨千古，乌容秘而不传？而不知者，反以女为生口实。因详述之，以告天上人间，千秋万世之情痴有如生者。
    　　　　幻史氏曰：余观生与女，发乎情，止乎礼义，岂寻常儿女子所得拟乎？当其适然相遭，理既允当，于势又便，况有阃内以作主合，如此而不遇，岂人生快意之事，造物者故厄之，使弗克有终耶？不然，生与女命实不犹耶了然迹其后先言行，女非有意负生者，形禁势格，变至无如何耳。而生也宁守经，毋达权，事固弗易为流俗道。悲夫！语云：“未免有情，谁能遣此？”余又感夫以礼相闲者之情，尤不能已已也。

    Explanation

    475

    I see and grieve for the situation, and I have composed a volume titled The Poem of Dejection and another titled The Words of Repentance for Love, in order to expand upon its meaning. The man and the woman loved and cared for each other so deeply, yet they ultimately never met, leaving a regret that will endure through the ages. How can such a story be kept secret and not passed down? Those who do not understand the situation, instead, use the woman as a pretext for the man.
