He possessed a keen and exceptional talent, and in poetry, classical prose, and imperial examinations, he was able to express his own unique ideas. He retreated to live outside the west city gate near Mochou Lake, rarely interacting with others. However, scholars and artists from all directions who were fond of literature, wine, and unrestrained behavior, when they came to Jinling, would go to great lengths to meet him.

    Wang Anjie, using his poetic and literary talents, also excelled in painting. In his depictions of water and rocks, figures, flowers, and birds, whenever he put pen to paper, there was always a deeper, subtle meaning. He once created two Ritual Pagoda paintings and two Bathing Buddha paintings for me, all of which were strikingly ancient in style, with none of the delicate grace of contemporary works, truly worthy of praise. After completing the paintings, he would often inscribe them with his own words. I often told others: "At just over twenty years old, Wang Anjie, if he were to focus his talents, he could surpass several generations; if he were to live another ten years, people would no longer speak of Xu Qingteng." His seal impressions directly emulate those of the Qin and Han dynasties, and he was willing to create them for me, which I have now arranged in order. My friend Fang Erzhi had a daughter whom he did not easily give in marriage, and he sought a husband for her in Jiangnan. After some time, he found Wang Anjie extraordinary and thus gave his daughter to him in marriage. Fang Erzhi was renowned in his generation and did not lightly give his approval, yet upon meeting Wang Anjie, he immediately gave his daughter to him in marriage, which shows how remarkable Wang Anjie must have been.

    517

    宓草亦作印章。古逸无近今余习，亦次于后。宓草不亚安节，绘事遂欲与兄并驱。同人咸曰：“元方、季方，难为兄弟也。”安节王母与两尊人及安节，皆落地不任荤，独宓草微能食干{鱼差}，人称其为“一门佛子”云。
    　　　　[张山来曰：安节兄弟三人，皆高士也。予仅识宓草，然阿兄阿弟，亦莫非神交，当不让端复专得之耳。]
    　　书姜次生印章前　豫仪周亮工减斋
    　　　　姜次生正学，浙兰溪人。性孤介，然于物无所忤。食饩于邑，甲申后弃去，一纵于酒，酒外唯寄意图章。得酒辄醉，醉辄呜呜歌元人《会稽太守词》。又好于长桥上鼓腹歌，众环听，生目不见，向人声乃益高。每醉辄歌，歌文必《会稽太守词》，不屑他调也。

    Explanation

    517

    Fuci also makes seals. His style is ancient and elegant, free from the remnants of modern practices, and it is second only to his brother's. Fuci's painting skills are no less than Anjie's, and he has now come to rival his brother in skill. Fellow artists all say, "Yuanfang and Jiefang, it is hard to be brothers!" Anjie's mother and the two respected elders, along with Anjie, all abstain from meat, but Fuci can eat dried fish. People call them "a family of Buddhists."

    [According to Zhang Shanlai: Anjie and his two brothers are all high-minded scholars. I only know Fuci, but his elder and younger brothers are also like spiritual friends, and they should not be inferior to Duan Fu in their expertise.]

    Book of Seals by Jiang Cisheng Zhengxue, Preface by Yu Yi Zhou Lianggong Jianzhi

    Jiang Cisheng Zhengxue is from Lanxi, Zhejiang. He is solitary and independent, yet he does not quarrel with anything. He received a stipend in the county, but after the year of Jia Shen (1644), he abandoned it. He indulged in wine, and outside of drinking, he only devoted himself to carving seals. Whenever he drank, he would get drunk, and when drunk, he would sing the Yuan Dynasty's 'Governor of Kuaiji' poem. He also liked to sing while patting his belly on a long bridge. The crowd gathered around to listen, but he did not see them; the more people there were, the louder he sang. Whenever he was drunk, he would sing, and the song he sang was always the 'Governor of Kuaiji' poem; he had no regard for other tunes.

    518

    方邵村侍御为丽水令。生来见，谓侍御曰：“公嗜图章，我制固佳，愿为公制数章。正学生平不知干谒，但嗜饮耳。公醉我，我为公制印。公意得，正学意得矣。”侍御乃与饮，醉即歌《会稽太守词》。于是侍御得生印最多，侍御署中酿亦为生罄矣。一夕，漏下数十刻，署中尽熟寐，忽剥啄甚。侍御惊起，以为寇且发，不则御史台霹雳符也。惊起询，则报曰：“姜生见。”侍御遣人谢曰：“夜分矣，请以昧爽。”生砰訇曰：“事甚急！”侍御以生得他闻传意外也，急趋迎之，执手问故。曰：“我适为公成一印，殊自满志，不及旦，急欲令公见也。事孰有急于此者乎？”遂出掌中握视之，侍御乃大笑。复曰：“如此印，不直一醉耶？”于是痛饮，辨明而去。去又于桥上歌《会稽太守词》。桥側饼师腐家起独早，竞来听之，谓此君“起乃更早，遂已醉耶？”生意乃快甚。

    Explanation

    518

    Mr. Jiang, a skilled seal engraver, visited the magistrate of Lishui, Mr. Fang Shaocun, and said, "You have a fondness for seals, and I have indeed made some good ones. I am willing to make several for you. I have never sought to curry favor, but I do love drinking. You get me drunk, and I will make a seal for you. If you are pleased, I will also be pleased." The magistrate then shared a drink with him, and when he was drunk, he sang the poem "Governor of Kuaiji." As a result, the magistrate received the most seals from Mr. Jiang, and the wine in the magistrate's office was also consumed by him. One night, several hours had passed, and everyone in the office was deeply asleep. Suddenly, there was a very loud knocking. The magistrate was startled awake, thinking that bandits were about to attack, or perhaps it was an urgent summons from the Censorate. He woke up and asked what was happening, and someone reported, "Mr. Jiang has come to see." The magistrate sent a messenger to say, "It is midnight, please come at dawn." Mr. Jiang replied loudly, "The matter is very urgent!" The magistrate, surprised by this unexpected visit, hurried to meet him, took his hand, and asked what was the matter. Mr. Jiang said, "I just made a seal for you, and I am very self-satisfied with it. Before dawn, I urgently want to make you see it. Which matter is more urgent than this?" He then took it out of his hand and held it to look at it, and the magistrate laughed heartily. He said, "Such a seal, not worth one drink?" They then drank heartily until daylight, and Mr. Jiang left after daylight. On the bridge, he sang the poem "Governor of Kuaiji" again. By the side of the bridge, the baker and curry vendor, who had woken up very early, rushed to listen to him. They said, "This man woke up even earlier, already drunk?" Mr. Jiang was very pleased.

    519

    生无妻，无子女，常自言曰：“曲蘖吾乡里。吾印必传，吾之嗣续也。吾何忧？”别侍御返里。年八十，卒。辛亥秋，侍御以生所为印示余，予入之谱，复櫽括楼岗太史述生事，录之于前。侍御曰：“每展玩生印，觉酒气拂拂从石间出。生歌《会稽太守词》声，犹恍惚吾耳根目际也！”
    　　　　[张山来曰：仆不识姜，然读此传时，亦觉耳中如听歌《会稽太守词》，酒气拂拂从歌声中出也。]
    　　
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷十六
    　　因树屋书影　周亮工减斋
    　　　　德州程正夫言：顺治癸巳正月十八日，夜风厉甚。恩县祁村陂中冰，卓立成山，广四丈，高二丈许，峰峦秀拔，溪壑回环，一磴委蛇相通。观者远近裹粮，至日千余人，祷祠焉。遍考诸书，古无此异，不知何祥也？余按正德中，文安县水忽僵立，是日天大寒，忽冻为冰柱，高五丈，围亦如之，中空而旁有穴。数日后，流贼过文安，民避入冰穴，赖以全活者甚众。正如此类。

    Explanation

    519

    A Man Without a Wife or Children

    This man was born without a wife or children. He often said to himself, "Fermented grains are my hometown. My impressions will surely be passed on, continuing my lineage. Why should I worry?" After parting with the official, he returned to his hometown. At the age of eighty, he passed away. In the autumn of the year Xin Hai, the official showed me the impressions the man had made, and I included them in my records. I also summarized the historian Lou Gang's account of the man's life, recording it beforehand. The official said, "Each time I examine and enjoy the man's impressions, I feel the light scent of alcohol coming from between the stones. The sound of the man singing the poem 'Governor of Kuaiji' is still vaguely in my ears and eyes!"

    [Zhang Shanlai said: I do not know Jiang, but when I read this biography, I also feel as if I am listening to the song 'Governor of Kuaiji,' with the light scent of alcohol coming from the song.]

    Volume 16 of the New Records of Yu Chou
    Shadows in the Tree-House: Zhou Lianggong's Reduced Study
    Cheng Zhengfu of Dezhou said: On the eighteenth day of the first month in the year Gui Si during the Shunzhi era, the wind was very strong at night. In the pond of Qi Village in En County, the ice stood upright and formed a mountain, four zhang wide and about two zhang high. The peaks and ridges were beautiful and prominent, and the streams and ravines were winding. One winding path connected them. Spectators from near and far carried food, and over a thousand people came on the day. They worshiped and prayed there. Thoroughly examining various books, there was no such strange phenomenon in ancient times. I do not know what omen it is. According to the middle of the Zhengde era, the water in Wen County suddenly stood rigidly. That day was very cold. Suddenly, it froze into an ice pillar, five zhang high and with the same circumference, hollow in the middle and with holes on the sides. A few days later, the wandering bandits passed through Wen County, and the people took refuge in the ice holes, many of whom were saved as a result. This is just like the previous incident.

    520

    小品中载有荐艺士于显贵者。其人固平易，显贵虽礼之，然未尝问其所长。濒行，其人曰：“辱公爱，有小技，愿献于公。”乃索素纸，为围棋盘，信手界画，无毫发谬。显贵惊叹。正统间，周伯器年九十，修《杭州志》，灯下书蝇头字，界画乌阑，不折纸为范，毫发不爽。章友直伯益，以篆名，官翰林待诏。同人闻其名，心未之服，咸求愿见笔法。伯益命粘纸各数张，作二图，其一纸纵横各作十九画，成一棋局；其一作十圆圈，成一射帖。其笔之粗细，间架疏密，无毫发之失。诸人叹服，再拜而去。古今绝技，亦有相同者如此。

    Explanation

    520

    A short piece records an anecdote about a talented artist being recommended to a noble person. The artist was modest and approachable, and the noble person treated him with courtesy, though he never inquired about his skills. As the artist was about to leave, he said, "I am honored by your favor. I have a small skill and wish to present it to you." He then asked for a plain sheet of paper and drew a Go board with freehand precision, without a single error. The noble person was amazed. During the Zhengtong period, Zhou Boqi, aged ninety, compiled the History of Hangzhou. He wrote tiny characters by the lamp and drew precise black grids without folding the paper as a guide, achieving perfect accuracy. Zhang Youzhi, also known as Boyi, was famous for his seal script and held the position of a scholar-official in the Hanlin Academy. Fellow scholars heard of his name but were not convinced, all seeking to see his calligraphy techniques. Bo Yi instructed them to paste several sheets of paper and created two diagrams: one with nineteen lines drawn horizontally and vertically, forming a chessboard; the other with ten circles, creating a shooting target. The thickness of his brush strokes and the spacing of the lines were flawless. The scholars admired and bowed twice before leaving. Such extraordinary skills, both ancient and modern, have their parallels.

    521

    [张山来曰：皖城石天外曾为余言：有某大僚，荐一人于某有司，数日未献一技。忽一日，辞去，主人饯之。此人曰：“某有薄枝，愿献于公。望公悉召幕中客共观之，可乎？”主人始惊愕，随邀众宾客至。询客何技，客曰：“吾善吃烟。”众大笑，因询“能吃几何？”曰：“多多益善！”于是置烟一斤，客吸之尽，初无所吐，众已奇之矣。又问：“仍可益乎？”曰：“可。”又益以烟若干。客又吸之尽。“请众客观吾技！”徐徐自口中喷前所吸烟，或为山水楼阁，或为人物，或为花木禽兽，如蜃搂海市，莫可名状。众客咸以为得未曾有，劝主人厚赠之。由此观之，诚未可轻量天下士也。]

    Explanation

    521

    Zhang Shanlai said: Once, Stone Tianwai of Wancheng told me: There was a high-ranking official who recommended someone to a certain official department. For several days, the person did not offer any skill. Suddenly, one day, he left, and the host saw him off. The person said, "I have a modest skill, and I wish to present it to you. I hope you will invite all the guests in the camp to watch, may I?" The host was initially surprised and then invited all the guests. The guests asked what skill the person had, and he replied, "I am good at smoking." The guests laughed and asked, "How much can you smoke?" He said, "The more, the better!" So they placed a pound of tobacco, and the person smoked it all, initially without spitting anything out, which already amazed the guests. They then asked, "Can you still smoke more?" He said, "Yes." They added more tobacco, and the person smoked it all again. "Please let the guests watch my skill!" he said. Slowly, he sprayed out the previously smoked tobacco from his mouth, forming mountains and rivers, palaces and pavilions, figures, flowers, trees, birds, and beasts, like a mirage, which could not be described. All the guests thought it was something they had never seen before and urged the host to give him a generous gift. From this, one can see that one should not lightly underestimate scholars from all over the world.

    522

    荆南居客麻城忠淳间，有一鹦鹉，见长老寿普来，忽鸣曰：“望慈悲！”长老曰：“小畜，谁教尔能言？”鹦鹉自后不复声。麻纵之，径赴僧侧，啾啁致谢。僧曰：“宜高飞，免再堕。”又求指示，僧令诵佛经。八年，僧至桃源，一小儿来谢曰：“吾麻氏鹦鹉也，荷方便，今在萧家作男子矣。”验之，胁下尚有翅毛。
    　　　　有宦闽者，携双鹦鹉归江右，两禽晨夕相依如昆季。宦者以一赠陈子右诗。韩子人谷亦得其一。陈、韩固亲串，过从无间，鹦鹉时互相问哥哥好。未几，陈子斋中有异物搏鹦鹉死，陈子痛之甚，既除地以瘗之，又语人谷，赋诗吊之。诗成，人谷特告其家羽，辄腾踯架上曰：“哥哥死！哥哥死！”伤惋不胜，遂不食，越日亦蜕去。二子广乞名词，为之志述。江右、三吴诸词人皆有作，因汇为一集，颜曰《羽声合刻》。

    Explanation

    522

    During the years of loyalty and purity in Macheng, in the southern region of Jingnan, there was a parrot. When the elder monk Shoupu came, the parrot suddenly cried out, "May I receive mercy!" The elder said, "Little creature, who taught you to speak?" The parrot remained silent thereafter. The owner set it free, and it flew directly to the side of the monk, chirping and expressing gratitude. The monk said, "You should fly high to avoid falling again." The parrot then asked for guidance, and the monk instructed it to recite Buddhist scriptures. Eight years later, when the monk arrived in Taoyuan, a young boy came to thank him, saying, "I am the parrot of the Ma family. I am grateful for your kindness. Now, I have been reborn as a boy in the Xiao family." Upon examination, it was found that he still had wing feathers on his sides.

    There was an official from Fujian who brought two parrots back to Jiangxi. The two birds depended on each other like brothers and sisters. The official gave one parrot to Chen Zi You Shi, and Han Zi Ren Gu also received one. Chen and Han were close relatives, and their interactions were frequent. The parrots often asked each other, "Brother, are you well?" Soon after, an unusual creature in Chen's study attacked and killed the parrot. Chen was deeply saddened and buried it in the ground. He then told Han, asking him to compose a poem in mourning. After the poem was written, Han specially informed the parrot's family, and the surviving parrot jumped and fluttered on the perch, crying, "Brother has died! Brother has died!" Overwhelmed with grief, the parrot stopped eating and died the next day. The two poets sought the names of scholars and wrote a memorial to commemorate the parrots. Poets from Jiangxi and the Three Wu regions all contributed works, which were compiled into a collection titled Feather Sound Compilation.

    523

    邓子左之为之序，序亦凄惻肆动。物固多情如此！又吾梁山货店市肆，养鹦鹉甚慧，东关口市肆，有“料哥”亦能言。两店携二鸟相较。鹦鹉歌一诗，“料哥”随和，音清越不相下。“料哥”再挑与言，不答一字。人问其故，曰：“彼音劣我而黠胜我，开口便为所窃矣。”臬司有爱子病笃，购以娱之。贾人笼之以献，鸚鹉悲愁不食，自歌曰：“我本山货店中鸟，不识台司衙内尊。真最是伤心怀旧主，难将巧语博新恩。”五日，苦口求归，乃返之山货店，垂颈气尽。万历年间事也。

    Explanation

    523

    Deng Zuo wrote the preface, and the preface is also filled with sorrow and emotion. Things are indeed so full of emotion! At my store in Liangshan, we kept a very intelligent parrot. In the market at Dongguan Kou, there was a "Liaoguo" that could also speak. The two stores brought the two birds together to compare them. The parrot sang a poem, and the "Liaoguo" harmonized, their voices clear and high-pitched, neither inferior to the other. The "Liaoguo" then challenged the parrot to speak, but the parrot did not reply with a single word. When asked why, it said, "Its voice is inferior to mine, but it is more cunning than me; if I open my mouth, it will take advantage of me." A provincial governor had a beloved son who was gravely ill, and he bought the parrot to entertain him. The merchant put the parrot in a cage and presented it. The parrot was sad and refused to eat, singing to itself, "I was originally a bird from the Liangshan store; I do not know the respect of the young master of the governor's office. Truly, it is most heartbroken to miss my old master; it is hard to win new favor with clever words." After five days, the parrot pleaded earnestly to return home, and so it was returned to the Liangshan store, where it hung its head and breathed its last. This happened during the Wanli reign.

    524

    [张山来曰：向闻有人供一高僧，其庭中鹦鹉，于无人时，向僧曰：“西来意，你教我个出笼计。”僧应之云：“出笼计，除非是两脚笔直，双眼紧闭！”少顷，鹦鹉足直目闭而死。主人悼惋，命解绦瘗之。解后，鹦鹉忽飞去。向僧谢曰：“西来意，多谢你个出笼计！”附记于此。]
    　　　　剑侠见于古传纪中甚伙，近不但无其人，且未闻其事。唯闻宋辕文尊公幼清孝廉，素好奇术，曾遇异人于淮上。席间谈剑术，其人曰：“世人胆怯，见鬼神辄惊悸欲死。魂魄尚不能定，安望授鬼神术？”宋曰：“特未见耳，乌足畏？”其人忽指坐后曰：“如此人，公那不畏？”回首顾之，座后辄有神，靛面赤髭，狰狞怪异，如世所塑灵官像。宋惊惧仆地，其人曰：“云得不畏耶？”又予姻陈州宋镜予光禄尊人圃田公，讳一韩。

    Explanation

    524

    **Zhang Shanlai said: Recently, I heard of a high-ranking monk who was attended to by someone. In his courtyard, there was a parrot. When no one was around, the parrot said to the monk, "What is the meaning from the West? Teach me a way to escape the cage." The monk replied, "A way to escape the cage, unless your feet are straight and your eyes are closed!" After a moment, the parrot stood with its feet straight and eyes closed and died. The owner mourned and ordered the rope to be untied and the parrot to be buried. After it was buried, the parrot suddenly flew away. It then thanked the monk, saying, "What is the meaning from the West? Thank you for teaching me a way to escape the cage!" This is recorded here as an addition.

    Swordsmen are mentioned many times in ancient legends and records, but in modern times, not only are there no such people, but their stories are also unheard of. I have only heard that Song Yuanwen's respected father, Youqing Xiao Lian, always had a fondness for strange techniques and once met a mysterious person in the Huai River area. During the meal, they discussed swordsmanship. The mysterious person said, "People are timid and are terrified to death when they see ghosts and deities. Their souls and spirits are not yet settled, how can they be taught the techniques of ghosts and deities?" Song Yuanwen said, "I have not seen them, so what is there to fear?" The mysterious person suddenly pointed behind Song Yuanwen and said, "Such a person, do you not fear?" Song Yuanwen turned around and saw a deity behind him, with a blue face and a red beard, looking fierce and strange, like the image of a deity as commonly depicted. Song Yuanwen was so frightened that he fell to the ground. The mysterious person said, "Do you not fear now?" Additionally, my wife's relative from Chen Prefecture, Song Jingyu, the Grand Censor, whose respected father is known as Puyuan, is named Yi Han."**

    525

    神庙时在兵垣，劾李宁远，疏至一二十上。宁远百计解之，卒不从。一夕，公独卧书室中，晨起，见室内几案盘盂，巾舄衣带，下至虎子之属，无不中分为二，痕无偏缺，有若生成。而户扃如故，夜中亦无少声息。公知宁远所为，即移疾归。光禄时侍养京邸，盖亲见之。乃知世不乏异术，特未之逢耳。蜀许寂好剑术，有二僧语之曰：“此侠也，愿公无学。神仙清净，事异于此。诸侠皆鬼，为阴物，妇人僧尼皆学之。”此言近理，世之好异者当知之。

    Explanation

    525

    During the reign of Emperor Shenzong, while serving in the Military Department, the official repeatedly impeached Li Ningyuan, submitting memorials up to twenty times. Li Ningyuan used every possible means to resolve the matter, but the impeachments finally did not yield. One night, the official was alone in his study. In the morning, he found that all the objects in the room—tables, trays, shoes, belts, and even the chamber pot—were split exactly in half, with no unevenness, as if they had been so since their creation. The door was still locked as usual, and there was no sound at night. The official knew what Li Ningyuan had done and immediately pretended illness to resign. Guanglu served as a caretaker in the capital and witnessed this personally. Thus, it is known that the world is not lacking in strange arts, but they have not been encountered.

    In Shu, Xu Ji was fond of swordsmanship, and two monks told him, "This is the way of the warrior, and I wish you would not learn it. The path of immortals is pure and different from this. All warriors are spirits, serving dark forces, and women and monks all learn it." This statement is close to the truth, and those who love the strange should know it.

    526

    [张山来曰：若我遇其人，当即恳靛面赤髭者为我泄愤矣，尚何所畏耶？]
    　　　　张瑶星语予：辛未秋，予觐先大夫于东牟，遇道人马绣头者，亦异人也。道人修髯伟干，黄发覆顶，舒之可长丈许，不栉不沐，而略无垢秽。自言生于正统甲子，至是约百八十余岁矣。行素女木，所至淫妪鸨姏，多从之游。时孙公元化开府于登，闻而恶之，呼至，将加责焉。道人曰：“公秉钺一方，选士如林，乃不能容一野人耶？”公厉声曰：“予选士以备用耳。若拥肿何所用？”道人曰：“万有一备指使，可乎？”时方大旱，公曰：“若能致雨乎？”曰：“易易耳！”问所须，曰：“须桌数百张，结坛于郊，公等竭诚，唯我命是从。

    Explanation

    526

    If Zhang Shanlai were to meet that person, he would immediately ask the man with a red face and a beard to vent his anger for him. What else would he fear? Zhang Yaoming told me: In the autumn of the Xinwei year, I met my deceased father in Dongmu and encountered a strange person named Ma Xiutou. He was an extraordinary individual. The man had a long beard and a tall, imposing figure, with yellow hair covering his head. When spread out, it could be as long as a few feet, and he did not comb or wash it, yet there was still no dirt or filth. He said he was born in the first year of Zhengtong and by now he is about one hundred and eighty years old. His behavior was unusual; wherever he went, there were many old women and prostitutes who followed him. At that time, Sun Yuanhua was appointed governor in Dengzhou. He heard about Ma Xiutou and hated him, calling him to punish him. The governor said, "I wield power in one region and select officials like trees in a forest, yet he cannot tolerate one wild man?" Ma Xiutou replied, "If I could be ready for any command, would that be acceptable?" At that time, there was a great drought. The governor asked, "Can you bring rain?" Ma Xiutou said, "It's easy." The governor asked what he needed, and he said, "I need hundreds of tables, to set up an altar in the suburbs. You all should be sincere and follow my commands."

    527

    稍龃龉者，不效矣。”公曰：“姑试之。不效，乃公不尔恕也！”命治坛如其式。凌晨，率僚吏往。道人至，则索烧酒一斗，并犬一器，啖之尽，乃登坛。命公等长跪坛下。时方溽暑，万里无纤云，道人东向而嘘，则有片云从其嘘处起，复东向而呼，则微风应之。少焉，浓云四布，雷电交作，雨下如注。道人高卧坛上，鼾声与雷声响答互应。地上水可二尺，诸公长跪泥淖中不敢动。历三时许，道人乃寤，曰：“雨足乎？”众欢呼曰：“足矣！”道人挥手一喝，而雨止云散，烈日如故。孙公踉跄起，扶掖而下，以所乘八座乘之，而骑从以归，归即送入先大夫署中。

    Explanation

    527

    Slight discrepancies will not succeed." The official said, "Let's try it. If it doesn't work, then I won't forgive you!" He ordered the altar to be set up according to its form. At dawn, he led the officials to the site. When the Taoist priest arrived, he asked for a dou of liquor and a dish of dog meat, eating it all, and then climbed the altar. He ordered the officials to kneel long at the base of the altar. It was a hot and humid summer day, with no cloud in the sky for thousands of miles. Facing east and exhaling, there was a small cloud rising from where he exhaled; facing east and calling out, there was a gentle breeze responding. Soon, dark clouds spread around, thunder and lightning clashed, and rain poured down. The Taoist priest lay high on the altar, his snoring echoing with the thunder. The water on the ground was about two feet deep, and the officials knelt in the mud, unable to move. After about three hours, the Taoist priest woke up and said, "Is the rain enough?" The crowd cheered, "It's enough!" With a wave of his hand and a shout, the rain stopped, the clouds dispersed, and the sun shone brightly again. The official staggered up and was helped down. He was placed on the eight-seated carriage, and the officials followed him back. Upon returning, he was sent to the residence of the elder official.

    528

    先大夫故好士，署中客约廿余人，每夕必列席共饮，饮必招道人与俱。道人言笑不倦，而多不食。或劝之食，则命取大罌，尽投诸肴核其中，以水沃之，一举而尽。复劝之食，则命取他席上肴核投罌中，尽之如初。乃至尽庖厨中数十人之馔，悉投悉尽。或戏曰：“能复食乎？”曰：“可！”则取席上诸柈盂盌盎之类，十五累之，举而大嚼，如嚼冰雪，齿声楚楚可听也。先大夫治兵庙岛，拉与俱，宿署楼上。楼滨海，时严冬，海上无日不雪，雪即数尺。人争塞向墐户，以避寒威，而道人夜必敞北窗，以首枕窗面卧。早起，雪覆身上如堆絮，道人拂袖而起，额上汗犹津津然。或投身海中，盘薄游泳，如弄潮儿。及登岸，遍身热气如蒸，而衣不少濡湿也。

    Explanation

    528

    My father, a man of great scholarly inclination, often hosted around twenty guests in his office. Every evening, they would gather to drink together, and invariably, a Daoist would be invited to join. The Daoist was always lively in conversation and laughter, though he rarely ate. When someone urged him to eat, he would command a large jar to be brought, into which all the dishes and fruits from the table were poured, then water was added to dilute the mixture, and he would consume it all in one go. If pressed to eat again, he would order more dishes from other tables to be added to the jar, and he would finish it as before. This process continued until all the food from the kitchen, enough for dozens of people, was consumed. Once, someone joked, "Can you eat again?" He replied, "Yes!" and took fifteen plates, bowls, and pots, piling them up and devouring them in large bites, as if chewing on ice or snow, the sound of his teeth grinding clearly audible. My father once led a military campaign to a temple island, taking the Daoist along, and they stayed in the office building on the upper floor. The building faced the sea, and it was a harsh winter, with snow falling every day, often several feet deep. While others rushed to block the windows and doors to keep out the cold, the Daoist would always open the northern window and rest his head against it to sleep. In the morning, his body would be covered in snow like a mound of cotton, but he would simply shake off the snow with a sweep of his sleeve and rise, his forehead still glistening with sweat. Sometimes, he would dive into the sea, swimming skillfully and gracefully like a tide rider. When he returned to shore, his entire body was hot as if steamed, yet his clothes were not at all damp.

    529

    既而往游东江，东江帅为刘兴治。道人至，则聚诸淫妪，如在登时。兴治闻之怒，呼而责之，将绳以法。道人曰：“公尸居余气，乃相吓耶？何能杀我，人将杀公耳！”兴治益怒，道人指其左右曰：“此皆杀公者也！俟城石转身，则其时矣。”兴治命责之，鞭扑交下，道人鼾睡自若，兴治无如何也。道人出，语其徒曰：“辱我甚，不可居矣。”乃往海中浴，浴竟，见有一木，大数围，知是土人物，从求得之。自持斧，略加刳凿，才可容足，辄坐其中，乱流浮海而去，不知所终。其后兴治以贪残失士，改筑岛城，城石尽转，而兴治为其下所刺。

    Explanation

    529

    Afterward, he went to travel to the eastern river, where the commander was Liu Xingzhi. When the monk arrived, he gathered many promiscuous women, as if it were a common occurrence. Liu Xingzhi heard about this and became angry, calling the monk to reprimand him and intending to punish him according to the law. The monk said, "You are a corpse with remaining breath, trying to scare me? How can you kill me? People will kill you!" Liu Xingzhi became even angrier, and the monk pointed to those around him and said, "These are all the ones who will kill you! When the stones of the city turn, that will be the time." Liu Xingzhi ordered him to be punished, and whips and beatings were applied, but the monk slept soundly and peacefully, indifferent to the situation. Liu Xingzhi could do nothing about it. The monk left and told his disciples, "I have been greatly humiliated; I cannot stay here anymore." He then went to bathe in the sea, and after bathing, he saw a large piece of wood, several encircling in size, which he knew was a local object. He asked for it and obtained it. He took an axe and roughly carved and hollowed it out, just enough to fit his feet, and sat inside it, floating across the sea amidst the currents and going away, no one knowing where he ended up. Later, Liu Xingzhi lost his followers due to his greedy and cruel behavior, rebuilt the island city, and all the stones of the city turned, and Liu Xingzhi was killed by his subordinates.

    530

    方道人之在署中也，每酒后，辄抚膺痛哭。先大夫叩其故，则指予曰：“郎君有仙才，而年不永。使从我游，不死可致也。”先大夫曰：“年几何？”曰：“尽明岁之正月。”次年壬申，春王四日，道人方与岛中诸将士轰饮次，忽西向而恸曰：“可惜张公，今日死矣！”盖登州城陷之日也。乃知向日酒后之言，盖托讽耳。

    Explanation

    530

    When the Daoist was in the office, after drinking, he would often clasp his chest and weep bitterly. My father asked the reason, and the Daoist pointed to me and said, "The young master has the talent of a sage, but his lifespan is not long. If he follows me, he can achieve immortality." My father asked, "How long?" The Daoist replied, "Until the end of the next year's first month." The following year, in the third month of the spring, on the fourth day, the Daoist was just drinking with the various generals on the island. Suddenly, he turned westward and wept bitterly, saying, "What a pity, General Zhang has died today." It was the day when the city of Dengzhou fell. Thus I realized that the words spoken after drinking were merely a veiled criticism.

    531

    予尝谓道人啸命风雷如反掌，预识休咎如列眉，傲慢公卿如观变场，绝寒暑饥饱如化人，而独不避秽行，与淫妪游，且比及顽童，曰“中有真阴，可采补也”。此大悖谬！岂世上自有此一种，如《楞严》所称“十种仙”，或唐人所称“通天狐”属耶？抑天上群仙，亦如人间显宦，不尽皆立品行、纫荪荃者耶？吾又安得叩九阍而问之？
    　　　　曲周陈公令桐，言其邑富翁子妇自父家还，明日偕卧不复起。家人呼之不应，抉户而入，烟扑鼻如硫黄。就床视之，衾半焦，火烁之，有孔，二体俱焚，唯一足在。火之焚人，理殊不可解。王虚舟曰：“焚砂石为龙火，焚金铁为佛火。焚人之火，是为欲火。佛言淫习交接，发于相磨，研磨不休，如是故有大猛火光，于中发动。意其研磨之极，欲火炽煽，煽而忽焰，遂以自焚。其不焚床第庐舍者，火生于欲，异于常火，亦如龙火止焚砂石，佛火止焚金铁耳陈公讳于阶。

    Explanation

    531

    I have once said that Daoists can summon wind and thunder as if turning their hands, foretell good and bad omens as if counting their eyebrows, treat high officials with disdain as if they were spectators in a theater, and transcend cold, heat, hunger, and fullness as if transformed. Yet, they do not avoid foul places or consort with licentious women, and even associate with ignorant children, saying, "There is true yin (essence) inside, which can be harvested and replenished." This is greatly absurd! Is there such a type of person in the world, like the "ten kinds of immortals" mentioned in the Lengyan Sutra, or the "heaven-piercing foxes" mentioned by Tang people? Or are the immortals in heaven also like powerful officials in the world, not all of whom are virtuous and refined? How can I knock on the nine gates (a metaphor for the celestial palace) and ask them?

    Mr. Chen, the magistrate of Tong, told me that a wealthy man's wife returned to her husband's home from her father's house and the next day lay down together and did not get up again. When the family called to her, she did not respond, so they broke the door open and found smoke filling the room like sulfur. When they approached the bed, they saw the quilt half burned, the fire had scorched it, leaving holes, and both bodies were burned, but only one foot remained. The fire that burned a person is a matter that is very hard to understand. Wang Xuzhou said, "Fire that burns sand and stone is dragon fire, and fire that burns gold and iron is Buddha fire. The fire that burns people is desire fire. The Buddha said that lustful habits and interactions, when ground together incessantly, result in a great blazing fire that erupts. It seems that when the grinding reaches its peak, desire fire becomes intense, and suddenly ignites, leading to self-immolation. The reason it did not burn the bed, house, or shelter is that the fire is born of desire, different from ordinary fire, just as dragon fire only burns sand and stone, and Buddha fire only burns gold and iron." Mr. Chen's courtesy name was Yu Jie.

    532

    [张山来曰：旧小说中，已有吞绣鞋、焚祅庙事矣。
    　　　　某道人坐功久，忽然火发，焚其领及帷。主人救之始熄。可见火无邪正，皆足为害也。此道人余曾见之。]
    　　　　亳州孙骨碌者，人像其形，故以“骨碌”称。生时有首有身，身上具肩，无臂手；身下具尻，无腿足，如截瓜然。其父无子，以其男体，姑育之。长而家益富，坐卧启处，饮食男女，一切需人为用。见宾客，皆人抱以出。立则竖而倚之门屏间，失倚则仆地。衣具袖为观美，领不{纟叩}襭，则前后转徙无定在。裙、袜、履，生平未尝设。生三子，长公登进士，次幼为诸生，今且貤封矣。此等世虽生不育，育亦贫且贱，而孙君独富贵，造化固不可测欤！

    Explanation

    532

    Zhang Shanlai said: In old novels, there are already tales of swallowing embroidered shoes and burning temples of evil spirits.

    A certain Taoist practitioner had been practicing for a long time when suddenly a fire broke out, consuming his collar and curtains. His host extinguished the fire. It can be seen that fire, whether good or evil, is all capable of causing harm. I have seen this Taoist practitioner myself.

    As for Sun Gulu of Bozhou, people likened his appearance to his name, hence he was called "Gulu." He was born with a head and a body, with shoulders on his body but no arms or hands; below his body, he had a buttocks but no legs or feet, resembling a cut melon. His father had no son, so he raised him as a male. As he grew older, the family became increasingly wealthy. For sitting, lying down, opening doors, and other daily activities, as well as eating, drinking, and interacting with women, everything required assistance from others. When he met guests, they were all carried out by others. When standing, he would lean against the door or screen, and if he lost his balance, he would fall to the ground. His clothes had sleeves for aesthetic purposes, but without a collar to tuck in, the front and back would shift without a fixed position. He never wore skirts, socks, or shoes throughout his life. He had three sons: the eldest passed the imperial examination and became a jinshi, the second was a scholar, and the third has now received posthumous honors. In this world, such people are often not born or not raised, and if raised, they are usually poor and lowly. Yet Sun Gulu alone became wealthy and honored—indeed, the workings of nature are unfathomable.

    533

    [张山来曰：此君之父，因无子而育之，可也。但不识何等女子居然肯嫁之乎？]
    　　　　海盐有优者金凤，少以色幸于严东楼。东楼昼非金不食，夜非金不寝也。严敗，金亦衰老，食贫里中。比有所谓《鸣凤记》，金复涂粉墨，身扮东楼矣。近阮怀宁自为剧，命家优演之。怀宁死，优儿散于他室。李优者，但有客命为怀宁所撰诸剧，辄辞不能，复约其同辈勿复演。询其故，曰：“阿翁姓字，不触起，尚免不得人说。每一演其撰剧，座客笑骂百端，使人懊恼竟日，不如辞以不能为善也。”此优胜金优远矣！不知怀宁地下何以见此优？

    Explanation

    533

    Zhang Shanlai said: "The father of this gentleman adopted him because he had no son, which is acceptable. But I wonder what kind of woman would actually agree to marry him?"

    In Haisalt, there was a performer named Jin Feng, who was favored by Yan Donglou for her beauty when she was young. Yan Donglou would not eat without Jin Feng during the day and would not sleep without her at night. After Yan's downfall, Jin Feng also grew old and lived in poverty in the village. Later, there was a play called The Ode to the Phoenix, and Jin Feng once again applied makeup and performed the role of Yan Donglou. Recently, Ruan Huaining wrote a play and ordered the family actors to perform it. After Ruan's death, the actors dispersed to other households. Li You, however, would refuse to perform any of Ruan's plays, and he even persuaded his peers not to perform them. When asked why, he said, "My father's name, if mentioned, would still not escape being talked about. Every time I performed his plays, the guests would laugh and scold in many ways, making me feel annoyed all day. It is better to decline and say I cannot perform them well." This actor far surpasses Jin Feng! How would Ruan Huaining in the afterlife see this actor?

    534

    闽人李春明者，为人长厚，闻有谈人暖昧事，辄塞耳走。人以“李塞耳”呼之。一日耳内奇痒，召工取之，内黄金二分，易银一钱四分，市谷一斛。内有大珠二颗，最圆美，市诸富室，得六百金。其年谷甚贱。夜就寝，梦有人提其耳曰：“邦有道谷。”寤而省曰：“神意得无使我积谷乎？”乃出金市谷，入三千石。次年，谷价腾贵，发粜得四千余金。家日起，至十数万，人以为厚德之报。大抵谈人闺阃，原非盛德事。使其事诚有之，与我何与？无而言之，则为诬善矣！斯事有无不必论，后生固当以为法矣。

    Explanation

    534

    A man from Fujian named Li Chunming was honest and kind. Whenever he heard people discussing private matters, he would plug his ears and walk away. People called him "Li Plugs His Ears." One day, his ear was strangely itchy, so he called a craftsman to remove it. Inside, he found two pieces of gold, which he exchanged for one and four-tenths of an ounce of silver, and bought a bushel of grain. Among the items were two large, perfectly round pearls, which he sold to wealthy households for six hundred coins. That year, the price of grain was very low. One night, as he was going to bed, he dreamed that someone pulled his ear and said, "When the state is well-governed, grain is abundant." Upon waking, he reflected, "Could this be a divine sign telling me to buy grain?" He then spent money to buy grain, accumulating three thousand bushels. The next year, the price of grain skyrocketed, and he sold it for over four thousand coins. His family's fortune began to grow, reaching tens of thousands of coins, and people believed it was a reward for his great virtue. Generally speaking, talking about people's private lives is not a virtuous act. If something is true, it has nothing to do with me; if it is not true but is said, it is to slander the good! Whether this event is true or not is not the point; young people should certainly take this as a model.

    535

    汀州黎媿曾为余言：广州民有以善射声名者，常挟毒矢入山中。值雷雨卒至，惊避入野祠。雷随入，{石籤}磾绕身者三匝，然终不为害。民跪而祈曰：“民诚罪，遽击何所逃？奈何格格悸人耶？”雷声渐引去，已复至，复出，如是者再，若将导之前者，终不害民，民忽悟曰：“神将用我矣，遂不霆。”逐雷声行，抵山下，见雷方吐火施鞭，奋击巨树。一朱衣女子，突从树中出，雷遽远树数舍，红衣下，雷复至；红衣出，则雷又远去。格斗久之，终不成击。民乃引毒矢伺红衣出，贯之，霹雳大作，遽拔其树。民归，入其室，家人竞言雷方入屋，震人几死，幸家无恙，唯釜翻，露硃书数字于底，不可识。有黄冠通雷文者，云是“助神威力，延寿一纪”八字也。山中人言，树平时无他异，亦终不知女子为何妖。按唐小说中，亦有神追朱衣女子，自树中出，久之渐上，有数点绯雨飞下，云是帝命诛飞天夜叉。此女得非其类耶？

    Explanation

    535

    Mr. Li Kui from Ting Prefecture once told me: There was a man in Guangzhou known for his skill in archery. He often carried poisoned arrows into the mountains. One day, a sudden thunderstorm occurred, and he was startled and fled into a shrine in the wild. The thunderstorm followed and entered the shrine, circling around his body three times, but ultimately did not cause harm. The man knelt and prayed, saying, "I truly have committed a sin, why should you strike me so suddenly? Why do you make such a terrifying noise?" The thunder gradually moved away, then returned, and then left again, like this twice, as if guiding him forward. Ultimately, it did not strike the man. The man suddenly realized, "The divine will be using me," and so it did not strike. He followed the sound of the thunder and arrived at the foot of the mountain, where he saw the thunderstorm spitting fire and wielding a whip, fiercely striking a large tree. Suddenly, a woman in red clothing emerged from the tree. The thunderstorm immediately moved several miles away from the tree. When the woman in red clothing descended, the thunderstorm returned; when the woman in red clothing emerged, the thunderstorm moved away again. They fought for a long time, but ultimately did not strike. The man then drew his poisoned arrow and waited for the woman in red clothing to emerge, striking her. A loud thunderclap sounded, and the tree was uprooted. The man returned home and entered his house. His family members all said that the thunderstorm had just entered the house, nearly killing someone. Fortunately, the house was unharmed, but only the pot overturned, revealing red characters on the bottom, which could not be recognized. There was a Taoist priest who understood the thunder's writing. He said it was the eight characters "to assist the divine power and extend life for a decade." People in the mountains said that the tree had no other unusual features, but they ultimately did not know what kind of demon the woman was. In the Tang dynasty novels, there is also a story of a divine being pursuing a woman in red clothing, emerging from a tree, and gradually ascending, with several points of red rain falling down, said to be the emperor's command to punish a flying night demon. Could this woman be of the same kind?

    536

    [张山来曰：减斋先生与先君子为莫逆交，予少时获睹《书影》，甲寅之变，书皆不存。今燕客先生来扬佐郡，余复恳得是书，不啻与父执相对也。]
    　　记桃核念珠　平湖高士奇澹人
    　　　　得念珠一百八枚，以山桃核为之，圆如小樱桃。一枚之中，刻罗汉三四尊，或五六尊，立者，坐者、课经者、荷杖者、入定于龛中者、荫树趺坐而说法者、环坐指画论议者、袒跣曲拳、和南而前趋而后侍者，合计之，为数五百。蒲团、竹笠、茶奁、荷策、瓶钵、经卷毕具。又有云龙风虎，狮象鸟兽，狻猊猿猱错杂其间。初视之，不甚了了。明窗净几，息心谛观，所刻罗汉，仅如一粟，梵相奇古，或衣文织绮绣，或衣袈裟水田絺褐，而神情风致，各萧散于松柏岩石，可谓艺之至矣！

    Explanation

    536

    Zhang Shanlai said: "Mr. Jianzai was a close friend of my late father. When I was young, I had the opportunity to see Shuying (《书影》). During the upheaval of the Jia Yin year, the books were all lost. Now, Mr. Yanke has come to Yangzhou to assist in governance, and I have once again earnestly obtained this book, which is no less valuable than facing my father's mentor."

    I obtained one hundred and eight rosary beads, made from mountain peach pits, round like small cherries. Each bead contains three to four or five to six bodhisattvas, some standing, some sitting, some reciting sutras, some carrying staffs, some meditating in niches, some sitting under trees and preaching, some sitting in a circle discussing, some barefoot and curled up, some bowing and walking forward and backward. In total, there are five hundred figures. There are also clouds, dragons, tigers, lions, elephants, birds, and beasts, as well as mythical beasts and monkeys, interspersed among them. At first glance, they are not very clear. But when viewed in a bright window with a clean desk, and with a calm mind, the carved bodhisattvas are as small as a grain of rice, with strange and ancient forms. Some are dressed in intricate and colorful woven fabrics, while others wear Buddhist robes with water-patterned designs or simple, coarse fabrics. Their expressions and styles are scattered among pine, cypress, rocks, and cliffs, truly the pinnacle of art!

    537

    向见崔铣郎中有《王氏笔管记》云：唐德州刺史王倚家，有笔一管，稍粗于常用，中刻《从军行》一铺，人马毛发，亭台远水，无不精绝。每事复刻《从军行》诗二句，如“庭前琪树已堪攀，塞外征人殊未还”之语。又《辍耕录》载：宋高宗朝，巧匠詹成雕刻精妙，所造鸟笼四面花版，皆于竹片上刻成宫室人物、山水花木禽鸟，其细若缕，而且玲珑活动。求之二百余年，无复此一人。今余所见念珠，雕镂之巧，若更胜于二物也。惜其姓名不可得而知。

    Explanation

    537

    I have seen in the writings of Cui Xi, a scholar-official, a record titled Wang's Bamboo Tube Record that states: During the Tang dynasty, in the household of Wang Yi, the governor of Dezhou, there was a brush pen slightly thicker than the usual one, with a set of From the Military Campaign poems engraved on it. Every detail, from the hair of people and horses to distant pavilions and water, was exquisitely perfect. Each event was accompanied by the engraving of two lines from the From the Military Campaign poem, such as the lines "The jade tree before the hall is already climbable, but the soldiers on the frontier have not yet returned." In The Record of Ceasing the Plow, it is recorded: During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, the skilled craftsman Zhan Cheng created exquisitely refined carvings. The birdcages he made, with floral panels on all sides, were all carved onto bamboo strips, depicting palaces, figures, landscapes, flowers, trees, birds, and animals. So fine that they seemed like threads, and they were also intricately movable. For over two hundred years, there has been no one else like him. Now, the prayer beads I have seen, with their intricate carvings, seem even more exquisite than these two objects. It is regrettable that their names cannot be known.

    538

    长洲周汝瑚言：“吴中人业此者，研思殚精，积八九年，及其成，仅能易半岁之粟，八口之家，不可以饱。故习兹艺者亦渐少矣。”噫！世之拙者，如荷担负锄，舆人御夫之流，蠢然无知，唯以其力日役于人，既足养其父母妻子，复有余钱，夜聚徒侣，饮酒呼卢以为笑乐。今子所云巧者，尽其心神目力，历寒暑岁月，犹未免于饥馁，是其巧为甚拙，而拙者似反胜于巧也！因以珊瑚、木难饰而囊诸古锦，更书答汝瑚之语，以戒后之恃其巧者。

    Explanation

    538

    Zhou Ruhu of Changzhou said: "In Wu, those who practice this craft devote all their thought and energy, accumulating over eight or nine years. When it is completed, it can only exchange for half a year's worth of grain, and for a family of eight, it cannot be enough to satisfy their hunger. Therefore, those who practice this art are also gradually decreasing." Ah! The clumsy people of the world, such as those who carry loads, use hoes, drive carts, and control horses, are stupidly unaware, using their strength to serve others daily, already able to support their parents, wives, and children, and also having some extra money, at night gathering with friends, drinking wine, and playing dice for amusement. Now the cleverness you speak of, exhausting their spirit, mind, and eyesight, through the cold and heat of years, still cannot escape from hunger and starvation, thus, their cleverness is actually very clumsy, and the clumsy ones seem to be better off than the clever ones. Therefore, using coral, wood, and ancient brocade to decorate and store them, writing a reply to Zhou Ruhu's words, to warn future generations who rely on their cleverness.

    539

    [张山来曰：末段议论，足酲巧人之梦。特恐此论一出，巧物不复可得见矣，奈何！]
    　　核工记　沧洲宋起凤紫庭
    　　　　季弟获桃坠一枚，五分许，横广四分。全核向背皆山，山坳插一城雉，历历可数。城颠具层楼，楼门洞敞，中有人，类司更卒，执桴鼓，若寒冻不胜者。枕山麓一寺，老松隐蔽三章，松下凿双户，可开合；户内一僧，侧首倾听。户虚掩如应门，洞开如延纳状，左右度之，无不宜。松外东来一衲，负卷帙踉跄行，若为佛事夜归者。对林一小陀，似闻足音仆仆前。核侧出浮屠七级，距滩半黍。近滩维一舟，蓬窗短舷间，有客凭几假寐，形若渐寤然。舟尾一小童，拥护嘘火，盖供客茗饮也。舣舟处，当寺阴，高阜钟阁踞焉。

    Explanation

    539

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: The concluding remarks are sufficient to sober the dreams of the clever ones. Especially afraid that once this theory is put forth, clever objects will no longer be seen, what to do?]
    　　Record of the Carving Craft　Cangzhou Song Qifeng Ziti
    　　　　My younger brother obtained a peach pit, about five parts long and four parts wide. The entire pit, front and back, is covered with mountains. In the valley of the mountains, there is a city wall with towers, clearly countable. At the top of the city wall, there are multi-story buildings, with doors wide open. Inside, there is a person, resembling a night watchman, holding drums, as if unable to withstand the cold. Beneath the foot of the mountain is a temple, with three old pines providing shade. Below the pines, there are two doors that can be opened and closed; inside, there is a monk, leaning his head to listen. The door is slightly open, as if responding to someone, and wide open, as if inviting someone in, and when viewed from all sides, everything is appropriate. Outside the pines, a monk comes from the east, carrying books and walking hurriedly, as if returning from a Buddhist ceremony at night. Facing the forest, a small monk seems to hear footsteps approaching. On the side of the pit, there is a seven-story pagoda, half a grain of rice away from the shore. Near the shore, there is a boat, with a guest leaning against a table, pretending to sleep, as if gradually waking up. At the back of the boat, there is a young boy, supporting and blowing fire, presumably to provide tea for the guest. The place where the boat is moored is under the shade of the temple, with a high mound and a bell tower situated there.

    540

    叩钟者貌爽爽自得，睡足徐兴乃尔。山顶月晦半规，杂疏星数点，下则波纹涨起，作潮来候，取诗“姑苏城外寒山寺，夜半钟声到客船”之句。计人凡七：僧四，客一，童一，卒一。宫室器具凡九：城一，楼一，招提一，浮屠一，阁一，炉灶一，钟鼓各一。景凡七：山水林木滩石四，星月灯火三。而人事如传更、报晓、候门、夜归、隐几、煎茶，统为六。各殊致殊意，且并其愁苦寒惧疑思诸态，俱一一肖之。语云：“纳须弥于芥子”，殆谓是与？然闻之：“尺绡绣经而唐微，水戏荐酒而隋替。”器之淫也，吾滋惧矣！先王著《考工》，盖早辨之焉。
    　　　　[张山来曰：家人以象为楮叶，杂之真叶中，不能辨审。若是，则曷不摘真楮叶玩之乎？今之鬼工桃核，精巧绝伦，人皆以其核也而宝之，庶不虚负此巧耳！]

    540

    The person who strikes the bell appears relaxed and self-assured, gradually waking up after a good sleep. On the mountain top, the moon is partially obscured, with a few sparse stars scattered around. Below, the waves rise, signaling the incoming tide. This scene evokes the famous line from Du Mu's poem: "At midnight, the bell from Hanshan Temple reaches the boat." There are seven people in total: four monks, one guest, one boy, and one guard. There are nine items of furniture and structures: one city, one tower, one temple, one pagoda, one pavilion, one stove, and one bell and drum each. There are seven scenes: four of mountains, water, forests, and stones, and three of stars, the moon, and lamps. The activities described include changing shifts, announcing dawn, waiting at the gate, returning at night, leaning on a desk, and brewing tea, totaling six. Each activity has its own unique character and meaning, and the various emotional states—sorrow, hardship, cold, fear, doubt, and contemplation—are all vividly portrayed. The idiom "to contain Mount Sumeru in a mustard seed" illustrates the concept of vastness in small things. However, the saying "excessive luxury led to the decline of the Tang and Sui dynasties" warns of the dangers of indulgence. The principles of craftsmanship and governance were already distinguished long ago in the ancient text Kaogongji. Zhang Shanlai notes that people often find it difficult to distinguish artificial leaves from real ones, just as it is hard to tell the difference between real and artificial. Today, the intricate craftsmanship of peach pits, which are so finely made that they are treasured, perhaps do not辜负 this skill.

    541

    张南邨先生传　泸州先著迁甫
    　　　　张南邨，名惣，字僧持。父兴公先生琪，以名宿教授里中，多达材弟子。南邨幼为诗、出语每不犹人。父友纪竺远一见其诗，称之曰“气清”，再则曰“骨清”，曰“神清”，已而目属之曰：“子必将以诗名江左矣！”入应天学，用才名交游贤俊，治古文辞，专力于诗。
    　　　　家世奉佛，南邨胎性不纳荤血。初犹食蟹，年八岁，父将携之见博山禅师，前一夕，南邨方持蟹，父见之，警曰：“儿将见博师，可食此乎？”南邨闻言，即置不食。自是蟹胥悉断除。杖人在天界，南邨亲近最久，东南古锥宿德，礼谒殆遍。以故生平多方外交，齑盂粥钵，宛然头陀。踪迹恒在僧寺中，或经年累月不返。少学《易》于中丞集生余公，余公戍武林，西泠其所熟游，故吴越往来尤数。而苕霅间故人，闻其至，每争延之。

    Explanation

    541

    The Biography of Mr. Zhang Nanzun by Luzhou Xian Zhu Qian Fu

    Zhang Nanzun, named Zong and courtesy name Sengchi. His father, Mr. Qi Xinggong, was a respected teacher in the village, with many talented disciples. As a child, Zhang Nanzun was skilled in poetry, and his speech was always unique. A friend of his father, Ji Zuyuan, upon seeing his poetry, praised it, saying, "It is clear in spirit," then, "It is clear in bone," and finally, "It is clear in essence." He then looked at Zhang Nanzun and said, "You will surely become famous for your poetry in the Jiangnan region!" Zhang Nanzun entered the Imperial College in Yingtian, using his talent and reputation to associate with the virtuous and talented, studying ancient prose and poetry, and focusing solely on poetry.

    The family had a tradition of Buddhism, and Zhang Nanzun had an innate nature that did not accept meat and blood. Initially, he still ate crabs. At the age of eight, his father planned to take him to meet Zen Master Bo Shan. The night before, Zhang Nanzun was holding a crab when his father saw him and warned, "You are going to meet Master Bo, can you eat this?" Upon hearing this, Zhang Nanzun immediately stopped eating. From then on, all crab dishes were completely eliminated. The staff holder at the Heavenly Realm, with whom Zhang Nanzun had the closest and longest relationship, was a Zen master in the southeast. He paid respects almost everywhere, engaging in many diplomatic activities, with simple food and utensils, resembling a wandering monk. His traces were always in the temples, sometimes he would not return for years. As a young man, he studied the Yijing from Vice-Chancellor Jisheng Yugu, who was stationed in Wulin. The West Lake was a place he was familiar with, so he traveled frequently between Wu and Yue. His old friends in the area of Taizhou and Zhejiang would always compete to invite him when they heard he was coming.

    542

    癖好山水，不惮险远，必往游。其游有章程要领：或独游，或携一童子，涂遇樵人禅客，即为伴侣。穷幽造深，饮泉摘果，即忘饥渴。于五岳则陟嵩岱，犹以不能遍历衡华为恨。若武夷、匡庐、九子、黄山、天台、厢荡诸山，所至削木柹为记，采树叶题诗，以为常。
    　　　　南邨为人，坦夷近情，不为矫激之言，不为崖异之行。取受从心，否塞任运，尤不以礼数恩义责望人。与人处，尤能寡怨忘隙。乍见或轻忽之，稍久必亲而敬焉。有屋数椽，不蔽风雨，家人恒至乏食。垢衣敝处，游士大夫间，举止迂野可爱。形体短小，虽老，精神可敌壮夫。遇良讌会，能通夜不眠，啸咏达旦。不择地而处，不择食而食，不择榻而寝。投足之所，即甚湫隘嚣杂，他人扫除未竟，视南邨已展卷矣。口腹之奉，不过盐豉菽乳；就枕即熟睡，无辗转不寐之时。盖胸无机事，不以美恶撄心，能致然耳。

    Explanation

    542

    He had a strong passion for natural landscapes, not afraid of danger and distance, and was determined to visit them. His travels were methodical and had clear guidelines: sometimes he traveled alone, sometimes with a young boy, and whenever he met woodcutters or monks on the road, he would take them as companions. He explored the depths and hidden places, drank from springs, and picked fruits, and in doing so, he forgot about hunger and thirst. Among the Five Great Mountains, he ascended Mount Song and Mount Tai, but he still regretted not being able to visit all the other peaks. For mountains like Wuyi, Kuanglu, Jiuzi, Huangshan, Tiantai, and Xiangdang, wherever he went, he would carve wooden tablets as markers and write poems on leaves, which he considered a regular practice.

    Nan Chun was a straightforward and simple person, not making exaggerated or forceful statements, nor engaging in extreme or unusual actions. He took and gave according to his heart, accepting misfortune and letting fate take its course, and he did not demand respect, kindness, or gratitude from others. When dealing with people, he could minimize grievances and forget grudges. At first sight, he might seem careless or indifferent, but after a while, he would become close and respectful. He had only a few small rooms, not sheltered from wind and rain, and his family often lacked food. He wore dirty and worn-out clothes and moved among scholars and officials, his manner simple and rustic, yet charming. He was short and small in stature, but even though old, his spirit was as strong as a young man's. At good banquets and gatherings, he could stay awake all night, singing and reciting poetry until dawn. He did not choose a place to live, did not choose what to eat, and did not choose a bed to sleep on. Wherever he put his foot, he could adapt to any environment, even if it was damp, narrow, and noisy. While others were still cleaning, he had already opened his book. His food was no more than salt, fermented beans, soybeans, and milk. He fell asleep immediately when he lay down, without tossing and turning. His mind was free from worldly affairs, and he did not let good or bad things disturb his mind, which was how he achieved this state.

    543

    尝远游，遇胠箧者再，中途几不能成归。人或怪其无恨色，曰：“失者偿之义也，又何问焉？”除夕自外返，去其家不远，止宿逆旅主人。次日日晡，始缓步而归，其性情安雅如此。
    　　　　群居未尝与人争，至论诗辄相持不下。宋诗行，虽贵卿巨子前，亦厉词折之。其论诗，不逞才，不使事，不染叫号，不涉怨诽，其宗旨也。自以襄阳、摩诘为师，于古歌行换韵大篇，暨古体千数百言，铺陈开合、局力宏富者，乃不谓善。自少至老，主此论不变。虽所见未尽然，亦可谓笃于自守者矣。南邨称诗五十年，远近之人，亦以诗归之。生乡名人王穆如、顾与治之后，与同时诸人并立，可指数，终竟如纪叟之言。

    Explanation

    543

    He once traveled far, encountering thieves twice, almost failing to return home halfway. People were surprised by his lack of anger, as he said, "It is the right thing to compensate those who have lost; why should I be angry?" On New Year's Eve, he returned home from outside, not far from his residence, staying at an inn. The next day at dusk, he slowly returned home, demonstrating his calm and refined nature.

    In group settings, he never argued with others, but when discussing poetry, they would debate without reaching a conclusion. His poems in the style of the Song Dynasty, even before high-ranking officials, he would use sharp words to refute them. His approach to poetry was not to show off his talent, did not use allusions, did not get caught up in shouting, and did not involve himself in complaints or accusations—these were his principles. He took Xiangyang and Wang Wei as his teachers, and did not consider large-scale ancient songs with frequent changes in rhyme, or ancient-style poems of several hundred lines that are extensive and rich in detail, to be good. From youth to old age, he adhered to this view without change. Although not everyone agreed with him, he can be said to be steadfast in his own views. Nan Village praised his poetry for fifty years, and people from far and near also regarded him as a poet. Born after the famous people Wang Mu Ru and Gu Yu Zhi of his hometown, alongside his contemporaries, he could be counted among them, in the end, he was like what Ji Gou said.

    544

    岁甲戌，年七十有六，夏得脾疾，治之寻愈。至冬复作，遂不起。子二：元子筠，正子淳。元子亦受诗，可不坠其声。予自僦居郭南，望衡密迩，相得甚欢。酒阑灯烬，每有知己之言，欲以身后为托，今不可作矣。世复安得和易素心、风雅不倦如斯人者乎！
    　　　　赞曰：策杖而去，裹粮而游，遇少倦而且休，至佳处而辄留。把酒而歌，执卷而吟，悠悠乎王、孟之音，有形神而无古今。不忤于世，不剜于天，可独可群，亦儒亦禅。束身止一棺，而遗文乃有千数百篇，称之为诗人，奚愧焉？
    　　　　[张山来曰：予慕南邨久，一旦迁甫为介，得以把臂入林。今读此，不胜人琴之感！]

    Explanation

    544

    In the year Ji Xu, at the age of seventy-six, I contracted a spleen ailment in the summer, which was quickly treated and I recovered. By winter, the illness recurred, and I did not recover. I have two sons: the eldest, Jun, and the second, Chun. The eldest son also studies poetry, and thus the family name can continue. I have rented a residence south of the city wall, and our homes are close neighbors. We have been very happy together. When the wine runs out and the lamp burns low, we often share deep and sincere words, and I wanted to entrust my affairs to him after my death, but now that is no longer possible. How can the world find people who are gentle and simple-hearted, never tired of elegance and refinement, like him!

    Praise says: He walks with a cane and travels with provisions, taking a break when he feels a little tired and staying at the best places. He holds wine and sings, holds a book and recites, with the graceful and leisurely style of Wang and Meng, having form and spirit but no distinction between past and present. He does not offend the world, nor does he offend heaven, and can be alone or with others, both Confucian and Buddhist. He is buried in a simple and humble manner, but his leftover writings amount to several hundred pieces. To call him a poet, what shame would there be?

    [Comment by Zhang Shanlai: I have admired Nan Cun for a long time, and one day Qian Fu acted as an intermediary, allowing me to enter the forest with him. Now reading this, I cannot bear the feeling of the story of man and qin!]

    545

    刘酒传　周亮工减斋
    　　　　刘酒，汴人，无名字，自呼曰“酒”，人称曰刘酒云。画人物，有清劲之致，酒后运笔，尤觉神来。人以为张平山后一人，酒不屑也。凡作画，皆书一“酒”字款，其似行书者次，似篆籀者，其得意笔也。尝为上洛郡王作画，王善之，曰：“张平山后一人！”酒意嗔，急索画曰：“尚未款。”乃卷入旁室，纵笔书百十大“酒”字于上下左右。王怒甚，裂其幅，驱之出。酒固怡然。酒于醉睡之外，唯解画，他一无所知。坡公云：“予奉使西邸，见书此数句，爱而录之，云：人间有漏仙，兀兀三杯醉。世上无眼禅，昏昏一枕睡。虽然没交涉，其奈略相似。相似尚如此，何况真个是。”酒索予颜于草堂，予书曰“略似庵”，以坡公所录前四句，去“醉”、“睡”字为联。酒得之，欣然意足也。酒与予交最久，无妻子，每谓予曰：“死以累君。”一日方持杯大饮，忽然脱去，开口而笑，杯犹在手。余感其宿昔之言，为买棺殓之。

    Explanation

    545

    The Biography of Liu Jiu by Zhou Lianggong, Jianshi

    Liu Jiu, a native of Bianjing, had no given name. He called himself "Jiu," and people referred to him as Liu Jiu. His paintings of figures were characterized by a clear and vigorous style. When he painted after drinking, his work seemed particularly inspired. People considered him the equal of Zhang Pingshan, but Liu Jiu did not regard himself as such. Whenever he painted, he would sign his work with the character "酒" (jiu). Among his signatures, those that resembled running script were second-rate, while those that resembled seal script were his most expressive and favored. Once, he was commissioned by Prince of Shangluo to paint. The prince admired his work and said, "You are the equal of Zhang Pingshan!" Liu Jiu became angry and quickly asked for his painting, saying, "It is not yet signed." He then rolled up the painting and took it into a side room, where he wrote the character "酒" (jiu) a hundred times or more in all directions. The prince was furious, tore the painting apart, and drove him out. Liu Jiu remained content. Apart from painting when drunk or asleep, he knew nothing else. Su Shi once said, "I served as an envoy in the Western Palace and saw these lines written, which I loved and recorded: 'There is a leaky immortal in the world, who is drunk after three cups. There is no eye-sealed Zen in the world, who sleeps soundly under a pillow. Although there is no connection, they are slightly similar. If they are similar like this, how much more so if they are truly the same.' Liu Jiu asked me to inscribe a name for his place in the thatched hut. I wrote 'Lue Si An' (Slightly Similar Hermitage), using the first four lines of Su Shi's poem, removing the characters '醉' (drunk) and '睡' (sleep) to form a couplet. Liu Jiu was pleased and felt satisfied. Liu Jiu and I were the closest of friends, and he had no wife or children. He often told me, 'I will burden you with my death.' One day, while holding a cup and drinking heartily, he suddenly passed away, smiling as he went, with the cup still in his hand. I was moved by his earlier words and bought a coffin to bury him.

    546

    [张山来曰：刘酒自画之外，无非酒者，其名酒，其款酒，其死亦酒，吾知其所画必醉仙也。]
    　　记古铁条　杭州詹钟玉去矜
    　　　　京师穷市上，有古铁条，垂三尺许，阔二寸有奇，形若革带之半，中虚而外绣涩，两面鼓钉隐起，不甚可辨。持此欲易钱数十文，人皆不顾去。积年余，有高丽使客三四人，旁睨良久，问：“此铁价几何？”鬻铁者谬云：“钱五百。”使客立解五百文授之。其人疑不决，即诡对曰：“此固吾邻人物，俟吾询主者。”顷之，使客复来。鬻者曰：“向几误，主者言非五金不可！”使客即割五金，无难色。其人又为大言曰：“公等误矣，吾曹市语，举大数以为言，五金盖五十金云。”使客曰：“吾诚不惜五十金，但不得更悔。”鬻铁者私念：一废铁夹条，增价五十金，借令失此售主，并乞数十文钱亦不可得。因曰：“吾以此博公多金，保无后言。公幸告我，此为何名？”使客请“先定要约，而后告子。”

    Explanation

    546

    Zhang Shanlai said: Liu Jiu, besides his paintings, is nothing but wine; his name, manner, and even his death are all associated with wine. I know that his paintings must depict a drunken immortal.

    Record of an Ancient Iron Bar by Zhan Zhongyu from Hangzhou

    In the poor market of the capital, there was an ancient iron bar, about three feet long and slightly more than two inches wide. Its shape resembled half of a leather belt, with a hollow interior and a rough, embroidered exterior. On both sides, there were raised knobs that were not very distinguishable. Someone tried to exchange it for several coins, but no one paid attention and left. After several years, a few Korean envoys looked at it for a long time and asked, "How much is this iron worth?" The seller falsely claimed, "Five hundred coins." The envoys immediately handed over five hundred coins. The seller hesitated and said, "This is someone from my neighbor's house; I will ask the owner." After a while, the envoys returned. The seller said, "I almost made a mistake; the owner said it cannot be less than five gold coins!" The envoys immediately cut out five gold coins without any hesitation. The seller then said, "You are mistaken; we use large numbers in our market language, so five gold coins mean fifty gold coins." The envoys said, "We truly do not mind fifty gold coins, but we cannot regret it." The seller thought to himself, "A piece of scrap iron, worth fifty gold coins, if I lose this buyer, I won't even get a few coins." He said, "I will give you many coins for this, and I guarantee no more words. Please tell me what this is called." The envoys requested, "Please first make an agreement, and then tell us."

    547

    时观者渐众，使客乃举五十金畀鬻铁者，而以若带者付其徒乘马疾驰去。度其去远，始告众曰：“此名定水带，昔神禹治水时，得此带九，以定九区，平水土。此乃九之一，若携归吾国，价累钜万，岂止五十金而已哉？”又问得此何所用，使客曰：“吾国航海，每苦海水咸不可饮。一投水带其中，虽咸卤立化甘泉，可无病汲，是以足珍耳。”市有好事随至高丽馆，请试验之。遂命汲苦水数石，杂盐搅之，投以水带，水带沸作鱼眼数十。少顷掬水饮之，甘冽乃胜山泉。遂各叹服而去。

    Explanation

    547

    As the crowd grew larger, the envoy presented fifty gold coins to the iron seller and handed over the belt to his disciple, who rode off on horseback quickly. After seeing them go far away, the envoy addressed the crowd, saying, "This is called the 'Ding Shui Dai.' In the time when the divine Yu controlled the floods, he obtained nine such belts, which were used to stabilize the nine regions and level the water and soil. This is one of the nine. If you bring it back to our country, its value would accumulate to millions, far more than just fifty gold coins!" The crowd asked what use it had, and the envoy replied, "In our country, we often suffer from the problem of seawater being too salty to drink. If you throw the water belt into it, even the salty water instantly turns into sweet spring water, and it can be drunk without causing illness. This is why it is so precious." There were those in the market who were curious and went to the Goryeo guesthouse to request a demonstration. The envoy ordered several buckets of brackish water to be drawn and mixed with salt, then threw in the water belt. The belt boiled, producing dozens of fish eyes. After a short while, when the water was scooped up and tasted, it was sweeter and clearer than mountain spring water. Everyone was impressed and left.

    548

    鬻铁者言，闯陷京师时，得自老中贵，盖先朝大内物也。嗟嗟！自经变故以来，凡天府奇珍异宝，流散人间、泯泯无闻者，何可胜数？独是带为高丽使所赏识，顿增身价百倍，不胫而走海外。物之显晦，固自有时哉！
    　　　　[张山来曰：既是神禹时物，不识高丽使人何以知之？殆不可解。]
    　　唐仲言传　周亮工减斋
    　　　　唐仲言，名汝询，华亭人，世业儒。仲言生五岁而瞽，未瞽即能识字，读《孝经》成诵。及瞽，但默坐，听诸兄呫哔而暗识之，积久遂淹贯。婚冠既毕，益令昆弟辈取六经子史，以及稗官野乘，皆以耳授。颠末原委，默自诠次，纯颣瑜瑕，剖别精核，盖从章句之粗，以冥搜微妙，心画心通，罔有遗堕矣。于是遂善属文，尤工于诗。海内人士，踵门造谒。仲言每一晋接，历久不忘，与之商榷今古，继以篇什。千言百首，成之俄顷，而音吐铿然，使听者忘疲。

    Explanation

    548

    A seller of iron said, during the time when the rebels breached the capital, he obtained it from an elderly eunuch, and it was a treasure of the former imperial palace. Alas! Since the great upheaval, all the rare and precious treasures of the imperial treasury, scattered among the people and now forgotten, how can they be counted? Only this belt was appreciated by the Korean envoy, and its value suddenly increased a hundredfold, traveling overseas without legs. The rise and fall of things are indeed determined by time!

    [Zhāng Shānlái said: If it is an artifact from the time of the divine Emperor Yu, how did the Korean envoy know about it? It is hard to understand.]

    The Story of Tang Zhongyan by Zhou Lianggong, known as Jianzai

    Tang Zhongyan, whose name was Ruxun, was from Huating. His family had been involved in Confucian studies. Zhongyan became blind at the age of five, but he could recognize characters even before he became blind, and he could recite the Classic of Filial Piety. When he became blind, he would sit quietly and memorize what his brothers whispered, and over time, he became thoroughly knowledgeable. After his marriage and coming of age, he further instructed his brothers to fetch the Six Classics and historical works, as well as miscellaneous historical records, all of which he learned by ear. He silently arranged and categorized them, distinguishing and analyzing the good and the bad, the pure and the impure. From the basic level of sentences and phrases, he delved into the subtle and profound, his heart drawing and understanding, nothing was left out or forgotten. Thus, he became skilled in writing, especially in poetry. People from all over the country came to visit him. Zhongyan never forgot anyone he met, and he discussed ancient and modern matters with them, following with poems and essays. He could write a thousand words or a hundred poems in a short time, and his words were clear and resonant, making listeners forget their fatigue.

    549

    子侄门徒辈，从旁抄录，一字亥豕，辄自觉察，不可欺也。貌甚寝而心极灵，常解唐诗。其所掇拾古文以为笺注者，自习见以及秘异，溯流从源，搜罗略尽，然必先经后史，不少紊淆。虽诗赋之属，所援引亦从年代次序之；如某字某句，秦、汉并用，则必博采秦人，不以汉先。详赡致精，有若此也。所著有《偏蓬集》《姑篾集》及《唐诗解》，共若干卷，行于世。钱虞山云：“唐较杜诗，时有新义。如解『沟壑疏放』句，云出于向秀赋『嵇志远而疏，吕心放而旷』，亦前人所未及也。”
    　　　　[张山来曰：古之瞽者，如师旷之徒，类多神解。或以为啬于目故专于心，想亦理当然耳。
    　　　　予向旅寓京师，居停主人双眸炯炯，同寓两人，其一为瞽者，其一眇一目，因号独眼龙。苟询以京师中昨日有何事，今日有何事，瞽者无不知，独眼龙知十之六七，居停主人仅识十之四五而已。附记于此，以供谈柄。]

    Explanation

    549

    The descendants and disciples of the author, copying from the side, would notice even the smallest error (亥豕, a typo) and could not be deceived. Though his appearance was unattractive, his mind was extremely sharp, and he often interpreted Tang poetry. The ancient texts he collected to use as annotations, ranging from common to rare and obscure, were traced back to their origins and collected extensively. However, he always prioritized the Five Classics over historical texts, ensuring no confusion. Even when citing poetry or prose, he followed chronological order; for example, if a word or phrase was used in both Qin and Han, he would cite Qin sources first, not Han. His work was thorough, detailed, and meticulous, as described. His works include Pianpeng Collection, Gumie Collection, and Tang Poetry Explained, totaling several volumes, which circulated in the world. Qian Yushan commented, "His interpretation of Tang poetry sometimes offered new insights compared to Du Fu's works. For instance, in explaining the phrase '沟壑疏放' (sparse and open like ravines), he cited a passage from Xiang Xiu's Zuo Zhuan: '嵇志远而疏，吕心放而旷' (Ji's aspirations were distant and sparse, Lu's heart was unrestrained and vast), which previous scholars had not noted."

    [According to Zhang Shanlai: Ancient blind people, like Shi Kuang, were often exceptionally perceptive. Some believed that blind people were more focused because they lacked visual distractions, which might be a reasonable explanation.
    When I stayed in Beijing, the host had bright, sharp eyes, and two other guests were present: one was blind, and the other had only one eye, earning the nickname "Dragon with One Eye." If asked about events in Beijing from yesterday or today, the blind person knew everything, the one-eyed person knew about 60-70%, and the host knew only about 40-50%. I added this anecdote for conversation or entertainment.]

    550

    李公起传　周亮工减斋
    　　　　李公起，名峻，鄞县人。父子静，官侍御，出按辽阳，卒于任。公起堕地而聋，虽聋，岐嶷孝弟。发及额，侍御公讣至，号恸无昼夜，咽枯而嘶，凡五日，水浆不入口，乃更哑。免丧，始尽取先世藏书纵读之，手自校雠，虽凌寒溽暑，弗倦也。既聋而问难辨证之路永绝，凡有疑义，俱于经史中嘿自剖析，无所罔殆。性好客，邮筒走天下，四方学士大夫亦乐趋之。宾主以案，相通以笔。有问奇者，则载纸往。粗及农桑，微如佛老，迨国家所有旂常典故、户口边疆，叩之必应，咸尽精核。或既书与客，又自寻绎，幽奇毕呈，而终无遗伕，转更遐畅矣。晚年尤好种植，奇花异卉，常满阶庭。舍旁有斐园、竹波轩、青罗阁诸胜，咸与客游处。性既宁澹，好学之外，嗜欲益清，反觉口耳为烦也。行世有《盟鸥集》《郢雪编》《永誉录》《砚史》，凡若干卷。

    Explanation

    550

    Li Gongqi, also known as Jun, was from Yin County. His father, Zijing, held the position of Censor and was dispatched to oversee Liao Yang, where he passed away in office. Li Gongqi was born deaf, yet despite his deafness, he was exceptionally virtuous from a young age, showing filial piety and fraternal love. When his father's death was announced, reaching his forehead, he wept and wailed day and night without pause, his throat parched and voice hoarse for five days, abstaining from food and water, and eventually became mute. After the mourning period, he began to thoroughly read all the books inherited from his ancestors, personally collating and correcting them, never tiring even in the face of extreme cold or heat. Although deaf, the paths of questioning and argumentation were forever closed to him, so for any doubts, he silently analyzed and dissected them within the classics and histories, never falling into confusion. By nature, he enjoyed entertaining guests, and through letters, he traveled the world, attracting scholars and officials from all directions who were happy to visit him. Host and guest communicated through tables, and they exchanged ideas with writing. If someone asked a strange question, he would bring paper to write down the answer. His knowledge ranged from basic agriculture and sericulture to subtle teachings of Buddhism and Daoism, and even up to the state's flags, rituals, customs, population, and borders. Whenever asked, he always provided precise and thorough answers. Sometimes, after writing an answer for a guest, he would also review it himself, revealing all the subtle and strange aspects, and the knowledge extended even further, becoming more expansive and profound. In his later years, he particularly enjoyed planting, and his courtyard was often filled with rare flowers and exotic plants. Beside his residence, there were beautiful spots such as Fei Garden, Bamboo Wave Pavilion, and Qing Luo Pavilion, all of which he visited with his guests. His nature was calm and tranquil, and beyond his love for learning, his desires became even purer, and he found the senses of hearing and speaking to be a bother. He published several works in his lifetime, including "The Collection of Allied Swans," "The Edited Snow of Ying," "The Record of Everlasting Praise," and "The History of Inkstones," totaling several volumes.

    551

    [张山来曰：以一人而兼聋哑二病，乃能淹博贯穿如此，那得不令人敬服？
    　　　　使此君与唐仲言相遇，则两无所见其奇矣。]
    　　书郑仰田事　钱谦益牧斋
    　　　　郑仰田者，泉之惠安人，忘其名。少椎鲁，不解治生，其父母贱恶之，逃之岭南，为寺僧种菜。寺僧饭僧及作务人，仰田面黧黑，补衣百结，居下坐，自顾踧踖无所容。有老僧长眉皓发，目光如水，呼仰田使上，指寺僧曰：“汝等皆不及也！”寺僧怒，噪而逐仰田。旬日无所归，号哭于野外。老僧迎谓曰：“吾迟子久矣！”偕入深山中，授以《拆字歌诀》。月余，遂能识字，因授以青囊袖中、壬遁、射覆诸家之术，无所不通晓。其行于世，以观梅拆字为端，久而与之游，能知人心曲隐微，及人事世运之伏匿，亦不言其所以然也。

    Explanation

    551

    Zhang Shanlai said: To have both deafness and muteness in one person and yet possess such extensive and integrated knowledge is truly remarkable, how can one not be impressed and respect such a person?
    If this gentleman met Tang Zhongyan, neither would see anything extraordinary.

    The Story of Zheng Yangtian, written by Qian Qianyi (Master Zhi)

    Zheng Yangtian was a person from Huian, Quanzhou, but his name is forgotten. In his youth, he was simple-minded and did not understand how to manage his livelihood. His parents looked down on him and disliked him, so he fled to the south of the mountains and worked as a vegetable gardener for a temple monk. The temple monk fed the monks and other workers. Zheng Yangtian had a dark complexion and tattered clothes, sat in the lowest seat, and felt awkward and out of place. There was an old monk with long eyebrows, white hair, and eyes like water. He called Zheng Yangtian to come forward and pointed to the other monks, saying, "You all fall short of him!" The monks shouted and chased Zheng Yangtian away. For ten days, he had nowhere to go and cried in the fields. The old monk came to him and said, "I have been waiting for you for a long time!" He took Zheng Yangtian into a deep mountain and taught him the Zhaizi Gejue (a method of divination using characters). After a month, Zheng Yangtian could read and write. Then, the old monk taught him various divination techniques, including those from the Qingnang (a collection of divination methods), Ren Dun (a form of divination), and Shefu (a method of divination by guessing hidden objects). He became proficient in all of them. In his practice, he started with the Guangmei Zhaizi (a method of divination using plum blossoms and characters). After a long time, he could understand the deepest and most hidden aspects of people's minds and the hidden aspects of human affairs and world events, but he did not explain how he knew this.

    552

    天启初，将卜相，南乐指“全”字为占。仰田曰：“全字从人从王，四画，当相四人。”问其姓名，曰：“全字省三画为士，当有姓带土者；省四画为丁，当有姓丁者；省两画纵横为木，当有名属木者；以所省之文全归之，当有名全者！”南乐曰：“木非林尚书乎？”曰：“独木不成林，名者，非姓也。”已而拜莆田、贵池、元城、涿州四相，一如其言。晋江李焻与奄党吴淳夫有郗，指“吞”字以问。仰田曰：“彼势能吞汝，非小敌也。

    Explanation

    552

    In the early years of the Tianqi era, the selection of chancellors was divined, and Nannle pointed to the character "全" for the divination. Yantian said, "The character '全' is composed of a person and a king, with four strokes, indicating that four chancellors will be appointed." When asked about their names, he replied, "Reducing three strokes from '全' forms the character '士', indicating someone with a surname containing the radical '土' (earth); reducing four strokes forms the character '丁', indicating someone with the surname '丁'; reducing two strokes horizontally and vertically forms the character '木', indicating someone with a name related to 'wood'; and the reduced strokes all return to '全', indicating someone with the name '全'!" Nannle asked, "Is '木' not the surname of the Minister of Works, Lin?" Yantian replied, "A single tree cannot form a forest; the name is not the surname." Later, the four chancellors from Putian, Guichi, Yuancheng, and Zhuozhou were appointed, just as he had predicted. Jiangxi Li Xian had a dispute with Wu Chunfu of the eunuch faction and pointed to the character "吞" for the divination. Yantian said, "They have the power to swallow you; this is not a small enemy."

    553

    从天从口，非其人吴姓乎？”“然则何如？”曰：“吴以口为头，彼头已落地矣，汝何忧？”逾年而吴伏法。魏奄召仰田问数，仰田蓬头突鬓，踉跄而往，长揖就坐。奄指“囚”字以问，群奄列侍，皆愕眙失色。仰田徐应曰：“囚字国中一人也！”奄大喜。出谓人曰：“囚则诚囚也，吾诡词以逃死耳。”之白门。奄势益炽，俞少卿密扣之。仰田昼奄卧屋梁下，梁上有断绠下垂，仰田指之曰：“如此矣”！未几，奄果自缢。其射决奇中，不可悉数，宋谢石不足道也。

    Explanation

    553

    From heaven and from the mouth, is it not the person with the surname Wu? "What then?" asked he. "Wu's head is his mouth, and it has already fallen to the ground, why are you worried?" The next year, Wu was executed according to the law. The eunuch summoned Yang Tian to ask about omens. Yang Tian, with disheveled hair and prominent eyebrows, staggered to go, performed a long bow, and took a seat. The eunuch pointed to the character '囚' (prisoner) and asked. All the eunuchs present were startled and pale. Yang Tian slowly replied, "The character '囚' (prisoner) is one person in the country!" The eunuch was greatly pleased. He said to others, "The prisoner is indeed a prisoner, I used clever words to escape death." He went to the White Gate. The power of the eunuchs became even stronger. Yu Shaoying secretly interrogated him. During the day, the eunuch lay under the beam of the house. There was a broken rope hanging down from the beam. Yang Tian pointed to it and said, "This is it!" Soon after, the eunuch indeed hanged himself. His accurate predictions cannot be counted, and the achievements of Song's Xie Shi are not worth mentioning.

    554

    丙子冬，前知余有急征之难，自闽来视余，自清江浦徒步入长安，为余刺探狱缓急。余抵德州，复自长安徒步来报。年八十二矣，行及奔马，两壮士尾之不能及。至郑州，风霾大作，脱鞋袜系之两臂，赤脚走百里，上程氏东壁楼，日未下舂，神色闲暇，鼻息呴呴然。谈笑大噱，至分夜而后寝。临行谓余：“七月彼当去位，公之狱解矣。然必明年而后出，吾当以残腊过虞山，为太夫人庀窀穸之事，公毋忧也。”余归，数往招之。己卯春，将袱被访余，忽谓家人曰：“明日有群僧扣门乞食，具数人餐以待，吾亦相随往矣。

    Explanation

    554

    In the winter of the year of the Fire Rat, he knew in advance that I was facing urgent troubles, and so he came from Fujian to visit me, walking on foot from Qingjiangpu to Chang'an, where he inquired about the urgency of my case. When I reached Dezhou, he walked back to Chang'an on foot to report to me. He was already eighty-two years old, yet his pace was as swift as a galloping horse, and two strong men could not keep up with him. Upon reaching Zhengzhou, a sandstorm arose, and he took off his shoes and socks, tying them to his arms, and walked barefoot for a hundred miles, climbing up to the east wall of the Cheng family mansion before the sun had set. His appearance was calm, and his breathing was steady. He talked, laughed, and joked, staying awake until late at night before finally going to sleep. Before leaving, he told me, "In July, he will leave his position, and your case will be resolved. However, you will only be released next year. I will pass through Mount Yushan during the last month of the year to prepare for my mother's funeral. Do not worry." I returned home and often went to invite him. In the spring of the year of the Water Rabbit, he was going to visit me with a bundle of bedding, but suddenly told his family, "There will be a group of monks knocking on the door to beg for food tomorrow. Prepare meals for several people to wait for them, and I will follow along as well."

    555

    ”质明，沐浴更衣，若有所须。群僧至，饮毕，入室端坐，奄然而逝。仰田遇人，无贤愚贵贱，一揖之外，箕踞啸傲，终日不知有人。人遗之钱帛即受，否亦不计。每见人深中多傲岸自好者，辄微言刺其隐，人亦不敢怨，惧其尽也。余尝谓仰田：“公非术士，古之异人也。”仰田笑曰：“吾行天下大矣，莫知我为异人；然则公亦异人也。”又尝语曰：“吾重茧狂走，为公急难，侯嬴有言：『七十老翁，何所求哉？』士为知己者死，纵令斫吾头去，颈上只一穴耳。”临终，谓其子曰：“三年后，往告虞山；更数年，寻我于虎丘寺之东。”仰田信人也，其言当不妄，书其语以俟之。
    　　　　[张山来曰：仰田以异人自负，唯牧斋知之，彼即有知己之感。然则异人亦好名乎？]

    Explanation

    555

    Early in the morning, after bathing and changing his clothes, as if preparing for something important, a group of monks arrived. After they drank, he entered the room and sat upright, then passed away suddenly and quietly. When 仰田 met people, regardless of their status or intelligence, he would only bow once, then sit casually with his legs spread apart and behave arrogantly or carefree, as if unaware of anyone around him. He would accept money and silk if given, but would not count it. Whenever he encountered people who were arrogant and self-conceited, he would subtly criticize their hidden flaws, and they would dare not complain, fearing his reprimand. I once told 仰田, "You are not a magician, but an extraordinary person from ancient times." 仰田 smiled and said, "I have traveled far and wide, and no one knows me as an extraordinary person; yet you too are an extraordinary person." He also said, "I have run tirelessly to help you in times of urgent need. 侯嬴 once said, 'What does a seventy-year-old man seek?' A true man will die for those who understand him. Even if I were to have my head cut off, there would only be one wound on my neck." On his deathbed, he told his son, "Three years later, go and inform 虞山; after several more years, look for me east of 虎丘寺." 仰田 is a trustworthy person, and his words are not false. I will write down his words and wait for the time to come.

    [张山来 said: 仰田 considered himself an extraordinary person, and only 牧斋 knew him. 牧斋 felt a sense of recognition. But does an extraordinary person also crave fame?]

    556

    记吴六奇将军事　吴江钮琇玉樵
    　　　　海宁查孝廉培继，字伊璜，才华丰艳，而风情潇洒，常谓“满眼悠悠，不堪酬对，海内奇杰，非从尘埃中物色，未可得也。”家居岁暮，命酒独酌。顷之，愁云四合，雪大如掌，因缓步至门，冀有乘兴佳客，相与赏玩。见一丐者，避雪庑下，强直而立。孝廉熟视良久，心窃异之，因呼之入，坐而问曰：“我闻街市间，有手不曳杖，口若衔枚，敝衣枵腹，而无饥寒之色，人皆称为『铁丐』者，是汝耶？”曰：“是也。”问：“能饮乎？”曰：“能。”因令侍童，以壶中余酒，倾瓯与饮。丐者举瓯立尽。孝廉大喜，复炽炭发醅，与之约曰：“汝以瓯饮，我以巵酬，竭此醅乃止。”丐尽三十余瓯，无醉容。而孝廉颓卧胡床矣。侍童扶掖入内，丐逡巡出，仍宿庑下。达旦雪霁，孝廉酒醒，谓其家人曰：“我昨与铁丐对饮甚欢，观其衣极褴楼，何以御此严寒？亟以我絮袍与之！”丐披袍而去，亦不求见致谢。

    Explanation

    556

    A Record of General Wu Liuqi's Early Days by Niuxiu Yujiao of Wujiang

    Zha Xiaolian Peiji, a scholar-official from Hai Ning, styled Yihuang, was renowned for his abundant talent and graceful demeanor. He often said, "All around me are aimless people, none of whom can be matched or responded to. Extraordinary talents within the realm cannot be found unless one looks for them among the dust and dirt." One New Year's Eve, he ordered wine and drank alone. Soon, dark clouds gathered, and the snow fell heavily, covering the ground like large palms. He then slowly walked to the door, hoping to meet a guest who would share his enthusiasm for enjoying the snow. He saw a beggar seeking shelter under the eaves, standing straight and firm. Zha Xiaolian watched him for a long time, secretly finding him unusual, and called him in. Sitting down, he asked, "I have heard that in the streets, there is a beggar who does not drag a cane, whose mouth seems to hold a mace, wears tattered clothes, and is empty-stomached, yet shows no signs of hunger or cold. People call him 'Iron Beggar.' Are you he?" The beggar replied, "Yes, I am." Zha asked, "Can you drink?" The beggar answered, "I can." Zha then ordered his servant to pour the remaining wine from the pot into a bowl and give it to the beggar to drink. The beggar raised the bowl and finished it in one gulp.
