When I was young, I was indifferent to fame and profit, and I had nothing to gain, I could not be self-satisfied, so I often entrusted my feelings to the mountains and waters. After passing the imperial examination, I was appointed to a position in the capital, but it was not what I liked.

    597

    己亥之夏，以母病告假归省。其秋，遂丁母艰。罔极未报，风木余悲，加以荆妻溘逝，稚子夭残，不能鼓缶，几致丧明。学不贞遇，为境所困，欲复寄踪山水之间，聊以不永怀而不永伤焉。《诗》云：“驾言出游，以写我忧”，此之谓也。
    　　　　庚子秋，束装策蹇，东抵晋阳，系舟石室之山。悬瓮难老之泉，柳溪汾晋之水，圆通白水之观，浮沉其中者累月。东出故关，道井陉，过真定，历清苑。观背水于获鹿，食麦饭于滹沱，望恒岳于曲阳，访金台于易水，仰伊祁于庆都，思轩辕于涿郡。已而北走军都，临居庸，登天寿，东浴阳泉，遂至渔阳。上崆峒，下玉田，涉卢龙，怀孤竹，浮沉其中者又累月。家世塞北，今到辽西，三过风景，约略相同。时值冬暮，层冰峨峨，飞雪千里，丛林如束，阴风怒号，不自知其悲从中来也。

    597

    In the summer of the year Jihai, I took leave to return home to visit my mother due to her illness. That autumn, I suffered the loss of my mother. The boundless filial piety has not been repaid, and the lingering sorrow of wind and trees remains. Added to this, my wife suddenly passed away, and my young child died prematurely. Unable to express my emotions, I almost lost my sight. My learning has not been met with steadfastness, and I am constrained by my circumstances. I wish to once again wander in the mountains and waters, to avoid perpetual remembrance and perpetual sorrow. The Book of Songs says, "To go out and travel, to express my worries," and this is what I mean.

    In the autumn of the year Gengzi, I packed my belongings and rode a slow horse, arriving in JinYang to the east. I moored my boat at the mountain of Shishi, near the spring of Xuanweng that is hard to age, by the waters of Liu Xi and Fen Jin, and the view of the white waters at Yuantong. I spent months floating and sinking in this place. I left the old pass to the east, passing through Jingxing, Zhending, and Qingyuan. I saw the backwater at Helu, ate millet rice by the Hutuo River, looked at Hengshan at Quya, visited Jintai by the Yishui River, looked up to Yiqi at Qingdu, and thought of the Yellow Emperor at Zhuo County. Then I went north to Jundu, near Juyong, climbed Tian寿, bathed in the eastern Yangquan, and reached Yuyang. I climbed Kongtong, descended to Yutian, crossed Lulong, and thought of Gushu. I spent more months floating and sinking in this place. My family's ancestral home is in the northern borderlands, and now I have reached Liaoxi. I have passed through the scenery three times, and it is approximately the same. It was the late winter, with layers of ice towering, snow flying for a thousand miles, forests like bundles, and the cold wind raging. Not knowing that sorrow came from within, I could not help but feel deeply saddened.

    598

    因而决计南行，返都中治装。适吾友李子景莲不得志于礼闱，遂与之偕。辛丑二月二十四日出都，此则吾南游之始也。
    　　　　都中攘攘，缁尘如雾。出春明门，觉日白而天青。过卢沟桥，至琉璃河。卢沟者，桑干也；琉璃河者，圣水也。南有昭烈故居，又有郦道元宅，注《水经》之所也。南至白沟，昔宋、辽分界之处。南至雄县，有湖，一望烟水瀰漫，极浦桅帆，云中隐现，亦河北巨观也。过任丘，有颛顼氏之故城。南至于河间，九河故道，漫灭不辨。滹沱、易、清，衡、漳、潞、卫，高、交、淇、濡，皆经其境以入海。府首曰献县，昔河间献王之都。南出阜城，至景州。景州，古条地，周亚夫封于此。有董家里，仲舒下帷之所也。

    Explanation

    598

    Therefore, I decided to travel south and returned to the capital to prepare for the journey. Just as my friend Li Zǐ Jǐnglián had not succeeded in the imperial examination, I accompanied him. On the twenty-fourth day of the second month in the year of Xīn Chǒu, we left the capital, marking the beginning of my southern journey.

    The capital was bustling, with dust like a fog. Leaving the Spring Bright Gate, I felt the sun was bright and the sky was clear. Crossing the Lugou Bridge, I reached the Lulí River. Lugou is the Sanggan River, and Lulí River is the Sacred Water. To the south, there was the former residence of Emperor Zhāo Liè, and also the residence of Lǐ Dàoyuán, who annotated the Shuǐjīng. Southeast to the White River, which was once the boundary between Song and Liao. Reaching Xióng County, there was a lake that looked like misty water, with distant shores and masts appearing and disappearing in the clouds, also a great sight in Hebei. Passing through Rènqiū, there was the old city of Emperor Zhuānxū. Reaching Héjiān, the old courses of the Nine Rivers are indistinguishable. The rivers Huītuó, Yì, Qīng, Héng, Zhāng, Lù, Wèi, Gāo, Jiāo, Qí, and Rú all flow through the region and into the sea. The first county is Xiàn County, formerly the capital of the Prince of Héjiān. Southeast to Fǔchéng, reaching Jǐngzhōu. Jǐngzhōu, ancient Tiáo land, where the Marquis Zhōu Yàfū was enfeoffed. There is the village of Dǒnglǐ, where Zhòngshū hung his curtain.

    599

    东至德州，入山东境。州城临运河，船桅如麻。南至平原，昔搏徒卖浆，毛公、薛公，以及东方生、管公明，皆奇士，今得毋有存焉者乎？平原君庙内有颜鲁公碑，惜匆匆过，未见也。东南至齐河。自涿州背西山而南，七日走九百里，极目平畴，至齐河始见山。齐河水清，抱县城如碧玉环，石桥跨之。两岸桃柳，新绿嫣红，临水映发，为徘徊桥上者移时。
    　　　　南四十里曰开山，遂入山。途中矫首欲望东岳，而适微雨。云山历乱，时于云外见高峰，以为是矣。曾不数里，又有高者。午后见一峰甚高，怪石突起，烟岚拥护，谓必是矣。已而川势东开，山形北较，远而望之，更有高者。盖余从泰山之北来，午前见背，午后见臂，至泰安州始当其面，而又值云封，故终日望而未之见也。

    Explanation

    599

    East to Dezhou, entering Shandong Province. The city is situated by the Grand Canal, with masts of ships like hemp. South to Pingyuan, where once there were those who wrestled and sold soup, such as Mao Gong, Xue Gong, and also Dongfang Sheng and Guan Gongming, all extraordinary men—do they still exist today? Within the Temple of the Plain Lord, there is a stele dedicated to Yan Zhenqing, but I passed through in haste and did not see it. Southeast to Qihé. From Zhuozhou, facing away from the Western Hills and heading south, covering nine hundred miles in seven days, gazing far over flat fields, until reaching Qihé when mountains first came into view. The water of Qihé is clear, embracing the county town like a jade ring, with a stone bridge spanning it. On both banks, peach and willow trees, new green and bright red, reflect and shine by the water, causing those who linger on the bridge to stay for a while.

    Forty li south lies Kai Shan, then entering the mountains. Along the way, lifting one's head to look towards Mount Tai, just as a light rain began. Clouds and mountains in disorder, sometimes seeing a high peak through the clouds, thinking it must be there. Not even a few miles further, there were even higher ones. In the afternoon, seeing a peak very high, with strange rocks jutting out, surrounded by mist and clouds, thinking it must be there. Soon after, the river opens to the east, the mountain shape extends to the north, looking far, there were even higher ones. I came from the north of Mount Tai, in the morning seeing its back, in the afternoon its arm, only when I reached Taian did I face it directly, but it was shrouded in clouds, so I gazed at it all day without seeing it clearly.

    600

    次早欲上，土人云：“不可，山顶有娘娘庙，领官票而后得入。票银人二钱，曰口税。”夫东岳自有神，所谓“娘娘庙”者，始于何代？功德何等？愚民引夫妇奔走求福，为民上者既不能禁，又因以为利！不得已，亦领票，得票欲上，人又云：“不可，山之高四十里，穷日乃至其颠。兹向午已迟，且天阴。下晴上犹阴，下阴上必雨，雨湿风冷，请以异日。”
    　　　　因而观城中之庙，庙去城之南门二百步许，而以北城为后垣；一城之中，庙居大半焉。阶墀多古柏，云汉武东封时所植。阶墀有碑，其文曰：“磅礴东海之西，中国之东，参穹秀灵，生同天地，形势巍然。古者帝王登之以观沧海，察地利，以安民生。祝曰：『泰山于敬则致，于礼则宜。自唐加神之号封，历代相沿至今。曩者元君失驭，海内鼎沸，生民涂炭。

    Explanation

    600

    Early the next morning, they intended to ascend the mountain, but the locals said, "No, there is a Lady Temple at the summit, and one must obtain an official ticket to enter. The ticket costs two cash per person, called 'mouth tax.'" Mount Tai has its own deity, but as for the "Lady Temple," from which dynasty did it originate? What are its merits? Simple folk lead husbands and wives to run around seeking blessings, and those in power cannot stop it, yet they profit from it! Reluctantly, they also obtained a ticket, and with the ticket, they wished to ascend, but others said, "No, the mountain is forty li high, and one can only reach its peak by the end of the day. It is already late in the morning, and the weather is overcast. If it is sunny below but still overcast above, it will rain if it is overcast below, so it will be wet and cold with rain, so please wait for another day."

    Therefore, they observed the temple in the city. The temple is about two hundred steps from the south gate of the city, with the northern city wall as its rear wall; in the entire city, temples occupy about half of the space. The steps and platforms are lined with ancient cypress trees, said to have been planted during the Eastern Expedition of Emperor Wu of Han. There is a stele on the steps and platforms, inscribed with the following text: "Majestic and vast, it stands west of the East Sea and east of China, with a splendid and spiritual presence, born of heaven and earth, with a grand and imposing form. Ancient emperors ascended it to view the vast sea, examine the land's advantages, and ensure the well-being of the people. The prayer says: 'Mount Tai is worthy of reverence and proper in ritual. Since the Tang dynasty, it has been consecrated with divine titles, and this tradition has continued through the ages. In the past, when the Yuan ruler lost control, the country was in turmoil, and the people suffered greatly.'"

    601

    予起布衣，承上天后土之命，百神阴佑，削平暴乱，正位称职，奉天地，享鬼神，以依时统一人民，法当式古。今寰宇既清，特修祀仪。因神有历代之封号，予起寒微，畏不敢效。盖神与穹昊同始，灵镇一方，其来不知岁月几何，神之所以灵，人莫能测。其职受命于上天后土，为人君者何敢与焉？惧不敢加号，特以『东岳之神』名其名，依时祭神，唯神鉴之。洪武三年六月二十日。”可谓辞严义正矣。庙中望山顶如屏风，中挂白练。问之，人曰：“南天门也。”因与景莲约，起二更，奋力急趋，鸡鸣至其颠，可观沧海日出也。

    Explanation

    601

    I rose from commoner status, receiving the mandate of Heaven and Earth, with the hundred spirits secretly protecting me, I eliminated violent disturbances, assumed the proper position and fulfilled my duties, serving Heaven and Earth and honoring the spirits, unifying the people according to the times, the law following ancient precedents. Now that the world is at peace, I have specially revised the rites of sacrifice. Because the deity has received titles from past dynasties, and I myself rose from humble origins, I am fearful and unwilling to imitate. The deity and the vast sky have the same origin, governing one region, its origin is unknown, and the length of time is indeterminate; the reason for the deity's power is beyond human comprehension. Its duty is ordained by Heaven and Earth, how dare a ruler interfere? Fearful and unwilling to add a title, I have especially named it "the God of Mount Tai," and will worship the deity according to the times, may the deity witness this. On the 20th day of the sixth month of the third year of Hongwu. This can be said to be solemn and just. From the temple, the mountain peak looks like a screen, with a white silk banner hanging in the middle. I asked about it, and someone said, "It is the Southern Heavenly Gate." Therefore, I made an appointment with Jinglian to start at the second watch of the night, rushing forward with great effort, reaching the top at the crow of the rooster, one can observe the sunrise over the vast sea.

    602

    如约起，遥见火光明灭，高与星乱。至则皆贫民男女数千，宿止道旁，然炬以丐钱。教养失而民鲜耻，可慨已！山足曰红门，红门以后，路皆石阶。时闻阶旁潺潺有水声。四更至回马岭，阶级愈峻，如行壁上。鸡鸣至玉皇庙，谓至顶矣，导者笑曰：“甫半耳！”因少憇。黎明，缘涧水，度石桥，见两峰对立，中有瀑布。时宿雨初晴，朝光澄彻，山岚护石，松翠浮空，瀑流飞响，清心韵耳。磴道从西峰上，有碑，题曰“五大夫松”。碑下仰望，见两峰之顶，高插云霄。心中窃拟谓此山颠也。攀登久之，回首遐眺，见松山顶在我足下；昨所见诸峰，在松山下；齐鲁数千里之山，又在诸峰之下。盖已飘飘凌云矣，不意峰回路转，更见高峰。

    Explanation

    602

    As agreed, he rose and distantly saw the firelight flickering, as high as the stars, mingling with them. Upon arriving, he found thousands of impoverished men and women staying overnight by the roadside, holding torches to beg for money. When education and moral cultivation are lost, the people have little sense of shame—this is indeed something to lament! At the foot of the mountain is called Red Gate. After Red Gate, the path is all stone steps. Occasionally, he heard the sound of water trickling beside the steps. At the fourth watch (around 3 AM), they reached Horseback Mountain, where the steps became steeper, as if walking on a wall. At the crow of the rooster (dawn), they reached the Jade Emperor Temple, thinking they had reached the summit, but the guide laughed and said, "We've only reached halfway!" So they took a short rest. At dawn, they followed the stream, crossing a stone bridge, and saw two peaks standing opposite each other, with a waterfall in between. At the time, after a night of rain, the morning light was clear and bright. The mist around the mountain protected the rocks, and the green pines floated in the air. The waterfall's sound flew and echoed, refreshing the mind and pleasing the ears. The path of stone steps ascended from the western peak, with a stele inscribed "Five Gentlemen Pines." Looking up from below the stele, they saw the tops of the two peaks reaching high into the clouds. In their hearts, they secretly thought this was the summit of the mountain. After climbing for a long time, they looked back and gazed far into the distance, seeing the summit of the pine-covered mountain beneath their feet; the peaks they saw yesterday were now below the pine-covered mountain; the mountains of Qi and Lu, spanning thousands of miles, were now below those peaks. It seemed as if they were already soaring among the clouds, but unexpectedly, as the path turned, they saw even higher peaks.

    603

    天门之峰，无点土，亦无寸草，石脉长而廉隅四出，骈植叠累，皱若莲菊。磴道直上十里，乃城中所望若白练者。盖吾从碑下望松山，似高于城中望天门；今于此地望天门，实高于碑下望松山。道旁石上刻四大字，曰“仰之弥高”，其信然矣！磴列铁柱，中贯铁索，授索而登，抱柱而息。比磴道尽，反无所见。盖下望天门，乃其绝顶；既至其上，又有高峰拥蔽焉。迂回攀跻，见所谓“娘娘庙”者在秦观峰下。正殿五间，而三门皆有铜栅，门内金钱，积深二、三尺。堂上有三铜碑，明末大珰所铸。余无可观。东庑檐下，石柱中断，余坐其上而休焉。俯视有字，拂拭辨之，则李斯篆也。其文曰：“盛德丞相臣斯、臣去疾、御史大夫臣德昧死言：臣请具刻诏书金石，刻因明白矣。臣昧死请。制曰：可。”笔法高古秀劲，非汉、晋人所能及。庙后后壁高十余丈，唐摩崖碑在焉。崖西洞中，有泉甘冽。崖后上里许，登秦观峰，乃泰山之颠也。

    Explanation

    603

    The peak of Tianmen Mountain is barren, without a single speck of soil or blade of grass. The stone ridges are long and jagged, extending outward in all directions, stacked and layered like plants, with wrinkles resembling those of lotus and chrysanthemum leaves. The steep path ascends directly for ten li, the one that appears from the city as a white ribbon. From beneath the stone tablet, looking up at Songshan, it seems higher than from the city looking at Tianmen; now, from this spot, Tianmen appears even higher than it did from beneath the tablet. On the side of the path, a stone bears four large characters: "The higher one looks, the higher it seems" (仰之弥高), indeed it is so! The path is lined with iron columns, through which iron chains run. One pulls the chain to climb, resting against the column. When the path ends, one sees nothing. From below, Tianmen appears as its summit; once one reaches the top, another towering peak looms ahead. Winding and climbing, one comes across the so-called "Niangniang Temple" at the base of Qin Guan Peak. The main hall has five bays, with bronze grilles at all three doors. Inside, copper coins are piled to a depth of two or three feet. On the hall, there are three bronze steles cast by the eunuchs of the late Ming dynasty. There is little else to see. Beneath the eaves of the eastern wing, a stone column is broken, and I sit upon it to rest. Looking down, I see writing, which I wipe clean to read: it is the seal script of Li Si. The text reads: "The Chancellor of High Virtue, Minister Si, Minister Qu Ji, and the Grand Historian, Minister De, respectfully submit: We request to engrave the imperial edict on gold and stone, so that it will be clear. We respectfully request. The Emperor commands: 'Proceed.'" The calligraphy is ancient, elegant, and vigorous, surpassing that of the Han and Jin dynasties. Behind the temple, the rear wall rises over ten zhang, with a Tang dynasty inscription carved on the cliff. In the cave to the west of the cliff, there is a sweet and refreshing spring. A few li up the cliff, one ascends to Qin Guan Peak, which is the summit of Mount Tai.

    604

    举头天外，俯视寰中，浩浩茫茫，四无涯际。东见青营，负山阻海；北顾塞垣，横亘千里，河朔诸州，星罗棋布。循太行而西，中州之沃衍，咸阳之阻隘，皆可指数。黄河由华阴走兖、徐，湾环若衣带。嵩山二室，如二卷石；淮阳之间，一望平芜。“登泰山而小天下”，果不诬也！峰颠有殿，庭中石崛起。意古者金泥玉检文皆封于此。门前石表，始皇所建，高二丈余而无字。日观在东，月观在西，高皆与秦观等。古迹名胜，不可遍睹。薄暮遂下，至松山而少憩。回思三观，如在天上。又下见朝阳洞，石穴幽邃。又下见水帘洞，流水蔽岩。下至山麓，见一巨人，与之并立，翘足伸手，而不能摹其顶。古者长狄在齐、鲁之间，岂其遗种与？

    Explanation

    604

    Lifting my head to the heavens, gazing down upon the world below, the vast and boundless expanse stretches out in all directions without limit. To the east, I see the green camp, backed by mountains and blocked by the sea; to the north, I look upon the Great Wall, stretching for thousands of miles, with the states of Hebei scattered like stars. Following the Taihang Mountains westward, the fertile lands of the Central Plains and the strategic pass of Xianyang can be counted on one's fingers. The Yellow River flows through Huayin, Yanzhou, and Xu Prefectures, winding like a belt. The two peaks of Mount Song resemble two rolled stones; between Huaiyang, the flat grasslands stretch out as far as the eye can see. "To look down on the world from Mount Tai," indeed it is not false! At the peak of the mountain stands a temple, with a stone rising in the courtyard. It seems that ancient texts sealed with gold and jade were stored here. In front of the temple, a stone tablet was built by Qin Shi Huang, standing over two zhang high but without any inscriptions. The Sun Observatory is to the east, the Moon Observatory to the west, both as high as the Qin Observatory. Ancient sites and scenic spots cannot all be seen. As evening falls, I descend to Song Mountain and rest briefly. Looking back at the three observatories, they seem to be in heaven. Below, I see the Dongyang Cave, with a deep and mysterious stone cave. Further down, I see the Water Curtain Cave, with water flowing over the rocks. Reaching the foot of the mountain, I see a giant, standing next to whom, one cannot reach the top with outstretched feet and hands. Could this giant be a descendant of the ancient Longdi people who lived between Qi and Lu?

    605

    次早，由泰安趋曲阜。曩在山上，视泰安城如掌大；汶水一线，环于城外；徂徕若堵，蹲于汶上。出泰安城，不见水与山也。行五十里，见大河广阔，乃汶水也。又五十里，见崇山巍峨，乃徂徕也。相去百里，而俯视不过数武，其高可想矣。徂徠之西曰梁父，对峙若门。从门南出，平畴沃衍，泗水西流。孔林在泗水南，洙水在孔林南，曲阜在洙水南，沂水在曲阜南。孔林方十余里，其树蔽天，其草蔽地。至圣墓，有红墙环立，墙中草树愈密，修干丛薄，侧不容人，而景色开明，初无幽阴之气。至圣墓，产蓍草、碑曰“大成至圣文宣王墓”。西偏小屋三间，颜曰“子贡庐墓处”。东南有泗水侯墓，正南有沂国公墓。墙东南有枯木，石栏护之，子贡手植楷也。旁有楷亭。其北有驻跸亭，人君谒墓更衣之所。门外有洙水桥，桥南高阜一带，辟其东南为门。门距曲阜可二里，道旁植柏，行列甚整，蔽日参天，皆数千年物也。

    Explanation

    605

    The next morning, we proceeded from Tai'an to Qufu. Previously, while on the mountain, we viewed Tai'an City as small as a palm; the Wei River flowed in a line around the city; Cuilai Mountain stood like a wall, crouching over the Wei River. Upon leaving Tai'an City, we no longer saw water or mountains. After traveling fifty li, we saw a vast river, which was the Wei River. Another fifty li later, we encountered a majestic mountain, which was Cuilai Mountain. The distance between the two was one hundred li, yet from above, it seemed no more than a few steps, allowing one to imagine its great height. To the west of Cuilai Mountain lies Liangfu Mountain, standing opposite each other like a gate. Exiting through the southern gate, we saw flat and fertile fields with the Si River flowing westward. The Confucius Cemetery lies to the south of the Si River, the Zhu River to the south of the Confucius Cemetery, Qufu to the south of the Zhu River, and the Yi River to the south of Qufu. The Confucius Cemetery spans over ten li, with trees that overshadow the sky and grass that covers the ground. The tomb of the Supreme Sage, surrounded by a red wall, has even denser vegetation inside, with tall trees growing thickly, leaving no room for people on the sides, yet the scenery is bright and open, free from any gloomy atmosphere. The tomb of the Supreme Sage produces yarrow grass, and the stele reads "Tomb of the Great Accomplished Supreme Sage and Writings of the Exalted King." To the west, there are three small houses, inscribed with "Zigan's Hut for Mourning." To the southeast, there is the tomb of the Duke of Si, and directly to the south, the tomb of the Duke of Yi. To the southeast of the wall, there is a dead tree surrounded by a stone railing, a camphor tree planted by Zigan. Next to it is a pavilion. To the north, there is a pavilion called Zhubi Pavilion, where emperors change clothes when visiting the tomb. Outside the gate, there is a bridge over the Zhu River, with a high and flat area to the south, a gate opened in the southeast. The gate is about two li away from Qufu, with cypress trees planted along the road, neatly arranged, towering high and overshadowing the sun, all thousands of years old.

    606

    入曲阜之北门，路东有复圣庙，庙前有陋巷。巷南折而西，则孔庙之东华门也。庙制如内廷宮殿，而柱以石为之，蛟龙盘旋，乃内廷所无。至圣与诸贤皆塑像。石刻至圣像有三。车服礼器，藏于衍圣公家；圣公入觐，不可得观。殿南有亭，颜曰“杏坛”，古杏数株，时值三月，杏花正开。坛南有先师手植桧，高三丈而无枝，文皆左纽。子贡之楷，虽不腐而色枯，此则生气勃发焉。大门内外丰碑无数。南有高楼曰奎文阁。阁南门下，汉、魏之碑十余，皆额尖而有圆孔。门外有水，上作五桥。桥南有门，门外有栅。自殿庭至栅内，苍松古柏，虬龙盘屈，不可名状。泰安汉柏，又不足道矣。

    Explanation

    606

    Entering through the northern gate of Qufu, to the east of the road is the Temple of the Second Sage, in front of which lies a humble lane. The lane turns south and then west, leading to the East Hua Gate of the Confucius Temple. The temple's architecture resembles that of the inner court palaces, with stone columns adorned with swirling dragon carvings, a feature not found in the inner court. The images of the Supreme Sage and the other sages are all in statues. There are three stone carvings of the Supreme Sage. The chariots, clothing, and ritual objects are stored in the home of the Duke Yansheng; when the Duke visits the court, these items cannot be seen. To the south of the hall is a pavilion named "Scholar's Platform," with several ancient apricot trees in full bloom in March. To the south of the platform is a cypress tree planted by the Master himself, standing three zhang high without branches, with its grain twisted to the left. Zigan's楷tree, though not decayed, has a withered color, while this cypress tree is full of vitality. Inside and outside the main gate, there are countless large stone tablets. To the south is a tall building called the Writings Pavilion. Below the south gate of the pavilion, there are more than ten steles from the Han and Wei dynasties, all with pointed tops and round holes. Outside the gate is a body of water with five bridges crossing it. To the south of the bridge is a gate, with a fence outside the gate. From the hall to the fence, there are ancient cypress and pine trees, their twisted branches like dragons, too numerous to describe. The ancient pines of Tai'an, moreover, are not even worth mentioning.

    607

    吾于是奋然兴也。夫孔子者，天所独生以教后世者也。考其生平，三岁丧父，七岁丧母，中年出妻，晚年丧子。夫哀死而伤离，宁独异于人哉？人观“志学”一章，七十年内，日进月益，不以遇之穷而少辍其功，盖其自待厚，而所见有大焉者矣！余乃戚戚欲以身殉，何其陋也！《诗》有之曰：“高山仰止，景行行止。”虽不能至，然心向往之。

    Explanation

    607

    I thus rise with determination. Confucius was one uniquely born by Heaven to teach future generations. Examining his entire life, he lost his father at age three, his mother at age seven, divorced his wife in middle age, and lost his son in his later years. How could he be any different from others in mourning death and feeling sorrow over separation? People who read the chapter "Aspiration to Learning" in the Analects see that over seventy years, he made daily progress and monthly improvements, not diminishing his efforts because of difficult circumstances. It is because he held himself to high standards and had great aspirations! I am so distressed that I want to sacrifice myself, how narrow-minded is this! The Book of Poetry says, "Look up to the high mountains; follow the bright paths." Even if one cannot reach them, one's heart still yearns for them.

    608

    曲阜东南有九龙山，其南曰马鞍山。两山之间，松楸茂密者，孟林也。林南为邹县。县南有孟庙，庙左有宣献夫人祠。夫人者，孟母也。滕县在邹南，地平旷，可以行井田。滕南有峄山，始皇刻石其上。峄东有陶河，过陶河至邳州。下邳乃子房击秦后潜匿之所。又项籍者，下相人也。下相在邳州。昔曹操决水灌吕布于下邳。今其地在山，不可灌。予尝徘徊其地，求下邳、下相之故城，及圯桥进履之所，而土人皆无知者。邳南落马湖，黄河所溢也。湖南曰宿迁，宋人迁宿于此。又南曰桃源，乃黄河之北岸也。

    Explanation

    608

    To the southeast of Qufu lies the Nine Dragons Mountain, and to the south of it is Maanshan. Between the two mountains, where cypress and pine trees grow densely, is the Meng Forest. To the south of the forest is Zou County. To the south of Zou County is the Meng Temple, and to the left of the temple is the Xuanxian Lady's Shrine. The lady is Meng Mu, the mother of Mencius. To the south of Zou is Teng County, with flat and open land suitable for implementing the well-field system. To the south of Teng is Yishan, where Emperor Qin Shi Huang inscribed stones. To the east of Yishan is the Tao River, and crossing the Tao River leads to Pizhou. Xiapu is the place where Zhang Liang, after defeating the Qin, went into hiding. Xiang Yu, a native of Xiang, is also from Pizhou. Cao Cao once flooded the city of Xiapu to defeat Lü Bu. Now the area is mountainous and cannot be flooded. I have wandered this area, searching for the ancient cities of Xiapu and Xiang, as well as the bridge where Zhang Liang met the old man, but found no locals who know about them. To the south of Pizhou is Luomahu Lake, which is part of the Yellow River's overflow. To the south of the lake is Suqian, where the Song people migrated. Further south is Taoyuan, which is on the northern bank of the Yellow River.

    609

    河自出天门，走平陆，无高下阻激之所；而驰波跳沫，汹涌澎湃，其猛鸷迅疾，天性然也。南至清江浦，黄河南曲，运河北曲，两河之间，不能一里，而运低于黄数十丈。河性冲突，设有不虞，淮阳其为鱼矣！淮安城西，有“韩侯钓台”。当淮阴未遇时，忍饥钓鱼城下，谁过而问之？及其云蒸龙变，向之落魄，皆为美谈。英雄成败有时，若此类湮没而不称者，可胜道哉！

    Explanation

    609

    The river emerges from the Heavenly Gate and flows over the flat land, encountering no high or low obstacles to impede its course. Yet, it surges with waves and froth, turbulent and powerful, swift and fierce, such is its natural disposition. It flows south to the Qingjiangpu, where the Yellow River makes a southern bend, and the Grand Canal makes a northern bend. The distance between the two rivers is less than a mile, yet the canal is dozens of feet lower than the Yellow River. The nature of the river is to collide and surge; if there is any unforeseen event, the people of Huaiyang will become fish! West of Huai'an city, there stands the "Han Hou's Fishing Terrace." When Han Xin, from Huaiyin, was struggling and fishing by the city wall, enduring hunger, who would pass by and ask about him? But when he underwent a dramatic transformation, like a cloud rising and a dragon changing, his earlier hardships became a subject of admiration. The success and failure of heroes are determined by timing; those who are forgotten and unappreciated, how many can be counted?

    610

    淮安南曰宝应，宝应南曰高邮，地多湖，四望皆水。高邮以南，始见田畴。江北暮春，似河北之盛夏。草长成茵，麦秀成浪，花剩余红，树凝浓绿，风景固殊焉。南至于扬州，扬州自古繁华地，当南北水陆之冲，舟车辐辏，士女游冶，兼以盐商聚处，僭拟无度，流俗相效，竞以奢靡，此其弊也。城内无可观，隋宫、迷楼、二十四桥之胜迹，今皆不存。琼花观内，止余故址。城北有天宁寺，谢东山之别业也。其西偏曰杏园。余尝寓杏园之僧舍，竹树蓊郁，池台清幽，想见王谢风流。杏园东曰虹桥，园亭罗列水次，游人棹酒船于其中。虹桥之北，则蜀岗也，欧阳文忠公建平山堂于其上。堂右有大明寺井，昔张又新作《煎茶水记》，谓扬子江中泠泉第一，惠山石泉第二，虎丘石井第三，丹阳寺井第四，扬州大明寺井第五，即此是也。

    Explanation

    610

    South of Huai'an is Baoying, and south of Baoying is Gaoyou, where the land is filled with lakes, and all around one sees water. South of Gaoyou, one begins to see fields. In the late spring of Jiangbei, it resembles the hot summer of Hebei. The grass grows thickly like a mat, and the wheat rippling like waves; the flowers still retain their red color, and the trees with dense, deep green foliage. The scenery is indeed distinct. Continuing south to Yangzhou, which has been a prosperous place since ancient times, located at the intersection of north-south water and land routes, it is bustling with boats and carriages, with men and women enjoying leisurely outings. Additionally, the salt merchants gather, behaving without restraint, and influencing the common people to indulge in extravagance, which is a drawback. There is nothing remarkable to see inside the city; the famous sites of the Sui palace, the Miliu, and the Twenty-Four Bridges no longer exist. Inside the Qionghua Temple, only the ruins remain. To the north of the city is the Tianning Temple, which was the private residence of Xie An. Its western part is called Xingyuan. I once stayed in a monk's lodging in Xingyuan, where the bamboo and trees were lush, and the ponds and terraces were serene, allowing one to imagine the elegance of the Wang and Xie families. To the east of Xingyuan is the Hongqiao Bridge, with gardens and pavilions arranged along the water, where tourists paddle their wine boats among them. North of the Hongqiao Bridge is Shugang, where Ouyang Wenzhong Gong built the Pingshan Hall on it. To the right of the hall is the well of the Daming Temple, which was mentioned by Zhang Youxin in his Record of Tea Water as the fifth-best spring: the Zhongling Spring of the Yangtze River is first, the stone spring of Huishan is second, the stone well of Tiger Hill is third, the temple well of Danyang is fourth, and the temple well of Yangzhou's Daming Temple is fifth.

    611

    东至于泰州，昔韩魏公知泰州，梦以手捧日者再，今其州堂犹颜曰“捧日”。南至于瓜州，遂渡江。扬子江阔而清，含虚混碧，上下澄鲜，金、焦在中，如踞镜面。金山四面皆楼阁，环绕层累，靓妆刻节。远望焦山，林木青苍。土人云：“焦山山里寺，金山寺里山”。惜余未上，于焦止见山，于金止见寺而已。

    Explanation

    611

    As for reaching up to Taizhou, in the past, Han Qi, the governor of Taizhou, dreamed of holding the sun with his hands twice. Today, the state hall of the city is still named "Holding the Sun." Reaching south to Guazhou, then crossing the river. The Yangtze River is broad and clear, containing the void and mixing with the blue, both above and below are clear and fresh, with Mountains Jin and Jiao in the middle, like sitting on a mirror surface. Mount Jin is surrounded by pavilions and towers, elaborately decorated and carved. Looking far at Mount Jiao, the trees are green and dark. The locals say, "Mount Jiao has a temple inside, Mount Jin has a temple inside." I regret not having climbed them, at Mount Jiao I only saw the mountain, at Mount Jin I only saw the temple.

    612

    过江，由小河入山，至镇江府。镇江古京口，四面阻山，形格势禁，以临天堑，实南北必争之地。孙仲谋始都此，筑城名曰“铁瓮”，府城其遗也。南至于丹阳，闻有练湖而未见。东南至常州，古延陵地，吴季子之所居。俗在三吴为淳朴。至丹阳西，见山绵亘百佘里。至无锡曰九龙山，其南峰曰惠山，惠山之东曰锡山，峰峦皆秀丽。登惠山，领石泉，清冽而甘且厚。下视无锡，群山拱峙，众水环流，名酒嘉鱼菱藕之薮，乐土也。昔泰伯择居于此。惠山之南曰夫椒，夫差败越之所也。夫椒之南曰阳山，越败夫差之地也。阳山以南，群峰列峙，巍然而葱郁者，灵岩、穹隆、支硎、玄墓、上方诸山也。灵岩之东，树林阴翳，有秀出于树中者，虎丘也。虎丘南六七里，苏州城也。姑苏控三江、跨五湖而通海。阊门内外，居货山积，行人水流，列肆招牌，灿若云锦。语其繁华，都门不逮。

    Explanation

    612

    Crossing the river, I entered the mountains via a small river and arrived at Zhenjiang Prefecture. Zhenjiang, anciently known as Jingkou, is surrounded by mountains on all sides, geographically constrained and strategically limited, facing the natural barrier of the Yangtze River, truly a place that must be contested by the north and south. Sun Quan first established his capital here, building a city named "Iron Pot," and the current city of the prefecture is its legacy. To the south, I reached Danyang, where I heard of Lake Lian but had not seen it. To the east-south, I came to Changzhou, anciently known as Yancheng, the place where Duke Ji Zi of Wu lived. The customs in the three Wu regions are simple and honest. To the west of Danyang, I saw mountains stretching for over a hundred miles. To Wuxi, it is called Jiulong Mountain, with its southern peak named Huishan, and east of Huishan is Xishan. All peaks are beautiful. Climbing Huishan, I led to the stone spring, clear and cool, sweet and rich. Looking down at Wuxi, surrounded by mountains, with many waters flowing around, it is a place famous for fine wine, delicious fish, lotus roots, and water chestnuts, a land of happiness. Formerly, Tai Bo chose to live here. South of Huishan is Fuchao, the place where Fu Chai defeated Yue. South of Fuchao is Yangshan, the place where Yue defeated Fu Chai. South of Yangshan, a series of peaks stand tall and lush, including Lingyan, Qionglong, Zhixing, Xuanmu, and Shangfang Mountains. East of Lingyan, a forest is shaded, with a beautiful peak standing out among the trees, called Tiger Hill. Six to seven miles south of Tiger Hill is Suzhou City. Gusu controls the three rivers, spans the five lakes, and connects to the sea. Inside and outside the Gate of Chuan, goods pile up like mountains, people flow like water, and shop signs shine like brocade. Speaking of its prosperity, even the capital cannot match it.

    613

    然俗浮靡、人夸诈，百工士庶，殚智竭力，以为奇技淫巧，所谓作无益以害有益者与？虎丘小而奇，外望一土阜，而中有洞壑。路旁岩下，有泉曰憨泉。泉侧有石，中裂若劈，“试剑石”也。曲折而上，一大磐石，平铺数百步，“千人坐”也。四围奇峰，峭拔若削，北辟一壑，中有清池，“剑池”也。剑池之西，又辟一壑，窈窕幽奇，而亦有池，虎丘石井也。剑池之东有亭，可中亭也。亭下池上，大刻“虎丘剑池”，颜鲁公书也。

    Explanation

    613

    Yet the customs are frivolous and luxurious, and people boast and deceive. Craftsmen and commoners exhaust their wisdom and strength in creating extraordinary skills and frivolous craftsmanship, which is what is meant by "making something harmful to benefit from it." Tiger Hill is small but unique; from the outside, it looks like a small hill, but inside, there are caves and ravines. Beside the path under a rock, there is a spring called Huan Spring. Near the spring, there is a stone that is split in the middle, resembling a cleavage or split, known as the Test Sword Stone. Winding up, there is a large flat rock that extends for several steps, called the Thousand People's Seat. Surrounded by steep and sharp peaks, to the north, there is a ravine containing a clear pool, known as the Sword Pool. To the west of the Sword Pool, another ravine is deep, secluded, and mysterious, also containing a pool, the Tiger Hill Stone Well. To the east of the Sword Pool, there is a pavilion, the Suitable Pavilion. Below the pavilion and above the pool, there is a large inscription reading "Tiger Hill Sword Pool," written by Yan Zhenqing.

    614

    又刻“生公讲堂”，李阳冰篆也。登虎丘而四望，竹树拥村，菱荷覆水，浓阴沉绿，天地皆青。然赋税重，民不堪命焉。灵岩秀而高，上有西施洞，山颠有寺，馆娃宫之故址也。门据横石，内辟清池。殿西有岩，流泉四出，回廊曲槛，周于岩上，又有二池焉。其清爽幽奇，令人乐而忘反。绝顶石上，刻曰“琴台”。登琴台，临太湖，太湖周八百里，包众山于其中，水清色白，长风一吹，波与山同。七十二峰，乍隐乍现于银涛雪浪中，滴翠浮青，宇内奇观也。

    Explanation

    614

    Again, the inscription "Sheng Gong's Lecture Hall" was carved by Li Yangbing in seal script. Climbing Tiger Hill and looking around in all directions, bamboo and trees surround the village, lotus and water chestnut cover the water. The dense shade is deep green, and the sky and earth are all green. However, the heavy taxes make the people unable to bear the burden. Lingyan Mountain is beautiful and high. Above it is the Xishi Cave. On the mountain top, there is a temple, the ruins of the Palace of Gua Wa. The gate is supported by a horizontal stone, and inside, there is a clear pond. West of the hall, there is a rock, and springs flow out in all directions. There is an encircling corridor with curved railings surrounding the rock. There are also two ponds. The place is refreshing and mysterious, making people happy and forget to return. On the summit, the stone is inscribed as "Qintai." Climbing Qintai, one overlooks Taihu Lake. Taihu Lake surrounds eight hundred miles, encompassing many mountains within it. The water is clear and white. When a strong wind blows, the waves and the mountains are the same. The seventy-two peaks appear and disappear intermittently in the silver waves and snowy surges. Dripping green and floating blue, it is a rare sight in the world.

    615

    南出吴江，由兰溪至浙东。嘉、杭之间，其俗善蚕，地皆种桑，家有塘以养鱼，村有港以通舟，麦禾蔚然，茂于桑下；静女提笼，儿童晒网。风致清幽，与三吴之繁华又别矣。出兰溪至塘棲，夹河左右，远望皆山，西南一带，尤高大而青苍者，则西湖上之诸峰也。南至武林门，棹舟竟入城内。出候潮门，至江口，一望浩渺，大不减扬子，而色与黄河同，则钱塘江也。钱塘、西湖之胜，自幼耳熟，既见江，急欲至湖上。居人曰：“游西湖者，陆轿而水船。”余曰：“不然，江山之观，一入轿船，则不能见其大。

    Explanation

    615

    Departing south from Wujiang, traveling from Lanxi to Zhejiang East. Between Jiaxing and Hangzhou, the local custom is good at silkworm rearing, the land is all planted with mulberry trees, every household has ponds to raise fish, every village has canals to connect boats, wheat and rice are lush, thriving under the mulberry trees; quiet girls carry baskets, children dry nets. The scenery is serene and secluded, different from the bustling prosperity of the Three Wu regions. Departing from Lanxi to Tangqi, on both sides of the river, from afar, they see mountains, in the southwest, especially the tall and green mountains, these are the peaks of West Lake. Sailing south to Wulin Gate, rowing boats into the city. Departing from Houchao Gate to the river mouth, a vast view, no less than Yangtze, but the color is the same as the Yellow River, this is the Qiantang River. The beauty of Qiantang and West Lake, familiar since childhood, after seeing the river, eager to reach the lake. The local people said, "To tour West Lake, one should take a sedan chair on land and a boat on water." I said, "Not so, the view of the mountains and rivers, once entering a sedan chair or boat, cannot be seen in its entirety."

    616

    且异境多在人踪罕至之处，轿与船不能到也。”因步行，登万松山而望西湖，一片空明，干峰紫翠，冠山为寺，架木作亭，楼台烟雨，绮丽清幽。向观画图，恐西湖不如画，今乃知画不足以尽西湖也。过松岭，渡长桥，至南屏。南屏之山，怪石攒列，下有古寺，所谓“南屏晚钟”也。北曰雷峰，有塔高而色紫，所谓“雷峰夕照”也。西曰苏堤，从南抵北，作六桥以通舟，植梅柳于其上，所谓“苏堤春晓”也。堤西有园亭，引湖为沼以蓄鱼，所谓“花港观鱼”也。堤东有洲，旁有三塔，影入洲中，所谓“三潭印月”也。潭北有亭，翼然水面者，湖心亭也。亭北突起而韶秀者，孤山也。

    Explanation

    616

    Moreover, the exotic places are often in remote areas where carriages and boats cannot reach. Therefore, we had to walk, climbing Mount Wansong to view West Lake. The lake was a vast expanse of clear, open water, with peaks of purple and green hues. The mountain was crowned with a temple, and wooden structures were built to form pavilions. Towering buildings were shrouded in misty rain, creating a gorgeous and serene landscape. Previously, when viewing paintings, I feared that West Lake might not be as beautiful as the paintings, but now I realize that paintings cannot fully capture the beauty of West Lake. Crossing the pine ridge and crossing the long bridge, we arrived at Nanping. The mountains of Nanping are lined with strange rocks, and below there is an ancient temple, known as "Nanping Evening Bell." To the north is Leifeng, which has a tall purple tower, known as "Leifeng Evening Glow." To the west is the Su Causeway, which runs from south to north, with six bridges built to connect boats, and willows and plum trees planted along it, known as "Su Causeway Spring Dawn." West of the causeway, there are garden pavilions, with a pond created by diverting part of the lake to house fish, known as "Flower Harbor Fish Watching." East of the causeway, there is a small island, with three towers nearby, their shadows reflected in the island, known as "Three Ponds Moon Reflection." North of the pond, there is a pavilion, gracefully overhanging the water, known as the Lake Heart Pavilion. North of the pavilion, there rises a solitary and beautiful mountain, known as Solitary Hill.

    617

    山有紫垣缭绕者，行宫也。其东直抵杭城者，白堤也。苏堤纵而白堤横，孤山介两堤之间焉。其西有岳武穆庙，庙外铁铸秦桧夫妇，而其首为人击碎。尝读史至国家兴亡之际，不能无疑于天也：当武穆提兵北伐，山东、河朔，豪杰响应，韩常内附，兀术外奔，使其予秦桧以暴疾，假武穆以遐年，复神州而返二圣，至易易耳！而顾不然，待其人之云亡，邦国殄瘁，易代而后，乃复祀武穆而击桧，岂天心悔过，而假手于人以盖前愆耶？抑天终不悔，而人奋其力与天争耶？人之言曰：“善恶之报，不于其身，必于其子孙。

    Explanation

    617

    A mountain surrounded by purple walls is the imperial residence. To the east, extending directly to Hangzhou City, is the Bai Causeway. The Su Causeway runs north-south, while the Bai Causeway runs east-west, with Solitary Mountain situated between the two. To the west of this lies the Temple of Yue Fei, where iron statues of Qin Hui and his wife stand, and their heads were struck and broken by people. Once reading history, reaching the time of a nation's rise and fall, one cannot help but doubt heaven: when Yue Fei led troops north to attack, heroes in Shandong and the northern plains responded, General Han Chang submitted, and Wujie fled. If Qin Hui had been given a sudden illness, and Yue Fei had been granted a long life, restoring the Central Plains and returning the two emperors would have been very easy! But it was not so; waiting for his death, the state was ruined, and after a change of dynasties, Yue Fei was worshipped again and Qin Hui was struck. Could it be that heaven regrets its mistake, using people to cover up past wrongs? Or does heaven never regret, and people strive with all their might to compete with heaven? People say, "Rewards and punishments for good and evil do not occur in their own lives, but in their descendants."

    618

    ”今闻秦氏盛而岳氏式微，此又何说焉？使天下好善而惡恶，人之好恶之心，何由而生也？天之好恶，既与人同，胡为误于其身，复误于其子孙，而终不悔耶？呜呼！此其故圣人知之矣！昔者圣人之作《易》也，君子长而小人消曰“泰”，小人长而君子消曰“否”。运之有否泰，数也，天之所不能违也。非小人得志而害君子，则运不成。故万世之人心，好君子而恶小人者，天之理之常；一时之气运，福小人而祸君子者，天之数之变。万物之于天，犹子之于父，臣之于君也。龙逄、比干，其君不以为忠；申生、伯奇，其父不以为孝。孝子不敢非其亲，忠臣不敢怼其君，而于天又何怨焉？

    Explanation

    618

    "I have heard that the Qin family is thriving while the Yue family is declining, but what can be said about this? If the world inclines towards good and away from evil, how does the heart's inclination towards good and evil arise? The heavens' inclination towards good and evil is the same as that of humans. Why do they err in their own lives and then pass on this error to their descendants, yet never repent? Alas! This reason is known by the sages! In the past, when the sages composed the Yijing (Book of Changes), they said that when the virtuous are strong and the wicked are weak, it is called 'Tài' (prosperity); when the wicked are strong and the virtuous are weak, it is called 'Fǒu' (adversity). The alternation between adversity and prosperity is a matter of fate, something that heaven cannot violate. If the wicked do not achieve power and harm the virtuous, then the cycle of fate will not be complete. Therefore, throughout all time, people's hearts incline towards the virtuous and away from the wicked, which is a constant law of heaven; in a given era, the prevailing trends may bring fortune to the wicked and misfortune to the virtuous, which is a change in the course of fate. All things in relation to heaven are like children in relation to their fathers and subjects in relation to their rulers. Lóng Páng and Bǐ Gān, their rulers did not consider them loyal; Shēn Shēng and Bó Qí, their fathers did not consider them filial. Filial sons dare not criticize their parents, and loyal ministers dare not blame their rulers. And what complaint can they have against heaven?"

    619

    庙西有坟，内有二冢，武穆王与其子云也。坟南亭台，临湖结构，朱栏碧槛，与绿水红莲相掩映，所谓“曲院风荷”也。初在南屏望湖，路人指曰：“高而顶有塔者，南高峰也；其遥与高同者，北高峰也。”兹由岳坟而西，道出北高蜂下，路旁皆山，苍松翠柏，蔽岫连云。林中徐步，忽见清溪，白石磷磷，落花沉涧，鸟语如簧。心中恍惚，冀有所遇。沿山深入，见一村落，酒帘树间，茶棚竹下，路西有坊，题曰“飞来峰”。过坊而西，乃见奇峰特峙，流水环周，洞在山腹，桥当洞口。渡桥入洞，岩壑空幻，石骨玲珑，乳泉滴沥，积而成池。洞顶怪石，如古树倒垂，云霞横出。孔穴贯串，八达四通，或巨或细，或暗或明。出洞西行，溪边岩下，石皆奇秀，卓立林间者，往往与松竹争长。山侧有放生池，池上有冷泉亭，高峰插天，修篁蔽日，流泉清池，环亭左右，盛夏正午，冷若深秋。亭北有寺，扁曰“云林”，未暇入也。

    Explanation

    619

    West of the temple lies a tomb, within which are two tombs, those of Wumu Wang and his son, Yún. To the south of the tomb, there are pavilions and buildings constructed near the lake, with red railings and green balustrades, reflecting the green water and red lotus flowers, a scene known as "Quyuan Fenghe." Initially, from Nanping Mountain, one can see the lake, and a passerby points out, "The tall peak with a tower at the top is the Southern High Peak; the distant peak of similar height is the Northern High Peak." Now, traveling west from Yue Mausoleum, the path passes under the Northern High Peak, with mountains along the way, covered in dark green pines and cypresses, reaching up to the clouds. Walking slowly in the forest, one suddenly sees a clear stream, with white stones visible and fallen flowers floating in the stream, the birds' chirping sounding like a musical instrument. Feeling dazed, one hopes to encounter something. Following the mountain path deeper, one sees a village, with wine banners hanging among trees and tea pavilions under bamboo. On the west side of the road, there is a gate, inscribed with the name "Feilai Peak." After passing through the gate and heading west, one sees a unique peak standing out, with a stream surrounding it, a cave in the mountain body, and a bridge at the cave entrance. Crossing the bridge into the cave, the interior is spacious and mysterious, with delicate rock formations, stalactites dripping water, forming a pool. Strange rocks on the cave ceiling resemble ancient trees hanging down and clouds and mist emerging. The cave's passages are interconnected, leading in all directions, varying in size and lighting conditions. Leaving the cave and heading west, the stones along the stream and under the rocks are uniquely beautiful, often competing with pines and bamboos in height. On the side of the mountain, there is a pond for releasing animals, above which is Lengquan Pavilion, with tall peaks reaching into the sky and tall bamboo shading the sun, a clear stream and pond surrounding the pavilion. Even during midsummer, the pavilion feels like early autumn. To the north of the pavilion, there is a temple, with the name "Yunlin Temple" written on it, but one does not have time to enter.

    620

    过寺而西，小园别墅，布置佳胜，纵目流览，忘其路之远近。幽林密箐，曲折其中，有时仰望，不见天日。心中惊疑，不知误入何境，欲一借问，而深山无人。林间企望，见一僧度岭而去。因亦至其岭上，天风南来，微闻鼓乐之声，寻声觅路，忽见一片瓦砾，屋坏墙存，土焦石黑。路闻人语云：“天竺新遭回禄。”见此乃悟身在天竺峰也。当是时，日将暮，予见天竺寺既已烧残，又四围幽壑深林，不类人境，惧其为虎豹之窟穴，山魑木魅所往来，因返。复至飞来峰下，寻前所见村落而歇焉。

    Explanation

    620

    After passing the temple and heading west, I came across a small garden villa, beautifully arranged and situated in an advantageous location. I strolled leisurely, gazing around, and forgot the distance of the path. The dense, secluded forest wound through the area, and sometimes when I looked up, I couldn't see the sky or sun. Feeling surprised and doubtful in my heart, I wondered what kind of place I had mistakenly entered. I wanted to ask someone once, but deep in the mountains, there was no one. Looking forward in the forest, I saw a monk crossing the ridge and going away. I also reached the top of that ridge, where the wind from the south blew, and I barely heard the sound of drums and music. Following the sound, I found a path and suddenly saw a pile of broken tiles and bricks, with walls still standing but the soil scorched and the stones blackened. On the road, I heard someone say, "The Tianzhu Temple has recently suffered a fire." Seeing this, I realized I was on Tianzhu Peak. At that time, the day was nearing its end, and I saw that the Tianzhu Temple had been partially burned down. Surrounded by deep ravines and dense forests, the place did not resemble a human dwelling. I feared it to be a lair of tigers and leopards, a place where mountain demons and tree spirits come and go, so I returned. I then found the village seen before and rested there, beneath the Feilai Peak.

    621

    次早，复至飞来峰，不入洞而登其颠，远望旭日出海，江潮涌金，晓雾成霞，山岚抹黛，景色变幻，林密怪奇，自疑此身或恐飞去。昔韩世忠忤秦桧，解官携酒，日游西湖，建翠微亭于飞来峰上。唯斯人也，而后称斯山也！下飞来峰，复至冷泉亭，问所谓灵隐，乃知扁“云林”者即是也。时值四月八日，寺于此日斋僧，远近僧来者甚众。本寺住持，披法衣上堂讲经。其大和尚曰帝辉，年可九十余矣，巍然据高座。首座二人，侍者八人，其下行列而拜跪者，可三百众。比丘与比丘尼咸在，其威仪俯仰皆娴谨，独惜所讲无所发明，即成书而诵之，其下不必尽闻，闻者不必尽解，徒听侍者拜云则拜，起云则起而已。

    Explanation

    621

    The next morning, he returned to Feilai Peak, not entering the cave but climbing to its summit. From afar, he gazed upon the rising sun over the sea, the golden tide of the river, the morning mist turning into a rosy glow, and the mountain mist painting the peaks in shades of blue. The scenery was ever-changing, and the dense forest was strange and wondrous. He felt as if his body might fly away. In ancient times, General Han Shizhong defied Qin Hui, resigned from his post, carried wine, and daily visited West Lake, where he built the Cuiwei Pavilion on Feilai Peak. Only such a person could truly appreciate this mountain! Descending from Feilai Peak, he arrived at the Cold Spring Pavilion. He asked about the so-called Lingyin Temple and learned that the one inscribed with "Cloud Forest" was it. It was the fourth day of the eighth month, and the temple held a vegetarian meal for monks on this day. The abbot of the temple, dressed in monastic robes, delivered a sermon. The chief abbot was named Dihui, who was about ninety years old, and he sat dignified on the high seat. There were two chief monks and eight attendants, and below them, about three hundred monks and nuns lined up, all showing respectful and disciplined behavior. However, the sermon was unoriginal, as if it were just a recitation of a book. The audience did not fully hear or understand it, and they only followed the movements of the attendants, bowing when they bowed and standing when they stood.

    622

    呜呼！佛法入中国，千余年矣，愚民绝其父子之天性、饮食男女之大欲而为僧，自宜求成佛，而佛又必不可成。不成佛而徒自苦，奚取于为僧？且此堂上堂下说法听法诸众，非不自知照本讽诵、随人跪起之不可以成佛，然而必为此者，盖有所不得已也。贫无所养，不能力作，因削发而为僧。而天下之愚夫愚妇，非为殿宇庄严、戒律威仪以耸动之，不能发其信心而得其布施。故此济济而楚楚者，名为学佛，实为救饥计也。井田久废，学校不兴，彼既无田可耕，又不闻圣人之道以为依归，穷而无所复入，其为僧，无足怪也。欧阳子曰：“佛法入中国，乘吾道之废缺而来。”韩子曰：“明先王之道以道之，鳏寡孤独废疾者有养也，则亦庶乎其可也。”

    Explanation

    622

    Alas! Buddhism has entered China for over a thousand years. Ordinary people, by becoming monks, sever their natural familial ties and basic desires for food and sexual relations, yet they naturally seek to become Buddhas, but it is impossible to achieve. If one cannot become a Buddha and only suffers, what is the point of being a monk? Moreover, there is a necessity behind their actions. The well-field system has long been abandoned, and schools have declined. They have no land to farm and have not heard the teachings of the sages to guide them. Being destitute and having no other means of support, their becoming monks is not surprising. Ouyang Xiu said, "Buddhism entered China when our own way (Confucianism) was in decline." Han Yu said, "If we clarify the ways of the ancient kings and provide for the destitute and disabled, then perhaps it can be managed."

    623

    飞来峰之东南，有下天竺。再入有中天竺，再入有上天竺，乃昨所睹烧残者，男女杂糅，犹在瓦砾场中烧香也。出天竺而南，至于忠肃公之坟。阳明先生题其门曰：“赤手挽银河，君自大名垂宇宙；青山埋白骨，我来何处哭英雄？”于坟之南，南高峰也。峰南度一岭而西，石壁嵯峨，下有岩洞，陶复陶穴，曰“石屋”，西上里许，有水乐洞。两洞并列，一有水而一无，从无水者入，与有水者通。其水塞洞，砰磅訇磕，而至洞口即入地，从不流出洞外，亦一奇也。又西上烟霞岭，极目皆山，幽深奇伟，更过于灵隐、天竺之间。问之人，云：“此中名山古刹甚多，屈指不能数其名，累月不能穷其境。”吾始知吾之足力不能遍至也，而遂还。

    Explanation

    623

    To the southeast of Feilai Peak, there is the Lower Tianzhu Temple. Further in lies the Middle Tianzhu Temple, and even deeper, the Upper Tianzhu Temple, which I saw yesterday, burned to ruins, with men and women mingled together, still burning incense in a field of tiles and rubble. Emerging from the Tianzhu area and heading south, one arrives at the tomb of the Loyal and Upright Official. Mr. Wang Yangming inscribed the gate with these words: "With bare hands, you pulled the Milky Way; your great name has been inscribed in the universe. Green hills bury white bones; where do I come to weep for heroes?" To the south of the tomb lies the South Peak. Crossing a ridge to the west of the peak, one encounters towering cliffs, beneath which lies a rock cave, known as "Stone House." A short distance to the west, there is the Water Music Cave. The two caves stand side by side, one filled with water and the other dry. Entering the dry cave leads to the water-filled one. The water fills the cave, pounding and echoing, and then disappears into the ground at the cave's entrance, never flowing out, which is also a marvel. Further west, one ascends the Smoke and Mist Ridge, where all one sees is mountains, deep, secluded, and magnificent, even more so than the area between Lingyin Temple and the Tianzhu Temples. Asking the locals, they said, "There are many famous mountains and ancient temples here, too numerous to count on fingers, and their beauty cannot be fully explored in months." I then realized that my strength could not reach all places, and so I returned.

    624

    次日，同年苏耕余载酒船相邀，予以湖上之景未遍观也，与之出清波门。城下多柳，而白堤多桥，所谓“柳浪闻莺”、“断桥残雪”也。循白堤，复至孤山，入行宫。行宫之制甚奇，复阁重廊，周回相通，凿石为基，削岩成壁，引水成池，植花成幄。桥水磴山，至于后宫。殿在山上，含岩石于殿中，注清泉于座下，一室之中，而山水之观毕具。左右高楼，近挹湖光。远吞山色，如登玉霄金阙，而望十洲三岛之仙踪也。放鹤亭在行宫东北，古梅巨石，清雅不群，惜亭殊巨丽，不似当日处士风流。下亭，复登舟，绕孤山之背，至昭庆寺而还。于湖中之景，不能十一，而已暮矣。予益信轿与船之不能远到，而游西湖者未尽见西湖也。

    Explanation

    624

    The next day, my fellow examinee Su Gengyu invited me aboard his wine-laden boat, and as I had not yet fully observed the scenery of the West Lake, I accompanied him out through Qingbo Gate. Beneath the city walls, there were many willows, while the Bai Causeway was adorned with numerous bridges, the famous spots known as "Willow Waves and Orioles" and "Broken Bridge with Residual Snow." Following the Bai Causeway, we arrived at Solitary Hill and entered the imperial palace. The structure of the palace was quite extraordinary, with overlapping buildings and corridors interconnected throughout. Stones were carved to form the foundation, and rocks were cut to create walls. Water was drawn to form ponds, and flowers were planted to create canopies. Bridges spanned the water, and steps led up the mountain, leading us to the rear palace. The hall was situated on the hill, incorporating rocks within its interior and flowing clear springs beneath the seats. Within a single room, all the scenic views of mountains and water were complete. High buildings stood on either side, close to the reflection of the lake, and far-reaching, they seemed to swallow the mountain colors. It was like ascending to the jade heavens and golden palaces, gazing upon the traces of the immortals in the Ten Isles and Three Islands. The Pavilion for Releasing Cranes was located to the northeast of the palace, surrounded by ancient plum trees and large stones, clear and elegant in its uniqueness. I regretted that the pavilion was unusually grand and beautiful, unlike the simple and refined style of a hermit. Descending from the pavilion, we boarded the boat again, circling the back of Solitary Hill and returning to Zhaqing Temple. Of the lake's scenery, we had only seen a fraction, and it was already evening. I came to believe that carriages and boats could not reach far, and thus those who visit West Lake do not see all of West Lake.

    625

    留数日，遂渡江而东。钱塘江中，亦有两山，仿佛金、焦，遥望海门，屹然对峙。惜时非八月，不能观大潮。渡江至萧山，萧山有湖，产蓴丝嘉鱼；旱则引湖水以溉田，潦泄于海，风景似西湖，而有用过之。萧山东则山阴道上矣，千岩万壑，大者奇伟，小者佳丽。山下皆水，大溪小港，经纬绣错。东至白鹤浦，有小山，舟人指曰：“禹戮防风氏之所也。”泛舟入山阴城，登卧龙山。出城至于鉴湖，昔明皇赐贺知章鉴湖一曲，后遂指此一曲为鉴湖，其实萧山、会稽、山阴三县之水，皆鉴湖也。尝登山而望之，三县桑田，其平如砥，想皆沧海所变。水在其中，渟满不流，而色清若镜，故曰鉴湖也。

    Explanation

    625

    For several days, I stayed, then crossed the river to the east. In the Qiantang River, there are also two mountains, resembling Jin and Jiao, which stand opposite each other from afar. It is a pity that it is not August, so one cannot see the great tide. Crossing the river to Xiaoshan, Xiaoshan has a lake that produces chun silk and fine fish; in drought, lake water is drawn to irrigate the fields; in flood, it is released into the sea. The scenery resembles West Lake, but is more useful. East of Xiaoshan is the Shanyin Road, with thousands of rocks and valleys, the large ones strange and magnificent, the small ones beautiful and charming. Below the mountains are waters, large streams and small creeks, interwoven like embroidery. East to Baigehu, there is a small mountain, and the boatman points and says, "This is where Yu killed Fangfeng." Sailing into the city of Shanyin, climb Mount Wulong. Leaving the city and reaching Mirror Lake, in the past, Emperor Mingzong granted a section of Mirror Lake to He Zhizhang, and later this section was called Mirror Lake. In fact, the waters of Xiaoshan, Kuaiji, and Shanyin counties are all part of Mirror Lake. Once climbing the mountain and looking down, the three counties' fields are as flat as a grindstone, and it is thought they were all once part of the sea. The water in it is still and does not flow, and its color is clear like a mirror, hence it is called Mirror Lake.

    626

    自鉴湖欲游吼山，鉴湖之水无波，故舟多夜行。梦中不知泊于何处，但闻雨声彻夜不绝，天明起视，初无雨。舟在巨潭，四围皆山，并无来路，不知舟何以得至潭中。潭南岩上，乳泉乱滴如檐溜。东峰有洞，水满其中。西峰怪石超出，长垂下注，若巨象舒鼻以饮潭水。其北竹林茂密，楼阁清幽，晓梦初醒，疑非尘世。舟人语曰：“此所谓曹溪。东有洞者狮山，西如鼻者象山，有楼阁者，石匮先生之书院也。”登楼四望，见楼后之山尤高峻，怪石森列，有如台者，如柱者，如首戴笠者，如巨人立者，所谓吼山也。下楼棹舟，由狮山之洞中，曲折行数百步而后出，如渔郎自桃源归也。吼山有空明庵，门前流水，门内清池，朱楼碧瓦，倒影池中，高岩峭壁，卓立楼后，瀑泉飞洒，常如骤雨。其奇不减曹溪也。

    Explanation

    626

    Wishing to travel from Jian Lake to Huoshan Mountain, the water of Jian Lake was calm, so the boat often sailed at night. In a dream, not knowing where we had moored, all one could hear was the incessant sound of rain throughout the night. Upon waking at dawn, there was initially no rain. The boat was in a vast pond, surrounded by mountains on all sides, with no way back, and one wondered how the boat had reached the pond. On the southern cliff of the pond, milk-like springs dripped randomly like rainwater from eaves. The eastern peak had a cave, filled with water inside. The western peak had peculiar stones protruding, hanging long and flowing downward, as if a giant elephant was stretching its trunk to drink from the pond. To the north, dense bamboo forests and serene pavilions; upon waking from a morning dream, one doubted it was not a worldly place. The boatman said, "This is what is called Caoxi. The eastern one with a cave is Shishan (Lion Mountain), the western one resembling a nose is Xiangshan (Elephant Mountain), and the one with pavilions is the academy of Shikuai Xiansheng." Climbing the tower to look around, one saw the mountain behind the tower especially high and steep, with strange stones arranged in a forest-like manner, some like platforms, some like columns, some like people wearing hats, and some like standing giants, this is what is called Huoshan (Roaring Mountain). Descending the tower and rowing the boat, through the cave of Shishan, winding through several hundred steps before emerging, like the fisherman returning from Peach Blossom Spring. Huoshan has the Kōngmíng Temple, in front of the temple, flowing water; inside the temple, a clear pool, red towers and green tiles, reflected in the pool, high cliffs and steep walls, standing tall behind the tower, waterfalls flying and splashing, often like sudden rain. Its wonders are not less than those of Caoxi.

    627

    吼山返棹，乃谒禹陵。禹陵之山，高圆若冢。众峰环拱，有如侍卫。陵侧有菲泉，泉东有庙，庙旁有窆石亭，相传葬禹时所用。石高五六尺，烟如柱，端有圆孔，似孔庙之汉碑。记曰：“公室视丰碑，三家視桓楹。”窆石似楹，盖葬碑也。由禹陵至南镇，南镇者，会稽山也。其最高者曰炉峰，其下有庙，为历代祭告之所。自南镇回舟，夜泊山阴城外，月几望矣。气霁云敛，月白江清，天水相涵，空明一片。人在舟中，身心朗彻，如琉璃合，恍然若有所悟。

    Explanation

    627

    Returning the oar after rowing through the howling mountains, we then visited the Yu Mausoleum. The mountain of the Yu Mausoleum is high and round, like a grave mound. Surrounded by peaks, it stands as if protected by guards. Beside the mausoleum is the Fei Spring, and east of the spring is a temple. Adjacent to the temple is the Pavilion of the Burial Stone, which is said to have been used during Yu's burial. The stone is about five or six feet high, smoke-like and pillar-shaped, with a round hole at the top, resembling a Han dynasty stele found in the Confucian temple. It is recorded: "The official residence views tall stone monuments, and the three major families view tall pillars." The burial stone resembles a pillar, likely a burial monument. From the Yu Mausoleum to the Southern Mountain, the Southern Mountain is Mount Kuaiji. Its highest peak is called Furnace Peak, and below it is a temple where sacrifices and prayers have been offered throughout the ages. Returning from the Southern Mountain, we anchored the boat outside the city of Shanyin at night. The moon was almost full. The weather cleared, and the clouds gathered, with a bright moon and clear river. The sky and water blend together, forming a vast expanse of clarity. People in the boat felt their minds and bodies clear and bright, like a piece of clear crystal, suddenly as if they had gained insight.

    628

    黎明至于兰亭。今之兰亭，非昔之兰亭矣。择平地而建亭，中立大碑，御书右军序于其上。亭前为石成渠，以为曲水，崎岖跼蹐，初无远致，且不可以流觞。左右各凿一池，以为是“鹅池”与“墨池”也。亭西里许，曰天章寺，而亦非旧矣。然此皆人为之者，故有废兴，若所谓“崇山峻岭”、“清流激湍”，则依然在。盖山阴之水不流，唯兰渚湍急，潺潺于茂林修竹之间，风致又别也。返城中，登蕺山。下有寺，乃右军之旧第，其南有题扇桥。山下有书院，刘念台讲学于此。

    Explanation

    628

    At dawn, I arrived at the Orchid Pavilion. This Orchid Pavilion is no longer the same as the one of old. A flat site was chosen to build the pavilion, with a large stele placed in the center, bearing an imperial inscription of Wang Xizhi's sequence on it. Before the pavilion, a stone channel was constructed, designed as a winding waterway, which was winding and cramped, lacking distant charm, and not suitable for floating cups. Pools were dug on either side, named "Goose Pond" and "Ink Pond." About a mile west of the pavilion is Tianzhang Temple, which is also not the same as the old one. All these places are man-made and thus subject to rise and fall. However, the famous descriptions of "tall mountains and steep hills," "clear streams and swift currents" still exist. The water of Shan Yin does not flow, but only the rapid current of Lan Zhu flows gently through the dense forest and tall bamboo, giving a unique charm. Returning to the city, I climbed Mount Jixi. Below the mountain is a temple, which was the former residence of Wang Xizhi. To the south of the temple is a bridge named "Fan Writing Bridge." At the foot of the mountain is a schoolhouse where Liu Niantai taught.

    629

    予棹舟在山阴道上三日夜，有山皆秀，无水不清，回环往复，不辨西东，登蕺山乃瞭然。盖绍兴之西南皆山，而东北近海。吼山在东，兰亭在西，禹凌、南镇在其南，北有梅山，下有梅市，梅福之所居也。远望南镇之西，有高于南镇者，曰秦望，始皇帝刻石于此。又禹穴非禹陵也，禹藏书于宛委之山，曰禹穴。又会稽有阳明洞，道书云第十一洞天，而余皆未至。游人惮于登陟，舟所可至者至之，若高远幽深、神圣仙灵之遗迹，则惧而不果去。抑吾在绍兴凡三望海：登下方山望海，登禹穴、登蕺山皆望海，第见茫茫沙草而已，实未尝见水。吾犹怅然以山海之奇未尽探也。

    Explanation

    629

    I rowed my boat along the mountain path for three days and nights. Every mountain was beautiful, and every stream was clear. The winding path made it difficult to tell east from west, but when I climbed Mount Jishan, everything became clear. The southwest of Shaoxing is all mountains, while the northeast is close to the sea. How Mountain is to the east, Orchid Pavilion is to the west, Yu's Shrine and South Temple are to the south, and to the north there is Mei Mountain, with Mei Market below, where Mei Fu once lived. Looking far to the west of South Temple, there is a mountain higher than South Temple, called Qin Lookout, where the First Emperor of Qin inscribed a stone. Moreover, Yu's Cave is not Yu's tomb; Yu hid his books on Wanyue Mountain, which is called Yu's Cave. Additionally, Kuaiji has Yangming Cave, which is said to be the eleventh heaven in Taoist tradition, but I have not visited any of these places. Tourists are reluctant to climb high and deep into the mountains, so they only go where the boat can reach. If the place is high, remote, deep, and a site of divine or immortal traces, they fear and do not venture further. Indeed, in Shaoxing, I have looked out to sea three times: from Below Mountain, from Yu's Cave, and from Mount Jishan. Each time, I only saw vast stretches of sand and grass, without actually seeing the water. I am still disappointed that I have not fully explored the wonders of the mountains and sea.

    630

    由绍兴复返杭州，登凤凰山，一名紫阳山，昔高宗南渡，广杭城，包此山于苑内，以为游观之所。左江右湖，登临彷徨，致足乐也。自杭州溯浙江，至于富阳。富阳之山，雄壮似燕秦诸塞，而青翠过之。富阳以南，川势渐窄，两山对峙，一水中流，群山倒影，上下皆青。出橦梓关，势渐开，远近布列，山皆妍媚。桐君山陡立江岸，其南内拓开一平原，石壁环峙，如天生城阙，则桐庐也。阻山临水，居民在山水之间，瓦青墙白，纤尘不染；其清华朗润，令人神恬。南至鸬鹚原，山势怪特，峰峦坌涌，密峙骈植，束江流如一线。入原口转而西，则富春也。南北皆山，其中皆水，不余寸土。

    Explanation

    630

    From Shaoxing, returning to Hangzhou, I climbed Phoenix Mountain, also known as Ziyang Mountain. In the past, Emperor Gaozong crossed south, expanded Hangzhou city, and included this mountain within the park, regarding it as a place for leisure and sightseeing. With a river on the left and a lake on the right, for climbing and wandering, it is truly delightful. From Hangzhou, tracing upstream along the Zhejiang River, reaching Fuyang, the mountains of Fuyang are majestic, resembling the passes of Yan and Qin, but greener. South of Fuyang, the river narrows, with two mountains facing each other, a river flowing between, the reflection of the mountains, both above and below, is green. Exiting the Tongzi Pass, the landscape gradually opens up, mountains are arranged far and near, all charming. Tongjun Mountain stands steeply by the riverbank, to the south, it opens up a plain, surrounded by cliffs, like a naturally formed city gate, that is Tonglu. Surrounded by mountains and water, residents live between the mountains and water, roofs are blue, walls are white, without a speck of dust; its clarity and freshness are soothing to the spirit. South to the Lucheng Plain, the mountain range is strange and distinctive, peaks and ridges rise densely, standing closely together, constraining the river to a single line. Entering the plain's mouth and turning west, it is the Fuchun River. North and south are mountains, in between is water, leaving no inch of land.

    631

    两“钓台”在北山下，石峰直起而顶方，旁有子陵祠。凡钓台左右之山，其颠皆有流泉，锦峰缥缈，上入高青，怪石峥嵘，下临沉碧。瀑流喷薄，堕玉飞珠，涧水层波，调笙鼓瑟。高山流水之观止矣！尝忆陶隐君语云：“高峰入云，清流见底；两峰石壁，五色交辉。青林翠竹，四时具备。晓雾将歇，猿鸟乱啼；夕日欲颓，沉鳞竞跃。实是欲界之仙都。”唯此地足以当之。西至于严州，高山四塞，大水环周，可称为天险。南入横溪，至于兰溪。自杭州至兰溪，四百余里，冈峦绵亘，雄于富阳，清于桐庐，奇于富春，秀于兰谿。人在舟中，高视远眺，不能坐卧。偶值偃仰，两岸之山，次第从船窗中过，如画图徐展。舟行之乐，无逾于此！

    Explanation

    631

    The two "fishing platforms" are located at the foot of the northern mountain, with stone peaks rising straight and having flat tops, and a Zilin Temple nearby. All the mountains around the fishing platforms have flowing springs at their peaks, with colorful peaks that are ethereal, reaching up into the high blue sky, rugged stones overlooking the deep blue waters below, waterfalls plunging like jade and flying pearls, streams with layered waves, as if tuning a flute and drumming a zither. The view of high mountains and flowing water is the ultimate! I often recall what Tao Yinjun said: "The high peaks reach into the clouds, and the clear streams are visible to the bottom; the two peaks have stone walls, and five colors shine together. The green forests and emerald bamboo are present throughout the four seasons. The morning mist is dissipating, and the apes and birds are singing chaotically; the evening sun is setting, and the fish are leaping. It is truly a fairyland in the desire realm." Only this place can live up to it. To the west, it reaches Yanzhou, with high mountains all around and great waters surrounding it, it can be called a natural fortress. To the south, it enters Hengxi and reaches Lanxi. From Hangzhou to Lanxi, it is over four hundred miles, with hills and ridges stretching continuously, more majestic than Fuyang, clearer than Tonglu, more wondrous than Fuchun, and more beautiful than Lanxi. People in the boat, looking up and out at a distance, cannot sit or lie down. Occasionally lying back, the mountains on both sides pass by the boat windows in sequence, like a painting slowly unrolling. The joy of traveling by boat is unparalleled here.

    632

    兰溪南曰金华，川势大开，极目平畴，远望崇山，烟云缭绕，摩天碍日。传闻其上有朝真、冰壶、双龙之洞，乃王方平叱石成羊之所也。西过龙游，至于衢州，凡西安道上之山，冈峦华簇，而滑瘦如削，烟岚高洁，刻露清秀。西南至常山，多枫桂，云眠树间，山横云上，高薄深林，令人有小山招隐之思。西至玉山，复登舟，至于广信，为江西界。山形粗猛突兀，横亘直竖，缘河罗列，皆一石特起，方圆平直，各自为象。

    Explanation

    632

    South of the Lan River lies Jinhua, where the river valley opens widely, and the eyes can see vast fields. From afar, towering mountains rise, shrouded in mist and clouds that scrape the sky and block the sun. It is said that above these mountains are the caves named Zhaoren, Binghu, and Shuanglong, places where the immortal Wang Fangping transformed stones into sheep. Traveling westward past Longyou, one arrives at Quzhou. Along the Western Route, the hills and ridges are beautifully clustered, yet slim and sharp like a knife, with high and pure mist, revealing clear and elegant features. Southwest to Changshan, the area is abundant with maple and camphor trees, where clouds sleep among the trees, and mountains rise above the clouds. The dense and elevated forests evoke a desire to retreat to a secluded mountain. Westward to Yushan, one takes a boat to Guangxin, marking the border of Jiangxi Province. The mountains here are rugged and prominent, stretching and standing straight along the river, each rising as a unique stone, round, square, flat, and straight, each with its own form.

    633

    西至弋阳，有龟峰山，众峰直起如筍，有青山头，峰顶皆圆，有如人首：或冠或冕，或螓或颀，或光如僧，或鬟如妓。寺隐丛篁，泉出古洞，棕榈芭蕉，延满岩谷，奇险幽秀，兼而有之。西北至贵溪，见“天然桥”，一石横两峰之颠，下空若洞，亦奇境也。闻贵溪有鬼谷山，鬼谷子之所居。又有象山，陆子静读书其上，尝曰：“云山谷石之奇，目所未睹，问之人而不知，知有龙虎山张真人而已。”西至安仁，地平旷。南至瑞洪，遂入鄱阳。自安仁以西，四望不见山。至瑞洪以南，四望并不见树，短草黄沙，烟水云天而已。湖水甚浊，波涛皆红。

    Explanation

    633

    Travelling westward to Yiyang, there is Gui Feng Mountain, with many peaks rising straight up like bamboo shoots. There are green mountains with rounded peaks, resembling human heads: some adorned with crowns or tiaras, some with short or tall foreheads, some shining like monks, some with hair styled like courtesans. A temple is hidden among dense bamboo groves, and a spring emerges from an ancient cave. Palm and banana plants spread throughout the rocky valleys, creating a landscape that is strange, dangerous, secluded, and beautiful. Moving northwest to Guixi, one sees the "Natural Bridge," a single stone spanning the tops of two peaks, with a hollow underside like a cave, another remarkable sight. It is said that there is Gui Gu Mountain in Guixi, where the legendary Gui Gu Zi lived. There is also Xiang Mountain, where Lu Jiujing studied, and he once remarked, "The wonders of the mountains and stones are so unique that they have never been seen before, and no one knows about them except for Zhang Zhenren of Longhu Mountain." Travelling westward to Anren, the land is flat and open. Moving south to Ruighong, one enters Poyang. From Anren westward, one cannot see any mountains in any direction. From Ruighong southward, one cannot see any trees in any direction. The landscape is short grass, yellow sand, and only smoke, water, and clouds. The lake water is very turbid, with waves that are all red.

    634

    出湖入章江，至南昌，登滕王阁。章江南来，渺瀰极目；彭蠡北汇，烟波万顷。东望平畴，天垂野阔。连峰千里，西列屏障，所谓“西山暮雨，南浦朝云。霞鹜齐飞，水天一色”，盖实录也！南昌阻风，泊舟生米渡。次蚤渡江，几至不测。语日“安不忘危”，又曰“千金之子，坐不垂堂”，余自维扬登舟，过扬子，泛吴淞，涉钱塘，溯桐溪，经鄱阳，在舟数月，侥幸无恙，习而安焉。设非遭此，遂安其危而忘垂堂之戒也，岂可哉？
    　　　　南至于丰城，观剑池。西入清江，至临江府。城东有合皂山，昔张道陵、丁令威、葛孝先皆居于此。西过新喻，山尤多：分宜之山清而秀，袁州之山奇而雄。至芦溪乃陆走，过萍乡复登舟，经醴陵，出涤口，至湘江，入湖南境。右江风俗，胜于三吴两浙：男事耕耘，兼以商贾；女皆纺织。所出麻枲绵葛松杉鱼虾米麦，不为奇技淫巧，其勤俭习事，有唐魏之风。独好诈而健讼，则楚俗也。

    634

    Exiting the lake and entering the Zhangjiang River, I reached Nanchang and climbed the Tengwang Pavilion. The Zhangjiang River flows southward, its waters vast and boundless, stretching to the horizon; to the north, the Poyang Lake converges, its misty waters covering ten thousand acres. Looking east, the flat fields stretch to the sky, the wilderness appearing vast. Continuous mountain ranges stretch for a thousand miles, forming a western barrier. These are the scenes described as "the evening rain on the western mountains, the morning clouds over the southern ferry; the rosy clouds and wild geese flying together, the water and sky blending into one color," which are indeed true records! In Nanchang, I was held up by the wind and anchored my boat at Shengmi Ferry. The next morning, crossing the river, I almost encountered unforeseen danger. The saying goes, "do not forget danger when you are safe," and it is also said, "a son of a thousand gold pieces does not sit near the eaves." Since boarding a boat in Weiyang, passing through Yangzi, sailing on the Wusong River, crossing the Qiantang River, retracing the Tongxi River, and traveling through Poyang, I have spent several months on the boat, surviving by sheer luck without incident, becoming accustomed to it. If not for this experience, I would become complacent in danger and forget the caution of not sitting near the eaves, which is unacceptable.

    Traveling south to Fencheng, I visited the Sword Pool. Westward into Qingjiang, I reached Linjiang Prefecture. To the east of the city lies Hepsao Mountain, where Zhang Daoling, Ding Lingwei, and Ge Xiaoxian once lived. Passing west through Xinyu, there are many mountains: the mountains of Fenyi are clear and elegant, while those of Yizhou are strange and majestic. Reaching Luxi, I traveled by land, passed through Pingxiang, boarded a boat again, passed through Liling, exited at Dikou, reached the Xiang River, and entered Hunan Province. The customs of the right bank of the river are superior to those of the Three Wu and Two Zhe regions: men engage in farming and also in trade; women all engage in weaving. The products of the region, such as hemp, flax, cotton, ramie, pine, cypress, fish, shrimp, rice, and wheat, are not strange or extravagant; the people are diligent, thrifty, and accustomed to hard work, with the customs of the Tang and Wei dynasties. They are particularly fond of deceit and litigation, which is a characteristic of Chu customs.

    635

    湘江之水清而文，两岸之山秀而雅，草多茅菅，扶疏猗靡，皆有蕙薄丛兰之致。每当五岭朝霞，三湘夜雨，或光风转蕙，皓月临枫，吟《离骚》《九歌》《招魂》之句，如睹泽畔之憔悴也，如逢芰衣荷裳之芳泽也，如闻湘灵山鬼之吟啸悲啼也。南至衡州，谒南岳。凡岳镇，非独形伟，其气盛也。向登泰山，郁郁葱葱，灵光焕发。渡江以来，名山无数，神采少减焉。兹见南岳，乃复如睹泰山，连峰争出，高不可止；复岭互藏，厚不可穷。石壁插青，流泉界白，气勃如蒸，岚深似黛。顶在云中，有若神龙，其首不见，而爪舒鳞跃，光怪陆离。“火维地荒，天假神柄”，应不诬也。衡山七十二峰，其最大者五：芙蓉、紫盖、石廪、天柱、祝融。南岳庙在祝融峰下，谒庙后，望五峰，其顶皆在云中。登舟南行数日，无时不矫首。古语云：“帆随湘转，望衡九面。”予九面望而卒未尝见其顶，始叹衡山之云之难开也！

    Explanation

    635

    The water of the Xiang River is clear and beautiful, and the mountains along the river are graceful and elegant. The grass is abundant and luxuriant, with the fragrance of orchids and lilies. Whenever the Five Ridges are bathed in morning mist and the Three Xiangs are drenched in night rain, or when the gentle breeze turns the fragrance of the plants and the bright moon shines on the maples, one can recite lines from the works of Qu Yuan, as if seeing the emaciated figure by the marsh, as if encountering the fragrance of the lotus robes, or as if hearing the singing and crying of the spirits of the Xiang River and the mountain ghosts. Traveling south to Hengzhou, one pays homage to Mount Heng. All great mountains are not only magnificent in form but also abundant in energy. Previously, when climbing Mount Tai, it was lush and vibrant, radiating spiritual light. Since crossing the river, countless famous mountains have been seen, but their divine aura is somewhat diminished. Now, seeing Mount Heng again, it is like seeing Mount Tai once more. The peaks rise one after another, reaching heights that cannot be surpassed; the ridges interlock, forming depths that cannot be fathomed. The green cliffs and white streams, the air is thick and steamy, and the mist is deep and dark. The summit is in the clouds, like a divine dragon, with its head unseen, but its claws and scales visible, and its light and color dazzling. "In the fiery realm of the wilderness, heaven grants divine power," this should not be false. Mount Heng has 72 peaks, with five being the largest: Cotton, Purple Canopy, Stone Granary, Pillar of Heaven, and Zhurong. The temple of Mount Heng is at the foot of Zhurong Peak. After paying respects at the temple, looking at the five peaks, their summits are all in the clouds. Traveling south by boat for several days, one cannot help but raise one's head. An ancient saying goes: "The sails follow the Xiang River, and one can see Mount Heng from nine directions." I looked from nine directions but never saw the summit, and began to marvel at how difficult it is to see through the clouds of Mount Heng.

    636

    西次祁阳，见唐亭，元次山之所建。西至于水州，自右江至衡阳，数千里间，土石多赤，一望红原绿草，碧树丹屋，烂若绘绚。至零陵，山黑而石白，天地之气一变。城下潇江，北合于湘。潇西之山皆幽奇，柳子厚多记之。西入湘口，水愈清，两岸之石，玲珑奇峭，不可指数，所谓少人而多石，其信然与！西至于全州，为粤西形胜之地，湘山崔嵬，高踞俯视，众山环拱，诸水会同。山下有光孝寺，无量寿佛示寂之所，云肉身在塔内。予入而谛观之，不似也。

    Explanation

    636

    Travelling westward to Qihyang, I saw Tang Pavilion, which was built by Yuan Zishan. Continuing westward to Shuizhou, the journey spanned thousands of miles from Youjiang to Hengyang, where the soil and stones were predominantly red. The landscape stretched out with red plains, green grass, green trees, and red houses, bright and colorful like a painting. Reaching Lingling, the mountains turned dark and the stones white, marking a change in the atmosphere. Below the city, the Xiao River flowed northward, merging with the Xiang River. The mountains west of the Xiao River were mysterious and peculiar, and Liu Zihou wrote extensively about them. Entering the Xiang River mouth, the water became clearer, with delicate and peculiar stones along the banks, too numerous to count. As the saying goes, there are few people but many stones—indeed, it is so! Reaching Quanzhou, it is a strategic location in western Guangxi, with Xiang Mountain towering high, overlooking the surrounding mountains and rivers. At the foot of the mountain lies the Guangxiao Temple, where the Buddha Amitabha attained Nirvana, said to have his physical body inside the pagoda. I entered the pagoda and carefully observed, but it did not resemble the Buddha.

    637

    南至于兴安，有阳海山。半山有分水岭，山脊流水，可以泛舟；至岭而分，其北流者为湘江，南流者为漓江，一水而相离，故曰湘漓也。志云：“临贺、始安、桂阳、揭阳、大庾为五岭。”《水经注》云：“湘水出零陵始安县。”然则兴安者始安也。予自长沙溯湘江至永全，挽舟直上，如登峻坂。山腰回舟，转入漓江，下桂林如建瓴。源泉混混，咫尺分流，而北入北海，南入南海，其岭之高可知矣。
    　　　　漓江初分，屈曲山间，别凿一渠以通舟。秦伐南越，史录凿此。汉戈船将军出零陵、下漓水，于此置阧，阧犹关也。诸葛武侯续修之。渠上有武侯祠，祠后有伏龙山，山石多怪，玲珑槎枒，连峰叠嶂，皆如米颠袖中之物。伏龙以西，群峰乱峙，四布罗列，如平沙万幕，八门五花；江如游骑纵横其中。前有高峰曰马头山，卓立俯视，如大将秉巨纛以出令也。

    Explanation

    637

    South to Xing'an, there is Yanghai Mountain. Halfway up the mountain is a watershed, with water flowing along the ridge, making it possible to navigate boats; when it reaches the ridge, it splits, with the water flowing north becoming the Xiang River and the water flowing south becoming the Li River, one river splitting into two, hence it is called Xiang and Li. Records state: "Linhe, Shian, Guiyang, Jieyang, and Dayu are the Five Ridges." The Shui Jing Zhu states: "The Xiang River originates from Shian County, Lingling." Thus, Xing'an is Shian. I traveled upstream from Changsha to Yongquan, pulling the boat straight up, as if climbing a steep slope. Turning the boat around at the mountain waist, entering the Li River, descending to Guilin is like pouring water from a high place. The source of the water is abundant, and it splits just a short distance away, flowing north into the North Sea and south into the South Sea, the height of the ridge can be seen from this.

    Initially, the Li River splits, winding through the mountains, a separate channel was excavated to allow passage of boats. During the Qin conquest of Nan Yue, historical records note this excavation. The Han general of the warship fleet departed from Lingling and descended the Li River, setting up a checkpoint here, which was akin to a pass. Zhuge Liang continued to repair it. There is a Zhuge Liang shrine on the channel, behind the shrine is Fulong Mountain, the mountain rocks are peculiar, delicate and twisted, with continuous peaks and overlapping ridges, all like objects in Mi Fu's sleeve. West of Fulong Mountain, many peaks stand in disorder, scattered and arranged like a vast plain with thousands of tents and eight gates with five flowers; the river flows through the peaks like cavalry moving freely. Ahead is a high peak called Mato Mountain, standing tall and looking down, like a great general holding a large banner to issue commands.

    638

    南过灵川，至于桂林。粵西高大中丞，予业师也，留署中过夏。时时跨马出游郊坰，负郭山水之胜皆见之。城中屹立者曰独秀山，高数百丈，下有石室，顶通光耀。其东北曰伏波山，高峭与独秀等，岩中悬石，下垂如柱。其西有叠彩岩，石纹华丽，岩腹有洞，冷风日夜不休，曰风洞。迎风而入，曲折崎岖，渐觉光明，忽然高敞；身入楼阁，户牖轩豁，栏槛回环；开户一望，水天无际，山林窈冥。盖漓江从城北来，两岸之山，怪怪奇奇，向在舟中，未尽见也。兹入洞内，黑走山腹，忽睹上界，皆成异境。舟泛银河，人至天台，亦若是矣。城南有刘仙崖，石洞如屋，内刻张平叔《赠桂林白龙洞刘真人歌》，道铅汞术甚详。城西有七星岩，上有棲霞洞。

    Explanation

    638

    <Translation> Traveling south through Lingchuan to reach Guilin, the author stayed in the official residence during the summer. He frequently rode outside the city walls, enjoying the scenic beauty of the mountains and waters near the city. In the city, the prominent mountain known as Duxiu Mountain rises hundreds of feet high, with a stone chamber at its base and a light shining through the top. To the northeast of the city is Fubo Mountain, equally high and steep, with a hanging stone that hangs down like a column. To the west of the city is the Stacked Color Rock, known for its colorful layers. Inside the rock, there is a cave with a constant cold breeze, called the Wind Cave. Entering the cave against the wind, the path is winding and rugged, gradually becoming bright, and suddenly opening up. The interior of the cave resembles a grand hall, with windows and corridors, and railings all around. Opening the door and looking out, the sky and water stretch endlessly, and the mountains and forests are deep and mysterious. The Li River flows from the north of the city, and the mountains along its banks are strange and wondrous, but they were not fully visible from a boat. Now, entering the cave, moving through the darkness inside the mountain, one suddenly sees a different world. Floating on a river in the Milky Way, or reaching the celestial realm, it is much the same. To the south of the city is Liu Xian Cliff, with a stone cave that looks like a house, inside of which are inscriptions by Zhang Pingshu of the "Song of the White Dragon Cave in Guilin for the True Person Liu," detailing alchemical techniques. To the west of the city is the Seven Stars Rock, with a cave above it called the Xiqia Cave. </Translation>

    639
