    石阶直下数百级，顶上水纹如波，中有鲤鱼，长丈余，头目鳞尾皆具。洞后深黑，秉炬进数百步，冷气迫人，同行者惧，遂偕出。闻土人道其中之景甚怪。王荆公云：“世上奇伟瑰怪非常之观，常在于险远，而为人所罕至。故非有志者不能至也。有志矣不随以止也，然力不足者，亦不能至也。有志与力而又不随以怠，至于幽暗昏忽，而无物以相之，亦不能至也。然力足以至焉，于人为可讥，而在己为有悔。尽吾志而不能至者，可以无悔矣。吾甚悔吾之未尽吾志而随人以止也！”其东有龙隐洞，清流从洞中出而入江。江中有山，轮囷若象鼻舒江中，舟行鼻内。江岸山上有洞，直透山背，以通天光，望之圆明如满月。志称“滨江三洞，水月最佳”者是也。

    Explanation

    639

    The stone steps descend straight down hundreds of levels, with water ripples on the top like waves. In the middle, there is a carp, more than a zhang long, with head, eyes, scales, and tail all present. Behind the cave is deep and dark, holding a torch and advancing hundreds of steps, the cold air presses on one. Those traveling together were afraid, so they all came out together. Hearing the locals say that the scenery inside is very strange. Wang Jingong said, "In the world, there are extraordinary and strange sights. They often exist in dangerous and remote places, rarely visited by people. Therefore, only those with determination can reach them. Even if one has determination, it does not follow that one will stop. However, those lacking strength cannot reach them either. Those with both determination and strength, yet do not follow with laziness. When it comes to dark and dim places, without something to guide one, one cannot reach them either. However, if one has the strength to reach them, it is something for others to criticize, and for oneself to regret. If one has done their best and still cannot reach them, there is no regret. I deeply regret not having done my best and stopping with others!" To the east, there is a Dragon-Hiding Cave, where clear water flows out of the cave and into the river. In the river, there is a mountain, shaped like an elephant's trunk, stretching out into the river, boats travel inside the trunk. On the mountain by the riverbank, there is a cave that goes straight through the back of the mountain, allowing sunlight to pass through, looking at it, it appears round and bright like a full moon. The records call it the best among the three caves by the river, known for its water and moonlight.

    640

    兹行也，在桂林之日为久，瑶苗土僮，蚺蛇，山羊，锦鸡，孔雀，黑白之猿，荔枝、佛手之树，黄皮、白蜡之林，芭蕉之心，长大如椽；天雨之花，其红射日。可谓见所未见。独其俗凶悍褊小，嗜利好杀。天地之灵，钟于物而不钟于人，何哉？予以六月初旬至桂林，七月暑退，登舟返棹。曩之至也，云峰吐火，稻穗涌波，荷蕊绽红，江流涨绿，署中偃仰。曾几何时，而稻禾全刈，木叶半黄，云白天晶，凉风萧瑟。回思江南暮春，莺飞草长，西湖梅雨，花落鸟啼，有如隔世！王右军云：“向之所欣，俯仰之间，已为陈迹。”亶其然矣！

    Explanation

    640

    This journey, in Guilin, lasted a long time. The Yao, Miao, and Zhuang people, king cobras, mountain goats, peacock pheasants, peacocks, black and white apes, lychee and Buddha's hand trees, yellow skin and white wax trees, the heart of the banana plant, which is large like a beam, and flowers that fall from the sky, their red color shining like the sun, all these are sights I had never seen before. However, the local customs are fierce, narrow-minded, and fond of killing. The divine or natural spirit is concentrated in things rather than people, why is that? I arrived in Guilin in early June, and in July, the heat subsided, and I boarded a boat to return. Upon my arrival, the clouds were like peaks spewing fire, the rice stalks swayed like waves, the lotus buds bloomed red, and the river turned green. How quickly time passes, and now the rice fields are fully harvested, the leaves are half yellow, the sky is clear and blue, and a cool, rustling wind blows. Recalling the late spring in the south, swallows fly, grass grows, the plum rains in West Lake, flowers fall, and birds sing. As Wang Xizhi said, "What we once enjoyed becomes old traces in a short while." Indeed so!

    641

    过全州，复入湘山寺，有匾曰“再来人”。予嗒然而笑。夫佛再出世，犹吾再入寺也，而何怪焉？过衡州，登合江亭，湘水南来，蒸水北至，两江合处，一峰特起，曰石鼓山，上有武侯祠。向读韩诗注云“合江亭旁有朱陵洞”，登其上而不见。返舟问榜人，云：“洞在亭下，当事者封其路，游人往往不得至焉。”在舟又望南岳，雾隐云封，终不能见其顶。江山之于人如友，或不期而遇，或千里相访而不值，何哉！北至于湘潭，有昭山。昭王南征至此。
    　　　　北至于沙城，城东有云母山，《列仙传》云“星沙云母，服之长生”者也。城北有罗洋山，城南曰妙高峰。湘江在城西，水西有岳麓山。志曰“衡山七十二峰，回雁为首，岳麓为足”是也。其颠有道乡台，昔邹志完谪长沙，守臣温益逐之，雨夜渡湘宿于此。后张敬夫为之筑台，朱子题曰“道乡”。道乡者，志完之别号也。

    Explanation

    641

    Passing through Quanzhou, I re-entered Xiangshan Temple, where there was a plaque that read "Returner." I laughed with a sense of resignation. That the Buddha would reappear in the world is like my re-entering the temple — what is there to be surprised about? Passing through Hengzhou, I climbed the Hejiang Pavilion. The Xiang River flows southward, and the Zheng River flows northward. Where the two rivers meet, a peak rises uniquely, called Shigushan, with a shrine to Zhuge Liang on top of it. I had read in the annotations to Han's poems that there is a Zhu Ling Cave beside the Hejiang Pavilion, but I could not find it upon climbing to the top. Returning to my boat, I asked the boatman, who told me, "The cave is below the pavilion, but those in charge have sealed off the path, so visitors often cannot reach it." From the boat, I looked at Nanyue, which was hidden by mist and clouds, and I could not see its peak. Mountains and rivers are like friends to people; sometimes they meet unexpectedly, and sometimes they are sought after for miles but cannot be found — why is this so? Moving north to Xiangtan, there is Zhaoshan, where the Marquis of Zhao marched south to this place. Moving north to Shacheng, there is Yumushan to the east of the city, as The Biographies of Immortals states, "Xingsha yunmu, when consumed, grants immortality." To the north of the city is Luoyangshan, and to the south is Miaogao Peak. The Xiang River is to the west of the city, and to the west of the river is Yuelushan. The records say, "The 72 peaks of Hengshan, with Huayang as the head and Yuelu as the foot." At the top is the Daoxiang Terrace. Once, Zou Zhiguan was exiled to Changsha, and the governor Wen Yi drove him away, so he crossed the Xiang River in the rain at night and stayed here. Later, Zhang Jingfu built a terrace for him, and Zhu Xi inscribed it as "Daoxiang." Daoxiang is Zou Zhiguan's alternative name.

    642

    闻志完初谪时，涕泣，其友怒曰：“使志完居京师，得寒疾不汗，五日死矣！独岭南能死人哉？”由今观之，向与志完同时在京师者，皆已湮没，而志完以谪特传，亦可以知所处矣。道乡台下有《岳麓寺碑》，李北海所书也。凡地之美恶，视乎其人，不择地而安之，皆可安也。予过五岭，泛三湘，望九嶷，历百越，皆古迁客骚人痛哭流涕之所。入而游焉，瘴花善红，蛮鸟能语，水清石怪，皆有会心。比及长沙，山林雅旷，水土平良，已如更始余民，复睹司隶雍容。贾太傅乃不自克，而抑郁以死。语云“少不更事”，太傅有焉。北过橘州，昔范质夫南谪，夫人每骂章惇。过橘州舟覆，公自负夫人以出，徐曰：“此亦章惇为之耶？”予性褊，服膺范公以自广。今过其地，想见其为人。

    Explanation

    642

    When Zhizheng was first exiled, he wept, and his friend angrily said, "If Zhizheng had stayed in the capital, he would have died from a cold without sweating within five days! Only the south of the Five Ridges could kill a person?" From today's perspective, those who were in the capital with Zhizheng at the time have all perished, while Zhizheng's exile has given him a special legacy, and one can also understand his situation. Below the platform at Daoxiang Temple is the stele of Yue Lu Temple, inscribed by Li Beihai. The beauty or ugliness of a place depends on the people who inhabit it; one can find peace and contentment anywhere, regardless of the location. I passed through the Five Ridges, sailed through the Three Xiangs, gazed at Jiuyi, and traveled through the various regions of Baiyue, all of which are places where exiled officials and poets have wept bitterly. Entering and exploring these places, I saw tropical flowers that were red, local birds that could speak, clear water, and strange stones, all of which had a deep connection. By the time I reached Changsha, the mountains and forests were elegant and vast, the water and soil were level and good, and it felt like being among the remnants of a new beginning, seeing the dignity and grace of the officials. Jia Dao, however, could not control himself and died of depression. The saying goes, "Lack of experience," and Jia Dao indeed lacked it. Passing through Juzhou in the north, in the past, when Fan Zhifu was exiled to the south, his wife often cursed Zhang Dun. When we passed through Juzhou, our boat capsized, and he carried his wife out of the boat, calmly saying, "Is this also the work of Zhang Dun?" I am narrow-minded, but I admire Fan Gong to broaden my horizons. Now passing through this place, I can imagine his character.

    643

    北至于湘阴，有黄陵庙，二妃之所溺也。其东有汨罗江，屈子之所沉也。过广陵，入洞庭，浩倍荡荡，四无涯涘。晚见红日落于水内，次早见炬火然灼水面，渐望渐高，乃明星也。吾游行天下，山吾皆以为卑，水吾皆以为狭，非果卑果狭也，目能穷其所至，则小之矣。物何大何小，因其所大而大之，则莫不大；因其所小而小之，则莫不小。苏子瞻曰：“覆杯水于地，芥浮于水，蚁附于芥，茫然不知其所济。少焉水涸，蚁即径去，见其类，出涕曰：几不复与子相见！岂知俯仰之间，有方轨八达之路乎？计四海之在天地之间也，犹杯水也；舟犹芥也，人犹蚁也，吾乌知蚁之附芥，不以为是乘桴浮海耶？其水涸而去，不以为是海变桑田耶？四海虽广，应亦有涯，目力不至，则望洋而叹。因所大而大之耳。”今在洞庭，吾目力穷焉，即以为洞庭为吾之海可也。

    Explanation

    643

    To the north, one reaches Xiangyin, where there is the Huangling Temple, the place where the two consorts of Emperor Shun drowned themselves. To the east of it lies the Miluo River, where Qu Yuan, the poet, drowned himself. Passing through Guangling and entering Dongting Lake, one finds it vast and boundless, with no visible limits in any direction. In the evening, one sees the red sun setting into the water, and the next morning, the reflection of fire illuminates the surface. Gradually, the stars rise higher, becoming visible as they shine. As I have traveled throughout the world, I have come to view all mountains as low and all waters as narrow, not because they are truly so, but because my eyes can see their limits, making them seem small. How large or small are things? If one perceives them as large, they appear immense; if one perceives them as small, they seem insignificant. Su Shi once said, "Pour a cup of water onto the ground, and a sprout of grass floats on it, with ants clinging to it, unaware of where they are going. Soon after, the water dries up, and the ants leave, meeting their kindred and shedding tears, saying, 'We almost did not meet again!' How could they know that in a moment, there could be roads stretching in all directions? Consider the four seas within the vast expanse of heaven and earth—they are like a cup of water; boats are like floating grass, and people are like ants. How could I know that the ants, clinging to the grass, do not think they are floating on the sea? When the water dries up, they do not think the sea has turned into land. The four seas are vast, but they must have limits; if one's vision cannot reach them, one will sigh at their vastness. It is because of their great size that they appear so." Now, standing by Dongting Lake, my eyes cannot see further, and I can consider Dongting Lake as my own sea.

    644

    自湘阴泊于磊石，又泊于鹿角，又泊于井罔，皆在湖中。时近中秋，天朗气清，所谓长烟一空，皓月千里，浮光耀金，静影沉璧者，吾见之焉。北至巴陵，岳阳楼在巴城上，而今不存矣。予登其址而望焉，见君山秀出，其东曰扁山，又东曰九龟山，皆在湖中。城南曰白鹤山，其侧有天岳岭，上有吕仙亭，亭前有岳武穆庙。昔武穆剋期八日，平杨幺于洞庭，居人德而祀之。庙貌巍然，据湖山之胜。夫岳阳为纯阳三过之所，宋滕子京重修之，范文正公作记，苏子美书，邵竦篆额。当其盛时，仙之所往来，贤士大夫所歌咏，今皆为荒榛蔓草颓垣；文墨之士无论矣，纯阳有仙术，亦不能留其所爱。武穆蹇蹇，雉罹于罗，徒以忠义之性，结于人心，而遗迹独存。然则人之不死，固自有道矣！

    Explanation

    644

    From Xiangyin, I anchored at Leishi, then at Lüjiao, and again at Jingwang, all within the lake. As the Mid-Autumn Festival approached, the sky was clear and the air crisp. The scene was as described: the long smoke had dissipated, a bright moon shone for thousands of miles, the floating light shimmered like gold, and the still reflection lay like a jade disc. I witnessed this serene beauty. Traveling north to Balin, Yueyang Tower stood on the city wall, but it no longer exists. I ascended its site and looked out, seeing Junshan Mountain standing prominently, to the east of which was Bian Mountain, and further east, Jiuqi Mountain, all within the lake. To the south of the city was Baihe Mountain, with Tianyue Ridge beside it, atop which stood Lüxianting Pavilion, and in front of the pavilion was the temple of Yue Wumu. In the past, Yue Wumu, with his steadfast loyalty, subdued Yang Yao in Dongting Lake within eight days. The local people were grateful and worshiped him. The temple was grand and majestic, situated in a beautiful location by the lake and mountains. Yueyang was a place visited three times by the Taoist sage Lü Dongbin. During the Song Dynasty, Teng Zijing rebuilt the tower, Fan Zhongyan wrote a record for it, Su Shunqin inscribed it, and Shao Song engraved the title in seal script. In its heyday, it was a place where immortals came and went, and where scholars and officials sang and composed poetry. Now, it is all overgrown with weeds and wild grass, reduced to ruins. Even the literati cannot help but lament, and even Lü Dongbin, with his divine arts, could not retain what he loved. Yue Wumu, though captured like a pheasant in a net, remains in the hearts of people due to his loyalty and righteousness, and his legacy alone endures. Thus, it is indeed true that people do not truly die, for they have their own way of being remembered.

    645

    在巴陵阻风五日，所谓阴风怒号，浊浪排空，薄暮冥冥，虎啸猿啼者，吾又见之焉。北出泾河口，入岷江。西北一望，荆襄汉沔，沃野千里。似燕赵两河之间，洋洋乎大国之风也。江南岸为临湘、嘉鱼、蒲圻之境，连延皆山。赤壁在嘉鱼，雄峙江浒，其上有“祭风台”。昔苏子瞻赋赤壁于黄州，武昌之下游也。考之史云：“刘备居樊口，进兵逆操，遇于赤壁。”则当在武昌上游。又操败后走华容，今嘉鱼与华容近，而黄州绝远，然则周郎赤壁，断在嘉鱼无疑也。

    Explanation

    645

    For five days, I was held back by a storm in Baling, where the fierce, cold wind howled, turbulent waves surged towards the sky, twilight grew dim, and the sounds of tigers roaring and apes crying filled the air—experiences I once again encountered. Traveling north from the mouth of the Jing River, I entered the Min River. Looking northwest, I saw the fertile plains of Jing, Xiang, Han, and Mian stretching for a thousand miles, reminiscent of the grand and prosperous style of the great nations of Yan and Zhao. The southern bank of the river is the territory of Linxiang, Jia Yu, and Puqi, all of which are surrounded by mountains. The Red Cliffs are located in Jia Yu, majestically standing by the riverbank, with a "Wind Sacrifice Platform" atop them. In the past, Su Shi (Su Dongpo) wrote about the Red Cliffs in Huangzhou, which is downstream from Wu Chang. According to historical records, "Liu Bei set up camp at Fan Kou, advanced against Cao Cao, and met him at the Red Cliffs." Therefore, the Red Cliffs must have been upstream from Wu Chang. After Cao Cao's defeat, he retreated through Huayang, which is close to Jia Yu but far from Huangzhou. Thus, it is certain that the Red Cliffs of Zhou Yu are in Jia Yu.

    646

    北至荆口，两山对峙，东曰惊矶，西曰大军。惊矶有达摩亭，乃拆苇渡江之所。北曰沔口，沔水又名沧浪，灵均遇渔父于此。沔口之北，西曰汉口，汉阳府也；东曰夏口，武昌府也。墉山为城，堑江为池。武昌城内包三山，汉阳城内有两湖。黄鹤楼号晴川阁，距两城之上，相望也。汉阳城外有大别山，下有锁穴，乃孙吴锁江之处。予尝登大别之颠以望三楚，荆衡连镇，江汉朝宗，远水动蜀，高树浮秦。水陆之冲，舟车辐輳，百货所聚，商贾云屯。其山川之雄壮，民物之繁华，南北两京而外，无过于此。然沱、潜，汉、沔之间，潇、湘、沅、澧之际，江漂湖汇，民多水患，盗贼乘之。楚俗慓轻，鲜思积聚，山薮水洳，流民鸠处，其人率啙窳，庞杂而难治，亦可虑也。

    Explanation

    646

    North to Jingkou, two mountains stand facing each other, the eastern one called Jingji and the western one called Dajun. At Jingji, there is the Damao Pavilion, where Bodhidharma is said to have crossed the river by tearing apart reeds. To the north is Miankou, where the Mian River is also known as the Canglang River, and where Qu Yuan encountered a fisherman. To the north of Miankou, the western side is called Hankou, which is the administrative region of Hanyang, and the eastern side is called Xia Kou, which is the administrative region of Wuchang. The city is fortified by hills that serve as walls and the river that acts as a moat. Wuchang City encompasses three mountains, and Hanyang City has two lakes. The Yellow Crane Tower, also known as the Qingchuan Pavilion, is located on the heights above the two cities, where they can see each other across the river. Outside of Hanyang City, there is Dabie Mountain, with a strategic location known as the lock point, which was historically controlled by the forces of Sun Wu. I have climbed to the top of Dabie Mountain to view the Three Provinces of Chu, where the towns of Jing and Xiang are interconnected, and the rivers Jang and Han flow towards the sea. The distant waters stir the land of Shu, and tall trees seem to float over Qin. This is a strategic transportation hub where boats and carriages converge, a bustling commercial center where goods and merchants gather. The majestic mountains and rivers, and the prosperous people and goods, make this region unparalleled outside of the two capitals of Beijing and Nanjing. However, between the Tuo, Qian, Han, and Mian rivers, and near the Xiang, Xiang, Yuan, and Li rivers, the rivers and lakes are interconnected, and the frequent flooding affects the population, providing opportunities for bandits. The customs of Chu are described as light and impetuous, rarely thinking about accumulating wealth. In the mountainous and marshy areas, refugees gather, and the people are generally lazy and unproductive, making the population diverse and difficult to govern, which is also a concern.

    647

    北入孝感应山，山接九宗，泽连云梦，峰高野阔，气势沉雄。北出武胜关，崇山峻岭，连延千里，右列方城，左拥穆陵，所谓“冥扼之塞”。《淮南子》云“山有九塞”，此其一也。北至于信阳，信阳古申国，东邻息。申、息者，楚之北门也。又东邻蔡，昔桓公侵蔡，蔡溃，遂伐楚，非上策也。由蔡至郢，崇山大小不可胜计，所谓“方城为城，汉水为池，无所用众”，非虚语也。能伐楚者莫如秦，出武关，下汉川，则撤荆襄之藩篱；出三峡，下夷陵，则扼鄂岳之要害。故秦并六国，亦地势然也。

    Explanation

    647

    Entering the Shaoyin Mountains in the north, the mountains connect with the Nine Ancestors, the marshes link with Yunmeng, the peaks are high and the wilderness vast, the atmosphere majestic and powerful. Exiting the Wusheng Pass in the north, tall mountains and steep ridges stretch for thousands of miles, on the right side there is Fangcheng, on the left side it is embraced by Mulingshan, which is known as a strategic pass. The Huainanzi states that "there are nine passes in the mountains," this being one of them. Reaching Xinyang in the north, Xinyang was the ancient State of Shen, east of it is Xi. Shen and Xi are the northern gates of Chu. Further east is Cai, in the past, Duke Huan of Qi invaded Cai, which collapsed, and then attacked Chu, which was not a wise strategy. From Cai to Ying, the mountains are numerous and vast, it is said that "Fangcheng is like a city, and the Han River is like a moat, making it unnecessary to use a large army," this is not an empty statement. No one can attack Chu better than Qin, by exiting the Wusheng Pass and descending into the Han River valley, one can dismantle the defenses of Jing and Xiang; by exiting the Three Gorges and descending into Yiling, one can control the vital points of E and Yue. Therefore, Qin's conquest of the six states was also due to the geographical advantages.

    648

    北过确山，至遂平，有楂桠山。唐李观及吴元济战于此。北至西平，有滍水，昔光武败王寻于昆阳，多杀士卒，滍水不流，即此也。北至于叶县，为沈诸梁之封邑。其北有黄城山，下有沮溺故里，子路问津处也。北渡汝水，至襄城，其南有首山。汝、蔡、颖、许之际，平畴沃衍，而首山雄峙其中。史称天下名山八，三在夷狄，五在中国，皆黄帝所尝游，首山其一也。昔黄帝问道于崆峒，遂游襄城，登具茨，访大隗。崆峒在郏鄏，而具茨在新郑，与首山相望也。襄城郑汜地，周襄王出居于此。

    Explanation

    648

    Passing north of Que Mountain, one reaches Suiping, where there is Chaya Mountain. During the Tang Dynasty, Li Guan and Wu Yuanji fought a battle here. Continuing north to Xiping, there is the River Zhui. In the past, Emperor Guangwu of Han defeated Wang Xun at Kunyang, resulting in the death of many soldiers, causing the River Zhui to stop flowing, which is the location referred to here. North of Ye County, it was the fief of Shen Zhuangliang. To the north of this, there is Huangcheng Mountain, below which lies the ancestral home of Ju Ni, the place where Zilu asked for directions. Crossing the River Ru to the north, one arrives at Xiangcheng, where to the south there is Mount Shou. Between Ru, Cai, Ying, and Xu, there are vast and fertile fields, with Mount Shou majestically standing among them. Historians say that there are eight famous mountains in the world, three of which are in the barbarian lands and five in China, all of which the Yellow Emperor once visited, and Mount Shou is one of them. In the past, the Yellow Emperor sought wisdom from Mount Kongtong, then traveled to Xiangcheng, climbed Mount Juzi, and visited Da Wei. Mount Kongtong is located in Jiafu, while Mount Juzi is in Xinzheng, facing each other across from Mount Shou. Xiangcheng is the land of Zheng and Fan, where King Xiang of Zhou lived outside the capital.

    649

    西至禹州，大禹之封邑，北至告城，古阳城地也。临颍水，面箕山，负嵩岳，左成皋，右伊阙，崇山四塞，清流漾洄。其高平处，有周公“测影台”，巨石屹立，高可七尺，下方五尺，上方三尺。《周礼?大司徒》以土圭之法测土深、正日影，以求地中。日南影短，日北影长，日至之影，尺有五寸，即此也。北至登封，介嵩山太、少二室之间，太室之颠，栉比若城垣；少室之峰，直起若台观。

    Explanation

    649

    West to Yuzhou, the fiefdom of the great Yu, north to Gaocheng, the ancient land of Yangcheng. Facing Yingshui River, facing Jishan Mountain, with Songshan behind it, to the left is Chenggao, and to the right is Yique. Surrounded by towering mountains, clear streams meander. On the high and flat ground, there is King Zhou Gong's 'Shadodometer Platform.' A huge stone stands upright, about seven chi high, with a base five chi wide and an upper part three chi wide. According to The Rites of Zhou: Grand Master of the Soil, using the method of the tugui to measure the depth of the earth and correct the shadow of the sun to find the center of the land. The shadow is shorter in the south and longer in the north. The shadow on the day of the solstice is fifteen inches long, which is this platform. North to Dengfeng, between the two peaks of Tai and Xiao of Songshan. The top of Tai Peak is like a city wall, and the peak of Xiao Peak rises straight up like a pavilion.

    650

    虽无岱宗衡华之高奇，而气象雍容，神彩秀朗，有如王者宅居中正，端冕垂绅，以朝万国，不大声色，而德意自远。中岳庙在太室之南，少林寺在少室之北。群峰围绕，界隔尘寰，水石清幽，灵区独辟。时值深秋，白云红叶，翠柏黄花，点缀宕岫，天然图画。岳阳、黄鹤，极江湖之浩渺；灵隐、少林，尽山岳之奇丽。睡常入梦，醒犹在目，非笔舌所能传也！在寺中问达摩遗迹，僧云：“寺西四五里深山之中，有古石洞，乃『九年面壁』之处。至今洞中犹有『达摩影』。”而予未见也。

    Explanation

    650

    Though lacking the grandeur and uniqueness of Mount Tai, Mount Heng, and Mount Hua, the atmosphere is dignified and serene, and the spirit is bright and clear, much like a king residing in the central and just position, wearing a ceremonial cap and a sash, receiving the nations, without loud and showy means, but the moral influence naturally extends far. The Central Mountain Temple is located to the south of the Great Hall of the Central Mountain, and the Shaolin Temple is to the north of the Lesser Hall of the Lesser Mountain. Surrounded by peaks, it is separated from the mundane world, with clear and secluded water and stones, a unique and sacred area. During the deep autumn, white clouds, red leaves, green pines, and yellow flowers adorn the mountain peaks, a natural painting. Yueyang Tower and Yellow Crane Tower, known for their views of the vastness of lakes and rivers; Lingyin Temple and Shaolin Temple, known for their scenic beauty and unique features. These scenes often appear in dreams and remain vivid even when awake, and cannot be fully described by words! Inquiring about the traces of Bodhidharma in the temple, the monk said: "A few miles west of the temple, in a deep mountain, there is an ancient stone cave, where Bodhidharma meditated facing a wall for nine years. To this day, the cave still has the 'shadow of Bodhidharma.'" But I have not seen it.

    651

    出嵩山，渡洛水，至偃师，道中见田横、许远之墓。北有缑山，子晋升仙之所也。北上北邙，望见洛阳，昔孟坚《两都》、平子《二京》诸赋，道洛阳之形胜甚悉，而予未暇观，至今犹耿耿焉。由孟津渡河至孟县。孟县者，河阳也，周襄王狩于此。北渡沁水，上太行，太行之上首起河内，尾抵蓟辽，碣石、恒山、析城、王屋，皆太行也。修坂造云，崇冈碍日。路皆青石，镜光油滑，实天下之至险。登太行而四望，九州之区，可以历指：秦、晋蔽山，吴、越阻水，青、齐负海，燕、赵沿边，中原平土，正在三河。周、鲁、宋、卫、陈、郑、蔡、许、邓、宿、杞、邾、沈、虞、邢、虢，《春秋》所书诸国，以及夏、殷、东汉、北宋、五代梁、唐之故都，皆在于此。总挽九州，阃阈华夏，土田肥美，物产茂实，所谓天下之中也，地之腹也，阴阳之所会、风雨之所和也。过太行而北，则吾山西境矣。

    651

    <translation> Exiting Mount Song, crossing the Luo River, and arriving at Yanshi, I saw the tombs of Tianhong and Xu Yuan along the way. To the north lies Gou Mountain, where Zi Jing ascended to immortality. Ascending Beimang Mountain to the north, I saw Luoyang. In the past, Ban Gu's *Two Capitals* and Zhang Heng's *Two Capitals* essays described the strategic advantages of Luoyang in great detail, but I have not had the chance to see them for myself, and this regret still lingers. Crossing the Yellow River via Mengjin, I arrived at Meng County. Meng County is also known as He Yang, where King Xiang of Zhou hunted. Crossing the Qin River and ascending Taihang Mountain, which rises in Henan and extends to Jizhou and Liaodong, with Jieshi, Hengshan, Xicheng, and Wangwu all part of Taihang. The roads are paved with polished green stones, smooth and reflective, truly the most dangerous path in the world. Climbing Taihang and looking out in all directions, the nine regions can be clearly identified: Qin and Jin are shielded by mountains, Wu and Yue are blocked by water, Qing and Qi are backed by the sea, Yan and Zhao are along the border, the Central Plains are flat land, located in the Three Rivers. The ancient states of Zhou, Lu, Song, Wei, Chen, Zheng, Cai, Xu, Deng, Su, Qi, Zhu, Shen, Yu, Xing, and Guo, as recorded in the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, as well as the ancient capitals of the Xia, Shang, Eastern Han, Northern Song, and the Five Dynasties' Liang and Tang, all lie within this region. Gathering the nine regions, this is the threshold of Huaxia, with fertile soil and abundant produce, it is said to be the heart of the world, the core of the land, where yin and yang meet and wind and rain harmonize. Crossing Taihang and going north, then it is the northern border of Shanxi. </translation>

    652

    总而计之，天下大势，水归二漕，山分三干，河出昆仑，江源岷蜀，始于西极，入于东溟。大河以北，水皆南流；大江以南，水皆北注。汉南入江，淮北入河，虽名“四渎”，犹之二也。太行九边，西接玉门，东抵朝鲜，是为北干。五岭、衡、巫，西接峨嵋，东抵会稽，是为南干。岷、嶓、华、嵩，是为中干。岱宗特起，不与嵩连，亦中干也。北方水位，故燕、秦、三晋之山，色黑而陂陀若波。东方木也，故齐、鲁、吴、越之山，色青而森秀若林。楚南、闽粤，峰尖而土赤；粤西、黔、蜀，石白而形方。天有五行，五方应之。江性宽缓，河流湍急，焦白鄱红，洞庭澄清，其大较也。

    Explanation

    652

    In summary, the great trends of the world show that water flows into two main canals, and mountains divide into three trunks. The Yellow River originates from the Kunlun Mountains, and the Yangtze River has its source in the Min and Sichuan regions, beginning at the western extremity and ending in the eastern sea. North of the Yellow River, water flows south; south of the Yangtze River, water flows north. The Han River flows into the Yangtze, and the Huai River flows into the Yellow River, although named as the "Four Rivers," they are still fundamentally two. The Taihang Mountains extend to the nine borders, west to Yumen, and east to Korea, forming the northern trunk. The Five Ranges, Heng, and Wuxia, west to Emei, and east to Kuaiji, form the southern trunk. The Min, Ba, Huai, and Song mountains form the central trunk. Mount Tai rises uniquely, not connected to Mount Song, and is also part of the central trunk. In the north, where water prevails, the mountains of Yan, Qin, and the three Jins are black and undulating like waves. In the east, where wood prevails, the mountains of Qi, Lu, Wu, and Yue are green and lush like forests. In the south of Chu, Fujian, and Guangdong, the peaks are sharp, and the soil is red. In the west of Guangdong, Guizhou, and Sichuan, the stones are white and square in shape. Heaven has the five elements, and the five directions correspond to them. The Yangtze has a broad and slow nature, while the rivers have swift currents. Jiaoluo is white, Poyang is red, and Dongting is clear, these being the general characteristics.

    653

    斯行也，四海滨其三，九州历其七，五岳睹其四，四渎见其全。帝王之所都，圣贤之所处，通都大邑，民物之所聚；山川险塞，英雄之所争；古迹名胜，文人学士之所歌咏，多见之焉。独所谓魁奇磊落、潜修独行之士，或伏处山颠水湄，溷迹渔樵负販之中，而予概未之见。岂造物者未之生耶？抑吾未之遇耶？抑虽遇之而不识耶？吾憾焉！然苟吾心之善取，则于山见仁者之静，于水见智者之动；其突兀汹涌，如睹勇士之叱咤；其沦涟娟秀，如睹淑人君子之温文也。然则谓吾日遇其人焉，可也。

    Explanation

    653

    This journey encompassed three of the four seas, seven of the nine regions, four of the five sacred mountains, and all four major rivers. It passed through the capitals of emperors and kings, the abodes of sages and virtuous men, major cities and towns where people and goods gathered; rugged mountains and rivers, natural barriers where heroes contended; ancient sites and famous scenic spots that poets and scholars have praised, many of which were seen. However, those outstanding and noble individuals who practice their virtues in seclusion and act independently, sometimes hiding in the high mountains and watersides, blending in with fishermen, woodcutters, and peddlers, I have not encountered any of them. Could it be that such people have not been created by nature? Or could it be that I have not encountered them? Or could it be that I have met them but failed to recognize them? I feel regretful! However, if my heart is good at discernment, then I can see the tranquility of the benevolent in the mountains and the dynamism of the wise in the water; the sudden and turbulent waves, like seeing a brave warrior's shout; the gentle and graceful ripples, like seeing the gentle demeanor of a virtuous gentleman. Therefore, it can be said that I encounter such people every day.

    654

    抑又思之，天地之化，阴阳而已，独阴不生，独阳不成，故大漠之北不毛，而交、广以南多水。文明发生，独此震旦之区而已。北走胡而南走越，三月而可至；昆仑至东海，半年之程耳。由此言之，大块亦甚小也。吾以二月出都，河北之地，草芽未生，至吴而花开，至越而花落，入楚而栽秧，至粵而食稻。粤西返棹，秋老天高，至河南而木叶尽脱，归山右而雨雪载涂。转盼之间，四序还周。由此言之，古今亦甚暂也！心不自得，而求适于外，故风景胜而生乐。性不自定，而寄生于形，故时物过而生悲。

    Explanation

    654

    But further consider this: the transformations of heaven and earth are governed by yin and yang alone. Yin alone does not give birth, and yang alone does not complete. Therefore, the northern deserts are barren, while the southern regions of Jiao and Guang are rich in water. Civilization has arisen uniquely in the land of Zhendan. Traveling north to the Hu and south to Yue, one can reach their destinations in three months; the journey from Kunlun to the East Sea takes half a year. From this, we can say that the world is also quite small. In February, I left the capital, and in the northern regions of Hebei, the grass had not yet sprouted. By the time I reached Wu, the flowers were blooming, and in Yue, they had already fallen. In Chu, I planted rice, and in Guangdong, I ate it. Returning westward to Yue, the autumn was old and the sky high, and in Henan, the leaves had all fallen. Returning to Shanxi, the roads were covered in rain and snow. In the blink of an eye, the four seasons complete their cycle. From this, we can say that both the past and present are fleeting. When the mind is not content within itself, it seeks satisfaction through external means, and thus beautiful scenery gives rise to joy. When the mind is unsettled, it finds its expression through the body, and thus the passing of time and the changing of seasons can evoke sadness.

    655

    乐宁有几，而悲无穷期焉！吾疑吾之自立于天地者无具也。宋景濂曰：“古之人如曾参、原宪，终身陋室，蓬蒿没户，而志竟充然，有若囊括于天地者，何也？毋亦有得于山水之外者乎？”孟子曰：“万物皆备于我矣。”老子曰：“不出户，知天下。”非虚言也。为地所囿，斯山川有畛域；为形所拘，斯见闻有阻碍。果其心与物化，而性与天通，则天地之所以高深，人物之所以荣悴，山河之所以流峙，有若烛照而数计焉！生风云于胸臆，呈海岳于窗几，不必耳接之而后闻、目触之而后见也。然则自兹以往，吾可以不游矣。然而吾乃无时不游也已。

    Explanation

    655

    Joy is finite, while sorrow is endless. I doubt that I have the means to stand independently in the world. Song Jinglian said, "Ancient sages like Zeng Shen and Yuan Xian lived in humble dwellings, with weeds growing up to the door, yet their spirits were vast and encompassed the world. Why is this so? Is it not because they found something beyond natural scenery that enriched their spirits?" Mencius said, "All things are ready in me." Laozi said, "Without leaving your door, you can know all the ways of the world." These are not empty words. Being confined by physical space limits one's perception of the world, and being hindered by physical limitations obstructs one's experiences and perceptions. If the mind and nature are in harmony with the external world, then the vastness of the world and the changes in life and nature can be perceived as clearly as counting with a candle. The inner world can be as vast and expansive as the sea and mountains, without needing direct sensory input. Therefore, from now on, I can no longer need to physically travel. However, I am always in a state of inner exploration.

    656

    [张山来曰：浩浩落落，万有一千余言。就其登涉所至，随笔点染铺叙，绮丽芊锦，亦复激昂慷慨，适足以囊括宇宙，开拓心胸。真千古奇文，至文妙文，不得仅赏其模山范水已也！]
    　　
    　　
    　　虞初新志卷十八
    　　圣师录　仁和王言慎旃
    　　　　子舆氏言：人之所以异于禽兽，以其存心。而禽兽之中，乃有麒麟凤凰，不践生草，不食生虫，酋耳但食残暴之虎，獬豸唯触不直之人；鸟能反哺，羊有跽乳，其存心皆可以为朝廷旌仁孝而扬德威。他如蟹至期而输稻，蜂轮值而卫王，唐明皇之象不肯为禄山作舞，昭宗之猿不肯为朱温起居，宋少帝之白鹇殉帝于海：是物知有君臣也。莺哀其子而肠断，猿抱母皮而死：是物知有父子也。平章之鸽，死殉其雄；郡佐之鹅，克和其配；汾水之旁有雁丘，盐城之湖有烈鸳：是物知有夫妇也。横空之鹤，代鹊杀蛇；北平王氏之猫，能哺他子：是物知有同类也。

    Explanation

    656

    Zhang Shanlai said: Vast and grand, this work contains over ten thousand words. Following the author's journey, it is written with vivid descriptions and elaborate narratives, both beautiful and stirring, fully capable of encompassing the universe and broadening the reader's horizons. Truly a timeless masterpiece, the highest and most exquisite form of writing, one should not merely appreciate its detailed descriptions of landscapes but also recognize its deeper significance and broader impact!

    Suyuchu Xinji, Volume Eighteen
    Shengshi Lu, by Wang Yan Shen Zhan from Renhe
    Zi Yu said: What distinguishes humans from animals is their intentions. Among animals, there are the mythical creatures like the qilin and phoenix, who do not tread on living grass or eat living insects, while the chui er only eats the flesh of tigers that have committed violence, and the xiezhi only touches those who are unjust. Birds can feed their parents in return, and sheep kneel to nurse, all of which can be used by the court to commend filial piety and righteousness. For example, crabs deliver rice on time, bees take turns guarding the king, the elephant of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang would not dance for An Lushan, the monkey of Emperor Zhaozong of Tang would not serve Zhu Wen, and the white pheasant of Emperor Zhao Bing of Song died with the emperor in the sea. These animals know of the relationship between ruler and subject. Swallows mourn their young to the point of breaking their hearts, and monkeys hold their mother's skin until death. These animals know of the relationship between parent and child. The dove of Pingzhang died to protect its mate, the goose of Junzuo harmonized with its partner, and by the Fen River there is a mound of geese, and in the salt lake of Yancheng there are pairs of mandarin ducks. These animals know of the relationship between husband and wife. The crane in the sky killed a snake to protect a sparrow, and the cat of the Wang family in Beiping could nurse another cat's kittens. These animals know of the relationship between their own kind.

    657

    陇山之鹦鹉思上皇，襄阳之燕殉王女，孙中舍之犬负米，姚生之马鸣冤，陈州之鹤伴老，鹤州之骡逸归：是物知忠于所事也。熊分果以饷堕坎之人，虎弭耳而舍抱哭之母，猓然性爱其类，杀其一而致百亡；鱼伤鬐触之儿，身亦触石而死：是物知有仁义也。翁媪之猴，日守待葬；侯家之鹿，断角以殉，至放生之鳖、释命之鸡，俱能图报救死之德：是物知感恩也。洪店奔牛，悲鸣而诉王臻之诬杀；夹道蝌蚪，昂首而诉商仆之戕生：是物知贤守令也。然则物何异于人哉？微独无异，抑恐世之不若者众矣！家公向欲汇集一帙，为《圣师录》，本诸扬子“圣人师万物”句，因病不果。予小子闲阅往籍，窃取其义而识之。博物君子，得无责其不备耶。

    Explanation

    657

    The parrot from Long Mountain longs for the former emperor, the swallow from Xiangyang devotes itself to the princess, the dog of Sun Zhongshè carries grain, the horse of Yao Shēng cries out for justice, the crane from Chen Prefecture accompanies the elderly, and the mule from He Prefecture returns home: these animals know loyalty to their duties. A bear shares fruit with someone who has fallen into a pit, a tiger lowers its ears and allows a crying mother to pass, a wild animal loves its kind, and killing one leads to the death of many; a fish dies after being touched by a child, and it too touches a stone and dies: these animals know righteousness. A monkey guards the burial site of elderly people, a deer breaks its antlers to die, and even a released turtle and a freed chicken can repay the kindness of saving their lives: these animals know gratitude. A cow runs to a store, crying out against false accusations, and tadpoles in a stream raise their heads to protest against the killing of life: these animals know the virtues of good officials. Therefore, how do animals differ from humans? They are not different at all, and perhaps there are many in the world who are not as virtuous! My respected teacher once intended to compile a volume titled "The Record of the Holy Teacher," based on Yang Xiong's phrase "The sage is the teacher of all things," but was unable to do so due to illness. I, a humble scholar, have leisurely read through ancient texts and secretly taken their meanings to record them. Learned gentlemen, would you not blame me for not being thorough?

    658

    白鹇
    　　　　崖山之败，陆秀夫抱祥兴帝，与俱赴水。时御舟一白鹇，奋击哀鸣，与笼坠水中死。
    　　　　鹤
    　　　　陈州倅卢某，蓄二鹤，甚驯。一创死，一哀鸣不食。卢勉饲之，乃就食。一日鸣绕卢侧，卢曰：“尔欲去，不尔羁也！”鹤振翮云际，数四徊翔乃去。卢老病无子，后三年，归卧黄蒲溪上。晚秋萧索，曳杖林间。忽有一鹤盘空，鸣声凄断。卢仰祝曰：“若非我陈州侣耶？果尔，即当下。”鹤竟投入怀中，以喙牵衣，旋舞不释。遂引之归。后卢殁，鹤亦不食死，家人瘗之墓左。

    Explanation

    658

    White Crane
    At the Battle of Yamen, Lu Xiufu carried Emperor Bing into the sea, and both committed suicide. At the time, there was a white crane on the imperial boat that fought and cried out in distress, falling with its cage into the water and dying.

    Crane
    A deputy official in Cen Prefecture, Lu, cultivated two cranes, which were very tame. One died from an injury, and the other refused to eat, mourning its loss. Lu encouraged the remaining crane to eat, and it eventually did so. One day, the crane circled around Lu, and he said, "If you want to go, I will not hold you back." The crane flapped its wings and soared into the clouds, circling several times before leaving. Lu was old, sick, and without children. Three years later, he returned to live on the banks of the Huangpu Creek. In late autumn, the atmosphere was desolate, and he walked in the woods with a cane. Suddenly, a crane appeared, circling in the sky and crying out in a heart-wrenching manner. Lu looked up and said, "Is this not my companion from Cen Prefecture? If so, then come down." The crane used its beak to pull at Lu's clothing and danced around him without letting go. Lu led the crane back home. After Lu's death, the crane also stopped eating and died. His family buried it next to his tomb.

    659

    雁
    　　　　元裕之好问，于金泰和乙丑，赴试并州。道逢捕雁者捕得二雁，一死，一脱网去。其脱网者，空中盘旋哀鸣，亦投地而死。裕之以金赎得二雁，瘗汾水，垒石力识，号曰“雁丘”。
    　　　　顾敬亭稼圃傍，有罗者得一雁，铩其羽，絷其足，立之汀畔以为媒。每见云中飞者，必昂首仰视。一日，其偶者见而下之，特然如土委地，交颈哀鸣，血尽而死。
    　　　　正德间，有张姓者，获一雁，置于中庭。明年有雁自天鸣，庭雁和之。久而天雁自下，彼此以头绞死于楼前。因名楼曰“双雁楼”。
    　　　　王一槐教谕铜陵，有民舍除夜燎烟，辟除不祥。一雁偶为烟触而下，其家以为不祥也，烹之。明日，一雁飞鸣屋顶，数日，亦堕而死。
    　　　　燕

    Explanation

    659

    In the year 1205 during the Tahe period of the Jin dynasty, Yuan Yuzhi Hao Wen, a scholar and poet, traveled to Pingzhou to take the exams. On his way, he encountered a goose hunter who had captured two geese. One of the geese died, while the other managed to escape from the net. The escaped goose circled in the air, crying mournfully, and eventually fell to the ground, where it died. Moved by this, Yuan Yuzhi Hao Wen redeemed the two geese with money, buried them by the Fen River, and marked the place with stones, naming it "Eagle Hill."

    Near the fields around the Conscientious Pavilion, a man set up a trap and captured a goose. He cut off its feathers, bound its feet, and stood it on the shore as a decoy. The trapped goose would look up at the geese flying in the sky. One day, its mate saw it and fell to the ground, where they both died after crying mournfully.

    During the Zhengde era (1506-1521) of the Ming dynasty, a man surnamed Zhang captured a goose and placed it in the courtyard. The next year, a goose flew down from the sky and called, and the goose in the courtyard responded. Eventually, the two geese died by strangling each other at the foot of the building. Therefore, the building was named "Double Goose Tower."

    Wang Yihua, a teacher in Tongling, lived in a household that burned incense at the end of the year to ward off evil. One goose was accidentally hit by the smoke and fell. The family considered it an omen and cooked it. The next day, another goose flew and called on the roof. After several days, it also fell and died.

    As for the swallows, there is no specific account provided in the source text.

    660

    襄阳卫敬瑜早丧。其妻，霸陵王整妹也，年十六，父母舅姑咸欲嫁之，誓不许，截耳置盘中为誓，乃止。户有燕巢，常双来去，后忽孤飞。女感之，谓曰：“能如我乎？”因以缕志其足。明年复来，孤飞如故，犹带前缕。女作诗曰：“昔年无偶去，今春犹独归。故人思既重，不肯复双飞。”自尔春来秋去，凡六七年。后复来，女已死，燕绕舍哀鸣。人告之葬处，即飞就墓，哀鸣不食而死。人因瘗之于旁，号曰“燕冢”。
    　　　　元贞二年，燕人柳汤佐家，双燕巢梁。一夕，家人持火照蝎，其雄惊坠，猫食之。雌朝夕悲鸣，哺雏成翼而去。明年，雌独来。人视巢中有二卵，疑其更偶，徐伺之，则二壳耳。春秋来去，凡六载皆然。
    　　　　夏氏子见梁间双燕，戏弹之，其雄死，雌者悲鸣逾时，自投于河，亦死。时人作《烈燕歌》。
    　　　　郁七家有燕将雏，巢久忽毁。邻燕成群衔泥，去来如织，顷刻巢复成。明日遂育数雏巢中，乃知事急燕来助力者。

    Explanation

    660

    Xiangyang's Wei Jingyu died at a young age. His wife, the sister of Wang Zheng from Baling, was sixteen years old. Her parents, uncles, and aunts all wanted to marry her off, but she vowed never to remarry. She cut off her ear and placed it in a dish as a pledge, and they stopped trying to force her to remarry. There was a swallow's nest in the house, and the swallows would usually fly back and forth together. Later, one of them flew alone. The woman was moved by this and said to it, "Can you be like me?" She then marked its foot with a thread. The next year, it returned alone, still carrying the thread. The woman wrote a poem: "Last year, you left without a mate; this spring, you still return alone. The memory of your mate is so deep that you will not fly with another." From then on, the swallows would come and go every spring and autumn for six or seven years. When they returned again, the woman had already died. The swallows circled the house, crying mournfully. People told them where she was buried, and they flew to the grave, crying and refusing to eat, until they died. People then buried them beside her, and called it the "Swallow Grave."

    In the second year of the Yuancheng era, in the home of Liu Tangzuo from Yan, a pair of swallows built a nest on a beam. One night, the family lit a fire to see a scorpion, and the male swallow was startled and fell, where it was eaten by a cat. The female swallow mourned day and night, fed the chicks until they could fly, and then left. The next year, the female swallow returned alone. People saw two eggs in the nest and wondered if she had found another mate. They watched closely and found that they were just empty shells. For six years, the swallows came and went alone each spring and autumn.

    A boy from the Xia family saw a pair of swallows on a beam and playfully shot at them. The male swallow died, and the female swallow mourned for a long time before throwing herself into the river and dying. At the time, people composed a song called "The Loyal Swallows."

    In the home of Yu Qi, a pair of swallows were raising their chicks. The nest, which had been intact for a long time, suddenly collapsed. Neighboring swallows gathered and carried mud, coming and going continuously, and in a short while, the nest was rebuilt. The next day, they raised several chicks in the nest, showing that when things are urgent, swallows come to help.

    661

    鹦鹉
    　　　　宋高宗时，陇山人进能言鹦鹉，高宗养之宫中。一日问曰：“尔思乡否？”曰：“岂不思尔？思之何益？”帝遣中贵送还陇山。数年之后，使过其地。鹦鹉问曰：“上皇安否？”曰：“崩矣！”鹦鹉悲鸣不已。
    　　　　关中商人，得能言鹦鹉于陇山，爱而食之甚勤。偶事下狱，归时叹恨不已。鹦鹉曰：“郎在狱数日，已不堪；鹦鹉遭闭累年，奈何？”商感而放之。后商同辈有过陇山者，鹦鹉必于林间问曰：“郎无恙否？幸寄声，幸寄声！”
    　　　　李迈庵自记：自滇游回，有仆染瘴而死。仆携有二鹦鹉，流泪三日不休，亦死。
    　　　　鹳
    　　　　高邮有鹳，双栖于南楼之上。或弋其雌，雄独孤栖。旬余，有鹳一班，偕一雄与共巢，若媒诱之者。然竟日弗偶，遂皆飞去。孤者哀鸣不已，忽钻嘴入巢隙，悬足而死。时游者群客见之，无不嗟讶，称为“烈鹳”，而竞为诗歌吊之，复有“烈鹳碑”。

    Explanation

    661

    Parrots

    During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, a man from Longshan presented a parrot that could speak. The emperor kept it in the palace. One day, he asked the parrot, "Do you miss your hometown?" The parrot replied, "How could I not miss you? What good does thinking about it do?" The emperor then sent a high-ranking eunuch to return the parrot to Longshan. After several years, when a messenger passed through the area, the parrot asked, "Is the former emperor well?" The messenger replied, "He has passed away." The parrot mourned incessantly.

    A merchant from Guanzhong obtained a parrot that could speak from Longshan. He loved and fed it very diligently. Once, the merchant was imprisoned. When he returned, he lamented endlessly. The parrot said, "Master, being in prison for a few days is already unbearable. What can a parrot do, being confined for years?" Moved by this, the merchant released the parrot. Later, when a fellow merchant passed through Longshan, the parrot would always ask in the forest, "Is Master well? Please convey a message, please convey a message!"

    Li Maian recorded it himself: After returning from a journey to Yunnan, a servant of his died of malaria. The servant had two parrots, which cried for three days without stopping and eventually died as well.

    Storks

    In Gaoyou, there was a stork that nested on the south tower. Someone hunted its female, leaving the male alone. After about ten days, a group of storks arrived with one male to share the nest, as if courting it. But they did not succeed all day and all flew away. The lone stork mourned incessantly. Suddenly, it thrust its beak into the nest crevice and hung its feet, dying. At that time, travelers and guests saw it and were all astonished. They called it the loyal stork and composed poetry in mourning for it, and there was even a stele inscribed in its honor.

    662

    卫衙梓巢鹳，父死于弩。顷之，众拥一雄来，匹其母。母哀鸣百拒之。雄却尽啄杀其四雏。母益哀顿以死。群凶乃挟其雄逸去。
    　　　　某氏园亭中，有古树，鹊巢其上，伏卵将雏。一日，二鹊徊翔屋上，悲鸣不已。顷之，有数鹊相向，鸣渐益近，百首皆向巢。忽数鹊对喙鸣，若相语状，飏去。少顷，一鹳横空来，阁阁有声，鹊亦尾其后。群鹊向而噪，若有所诉。鹳复作声，若允所请。瞥而上，捣巢，衔一赤蛇吞之。群鹊喧舞，若庆且谢者。盖鹊招鹳搏蛇相救也。
    　　　　华亭董氏，庭前有虬松一株，枝干扶疏，亭亭如盖，有双鹳结巢其颠。后雄被弹死，其雌孑然独处，日夕哀鸣，越数日亦死。

    Explanation

    662

    In the place named Wei Ya Zi, a stork nested in a magpie's nest, and its father was killed by a crossbow. Soon after, a group gathered a male stork, and it paired with the mother. The mother lamented and rejected him a hundred times. The male stork, however, pecked and killed all four of her chicks. The mother became even more sorrowful and weak, eventually dying. The group of villains then took the male stork and fled.

    In a certain garden pavilion, there was an ancient tree where a magpie had built its nest. The magpie was incubating eggs and raising chicks. One day, two magpies circled the roof, mourning endlessly. After a while, several other magpies gathered, facing each other, and their calls gradually grew closer. All their heads turned towards the nest. Several magpies called with their beaks, as if they were talking to each other, and then flew away. A short while later, a stork flew across the sky, making a sound like a call. The magpies followed it. The group of magpies faced the stork and called, as if they were making a complaint. The stork responded with a sound, as if agreeing to their request. It quickly flew up, destroyed the nest, and swallowed a red snake. The magpies cheered and danced, as if celebrating and thanking the stork. It seems that the magpies had summoned the stork to fight the snake and save them.

    In the courtyard of the Dong family in Huating, there was a twisted pine tree with spreading branches and trunk, standing tall like an umbrella. Two storks had built their nests at the top. Later, the male stork was shot and killed by a crossbow. The female stork lived alone, mourning day and night. After several days, she also died.

    663

    泰州盐场僧寺，楼窗外树上，有鹳巢焉。雌鹳伏卵其间。村民伺雄觅食，潜以鹅卵易之，鹳不知也。久之，雏破卵出，则鹅也，雄鹳讶其不类，谓雌与他禽合，怒而噪之。雌者亦鸣不已。既而雄者飞去。少顷，诸鹳群集，视其雏，咸向雌而噪。雌者无以自明，以喙钻墙隙死。吴嘉纪野人作诗纪其事。
    　　　　黄莺
    　　　　有人取黄莺雏养于竹笼中，其雌雄接翼晓夜哀鸣于笼外，则更来哺之；人或在前，略无所畏。积数日不放出笼，其雄雌缭绕飞鸣，无从而入，一投火中，一触笼而死。剖腹视之，其肠寸断。
    　　　　鸳鸯
    　　　　成化六年十月，盐城天纵湖渔父，见鸳鸯甚多。一日，弋其雄者烹之，其雌者随棹飞鸣不去。渔父方启釜，即投沸汤中死。
    　　　　鹊
    　　　　大慈山之阳，有拱木，上有二鹊，各巢而生子。其母一为鸷鸟所搏，二子失母，其鸣啁啁。其一方哺子，见而怜之，赴而救之，即衔置一处，哺之若其子然。

    Explanation

    663

    Final Translation

    In the Buddhist temple in the salt fields of Taizhou, on a tree outside the window, there was a stork's nest. The female stork was incubating eggs. Villagers waited for the male to go out for food and secretly replaced the eggs with goose eggs. The storks were unaware of this. After a long time, when the chicks hatched, they were geese. The male stork was surprised by their difference and thought the female had mated with another bird. He angrily scolded her. The female also continued to call. Later, the male flew away. A short while later, many storks gathered, looked at the chicks, and scolded the female. The female had no way to prove her innocence and died by pecking through a wall crack. Wu Jiaji, a rustic, wrote a poem to record this event.

    The Oriole

    Someone took a young oriole and raised it in a bamboo cage. The male and female orioles called out sadly outside the cage day and night and would come to feed it. Even if someone was present, they were not afraid at all. After several days, the oriole was not released from the cage. The male and female orioles flew around and called, unable to enter. One flew into the fire and one touched the cage and died. When their bellies were cut open, their intestines were cut into pieces.

    The Mandarin Ducks

    In October of the sixth year of the Chenghua era, a fisherman from Tianzong Lake in Yancheng saw many mandarin ducks. One day, he shot the male and cooked it. The female followed the boat, flying and calling, refusing to leave. The fisherman was just about to open the pot, and the female flew into the boiling water and died.

    The Magpies

    On the sunny side of Daci Mountain, there was a large tree. There were two magpies, each building a nest and having offspring. One of the mothers was caught by a fierce bird. The two chicks lost their mother and called out. The other magpie, while feeding its own chicks, saw them and pitied them, rushing to save them. It immediately carried them to a place and fed them as if they were its own chicks.

    664

    鸽
    　　　　江浙平章巙巙家养二鸽。其雄毙于狸奴，家奴以他雄配之，遂斗而死。谢子兰作《义鸽诗》吊之。
    　　　　鹅
    　　　　天宝末，德清沈朝家有鹅，育卵而肠出以死。其雏悲鸣不复食，啄败荐复之，又衔刍草母前，若祭奠状，长吁数声而死。沈氏异而埋之，后人呼为“孝鹅冢”云。
    　　　　汤邻初焕佐郡江右，在任生女。及周，郡人馈以鹅，颈为盒担压折，折成“之”字，怜而畜之。后罢郡归，亲党又馈以鹅，乃缺一掌者，亦怜而畜之。一雌一雄，遂成配偶。雄曰“鸟郎”，雌曰“苍女”，呼其名，即应声至。行则让缺掌者先，食则让折颈者先。畜至三十余年，迨汤夫人殁，二鹅哀号数昼夜，绝食，偕死于柩下。
    　　　　常州陈四畜黑白二鹅，两窠相并，各哺数雏。一日黑者死，众雏失怙悲鸣。白者每晨至其窠，呼雏与己雏同啄。晚必先领归窠，始引己雏入宿。人皆见而义之。

    Explanation

    664

    Doves
    　　　　The governor of Jiangzhe, Kublai, kept two doves. One of the males died at the paws of a cat, and a servant mated it with another male, which led to a fight and the second male's death. Xie Zilan wrote a poem titled "Poem of the Righteous Dove" to mourn them.
    　　　　Geese
    　　　　At the end of the Tianbao era, a household in Deqing, owned by Shen Chao, had a goose that died while hatching eggs, with its intestines protruding. Its chick mourned and refused to eat, pecking at the bedding and covering it again, then carrying grass to the mother's grave, as if performing a funeral offering, and finally sighing several times before dying. The Shen family buried it, and later people called the grave "The Tomb of the Filial Goose."
    　　　　Tang Linchuhuan, an assistant governor in Jiangxi, had a daughter during his term. On her first birthday, the people of the county gave him a goose, whose neck was crushed by a box, forming a "之" shape. He pitied the goose and kept it. Later, when he returned home from his term, relatives gave him another goose, which had a missing foot. He also pitied this goose and kept it. The two geese, one male and one female, became a pair. The male was named "Birdman," and the female "Gray Lady." Calling their names, they would respond. When walking, the one with the missing foot went first, and when eating, the one with the broken neck went first. They were kept for over thirty years. When Mrs. Tang died, the two geese mourned for several days and nights, refused to eat, and died together under the coffin.
    　　　　Chen Si from Changzhou kept two geese, one white and one black, whose nests were side by side, each feeding several chicks. One day, the black goose died, and the chicks lost their mother and mourned. The white goose would visit the black goose's nest every morning, calling the chicks to feed with her own. In the evening, she would lead them back to their own nest first, then guide her own chicks to sleep. People saw this and praised the white goose's behavior as virtuous.

    665

    鸡
    　　　　衢州里胥至贫民家督赋，民只有一哺鸡，拟烹之。胥恍忽见桑林间有黄衣女子乞命。里胥惊恻。少间，见民持刀取哺鸡，意疑之，止勿杀。后再至，见鸡率群雏，向前踊跃，有似相感之状。胥行百步遇虎，忽见鸡飞扑虎眼，胥因奔免。
    　　　　象
    　　　　唐明皇尝教象拜舞。天宝之乱，禄山大宴其曹，出象绐之曰：“此象自南海奔来，知吾有天命，虽异类必拜舞。”左右命之拜，象皆努目昂首不肯拜；命之舞，努目敛足不肯舞。禄山怒，尽杀之。
    　　　　上元中，华容县有象入庄家中庭卧。其足下有槎，人为出之。象乃伏，令人骑入深山。以鼻掊土，得象牙数十以报之。
    　　　　元有驾象，明太祖登极，不肯拜跪，竟死殳下。
    　　　　明广西有象，封定南公。吴三桂入桂，欲将象解京，象昂首直触。象奴百计劝勉，终不服。三桂大怒，刀矢不能伤，以火炮攻毙之。

    Explanation

    665

    Translation

    Chicken

    In Quzhou, a village official went to a poor household to collect taxes. The family had only one chicken to feed them, and they planned to cook it. Suddenly, the official saw a woman in yellow clothing begging for her life in the mulberry grove. The official was startled and moved. A short while later, he saw the man holding a knife to slaughter the chicken. The official suspected something and stopped him from killing the chicken. When he returned later, he saw the chicken leading its chicks, jumping and running forward, as if showing a state of mutual affection. As the official walked a hundred steps, he encountered a tiger. Suddenly, the chicken flew and pounced on the tiger's eye, and the official fled and escaped.

    Elephant

    Emperor Xuanzong of Tang once trained an elephant to bow and dance. During the An Lushan Rebellion, An Lushan held a grand banquet for his subordinates and brought out the elephant, deceiving them by saying, "This elephant rushed from the South Sea, knowing I have a divine mandate, and even if it is a different species, it must bow and dance." His attendants ordered the elephant to bow, but the elephant glared and raised its head, refusing to bow; they then ordered it to dance, and the elephant glared and retracted its feet, refusing to dance. An Lushan became furious and killed them all.

    In the early Tang Dynasty, in Huarong County, there was an elephant that entered a farmyard and lay down in the courtyard. There was an obstacle under its feet, and a person removed it. The elephant then crouched down, allowing someone to ride it into the deep mountains. It dug into the soil with its trunk and found dozens of tusks to return as a gift.

    During the Yuan Dynasty, there was an elephant used as a mount. When Emperor Hongwu ascended the throne, the elephant refused to bow or kneel, and died under the spear.

    In the Ming Dynasty, there was an elephant in Guangxi that was bestowed the title of Duke of Dingnan. Wu Sangui entered Guangxi and wanted to send the elephant to the capital. The elephant raised its head and charged directly. The elephant handlers tried every method to persuade and encourage it, but it finally refused. Wu Sangui became furious, and neither knives nor arrows could harm it; he attacked and killed it with cannons.

    666

    鹿
    　　　　银台侯广成家，放一鹿于尧峰，且数年。侯死，鹿跳踯断角，累日不食，亦死。山僧怜而葬之，碣曰“义鹿冢”。
    　　　　熊
    　　　　晋升平中，有人入山射鹿，忽堕一坎内，见熊子数头。须臾，有大熊入，瞪视此人。人谓必害己，良久，大熊出果分与诸子，末后作一分着此人。此人饥久，冒死取啖之。既而转狎习。每旦，熊母觅食还，辄分果，此人赖以支命。后熊子大，其母一一负将出。子既出尽，此人自分死坎中，乃熊母复还，入坐人边。人解其意，便抱熊足，熊即跳出，遂得不死。
    　　　　虎
    　　　　后汉人都区宝者，居父丧。邻人格虎，走趋其庐中，即以簑衣覆藏之。邻人寻迹问，宝曰：“虎岂有可念而藏之乎？”后此虎送禽兽至，若助祭然。宝由是知名。
    　　　　上虞杨威，少失父，事母至孝。常与母入山采薪，为虎所逼。自计不能御，于是抱母，且号且行。虎见其情，遂弭耳去。

    Explanation

    666

    The Deer

    At the home of Duke Guangcheng of Yintai, a deer was released at Yao Peak, and it remained there for several years. After the duke's death, the deer, in its frantic state, broke its antlers and stopped eating for several days before it also died. A mountain monk felt pity and buried it, erecting a stone tablet that read "Righteous Deer Tomb."

    The Bear

    During the Jin Dynasty, a man entered the mountains to shoot a deer. Suddenly, he fell into a pit and saw several bear cubs. Soon after, a large bear entered, glaring at the man. The man thought he would surely be harmed, but after a long while, the bear brought out some fruit and divided it among the cubs, leaving a portion for the man. Having been hungry for a long time, the man dared to take and eat it. Gradually, the bear became familiar and accustomed to him. Every morning, the bear mother would return with food, always dividing the fruit, which the man relied on to sustain his life. Later, when the bear cubs grew up, their mother carried them one by one out of the pit. When all the cubs were taken out, the man thought he would die in the pit, but the bear mother returned, entered and sat by him. Understanding her intention, the man hugged the bear's foot, and the bear immediately jumped out, and he thus survived.

    The Tiger

    In the Han Dynasty, a man named Du Qubao was in mourning for his father. A neighbor trapped a tiger, which ran to his hut. Du Qubao immediately covered and hid the tiger with a rain cape. When the neighbor asked about the tiger's whereabouts, Du Qubao said, "How could a tiger be pitied and hidden?" Later, this tiger brought wild animals to him as if assisting in a sacrifice. Du Qubao thus became known.

    Yang Wei of Shangyu

    Yang Wei of Shangyu lost his father at a young age and was filial to his mother. He often went into the mountains with his mother to gather firewood. Once, they were cornered by a tiger. Thinking he could not defend himself, he hugged his mother and wept while walking. The tiger, seeing his sincerity, quietly left.

    667

    猿猴
    　　　　唐昭宗有猿，随班起居，赐以绯袍。朱梁篡位，取此猿令殿下起居。猿见全忠，径趋其所，跳跃奋击。遂令杀之。
    　　　　吉州有捕猿，杀其母，以皮并其子卖之龙泉萧氏。示以母皮，抱之跳踯，遂毙。萧氏子为作《孝猿传》。
    　　　　邓芝射中猿母，见猿子为拔箭，以木叶塞疮口，悲哀不已，为母吮血。芝遂投弩而叹曰：“山兽犹哀母，人可不如猿？吾不猎矣！”
    　　　　咸熙中，有翁媪弄猴于瑞昌门外。一日媼死，翁葬之。未几翁死，无人葬。猴守之。日久，人怜而葬之，咸称为“义猴”。
    　　　　正德辛巳，有夫妇以弄猴为衣食者，十年矣，寓于嘉州之白塔山。主者死，葬于塔之左；猴日夜号。其妇更招一丐者为夫，猴举首揶揄之。妇弄猴使作技，猴伏地不为，鞭之辄奋叫。入夜，走主者之墓，抱土悲号，七日而死。

    Explanation

    667

    Apes and Monkeys

    Emperor Zhaozong of Tang had an ape that accompanied him in his daily life and was bestowed with a red robe. When Zhu Wen usurped the throne and established the Later Liang, he took this ape to accompany the prince. The ape, seeing Zhu Wen, rushed directly to him, jumping and attacking vigorously. Consequently, he ordered the ape to be killed.

    In Ji Prefecture, there was a captured ape whose mother was killed, and both the skin and the child were sold to the Xiao family of Longquan. When shown the skin of its mother, the young ape hugged it, jumping around, and then died. The son of the Xiao family wrote a biography of the filial monkey.

    Deng Zhi shot the mother of an ape and saw the young ape remove the arrow and use a leaf to plug the wound. The young ape continued to grieve and suckled blood for its mother. Deng Zhi then threw down his bow and sighed, saying, "Even mountain beasts mourn their mothers; can humans be less filial than apes? I will no longer hunt!"

    During the Xianxi era, an elderly couple entertained a monkey outside Ruiyang Gate. One day, the old woman died, and the old man buried her. Shortly after, the old man died, and there was no one to bury him. The monkey guarded the grave. After a long time, people pitied it and buried it, and they all called it the "righteous monkey."

    In the Xinhai year of Zhengde, a couple who made a living from entertaining a monkey had been doing so for ten years, living in the White Pagoda Mountain in Jia Prefecture. The owner died and was buried to the left of the pagoda. The monkey cried day and night. The wife then took in a beggar as a new husband, and the monkey raised its head and mocked him. The wife made the monkey perform tricks, but the monkey lay on the ground and refused, and when she whipped it, it would shout. At night, it went to the owner's grave, hugged the earth, and cried, and died after seven days.

    668

    汪学使可受，初尹金华。有丐者行山中，见群儿缚一小猴而虐之。丐者买而教之戏，日乞于市，得钱甚多。他丐忌且羡，因酒醉丐者，诱至空窑，椎杀于窑中。异日绳其猴，复使作戏。而汪公呵导声遽至，猴即啮断绳，突走公之前，作冤诉状。公遣人随而往，得尸窑中。亟捕他丐鞫问，伏法。合邑骇而悼之，买棺焚丐者尸。烈焰方发，猴哀叫跃入，死矣。
    　　　　牛
    　　　　齐河县洪店，有盗杀人于王臻户前。众执臻，已诬服久矣。知县赵清过洪店，一牛奔清前，跪而悲鸣，若有所诉。清曰：“谁氏之牛？”众曰：“王臻牛也。”清曰：“臻其有冤乎？”抵邑，即辩释臻父子。后鞫大盗王山，得其杀人状。齐河人称神明，作《义牛记》。

    Explanation

    668

    Mr. Wang, the scholar-official, initially served as governor of Jinhua Prefecture. One day, a beggar was walking in the mountains and saw a group of children tying up a small monkey and maltreating it. The beggar bought the monkey and taught it to perform tricks, begging in the market every day and earning a lot of money. Other beggars envied and took advantage of him, luring him to an abandoned kiln and beating him to death. Later, they tied up the monkey again and made it perform tricks. Suddenly, Mr. Wang arrived, shouting and guiding, and the monkey bit through the rope, ran to him, and made a petition of grievance. Mr. Wang sent people to follow the monkey and found the body in the kiln. He immediately arrested the other beggars and interrogated them, leading to their confession and punishment. The entire county was shocked and mourned, and they bought a coffin to burn the beggar's body. As the fierce flames were just starting, the monkey cried out in sorrow, jumped into the fire, and died.

    In the village of Hongdian in Qihé County, a notorious bandit killed someone in front of Wang Zhen's house. The crowd arrested Wang Zhen, and he had already been wrongfully convicted for a long time. The magistrate Zhao Qing passed through Hongdian, and a cow ran to him, knelt down, and mournfully neighed, as if there was something to report. Zhao Qing asked, "Whose cow is this?" The crowd replied, "It is Wang Zhen's cow." Zhao Qing said, "Does Wang Zhen have a grievance?" Upon arriving at the county, he immediately acquitted Wang Zhen and his son. Later, he interrogated the notorious bandit Wang Shan and found murder charges against him. The people of Qihé County called him a god and wrote "The Story of the Righteous Cow."

    669

    天长县民戴某朝出，其妻牧牛于野。平昔豢犬随之，俄入草芥不出。戴妻牵牛寻之，未百步，见虎据丛而食犬。虎见人至，弃犬趋人。戴已为虎搏矣。牛见主有难，忿然而前。虎又释人而应牛。二物交加哮吼，虎张爪牙，牛以二角奔击。逾时，牛竟胜虎，戴乃得免。
    　　　　嘉靖乙卯，胡抚镇贤统兵御倭，至临山，少憩树下。见屠儿将解一牛，一犊尚随乳，将利刃衔至车沟内，以蹄蹈没泥中，屠儿遍索不获。
    　　　　犬
    　　　　孙吴时，襄阳纪信纯，一犬名乌龙，行住相随。一日，城外大醉，归家不及，卧草中。太守邓瑕出猎，纵火爇草，犬以口衔纯衣，不动。有溪相去三、五十步，犬入水湿身，来卧处周回，以身湿之。火至湿处即灭。犬困乏，致毙于侧。信纯获免，醒见犬死毛湿，观火踪迹，因而痛哭。闻于太守，命具棺衾葬之。今纪南有“义犬冢”，高十余丈。

    Explanation

    669

    A man from Tianchang County named Dai went out in the morning, and his wife grazed cattle in the fields. She usually kept a dog as a pet that followed her everywhere. Soon, the dog entered the grass and did not come out. Dai's wife led the cattle to search for it. They had not gone more than a hundred steps when they saw a tiger sitting in the bushes eating the dog. The tiger saw the person coming and abandoned the dog to approach the person. Dai was already being attacked by the tiger. The cattle saw the master in danger and angrily moved forward. The tiger then released the person and attacked the cattle. The two animals roared and growled at each other. The tiger spread its claws and teeth, and the cattle charged with its two horns. After some time, the cattle defeated the tiger, and Dai was saved.

    In the year Jiajing Bimao (1555), General Hu Xian led troops to fight against the Japanese pirates and arrived at Linshan, where he rested under a tree. He saw a butcher preparing to slaughter a cow, and a calf was still nursing. The butcher took the sharp knife and buried it in the mud with his hoof. He searched everywhere but could not find it.

    There was a dog.

    During the period of the Three Kingdoms, a man from Xiangyang named Ji Xunchun had a dog named Wulong that followed him wherever he went. One day, he drank heavily outside the city and did not make it home in time, so he lay down in the grass. Governor Deng Xia went hunting and set fire to the grass. The dog held Ji Xunchun's clothes in its mouth and did not move. There was a stream about thirty to fifty steps away. The dog went into the water to get wet and then came back to wet the grass around Ji Xunchun. The fire went out when it reached the wet grass. The dog became exhausted and died beside Ji Xunchun. Ji Xunchun was saved, woke up to see the dog dead with wet fur, and saw the fire's traces, and thus cried out in pain. He reported this to the governor, who ordered a coffin and shroud to bury the dog. Today in Jinan, there is a "Loyal Dog Tomb" that is over ten zhang tall.

    670

    晋泰兴二年，吴人华隆，好弋猎。畜一犬，号曰“的尾”，每将自随。隆后至江边，被一大蛇围绕周身，犬遂咋蛇死焉，而华隆僵卧无所知矣。犬彷徨嗥吠，往复路间。家人怪其如此，因随犬往，隆闷绝委地，载归，二日乃苏。隆未苏之际，犬终不食。
    　　　　太和中，杨生养狗，甚爱之。后生醉酒，行大泽，草中眠。时冬月，野火起，风又猛。狗号呼，生不觉。前有一坑水，狗便走往水中，还以身洒生左右。草沾水得着地，火寻过去。他日又闇行，堕于空井中，狗呻吟彻晓。有人过，怪之，往视，见生在井。生曰：“君可出我，当厚报君！”人曰：“以此狗相与，便当相出。”生曰：“此狗曾活我于已死，不得相与。余无所惜。”人曰：“若尔，便不相出。”狗因下头向井。生知其意，乃语人，以狗相与。人乃出之，系狗而去。后五日，狗夜走归。

    Explanation

    670

    During the second year of the Tai Xing era of the Jin Dynasty, a man from the Wu state named Hua Long enjoyed hunting. He kept a dog, which he named "The Tail," and always accompanied him. Later, Hua Long reached the riverside, where he was wrapped around the entire body by a large snake. The dog bit the snake and killed it, but Hua Long lay motionless and unconscious. The dog wandered and barked back and forth along the path. Hua Long's family was puzzled by this and followed the dog. They found Hua Long unconscious and collapsed to the ground; they carried him home, and after two days he revived. While Hua Long was still unconscious, the dog refused to eat.

    During the middle of the Taihe era of the Jin Dynasty, Yang Sheng kept a dog and loved it very much. Later, Yang Sheng got drunk and lay down to sleep in the grass in a vast marsh. It was winter, and a wild fire broke out, with the wind blowing fiercely. The dog barked and called out, but Yang Sheng did not notice. There was a pit of water ahead, and the dog ran to the water, then splashed water over Yang Sheng's body. The grass, wetted by the water, touched the ground, and the fire soon passed by. One day, while walking in the dark, he fell into an empty well. The dog groaned all night. A passerby was puzzled and went to look, finding Yang Sheng in the well. Yang Sheng said, "You can save me, and I will reward you generously!" The passerby replied, "If you give me this dog, I will save you." Yang Sheng said, "This dog once saved me from death; I cannot give it to you. I have nothing else to spare." The passerby said, "If you do that, I won't save you." The dog lowered its head toward the well. Yang Sheng understood the dog's intention and told the passerby, offering the dog in exchange. The passerby tied up the dog and left. Five days later, the dog ran back home at night.

    671

    袁粲值萧道成将革命，自以身受顾托，谋起义，遂遇害。有儿方数岁，乳母携投粲门生狄灵庆。庆曰：“吾闻出郎君者厚賞。”乳母号呼曰：“公昔有恩于汝，故冒难归汝。若杀郎君以求利，神明有知，行见汝族灭也！”儿竟死。儿存时，尝骑一大{宁毛}狗戏。死后年余，忽有狗入庆家，遇庆入庭，啮杀之，并其妻。即向所骑狗也。
    　　　　饶州乐平民章华，元和初，尝养一犬。每樵采入山，犬必随。三年冬，比舍有王华者，同上山采柴，犬亦随之。忽有一虎榛中跃出，搏王华，盘踞于地，然犹未伤。章华叫喝且走，虎遂舍王华，来趁章华。既获，复坐之。时犬潜在深草，见章被衔，突出跃上虎头，咋虎之鼻。虎不意其来，惊惧而走。二人皆僵卧如沉醉者。其犬以鼻袭章口取气，即吐出涎水，如此数次，章稍苏。犬乃复以口袭王华之口，亦如前状。良久，王华能行，相引而起。犬惫，伏不能起，一夕而毙。

    671

    Translated Text:
    Yuan Can encountered Xiao Daocheng, who was about to usurp the throne, and considered himself as having been entrusted with a responsibility. He planned to rebel and was subsequently killed. He had a child who was just a few years old. The wet nurse took the child to Yuan Can's disciple Di Lingqing. Di Lingqing said: "I heard that those who betray the young master will receive a generous reward." The wet nurse cried out: "You once had kindness for me, so I took the risk to entrust the child to you. If you kill the young master for profit, may the gods see that your family will be destroyed!" The child eventually died. When the child was alive, he once rode a large Ningmao dog for fun. More than a year after the child's death, a dog suddenly entered Di Lingqing's house, encountered him in the courtyard, and bit him and his wife to death. It was the same dog that the child had ridden.

    Zhang Hua, a commoner from Le Ping in Raozhou, during the early Yuanhe period, once kept a dog. Every time he went into the mountains to gather firewood, the dog would accompany him. In the third year of winter, a neighbor named Wang Hua went up the mountain with him to collect firewood, and the dog followed them as well. Suddenly, a tiger leaped out from the dense underbrush and attacked Wang Hua, lying down on the ground, but it did not yet harm him. Zhang Hua shouted and ran away, and the tiger left Wang Hua to chase Zhang Hua. After catching him, the tiger sat on top of him. At that moment, the dog was hiding in the deep grass, saw Zhang Hua being bitten, and suddenly jumped onto the tiger's head, biting its nose. The tiger was surprised and frightened, and ran away. Both men lay motionless as if they were drunk. The dog then used its nose to touch Zhang Hua's mouth to breathe, and immediately spat out saliva, doing this several times until Zhang Hua began to recover. The dog then repeated this action with Wang Hua's mouth, in the same manner. After a long time, Wang Hua was able to move, and they helped each other up. The dog was exhausted and could not get up, and died that night.

    672

    唐禁军大校齐琼，家畜良犬四，常畋回广囿，辄饲以粱肉。其一独填茹咽喉齿牙间以出，如隐丛薄，然后食，食已，则复至。齐窃异之。一日令仆伺其所往，则北垣枯窦，有母存焉，老瘠疥秽，吐哺以饲。齐奇叹久之，乃命箧牝犬归，以败茵席之，余饼饵饱之。犬则摇尾俯首，若怀知感。尔后擒奸逐狡，指顾如飞将，扈猎驾前，必获丰赏。逾年牝死，犬加勤效。后齐卒，犬日夜嗥吠，越月，将有事于丘陇，则留犬以御奸盗。及悬棺之夕，犬独来，足踣土城，拗首叩棺见血。掩土未毕，犬亦至毙。

    Explanation

    672

    In the Tang dynasty, there was a prominent military officer named Qi Jiong, who kept four excellent dogs as pets. Whenever he returned from hunting in a large enclosed hunting ground, he would always feed them with high-quality grain and meat. One of the dogs would chew something in its throat and teeth and then come out, as if hiding in thick vegetation, before eating. After eating, it would return. Qi Jiong found this behavior strange. One day, he ordered a servant to watch where the dog went, and discovered that it had gone to a dry hole in the north wall, where there was an old, thin, and dirty mother. The dog would spit out food to feed the mother. Qi Jiong was amazed and expressed admiration for a long time. He then ordered the female dog to be stored and returned, using worn-out mats or carpets, and fed it with leftover biscuits or cakes. The dog wagged its tail and lowered its head, as if it had a sense of gratitude or appreciation. Later, the dog captured evil and chased away the cunning, acting like a flying general, and accompanied the emperor during hunting, always receiving rich rewards. After a year, the female dog died, and the dog became even more diligent and served more effectively. After Qi Jiong died, the dog howled and barked day and night. A month later, when there was a task to be performed at the grave, the dog was left to guard against thieves. When the coffin was suspended, the dog came alone, stumbled against the earth wall, and barked at the coffin, causing blood to appear. Before the earth was fully covered, the dog also died.

    673

    会稽张然滞役，有少妇无子，唯与一奴守舍，奴遂与妇通焉。然素养一犬，名“乌龙”，常以自随。后归，奴欲谋杀然，盛作饮食。妇曰：“与君当大別离，君可强啖！”奴已张弓拔矢，须然食毕。然涕泣不能食，以肉及饭掷狗，祝曰：“养汝经年，吾当将死，汝能救我否？”犬得食，不啖，唯注眼视奴。然拍膝大呼曰：“乌龙！”犬应声伤奴。奴失刀遂倒，狗咋其阴。然因取刀杀奴，以妻付县杀之。
    　　　　五代南唐时，江州陈氏，族七百口，畜犬百余，共一牢而食。一犬不至，诸犬不食。
    　　　　上党人卢言，尝见一犬羸瘦将死，悯而收养。一日醉寝，而邻火发。犬忙迫，乃上床于言首噑吠，又衔衣拽之。言惊起，火已爇其屋柱，突烟而出，始得免。

    Explanation

    673

    Zhang Ran from Kuaiji was detained in service. He had a young wife without children, and only one servant to guard the house. The servant then had an affair with the woman. Zhang Ran kept a dog named "Wulong," which he always had with him. After returning home, the servant wanted to kill Zhang Ran and prepared food and drink. The woman said, "We will part forever, please eat!" The servant had already drawn his bow and arrow, waiting for Zhang Ran to finish eating. Zhang Ran wept and could not eat, so he threw meat and rice to the dog and said, "Having raised you for a year, I am about to die, can you save me?" The dog took the food but did not eat it, just stared at the servant with its eyes. Zhang Ran pounded his knee and shouted, "Wulong!" The dog responded by attacking the servant. The servant dropped his knife and fell down, and the dog bit his genitalia. Zhang Ran then took the knife and killed the servant, and handed the woman over to the county authorities to be executed.

    During the reign of the Southern Tang dynasty in the Five Dynasties, the Chen family from Jiangzhou had a clan of seven hundred people and kept over a hundred dogs. They all ate together in one pen. If one dog did not come, the others would not eat.

    Lu Yan from Shangdang once saw a dog that was thin and about to die. He pitied it and took it in. One day, while drunk and asleep, a fire broke out in the neighbor's house. The dog rushed over, barked at Yan's head, and bit his clothes, pulling him. Yan was startled awake, and the fire had already reached his house pillar. He rushed out through the smoke and narrowly escaped.

    674

    扶风县西有大和寺，在高岗之上，其下有龛，豁若堂。中有贫者赵叟家焉。叟无妻儿，病足伛偻，常策杖行邑里中。人哀其老病，且穷无所归，率给以食。叟既得食，常先聚群犬以食之。后岁余，叟病寒，卧于龛中。时大雪无衣，裸形俯地，且战且呻。其群犬俱聚于叟前，摇尾而嗥，已而环其袵席，竟以身蔽叟体，由是寒少解。后旬余，竟以寒死其龛中。犬皆哀鸣，昼夜不歇，数日方去。
    　　　　杨光远叛于青州，有孙中舍居围城中，族在西州别墅。城闭久，食尽，举家愁叹。犬彷徨其侧，似有忧思。中舍因囑曰：“尔能为我至庄取米耶？”犬摇尾若应状。至夜，置一布囊，并简系犬背上。犬由水窦出，至庄鸣吠。居者开门，识其犬，取简视之，令负米还。如此数月，以至城开。孙氏合门赖以不馁。愈爱畜此犬。后数年毙，葬于别墅。至其孙彭年，语龙图赵师民，刻石表其墓，日“灵犬志”。

    Explanation

    674

    In the west of Fufeng County, there was a Great Harmony Temple situated on a high hill. Beneath the hill, there was a niche that was spacious like a hall. Inside this niche lived a poor old man named Zhao. Zhao had no wife or children; he suffered from a bent back due to illness and old age, and he often walked with a cane through the village. People pitied his old age and illness, as well as his poverty and lack of a home, and generally provided him with food. Whenever Zhao received food, he would first gather a group of dogs to feed them. After more than a year, Zhao fell ill with a cold and lay in the niche. It was a time of heavy snow, and he had no clothes, lying naked on the ground, shivering and groaning. His group of dogs gathered in front of him, wagging their tails and barking, then circled his bedding, finally covering his body with their own, thereby somewhat alleviating the cold. After about ten days, Zhao ultimately died of cold in the niche. The dogs mournfully howled, day and night without stopping, for several days before they left.

    During the rebellion of Yang Guangyuan in Qingzhou, there was a man named Sun Zhongsha living in a besieged city, while his family resided in a villa in Xizhou. The city was closed for a long time, and food ran out, causing the entire family to grieve and sigh. The dog wandered around him, seeming to have worries. Sun Zhongsha then told the dog, "Can you go to the estate and fetch rice for me?" The dog wagged its tail as if agreeing. At night, he placed a cloth bag and tied a note to the dog's back. The dog went out through a water hole, reached the estate, and barked. The residents opened the door, recognized the dog, took the note and read it, then instructed the dog to carry rice back. This continued for several months, until the city was opened. The Sun family depended on this to avoid starvation. They loved and raised the dog even more. After several years, the dog died and was buried in the villa. When it came to Sun Pengnian, his grandson, he told Longtu Zhao Shimin, who carved a stone tablet to mark the dog's grave, calling it "The Record of the Spirit Dog."

    675

    淳熙中，王日就，字成德，分水县人，少负侠气。夜猎，从骑四出。有畜犬，呜呜衔衣，捶之不却，且道且前。怪之，亟随以归。明日复视其处，虎迹纵横，叹曰：“犬，人畜也，犹知爱主。吾奉父母遗体，不自爱，可乎？”遂散其徒读书。
    　　　　湖州颜氏，夫妇出佣，留五岁女守家，溺门前池内。家有畜犬，入水负至岸，复狂奔至佣主家作呼导状。颜惊骇归家，见女伏地，奄奄气息，急救乃苏。
    　　　　滁州一寺僧被盗杀死，徒往报官，畜犬尾其后。至一酒肆中，盗方群聚纵饮，犬忽奔噬盗足。众以为异，执之到官，讯服。

    Explanation

    675

    During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song, Wang Rijiu, courtesy name Chengde, was a man from Fenshui County. He was young and had a spirit of chivalry. One night, he went out hunting with four horsemen. His dog barked and bit his clothes, refusing to let him go, and led him forward while barking. Finding this strange, he quickly followed the dog back home. The next day, he returned to the spot and found tiger tracks scattered about. He sighed and said, "Dogs are animals kept by humans, yet they still know to love their masters. How can I, who have received my body from my parents, not take care of myself?" Thus, he disbanded his followers and devoted himself to studying.

    In Huzhou, there was a family named Yan. The husband and wife went out to work, leaving their five-year-old daughter to watch the house. She drowned in the pond in front of the house. The family had a dog that jumped into the water and carried her to the shore. The dog then wildly ran to the master's house, making a calling and guiding sound. The Yan family was terrified and rushed home to find their daughter lying on the ground, barely breathing. They rushed to save her, and she eventually recovered.

    In Chuzhou, a monk at a temple was killed by bandits. His disciples went to report the crime to the authorities, and the temple's dog followed them. They arrived at an inn where the bandits were drinking together. Suddenly, the dog rushed at the bandits' feet and bit them. The crowd found this strange and took the bandits to the authorities, who interrogated them and made them confess.

    676

    沈处士恒吉，尝畜一金丝犬，长不过尺，甚驯。处士日宴客，犬必卧几下。后三载，处士病，犬即不食。数日，处士卒，殓于正寝，犬盘旋而号，竞夕方罢。停柩者期年，犬日夜卧其侧。将葬，遂一触而毙。
    　　　　刘釗，铁岭卫人，畜一犬，出入必从。钊常以马负薪山中，犬亦从。一日，犬忽独归，向钊子国勋鸣跃不已。勋异之，随其所往，见钊为盗所杀，弃尸石间，取其马去。勋为营葬毕，人皆罢归，犬独守冢不去，日夜悲泣，泪湿草土。数日，抉土及棺，死棺旁。
    　　　　淮安城中民家，有母犬，烹而食之。其三子犬，各衔母骨抱土埋之，伏地悲鸣不绝。里人见而异之，共传为孝犬。
    　　　　常州芮氏，家贫，日饲犬以糠粃。其邻为富室姚氏，犬多余食，所限仅一小竹篱。姚犬每向篱窦低声摇尾，若招呼状。芮犬蟠曲卧地，唯昂首相应，绝不过食其余粒。如是以为常。

    Explanation

    676

    Shen Hengji, a recluse, once kept a golden-haired dog, no more than a foot long, which was very tame. Shen would entertain guests every day, and the dog would always lie beneath the table. After three years, Shen fell ill, and the dog stopped eating. A few days later, Shen died and was laid to rest in his main chamber. The dog circled and howled, continuing until late into the night. For a year, the dog lay beside the coffin day and night. On the day of the burial, it died upon touching the coffin.

    Liu Zhao, a resident of Tieling Wei, kept a dog that always followed him wherever he went. Liu would often carry firewood on horseback into the mountains, and the dog would accompany him. One day, the dog suddenly returned alone and signaled to Liu's son Guo Xun by barking and jumping. Guo Xun was surprised and followed the dog, discovering that Liu had been killed by bandits, his body left in a stone crevice, and his horse taken away. After Guo Xun had arranged for Liu's burial, everyone else returned home, but the dog stayed behind at the grave, weeping day and night, its tears wetting the grass and earth. After several days, the dog dug through the soil until it reached the coffin and died beside it.

    In a household in Huai'an, a mother dog was cooked and eaten. Her three puppies, each carrying a piece of her bones, buried them in the ground, lying on the ground and weeping continuously. The villagers were amazed by this and began to call the puppies "filial dogs."

    In Changzhou, the Rui family was poor and fed their dog with bran. Their neighbor, the wealthy Yao family, had an abundance of food for their dog, which was separated from the Rui family's dog by a small bamboo fence. The Yao family's dog would often wag its tail and make low sounds near the fence, as if calling. The Rui family's dog would lie curled up on the ground, raising its head to respond, but never eating any of the food from the other side. This became their regular routine.

    677

    马
    　　　　秦叔宝所乘马，号“忽雷驳”，常饮以酒。每于月明中试，能竖越三领黑毡。及胡公卒，嘶鸣不食而死。
    　　　　伪蜀渠阳邻山，有富民王行思，尝养一马，甚爱之，饲秣甚于他马。一日乘往本郡，遇夏潦暴涨。舟子先渡马，回舟以迎行思，至中流，风起船覆。其马自岸奔入骇浪，接其主，苍茫之中，遽免沉溺。
    　　　　毕再遇，兗州将家也。开禧中，用兵累有功，金人认其旗帜即避之。后居于霅。有战马，号“黑大虫”，骏驵异常，独主翁能御之。再遇死，其家以铁絙羁之圉中。适遇岳祠迎神，闻金鼓声，意谓赴敌。马嘶，奋迅断垣而出。其家虑伤人，命健卒十人挽之而归，因好言戒之云：“将军已死，汝莫生事累我家！“马耸耳以听，汪然出涕，喑哑长鸣数声而毙。

    Explanation

    677

    Horses

    The horse ridden by Qin Shubao was named "Hulei Bo." It was often given wine to drink. Every time it was tested under the moonlight, it could jump over three layers of black felt. When General Hu died, the horse neighed and refused to eat, then died.

    In the vicinity of Quyang Mountain in pseudo-Shu, there was a wealthy man named Wang Xingsi. He once raised a horse, which he loved very much, and fed it better than other horses.
